Weekly Research Papers — Innovation Rankings

Papers from Google Scholar alerts & email — Jun 20 – Jun 27, 2026

189 Papers Ranked
41 Thematic Groups

Innovation Ranking Criteria

Papers are ranked from most to least innovative based on five criteria: (1) novelty of problem formulation or paradigm shift, (2) creativity of methodology, (3) surprisingness of results or insights, (4) potential impact on the field, and (5) degree of departure from existing approaches. Each paper includes a justification explaining its placement.

≥90 Groundbreaking
≥75 Highly Innovative
≥60 Innovative
≥40 Moderately Innovative
≥20 Incremental
<20 Routine

Innovation Rankings

#1 — Highly Innovative (80)

OpenThoughts-Agent: Data Recipes for Agentic Models (27 ▲ )

Negin Raoof, Richard Zhuang, Marianna Nezhurina, Etash Guha, Atula Tejaswi, Ryan Marten, Charlie F. Ruan, Tyler Griggs, Alexander Glenn Shaw, Hritik Bansal, E. Kelly Buchanan, Artem Gazizov, Reinhard Heckel, Chinmay Hegde, Sankalp Jajee, Daanish Khazi, Emmanouil Koukoumidis, Xiangyi Li, Hange Liu, Shlok Natarajan, Harsh Raj, Nicholas Roberts, Ethan Shen, Nishad Singhi, Michael Siu, Ashima Suvarna, Hanwen Xing, Patrick Yubeaton, Robert Zhang, Leon Liangyu Chen, Xiaokun Chen, Steven Dillmann, Saadia Gabriel, Xunyi Jiang, Anurag Kashyap, Boxuan Li, Yein Park, Minh Pham, Sujay Sanghavi, Lin Shi, Ke Sun, Yixin Wang, Zhiwei Xu, Erica Zhang, Siyan Zhao, Wanjia Zhao, Jenia Jitsev, Alex Dimakis, Benjamin Feuer, Ludwig Schmidt · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a six-stage SFT data curation pipeline (OpenThoughts-Agent) validated through 100+ controlled ablation experiments, along with a 100K-example training set and OpenThinkerAgent-32B, the strongest open-data agentic model at ≤32B scale averaged across seven diverse benchmarks. - Why it's important: Prior open data efforts (SWE-Smith, SERA, Nemotron-Terminal) each target a single benchmark, leaving the community without actionable guidance on which data choices drive generalization across diverse agentic tasks — a gap that makes it nearly impossible to improve broadly capable open agents systematically. - How it does it: Each of six pipeline stages (task sourcing, mixing, augmentation, filtering, teacher selection, rollout filtering) is ablated independently by training Qwen3-8B on 10K trajectories and measuring z-score-normalized accuracy across three core benchmarks; a synthetic task augmentation strategy then overcomes the task-diversity bottleneck that causes upsampling to plateau. - The good: Task source selection accounts for up to 30pp variation on SWE-Bench Verified-100 and the final model reaches 54.0% on SWE-Bench Verified and 26.2% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, outperforming Nemotron-Terminal-32B (41.9% and 25.1%) while also generalizing to five held-out OOD benchmarks. - The missing: The RL investigation is limited to 8B scale due to compute constraints, so whether the SFT+RL recipe transfers to 32B is untested; the base model contribution (Qwen3 pretraining) is never isolated from data pipeline effects. - Read it or not: Essential for anyone building or studying open-data agentic model training pipelines; the empirical prescriptions are directly actionable and would be expensive to rediscover independently.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (88) is the decisive driver and is exceptional for this cluster: standard error across three stochastic re-runs, a compute-controlled ablation (Table 15) that disentangles token-budget from trajectory-quality, held-out OOD benchmarks evaluated only after pipeline decisions are frozen, and full release of data, code, and weights. Novelty (70) is solid through breadth rather than a single new mechanism, with counter-intuitive findings (teacher-model inversion, augmentation futility, diversity-as-bottleneck) that contradict field assumptions. Non-Obviousness (78) is high because the 'stronger teacher is a worse teacher' and task-source-dominance results are surprising. Completeness (85) and Resilience (80) are strong: limitations confronted directly, leakage prevented, run-to-run variance formally characterized. Practical Impact (80) reflects the released dataset, pipeline, and the strongest open-data agentic model at <=32B. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*88+0.15*78+0.15*85+0.15*80+0.10*80 = 80. This is the only paper in the cluster that reaches the Highly Innovative band, and it most closely resembles the user's positive calibration set (controlled, multi-lineage, rigor-first).
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 88 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 85 Resilience 80 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Training broadly capable agentic language models requires high-quality (task, trajectory) pairs for supervised fine-tuning, but publicly documented curation pipelines are scarce. Existing efforts — SWE-Smith, SERA, Nemotron-Terminal, OpenSWE — optimize for a single benchmark (SWE-Bench or Terminal-Bench) and provide little detail on data choices. As a result, the open research community cannot understand which curation decisions drive generalization across the diverse agentic tasks agents encounter in practice: software engineering, terminal use, tool calling, healthcare, finance, and general assistance. The gap is illustrated by DeepSeekV4, whose 50-page paper describes training data in only two paragraphs despite open-sourcing its weights. Key Idea The central insight is that task source selection is the dominant lever in agentic SFT pipelines — causing a 30pp spread on SWE-Bench Verified-100 across 95 sourcing strategies, larger than any other pipeline stage — and that stronger teacher models are not better teachers. GLM-4.7-AWQ, a weaker benchmark performer, outperforms GPT-5.3-Codex by roughly 5pp on Terminal-Bench 2.0 when used as the trajectory-generation teacher. A complementary insight is that task-description diversity, not rollout volume from fixed sources, determines whether performance continues improving beyond ~32K examples: synthetic augmentation of task descriptions unlocks continued gains to 100K, while upsampling plateaus. Methodology The pipeline has six stages, each ablated independently with Qwen3-8B fine-tuned on 10K trajectories (160 GPU-hours per run on GH200s), evaluated by computing per-benchmark z-scores and averaging across three core benchmarks (SWE-Bench Verified-100, OT-TBLite, Terminal-Bench 2.0). Stage 1 (Source Tasks) evaluates 95 task generation strategies spanning synthetic issue resolution, human-written infrastructure questions, competitive programming, and more; top performers include SWESmith, StackExchange SuperUser/Tezos, and IssueTasks. Stage 2 (Mix Tasks) evaluates top-N mixing with uniform sampling; top-4 to top-8 outperforms single-source by avoiding over-specialization. Stage 3 (Augment Tasks) tests LLM-driven augmentation (Constrain→Harden, multi-source mixing, trace hints); no intervention reliably outperforms the un-augmented baseline. Stage 4 (Filter Tasks) uses GPT-5 response length as a difficulty proxy; selecting the longest-response tasks yields +3pp average improvement over random. Stage 5 (Teacher Model) ablates five proprietary or semi-open teachers; GLM-4.7-AWQ outperforms GPT-5.3-Codex by ~5pp on Terminal-Bench 2.0. Stage 6 (Filter Rollouts) applies heuristics; retaining trajectories with ≥5 turns outperforms timeout-filtering and subagent-trace removal, and a compute-controlled ablation (Table 15, matched ~145M token budget) confirms the gain reflects trajectory quality rather than additional training compute. The final 100K dataset uses synthetic instruction rewriting to expand the Tezos subset from 997 to 21K+ unique task descriptions, resolving the diversity bottleneck. For RL, the paper ablates eight data sources on 8B models using RLOO with binary rewards from verifier success; pymethods2test (competitive programming recast as Python contracts) ranks first. The SFT+RL combination starts from a moderately strong SFT checkpoint, consistent with prior findings that undertrained SFT models benefit more from RL. Key Results OpenThinkerAgent-32B achieves 44.8% average across seven agentic benchmarks: 54.0% on SWE-Bench Verified (500 tasks), 26.2% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, 85.9% on BFCL-Parity, 47.8% on MedAgentBench, 23.6% on GAIA-127, and 44.0% on FinanceAgent-Terminal. This is 3.9pp above Nemotron-Terminal-32B (40.9%) and exceeds all open-data ≤32B models in Table 1 on the average. Scaling curves show the OT-Agent dataset outperforms Nemotron-Terminal-Corpus at every training set size in compute-controlled comparisons (Figure 1). At 8B scale, OT-Agent-ColdSFT+RL-8B achieves 27.9% average across seven benchmarks, above the strongest existing ≤8B baselines. Task source ablation produces the largest spread of any pipeline stage: Terminal-Bench 2.0 accuracy ranges from 10.9% (worst source) to 0.4% (best source) when varying only source, confirming sourcing dominates. Run-to-run RL reproducibility is characterized at ~1.6pp ID and ~2.0pp OOD spread, substantially above benchmark evaluation noise and substantially below the reported SFT and RL gains. The behavioral analysis of RL (Appendix F) reveals that pymethods2test induces genuine exploratory expansion (think tokens +116%, tool calls +31%, self-correction +81%) while llm-verifier-freelancer induces compaction, and an LLM judge (gpt-5-2025-08-07) prefers the post-RL hero policy on 83.3% of 30 same-task trace pairs. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The pipeline applies established supervised fine-tuning and RLOO over existing task sources; what is genuinely novel is the breadth of ablation coverage (95 task sources, five teacher models, six pipeline stages, eight RL sources) and the empirical findings that contradict common assumptions: stronger teachers are not better teachers, LLM augmentation of task descriptions does not help, and task-source diversity is the binding constraint at scale. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations report standard error across three stochastic re-runs per benchmark. The compute-controlled ablation (Table 15) properly disentangles token-budget effects from trajectory quality effects by equating training tokens, confirming the min-turns filter finding is causal. Five held-out OOD benchmarks are evaluated only after pipeline decisions are fixed, preventing leakage into stage selection. Run-to-run RL reproducibility is formally characterized with three near-replicate runs (Table 20). Code, pipeline, training data, and model weights are publicly released at openthoughts.ai, with pinned commit hashes for all frameworks (Appendix D.8). Two methodological concerns: (1) All SFT ablations use Qwen3-8B as the base model, so stage rankings may not transfer to other model families. (2) The paper could not fully reproduce the published OpenSWE 62.4% SWE-Bench Verified number (measured 44.0% on SWE-Bench Verified-100 subset) due to unpublished scaffold components, flagging a reproducibility gap in the broader ecosystem rather than in this paper's own work. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile (SFT training, RL training, benchmark evaluation on live agent harnesses). - Practical: Training requires 24 nodes of 4×GH200 per node for 32B SFT (~5h wall time at 100K scale) and 24×A100-80GB for RL (~46h); beyond single-GPU access but within reach of HPC-equipped academic groups. - Efficient: Each 10K ablation costs 160 GPU-hours on GH200s, enabling dozens of parallel ablations; the final 100K run is proportionally more expensive but the pipeline is fully described and reproducible. - Effective: OpenThinkerAgent-32B achieves best open-data performance on a 7-benchmark diverse suite, providing evidence that the pipeline solves the stated generalization problem rather than only optimizing proxy benchmarks. - Scalable: Synthetic task augmentation overcomes the upsampling plateau at 31.6K and continues improving to 100K; behavior at multi-million-trajectory scales is explicitly flagged as untested. The paper delivers software artifacts: training sets, data pipeline code, and trained model weights at openthoughts.ai. Strengths - The 95-source task ablation is the most comprehensive public ranking of agentic data sources, providing empirically grounded guidance for a decision that is typically made opaquely or not documented. - The teacher-model inversion finding — GPT-5.3-Codex is the worst teacher despite being the strongest benchmark performer — is well-controlled across five candidates and directly actionable, contradicting a widespread implicit assumption in the field. - The compute-controlled ablation (Table 15) isolating trajectory quality from token-budget effects is methodologically sound and necessary; without it the min-turns filter finding would be confounded. - The mechanistic behavioral analysis of RL learning dynamics (Appendix F, Tables 21-22) traces per-trace shifts in think tokens, tool calls, self-correction, and task-completion rate, providing a causal account of why pymethods2test induces exploration while llm-verifier-freelancer induces compaction — going well beyond accuracy-number reporting. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: RL investigation is limited to 8B scale due to compute; whether the RL recipe transfers to 32B is open; the base model choice (Qwen3 family throughout) is not ablated so its contribution to final performance is not isolated; the largest training set is 100K trajectories and whether trends extrapolate to multi-million-trajectory regimes is untested. These are confronted directly in the conclusion, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All SFT ablations use Qwen3-8B as base, so the pipeline stage rankings (e.g., which sources are top-4, which filters help most) may not transfer to Llama-4, Mistral, or other families — the paper cannot claim universally optimal recipes. - The teacher model ablation covers five models, all proprietary or semi-open; the effect of fully open-weight teachers at similar capability tiers is unstudied, limiting actionability for resource-constrained settings. - The "stronger model ≠ better teacher" finding lacks a mechanistic explanation — the paper establishes the effect empirically but does not explain why GLM-4.7-AWQ generates better training signal (trajectory length distribution, error diversity, instruction-following fidelity?) than GPT-5.3-Codex. - The RL section uses a single algorithm (RLOO) from a single SFT starting point; interactions between SFT data quality and RL algorithm choice (e.g., PPO, GRPO with different KL settings) are not explored. - Task augmentation (Stage 3) is tested only with LLM-driven instruction rewriting and constraint/hardening; retrieval-based augmentation or cross-task interpolation are not evaluated, leaving open whether augmentation is fundamentally unhelpful or just the specific LLM-driven variants tested. Bottom Line Any researcher building or studying open-data agentic model training should read this paper — it is the most systematic public audit of SFT and RL data pipeline choices for agentic models, and its findings (task-source dominance, teacher-model inversion, augmentation futility, diversity as the scaling bottleneck) are directly actionable and would be expensive to rediscover independently.
Recommended 2606.24855 PDF
#2 — Highly Innovative (80)

OrthoReg: Orthogonal Regularization for Hybrid Symbolic - Neural Dynamical Systems

T Richter, N Kilbertus · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OrthoReg (Orthogonal Regularization), a training regularizer for hybrid symbolic-neural dynamical system models that directly penalizes squared empirical inner products between the neural augmentation and each symbolic library basis function, enforcing complementary decomposition of the vector field. - Why it's important: When the symbolic component of a hybrid model is discovered from data via sparse regression, the neural augmentation can relearn symbolic structure rather than capturing genuinely novel dynamics, destroying interpretability even when recovery is theoretically possible — and standard L² regularization provably fails to prevent this. - How it does it: OrthoReg adds the term λ Σⱼ ⟨f̂_aug(·; ϑ), φⱼ⟩²_D to the joint training objective, where the inner products are computed over the training sample, so the neural output is pushed toward the orthogonal complement of the symbolic library span under the empirical inner product. - The good: On the modified damped pendulum under partial library mismatch, OrthoReg achieves F1=0.93±0.15 for symbolic recovery (vs. 0.61±0.03 for the L²-regularized hybrid and 0.43±0.09 for pure symbolic) while reducing parametric-extrapolation OOD derivative MSE by more than two orders of magnitude relative to the L² baseline. - The missing: Population-level generalization guarantees for the learned augmentation are absent — the orthogonality certificate holds under the empirical inner product used for training but a uniform-convergence argument covering the full augmentation hypothesis class is explicitly deferred to future work. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building hybrid symbolic-neural ODE models with SINDy-style sparse discovery — the theoretical failure analysis of L² regularization alone justifies the read, and the method is a drop-in replacement with under 15% wall-clock overhead.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (82) and Non-Obviousness (82) drive the score: the formal identification that L2 regularization can INDUCE rather than prevent symbolic-neural overlap under sparse discovery (Theorem A.2 with constructive counterexample) is a counter-intuitive result not predicted by prior APHYNITY-style work, and the evaluation reports 5 seeds, systematic ablations across mismatch/noise/sample-size, held-out hyperparameter selection, and public code. Novelty (75) is high as NEW METHOD plus NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. Completeness (85) and Resilience (80) are strong because the paper reports the regimes where OrthoReg helps least and confronts the missing population-level guarantee directly. Practical Impact (70) reflects a released artifact bounded to low-dimensional ODEs (n<=4, M<=45). Preprint status caps Rigor below 90. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*82 + 0.15*82 + 0.15*85 + 0.15*80 + 0.10*70 = 80.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 82 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 85 Resilience 80 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Hybrid dynamical systems models decompose an unknown vector field into a symbolic/mechanistic component — a sparse linear combination of library functions such as polynomials and trigonometric terms — and a neural augmentation that captures residual dynamics the library cannot express. The intended division of labor is that the symbolic part captures what the library can represent, while the neural part captures only what it cannot. This contract breaks in the practically important regime where the symbolic component is discovered from data via sparse regression (SINDy-style L1 penalties): sparsity constraints shrink all library coefficients, leaving a nontrivial residual inside the symbolic span. A neural augmentation subsequently fitted to this residual will relearn symbolic structure rather than capturing out-of-library dynamics, yielding hybrid models that are both redundant and uninterpretable. Standard L² regularization on the augmentation, which suffices when the symbolic subspace is fixed (as in the APHYNITY framework of Yin et al. 2021), relies on a projection argument that breaks under joint optimization with continuous sparsity constraints — the neural component can satisfy a small L² norm while still being non-orthogonal to the symbolic span. Scientists who require interpretable governing equations from partially known dynamical systems in epidemiology, climate modeling, and mechanics face the risk that the symbolic explanation is absorbed by the neural component with no formal mechanism to prevent it. Key Idea The core technical insight is to replace L² norm regularization on the neural augmentation with a penalty on the empirical inner products between the neural output and each symbolic library basis function individually. Rather than controlling the magnitude of the augmentation (which does not preclude overlap with the symbolic span), OrthoReg controls the directional alignment, pushing the augmentation toward the orthogonal complement of span{φ₁, ..., φ_M} under the empirical inner product. A concrete counterexample (Example 1.1 / Theorem A.2) shows that under joint optimization with L1 sparsity, the L² minimizer yields an augmentation with nonzero inner products against the library basis even when the true function lies entirely within the library span — L² penalizes magnitude but not direction. OrthoReg targets direction directly. Methodology The model is f̂(x) = f̂_phy(x; w) + f̂_aug(x; ϑ), where f̂_phy = Σⱼ wⱼ φⱼ(x) is a sparse linear combination of M fixed library functions and f̂_aug is a 3-hidden-layer MLP (width 128, tanh activations). The OrthoReg objective (Equation 7) is: min_{w,ϑ} L_fit(w, ϑ) + μ||w||₁ + λ Σⱼ ⟨f̂_aug(·; ϑ), φⱼ⟩²_D where ⟨f, g⟩_D = (1/N) Σᵢ f(xᵢ)ᵀg(xᵢ) is the empirical inner product over sampled states. L_fit is either a vector-field regression loss (when derivative targets are available) or a one-step prediction loss. Three critical design decisions: (1) Joint optimization over w and ϑ in a single additive objective — staged or alternating updates restore symbolic overlap (Proposition C.1 proves this formally for unconstrained residual learning and compositional architectures, making joint training necessary for the certificate); (2) Inner products estimated over the full training batch in main experiments to eliminate mini-batch squared-mean bias (a two-batch unbiased estimator is available for memory-constrained settings); (3) L1 sparsity on w with post-hoc coefficient thresholding at 10⁻³ for support recovery metrics. The theoretical guarantees come in two forms: Theorem 4.1 / B.1 (penalty bound) shows the sum of squared inner products at the global minimizer is at most (1/λ) times the best pure-symbolic fit cost, so violations vanish as λ→∞; Theorem B.2 (orthogonal error decomposition) shows that when empirical orthogonality holds, the squared prediction error decomposes exactly as ||in-library error||² + ||orthogonal-complement error||², providing geometric meaning to the split. The per-iteration overhead is O(BnM) arithmetic operations (B = batch size, n = state dimension, M = library size), adding 5–15% wall-clock time. Key Results Four synthetic dynamical systems serve as benchmarks: a modified damped pendulum with three out-of-library forcing terms, a Duffing oscillator (cubic term absent from the polynomial library), a Lotka-Volterra predator-prey system (sanity check / low headroom), and a tweaked SIR epidemiological model (severe mismatch with time-dependent and state-dependent perturbations). Baselines are pure symbolic (SINDy-style L1 regression), L²-regularized hybrid (the APHYNITY-inspired baseline), PINNs, and Universal ODEs. All methods use the same MLP capacity; five seeds are reported throughout. Primary results — damped pendulum (medium missing dynamics, β₁=0.3, β₂=0.25, β₃=0.15): OrthoReg achieves F1=0.93±0.15 vs. L² hybrid 0.61±0.03 vs. pure symbolic 0.43±0.09 for symbolic support recovery; #Terms=3.6±1.3 vs. 6.8±0.4 vs. 11.4±2.9. Parametric-extrapolation (OOD-T3) derivative MSE: OrthoReg 0.02±0.00 vs. L² 7.90±4.02 vs. pure 34.00±22.22 — more than two orders of magnitude improvement. In-distribution state MSE is higher for OrthoReg (1.89±0.01) than L² (1.41±0.05), a tradeoff consistent with the orthogonal-complement decomposition theory. Duffing oscillator: OrthoReg F1=0.63±0.05 vs. L² 0.59±0.04 vs. pure 0.40±0.05; OOD-T2 (cross-basin) MSE 4.93±0.35 vs. pure 11.03±6.09. Lotka-Volterra: modest F1 gain (0.24 vs. 0.22). SIR: OrthoReg achieves highest F1 (0.20±0.06) and fewest terms (6.8±3.3) but sacrifices ID accuracy. Comparison to pure neural baselines (Table 2): PINNs and Universal ODEs have no symbolic recovery (F1 undefined), while OrthoReg is competitive on OOD-T2 and OOD-T3 trajectory MSE while uniquely recovering symbolic structure. Ablations confirm gains are largest under partial library mismatch, diminish under heavy mismatch (β₁=β₂=β₃=2.0), and are robust to irregular sampling. All methods converge to similar performance at noise level ε≥0.1, indicating measurement noise limits symbolic recovery independently of regularization approach. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The OrthoReg penalty — directly regularizing empirical inner products between neural outputs and library basis functions within a joint sparse-discovery objective — has not previously appeared in hybrid symbolic-neural modeling literature. The genuinely novel contribution is the formal identification (Theorem A.2) that L² regularization can induce rather than prevent symbolic-neural overlap under sparse discovery, and the demonstration that inner-product penalties restore the desired complementary decomposition with quantitative guarantees. The MLP augmentation, SINDy-style library, and L1 sparsity are all standard; what is novel is the orthogonality regularizer and its theoretical grounding within the joint-optimization framework. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are systematic and well-designed: missing-dynamics difficulty (low/medium/high), sampling regularity (regular vs. irregular Δt), regularization strength λ (0.01 to 10), sample size (100 to 5000), and noise level (ε∈{0.0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5}). All quantitative results report means and standard deviations across 5 seeds. Hyperparameters (λ, μ) are selected on a held-out in-distribution validation split disjoint from all test splits, and the same final hyperparameters are shared across all four systems. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/rictertill/OrthoReg. Proofs are complete within the empirical-inner-product framework with all theorems given full proofs in the appendix. One methodological concern: all experiments use synthetic ground-truth dynamical systems with known governing equations — no evaluation on real experimental data is included. A second concern: the population-level guarantee requires uniform convergence over the augmentation hypothesis class, which is explicitly acknowledged as missing; the certificate is empirical-inner-product–only. Both are acknowledged forthrightly. This is a preprint; results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL — all primary claims rest on experiments on synthetic dynamical systems with known ground truth. - Practical: Runs on a single general-purpose GPU with 64 GB RAM and small CPU allocation; workloads are low-dimensional ODEs (n≤4 states) with MLPs of width 64–128 and 2000 training epochs — fully compatible with commercially available hardware. - Efficient: Adds O(BnM) arithmetic per batch for the orthogonality penalty and 5–15% wall-clock overhead over the L²-regularized hybrid; memory overhead is under 360 KB for M=45, B=1000, well below typical activation cache sizes. - Effective: On the primary benchmark (damped pendulum, partial mismatch), OrthoReg achieves dramatically better symbolic recovery (F1 +0.32 vs. L² baseline) and >100× OOD-T3 MSE reduction; gains are more modest on Lotka-Volterra and the SIR model trades ID accuracy for OOD and sparsity gains. - Scalable: Tested only on ODEs with n≤4 states and M≤45 library terms; no experiments at higher state dimensions or larger trajectory datasets. The O(BnM) penalty cost scales linearly in M; performance at n=50+ states or M=500+ library terms is untested and unclaimed. Delivers: A publicly released Python software artifact (https://github.com/rictertill/OrthoReg) and proof techniques (orthogonal error decomposition, penalty-bound analysis) applicable to empirical-inner-product regularization in hybrid models. Strengths - The theoretical failure analysis of L² regularization under sparse discovery (Theorem A.2 with constructive counterexample in Example 1.1) is a genuine contribution that clarifies a subtle but consequential gap in prior work; APHYNITY's guarantees were not previously known to break in this regime. - Theorem B.2 (orthogonal error decomposition) gives the in-library / orthogonal-complement split exact geometric meaning under empirical orthogonality, connecting the regularizer to approximation theory in a principled way. - The ablation protocol is thorough and honest: the paper tests and reports conditions where OrthoReg helps least (low mismatch, high noise, severe mismatch) alongside its best-case results, giving practitioners a clear map of the method's operating regime. - Proposition C.1 formalizes why staged or compositional pipelines reintroduce symbolic overlap even with an orthogonality regularizer, providing a formal guard against naive architectural extensions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors confront four limitations directly in Appendix I: (1) Population-level guarantees for the learned augmentation require a uniform-convergence argument over the augmentation hypothesis class, deferred to future work. (2) Theoretical guarantees cover additive architectures with joint optimization; compositional architectures (f = T_w + N_ϑ ∘ T_w) can reintroduce symbolic overlap (Proposition C.1). (3) OrthoReg is formulated for continuous sparsity relaxations (L1) and does not directly extend to selection-refit methods (STLSQ, genetic programming, transformer-based symbolic search), where the objective structure changes after support selection. (4) Noise robustness is limited by derivative estimation error — at ε≥0.1, smoothed finite-difference targets are noise-dominated, and orthogonal regularization does not materially improve recoverability. These are presented directly and are a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All benchmarks use synthetic data from known dynamical systems with exact governing equations used to compute F1. There is no evaluation on real experimental data where measurement noise, partial observability, and unmodeled effects are harder to characterize; the practical F1 gains may not transfer to settings where the ground-truth symbolic support is unknown. - The paper evaluates only low-dimensional ODEs (n≤4 states, M≤45 library terms). Whether OrthoReg remains beneficial as the library grows to hundreds of terms — where the O(BnM) penalty cost grows and the probability of near-collinear library functions increases — is untested. - The consistent in-distribution MSE penalty from OrthoReg (e.g., 1.89 vs. 1.41 on the pendulum) is framed as theoretically expected but receives no practical guidance: practitioners fitting dynamical system models often use ID fit as a convergence diagnostic, and the tradeoff is not characterized as a function of λ or mismatch strength. - No wall-clock or convergence comparison against canonical PySINDy STLSQ under equal computational budget is provided, making it difficult to assess whether the hybrid OrthoReg approach is preferable to running selection-refit symbolic regression with more compute. Bottom Line Practitioners building hybrid symbolic-neural ODE models with SINDy-style sparse discovery should replace L² augmentation regularization with OrthoReg — the method adds under 15% wall-clock cost, yields substantially better symbolic recovery and OOD generalization on the tested benchmarks, and provides a theoretically grounded decomposition that L² regularization cannot guarantee in the sparse-discovery regime.
Search Results 2606.19145 PDF
#3 — Highly Innovative (80)

Hollow- LLM Attack: Computationally Trivial Weights in Zero-Knowledge Verification of LLM Inference

C Gong, B Liu, M Li · mengyuan-l.github.io · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Hollow-LLM attack, in which a dishonest provider constructs algebraic "ghost weights" that satisfy all zero-knowledge proof circuit equations for a large declared outer model while routing all serve-time computation through a much smaller inner model. - Why it's important: Zero-knowledge Machine Learning (zkML) systems like zkLLM and zkGPT are being positioned as cryptographic trust anchors for remote LLM serving, and the community widely interprets valid proofs as certifying computational scale; this paper shows they do not, creating a profitable equilibrium where providers over-claim model size at no quality cost. - How it does it: The attack exploits four algebraic invariances of pre-LayerNorm Transformer blocks (residual identity, FFN zero-padding, LayerNorm replicate invariance, elementwise nonlinearity replication) to embed a small inner model into a larger declared architecture whose circuit is satisfied equation-by-equation without performing the implied computation. - The good: Two training-free constructions (Attack A inflating depth, Attack B inflating width) are validated end-to-end on zkGPT: Table 2 shows serve-side latency fixed at 1× while ZKP proving cost scales 1.99×–5.89× with the declared architecture, and the Attack A+B composition reproduces the proving cost of an honest large model (IM2) while executing only the compact inner model (IM1). - The missing: All experiments use a 6-layer, 512-dimensional toy model; the paper does not demonstrate the witness-editing pipeline at production scale (13B+ parameters), and no code is released. - Read it or not: Security and ML researchers building or evaluating ZK-verified inference systems must read this; practitioners setting ZK-based service-level agreements should read Section 3 (threat model) and Section 6 (discussion) at minimum.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (end-to-end attack with a theoretical algebraic core). Novelty (88) is high: the paper formalizes the 'effort gap' — NP-membership certification versus computational-effort binding — a NEW PROBLEM the zkML community had not articulated despite shipping multiple end-to-end systems. Non-Obviousness (88) is equally high: the four pre-LN Transformer invariances yielding closed-form, training-free ghost weights are not anticipated by prior work, and the positional-encoding compatibility analysis (sinusoidal blocks Attack B) adds real depth. Rigor (75) is solid — S&P 2026 peer-reviewed, deterministic evaluation, clean additivity of A+B costs in Table 2 — but pulled down by no code release and a 6-layer/512-dim toy model that never exercises the witness-editing pipeline at 1B+ scale. Completeness (80) and Resilience (85) are strong because limitations are confronted directly and claims are scoped to the proof-level guarantee rather than over-extended. Practical Impact (55) is the main drag: no software artifact or weights are released and no commercial ZK LLM service yet exists, so impact is conceptual and forward-looking. Weighted: 0.20*88 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*88 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*85 + 0.10*55 = 80.
empirical Novelty 88 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 88 Completeness 80 Resilience 85 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLMs are predominantly served from remote platforms, and users cannot verify whether the provider executes the advertised model rather than a cheaper substitute. Zero-knowledge LLM inference (zkML) has been proposed as a cryptographic solution: the provider commits to private weights and generates a succinct proof asserting that each output is consistent with executing the declared public architecture under those committed weights. Systems such as zkLLM (13B parameters, proof in under 15 minutes) and zkGPT (GPT-2 verification in roughly 25 seconds) demonstrate practical feasibility. The community and major blockchain/crypto data providers (Ledger Academy, CoinGecko) have widely interpreted these proofs as certifying both correctness and computational scale. This paper identifies a fundamental mismatch: ZK proofs certify membership in an NP relation (there exist weights consistent with the declared architecture that produce this output), but the NP relation does not bind the amount of computation actually expended to find that output. A dishonest provider can therefore supply a trivially valid witness that satisfies all circuit constraints while serving queries with a far smaller model, yielding a profitable equilibrium: correct outputs at inner-model cost, sold under large-model pricing and compliance narratives. Key Idea The core technical insight is that pre-LN Transformer blocks admit a small set of exact algebraic invariances (residual identity, FFN zero-padding, LayerNorm replicate invariance under coordinate replication, and elementwise nonlinearity commutativity with the replication operator) that jointly allow an inner model to be embedded into a larger outer architecture such that every equation in the verifier's arithmetic circuit is satisfied, the inner model's input-output mapping is preserved exactly, and the ghost weights can be derived by closed-form algebraic transformation with no training or optimization. Methodology The paper presents two training-free ghost-weight constructions, both targeting the pre-LN decoder-only Transformer used in contemporary LLMs. Attack A (depth inflation via zero-work residual blocks) starts with an inner model A_in of depth L_in and declares an outer model A_out of depth L_out > L_in. For layers ℓ ≤ L_in, attention and FFN weights are copied directly (with zero-row/column padding if the FFN width is expanded). For added layers ℓ > L_in, all attention projection matrices and FFN weight matrices are set to zero; by residual identity and FFN zero-padding, each added block computes x → x + 0 = x on the verifier's arithmetic domain, contributing zero effective depth while remaining fully visible in the circuit. Witness editing at audit time replaces the per-token intermediate activations of the outer circuit's added layers with the pass-through values derived from the inner model's cached intermediates. Attack B (width inflation via replicated coordinates) inflates declared width from d_model to m·d_model (m ≥ 1). The hidden state is replicated as x' = Rx (m identical copies concatenated). Attention projections are arranged as block-diagonal matrices (m copies of the inner projections), so each block computes identically and softmax over key positions is taken over the same indices across all blocks. FFN weights are similarly block-diagonal for W_1 and horizontally concatenated for W_2. The output decoder is either zero-padded (applying the inner D to the first block only) or averaged (D' = 1/m [D … D]), both yielding logits identical to the inner model. For tied decoders, a final 1/m rescaling of the last LayerNorm output is applied before tying, which the paper shows preserves exact logit equality. LayerNorm replicate invariance (mean and variance of m identical blocks equal those of any single block) ensures that normalized activations feeding attention and FFN are preserved under replication. Attack A+B composes both transformations, simultaneously inflating depth and width. The paper verifies that the two transformations affect separate parts of the declared circuit and compose without interference (Table 2, A+B row matches the sum of A and B overheads exactly). The paper additionally analyzes positional encoding compatibility: learned absolute tables are compatible with both attacks (reused or replicated). Deterministic sinusoidal encodings are compatible with Attack A but block Attack B because the circuit enforces p_t = g(t, d_model,out) as a fixed function of d_model,out; with m > 1, g(t, m·d_model,in) ≠ R·g(t, d_model,in) in general, making the mismatch ZK-detectable. RoPE is compatible with both attacks by aligning rotation frequencies per replicated head. Key Results Experiments run on a local workstation (Intel Core Ultra 7 265F, 20 cores, Ubuntu 24.04) using zkGPT's ZKP procedure as the concrete zkML backend. The inner model IM1 is a 6-layer, 512-dimensional GPT-2-style backbone; the declared outer architecture is varied systematically. Fixed prompts of T=64 tokens with greedy decoding ensure determinism. Baselines include IM1 (honest, small), IM2 (honest large: 12-layer, 1024-dim), and the three attack variants. Table 2 key numbers (all serve-side costs normalized to IM1 baseline): - Attack A (6→12 layers): serve prefill 1×, decode 1×; ZKP Gates 1.99×, Prover 1.69×, Verify 1.72×, Proof 83.5 KB. - Attack B (512→1024 dim): serve prefill 1×, decode 1×; ZKP Gates 2.95×, Prover 1.87×, Verify 1.30×, Proof 48.2 KB. - Attack A+B (12 layers, 1024 dim): serve prefill 1×, decode 1×; ZKP Gates 5.89×, Prover 4.81×, Verify 2.48×, Proof 88.3 KB. - Honest IM2 (12 layers, 1024 dim): serve prefill 2.4×, decode 3.1×; ZKP Gates 5.89×, Prover 4.74×, Verify 2.56×, Proof 88.3 KB. The A+B configuration reproduces IM2's proving overhead while keeping serve-side at 1×, directly demonstrating the serve/prove asymmetry. Perplexity is unchanged across all attack variants, confirming semantic squivalence. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The formalization of the "effort gap" — the distinction between NP membership certification and computational effort binding — as a deployment-level assurance gap in zkML is a genuinely new contribution; the community had not articulated this property gap despite building multiple end-to-end zkML systems. The two ghost-weight constructions are new algebraic results specific to pre-LN Transformers. The empirical serve/prove separation is a new measurement demonstrating the practical magnitude of the asymmetry on a real zkML circuit. The paper does not claim to break ZKP soundness; the novelty lies in exposing what the existing proof system does and does not certify. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present in Table 2 and structured to isolate the contribution of each attack variant and their composition; the clean additivity of A+B costs confirms that the constructions do not interfere. The deterministic evaluation setting (fixed seed, greedy decoding, fixed T=64) makes experiments fully reproducible in principle. Perplexity invariance is verified across all configurations, directly testing semantic squivalence. Code is not stated as publicly released; the paper delivers constructions and analysis in paper form only. This is a meaningful reproducibility gap for a security paper whose attack validity depends on correct witness editing implementation. The paper was accepted at IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy (S&P) 2026 (Appendix A contains the meta-review), so it carries peer-reviewed status rather than preprint-only confidence. One methodological note: all experiments use a toy-scale model. The paper explicitly justifies this as sufficient to verify the structural property (the serve/prove separation depends only on the algebraic invariances, not on model scale), but this means the practical witness-editing pipeline has not been tested at the parameter counts where the economic incentives are strongest. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL (end-to-end attack construction and ZKP evaluation) with a theoretical algebraic component. - Practical: The ghost weight construction requires only the inner model weights and the target outer architecture specification; the algebraic transformation is closed-form and runs on standard hardware with no specialized resources. - Efficient: Serve-side cost is exactly equal to the inner model (1× across all attack variants in Table 2), and the one-time ghost weight construction is a matrix reshaping operation whose cost is negligible relative to inference. - Effective: The attack satisfies all three success criteria (accepted ZK proof, exact transcript alignment, proof indistinguishability) on the zkGPT circuit; the serving saves 2.4×–3.1× prefill/decode cost versus an honest deployment of the declared large model IM2. - Scalable: Not tested beyond the 6-layer, 512-dim toy model. The algebraic invariances are claimed to hold at arbitrary scale by construction, but the witness-editing pipeline — which must handle all per-token intermediate tensors for L_in layers and zero them for added layers — has not been exercised at 1B+ parameter scale. The work delivers theoretical constructions and experimental validation in paper form; no software artifact or trained model is released. Strengths - The formalization of the effort gap as a property distinct from ZKP soundness is conceptually precise and fills a gap the community had not articulated, despite multiple deployed systems implicitly relying on the incorrect assumption. - Both ghost-weight constructions are training-free, closed-form algebraic transformations, making the attacks both practically instantiatable and theoretically clean; this avoids the usual ambiguity of optimization-based attacks. - The positional encoding compatibility analysis (Section 4.4) is thorough and non-obvious: correctly identifying that deterministic sinusoidal encodings block Attack B's width inflation while leaving Attack A and learned/RoPE schemes intact adds real technical depth and scope-limits the claim honestly. - Table 2's experimental design — fixing the inner model and varying only the declared outer architecture — cleanly isolates the causal relationship between declared circuit size and ZKP cost, providing direct quantitative evidence for the serve/prove asymmetry. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge that the attack is scoped to the proof-level guarantee and excludes deployment-level detection mechanisms (behavioral fingerprinting, telemetry, timing side-channels, reference-based comparisons). They note that overly aggressive over-claiming may raise suspicion and that the threshold at which enlargement becomes detectable is beyond the scope of the paper. They acknowledge that deterministic sinusoidal encodings block Attack B. They note the paper targets an emerging threat (no commercial ZK LLM inference service currently exists). These limitations are confronted directly rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All experiments use a 6-layer, 512-dim toy model. The paper claims the constructions hold at arbitrary scale by algebraic argument, but does not demonstrate the witness-editing pipeline (which must correctly handle all intermediate tensor slots for the outer circuit) at 1B+ parameter scale, where ZKP proof generation already takes 21.8 seconds for GPT-2 at 32 threads per the cited zkGPT numbers. Scaling behavior of the witness editing step is unstated. - No code release. For a security paper whose central claim is that a specific construction passes real zkML circuit verification, code release is the expected reproducibility standard. - The economic analysis is qualitative. The paper argues that "modest over-claiming" is economically significant and stealthy, but does not model the specific scale ratios at which the serving cost savings (in cloud GPU dollars) exceed the reputational/detection risk — the core attacker optimization problem. - Proposed countermeasures (sparsity-based ZKPs, behavioral audits, TEEs) are discussed in approximately one page without formal analysis of the adversary's cost under any proposed defense; no lower bound is given for the attacker's remaining advantage after deploying any specific countermeasure. - The paper does not analyze interaction between ghost weights and adversarial backdoor weights. Concurrent work (cited as [1]–[3]) shows LLMs can carry hidden backdoors; whether ghost weights can simultaneously embed behavioral backdoors while passing ZK verification is an unexamined compounding threat. Bottom Line This paper makes a technically sound and practically important contribution by formalizing and concretely instantiating a previously overlooked vulnerability in zkML systems; anyone building or procuring ZK-verified LLM inference infrastructure should read it before the economics create real-world exploitation incentives.
Search Results
#4 — Highly Innovative (80)

Deeper is Not Always Better: Mitigating the Alignment Tax via Confident Layer Decoding (16 ▲ )

Xuanming Zhang, Sining Zhoubian, Yuxuan Chen, Tianyi Tang, An Yang, Sean Du, Chujie Zheng, Fei Huang, Dayiheng Liu, Gao Huang, Jingren Zhou · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Confident Decoding, a training-free decoding strategy that dynamically selects the near-final transformer layer with minimum predictive entropy per token, replacing the standard assumption that the last layer always produces the best next-token distribution. - Why it's important: Post-training alignment (RLHF/DPO) introduces a "planning-pragmatics tradeoff" where final layers corrupt carefully constructed intermediate reasoning chains by pushing toward generic, safe continuations, causing measurable accuracy collapse on hard reasoning tasks. - How it does it: A conservative backward scan over a configurable near-final layer window (default K=10) identifies the first local Shannon entropy minimum encountered scanning from layer L inward, and emits logits from that layer instead of the last layer, leaving the full forward pass and KV cache unchanged. - The good: On the hardest Level 4 problems in Omni-MATH using gpt-oss-20b, Confident Decoding raises accuracy from 1.1% to 23.5% (+22.4 pp absolute), with <2% wall-clock latency overhead and zero additional KV-cache memory cost. - The missing: The method degrades on compact hybrid architectures (Qwen3.5-9B drops from 64.6 to 62.1 on GPQA-Diamond), and the theoretical dominance condition (Δη > 2ε_max) is assumed rather than measured numerically. - Read it or not: Essential for anyone deploying aligned LLMs on complex reasoning tasks, particularly at scale; skip if working exclusively with compact or shallow hybrid architectures where the refinement corridor is too narrow.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (the systems integration raises Impact but the contribution is an empirical decoding method). This is the stronger paper and resembles the user's positive set in mechanism-first framing and controlled causal ablation. Rigor (78) is high: six model families, seven benchmarks, error bars, K/p sweeps, released code, and a clean instruct-vs-base experiment that causally isolates the alignment tax; it is docked for the shared-institution backbone (Qwen Team), re-implemented baselines, and missing error bars on the difficulty-stratified tables. Novelty (78) reflects a genuine NEW METHOD plus NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING — the per-token entropy-trough selection and the documented sparsity (only 16.2% of tokens show Phase III rebound) are not predicted by prior contrastive-decoding work. Non-Obviousness (82) is the top dimension: the entropy-valley-before-alignment-perturbation insight reveals hidden depth structure. Completeness (80) credits the explicit failure-mode formalization and honest scope (regressions on Qwen3.5-9B reported, not hidden), and Resilience (78) reflects well-bounded claims, though the theoretical dominance condition Delta-eta > 2*eps_max is assumed rather than measured. Practical Impact (85) is high: training-free, zero KV-memory overhead, <2% latency, and a released vLLM integration. Weighted: 0.20*78 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*82 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*78 + 0.10*85 = 80.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 80 Resilience 78 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Autoregressive LLMs decode from the final transformer layer under the monotonic-depth assumption: deeper representations are presumed more reliable. Analysis of residual-stream contribution norms and directional cosine similarity across decoder depth reveals a recurring three-phase structure — Guess (shallow layers form coarse initial representations), Refine (intermediate layers progressively integrate contextual dependencies along a stable semantic trajectory), and Perturb (the final few layers introduce a structurally significant directional deflection that partially overwrites the refined semantic state). This Phase III perturbation is empirically linked to post-training alignment objectives: RLHF, DPO, and SFT procedures optimize final-layer distributions toward high-frequency, safe, generic continuations. For safety-oriented or conversational queries where the target distribution aligns with P_align, this is benign. For complex multi-hop reasoning, the alignment prior conflicts with the task-specific prediction crystallized in Phase II, degrading output quality precisely on hard problems where the cost is highest. Key Idea The Shannon entropy H(p^(l)) of the per-layer token prediction, computed by projecting intermediate residual states through the frozen unembedding matrix W_U, follows a characteristic descent-then-rebound trajectory for a subset of tokens: entropy falls monotonically through Phase II as the model commits to a reasoning-specific prediction, reaches a minimum (the "entropy valley" or "trough") at the Phase II/III boundary, and then rises in the final layers when alignment noise redirects the distribution. This entropy minimum is a reliable, per-token proxy for the point of maximum model confidence before alignment perturbation. Selecting logits from this trough layer isolates the reasoning signal without retraining or modifying model weights. Methodology The algorithm captures residual stream states {x^(ℓ)} for all L layers during the unmodified forward pass. At each decode step, each captured state is normalized (RMSNorm applied) and projected through W_U to yield a softmax distribution p^(ℓ) and entropy H^(ℓ). A conservative backward scan over the candidate window C = {L-M+1,...,L} (size M controlled by lookback parameter K, default 10) selects the deepest layer ℓ where the entropy strictly decreases from the final layer inward — formally, ℓ = min{ℓ ∈ [L-K+1, L] : H^(ℓ) < H^(ℓ+1) < ... < H^(L)}. The scan freezes on the first non-improvement (preventing overshooting into Phase I noise) and falls back to ℓ = L with probability 1-p (default p=1.0, deterministic valley selection). Three critical design decisions: (1) using the conservative backward scan rather than the global argmin avoids selecting Phase I high-entropy layers; (2) per-token adaptive selection is necessary because Phase III is a per-token phenomenon — 16.2% of tokens on Qwen3.5-35B-A3B exhibit Phase III entropy rebound while 83.8% do not; (3) applying the final-layer RMSNorm to each intermediate state before projection addresses the basis-shift problem while keeping the method purely training-free. The vLLM implementation captures residual tensors in an eager-scope wrapper surrounding the compiled inner model, uses shape-aware buffering under continuous batching, and vectorizes the backward scan as fused tensor operations. The theoretical justification frames layer selection as an optimal stopping problem: the observable entropy Ĥ(l) = H(l) + ε^(l) + η^(l), where ε is bounded projection noise and η is the per-token alignment perturbation. Theorem 1 (Minimax Optimality) proves that the conservative backward scan guarantees the selected layer lies in [V_onset, V*] — neutralizing Phase III alignment perturbation while bounding semantic precision loss to a theoretically negligible integral. Key Results Across six open-weight model families (Qwen3.5-27B, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, Qwen3.5-122B-A10B, Gemma-4-31B, gpt-oss-20b, gpt-oss-120B) on seven benchmarks: GPQA-Diamond gains range from +1.2 pp (Qwen3.5-27B: 78.2→79.4) to +6.5 pp (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B: 76.3→82.8); HLE gains reach +2.0 pp (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B: 9.2→11.2); LiveCodeBench v6 sees the largest gain at +10.1 pp on Qwen3.5-27B (63.9→74.0); LongBench-v2 and WritingBench show marginal deltas (mostly 0.0–0.5 pp), confirming that context-saturated tasks suppress Phase III perturbation. Safety (Air-Bench) consistently improves or holds. Difficulty stratification on Omni-MATH Level 4 with gpt-oss-20b shows +22.4 pp (1.1→23.5); Level 1 shows -4.3 pp (97.0→92.7) consistent with theory (simple tokens do not require Phase II reasoning). The instruct-vs-base ablation confirms the alignment-tax hypothesis: Confident Decoding yields +2.6 pp average on the Instruct variant vs. +1.1 pp on Base, with HLE inverted under standard decoding (Instruct: 9.2% < Base: 8.0%) then recovered (Instruct: 11.2% > Base: 6.3%). Compared to DoLa (77.3 GPQA-D) and SLED (78.8 GPQA-D), Confident Decoding (82.8) exceeds both on Qwen3.5-35B-A3B. Degradation is observed on Qwen3.5-9B for GPQA-D (64.6→62.1) and Omni-MATH (49.1→47.1). All benchmarks use official evaluation repositories and community-standard prompts; HLE and Air-Bench scoring uses LLM-as-judge (gpt-5.4). This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.21906v1, June 2026), not peer reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The entropy-trough backward scan for per-token layer selection is a new algorithm, distinct from contrastive decoding methods (DoLa, SLED) which contrast distributions across layer pairs and assume representational homogeneity. The empirical finding that Phase III perturbation is a sparse per-token phenomenon (16.2% of tokens affected) rather than a universal architectural boundary is genuinely novel and mechanistically documented. The theoretical optimal-stopping formalization of dynamic layer selection is new. The combination of training-free, zero-memory-overhead deployment with meaningful accuracy gains on hard reasoning tasks at the 120B scale is not previously demonstrated. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present: lookback window K and fallback probability p are both swept on GPQA-Diamond (Appendix A, Table 7), and the deterministic valley configuration (K=10, p=1.0, T=0.0) is verified as optimal. The instruct-vs-base comparison (Table 2) provides a clean causal isolation of the alignment tax. The three-phase layer dynamics are replicated on a second backbone (Qwen3.5-9B, Appendix B). Error bars (±1σ) are reported in Table 1. Code is released at https://github.com/QwenLM/Confident-Decoding. Methodological concerns: (1) the primary backbone and the authors share the same institutional affiliation (Qwen Team, Alibaba), which is not Goodhart's Law but does suggest selection bias risk in which architectures receive the most detailed analysis; (2) DoLa and SLED baselines required re-implementation for MoE architectures — the paper validates these re-implementations on LLaMA-family models, a reasonable but not independently verified mitigation; (3) difficulty-stratified results (Tables 3-4) report no error bars; (4) the theoretical dominance condition Δη > 2ε_max is stated as an assumption, not empirically measured. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (vLLM production integration implemented and described). - Practical: Runs on any hardware that supports standard vLLM deployment; requires no weight modification, no auxiliary model, no additional training. - Efficient: Empirical mean overhead is 0.87% additional FLOPs (0.116 extra unembedding projections per token) and <2% wall-clock latency, because 88.5% of tokens already satisfy the valley condition at L-1 and trigger zero additional projections; worst-case scan (K=10) is 74.64% FLOPs overhead but occurs on <12% of tokens. - Effective: Effective on larger and MoE models for complex reasoning; shows regressions on Qwen3.5-9B for reasoning-intensive benchmarks (GPQA-D: -2.5 pp, Omni-MATH: -2.1 pp); the failure mode is understood and delineated by architectural depth and homogeneity criteria. - Scalable: Tested at scales up to Qwen3.5-122B-A10B and gpt-oss-120B; gains are positive and substantial at 120B (GPQA-D: +4.5 pp, HLE: +1.8 pp); not tested beyond 120B active parameters. The work delivers a production software artifact (vLLM integration, open-source repository). Strengths - The per-token heterogeneity finding (Figure 4) is the paper's strongest empirical contribution: quantifying that only 16.2% of tokens exhibit Phase III entropy rebound on Qwen3.5-35B-A3B rigorously distinguishes this approach from static early-exit methods and justifies the adaptive per-token selection. - The instruct-vs-base ablation (Table 2) is a well-designed causal experiment: the amplified gain for the Instruct model (+2.6 pp vs. +1.1 pp average) and the HLE inversion (standard decoding: Instruct 9.2% < Base 8.0%; Confident Decoding: Instruct 11.2% > Base 6.3%) constitute credible evidence that the alignment tax is a post-training artifact, not a static architectural flaw. - The degradation analysis (Appendix E) explicitly identifies the failure conditions (shallow hybrid architectures with narrow refinement corridors) and formalizes them through the probe-mismatch mechanism — a credibility-positive move that makes the scope of the method interpretable. - The computational overhead analysis (Table 5) is thorough: reporting both theoretical worst-case (74.64%) and empirical mean (0.87%) overhead with a mechanistic explanation of the lazy evaluation dynamic avoids the common practice of reporting only the favorable empirical number. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state two limitations: (1) Confident Decoding is constrained by the structural alignment of W_U with intermediate residual states, and representations from shallow layers may still suffer from vocabulary mismatch — the theoretical framework bounds this projection noise but cannot eliminate it; (2) the approach mitigates the alignment tax symptomatically at decoding time rather than addressing the root cause during training. They directly propose future training-side remedies (applying alignment penalties exclusively to designated routing heads, geometrically precise reward functions using layer-wise entropy metrics). Both limitations are confronted directly, not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All per-difficulty ablations (Tables 3-4) and most detailed mechanistic analysis use Qwen-family or gpt-oss models; an independent replication on LLaMA-4 or Mistral Large would substantiate the generality claim independently of the authors' institutional models. - The extraordinary +10.1 pp gain on LiveCodeBench v6 for Qwen3.5-27B is the largest absolute result in the paper, but no difficulty stratification or token-level analysis is provided for the coding benchmark — the mechanism producing this gain is unexamined. - Air-Bench evaluation uses gpt-5.4 as a judge model; gpt-oss belongs to the same model family as the judge, which introduces potential correlated bias in the reported +5.0 pp safety improvement for gpt-oss-20b. - The theoretical optimality proof assumes Δη^(l) > 2ε_max (alignment perturbation dominates projection noise) in Phase III, but the paper provides no quantitative estimate of either ε_max or Δη^(l) from the empirical entropy trajectories, leaving the gap between theory and experiment implicit. Bottom Line Confident Decoding delivers meaningful accuracy improvements on hard reasoning benchmarks (+22.4 pp on Omni-MATH Level 4, +10.1 pp on LiveCodeBench v6) with near-zero deployment overhead and a credible mechanistic account, making it immediately actionable for any practitioner running aligned LLMs on complex reasoning tasks at scale on sufficiently deep architectures.
Recommended 2606.21906 PDF
#5 — Highly Innovative (77)

ParallelKernelBench: Can LLMs Write Fast Multi-GPU Kernels?

W Chan, N Paek, S Guo, S Arora, DY Fu · openreview.net · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ParallelKernelBench (PKB), a benchmark of 87 real-world multi-GPU kernel optimization problems paired with a communication-aware roofline evaluation framework for assessing LLM-generated distributed CUDA kernels. - Why it's important: Communication via NVLink is the dominant runtime bottleneck in large-scale AI training and inference (all-reduce alone accounts for over 20% of end-to-end Llama-3.3-70B inference latency on a DGX H100), yet no benchmark existed to measure or drive LLM progress on writing multi-GPU kernels that exploit fine-grained device-side communication primitives. - How it does it: PKB gives each model a PyTorch+NCCL reference implementation and a hardware topology description, challenges it to rewrite the kernel using CUDA symmetric memory and NVLink P2P via UVA, then evaluates correctness and hardware utilization against a phase-aware roofline that adds NVLink bandwidth as a third ceiling alongside compute and HBM. - The good: Despite low aggregate success rates, models occasionally produce net-new kernels outperforming existing open-source implementations: Gemini 3 Pro generates a Ulysses all-to-all kernel achieving 3.44× speedup over the PyTorch+NCCL reference at T=1024, and a NeMo vocab-parallel log-probability kernel achieving 2.05× at seq_len=2048. - The missing: All evaluations run on a single 4-GPU intra-node H100 NVLink configuration; inter-node fabrics (RoCE, InfiniBand), larger world sizes, and other accelerators are entirely unevaluated, and it is unknown whether the speedups versus the unoverlapped baseline reflect NVLink P2P efficiency or simply overlap that well-tuned NCCL already provides. - Read it or not: Essential for researchers building LLM-based kernel synthesis pipelines or studying the gap between single-GPU and multi-GPU code generation capabilities; less urgent for practitioners not touching distributed GPU infrastructure.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. This is the strongest paper in the cluster. Rigor (80) is high: 5 randomized correctness trials, 500 warmup + 100 timed iterations with ±1 SD error bars, per-problem symbolic cost-model files enabling roofline computation at arbitrary shapes, and an open benchmark environment. Novelty (78) is real: the first benchmark for LLM-generated multi-GPU distributed CUDA kernels plus a communication-aware phase-aware roofline as an automatic metric. Non-Obviousness (72) reflects the finding that frontier models default to copy-engine/SM primitives and near-0% NVLS/TMA usage despite strong single-GPU performance. Completeness (80) and Resilience (72) are solid, with honestly stated intra-node-only scope; the unoverlapped-NCCL baseline confound and lack of significance tests on 87 problems are the main weaknesses. Impact (80) reflects production-repo curation and a released harness. Weighted: 0.20*78 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*80 = 77.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 80 Resilience 72 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Modern large-scale AI training and inference require kernels spanning multiple GPUs, and communication via interconnects like NVLink increasingly dominates runtime. For Llama-3.3-70B, all-reduce alone accounts for over 20% of end-to-end inference latency on a DGX H100 even with NVLink and in-switch reductions. Prior LLM-driven kernel generation benchmarks—KernelBench (250 problems), FlashInfer-Bench (18), SOL-ExecBench (235), MultiKernelBench (285)—target single-GPU workloads exclusively, where the standard compute-and-memory roofline applies directly. Multi-GPU settings introduce three distinct challenges absent from the single-GPU case: the design space grows combinatorially as practitioners compose tensor, expert, data, context, and sequence parallelism strategies; the standard memory-compute roofline fails to capture communication bottlenecks; and hardware path selection for inter-GPU data movement (copy engine, TMA, SM load/store, NVLink SHARP via multimem instructions) introduces distinct performance tradeoffs. The GPU kernel optimization community lacks any standardized benchmark or evaluation methodology for this regime, making it impossible to measure LLM capability on distributed kernel generation or to guide model development toward closing the gap. Key Idea The central insight is that evaluating multi-GPU kernels requires a communication-aware roofline that adds NVLink bandwidth as a third hardware ceiling and partitions workloads into phases within which compute, HBM traffic, and communication can freely overlap but across which hard data dependencies force serialization. This phase-aware model, instantiated as per-problem Python cost files with symbolic FLOP/HBM/communication volume formulas, enables the roof_u metric to automatically assess whether a generated kernel uses the available hardware well—not just whether it beats the naive unoverlapped PyTorch+NCCL baseline—and reveals that the baseline itself falls far below the hardware ceiling on the vast majority of PKB problems. Methodology Each PKB task presents the model with: (i) a reference solution() in PyTorch using torch.distributed NCCL collectives; (ii) a hardware topology description specifying 4x H100 80GB SXM5 GPUs with 4th-generation NVLink, NVSwitch, and CUDA 12.6; (iii) a minimal in-context example demonstrating symmetric memory allocation, pointer caching, and NVLink P2P transfer via UVA. Models must produce a rewritten solution() using custom CUDA kernels with PyTorch's symmetric heap API. Correctness uses 5 trials with randomized per-rank BF16 inputs; performance uses 500 warmup plus 100 timed profiling iterations (mean ± 1 SD across 5 independent trials). The primary scalar metric is fast_p: fraction of tasks where the generated kernel is both correct and achieves at least p× speedup over the unoverlapped PyTorch+NCCL reference, with fast_1 as the standard threshold. The communication-aware roofline computes T_roof,i = Σ_k max(W_k/P_peak, Q_mem,k/B_mem, Q_comm,k/B_comm) summed over phases, using H100 SXM5 specs: 989 TFLOP/s BF16, 3.35 TB/s HBM, 450 GB/s NVLink per direction. The roof_u metric reports the fraction of tasks where the generated kernel is correct and achieves at least fraction u of its problem-specific hardware ceiling. An agentic setting wraps generation in a mini-SWE-agent loop with terminal access for up to 25 iterations, evaluated on Gemini 3 Pro only. The 87 problems span six abstraction tiers (bare collectives, transition kernels, layer fragments, end-to-end composites, optimizer/update kernels, application-specific) and are drawn from 22+ production repositories including NVIDIA/NeMo-RL, HazyResearch/ThunderKittens, NVIDIA/Megatron-LM, deepspeedai/DeepSpeed, deepseek-ai/DeepEP, facebookresearch/sam3, and others. Key Results Single-shot correctness on 87 problems: GPT-5.5 achieves 36/87 correct at pass@3 (41.4%) and 27/87 fast_1@3 (31.0%); Claude Opus 4.7 achieves 31/87 correct and 20/87 fast_1; Gemini 3 Pro achieves 30/87 correct and 19/87 fast_1; GLM-5.1 achieves 7/87 correct and 3/87 fast_1; DeepSeek V4 Pro achieves 3/87 correct and 1/87 fast_1 at pass@3. With agentic harness (Gemini 3 Pro, 25 iterations): 31/87 correct (40.2%) and 26/87 fast_1 (29.8%), up from 24 correct and 12 fast_1 single-shot—a meaningful gain that plateaus quickly. The communication-aware roofline analysis finds that 100% of the 65 NVLink-bound and 16 HBM-bound PyTorch+NCCL baselines fall below 50% of their hardware ceiling, confirming substantial optimization headroom. Qualitatively, models gravitate toward copy engine (CE) and SM load/store (LSA) mechanisms; NVLS/multimem and TMA usage is near 0% across all models, indicating failure to learn advanced communication paths. Syntax failures dominate open-source models; reasoning models eliminate syntax errors but correctness remains low, indicating the bottleneck is distributed reasoning rather than API fluency. Net-new kernels with no existing optimized open-source implementations include: Ulysses all-to-all achieving 3.44× at T=1024 (sustained >1.8× through T=16384), NeMo vocab-parallel log-probability achieving 2.05× at seq_len=2048, Hyena CP achieving 1.72× at l=2048 (degrading to 0.87× at l=4096), SAM3 IoU suppression achieving 1.40× at 256×256 (degrading to 0.15× at 1024×1024). Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. PKB is the first benchmark targeting LLM-generated multi-GPU distributed CUDA kernels; the communication-aware roofline extended with NVLink bandwidth and phase-aware dependency modeling as an automatic evaluation metric across a diverse distributed kernel suite is a genuine methodological contribution. The empirical finding that frontier LLMs systematically default to simple communication primitives and achieve correctness on only 32% of tasks single-shot—despite strong performance on single-GPU KernelBench—is a concrete, non-obvious finding about current model capability boundaries. The parallelism taxonomy used to curate problems from production repositories extends KernelBench-style benchmark construction in a meaningful direction; no new algorithmic or theoretical contribution is claimed. Rigor & Reproducibility Correctness uses 5 independent randomized per-rank input trials; performance uses 500 warmup + 100 timed iterations with ±1 SD error bars across 5 independent trials reported in Appendix E, showing tight variance for the large majority of problems. Per-problem Python cost model files are included with the benchmark and declare symbolic FLOP/HBM/communication formulas, enabling T_roof computation at arbitrary shapes. The benchmark and evaluation harness are released as an open benchmark environment (code URL not stated in the paper). Evaluated models are identified by version (GPT-5.5, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3 Pro, GLM-5.1, DeepSeek V4 Pro), though third-party reproducibility of GPT-5.5 and Gemini 3 Pro results depends on API access and model versioning. The paper is a preprint under ICML review and has not yet been peer-reviewed. One methodological concern: all evaluations run on a single hardware configuration (one 4x H100 SXM5 node), so results may not generalize to other NVLink generations, node counts, or AMD topologies. A second concern: the net-new kernel speedups compare against the unoverlapped PyTorch+NCCL reference, not against a tuned NCCL baseline that already overlaps compute and communication—the fraction of speedup attributable to overlap alone versus NVLink P2P efficiency is not isolated. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (benchmark with evaluation framework and per-problem cost models). - Practical: The harness runs on a single 4x H100 node with NVSwitch; this hardware is commercially available but expensive, and the intra-node scope keeps infrastructure requirements manageable compared to multi-node alternatives. - Efficient: Evaluation overhead per problem is modest (600 GPU kernel launches plus model API calls), but costs across 87 problems × 5 models × multiple sampling attempts are non-trivial; API call costs are not reported. - Effective: PKB correctly identifies that PyTorch+NCCL baselines are far below hardware ceilings on 93% of communication- and memory-bound tasks, and the roof_u metric distinguishes hardware utilization from raw speedup—both serve the stated goal of revealing optimization headroom. - Scalable: Explicitly limited to 4-GPU intra-node NVLink; larger world sizes, inter-node fabrics, and multi-node scenarios are left to future work and not tested. The work delivers an open benchmark environment (software artifact) with per-problem cost model files and evaluation harness. Strengths - The communication-aware phase-aware roofline, instantiated as per-problem symbolic cost models, is a methodologically sound extension of the classical roofline that provides an automatic, hardware-grounded performance ceiling for each distributed problem—this is more rigorous than prior benchmarks that compare only against the PyTorch reference. - Problem curation from 22+ production repositories (NeMo-RL, ThunderKittens, Megatron-LM, DeepSpeed, DeepEP, SAM3, etc.) ensures ecological validity; problems reflect actual bottlenecks in deployed AI systems at production scale, not synthetic microbenchmarks. - The qualitative kernel analysis (Section 6 and Appendix G) is unusually detailed for a benchmark paper: it shows concrete LLM-generated CUDA code alongside profiler traces, speedup curves across problem dimensions, and mechanistic explanations of optimization strategies (NVLink pull kernels, vectorized peer writes, fused warp-shuffle reductions)—making the outputs useful for understanding model capabilities rather than just recording pass rates. - The cross-model failure mode analysis (syntax errors vs. output mismatches vs. deadlocks) cleanly identifies that the bottleneck shifts from API fluency to distributed reasoning as model capability increases, which is an actionable finding for researchers targeting future improvement. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper explicitly states in Appendix H: (1) PKB is intra-node only and excludes inter-node communication over RoCE and InfiniBand; (2) pipeline parallelism is underrepresented (1 problem) because its performance is governed by scheduling rather than kernel design; (3) prefill/decode disaggregation is excluded as a scheduling concern; (4) the agentic evaluation covers only Gemini 3 Pro due to harness complexity; (5) gains from iterative refinement plateau quickly, revealing a ceiling imposed by model capacity to reason about distributed memory. The authors confront these limitations directly and frame them as concrete future work directions, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps With only 87 problems, model rankings could be sensitive to the specific problem mix—no statistical significance test or confidence interval on fast_1 scores is reported, and a difference of 3–4 problems between adjacent models may not be meaningful. The fast_1 metric conflates two distinct failure modes—incorrect output and correct but slower—without reporting what fraction of correct kernels are slower than the baseline versus simply wrong; this matters for understanding where models fail. The agentic evaluation covers only Gemini 3 Pro; since single-shot rankings differ substantially across models, it is unknown whether the 40.2% agentic ceiling is model-specific or reflects a fundamental limit of the benchmark. The net-new speedup numbers (3.44×, 2.06×, 1.72×, 1.40×) all compare against the unoverlapped PyTorch+NCCL reference; a fair comparison should include a hand-tuned or NCCL-optimal baseline that already overlaps communication and compute where possible, to separate PKB's claimed optimization opportunity from what NCCL already achieves with proper scheduling. The benchmark covers BF16 precision throughout; FP8 kernels, which are increasingly production-critical for H100 training, are not evaluated. Bottom Line PKB fills a genuine methodological gap with a well-constructed benchmark, a sound evaluation framework, and concrete empirical evidence that frontier LLMs are far from autonomous multi-GPU kernel optimization—practitioners working on distributed AI infrastructure or LLM-based code synthesis should read it, though the single-node NVLink scope means its findings do not yet characterize the inter-node regime where real-world scale training actually runs.
Citations
#6 — Highly Innovative (76)

JetSpec: Breaking the Scaling Ceiling of Speculative Decoding with Parallel Tree Drafting (24 ▲ )

Lanxiang Hu, Zhaoxiang Feng, Yulun Wu, Haoran Yuan, Yujie Zhao, Yu-Yang Qian, Bojun Wang, Peng Zhao, Daxin Jiang, Yibo Zhu, Tajana Rosing, Hao Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Jun 2026
