TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EBench, a simulation benchmark that diagnoses generalist mobile manipulation policies along 5 capability axes and 4 generalization dimensions, decomposing any aggregate success rate into an interpretable per-dimension capability profile. - Why it's important: Existing benchmarks report a single scalar success rate that conceals dramatic within-cohort heterogeneity: four VLAs with near-identical aggregate test SRs (24.4–29.5%) differ by up to 29 percentage points on specific task types, making it impossible to identify where a model is strong or where it breaks. - How it does it: EBench annotates each of 26 tasks along 5 dimensions (scene, atomic skill, horizon, precision, operating mode) and enforces 4 controlled OOD generalization axes (background, object, instruction, mix) via asset-level train/test isolation, so aggregate scores decompose directly into interpretable coordinates without requiring separate benchmarks. - The good: EBench is the only benchmark among EBench, LIBERO, and RoboTwin 2.0 that reliably surfaces the contribution of large-scale pretraining: pretrained models outperform from-scratch by 9–21 SR points on EBench, while LIBERO and RoboTwin 2.0 from-scratch models match or exceed their pretrained counterparts. - The missing: With only 26 tasks across 9 scene categories, scene-level permutation tests are severely underpowered (1–6 tasks per scene), making scene-level rankings statistically unreliable; the paper's controlled appendix flags this but the main-text figures present scene rankings without the caveat. - Read it or not: Essential for researchers designing or comparing generalist manipulation policies, because the multi-dimensional breakdown and public leaderboard expose capability structure that no existing benchmark provides.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (78) is the strongest dimension: three independent seeds with mean ± SD, 10,000-iteration permutation tests with honest acknowledgment of T=26 under-power, a camera-perspective ablation, and full public release of code, scoring library, splits, the 91.4-hour dataset, and a leaderboard. Novelty (70) is moderate; this is a new benchmark with structured co-design of five capability axes and four OOD dimensions, not a new algorithm. Non-Obviousness (72) is lifted by the finding that four VLAs with near-identical aggregate SR (24.4-29.5%) diverge by up to 29 points per capability, and that only EBench surfaces the pretraining contribution. Completeness (75) and Resilience (72) are held back by scene-level rankings shown in main-text figures without the under-power caveat, the open-loop evaluation protocol that likely disadvantages dexterous tasks, and no data-quantity ablation (200 vs 400 episodes) confounding the mobile-dexterous gap. Practical Impact (75) reflects a usable diagnostic that runs on 8 RTX 4090s in under 2 hours, though sim-real correlation is untested. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*75 = 74.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Generalist robot manipulation policies now achieve competitive aggregate success rates on established simulation suites, but those single-scalar metrics cannot answer where a model is strong, where it fails, or how performance shifts under distribution change. Existing suites cover narrow regimes: RLBench, CALVIN, and LIBERO address short-horizon fixed-base pick-and-place; RoboCasa and RoboTwin broaden scene diversity but focus on a single manipulation paradigm (tabletop, mobile, or long-horizon) per suite; diagnostic benchmarks like RMBench probe only one capability axis (memory). No suite simultaneously evaluates long-horizon, dexterous-and-precise, and mobile manipulation under a unified protocol, nor pairs every task with structured capability annotations and controlled OOD axes. Key Idea The central insight is that any aggregate success rate can be decomposed into an interpretable 5-dimensional capability profile if tasks are annotated at design time along scene type, atomic skill, temporal horizon, precision tolerance, and operating mode. Combining this with 4 controlled generalization axes enforced through asset-level train/test isolation (scene textures, object instances, and instruction paraphrases used for training drawn from a pool disjoint from the test pool) lets practitioners identify not just whether a model is good overall, but which specific capability dimensions it masters and which distribution shifts expose its weaknesses. Methodology EBench comprises 26 tasks in three families: Mobile Pick-and-Place (10 tasks, 600–1,000 simulation steps per episode), Mobile Long-Horizon (9 multi-stage tasks, 3,000–5,000 steps), and Table-Top Dexterous-and-Precise (7 fixed-base tasks, 1,500–3,500 steps, sub-centimeter insertion/alignment). Physics runs at 60 Hz in IsaacSim. Data synthesis uses two complementary tracks: (1) human teleoperation via kinematically isomorphic actor-follower setup for the 7 dexterous tasks (400 episodes each), preserving the reactive micro-corrections required for contact-rich manipulation; (2) key-frame end-effector pose specification followed by cuRobo collision-free minimum-jerk trajectory planning for the remaining 19 mobile and long-horizon tasks (200 episodes each), re-rendered under randomized backgrounds, objects, and lighting to produce generalization variants. Total dataset: 91.4 hours, 6,600 episodes in LeRobot format. Each task is annotated along 5 axes (scene ×9 categories, atomic skill ×11, range ×2, precision ×3, operating mode ×2). The 4 generalization dimensions are: Background (unseen textures and lighting, physics unchanged), Object (unseen geometry and mass within category), Instruction (paraphrased natural language), and Mix (all three jointly). The TEST split comprises 510 episodes (20 per task for 24 tasks, 15 per task for 2 long-horizon tasks) drawn entirely from the disjoint asset pool. Evaluation uses a composable scoring library assembled from shared primitives: scene-graph relations (e.g., "cup on tray"), articulation joint angles, object tilt and orientation, and end-effector pose tolerances. Tasks are declared as ordered stage sequences; Score advances at each stage, SR fires only on full completion. Four VLAs are post-trained from pretrained checkpoints under an identical recipe: 200K gradient steps, batch 128, AdamW, cosine LR schedule, peak lr=1e-5, action-chunk prediction horizon 50 steps, open-loop execution of first 30. Each model is evaluated across 3 independent seeds; results reported as mean ± SD. Critical design decisions: (1) Dual-track synthesis is well-justified — teleoperation cannot scale to long-horizon mobile tasks (compound failure probability), and motion planning cannot generate the reactive contact needed for sub-centimeter insertion. (2) Asset-level train/test isolation prevents leakage at the object and scene level while sharing the same randomization pipeline. (3) Task-level permutation tests (10,000 iterations) replace linear regression for controlled breakdown analysis, correctly identified as under-powered at T=26 with >20 tag categories. Key Results The four models achieve near-identical aggregate Test SRs — π0: 24.4%, XVLA: 24.7%, InternVLA-A1: 27.6%, π0.5: 29.5% — within a 5-point band. Yet capability profiles diverge by tens of points. On operating mode: InternVLA-A1 achieves ~34.8% SR on mobile tasks (Δ=+30.9% over fixed-base, p=0.008, the most statistically significant tag effect) but collapses to 5.8% on dexterous fixed-base tasks — a 29-point gap. π0 shows the most balanced profile, with an 11-point mobile-dexterous spread. On precision: π0 leads at high-precision tasks (13.8% SR); all other models fall to single-digit SR. π0.5 leads low-precision tasks at 44.2% SR. On horizon: long-horizon tasks expose 15-point SR drops for XVLA (28.9% short → 13.5% long), suggesting weak temporal credit assignment in its modular decoder. On atomic skill: no single model dominates all 11 skills. π0 leads Pull (47%) and Press (50%); XVLA leads Push (73.8%) but scores 5.8% on Handover; InternVLA-A1 wins Move and Sweep but scores 0% on Press and Flip; π0.5 is the only model with no catastrophic-zero categories. Five universally hard tasks (shop, bottle, peg_in_hole, collect_coffee_beans, flip_cup_collect_cookies) score ≤5% SR across all models and splits. On generalization: background and instruction perturbations are relatively mild (27–35% SR), object swaps harder (21–29%), and Mix the most challenging (18–23%). Train-test retention: π0.5 retains best (SR ratio 0.92), InternVLA-A1 worst (0.83), consistent with InternVLA-A1 overfitting the training distribution. Pretraining sensitivity (Section 7, comparison across EBench, LIBERO, RoboTwin 2.0): EBench shows 9–21 SR point pretrained-vs-from-scratch gaps (π0: 11.2→24.4%, π0.5: 8.5→29.5%, XVLA: 15.7→24.7%). On LIBERO, all five architectures score 94–98% regardless of pretraining (no measurable gap). On RoboTwin 2.0 Hard, from-scratch models reach 88–98%, exceeding pretrained baselines of 58–77%. Full evaluation completes in under 2 hours on 8 RTX 4090 GPUs. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the structured co-design of 5 capability axes and 4 generalization dimensions into a single benchmark spanning three manipulation regimes, enabling decomposed diagnosis that no existing suite provides. The dual-track synthesis pipeline and composable scoring library are engineering contributions. The statistical methodology (permutation-based controlled breakdown) is an application of existing methods to benchmark design, not a new algorithm. The cross-benchmark pretraining sensitivity study is a new empirical finding. Rigor & Reproducibility Three independent evaluation seeds per model with mean ± SD reported, appropriate for IsaacSim's non-deterministic renderer. Controlled breakdown analysis uses 10,000-iteration task-level permutation tests — methodologically sound for T=26, and the authors correctly explain why linear regression is under-powered. Code, task configurations, evaluation library, all splits, and the 91.4-hour training dataset are publicly released (project page, GitHub, and leaderboard linked in the paper). A camera-perspective sensitivity appendix ablates headview vs. overview streams. Methodological concerns: (1) All baselines are post-trained on EBench data with a uniform recipe; the "pretrained" condition reflects pretrained initialization + EBench fine-tuning, not zero-shot transfer. (2) Scene-level permutation p-values are uniformly non-significant (p > 0.2 for nearly all cells), correctly noted in the appendix but not foregrounded in main-text scene-ranking figures. (3) No ablation on data quantity or quality: mobile/long-horizon tasks receive 200 motion-planned episodes vs. 400 teleoperated episodes for dexterous tasks — the observed mobile-dexterous performance gap could partly reflect this asymmetry rather than pure architectural differences. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Full benchmark validation runs on 8 RTX 4090 GPUs in ~30 minutes; full validation + test in under 2 hours. Consumer-grade hardware suffices. - Efficient: The 91.4-hour synthesis dataset is generated via cuRobo motion planning and actor-follower teleoperation rather than exclusively costly human demonstration; the distributed evaluation runner is open-source. - Effective: The benchmark demonstrably separates models with identical aggregate scores into distinct capability profiles and is the only benchmark among LIBERO, RoboTwin 2.0, and EBench that captures the pretraining contribution (9–21 SR point gap). Effective as a diagnostic tool, though sim-real correlation is untested. - Scalable: 26 tasks sparsely covering 9 scene categories; the authors acknowledge this limits statistical power and plan expansion to 50+ tasks. Camera-perspective sensitivity is small relative to cross-model differences (~8 SR points max vs. ~30 points for pretrain gap), so the evaluation is robust to this configuration choice. Software artifact: full benchmark codebase (evaluation runner, scoring library, synthesis pipeline, dataset, leaderboard) is publicly released. Strengths - The 5-axis capability taxonomy reveals within-cohort heterogeneity of up to 29 SR points (InternVLA-A1's mobile-dexterous gap) that aggregate scalars fully conceal; this is a substantive contribution to benchmark methodology, not just a task collection. - The permutation-test-based controlled breakdown is the statistically appropriate tool for T=26 with correlated tags — the authors identify the regression under-power problem and use the right method. - The cross-benchmark pretraining sensitivity study is a meaningful empirical finding: demonstrating that LIBERO and RoboTwin 2.0 cannot distinguish pretrained from from-scratch models positions EBench as uniquely suited to evaluating general-purpose pretraining investments. - The dual-track synthesis pipeline (teleoperation for dexterous tasks, cuRobo for mobile/long-horizon) is a principled engineering solution to a genuine data-collection bottleneck, yielding a diverse public dataset. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that EBench operates entirely in simulation and they do not claim simulation scores predict real-robot performance; they plan a sim-real correlation study in future work. They acknowledge that 26 tasks cover 9 scene categories sparsely, limiting multi-way regression, and list expanding to 50+ tasks as a roadmap item. These are confronted directly in a dedicated limitations section, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The open-loop action-chunking evaluation protocol (50-step prediction, 30-step execution) is likely a systematic disadvantage for contact-rich dexterous tasks that require reactive closed-loop correction; the observed dexterous performance floor may reflect evaluation protocol mismatch as much as model limitation. - No ablation separates the effect of demonstration count (200 vs. 400 episodes per task) from task difficulty or architecture; the mobile-dexterous performance gap could be confounded by this data quantity asymmetry. - Scene-level rankings in the main text (e.g., π0.5 at 60% in Bedroom, InternVLA-A1 leading Kitchen at 44.5%) are presented without statistical caveats despite all scene permutation p-values exceeding 0.1; these rankings likely reflect operating-mode and precision composition of each scene rather than scene-specific priors, as the appendix notes but does not emphasize. - The five universally hard tasks (≤5% SR across all models) are proposed as a "hard suite" stress test, but with only 5 tasks, cross-model comparisons on this subset will have very high variance. Bottom Line EBench is a well-engineered diagnostic benchmark that fills a genuine gap: practitioners building or comparing generalist VLA policies will find the multi-dimensional capability profiles and public leaderboard directly actionable, and no existing alternative provides equivalent structured decomposition across mobile, long-horizon, and dexterous regimes in a single evaluation suite.
Recommended 2606.18239 PDF
#17 — Innovative (74)

ViQ: Text-Aligned Visual Quantized Representations at Any Resolution (36 ▲ )

Xumin Yu, Zuyan Liu, Zhenyu Yang, Yuhao Dong, Shengsheng Qian, Jiwen Lu, Han Hu, Yongming Rao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ViQ, a two-stage training framework that converts continuous visual features into discrete quantized tokens while preserving both high-level semantic understanding and low-level reconstruction fidelity at native (arbitrary) image resolutions. - Why it's important: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) pay a heavy compute tax loading high-dimensional continuous visual features per training step; prior discrete visual tokenizers either lose fine-grained detail or collapse semantic structure upon quantization, limiting their practical viability as a drop-in replacement for continuous encoders. - How it does it: ViQ first trains a visual encoder with joint text-supervision and self-distillation losses to embed rich semantics into a continuous feature space at any resolution, then progressively compresses those features through L∞-normed bottleneck layers into a 6-dimensional Finite Scalar Quantization (FSQ) space augmented with 2D Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) and multi-head attention expansion. - The good: Using a 1.3B-parameter visual encoder, ViQ achieves an average of 57.2 (9-benchmark suite, Qwen2.5-1.5B backbone) and 63.9 (Qwen2.5-7B), matching InternViT-2.5-6B (57.0 / 63.8) at less than a quarter of the encoder parameter count, while delivering 20%–78% training step speedups over continuous encoders across 0.5B–7B LLM scales. - The missing: Reconstruction quality trails the strongest discrete competitor UniTok by 2.59 dB PSNR (22.73 vs. 25.32) and rFID 0.62 vs. 0.37 on 256×256 ImageNet-1K, a gap the paper acknowledges only briefly and does not ablate with orthogonal remedies (residual VQ, multi-scale tokenization). - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on unified discrete visual representations or MLLM training efficiency; practitioners who primarily need image generation quality should check UniTok first.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (the contribution is a discrete visual tokenizer measured by understanding-benchmark transfer, though it carries strong Systems characteristics). Rigor (78) drives the composite up: systematic ablations isolate proximal steps, bottleneck dimension, codebook size, and position encoding, the comparison protocol is controlled (fixed 2000K samples, same any-resolution pipeline), and both code and weights are released — but all results are single-run with no error bars, it is a June 2026 preprint, and the eval suite overlaps the Stage-1 training distribution. Novelty (70) is a non-trivial NEW METHOD (proximal L∞-bottleneck compression before FSQ) rather than a new formulation. Non-Obviousness (75) is genuine: the 8-point ablation gap (60.9→68.7) validates that quantizing a pre-constrained latent geometry was not the obvious path. Impact (80) reflects released weights, pre-extractable offline codes, and measured 46–78% training speedups, tempered by no generation evaluation and an OCRBench gap (636 vs 690). Completeness (72) and Resilience (70) are pulled down by the unresolved SigLIP2-g-initialization confound and unsupported extreme-resolution claims. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*80 = 74.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Multimodal large language models require visual features to be semantically rich yet compatible with the discrete token-based processing that governs language models. Dominant continuous visual encoders (CLIP, SigLIP, DINOv2, InternViT) output high-dimensional, dense floating-point tensors per image patch, which impose significant memory and compute overhead during MLLM training and inference. Prior discrete visual tokenizers address this by compressing images into integer codebook indices, but face a fundamental tension: autoencoders optimized for pixel-level reconstruction (VQ-VAE variants) lose semantic structure, while contrastively trained encoders (QLIP, UniTok) degrade fine-grained visual detail upon aggressive dimensionality compression. Existing quantized multimodal encoders lag continuous counterparts by large margins on document OCR, chart recognition, and visual question answering tasks, specifically because high-frequency spatial detail is discarded during the continuous-to-discrete mapping. Key Idea The core insight is that quantizing a high-dimensional continuous feature space directly causes severe precision loss because the codebook vectors cannot densely cover the space. ViQ solves this by first training a proximal representation — using an L∞-normed bottleneck that projects features onto a hypercube surface — which constrains and regularizes the latent geometry before any discretization occurs. This proximal step progressively narrows the Euclidean gap between features and their nearest codebook anchors, making the subsequent FSQ assignment low-distortion. Text-aligned pre-training ensures the continuous space being compressed already carries multimodal semantics, so no semantic-recovery step is needed post-quantization. Methodology ViQ training runs in two major stages. Stage 1 (text-aligned pre-training) fine-tunes a SigLIP2-g visual encoder (C=1536 output dimensions) using: (a) a text supervision loss (cross-entropy with a frozen Qwen2.5-VL-0.5B backbone, L_text = CE[LLM(ViQ(I)), A]) and (b) a self-distillation loss aligning the student's class token to the original fixed-resolution SigLIP2-g teacher's class token via cosine similarity. Any-resolution support comes from replacing the fixed positional embedding with a resizable version following NaViT and variable-length token packing from OryxViT. Training progresses from 384² fixed resolution to native resolutions up to 768². Stage 2 is split into two substages. Stage 2-1 (proximal representation learning) introduces a bottleneck layer compressing C=1536 to D=128, then applies L∞ normalization to project features onto a hypercube, and adds a VAE-latent reconstruction loss L_recon = NLL(f̂, Encoder(x)) supervised by a frozen Qwen-Image VAE encoder. The full objective is L_total = λ_text·L_text + λ_distill·L_distill + λ_recon·L_recon. Stage 2-2 (quantization training) replaces the L∞ norm with an FSQ module operating on a d=6 dimensional feature space with levels [8,8,8,5,5,5] (codebook size 64,000). Before quantization, each N-patch feature sequence is expanded 2×2 via multi-head self-attention (projecting R^{B×N×d} → R^{B×4N×d}) to increase representational capacity; after quantization, the sequence is projected back to the original length. 2D RoPE encodes height-width position explicitly prior to the quantization step. Implementation: 128 A100 GPUs for Stage 1 (~6B vision-language tokens total), 256 A100 GPUs for Stage 2 (~31B vision-language tokens). Critical design decisions: (1) The bottleneck dimension D=128 is well-justified by ablation — 32 is too narrow and 512 or 1536 both underperform, confirming the proximal-compression benefit. (2) FSQ over SimVQ or VQ-with-learnable-codebook is justified empirically (64.4 avg vs. 60.9 for direct SimVQ) and algorithmically (no codebook collapse issue). (3) RoPE-2D over learnable positional embeddings is justified (68.7 vs. 65.7) because learnable embeddings raise optimization difficulty for the VQ module. Key Results On multimodal understanding across 9 benchmarks (MMStar, MMMU, SimpleVQA, InfoVQA, TextVQA, DocVQA, OCRBench, AI2D, ChartQA) with fixed 2000K-sample fine-tuning data from LLaVA-OneVision: - Qwen2.5-1.5B backbone: ViQ avg 57.2 vs. InternViT-2.5-6B avg 57.0 (ViQ uses 1.3B vs. 6B encoder params); ViQ OCRBench 636 vs. 690 for InternViT (still the leader on that task). - Qwen2.5-7B backbone: ViQ avg 63.9 vs. InternViT-2.5-6B avg 63.8; ViQ TextVQA 88.9 vs. 85.7 for InternViT. - Prior quantized encoders QLIP and UniTok score 29.7 and 33.0 average respectively (Qwen2.5-1.5B), showing ViQ outperforms them by 24–28 points absolute. - Training efficiency (Figure 3, single node): 0.5B LLM forward pass 70% faster at 4k sequence length, 78% at 16k; 7B LLM 46% faster at 4k, 65% at 16k; full training step >20% at 4k, >40% at 16k. - Reconstruction on 256×256 ImageNet-1K (Table 3): ViQ PSNR 22.73, SSIM 0.66, rFID 0.62; UniTok leads at 25.32 / 0.77 / 0.37; ViQ is second among understanding-optimized discrete tokenizers in rFID. - Storage: ViQ codes require HW/32 bytes per image — 96× compression vs. raw RGB. Baselines are generally current and strong (SigLIP2-g, AIMv2, InternViT-2.5, OryxViT). The paper applies a fair-comparison protocol (identical 2000K fine-tuning samples, same LLaVA-NeXT any-resolution pipeline for fixed-resolution encoders). However, ViQ itself is also initialized from SigLIP2-g, making the comparison against SigLIP2-g a controlled transfer rather than an entirely independent method comparison. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The proximal representation learning strategy (progressive L∞-constrained bottleneck compression before FSQ) and the combination of text-aligned pre-training with reconstruction supervision to bridge semantics and low-level fidelity in a single discrete tokenizer are genuinely novel; applying FSQ and RoPE individually to vision tokenizers is not new, but their integration into a unified two-stage any-resolution quantization pipeline is a non-trivial composition. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations (Table 4) systematically isolate proximal representation steps, bottleneck dimensions, codebook sizes, position encoding methods, and loss combinations, providing clear evidence for each design choice. The comparison protocol (fixed 2000K samples, same pipeline for baselines) is carefully controlled. Error bars and significance tests are absent throughout — results are single-run. Code is publicly released (https://github.com/yuxumin/ViQ) and weights are on HuggingFace (https://huggingface.co/XuminYu/ViQ_weights), which aids reproducibility. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.27313v1, June 25 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. A methodological concern: the evaluation benchmark suite (especially OCR and chart tasks) overlaps heavily with the kind of high-resolution document understanding data used in Stage 1 training, so the evaluation may not be fully out-of-distribution for the encoder. The efficiency experiments are conducted on a single node to avoid inter-node communication noise, which is a reasonable but somewhat idealized setup. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (a built and released visual tokenizer evaluated by deployment in MLLM training). - Practical: ViQ runs on standard NVIDIA A100/H100 hardware; discrete codes can be extracted offline and cached, reducing per-training-step cost to a projection layer. - Efficient: Stage 2 quantization training requires 256 A100 GPUs for ~31B tokens, which is non-trivial; however, once trained, inference uses the 1.3B-parameter encoder and produces compact 6-dimensional codes with 96× storage compression over raw pixels. - Effective: ViQ solves the stated problem — achieving multimodal understanding parity with 6B-parameter continuous encoders using 1.3B parameters — though it still trails the best continuous encoder (InternViT-2.5-6B) on the most detail-intensive benchmark (OCRBench: 636 vs. 690). - Scalable: Tested with LLM backbones from 0.5B to 7B; scaling to 70B+ backbones and to resolutions beyond 768² is not evaluated, only claimed as future work. The native-resolution mechanism is architecturally sound (RoPE + variable-length tokens), but extreme resolution behavior is unknown. The work delivers a trained model (weights released), code for inference and fine-tuning, and the pre-extracted discrete codes can be stored and reused across training runs. Strengths - The proximal representation learning strategy is the paper's clearest intellectual contribution: the ablation (Table 4a) shows direct continuous-to-FSQ quantization yields 60.9 avg while the full proximal pipeline reaches 68.7 — an 8-point gap that validates the core claim concretely. - The training efficiency gains are large and directly measured (46%–78% forward pass speedup depending on model and sequence length), not estimated, and arise from a principled architectural property (discrete codes are pre-extractable offline) rather than from a hardware-specific trick. - The any-resolution design is handled cleanly: NaViT-style positional embedding replacement plus OryxViT variable-length packing, with empirical validation that native resolution improves over fixed-resolution baselines. - Releasing weights and code at submission time for a system of this compute scale is a genuine positive for reproducibility. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge two limitations: (1) the efficiency and effectiveness have only been validated with LLMs from 0.5B to 7B, leaving synergistic effects with models at 70B+ unexplored; (2) the proximal representation quality depends on the diversity and quality of pre-training data, so data biases could affect zero-shot generalization in highly specialized or out-of-distribution domains. The authors confront these directly and credibly, without minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The reconstruction gap relative to UniTok (PSNR 22.73 vs. 25.32, rFID 0.62 vs. 0.37) is meaningful for any generative downstream use case (image synthesis, editing), but the paper does not evaluate image generation at all. This omits the most natural application domain for a tokenizer with a 64,000-entry codebook. - The multi-head attention expansion (N→4N patches before quantization) adds a non-trivial compute overhead per forward pass not separately reported in the efficiency analysis; the reported speedups are measured against SigLIP2-g but include this expansion cost implicitly. - All multimodal evaluations use the same fixed 2000K-sample LLaVA-OneVision dataset. Performance under larger or domain-specific fine-tuning data regimes is not shown, which limits confidence in the generality of the advantage. - The encoder is initialized from SigLIP2-g and fine-tuned; it is unclear how much of the strong performance is attributable to the SigLIP2-g initialization vs. the ViQ-specific training stages. A random-init or lighter encoder baseline is absent. - Extreme-resolution stress tests (4K images, long document pages) are mentioned as motivation but not evaluated; the 768² training ceiling leaves this claim empirically unsupported. Bottom Line ViQ is a well-executed, reproducible system that credibly closes the quantized-vs-continuous visual encoder gap for multimodal understanding, and practitioners building MLLM training pipelines who want discrete visual tokens with meaningful efficiency gains should read it; those focused on generation quality or extreme-resolution tasks should treat the claims cautiously until further evaluation.
Recommended 2606.27313 PDF
#18 — Innovative (74)

StylisticBias: A Few Human Visual Cues Drive Most Social Biases in MLLMs (2 ▲ )

Shaghayegh Kolli, Timo Cavelius, Nafiseh Nikeghbal, Samantha Dalal, Jana Diesner · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces StylisticBias, a controlled benchmark of 500 synthetic base faces with ~25K single-attribute visual variations that measures how specific stylistic cues shift social judgment scores in multimodal large language models. - Why it's important: Prior MLLM bias audits compare model outputs across demographic groups or individuals, conflating identity with appearance; this benchmark isolates which stylistic signals (fashion, hair, makeup, eyewear, etc.) actually drive social judgments in consequential domains like hiring and judicial assessment. - How it does it: For each of 500 AI-generated base faces, ~50 single-attribute edits are generated via Nano Banana (Gemini 2.5 Flash Image) while holding identity fixed; six MLLMs then make binary forced-choice social judgments across 25 scenarios under 4 prompt orderings x 3 random seeds, yielding ~4.72M judgment calls per model. - The good: ~15 visual attributes account for ~80% of total |SBS|, and worn/distressed clothing produces a 1.38x larger negative shift than formal attire produces a positive shift (median |SBS| = 0.167 vs. 0.121, p < 2.3x10^-11), with both findings well-supported by rigorous statistical testing across all six models. - The missing: The benchmark relies entirely on synthetic images generated by Imagen 4 and Nano Banana, and the paper includes no validation that the attribute-level effects found on synthetic faces transfer to real photographs, which is the central external validity gap. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML fairness researchers and MLLM auditors; practitioners deploying real-world systems should read it with the synthetic-image caveat in mind, as the attribute concentration and semantic alignment findings are specific enough to guide audit prioritization.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (introduces and evaluates a benchmark). Rigor (78) is the top contributor: 12 prompts per image-scenario pair (4 orderings x 3 seeds), Kruskal-Wallis / Mann-Whitney / Wilcoxon testing with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction, linear mixed-effects variance decomposition, 90% human-validated images, and public dataset + code release — strong by fairness-auditing standards, though it is a preprint, excludes proprietary models, and runs entirely on synthetic faces. Novelty (75) is a NEW BENCHMARK plus genuinely new findings: within-identity single-attribute isolation enables causal attribution that demographic-comparison work structurally cannot. Non-Obviousness (72) credits the semantic-alignment-bias result and the ~15-attributes-explain-80% concentration. Completeness (75) is a positive — the authors confront the synthetic-only and curated-subset limitations directly rather than minimizing them. Resilience (68) is the one dimension that pulls the composite down: the absence of any real-photograph replication leaves the central external-validity claim untested. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*70 = 74.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 75 Resilience 68 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation MLLMs deployed in consequential settings (hiring support, content moderation, judicial assessment) produce social judgments that are influenced by the visual appearance of people in images. Prior work measuring appearance-driven bias compares model outputs across different individuals or demographic groups, making it impossible to determine which specific visual attributes drive observed differences versus underlying identity representations. The question of which stylistic cues (clothing style, hair, makeup, eyewear) shift MLLM social judgments, and by how much, has not been answered at the attribute level. The gap matters because targeted mitigation requires knowing which attributes are responsible for bias rather than simply knowing that bias exists across groups; an audit that reports "obese faces are judged more harshly" cannot tell a model developer or deployer which specific visual signals to address. Key Idea The core technical insight is to fix face identity and vary exactly one visual attribute at a time across a large set of synthetic identities, then measure the signed difference in MLLM preference scores between the base image and its single-attribute variant (the prediction shift Δ_i(x_v) = φ_i(x_v) − φ_i(x_b)). This within-identity comparison eliminates identity confounds that plague between-individual comparisons. The secondary empirical insight is "semantic alignment bias": models are most sensitive to visual cues that are culturally associated with the judged trait (e.g., clothing style drives "Stylish vs. Unstylish" far more than it drives "Honest vs. Dishonest"), and this semantic alignment pattern is structurally consistent across all six evaluated architectures. Methodology The pipeline has two stages: benchmark generation and benchmark evaluation. In the generation stage, 500 photorealistic base faces are synthesized using Imagen 4 via structured prompts spanning age (young, middle-aged, elderly), gender (male, female), ethnicity (Asian, African, European, Middle Eastern, Latino), and body type (thin, normal, obese), yielding 90 demographic configurations. For each base face, ~50 single-attribute variations are generated using Nano Banana, modifying one of: skin irregularities, hair color, hair length, hair style, facial hair (male), makeup, lip makeup (female), tattoos, eyewear, piercings, accessories, or fashion style. Clothing variations require full-body portraits; all others use head-and-shoulders framing. A plausibility pass removes incoherent demographic combinations, and a curation pass removes visually subtle or near-redundant values, reducing the original 25K images to 15,726 evaluated images across 34 values in 12 attribute categories. Human validators reviewed 90% of generated images; 98% passed plausibility, identity consistency, and attribute-realization checks. In the evaluation stage, six open-source MLLMs (LLaVA-v1.6-Mistral-7B, Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, Pixtral-12B, InternVL3-14B, Gemma-3-12B-IT, Gemma-4-E4B-IT, ranging from 4B to 14B active parameters) are queried zero-shot at temperature 0.2 with max 16 output tokens. For each (image, scenario) pair, 4 prompt orderings x 3 random seeds = 12 prompts are used, yielding order-invariant preference scores φ_i(x). The 25 binary forced-choice scenarios span personality/social, interpersonal, behavioral, and socioeconomic/appearance dimensions grounded in the warmth-competence framework. Three metrics are reported: Variation Strength (VS, between-group dispersion in preference scores across demographic groups), Signed Bias Shift (SBS, average attribute-induced preference shift across all image-scenario pairs), and statistical significance via Kruskal-Wallis (age, body type, ethnicity), Mann-Whitney U (gender), and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests, all with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction at α = 0.05. Linear mixed-effects models with random intercepts per face identity quantify variance attribution (marginal R² = 0.594). Critical design decisions: (a) single-attribute edits with identity preservation — well-justified by the causal attribution goal; (b) forced-choice binary judgments with multiple orderings/seeds — well-justified to eliminate prompt-sensitivity artifacts; (c) synthetic images only — deliberately chosen for controllability but at the cost of real-world generalizability, which the authors acknowledge. Key Results Body type (VS = 0.069) and age (VS = 0.075) are the strongest demographic drivers of social judgment, reaching significance in 76% and 78% of scenarios on average. Ethnicity (VS = 0.038) and gender (VS = 0.030) show substantially smaller effects; LLaVA-v1.6 reaches significance in 96% of body type scenarios but only 44% of ethnicity scenarios, challenging the assumption that demographic signals are uniformly salient. Among stylistic attributes, approximately 15 attributes account for ~80% of total |SBS|. Fashion style produces the largest positive aggregate SBS (+0.046), followed by facial hair (+0.042), makeup & lips (+0.037), and eyewear (+0.035). Hair style (−0.023) and skin irregularities (−0.021 to −0.019) produce consistently negative SBS. Worn/distressed clothing produces a median |SBS| of 0.167 vs. 0.121 for formal/business attire, a 1.38x asymmetry (one-sided WSRT, p < 2.3x10^-11). Age moderates fashion effects monotonically across all styles: smart casual SBS rises from +0.082 for young faces to +0.173 for elderly faces (2x amplification). Three cues show gender-dependent sign reversals: facial tattoos (male −0.006 [ns], female +0.033, p < 0.001) and multiple piercings (male −0.023, female +0.011). Semantic alignment bias is quantified: scenario category explains more variance in prediction shifts (η²_p = 0.248) than variation category (η²_p = 0.153). "Stylish vs. Unstylish" reaches SBS ≈ +0.244 and "Wealthy vs. Poor" SBS ≈ +0.114, while "Honest," "Loyal," and "Trustworthy" remain near zero. Across models, Gemma-3 shows the highest rate of large individual shifts (|Δ| ≥ 0.25 in 30.1% of cases) while Qwen3 is most conservative (80.0% near-zero shifts). Models agree substantially on which cues matter: the per-scenario mean Δ correlation between Gemma-3 and Gemma-4 is r = 0.75 (slope = 0.39), indicating shared bias structure with magnitude attenuation in the larger model. Total judgment calls: ~28.3 million across six models. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. StylisticBias is the primary contribution: a controlled, attribute-level MLLM bias evaluation dataset with synthetic identity isolation that enables within-identity causal attribution, a design not previously applied to MLLM fairness auditing at this scale. The empirical findings — bias concentration in ~15 attributes, semantic alignment bias, asymmetric negativity effects, and cross-model structural consistency — are genuinely novel and distinguish this from prior demographic-comparison work. The attribute-level isolation design is novel in the MLLM fairness context; the individual components (synthetic face generation, forced-choice evaluation, BH-corrected significance testing) are standard. Rigor & Reproducibility The study uses 12 prompts per image-scenario pair (4 orderings x 3 seeds) to control for prompt sensitivity, which is a genuine methodological strength and addresses a well-documented MLLM evaluation confound. Statistical testing covers Kruskal-Wallis, Mann-Whitney U, and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests, all with BH correction, and effect sizes (partial η²_p, Cohen's d) are reported throughout. Linear mixed-effects models decompose variance. Human validation reviewed 90% of images with a 98% pass rate. Code and dataset are publicly released at github.com/timo-cavelius/StylisticBias and hf.co/datasets/shaghayeh/stylistic-bias-dataset. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.20527v1, 18 Jun 2026) — not peer-reviewed. The six models evaluated are all open-source; proprietary models (GPT-4V, Claude, Gemini) are absent, limiting coverage of the models most frequently deployed in consequential settings. No ablation tests the sensitivity of findings to the choice of image generator or the variation-reduction filtering strategy. The benchmark is developed and evaluated entirely on synthetic images with no real-photograph replication, raising an external validity concern about whether synthetic-image SBS predicts real-deployment SBS. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (introduces and evaluates a benchmark dataset). - Practical: The evaluation pipeline runs on six off-the-shelf open-source MLLMs (7B-14B parameters) accessible on standard GPU hardware; benchmark images are publicly released. - Efficient: ~4.72M judgment calls per model at 16 output tokens each is computationally substantial; the variation reduction from 25K to 15,726 images (cutting ~40%) is a practical concession to compute that the authors document explicitly. - Effective: The benchmark measures attribute-level bias under controlled synthetic conditions with strong statistical power; whether the magnitude of effects observed on synthetic faces matches real-deployment magnitudes is untested. - Scalable: The design extends straightforwardly to more identities or attributes, but the total compute (28.3M judgment calls for 6 models) scales linearly with both dimensions; no stress-scale experiments are reported. Delivers: a dataset (15,726 images + judgment records), code pipeline, and a set of empirical findings; no trained model or inference library. Strengths - The single-attribute-edit design with fixed identity is the correct experimental unit for causal attribution of visual bias; it cleanly separates stylistic effects from identity effects in a way that demographic group comparisons structurally cannot. - The finding that ~15 attributes account for ~80% of total |SBS| is specific, quantified, and directly actionable for audit prioritization, telling practitioners where to concentrate measurement effort. - The prompt-sensitivity controls (4 orderings x 3 seeds per pair) and BH-corrected multi-hypothesis testing are thorough and address the two most common methodological criticisms of LLM evaluation studies. - The cross-model consistency finding (r = 0.75 between Gemma-3 and Gemma-4) elevates the findings from model-specific observations to claims about the shared learned structure of appearance-driven social judgment across open-source MLLM architectures. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors identify two main limitations directly. First, the benchmark uses controlled synthetic images rather than real photographs; this is a deliberate design choice for attribute isolation, and the authors acknowledge that results characterize model behavior in a controlled synthetic setting and may not capture the full real-world photograph distribution. Second, the demographic and attribute spaces are curated subsets rather than exhaustive covers, chosen for tractability; the paper acknowledges this limits the ability to explain the mechanisms that produce the observed effects. The authors confront both limitations directly rather than minimizing them, and provide specific justification for each design decision — a credibility positive in this framework. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No real-image validation: the paper includes no replication on real photographs (e.g., a matched subset from a public face dataset) to verify that synthetic-image SBS predicts real-image SBS. This is the central external validity concern and would take moderate effort to address even partially. - Proprietary models excluded: GPT-4V, Claude, and Gemini Vision are not evaluated. These models are disproportionately represented in consequential deployment contexts (hiring tools, content moderation systems), and their exclusion limits the practical relevance of the per-model findings to open-source-only deployments. - Aggregate SBS masking: the paper notes (in Section 5.2) that averaging SBS across demographic subgroups can yield near-zero values when effects cancel (e.g., piercings shift male and female faces in opposite directions), but does not provide a fully decomposed subgroup analysis for all 34 attribute values — only illustrates the issue for selected cases. - Causal direction ambiguity: the design measures whether an attribute shifts a preference score, not whether the shift reflects a learned stereotype versus a legitimate learned association. All non-zero SBS is treated as bias without discussing this distinction, which matters for normative claims about harm. - Base-face age imbalance: the sample skews toward young adults (260 of 500) vs. middle-aged (124) and elderly (116), which could understate age-interaction effects for underrepresented age groups. Bottom Line StylisticBias is the most methodologically disciplined attribute-level MLLM bias benchmark to date, and its core findings — bias concentration in ~15 stylistic cues and the semantic alignment effect — are specific enough to immediately change how practitioners prioritize appearance-bias audits, making it worth reading for anyone working on MLLM fairness or deployment.
Recommended 2606.20527 PDF
#19 — Innovative (73)

MemGUI-Agent: An End-to-End Long-Horizon Mobile GUI Agent with Proactive Context Management (33 ▲ )

Guangyi Liu, Gao Wu, Congxiao Liu, Pengxiang Zhao, Liang Liu, Mading Li, Qi Zhang, Mengyan Wang, Liang Guo, Yong Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MemGUI-Agent, an end-to-end long-horizon mobile GUI agent built on ConAct (Context-as-Action), a paradigm that treats history folding, UI memory writes, and self-describing step outputs as first-class policy actions emitted by the same MLLM that selects UI interactions. - Why it's important: Current mobile GUI agents collapse on tasks averaging 36+ steps and spanning multiple apps because passive context accumulation causes prompt explosion and silently drops task-critical facts such as prices, phone numbers, and copied text — making reliable phone automation intractable without solving context management first. - How it does it: ConAct extends the agent's action space with memory_add/update/delete operations and a mandatory per-step folding directive, both predicted in a single forward pass alongside the UI action, so context curation is task-aware rather than mechanical. - The good: Zero-shot ConAct applied to Qwen3-VL-235B-Thinking achieves 62.5% Pass@3 on MemGUI-Bench, surpassing the best agentic framework (M3A on Gemini-2.5-Pro at 50.0% Pass@3) with a simpler end-to-end architecture requiring no external memory module. - The missing: The ablation is limited to the 40-task MemGUI-Bench-40 subset with no significance tests, and MemGUI-3K training data derives 86.8% of tasks from simplifications of the same 128 MemGUI-Bench seed tasks used for evaluation — a structural overlap the paper does not acknowledge. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners building long-horizon GUI or agentic systems should read this; ConAct is immediately implementable and MemGUI-3K fills a genuine data gap, though iOS, desktop, and web applicability remain untested.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (end-to-end GUI agent with released weights, dataset, and code). Practical Impact (85) dominates the weighted score — MemGUI-8B-SFT weights, the 64,430-step MemGUI-3K dataset, and training/eval code are all released, with large interpretable gains on hard 41+ step tasks (+18.5 P@1) and OOD transfer to MobileWorld. Novelty (72) is real but integrative — ConAct unifies UI action and context curation in one forward pass, though UI memory, folding, and self-describing outputs each have precursors. Rigor (62) is the main drag: no significance tests on results separated by 1-3 points, single-epoch single-seed LoRA, and 86.8% of training tasks are simplifications of the 128 evaluation seed tasks with only task-ID overlap analyzed. Resilience (65) suffers because the headline zero-shot gains depend entirely on the same model used as the data teacher. Weighted: 0.15*72+0.15*62+0.10*70+0.15*70+0.15*65+0.30*85 = 73.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Long-horizon mobile GUI automation requires retaining intermediate facts — prices, contact numbers, article titles, copied text — across 20–40+ steps and across multiple app transitions. MLLM-based end-to-end agents currently use passive context strategies: Action-Thought trace appending, multi-turn prompts, rule-based truncation, or no-history prompting. These strategies fail in complementary ways: passive accumulation causes linear prompt growth (prompt explosion), while mechanical truncation discards UI-derived facts that cannot be re-derived without re-visiting prior screens. The strongest open-data 8B end-to-end model achieves only 9.4% Pass@1 on MemGUI-Bench (avg. 36.2 steps), and success rates on MemGUI-Bench fall from 71.6% on short AndroidWorld tasks (avg. 8.4 steps) to 11.7% at the MemGUI-Bench horizon. The failure is attributed to context management, not model capability: agents can perceive relevant UI but lose what they observed several screens earlier. Key Idea The central insight is that deciding what to compress, what to memorize verbatim, and what to retain in the recent-step record is a policy decision that must be made with task-level awareness — and therefore should be co-predicted by the same model, in the same forward pass, as the UI action decision. ConAct formalizes this by expanding the action space to include memory_add/update/delete operations and mandatory history-folding directives, issued as structured output fields at every step. This turns passive history accumulation into active, task-aware context curation without introducing a separate summarizer, retriever, or memory agent. Methodology The agent maintains structured state S_t = (G, H_t, M_t, L_t): task goal G, Folded Action History H_t (compressed trajectory summaries), Folded UI State M_t (persistent UI-derived facts as id/description/content triples), and Recent Step Record L_t (latest-step details). At each step the MLLM emits a 5-part structured output: <thinking> (reasoning), <folding> (JSON range+summary directive specifying which history span to compress), <tool_call> (one UI or memory action), <ui_observation> (detailed verbatim screen description), and <action_intent> (next-step plan). The folding directive operates at two granularities: step-level distillation keeps the latest step as a compact record, while span-level abstraction summarizes a completed sub-task spanning multiple steps into one reusable record, pushing context growth from linear toward sub-linear. Memory items are stored as structured triples; memory writes store complete verbatim content (a full price or phone number), not summaries or references. Critical design decisions: (1) requiring verbatim content in memory preserves exact values across screen changes; (2) making folding mandatory from step 2 prevents regression to passive accumulation; (3) self-describing fields (ui_observation, action_intent) supply grounded content for future folding and memory actions without re-querying the screenshot. MemGUI-3K is constructed by expanding the 128 MemGUI-Bench seed tasks via entity substitution (456 tasks), memory-operation augmentation (289 tasks), and task simplification (6,558 tasks), yielding a 7,303-task pool from which 5,293 enter rollout using Qwen3-VL-235B-Thinking as teacher with the full 5-part ConAct protocol. Trajectories are evaluated by MemGUI-Eval with step-level reasonableness filtering (75.7% step acceptance rate); 2,956 successful trajectories (57,951 train / 6,479 test SFT samples) remain after filtering. MemGUI-8B-SFT is fine-tuned from Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct using LoRA (rank 8, alpha 32, lr=1e-4, 1 epoch, effective batch size 128 across 8×80GB GPUs, 535 minutes). Key Results On MemGUI-Bench (128 tasks, 26 Android apps, up to 3 attempts): MemGUI-Agent-235B (zero-shot ConAct on Qwen3-VL-235B-Thinking) achieves 37.5% Pass@1 and 62.5% Pass@3, exceeding the best agentic framework M3A (Gemini-2.5-Pro, 35.7% P@1 / 50.0% P@3) and improving +13.3 P@1 / +15.6 P@3 over its own ReAct baseline (24.2% / 46.9%). MemGUI-8B-SFT reaches 23.4% P@1 and 39.6% P@3, the best open-data 8B result, with gains concentrated on hard tasks (41+ steps): +18.5 P@1, +26.3 P@3, +23.1 IRR over the 8B ReAct baseline. On out-of-distribution MobileWorld GUI-Only (117 tasks, single attempt), MemGUI-Agent-235B achieves 29.1% SR (+14.6 over Qwen3-VL-235B baseline), and MemGUI-8B-SFT achieves 17.9% SR (+8.5 over 8B baseline). The ConAct ablation on MemGUI-Bench-40 (zero-shot 235B-Thinking) shows: ReAct baseline 5.0% P@1 / 27.5% P@3; adding UI memory actions alone reaches 17.5% / 42.5%; adding history folding 22.5% / 32.5%; adding self-describing step 25.0% / 40.0%; full ConAct 40.0% / 62.5%. Token efficiency: ConAct saves approximately 1.5k input tokens at step 150 compared to ReAct with substantially flatter growth. Offline skill evaluation on the MemGUI-3K test set (gold context, 6,479 steps) shows memory trigger F1 rising from 19.9% (8B-Inst.) to 48.0% (8B-SFT), deep fold ratio from 8.8% to 26.1%, and format compliance from 94.9% to 99.9%. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The genuinely novel contribution is the ConAct formalism that unifies UI action selection and context management under a single end-to-end policy without external modules, and MemGUI-3K as the first large-scale annotated trajectory dataset for proactive mobile GUI context management (2,956 trajectories, 64,430 steps). Individual components — UI memory, history compression, self-describing outputs — each have precursors in prior work on web agents (AgentFold, MEM1, MemAgent); the contribution is their principled integration into a policy-level action space with a formal state-transition semantics, combined with data construction infrastructure to make the behavior learnable at 8B scale. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations (Table 5) isolate all three ConAct components individually on MemGUI-Bench-40 with 3 attempts per task; results show clear additive gains and complementarity. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported for any result, which is a concern given that some Pass@1 differences across baselines span only 1–3 percentage points. Code is promised at github.com/kwai/MemGUI-Agent, dataset at lgy0404/MemGUI-3K, and model weights at lgy0404/MemGUI-8B-SFT. One methodological concern: 86.8% of MemGUI-3K training tasks are simplified variants of the 128 MemGUI-Bench seed tasks, and the teacher rollout uses Qwen3-VL-235B-Thinking — the same model whose zero-shot ConAct performance is a headline result. The paper reports zero task-ID overlap with the 128 MemGUI-Bench evaluation tasks, but the structural relatedness between task simplifications and their source seed tasks is not analyzed. MemGUI-8B-SFT trains for exactly 1 epoch with a single seed; no variance across training runs is reported. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — an end-to-end GUI agent system evaluated by deployment on a live snapshot-based Android emulator. - Practical: Yes — the 8B SFT variant requires standard 80GB GPU hardware for training; inference runs on Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct which is publicly available, and the 235B variant requires high-memory inference but runs on commercially available clusters. - Efficient: ConAct reduces input tokens by approximately 1.5k at step 150 vs. ReAct due to sub-linear context growth; however, the mandatory 5-part structured output per step adds generation overhead, and the one-action-per-step constraint means memory writes consume a full step rather than being free operations. - Effective: Evidence is strong — MemGUI-Bench gains are large (+13 to +35 P@1 depending on baseline), the failure analysis shows a clean 41% reduction in total failures (99→58) driven by process hallucination (−22) and output hallucination (−17), and out-of-distribution transfer to MobileWorld demonstrates that context-management skills generalize across apps and evaluation protocols. - Scalable: Tested on tasks averaging 36.2 steps (hard tasks 41+); token growth is shown to be substantially flatter than ReAct at 150 steps, but behavior at 200+ steps is not demonstrated and no analysis is provided of cases where aggressive compression causes counter-productive information loss. The work releases software artifacts: trained model weights (MemGUI-8B-SFT), dataset (MemGUI-3K with trajectory-level and SFT formats), and evaluation and training code. Strengths - ConAct's architecture is unusually clean: a single MLLM predicts UI actions and context curation in one forward pass, eliminating the retriever, external memory store, and compression agent found in competing agentic frameworks, which makes the system substantially simpler to deploy and debug. - MemGUI-3K fills a genuine gap — it is the first large-scale annotated dataset for proactive mobile GUI context management, with 64,430 steps averaging 28.8 per trajectory, 1.9× longer than the previous largest mobile GUI SFT dataset (GUIOdyssey at 15.3 steps), and includes fine-grained step-level reasonableness annotations that filter counterproductive training signal. - The failure taxonomy analysis (Figure 7) provides a principled diagnosis: ConAct specifically reduces process hallucination (−42%) and output hallucination (−57%), while knowledge deficiency and intent misunderstanding remain stable — directly supporting the mechanistic claim that context management, not model knowledge, is the bottleneck. - OOD transfer to MobileWorld (+14.6 P@1 for 235B, +8.5 for 8B) with different apps, environment, and evaluation protocol demonstrates that ConAct teaches generalizable context-management behavior rather than MemGUI-Bench-specific pattern matching. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors state in a dedicated Limitations section that experiments focus exclusively on Android-style mobile GUI environments and that extending ConAct to iOS, desktop, and web interfaces remains future work. No additional failure modes, data biases, or evaluation concerns are discussed — the limitations section is notably brief relative to the paper's scope. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 86.8% task-simplification overlap between MemGUI-3K and MemGUI-Bench seed tasks is not analyzed; while zero task-ID overlap is reported, structurally similar tasks in training vs. evaluation creates a potential benchmark proximity effect that the paper does not address or bound. - No significance tests or confidence intervals are reported for any online benchmark result; this matters because several Pass@1 differences between systems are below 3 percentage points, and MemGUI-Bench-40 (used for ablations) contains only 40 tasks with 3 attempts each — a small sample for reliable component attribution. - The ConAct zero-shot gains rely entirely on Qwen3-VL-235B-Thinking, which is also the teacher for MemGUI-3K; no ablation applies ConAct zero-shot to a different strong frontier model (e.g., Gemini-2.5-Pro or a GPT-4o-class model), so it is unclear whether ConAct gains are general or specific to this model family's instruction-following characteristics. - Token savings (~1.5k at step 150) are modest relative to 128k+ context windows of modern MLLMs; the paper does not characterize when span-level folding causes harmful information loss, nor does it report cases where memory writes store incorrect facts later used to produce wrong outputs. - Single-epoch LoRA training with a single seed and no ablation over training data scale makes it impossible to assess how sensitive MemGUI-8B-SFT's performance is to the training recipe. Bottom Line Practitioners building long-horizon GUI or agentic systems will find ConAct directly actionable and MemGUI-3K concretely useful — the formalism is clean, the gains on hard tasks are large and interpretable, and the dataset and model release make the contribution reproducible; this paper should be read by anyone working on context management in multi-step agents.
Recommended 2606.19926 PDF
#20 — Innovative (73)

Noise-resistant discovery of identifier-sound object-centric process models with dynamic identity relations

JN van Detten, P Schumacher, B Knopp, SJJ Leemans · link.springer.com · business process mining or process mining - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces extended object-centric process tree and Petri net formalisms plus a noise-resistant discovery algorithm that automatically constructs identifier-sound process models capturing ten types of dynamic identity relations between business objects from object-centric event logs. - Why it's important: Existing object-centric discovery algorithms either ignore identity relations (producing imprecise models that permit impossible object combinations) or lack soundness guarantees, blocking reliable conformance checking and deviation detection on ERP and CRM logs. - How it does it: It extends the DF²-Miner with noise thresholds over multiplicities, activity counts, and edge frequencies, then checks all language-equivalent candidate trees for the ten identity relation types in a partial-order-driven sequence, wrapping each detected relation in a joint τ-transition construction proven to preserve identifier-soundness by induction. - The good: Identifier-soundness is proven formally by induction across all ten relation types and all translation cases, and the algorithm runs in under 60 seconds on 9 of 10 tested logs (up to 38,528 events, 12 object types) across all 36 parameter combinations. - The missing: No conformance-checking experiment quantifies the precision improvement that is only proven theoretically; object splits and merges were never detected on any evaluated log, leaving that sub-formalism practically unvalidated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for process mining researchers and practitioners who need formally guaranteed sound models for object-centric ERP processes; safely skippable for those working in flat-trace process mining or needing large-scale validated precision numbers.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (Python implementation evaluated on 10 real OCEL 2.0 logs, though the core contribution is a formal one). Novelty (80) is high: the first identifier-sound discovery algorithm for dynamic identity relations, backed by the first systematic ten-type relation taxonomy and an extended Petri-net/process-tree formalism. Non-Obviousness (80) is strong because the joint τ-transition fuse/split construction and the language-equivalent candidate-tree enumeration are subtle correctness steps a competent practitioner would not reach for by default. Rigor (72) reflects a sound inductive soundness proof, public code, and peer review, but is held back by no empirical precision validation, no per-relation ablations, and single-run timings. Completeness (72) and Resilience (70) are pulled down by the central precision-improvement claim being proven only analytically (object-centric conformance is too slow to test) and by object split/merge never being detected on any log, leaving that sub-formalism unvalidated. Impact (60): a real public artifact, but niche and bottlenecked by infeasible downstream conformance checking. Weighted: 0.20*80 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*60 = 73.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Real business processes recorded in ERP and CRM systems involve multiple interacting objects of different types simultaneously. In an order-to-cash process, for example, the exact set of items associated with a sales order during placement should be the same set invoiced, packed, delivered, and collected. These identity-based constraints between object types form dynamically during process execution rather than being fixed in advance. Object-centric process mining must discover models that reflect both the control flow of each object type and these inter-type identity constraints. Without identity relations, discovered models accept behaviors where arbitrary combinations of objects interact — producing analytically useless models. Prior approaches either ignore identity relations entirely, treat them as statically pre-specified (which cannot account for identities first observed mid-execution), or model them without guaranteeing identifier-soundness — the property that the model has no deadlocks, no dead transitions, and that all objects can reach a terminal state. Violation of identifier-soundness blocks the applicability of downstream conformance-checking and deviation-detection techniques. Key Idea The core technical insight is that any dynamic identity relation between two sets of object types T₁ and T₂ can be uniformly encoded in an extended object-centric Petri net as a pair of joint τ-transitions — one that fuses individual object tokens into a combined object-set token (enforcing the coupling), and one that later splits them back — and that this before/during/after construction preserves identifier-soundness by construction if the subnet it wraps is already identifier-sound. By restricting the embedding of identity-relation operators in process trees to exactly the configurations that admit this inductive argument, the extended tree formalism guarantees identifier-soundness for every tree that can be written in the language, trading some modeling flexibility for a structural soundness certificate that does not require runtime verification. Methodology The approach has five layers. First, ten identity relation types are formally defined over object-centric event logs via first-order conditions on event-object involvement sets: strict synchronization (exact same object sets across a group of activities), partitioning and overlapping subset synchronizations (subsets of the strictly synchronized objects used in additional activities), general implication (identities of T₁ determine identities of T₂ but not vice versa), temporally ordered implication (one-to-many unfolding sequentially), temporally concurrent implication (one-to-many unfolding simultaneously), k-batched implication (bounded concurrent capacity), and object split/merge (objects of one type consumed and new objects of another type created). Second, object-centric Petri nets are extended by allowing places to hold object-set tokens and transitions to carry functions mapping input to output token combinations; object-centric process trees gain ten new parameterized identity-relation operator nodes with structural restrictions enforced to ensure the inductive soundness proof closes. The proof proceeds via four lemmas covering identity-relation operator translations, split/merge translations, control-flow operator translations, and leaf node translations, concluding in Theorem 1. Third, the DF²-Miner is extended with a single noise threshold μ₁ ∈ [0,1] applied uniformly to object multiplicity properties, activity node retention, and edge frequency filtering — a deliberate simplification trading parameter richness for practical usability. Fourth, because the DF²-Miner does not guarantee a syntactically normal-form tree, the algorithm uses published language-preserving transformation rules to enumerate all syntactically distinct language-equivalent candidate trees. Fifth, for each candidate tree the algorithm recursively checks all pairs of candidate type sets for identity relations under a second noise threshold μ₂, following the detection order induced by the formal partial-order among relation types (strict synchronization detected before subset synchronizations, which are detected before implications, which are detected before splits/merges). The candidate tree with the most unique identity relations is returned. Fitness equality and precision improvement under μ₂ = 1 are proven analytically by induction on the replay semantics. Key Results Runtime: applied to 10 public OCEL 2.0 logs (1,798 to 38,528 events, 532 to 23,771 objects, 3–12 object types, 9–24 activities) on an Intel Core i9-13900 HX with 64 GB RAM. Across all 36 parameter combinations per log (μ₁, μ₂ ∈ {0.5, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 0.99}), average runtimes ranged from 2.85s (PM4PY Github) to 20.84s (Hinge Production), with a maximum of 126 seconds. All synchronization and implication subtypes were observed across the 10 logs. No object split or merge was detected on any log. Identity relation counts ranged from 2.98 per log (μ₁ = 0.5 strictest retention) to 6.71 (μ₁ = 0.99), consistent with the expectation that retaining more activities creates more detectable relation opportunities. Stricter μ₂ produced fewer but more restrictive relation types. The example analysis on a recruiting log (10, 6,980 events) at μ₁ = 0.9 found a synchronization between applicants and applications at μ₂ = 0.7 indicating roughly 30% of applicants are linked to multiple applications simultaneously, flagging a rework pattern. On an order management log (01, 21,008 events) at μ₁ = 0.9, strict synchronization between items and orders was found during order confirmation and payment at μ₂ = 1.0, but a subset synchronization appeared at lower thresholds, indicating items in the placement step differ from items in fulfillment — an analytically actionable anomaly. No quantitative precision improvement is measured; the formal guarantee is cited in lieu of an experiment. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMALISM. The paper is genuinely novel on three dimensions: (a) the first complete formal taxonomy of ten dynamic identity relation types in object-centric event logs derived from a systematic literature survey rather than invented ad hoc; (b) extended Petri net and process tree formalisms that represent all ten types while guaranteeing identifier-soundness by construction — no prior formalism with automated discovery achieves this; (c) the first discovery algorithm that constructs identifier-sound object-centric process models with dynamic identity relations from noisy logs. Individual building blocks (DF²-Miner, object-centric process trees, the extended Petri net token model) are prior work by the same group; the integration, soundness proof, and noise handling are new. Rigor & Reproducibility Theorem 1 is proven by induction with four supporting lemmas; the proofs are structural and presented in sufficient detail within the paper. Fitness and precision guarantees are analytic, not empirical — no experiment measures actual precision improvement because object-centric conformance checking is currently too slow on large object sets (explicitly acknowledged). No ablations isolate the contribution of individual identity relation types. Runtimes are single-run measurements with no error bars or repeated trials. Code and all object-centric logs are publicly available at github.com/Nik314/Dynamic-Identity-Relations; source logs come from the OCEL 2.0 public library (ocel-standard.org). The paper is peer-reviewed (received December 2025, accepted June 2026, published Process Science 2026). A methodological concern: the detection order among relation types is asserted to be the uniquely reasonable ordering based on formal partial-order relationships, but no experiment tests alternative orderings on real data. The failure to detect object split/merge in any log is noted but not resolved — the authors cannot distinguish between structural over-restriction and genuine absence. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS — a Python implementation is evaluated on real logs. - Practical: Runs on a commodity desktop (Intel i9, 64 GB RAM); publicly available Python implementation; input format is OCEL 2.0, an adopted standard with library support. - Efficient: Median runtime under 20 seconds across all tested configurations. Maximum 126 seconds. No asymptotic complexity analysis; runtime correlates empirically with activity-to-object-type connectivity density. - Effective: The algorithm consistently detects 3–7 identity relations per log and the example analyses generate actionable hypotheses about rework and anomalies. Formal fitness preservation is proven; precision improvement is claimed but not measured. - Scalable: Tested up to 38,528 events and 12 object types, which is moderate for process mining. No experiment at 10× scale; no analysis of how runtime grows with log size, object type count, or activity count beyond the empirical scatter. The work delivers a Python software artifact and a public GitHub repository. No trained model or dataset is contributed beyond re-packaging OCEL 2.0 public logs. Strengths - The identifier-soundness guarantee by construction is a hard formal result proven by induction, not an empirical claim. It is the first such guarantee for a discovery algorithm covering dynamic identity relations, which makes this work directly useful for any downstream analysis that requires sound models. - The taxonomy of ten relation types is the most systematic coverage of the design space to date, grounded in a comparative literature survey (Table 1) that maps prior formalisms to relation types — making the paper a useful reference even independent of the algorithm. - The noise-resistant DF²-Miner extension addresses a practical necessity for ERP data: three qualitatively different noise sources (multiplicity, activity count, edge frequency) are handled without requiring separate parameterization per dimension. - The language-equivalent candidate tree enumeration is a non-obvious step that prevents the syntactic structure of the initial DF²-Miner output from artificially limiting which identity relations can be detected — this is a subtle but important correctness contribution. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The structural restrictions of extended object-centric process trees may be too strict, potentially preventing detection of identity relations that are genuinely present but incompatible with the allowed embedding structure; the failure to detect any object splits or merges in all 10 evaluated logs is directly attributed to this as a plausible cause. Conformance checking to quantify actual precision improvement is computationally infeasible on object-centric logs with large object sets using current methods, so the precision improvement proof cannot be validated experimentally. Future work is identified as relieving structural restrictions while preserving identifier-soundness and developing efficient conformance checking for identity relations. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The evaluation uses exclusively public OCEL 2.0 benchmark logs, which are small to moderate (max 38,528 events, 12 object types) and may not reflect production ERP log complexity. No experiment on a real SAP or Oracle production log is included, so generalizability to logs with hundreds of object types or millions of events is uncharacterized. - A single threshold μ₁ governing three qualitatively different noise phenomena (multiplicities, node frequency, edge frequency) will over-filter in some dimensions while under-filtering in others on any real log. No experiment teases apart which noise dimension drives the observed identity-relation count sensitivity. - No head-to-head comparison against any alternative approach (OC-DECLARE, typed Jackson nets, synchronization-sensitive OCPNs) on the same logs, even informally. The related work table (Table 1) establishes positioning but without experimental corroboration. - Runtime is reported as single measurements without variance; on the logs that reached 126 seconds, it is unclear whether this is deterministic or variable, and the scaling behavior with log size is not characterized beyond observing a correlation with activity-type connectivity. - The partial-order-driven detection priority is justified formally but not empirically: it is possible that on some logs a different priority order yields more informative models (e.g., detecting a coarser implication rather than a stricter synchronization that absorbs it). This is unexamined. Bottom Line A process mining researcher working on object-centric analysis of ERP or CRM logs gains the first formally guaranteed identifier-sound discovery algorithm with dynamic identity relations, backed by a public implementation — but should note that claimed precision improvements are unquantified experimentally and the evaluation scale is modest.
Search Results
#21 — Innovative (73)

Automated Graph Machine Learning

XIN WANG, H LI, H CHEN, Z ZHANG, W ZHU · mn.cs.tsinghua.edu.cn · karypis - new results
