TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AutoGL, the first dedicated open-source library for automated graph machine learning, paired with NAS-Bench-Graph, a pre-computed benchmark covering 26,206 GNN architectures evaluated on nine datasets, and a systematic survey of hyperparameter optimization (HPO) and neural architecture search (NAS) methods for graph neural networks. - Why it's important: Over 100 general-purpose GNN architectures were published in 2021 alone, making manual hyperparameter and architecture selection infeasible at scale, and no reproducible benchmark previously existed for comparing graph NAS algorithms. - How it does it: AutoGL pipelines five modules (auto feature engineering, NAS, model training, HPO, auto ensemble) on top of PyTorch Geometric, while NAS-Bench-Graph exhaustively pre-trains all 26,206 architectures into look-up tables that enable cheap surrogate comparisons without re-training. - The good: The ablation study cleanly isolates HPO as the dominant AutoGL module — removing it drops ogbn-arXiv node classification accuracy by more than 30 percentage points — and NAS-Bench-Graph applies Wilcoxon signed-rank tests across all method comparisons, a level of statistical rigor above the norm for this subfield. - The missing: AutoGL baselines (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE) are evaluated without their own HPO runs, so the pipeline's apparent accuracy gains are partly an apples-to-oranges comparison; billion-scale graph evaluation is absent; and several recently surveyed GNAS methods (CARNAS, GASIM, MTGC3, BiGNAS) are explicitly listed as unsupported in the library. - Read it or not: Practitioners building GNN AutoML pipelines and researchers benchmarking graph NAS algorithms should read this; researchers focused on OOD generalization, billion-node graphs, or the most recent GNAS methods will find the experimental scope and library coverage outdated relative to the manuscript's own survey.
Why this rank: Systems profile (the contribution is a released library plus a pre-computed benchmark). Impact (80) dominates under systems weighting: AutoGL is the first dedicated graph-AutoML library and NAS-Bench-Graph is ~8,000 GPU-hours of exhaustively pre-trained LUTs (26,206 architectures x 9 datasets) that turn graph-NAS comparison into cheap table look-ups, with Docker reproducibility scripts, released code, and data. Rigor (75) is well above the subfield norm: Wilcoxon signed-rank tests with p-values, 3-5 seeds with standard deviations, a clean four-condition ablation isolating HPO as the dominant module, and auxiliary experiments that test the benchmark's own anchor-bias and single-input-edge limitations; it is peer-reviewed (J. ACM 2026), not a preprint. Completeness (72) and Resilience (70) benefit from an explicit four-item limitations section confronted honestly rather than minimized. The ceiling is set by the unfair baseline comparison (GNN baselines run without HPO, so the 'AutoGL advantage' partly measures HPO-vs-no-HPO) and the five-year gap between the v0.2.0-pre library and publication; Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (60) reflect that the value is synthesis and infrastructure, not new algorithms. Weighted: 0.15*70 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*60 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*70 + 0.30*80 = 73.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Graph machine learning tasks require choosing both hyperparameters (dropout rate, learning rate, layer count, weight decay) and architectural decisions (aggregation functions, connectivity patterns, pooling methods) that are highly sensitive to dataset topology. A model performing well on one graph dataset regularly degrades on another — the paper demonstrates this concretely: hyperparameters tuned on Coauthor-CS reduce GCN accuracy from 76.6% to 66.6% on Amazon-Computers. The existing literature compounds this by producing over 100 new general-purpose GNN architectures per year, making manual selection prohibitively expensive. Existing AutoML libraries (AutoKeras, AutoGluon, TPOT) were designed for tabular and image data and cannot be directly applied to graphs due to non-Euclidean structure, GNN-specific building blocks, and the interconnected nature of graph nodes. Existing graph libraries (PyTorch Geometric, DGL, NetworkX) offer no AutoML support. No unified, reproducible benchmark existed for comparing graph NAS algorithms, making published results unreliable across labs and settings. Key Idea The paper's organizing insight is that HPO and NAS for graphs require three graph-specific adaptations over their standard counterparts: (1) scalability adaptations — subgraph sampling or graph coarsening to proxy large graphs during optimization (as in AutoNE and JITuNE); (2) search space adaptations — replacing CNN-style micro/macro spaces with GNN-specific components (aggregation functions such as SUM/MEAN/MAX, attention weights, pooling methods, and cross-layer connectivity); and (3) performance estimation adaptations — restricting weight-sharing parameter inheritance to architectures sharing weight shapes, attention functions, and activation functions, because the interpretation of shared weights in GNNs differs from CNNs. AutoGL integrates these adaptations into a modular pipeline; NAS-Bench-Graph operationalizes fair evaluation by pre-computing all architecture evaluations so algorithm comparisons reduce to look-up table queries. Methodology The paper delivers three artifacts: a structured survey, the AutoGL library, and the NAS-Bench-Graph benchmark. The survey covers HPO methods organized around the scalability challenge: AutoNE (subgraph sampling + Bayesian optimization transfer), e-AutoGR (explainable graph features + HyperDeco decorrelation), JITuNE (graph coarsening as hierarchical synopsis), HESGA (hierarchical early-stopping evolutionary algorithm), HyperU-GCN (uncertainty-aware bilevel HPO via variational hypernetworks), KGTuner (two-stage HP search transferring subgraph configurations to full knowledge graphs), and others. NAS coverage spans 50+ methods organized across three axes: search space (micro — aggregation function, attention weights, activation, dimensionality; macro — skip connections, dense connections; pooling — row/column-wise, hierarchical, attention sum), search strategy (RL controller + RNN, differentiable/DARTS-style, evolutionary), and performance estimation (low-fidelity/early stopping, weight sharing, one-shot). Recent featured works cover architecture-structure co-learning (GASSO), robustness and OOD generalization (G-RNA, GRACES, OMG-NAS, DCGAS, CARNAS, GASIM), large-scale NAS (GAUSS, PasCa), heterogeneous graphs (PMMM, DHGAS), graph transformers (AutoGT), foundation model NAS (AutoGFM), multi-task NAS (MTGC3), cross-domain recommendation (BiGNAS), hardware co-design (G-Cos, FGNAS, LPGNAS). A unifying invariance taxonomy (Section 4.4.9) classifies these into structural, distributional, resource, and modality invariance. AutoGL is built on PyTorch Geometric v2.0 and implements five modules: (1) Auto Feature Engineering with 24 operations (graphlets, EigenGNN, PageRank, local degree profile, one-hot degrees) organized as generators/selectors/graph features; (2) NAS wrapping GASSO, G-RNA, random search, RL, and EA via a BaseNAS class; (3) Model Training supporting GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, GIN, and Top-K Pooling via BaseModel/BaseTrainer; (4) HPO supporting AutoNE, e-AutoGR, random search, and TPE via BaseHPOptimizer; (5) Auto Ensemble with voting and stacking (GLM/GBM meta-models). An AutoGL Solver API integrates all five modules sequentially. NAS-Bench-Graph defines a DAG-based macro search space where the computational graph has 6 nodes, single-input edges only (9 topological configurations), and 7 GNN operation types (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, GIN, ChebNet, ARMA, k-GNN), yielding 26,206 unique architectures after accounting for isomorphisms. Hyperparameters (pre/post layers, hidden dimension, dropout, optimizer, LR, weight decay, epochs) are fixed per dataset by optimizing over 30 anchor architectures with random search. All 26,206 architectures are exhaustively evaluated on 9 datasets (Cora through ogbn-proteins) with 3 random seeds each, recording accuracy, latency, and parameter count per epoch. Total compute cost: approximately 8,000 GPU hours. Three design decisions stand out: the fixed single-hyperparameter-per-dataset regime in NAS-Bench-Graph is well-justified for tractability (billions of (arch, HP) pairs otherwise), but explicitly introduces anchor bias; the single-input-edge DAG constraint is justified by empirical evidence that multi-branch patterns are less prevalent in GNNs than CNNs; and the library baseline comparisons do not apply HPO to the individual GNN baselines, which limits the interpretability of AutoGL's accuracy gains. Key Results AutoGL node classification (Table 6): 83.2±0.6% on Cora (vs. GCN 80.9±0.7%, GAT 82.3±0.7%, GraphSAGE 74.5±1.8%), 72.4±0.6% on CiteSeer, 79.3±0.4% on PubMed, 54.5±0.4% on ogbn-arXiv. AutoGL link prediction AUC (Table 7): 94.2±0.3% on Cora, 97.5±0.1% on PubMed, 96.4±0.4% on ogbn-arXiv — consistently above all baselines. AutoGL graph classification (Table 8): 87.6±6.0% on MUTAG (vs. Top-K Pooling 80.8±7.1% and GIN 82.7±6.9%), 73.3±4.4% on PROTEINS, 72.1±5.0% on IMDB-B. The ablation (Table 17) shows removing HPO drops ogbn-arXiv from 54.5% to 22.3% — the dominant effect; removing ensemble or feature engineering causes only minor drops (≤1.3% on Cora). NAS-Bench-Graph results (Table 20, 2% architecture budget): GNAS achieves 82.04% on Cora; all NAS algorithms outperform the top-5% baseline (80.63%). Random Search wins on 2 of 9 datasets outright and remains competitive across all. RL fails to complete within 24 hours on 8 of 9 datasets at 5% and 10% budgets, while Random Search and EA complete all datasets within 2.2 hours at 10% budget. Wilcoxon signed-rank tests (Table 22): Random Search significantly outperforms RL at 1% budget (p=0.0143) and EA at 5% (p=0.0266) and 10% (p=0.0109); EA shows no significant gain from larger budgets (p>0.05 for all budget comparisons), suggesting premature convergence. A notable finding: the HPO non-transferability experiment (Table 15) shows hyperparameters tuned on Coauthor-CS reduce GCN from 76.6% to 66.6% on Amazon-Computers — confirming the dataset-specificity motivating the whole work. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (AutoGL library), NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (NAS-Bench-Graph), SURVEY. AutoGL is the first dedicated open-source library unifying HPO, NAS, feature engineering, model training, and ensemble for graph tasks in a single framework — that is genuine. NAS-Bench-Graph is the first NAS benchmark specifically for GNNs at this scale and coverage. The survey's "invariance" organizing taxonomy (Section 4.4.9) is a new conceptual contribution. The individual HPO and NAS methods reviewed are not novel to this paper; the survey's value is synthesis and organization, not new algorithms. Rigor & Reproducibility AutoGL code is publicly available at https://github.com/THUMNLab/AutoGL/ (v0.2.0-pre, released July 2021; the paper notes users should visit the website for the most up-to-date version). NAS-Bench-Graph data is at https://figshare.com/articles/dataset/NAS-bench-Graph/20070371 and code at https://github.com/THUMNLab/NAS-Bench-Graph, with reproducibility scripts including Docker container instructions. All NAS-Bench-Graph experiments use 3 seeds; AutoGL experiments use 5 runs with standard deviations reported. Wilcoxon signed-rank tests with p-values and win/tie/loss counts are applied to NAS comparisons (Table 22) — above-average statistical rigor for this subfield. The four-condition AutoGL ablation (Table 17) cleanly isolates module contributions. The paper explicitly acknowledges and tests two benchmark limitations: anchor bias (via auxiliary LUT slice experiments, Table 24) and the single-input-edge constraint (via reduced operator space experiments), finding conclusions are largely robust to both. The main concern is that AutoGL baselines (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, GATv2, GraphConv) are run without HPO, so the pipeline's accuracy advantage conflates AutoML benefits with the absence of tuning in the comparison group. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: AutoGL runs on commercially available GPUs (NVIDIA V100 16 GB and RTX 3090 24 GB tested); citation-scale tasks require only 140–420 MB; ogbn-arXiv link prediction requires 75 GB, necessitating high-memory server hardware. - Efficient: Training overhead is approximately 1.2× on small graphs (13.6s vs. 11.8s GCN on Cora) and memory overhead is 1.8–4.4× depending on task; the authors recommend HPO budgets of 10–20 trials for large graphs to contain cost, and early stopping (patience 5–10 epochs) to reduce per-trial expense. - Effective: AutoGL consistently outperforms all tested individual GNN baselines on node, link, and graph classification; however, baselines are not HPO-tuned, and the ablation shows ensemble and feature engineering contribute minimally on standard benchmarks, so effectiveness on real-world heterogeneous or OOD graphs is not established. - Scalable: Tested up to ogbn-arXiv (169,343 nodes, 1.2M edges) and ogbn-proteins (132,534 nodes, 39.6M edges); billion-node graphs are explicitly identified as an unsolved open problem for both HPO and NAS in AutoGL. The paper delivers AutoGL as a working Python library, NAS-Bench-Graph as a pre-computed LUT dataset, and reproducibility scripts for both. Strengths - NAS-Bench-Graph is a genuine infrastructure contribution: exhaustive pre-training of 26,206 architectures across 9 datasets at approximately 8,000 GPU hours, with Wilcoxon statistical testing to compare NAS algorithms — the first benchmark of its kind for graph NAS, directly enabling reproducible comparison work. - The ablation study (Table 17) is specific and honest: it isolates HPO as the dominant module, identifies the 30+ percentage point drop on ogbn-arXiv without HPO, and correctly notes that ensemble and feature engineering provide only marginal gains on standard citation benchmarks — this level of self-critical analysis is unusual for a system paper. - The "invariance" organizing principle (Section 4.4.9) provides a coherent conceptual framework linking structural robustness, OOD generalization, resource awareness, and heterogeneity into a single taxonomy, which genuinely clarifies the research landscape rather than just cataloging methods. - The paper honestly confronts both benchmark limitations (anchor bias, single-input-edge DAG) with follow-up experiments (LUT slice analysis, auxiliary reduced-space experiments, Table 24) rather than dismissing them. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify four limitations in Section 9: (1) scalability — both HPO and NAS incur significant computational cost for billion-scale graphs; (2) interpretability — AutoML results are black-box, with NAS explainability essentially unexplored; (3) OOD generalization — existing HPO and NAS configurations do not transfer well across datasets or distributions; (4) evaluation scope — experiments rely on widely used but small citation-network benchmarks that may not reflect real-world graph complexity. Within the benchmark section, the authors additionally acknowledge anchor bias in hyperparameter selection (Section 6.3.3), the single-input-edge constraint limiting multi-branch architectures, and hardware heterogeneity across datasets. The authors confront these directly and without minimization — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The AutoGL accuracy comparisons (Tables 6–8) pit the full AutoGL pipeline against individual GNN models run without HPO. The ablation shows HPO dominates; this means the apparent AutoGL advantage largely measures "HPO vs. no HPO," not the value of the full system. A fair comparison would apply TPE independently to each baseline GNN with the same trial budget. - Several of the most recent GNAS methods highlighted in the Section 4.4 featured-works survey (CARNAS, GASIM, MTGC3, BiGNAS, G-Cos, FGNAS) are explicitly listed as "Unsupported" in AutoGL (Table 3). A paper marketing AutoGL as the world's first dedicated automated graph ML library while not yet integrating a third of the methods it surveys as important creates a gap between library claims and coverage. - NAS-Bench-Graph fixes a single hyperparameter configuration per dataset for all 26,206 architectures. Architectures that are particularly sensitive to hyperparameter choice — or that require different settings than those favored by the 30 anchor models — will be systematically underranked, a form of evaluation bias not fully resolved by the LUT slice experiments. - The survey's coverage of HPO for knowledge graph embedding (KGTuner, Lloyd et al.) and graph NAS for spatio-temporal graphs is cursory relative to the attention given to citation-graph NAS; practitioners in these domains get thin guidance. - AutoGL v0.2.0-pre was released in July 2021; the paper appears in J. ACM 2026. The five-year gap between library version and publication date raises questions about whether the library's current state matches the paper's descriptions, particularly for the DGL backend and PyG 2.0 compatibility claimed in Section 5.5. Bottom Line Researchers who need a reproducible NAS benchmark for GNNs or a unified AutoML starting point for graph tasks gain concrete, usable artifacts from this work, but practitioners should verify AutoGL's current library state against the v0.2.0-pre baseline described in the paper and should not treat the accuracy gains over baselines as evidence that the full pipeline delivers value beyond well-tuned HPO alone.
Search Results
#22 — Innovative (73)

DuET: Dual-View Tensor-to-Topology Spectral Adapter for Enhancing Sparse Tensor Factorization

JG Jang, J He, AJ Margenot, H Tong · jungijang.github.io · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DuET, a model-agnostic plug-in adapter that stabilizes sparse tensor factorization by injecting dual-view topology-derived spectral priors into latent factor matrices via learned mode-wise gated residual refinement, without modifying the underlying TF predictor. - Why it's important: Real-world tensors can have over 99.99% unobserved entries (e.g., 0.006% density in Gowalla), causing infrequent entities to receive noisy, high-variance latent factors that degrade generalization; no existing TF method has a principled mechanism to correct latent factor unreliability independently of the scoring architecture. - How it does it: DuET constructs two complementary topological views directly from observed tuples — a tuple-induced hypergraph (incidence view, capturing cross-mode N-way dependencies) and per-mode co-occurrence matrices from tensor unfoldings (marginal view, capturing intra-mode proximity) — denoises each via implicit low-rank spectral filtering, and injects the result into TF embeddings through a two-stage learnable gated residual operator proven to be non-expansive in Frobenius norm. - The good: DuET improves NDCG@10 by 20.45% to 59.51% on average across six TF backbones on five retrieval datasets, is compatible with 5–6 of 6 backbones in most experimental settings, and Proposition 3 provides a formal non-expansiveness guarantee under repeated refinement. - The missing: All evaluated tensors have at most four modes and moderate entity counts (max ~70,000 per mode in Yelp); scalability to order-5+ tensors or very large entity sets is claimed but not empirically tested, and no per-metric standard deviations are reported despite five independent runs. - Read it or not: Practitioners building sparse tensor factorization pipelines for recommendation, check-in data, or biomedical interaction networks should read this; researchers focused on knowledge graph completion or temporal TF should skim, as t-SVD-based and temporal-specific models are explicitly excluded from DuET's scope.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (75) leads: 9 datasets, 6 TF backbones, two tasks, and ablations isolating dual-view vs single-view, spectral vs non-spectral, and adaptive vs fixed gating, with code and datasets released. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (75) are solid — the tuple-only dual-view topology induction plus a formal Frobenius non-expansiveness guarantee (Proposition 3) is a real assembly, though individual components are known. Resilience (72) is tempered by no standard deviations across five runs (small completion gains may be noise) and a spectral rank q=32 tuned on one dataset then applied uniformly. Completeness (75) benefits from honest failure-case reporting (CP on FS-NYC, M²DMTF on NIPS). Practical Impact (65) reflects a genuine drop-in adapter in a narrower TF niche. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*65 = 73.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Tensor factorization (TF) is the standard tool for analyzing multi-relational data such as check-in logs, interaction networks, and biomedical association matrices, all representable as sparse N-way tensors. Under extreme sparsity — FS-TKY has 0.0063% density, Gowalla 0.0059%, FS-NYC 0.0077% — many entities appear in only a handful of observed tuples. TF methods update each entity's latent vector only when that entity appears in a training tuple, so infrequent entities receive noisy, undertrained representations. This high-variance noise propagates through shared latent spaces and systematically degrades predictions on unobserved entries. Prior TF work has addressed this by improving the predictor (scoring functions, expressive decompositions, attention) rather than by stabilizing the latent representations themselves, leaving no mechanism to correct for representation unreliability caused by structural imbalance in the observation set. Key Idea The core insight is that observed tuples, even under extreme sparsity, implicitly encode relational topology sufficient to construct useful structural priors — no auxiliary side information is required. By treating the observed tuple set simultaneously as a tuple-induced hypergraph (where each tuple is a hyperedge connecting all participating entities) and as a collection of per-mode co-occurrence matrices (obtained by unfolding the tensor), DuET derives two complementary structural views from the same sparse data. Denoising these views via low-rank spectral filtering before injecting them into latent factors allows the model to exploit smooth structural regularities without materializing large adjacency matrices or relying on external relational data. Methodology DuET operates as a preprocessing adapter inserted between the TF embedding lookup and the TF predictor, with three algorithmic stages. Dual-View Topology Induction. For the incidence view, DuET constructs a hypergraph G_H = (V, E) where |V| = sum of all entity counts and |E| = |Omega_o| (observed tuples). The incidence matrix B ∈ {0,1}^{|V|×|E|} encodes entity participation; construction costs O(N|Omega_o|). A clique-expanded adjacency A = BB^T − Diag(diag(BB^T)) provides pairwise co-occurrence counts. For the marginal view, each mode n produces a binary unfolding X_{(n)} ∈ {0,1}^{I_n × J_n} where J_n = product of all other mode sizes; S^{(n)} = X_{(n)}X_{(n)}^T − Diag(diag(X_{(n)}X_{(n)}^T)) captures intra-mode co-occurrence similarity at O(sum|Omega_o|·d_n) time. Per-View Spectral Denoising. Rather than materializing G(W) = WW^T − Diag(diag(WW^T)) explicitly (which is O(n²) space), DuET computes only the top-q eigenpairs of the symmetric normalized operator M(W) = D^{-1/2}G(W)D^{-1/2} using an iterative eigensolver with sparse matrix-vector products. The monotone spectral filter g(λ) = (1+λ)/2 maps eigenvalues from [-1,1] to [0,1], down-weighting high-frequency noisy components while preserving dominant smooth signals. This yields denoised propagation operators P(W) = U_q g(Λ_q) U_q^T applied implicitly as P(W)V = U_q(g(Λ_q)(U_q^T V)), costing O(qK·sum I_n) memory and O(T·nnz(W)) per eigensolver run. Two-Stage Gated Refinement. Stage 1 applies incidence-view fusion: Z = (I − Φ_τ)V + Φ_τ^{1/2} F_{inc} Φ_τ^{1/2} V, where Φ_τ = blkdiag(τ_1 I_{I_1}, …, τ_N I_{I_N}) and τ_n ∈ (0,1) are learnable scalars per mode. The symmetric gating form Φ^{1/2}FΦ^{1/2} is a deliberate design choice to preserve non-expansiveness (justified in Proposition 3). Stage 2 applies marginal-view residual mixing: Y = Z + Φ_ρ(F_{marg}Z − Z), where Φ_ρ = blkdiag(ρ_1 I, …, ρ_N I) and ρ_n ∈ (0,1) are mode-wise gates. Both stages are iterated L times. Three critical design decisions with justification: (1) per-view spectral denoising rather than naive graph propagation, justified by Table 4 showing DuET outperforms GAT, ChebNet, APPNP, and LEConv on retrieval and completion; (2) dual-view over single-view, justified by Table 6 ablation where dual-view achieves best performance in both retrieval and completion; (3) adaptive mode-wise gates over fixed fusion coefficients, justified by Table 8 where adaptive gating consistently helps retrieval. Proposition 3 proves the composite two-stage operator T: V → Y is non-expansive in Frobenius norm, so the Frobenius ball Ω_C = {V : ||V||_F ≤ C} is invariant under T. Key Results Retrieval task (NDCG@10 across FS-TKY, FS-NYC, Gowalla, PPT-Ohmnet, TFG-Ohmnet): DuET improves performance in 5–6 of 6 backbones per dataset, with average relative gains of 20.45%–59.51% depending on backbone and dataset combination. The largest reported single gain is 52.1% NDCG@10 improvement on FS-TKY. CostCo + DuET achieves the top NDCG@10 on four of five retrieval datasets. Completion task (RMSE on ML-100k, Yelp, Bitcoin, NIPS): DuET reduces error in 58 of 72 cases (80.6%). Notable examples: CP RMSE on ML-100k drops from 1.387 to 0.979 (29.4%); Tucker RMSE on ML-100k drops from 1.148 to 0.978 (14.8%); M²DMTF RMSE on ML-100k drops from 1.783 to 1.701 (4.6%). Comparative study (Table 4): DuET outperforms GNN-based propagation (GAT, ChebNet, APPNP, LEConv) and graph regularization baselines on FS-NYC and TFG for retrieval, and on ML-100k and Yelp for completion. DuET also outperforms hypergraph neural networks (HyperConv, HGNN, HNHN) on most datasets, with the exception that HGNN matches DuET within 1% on Yelp RMSE. Failure cases: CP + DuET on FS-NYC NDCG@10 slightly degrades; M²DMTF + DuET occasionally increases RMSE on NIPS. Retrieval improvements (20–60%) are systematically larger than completion improvements (1–16%), a discrepancy the authors attribute to ranking benefiting more from topology-driven embedding smoothing than regression does. All baselines are current TF methods from 2020–2024; the evaluation uses five independent runs with averaged results. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The combination of tuple-only dual-view topology induction, implicit per-view low-rank spectral denoising, and non-expansive mode-wise gated residual injection as a model-agnostic TF adapter is not previously established. Individual components (spectral graph filtering, GNN-based TF regularization, hypergraph representation of tensors) are known, but their assembly into a theoretically stable, auxiliary-data-free plug-in with a formal Frobenius non-expansiveness guarantee is a genuine contribution. The marginal-view topology induction from tensor unfoldings is standard in the TF literature; the incidence-view hypergraph construction for structural denoising and its combination with mode-wise adaptive gating is the substantively new element. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and meaningful: Table 6 tests incidence-only versus marginal-only versus dual-view; Table 7 tests spectral versus non-spectral propagation; Table 8 tests fixed versus adaptive gating; Section 4.4 analyzes learned gate values across settings. Each method is evaluated over five independent runs. However, no standard deviations or confidence intervals appear in Tables 2–3, making it impossible to assess whether small gains (e.g., 1–2% RMSE improvements) are statistically reliable. Code and datasets are released at https://github.com/jungijang/DuET. Hyperparameters are reported in Appendix C.2 with a grid search. A mild Goodhart concern: the spectral rank q = 32 is selected based on FS-NYC sensitivity analysis and then applied uniformly to all datasets, which may overfit the topology to the best-performing dataset without accounting for dataset-specific sparsity structure. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: DuET runs on commodity GPU hardware using PyTorch and Adam; all nine datasets fit on a single GPU with the reported embedding sizes (r ∈ {10, 20, 30}). - Efficient: Training overhead is marginal for retrieval tasks (Figure 5); inference overhead for completion is sub-millisecond (<0.004 seconds), making the adapter latency negligible relative to baseline inference time; memory scales O(nnz(W) + Nq·sum I_n), linear in entity count. - Effective: DuET demonstrably improves NDCG@10 on 5–6/6 backbones per dataset in most retrieval settings and reduces RMSE in 80% of completion cases; however, completion gains are modest and occasionally reversed for complex nonlinear models (M²DMTF, NIPS dataset). - Scalable: The theoretical complexity is O(L·V_total·q·K) per Proposition 2, claimed to be tractable for large tensors. The largest evaluated tensor has ~3.1M observed interactions (NIPS) and ~82K entities in one mode; no experiments test tensors with millions of entities per mode or order N > 4, so the scalability claim rests on asymptotic analysis rather than empirical validation. The paper delivers a software artifact at https://github.com/jungijang/DuET. Strengths - The non-expansiveness proof (Proposition 3 with supporting Lemmas 1 and 2 in Appendix B) is a genuine theoretical contribution that distinguishes DuET from ad-hoc GNN regularization; the Frobenius ball invariance result provides a formal stability guarantee lacking in all compared baselines. - Implicit spectral denoising via matrix-vector products avoids O(n²) adjacency materialization, making the approach computationally practical even for tensors where explicit graph construction is prohibitive. - The evaluation scope — 9 datasets, 6 TF backbones, two tasks, plus GNN propagation and hypergraph neural network comparisons — provides credible evidence of compatibility and generality, not just cherry-picked best cases. - The gate analysis (Section 4.4) reveals interpretable mode-specific behavior: τ_n gates remain balanced across modes (controlling inter-mode coupling), while ρ_n gates take larger values for temporal modes and smaller for entity modes, showing DuET exploits temporal locality while suppressing noisy entity co-occurrences — evidence of genuine learned adaptation rather than uniform smoothing. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge three limitations: (1) CP + DuET degrades on FS-NYC, attributed to structural mismatch between CP's low-rank multilinear structure and strongly correlated topology-induced priors from that dataset; (2) sigmoid gate parameterization leaves near-zero leakage signal even when gates approach zero, which slightly interferes with the CP baseline in low-rank settings; (3) dynamic and streaming tensor settings, where structural priors must update continuously as new interactions arrive, are outside the current scope. The authors confront these limitations rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. They also note completion improvements are more modest and less consistent than retrieval improvements, explicitly analyzing the mechanism behind this discrepancy. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No standard deviations or significance tests are reported across five runs, making it impossible to determine whether small completion gains (1–3% RMSE) are statistically reliable or within run-to-run noise. - The spectral rank q = 32 is selected on a single dataset (FS-NYC retrieval) and applied uniformly; the sensitivity study does not explore interactions between q and tensor density, suggesting this hyperparameter may be undertuned or overtuned on other datasets. - Evaluation is restricted to tensors with N ≤ 4 modes and at most ~70,000 entities per mode; the approach's behavior on higher-order tensors (N ≥ 5) or entity sets with millions of members is not characterized. - The paper does not compare against regularization methods that inject structural priors directly into the TF loss function (e.g., tensor ring decomposition with graph Laplacian penalties, or methods with explicit manifold regularization), which represent a direct alternative to the post-embedding adapter approach. - The "model-agnostic" claim is qualified but not prominently scoped: t-SVD-based methods and temporal-specific TF methods are excluded, and the adapter is tested only on mode-wise factor methods; practitioners using frontal-slice or temporal decompositions receive no guidance. - The failure analysis for M²DMTF on NIPS (completion error increases) attributes the degradation to mode-wise MLP structure without providing supporting evidence or ablation specific to that case. Bottom Line Practitioners running sparse tensor factorization for recommendation or biomedical interaction analysis gain a concrete, theoretically grounded drop-in adapter with strong and consistent retrieval improvements, making this paper worth reading for that audience; the completion gains are real but modest, and the scalability claims require independent validation before trusting DuET on very large tensors.
Citations
#23 — Innovative (72)

CEO-Bench: Can Agents Play the Long Game?

H Chen, K Narasimhan, Z Liu · arxiv.org · generating environments for agent testing in enterprises - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CEO-BENCH, a benchmark that evaluates long-horizon LLM agent capabilities by simulating 500 days of operating a fictional SaaS startup through a programmable Python interface with a 19-table business database, 34 tools, and a mechanistic non-stationary market simulator. - Why it's important: Existing agent benchmarks measure short-horizon tasks with clear goals and rapid feedback; they leave entirely untested the sustained strategic planning, noisy indirect information gathering, and competitive adaptation that characterize consequential real-world decisions. - How it does it: Customer behavior, reputation propagation, competitor dynamics, and macroeconomic cycles are all governed by explicit mathematical rules (participation curves, Ornstein-Uhlenbeck PMI process, log-normal competitor shocks) rather than LLM judges, and agents are scored purely on final cash balance — making outcomes unambiguous and ungameable through verbal promises. - The good: Only 2 of 10 evaluated frontier models (Claude Opus 4.8 at $27.8M and GPT-5.5 at $21.3M best-run cash) finish above the $1M starting balance, while a simple rule-based heuristic achieves $15.76M, demonstrating a genuine and measurable capability gap across the current frontier. - The missing: Each model is evaluated with only 3 runs under fixed random seed 42 — standard deviations reach ±136 days survival for some models — and no human performance baseline is established, so the absolute difficulty of the task relative to skilled human judgment remains unanchored. - Read it or not: Essential for researchers building or evaluating long-horizon agents; the behavioral breakdown (forecast accuracy, ad-allocation efficiency, competitor detection lag) gives actionable diagnostic signal that headline benchmarks do not.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (78) is high — a 500-day fully-specified mechanistic simulator (49 equations) with partial observability and adaptive competitors is a genuinely new long-horizon evaluation environment that exposes capability gaps invisible to short-horizon benchmarks. Practical Impact (80) is strong: released code plus trajectory viewer, feasible multi-run cost ($0.75-$213/run), and tunable difficulty. Non-Obviousness (70) and Completeness (75) are solid — the harness-confound finding (Claude Code suppresses agentic behavior) and behavioral sub-skill decomposition are useful, and limitations are stated credibly. Rigor (55) is the main drag: only 3 runs at fixed seed 42, headline best-run rather than mean cash, no significance tests, no human baseline, and a best-of-24 grid-search baseline overfit to the same seed. Resilience (65) is moderate given the variance (±229 survival days). Weighted composite: 0.15*78 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.30*80 = 72.
systems Novelty 78 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 75 Resilience 65 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM agents have become reliable at isolated short-horizon tasks — fixing GitHub issues, following service dialogues, completing web workflows — but these share a common structure: a clear goal, fast feedback, and decisions that do not compound over time. Consequential human decisions are different: they require choosing what to prioritize when resources are scarce, interpreting noisy indirect signals, adapting strategy as competitors respond, and maintaining coherent execution over long time horizons where the effect of any single decision may not surface for weeks. Existing long-horizon benchmarks such as Vending-Bench and AccountingBench extend evaluation beyond one-shot tasks but narrow the action space and keep the environment largely stable or directly observable. CEO-BENCH specifically targets four capabilities that remain untested: navigating 500-day horizons under uncertainty, acquiring information from noisy indirect evidence, adapting to a changing world, and orchestrating many interdependent decisions toward a coherent goal. Key Idea The central insight is that a mechanistic simulator with explicit mathematical rules — rather than an LLM as judge — can generate a partially observable, non-stationary environment rich enough to require genuine strategic reasoning while remaining reproducible and resistant to benchmark gaming. By grounding customer subscription decisions in microeconomic participation rules (Mussa-Rosen, 1978), competitor catch-up in adaptive quality-bank mechanics, reputation propagation across 26 customer groups, and macroeconomics in an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck PMI process, the benchmark creates a world where cash is a credible signal of strategic competence rather than a proxy that can be gamed through surface-level verbal behavior. Methodology CEO-BENCH simulates a fictional subscription software company (NovaMind) over 500 days starting with $1M cash and zero customers. Each simulated week the agent takes unlimited turns across 34 tools in 8 categories: pricing, marketing and growth, product quality and R&D, infrastructure and reliability, enterprise sales, information acquisition, public communication, and database query. The agent interacts through a Python package (novamind_api) exposed in a minimal terminal harness (bash, read-file, edit-file); context is managed by clearing action history weekly and retaining only the system prompt and an agent-editable memory file. The world mechanics are fully specified by 49 equations. Customer subscription follows a participation-curve surplus rule (Eq. 5-6): a customer subscribes only if perceived quality at the offered price exceeds the customer's price-indexed quality floor. Perceived quality (Eq. 15) combines delivered quality, customer relationship score, tenure log-bonus, support open issues, quota saturation penalty, and in-app ad load penalty. Satisfaction is a memory-bearing exponential moving average with loss-aversion asymmetry (Eq. 16-17). Reputation propagates across groups with cross-group spillovers scaled by a configured influence matrix (Eq. 22-25). Customer acquisition integrates reputation, market saturation, macroeconomic cycle, social media reactions, and demand surges (Eq. 28-30). The macroeconomic background is an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process around a sinusoidal PMI cycle (Eq. 37). Competitors raise customer quality expectations through both stationary periodic events and adaptive catch-up to the agent's publicly visible development gains (Eq. 34-36). Stochasticity uses per-component independent RNG seeds so that calling the market-research tool multiple times always returns the same sequence regardless of actions in other areas. Key design decisions: (1) mechanistic rules throughout rather than LLM-as-judge, justified to prevent exploitation through verbal promises as seen in prior benchmarks; (2) per-component seed isolation to maintain consistency under stochasticity; (3) weekly context refresh to enable 500-day episodes without context overflow. Ten models are evaluated at 3 runs each: Claude Opus 4.8 (max), GPT-5.5 (xhigh), Claude Opus 4.7 (max), Claude Sonnet 4.6 (max), Claude Haiku 4.5 (thinking), Kimi K2.6 (reasoning), GLM 5.1 (reasoning), Gemini 3 Flash (preview high), DeepSeek V4 Pro (reasoning), Grok 4.20 (reasoning). Key Results Best-run final cash: Claude Opus 4.8 ($27.8M), GPT-5.5 ($21.3M), rule-based heuristic ($15.76M), Claude Opus 4.7 ($389K), Kimi K2.6 ($98K), Claude Sonnet 4.6 ($69.8K), all remaining models $0 (bankrupt in best run). Bankruptcy counts across 3 runs: 6 of 10 models go bankrupt in all 3 runs; only Claude Opus 4.8 (0 bankruptcies) and Claude Opus 4.7 (0 bankruptcies) never go bankrupt, though Opus 4.7 finishes far below the starting balance. Mean survival days: Claude Opus 4.8 and Opus 4.7 at 500.0 ± 0.0; GPT-5.5 at 333.7 ± 229.7 (high variance); Grok 4.20 at 28.3 ± 8.5. The estimated cash upper bound is approximately $2.2B, indicating the benchmark is far from saturated. Behavioral skill decomposition (Fig. 12): ad-channel allocation efficiency (fraction of spend on best channel per group) is 43% for Claude Opus 4.8, 33% for GPT-5.5, 14% for Claude Opus 4.7, and ~10% for remaining models, against a random-guessing baseline of 20%. Four-week cash forecast error: Claude Opus 4.8 (8%), GPT-5.5 (25%), Claude Opus 4.7 (48%), other models up to 179%. Competitor detection lag: top three models detect at ~1 week vs. ~2 weeks for others. Planning frequency (weekly "if" count in agent memos): Claude Opus 4.8 (8.57), GPT-5.5 (7.47), others (2.63-2.74). Targeted development spending: GPT-5.5 and Claude Opus 4.8 direct ~89-90% of development spend to group-specific targeted improvements vs. 43% for the remaining model average and 10% for Kimi K2.6. Harness ablation: replacing the custom minimal harness with Claude Code for Opus 4.7 reduces average turns per day from 1.27 to 0.32 with substantially worse cash outcomes; replacing custom harness with Codex for GPT-5.5 reduces turns from 4.79 to 2.20 with similarly degraded performance. Difficulty ablation: removing the adaptive competitor eliminates most of the task difficulty (models reach $100M+ range); a 50-day horizon reduction still leaves only GPT-5.5 profitable, showing the coordination problem persists even at shorter scales. Novelty Classification NEW BENCHMARK, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the combination of (a) 500-day mechanistic simulation with full mathematical specification, (b) partial observability through indirect database signals rather than direct state readout, (c) non-stationary competitive pressure via adaptive catch-up mechanics, and (d) a composable Python action interface that lets agents build custom workflows rather than calling fixed APIs. The individual components — microeconomic participation rules, OU macro processes, SQL databases, LLM-as-agent evaluation — are all known; the contribution is integrating them into a coherent evaluation environment that exposes capability gaps invisible to existing benchmarks. Rigor & Reproducibility Three runs per model with fixed seed 42 is the primary weakness: standard deviations on survival days (±229.7 for GPT-5.5, ±136.0 for Claude Sonnet 4.6) demonstrate high variance, and the paper reports best-run cash rather than mean cash for the headline comparison — a reasonable choice given bankruptcy dynamics but one that selects optimistically. No significance tests are reported. The rule-based baseline is a best-of-24 grid search over configurations evaluated on seed 42, the same seed used for all model runs, which could inflate the baseline. Agent trajectories are released through an interactive viewer; the simulator code is released on GitHub. No trained model weights are involved. No human performance baseline is established, leaving the absolute difficulty of the task unanchored relative to human judgment. Deployability (SYSTEMS) The paper's profile is SYSTEMS — the contribution is a built simulation environment evaluated through deployment. - Practical: The benchmark runs through standard API calls; per-run costs range from $0.75 (Grok 4.20) to $213.41 (Claude Opus 4.8), making multi-run evaluation feasible on a research budget. - Efficient: The weekly context-clearing harness manages 500-day episodes within model context limits; the per-component seed design allows reproducible stochasticity without prohibitive cost from full determinism. - Effective: The benchmark differentiates models at multiple levels — bankruptcy rate, final cash magnitude, and behavioral sub-skills — and the sub-skill metrics correlate with outcome in the expected direction, suggesting the simulator is measuring what it claims. - Scalable: Competitor difficulty is a tunable parameter demonstrated across five settings (no competitor, stationary, adaptive u ∈ {0.1, 0.2, 0.3}); the 26-group, 19-table structure can in principle be extended, though no scaling experiment beyond the current configuration is reported. The benchmark is released as a software artifact with code and trajectory data. Strengths - The mechanistic world design is the benchmark's strongest property: because every customer decision, reputation shift, and revenue event follows an explicit equation, agents cannot game scores through persuasive language directed at an LLM judge. This is a direct fix for a documented failure mode in Vending-Bench. - The harness ablation (Section 4.3) is an underappreciated finding: switching Claude Opus 4.7 from the custom harness to Claude Code reduces actions per day from 1.27 to 0.32 with markedly worse cash outcomes, revealing that coding-oriented system prompts actively suppress agentic task behavior and that harness design confounds model comparisons in the literature. - The behavioral decomposition (Section 3.4) maps aggregate performance to specific measurable skills — ad allocation efficiency, forecast error, competitor detection latency, planning frequency — giving researchers actionable diagnostic signal rather than a single leaderboard number. - The full mathematical specification (49 equations, 33 pages) makes the simulator analyzable, extensible, and reproducible in a way that opaque LLM-judged environments are not. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note in Section 6.1 that (1) the simulator abstracts product quality to a scalar, preventing evaluation of qualitative product reasoning; (2) real-world startup concerns including compliance, security, and fundraising are excluded; and (3) discrepancies between the simulation and real business dynamics are inevitable. These are stated directly and credibly, not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Three runs per model is insufficient given the observed variance. The best-run selection procedure, combined with the high standard deviations in Table 3, means that headline cash figures reflect a mix of model quality and run-specific luck. A 10-run minimum with mean and median reporting would substantially increase confidence. - No human baseline is provided. Without knowing what a domain-expert human achieves, it is impossible to assess whether Claude Opus 4.8's $27.8M represents 1% or 50% of human-level performance. The rule-based heuristic at $15.76M is a useful sanity check but not a human proxy. - The rule-based baseline is a best-of-24 grid search on seed 42, the same seed used in all model evaluations. This means the baseline may be overfit to seed 42 and the comparison between LLM agents and the baseline conflates model quality with seed sensitivity. - The custom harness is motivated by empirical performance, but it is unclear whether the harness systematically favors certain model families over others. The finding that Claude Code underperforms is suggestive, but the paper does not test whether a different custom harness design might advantage different models. - All 10 evaluated models are closed-weight frontier systems; no open-weight models appear in the primary comparison table, limiting the benchmark's utility for the open-source research community. - The qualitative behavioral analysis relies on visual inspection of strategy memos and uses the count of the word "if" as a planning proxy — a crude signal that captures hypothetical reasoning only indirectly and could be gamed by verbose models.
