#24 — Innovative (72)

Human-Centric Information Access through Individual and Population Level Alignment

H Wu · mcgill.scholaris.ca · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This dissertation introduces five peer-reviewed methods — TIL (Triplet Importance Learning), IMCAT (Intent-aware Multi-source Contrastive Alignment for Tag-enhanced recommendation), GPR4DUR (Gaussian Process Regression for Density-based User Representation), JME (Joint Multisided Exposure Fairness), and Multi-FR — that collectively reframe the objective of information access systems from relevance maximization to human alignment at both individual and population levels. - Why it's important: Search engines and recommender systems trained on implicit feedback systematically misread noisy behavioral signals as genuine preferences, collapse multi-faceted user intent into a single point embedding, and distribute exposure inequitably across providers, creating compounding accuracy and fairness harms that affect billions of daily interactions. - How it does it: The dissertation's core technical moves are: bilevel optimization over per-triplet importance weights (TIL), contrastive intent disentanglement from auxiliary tag data (IMCAT), Gaussian Process Regression to replace point-estimate user embeddings with full preference distributions (GPR4DUR), relevance-disparity decomposition of multisided fairness metrics (JME), and Multiple Gradient Descent for Pareto-efficient multi-objective fairness optimization (Multi-FR). - The good: TIL-MI allows a simple NeuMF backbone to match LightGCN's Recall@20 on three benchmark datasets with more than 50% reduction in training time, and the GPR4DUR approach (NeurIPS 2024) provides explicit epistemic uncertainty quantification for sparse-data users, a gap no prior point-estimate recommender system addressed. - The missing: The five contributions were developed largely independently and tested on different datasets, so no single experiment validates how the individual-level and population-level alignment methods interact or compose; joint behavior under realistic deployment conditions is unverified. - Read it or not: Researchers working on implicit-feedback recommendation, fairness in ranking, or uncertainty-aware user modeling will find at least two or three chapters directly relevant; practitioners seeking a single deployable system should note that no unified implementation is provided.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (78) is the strongest dimension: paired t-tests at p≤0.01, five seeded runs, a 50%-noise stress test, peer-reviewed components (GPR4DUR at NeurIPS 2024), and released TIL code. Novelty (75) is high across five contributions, with the bilevel triplet-importance learning and Pareto-efficient multi-stakeholder fairness via MGD being genuine new formulations. Completeness (68) and Resilience (68) pull the composite down: the five methods are tested on different datasets so composition is never validated, Chapter 3 baselines are 2019–2021 (SimGCL/SGDL absent), and the fairness work uses binary gender only. Non-Obviousness (72) and Practical Impact (65, only TIL code confirmed, no unified system) round it out. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*65 = 72.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 68 Resilience 68 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Search engines and recommender systems dominate how users discover content and how providers obtain attention. Their standard optimization target — short-term relevance metrics such as click-through rate or Recall@k — fails to account for two structural mismatches. At the individual level, implicit interaction signals (clicks, dwell time, purchase history) are inherently noisy: a click may reflect curiosity, clickbait, or accidental engagement rather than genuine preference, yet Bayesian Personalized Ranking (BPR) and its derivatives treat all training triplets as equally informative. Beyond noise, individual users hold multiple simultaneous latent intents — a restaurant visitor cares separately about taste and service — but standard embeddings collapse all intents into one vector, obscuring the reason behind each interaction and degrading cold-start performance for long-tail items. At the population level, accuracy-first optimization amplifies rich-get-richer dynamics that systematically under-expose niche providers and specific demographic groups; existing fairness interventions treat consumer and provider fairness as separate single-objective problems and rely on hand-tuned weighting coefficients, producing solutions that are neither principled nor Pareto-efficient. Key Idea: The dissertation rests on three nested insights: (1) the informativeness of a training triplet (u, i, j) is a function of all three elements jointly, not of the positive or negative item alone, and can be learned via bilevel optimization without enumerating individual weights; (2) user intent can be explicitly disentangled into K latent factors using auxiliary semantic signals and multi-source contrastive alignment, with distributional representations via Gaussian Process Regression further capturing epistemic uncertainty beyond discrete intent clusters; and (3) multisided fairness in ranking decomposes cleanly into relevance and disparity components for each stakeholder type, enabling Pareto-efficient joint optimization via Multiple Gradient Descent without manual trade-off specification. Methodology: The dissertation contains five methodological contributions organized in two parts. Part II — Individual-Level Alignment. Chapter 3 introduces Triplet Importance Learning (TIL), which augments the BPR pairwise loss with a learned importance weight w_uij = sigmoid(W2 · relu(W1 · s_uij + b1) + b2), where s_uij encodes proximity of the positive and negative items to the user's aggregate interest representation (Uni-Interest strategy) or to K cluster centers learned via end-to-end Student-t soft assignment with KL-divergence clustering loss (Multi-Interest strategy). The weight generator (a two-layer MLP) is decoupled from the backbone embedding parameters via bilevel optimization: the inner level minimizes the importance-weighted BPR loss over embedding parameters Θ; the outer level minimizes the vanilla BPR loss over weight-generator parameters Λ, using a first-order gradient approximation (DARTS-style) to avoid expensive second-order computation. Chapter 4 introduces IMCAT, a contrastive alignment framework that maps users and items into K intent-specific subspaces using auxiliary tag data. The Intent-aware Multi-source Contrastive Alignment (IMCA) module constructs positive pairs from the top-m tag-cluster neighbors of an item across multiple tag sources, and the Intent-aware Set-to-Set Alignment (ISA) module pulls together intra-intent item sets while pushing apart inter-intent sets. The joint objective combines BPR ranking loss, IMCA contrastive loss, and ISA set-alignment loss. Chapter 5 introduces GPR4DUR, which replaces each user's point embedding with a Gaussian Process distribution over the item embedding space. Given a user's interaction history as input, GPR predicts a distribution over preference regions; items are ranked by expected relevance integrated over this distribution, supporting Thompson-sampling-style exploration for sparse users. Part III — Population-Level Alignment. Chapter 6 introduces Joint Multisided Exposure (JME) fairness, a theoretical framework that decomposes six existing fairness metrics (individual-item-fairness, group-group-fairness, etc.) into relevance and disparity components for stochastic ranking policies (Plackett-Luce randomization). Kendall rank correlation analysis across these six dimensions reveals that metrics sharing the same disparity component correlate strongly regardless of relevance formulation, a structural insight absent from prior work. Chapter 7 introduces Multi-FR, which formulates fairness-aware recommendation as a multi-objective optimization (MOO) problem over differentiable fairness objectives (consumer-side Disparity_u, producer-side Disparity_i, Gini coefficient, popularity rate, diversity) and recommendation accuracy (Recall, NDCG). Ranking is made differentiable via a smooth approximation of the indicator function. The Multiple Gradient Descent (MGD) algorithm, adapted from multi-task learning, finds Pareto-stationary solutions without requiring hand-specified loss weights, theoretically guaranteeing Pareto efficiency. Key Results: For TIL (Chapter 3): evaluated on Amazon_CD (11K users, 33K items), Gowalla (30K users, 41K items), and Last.fm (23K users, 34K items), with four backbone models (MF, NeuMF, MGCCF, LightGCN). TIL-MI achieves the highest R@20 and N@20 in almost all 24 backbone×dataset combinations (p ≤ 0.01 by paired t-test vs best baseline). On Last.fm with LightGCN, TIL-MI reaches R@20 = 24.85 and N@20 = 15.35, compared to LightGCN+BPR at R@20 = 23.31 and N@20 = 14.25. Critically, NeuMF+TIL-MI matches vanilla LightGCN performance on all three datasets with more than 50% reduction in training time per iteration. Robustness experiments under 50%-noise injection on positive and negative samples show TIL-UI and TIL-MI exhibit the smallest performance drop across all noise conditions. For IMCAT (Chapter 4): L-IMCAT achieves best or second-best R@20 and N@20 across multiple tag-enhanced datasets (p ≤ 0.01 vs best baseline). Ablation shows ISA module contributes especially to long-tail items and cold-start users. For GPR4DUR (Chapter 5): evaluated on MovieLens and Amazon datasets, GPR4DUR outperforms point-estimate baselines on retrieval and ranking tasks, particularly for low-activity users. An online simulation confirms higher cumulative interest coverage compared to deterministic policies. For JME (Chapter 6): empirical analysis on MovieLens1M demonstrates that six fairness metrics decompose into measurable disparity and relevance trade-off curves; stochasticity parameter β provides a continuous trade-off dial between disparity and relevance components. For Multi-FR (Chapter 7): evaluated on MovieLens100K, MovieLens1M, and FM-reduced. Multi-FR matches or exceeds competing fairness-aware methods on accuracy metrics (Recall@20, NDCG@20) while achieving lower Disparity_u, Disparity_i, Gini, and better diversity simultaneously, without manual tuning of fairness-accuracy trade-off weights; training time scales linearly with the number of fairness constraints. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (primary, across all five contributions). NEW FORMULATION for JME (decomposing multisided fairness into relevance and disparity components) and for Multi-FR (casting fairness-aware recommendation as Pareto-efficient MOO). NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING in JME (structural correlations among fairness metrics via Kendall analysis). The genuinely novel elements are: (1) triplet-level joint importance learning for both positive and negative items simultaneously via bilevel optimization — prior work addressed only one side; (2) distributional user representation via GPR for explicit epistemic uncertainty — prior uncertainty-aware recommenders used Gaussian embeddings with fixed variance; (3) Pareto-efficient multi-stakeholder fairness optimization without hand-tuned weights. What is competent application of known ideas: the contrastive learning approach in IMCAT follows CLIP-style alignment; the MOO mechanism in Multi-FR adapts the Sener-Koltun multi-task learning algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility: Chapter 3: experiments run 5 times with fixed seeds; average reported; paired t-test significance at p ≤ 0.01; ablations for Uni-Interest vs Multi-Interest vs K-means variant (TIL-MIK) confirm both end-to-end clustering and multi-interest modeling contribute independently; robustness analysis under controlled noise injection is a genuine stress test absent from most comparable papers. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/haolun-wu/TIL. Chapter 4: ablation table (Table 4.3) isolates IMCA and ISA modules; separate analysis for cold-start users and long-tail items; paired t-test at p ≤ 0.01. Chapter 5: offline evaluation on retrieval and ranking tasks plus online simulation; significance testing present. Chapter 6: a theoretical framework paper; metric decomposition proofs and empirical validation of the trade-off curves on MovieLens1M. Chapter 7: paired t-test at p ≤ 0.01; comparison of MOO vs grid-search on BPRMF backbone (Figure 7.2) shows MOO achieves similar Pareto frontier with far less compute. No concerns about Goodhart's Law or benchmark gaming are evident; the dissertation evaluates on established public datasets (MovieLens, Amazon, Gowalla, Last.fm) with standard splits. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL across all five chapters. - Practical: All experiments run on Nvidia Tesla V100 32GB GPUs with embedding size 64 and batch size 5000; the framework plugs into existing MF, NeuMF, MGCCF, and LightGCN backbones without architectural change. The bilevel optimization adds a second MLP (O(d²) parameters) per model, which is negligible. - Efficient: TIL-MI adds modest overhead: NeuMF+TIL-MI requires more than 50% less training time per iteration than LightGCN+BPR while matching its accuracy. GPR4DUR has O(n³) kernel inversion per user, which the thesis acknowledges is the primary scalability concern and addresses through approximations; exact scale-up performance not tested beyond standard benchmark sizes (~30K users). Multi-FR training time scales linearly with the number of fairness constraints (Table 7.9). - Effective: TIL-MI consistently improves Recall@20 and NDCG@20 over all baselines across all backbone/dataset combinations with statistical significance. JME provides actionable insight: the disparity-relevance decomposition makes trade-offs explicit and tunable via the stochasticity parameter. Multi-FR achieves simultaneous improvement on accuracy and multiple fairness metrics, which prior single-objective fairness methods could not guarantee. - Scalable: TIL, IMCAT, and Multi-FR are evaluated on datasets up to ~1M interactions (MovieLens1M for Chapters 6 and 7; Gowalla with 1M interactions for Chapter 3). GPR4DUR's exact cubic-complexity kernel is not evaluated at industrial scale; the thesis identifies this as a limitation. No experiment approaches web-scale (10⁸+ interactions). The dissertation delivers code for TIL (GitHub confirmed) and trained models are implicitly reproducible; not all five chapter codebases are confirmed released. Strengths: - The bilevel optimization formulation for TIL is technically sound: decoupling weight learning from embedding learning avoids the degenerate zero-weight solution that naive joint optimization produces, and this is explicitly proved and empirically demonstrated. - The robustness analysis in Chapter 3 (50% noise injection on both positive and negative sides independently and jointly) is a genuine stress test that directly validates the paper's core claim about noise tolerance. - The JME decomposition (Chapter 6) provides a diagnostic tool that practitioners can apply to any existing stochastic ranker to understand whether a given fairness failure is caused by relevance or disparity, which is more actionable than a single composite metric. - The Multi-FR framework (Chapter 7) addresses a real gap: prior work required hand-tuning loss weights for fairness-accuracy trade-offs, producing solutions that are sensitive to hyperparameter choice and not guaranteed Pareto-optimal; MGD-based optimization resolves both issues simultaneously. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The dissertation acknowledges in Chapter 8 that: (1) GPR4DUR faces scalability challenges at large user/item counts due to cubic complexity in kernel inversion; (2) the work does not address interactive or conversational alignment (listed as future direction); (3) longitudinal fairness and temporal ecosystem dynamics are not captured — the fairness methods are evaluated at a single snapshot, not over repeated interactions where feedback loops could erode fairness gains. The authors confront these boundaries directly, which increases credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The five chapters are evaluated on different datasets with different backbone models, which prevents direct comparison of relative impact across contributions and leaves the question of composition unanswered — does applying TIL together with Multi-FR produce additive gains or interference? The baselines in Chapter 3 (Gowalla, Last.fm, Amazon_CD) are from 2019–2021; more recent denoising methods (e.g., SGDL, SimGCL) are absent, which weakens the claimed superiority claim. IMCAT's contrastive approach (Chapter 4) is structurally similar to contemporaneous self-supervised recommendation papers (SGL, SimGCL) that the thesis does not directly compare against on the same datasets. GPR4DUR's online simulation is self-constructed rather than a live A/B test, which limits the strength of its practical-impact claim. The fairness evaluations in Chapters 6 and 7 use movie datasets with binary gender as the sensitive attribute, which is both narrow (single demographic dimension) and potentially misaligned with real-world multidimensional fairness concerns. Bottom Line: The dissertation delivers genuine algorithmic contributions across both noise-robust preference modeling and principled fairness optimization, with the TIL bilevel-optimization framework and the Multi-FR Pareto-efficient multi-objective formulation being the most technically mature and cleanly evaluated; researchers in implicit-feedback recommendation and multisided fairness in ranking will find these two chapters immediately useful, while the GPR4DUR contribution is significant in concept but limited in scalability evidence.
Search Results
#25 — Innovative (71)

IroKnight: Ownership-Preserving Neural Acceleration for Inference Serving

H Santhanam, ARA Rajan, H Esmaeilzadeh · iroknight.ai · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces IroKnight, an NPU microarchitecture that keeps all on-chip storage (buffers, pipe-registers, SRAM) encrypted and integrity-protected throughout LLM inference, achieving near-FHE-level data ownership at 3.3% runtime and at most 18% energy overhead. - Why it's important: Cloud-hosted LLM inference forces data owners to surrender cryptographic control of sensitive queries and model weights to untrusted cloud infrastructure, a critical liability in healthcare, finance, and national-security applications where microarchitectural exploits have repeatedly leaked plaintext from on-chip NPU storage. - How it does it: Two domain-specific insights: LLM operators follow regular affine-walk access patterns that let PadGen units pre-compute AES-GCM pads one cycle ahead so XOR encryption/decryption occurs inside the ALU without stalling; and the Galois-Field multiply plus XOR-accumulate structure of AES-GCM integrity hashing mirrors the MACC flow of GEMM, so authentication piggybacks onto the existing systolic data path at negligible cost. - The good: Across eight LLMs (Llama3-1B through GPT-OSS-120B on 1-16 NPUs), IroKnight with encryption-only incurs less than 1% latency overhead versus a conventional NPU, while the FHE NPU baseline is 713×-985× slower on the same models. - The missing: The threat model explicitly excludes High Bandwidth Memory from the Trusted Computing Base — citing HBM rowhammer and disturbance attacks but accepting this gap — and the paper provides no side-channel analysis against power or electromagnetic probing. - Read it or not: Essential reading for computer architects designing secure inference infrastructure and for ML systems researchers who must deploy regulated-domain workloads on untrusted cloud NPUs; skippable for those whose threat model ends at off-chip encryption (TEEs) or who work exclusively on training.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (60, weight 0.30) is the dominant term and the main drag: IroKnight requires custom NPU silicon, is not deployable on existing commercial NPUs, and the RTL/simulation code is not released (commercial affiliation), so practical reach is gated. Novelty (80) and Non-Obviousness (85) are high: the pad-pre-computation-mirrors-Run-Ahead-Index-Generator insight and the AES-GCM-authentication-mirrors-MACC insight are sharp, genuinely new architectural observations. Rigor (72) is solid for a systems paper, with cycle-accurate simulation, RTL synthesis, and sensitivity sweeps across eight LLMs (1B-120B), but no public artifact and the FHE comparison uses an optimistic projected model. Completeness (75) and Resilience (72) benefit from direct confrontation of the HBM-exclusion and side-channel gaps, though the HBM risk surface is under-quantified and there is no comparison to Graviton or PipeLLM. Weighted: 0.15*80 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*85 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.30*60 ≈ 71.
systems Novelty 80 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 85 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Cloud-hosted LLM inference requires both the data owner (e.g., a hospital submitting patient records as queries) and the model provider (e.g., an AI company supplying weights) to relinquish cryptographic control of their assets to the cloud operator and the entire underlying software stack. This is not a hypothetical concern: publicly documented CVEs show that adversaries have exploited software vulnerabilities in Samsung, Qualcomm, Huawei, and AMD NPU drivers to execute arbitrary code and exfiltrate on-chip data from buffers, caches, registers, and SRAM (CVE-2020-28343, CVE-2022-22265, CVE-2021-22388, CVE-2024-36336, among others). Trusted Execution Environments preserve ownership only outside the chip; once data enters the enclave it resides in plaintext on-chip, and prior microarchitectural attacks have repeatedly broken this boundary. FHE preserves cryptographic ownership by computing on ciphertext throughout, but the best current FHE NPUs still incur 713×-985× runtime and 871×-7396× energy overheads relative to a conventional NPU at LLM scale — making FHE a research target rather than a deployable baseline for LLM serving today. The gap IroKnight addresses is the absence of a hardware design point that offers cryptographic data ownership at near-line-rate performance. Key Idea The core insight is that AES-GCM stream-cipher encryption reduces to a bitwise XOR between plaintext and a pad, and the pad depends only on the data's address and version number — not on the data itself. LLM operators (MatMul tiles, softmax, sigmoid, vector reductions) execute as affine walks over on-chip storage with statically known strides and offsets, so pads can be generated one cycle ahead of the ALU by a pipelined PadGen unit that advances the address counter in parallel with the Run-Ahead Index Generator that already drives the Weight Buffer. This converts what would be a multi-cycle encrypt/decrypt penalty into a zero-latency XOR at ALU inputs and outputs. The analogous insight for integrity is that AES-GCM authentication computes hash(data) via Galois-Field multiply then XOR-accumulate — structurally identical to the MACC (Multiply-ACCumulate) that systolic arrays already perform — so a mirrored parallel path threading through the same PE connectivity computes hashes alongside computation without adding to the critical path. Methodology IroKnight modifies a TPU-like systolic array (128×128 PEs, 1 GHz, INT8, 18 MB scratchpad, 16 GB HBM, 1 TB/s memory bandwidth per Table I). The pipeline has three added components: (1) Systolic PadGen — each PE gains a pipelined PadGen unit beside its MACC ALU. The PadGen receives addresses one cycle early from the Run-Ahead Index Generator, hashes them through ten rounds of AES-GCM key expansion (key addition, S-box substitution, row/column mixing), and outputs 128-bit pads. These are immediately XORed with the ciphertext operands at ALU inputs and with ALU outputs before write-back. Pad uniqueness uses per-tile version numbers tracking write counts; read-only weight tiles have constant version numbers; output tiles use the physical address of the destination as a personalization factor. (2) Vector Engine PadGen — non-GEMM operators (softmax, sigmoid, vector add) on the vector engine receive compile-time-known base/stride/offset values, enabling a Run-Ahead Address Generator to supply pipelined PadGens with addresses before the vector instruction's ALU stage. The 128-bit granularity of AES-GCM is shared across sixteen 8-bit PEs per unit. (3) Authentication path — each PE adds a Galois-Field multiplier and XOR-accumulator alongside the MACC ALU. For weights, authentication runs as weights trickle into the Weight Buffer (write-enable signal triggers hashing). For input activations, the first systolic column hashes arriving ciphertext sub-vectors. For output activations, the last systolic row hashes departing elements. The bottom-left PE carries two pairs (inputs + outputs). Hashes accompany tiles to off-chip memory; on load, hashes are recomputed and compared. The cryptographic algorithm is AES-GCM (NIST-approved, used in NVIDIA H100/H200 confidential computing and Intel AES-NI). Both variants are implemented in SystemVerilog and synthesized with Synopsys Design Compiler 2023.09, FreePDK 45nm, scaled to 7nm using DeepScaleTool. Evaluation uses a cycle-accurate open-source NPU simulator extended with explicit models of pad generation, mid-execution authentication, and off-chip hash memory traffic. FHE comparison uses an optimistic model following Kim et al. (ISCA 2023) with a 180 MB on-chip buffer for ciphertexts and specialized units for non-linear operators. NPU TEE comparison follows Hua et al. (MICRO 2019) with both tile-level and layer-level scratchpad clearing. Critical design decisions: (a) Pad pre-computation via Run-Ahead Index Generator — well-justified because LLM access regularity is a known, exploited property of tiled systolic execution; (b) Galois-Field multiply decomposed into smaller sub-multipliers matching activation/weight bitwidth (e.g., 128×8) rather than the standard 128×128 — well-justified by the absence of carry in binary polynomial arithmetic; (c) Version number tracking per tile rather than per word — accepted as an engineering tradeoff with a careful argument about correctness, but adds complexity for tracking output tile partial writes. Key Results All LLM results use 1024 input and 1024 output tokens, batch size 1, swept from 256-4096 tokens for sensitivity. Baselines: conventional NPU (plaintext), FHE NPU, NPU TEE with tile-level and layer-level clearing. Latency (Table III): IroKnight with encryption only incurs 0.2% latency overhead across all eight LLMs (ranging 0.3%-0.4% in sensitivity sweeps). IroKnight with encryption and authentication incurs 3.3% overhead (3.1%-3.4% in sensitivity). NPU TEE with tile-level clearing incurs 19.6% runtime overhead; layer-level clearing incurs 5.1%; IroKnight thus beats tile-level by 6× and layer-level by 1.5× on runtime. FHE NPU latencies: 985× for GPT-OSS-120B, 713× for Llama4-Scout, 819× for Llama3-8B, 607× for OPT-66B. Energy (Table IV): IroKnight with encryption increases energy at most 28% across LLMs (the ceiling occurs at short sequence lengths where compute fraction is higher; at 4096 tokens it drops to 21% for GPT-OSS-120B). With authentication added, the ceiling is 18% at short sequences, falling to 15% at long sequences due to KV-cache amortization. FHE energy overheads: 7396× for GPT-OSS-120B, 871× for Llama3-70B, 2282× for Llama3-8B. NPU TEE energy with tile-level clearing: 5.4%; layer-level: 4.9%. IroKnight's energy overhead (15%) exceeds NPU TEE energy overhead, reflecting the off-chip hash memory traffic for authentication; encryption-only (up to 28%) also exceeds TEE because IroKnight encrypts all on-chip data rather than just off-chip traffic. Non-LLM networks (Table III/IV): BERT, MobileNet-v2, ResNet-50, ViT are compute-bound (small model footprint, no massive HBM traffic), so IroKnight with encryption incurs 3.55× and 3.39× energy overhead for BERT and ResNet-50 respectively, rising to 3.80× and 3.92× with authentication. Latency overhead for these remains 2%-3%. Area and TDP (Table II): Conventional NPU baseline is 9.4 mm² / 30 W. Encryption variant: 16.9 mm² / 52.9 W (1.79× area, 1.76× TDP). Encryption + authentication: 18.4 mm² / 65.5 W (1.95× area, 2.18× TDP). Critical path remains SRAM reads. Sensitivity: Overhead is stable across memory bandwidths 1-4 TB/s; batch sizes 1-16 (encryption maintains <1% latency, energy rises to 55% at batch 16 due to increased compute-to-memory ratio); PE array sizes 32×32 to 256×256; speculative decoding acceptance rates 0%-100% (0.2%-0.5% latency for encryption, 3.1%-3.3% with authentication); token pruning 0%-80% (0.3%-0.7% latency for encryption). Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM / NEW METHOD. IroKnight is genuinely novel in establishing that same-cycle in-ALU AES-GCM stream cipher encryption is achievable in NPU systolic engines at near-line-rate performance, and that GEMM's MACC structure can be exploited to ingest authentication at zero pipeline overhead. Prior general-purpose ALU-only decryption schemes (EDAP, Sequestered Encryption, Mojo-V) retain plaintext in on-chip caches or registers across multiple instructions — a well-exploited attack surface that IroKnight eliminates. The two domain-specific insights (access regularity enables pad pre-computation; MACC mirrors GF authentication) are not individually new in isolation but their joint application to NPU design for LLM serving is original. The threat model (TCB = only ALUs + PadGen circuits) is also more aggressive than prior NPU TEE work. Rigor & Reproducibility Profile: SYSTEMS (hardware NPU design evaluated by cycle-accurate simulation and RTL synthesis). The paper includes sensitivity sweeps over memory bandwidth, batch size, PE array dimensions, speculative decoding acceptance rate, and token pruning fraction — covering the main design axes. Synthesis results (area, TDP, critical path) are reported from Synopsys Design Compiler 2023.09 on FreePDK 45nm, scaled to 7nm. The simulation infrastructure is described at sufficient detail to understand accounting (cycle-accurate activity-level energies, CACTI 6.0 for SRAM, PCIe Gen4, prior HBM access latency model from the literature). No error bars or multiple seeds are reported, appropriate for deterministic hardware simulation. Methodological concerns: (1) The FHE NPU comparison uses an optimistic model that the authors explicitly acknowledge may understate FHE's overheads — a principled choice given FHE has not been demonstrated at 70B-120B model scale, but it means the 713×-985× speedup claim is a lower bound on IroKnight's advantage. (2) RTL and simulation code are not stated to be publicly available; the authors are affiliated with IroKnight AI, Inc., a commercial entity, which suggests the implementation may not be open-sourced (not stated). (3) The security argument for AES-GCM's observability boundary rests on digital logic testing principles (Goldstein 2003, cited as [64]) rather than a formal cryptographic proof; this is a reasonable engineering argument but not a reduction to a formal security model. Deployability (SYSTEMS profile) - Practical: IroKnight requires custom NPU hardware; it is not deployable on existing commercial NPUs, but it is designed as a drop-in replacement for TPU-like NPUs using commercially available process nodes (7nm), AES-GCM (NIST-standardized, NVIDIA H100/H200 supported), and RSA key exchange. - Efficient: Area overhead is 1.79×-1.95× and TDP overhead is 1.76×-2.18× versus the baseline NPU; LLM latency overhead is 0.2%-3.3%; energy overhead is up to 28% for LLMs but 3.5×-4.7× for smaller compute-bound networks, making IroKnight less favorable for non-LLM workloads. - Effective: The evidence shows IroKnight keeps all on-chip storage encrypted and authenticated throughout inference, including for KV caching, speculative decoding, and token pruning — the paper addresses these inference-time optimizations explicitly and demonstrates they do not degrade ownership. - Scalable: Tested at production-relevant scale (GPT-OSS-120B on 16 NPUs, Llama4-Scout on 16 NPUs) using Microsoft DeepSpeed for tensor and expert parallelism; inter-chip secure channel establishment is described and authenticated; the overhead remains stable at 3.3% latency across the full model range tested. The work delivers a synthesized RTL design (SystemVerilog) and a calibrated cycle-accurate simulation infrastructure, but public release is not stated. Strengths - The identification that AES-GCM pad generation can mirror the Run-Ahead Index Generator's timing is a clean, non-obvious architectural insight that converts an apparently serial dependency (pad → ALU) into a parallel one — this is the paper's strongest intellectual contribution. - The authentication-via-MACC-mirroring insight is similarly sharp: by recognizing that GF(2^128) multiply and XOR-accumulate are isomorphic to MACC over a different algebraic structure, the paper avoids any pipeline stall or separate authentication pass. - The evaluation scope is serious: eight LLMs spanning 1B to 120B parameters including MoE architectures (GPT-OSS-120B, Llama4-Scout), with multi-NPU tensor and expert parallelism, plus sensitivity sweeps covering the relevant hardware design space. - The threat model is more rigorous than NPU TEE prior work: IroKnight excludes HBM from the TCB and excludes all on-chip storage structures from the plaintext domain, whereas TEEs expose all of these, and the paper provides substantial empirical evidence (CVE citations) motivating this choice. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that IroKnight does not address power analysis or electromagnetic probing attacks, both of which require physically breaking into the datacenter and attaching specialized probes for long periods — characterized as unrealistic for LLM serving. HBM is excluded from the TCB despite recent HBM rowhammer and disturbance attack demonstrations, with the authors acknowledging this gap directly. The paper does not claim to replace FHE's theoretical guarantees, only to provide a practical architectural design point between TEEs and FHE. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The HBM exclusion is accepted but underexplored: the paper cites six HBM attack papers ([71]-[76]) but does not quantify what data — specifically, which model parameters or KV-cache entries — transits HBM in plaintext and what an adversary could learn from HBM-level observation alone. Given that model weights are stored in HBM encrypted (the paper states this), the risk surface is primarily KV-cache and intermediate activation traffic over HBM, but the threat this poses to data ownership is not analyzed. - For non-LLM networks (BERT, ResNet-50, ViT), IroKnight incurs 3.55×-4.68× energy overhead — a regime where the compute-bound nature of these workloads removes the HBM-dominance effect that makes LLM overheads low. The paper reports this but does not discuss deployment implications for operators running mixed LLM/non-LLM workloads on the same hardware. - The security argument relies on the observability principle from digital logic (combinational gate outputs are unobservable unless stored) without reduction to a formal cryptographic model. An adversary with sub-nanosecond physical access during ALU operation (e.g., via hardware trojans in the foundry) is outside the threat model but undiscussed. - The paper does not compare against Graviton (OSDI 2018) or Pipellm (ASPLOS 2025), both of which address secure LLM inference with speculative pipelining and encryption; the related work section cites these but the evaluation does not include them as baselines. - RSA key exchange for attestation and inter-chip channel setup is described but not evaluated for overhead; for multi-NPU serving at large scale (16 NPUs, frequent model swaps), the key management cost is non-trivial and not quantified. Bottom Line IroKnight is a well-executed architecture paper that identifies two genuine domain-specific insights enabling practical ownership-preserving LLM inference; any architect designing next-generation secure inference hardware or evaluating alternatives to TEEs for regulated-domain cloud deployment should read it.

#26 — Innovative (71)

GateMem: Benchmarking Memory Governance in Multi-Principal Shared-Memory Agents (12 ▲ )

Zhe Ren, Yibo Yang, Yimeng Chen, Zijun Zhao, Benshuo Fu, Zhihao Shu, Bingjie Zhang, Yangyang Xu, Dandan Guo, Shuicheng Yan · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GateMem, a benchmark that evaluates LLM memory agents across three joint governance dimensions — authorized utility, access-control enforcement, and post-deletion non-recovery — in multi-principal shared-memory settings spanning medical, office, education, and household domains. - Why it's important: Shared institutional deployments (clinical teams, enterprise staff, campus communities, households) require agents that simultaneously serve authorized users and reliably withhold or forget information from unauthorized ones; existing benchmarks measure only recall, leaving governance failures entirely unmeasured. - How it does it: Constructs 91 long-form multi-party interaction episodes with 2,218 hidden checkpoints, evaluates seven baseline memory architectures across six backbone LLMs using an LLM-as-judge protocol, and scores systems with a multiplicative Memory Governance Score (MGS = U × (1−A) × (1−F)) that prevents masking one failure mode with excellence on another. - The good: No evaluated method simultaneously achieves strong utility and reliable governance across any domain-backbone combination; LONG-CONTEXT leaks >20% in multiple settings despite high utility, a finding that recall-only benchmarks structurally cannot surface. - The missing: All 91 episodes are LLM-generated rather than drawn from real institutional logs, which risks systematic blind spots in the attack distribution and limits ecological validity. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone building or evaluating memory-augmented LLM agents for multi-user institutional settings; the benchmark, leaderboard, and harness are publicly released and ready to use.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (benchmark paper comparing existing systems). Novelty (75) is strong — formulating shared-memory governance as a joint tripartite problem (utility, access control, active forgetting) with a multiplicative MGS that blocks masking one failure with another is a new formulation, not a recall variant. Rigor (68) is held up by unusually careful judge validation (kappa >= 0.937, <=1.04 pt human-vs-judge gap on stratified Medical) but pulled down by all-LLM-generated episodes, incomplete baseline x backbone runs, and human validation in only one domain on a preprint. Completeness (70) and Resilience (68) are good — the attack taxonomy goes beyond obvious cases and limitations (interface-level vs. certified erasure, no governance-trained baseline) are stated directly. Practical Impact (72) reflects a released, ready-to-run benchmark, harness, and leaderboard. Weighted: 0.20*75+0.25*68+0.15*72+0.15*70+0.15*68+0.10*72 = 71.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 70 Resilience 68 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Existing memory benchmarks (LoCoMo, LongMemEval, PersonaMem, MemBench, EverMemBench, and others) treat agent memory as a private cache owned by a single user, where success equals recall accuracy. Real institutional deployments operate on a fundamentally different model: a clinical assistant shared by physicians, nurses, patients, and family members writes and queries a common memory pool under role-specific permissions; an enterprise assistant serves managers, contractors, legal staff, and executives with overlapping but non-identical access scopes; a campus assistant handles professors, students, advisors, parents, and registrars with fluid and sometimes conflicting authorization states. In these settings, high recall without governance is a security liability — an agent that accurately recalls a deleted lab result for an unauthorized family member has failed, regardless of factual correctness. The specific gap is that no prior benchmark jointly evaluates authorized utility, access-control boundary enforcement, and post-deletion non-recovery in a genuinely multi-principal shared context. Key Idea The core insight is that memory governance in shared deployments is inherently tripartite and cannot be decomposed: a system must simultaneously serve authorized queries (Utility), withhold information from unauthorized or over-scoped requesters (Access Control), and cease retrieving or confirming information after explicit deletion (Active Forgetting). These three objectives are in tension — full-context prompting boosts utility but surfaces information that should be withheld; policy-aware retrieval reduces leakage but causes over-refusal of authorized queries; structured external memory systems introduce organization but do not automatically enforce access boundaries or respect deletions. The Memory Governance Score (MGS = U × (1−A) × (1−F)) operationalizes this coupling multiplicatively: a system cannot compensate for severe leakage with high utility, nor for over-refusal with perfect forgetting compliance. Methodology GateMem is constructed through a three-stage pipeline. First, domain and policy design defines four institutional domains — Medical, Office, Education, Household — each with a distinct principal set, role taxonomy, and scope-specific access rule graph. Medical episodes involve up to 15 principals per episode (physicians, nurses, patients, family, pharmacists, schedulers) with permissions defined over lab results, medication details, treatment plans, and clinical interpretations. Office episodes involve project managers, engineers, finance staff, legal staff, contractors, and executives with access scopes over project confidentiality, commercial terms, staffing decisions, and incident details. Second, LLM-assisted episode construction generates 91 long-form interaction traces, each averaging 223 turns. Each turn records speaker, timestamp, turn type (ordinary dialogue, note update, portal message, lab result, scheduling change, deletion request), and natural-language content. Turn types are expressed in natural language and not exposed to the evaluated agent as explicit memory operation labels. Episodes are designed with utility memory anchors, access-overreach distractors, and deletion-to-recovery gaps. Third, 2,218 hidden checkpoints are inserted at turn boundaries. Each checkpoint contains a visible input (episode ID, turn boundary, authenticated requester identity, natural-language query) and a hidden annotation (checkpoint category, expected normalized action from {answer, answer_redacted, refuse, no_memory}, judge specification, and leak targets). The three checkpoint categories are Utility (728 checkpoints), Access Control (727 checkpoints), and Active Forgetting (763 checkpoints). Leak targets are string or regex patterns that must not appear in agent outputs for access-control and active-forgetting checkpoints. A four-stage quality control pipeline validates every episode: schema consistency, chain-of-evidence validation (gold answers must be supported by the preceding interaction history), deletion-chain closure (sensitive values must appear before deletion and only be targeted afterward), and manual leak-target inspection. Evaluation uses GPT-4o as judge with a structured output protocol. Effective Utility U requires both correct action and factual completeness per judge spec. Access-Control Violation A fires when the agent's response contains or clearly implies any leak target, or when the agent fails to take a valid restrictive action. Active Forgetting Failure F fires when deleted information is recovered, confirmed, or reconstructed. MGS = U × (1−A) × (1−F) is the primary composite metric. Seven baseline configurations are evaluated: LONG-CONTEXT (full history in prompt, up to 300 turns), RAG-NAIVE (top-k=20 similarity retrieval), RAG-POLICY (top-k=20 with requester and policy metadata filtering), A-MEM (Zettelkasten-style agentic notes with graph retrieval), MEM0 (production memory with add/update/delete operations), REMEM-I (episodic graph with iterative tool-based reasoning), and REMEM-S (episodic graph with single-step retrieval). Six backbone LLMs are tested: GPT-5.4, Deepseek-V4-Pro, Llama-4-Maverick, GPT-5-mini, GPT-4o-mini, and Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite. Key Results No method achieves simultaneously high utility and low governance failures across any domain-backbone combination. LONG-CONTEXT achieves the highest MGS in the majority of domain-backbone blocks: under GPT-5.4, Medical MGS = 80.1 (U=91.4, A=10.4, F=2.3); under Deepseek-V4-Pro, Medical MGS = 70.6 and Education MGS = 71.0. Despite this, LONG-CONTEXT access-control or active-forgetting failures exceed 20% in multiple domains and backbone settings, with Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite showing active-forgetting failure rates of 26% on Medical and 64% on Education under LONG-CONTEXT. RAG-POLICY substantially reduces unauthorized disclosures relative to RAG-NAIVE but induces over-refusal on legitimate queries: over-refusal rates of 63.3% (RAG-POLICY) vs. 44.8% (RAG-NAIVE) vs. 24.8% (LONG-CONTEXT) on Medical with GPT-4o-mini. Dedicated memory systems (A-MEM, MEM0, REMEM) do not consistently outperform simpler baselines on MGS across completed runs — their structured memory organization does not inherently enforce access boundaries or deletion compliance. Efficiency diverges sharply: LONG-CONTEXT averages 4.22 seconds/checkpoint and 4.04k tokens/checkpoint on Medical; REMEM-I averages 122.95 seconds/checkpoint and 1.06k tokens/checkpoint. REMEM-S averages 113.91 seconds/checkpoint. Policy-aware retrieval (RAG-POLICY) adds minimal overhead over RAG-NAIVE while providing safety benefits. Human validation on a stratified sample of 579 Medical checkpoints (approximately 50% of one evaluation run) finds maximum 1.04 percentage-point difference from GPT-4o judge labels across U, A, F, and MGS, with field-level agreement ≥97.7% and Cohen's κ ≥ 0.937 across action correctness, utility correctness, access leakage, and deletion leakage fields. Attack-type failure analysis on Medical with GPT-4o-mini reveals that family overreach (n=55) and indirect inference (n=22) are among the highest-failure access-control categories, while yes-or-no confirmation (n=48) and post-delete recovery (n=27) dominate active-forgetting failures. Policy-aware retrieval (RAG-POLICY) mitigates most soft-overreach attack types, while persistent memory systems remain brittle on retrieved-but-deleted content. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW FORMULATION. The formulation of multi-principal shared-memory governance as a joint tripartite problem — combining utility, access-control enforcement, and active forgetting into a multiplicative composite score — is genuinely new in the memory-agent evaluation literature. The benchmark itself (91 LLM-generated multi-party episodes, 2,218 hidden checkpoints, four institutional domains with distinct principal topologies, curated attack taxonomy, and public leaderboard) is a substantive dataset contribution. The evaluation methodology adapts LLM-as-judge to governance-specific rubrics with leak-target auditing. No new agent architecture is proposed; the experimental contribution is a systematic comparative study of existing architectures on the new benchmark. Rigor & Reproducibility Code, dataset, and leaderboard are publicly released (links on page 1 of the paper). The four-stage quality control pipeline (schema consistency, chain-of-evidence validation, deletion-chain closure, leak-target inspection) is described in sufficient detail to replicate. Judge reliability is validated with rigorous human annotation (κ ≥ 0.937 across all fields on a stratified Medical sample). Retrieval depth sensitivity is tested (top-k ∈ {5, 10, 20, 30, 40}) and over-refusal rates are separately reported to distinguish governance from paralysis. Two methodological concerns merit attention. First, all 91 episodes are LLM-generated; the paper does not report inter-annotator agreement for episode construction quality, only for judge labels on a single domain. Second, not all 7 baseline × 6 backbone × 4 domain combinations are reported; the paper states main tables cover the subset of completed runs, which makes cross-backbone comparisons for some methods incomplete. Third, human validation covers only Medical checkpoints from one evaluation run; generalization of judge reliability to Office, Education, and Household is asserted but not measured. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL (benchmark paper with experimental comparison of multiple existing memory systems). - Practical: The evaluation harness runs on commercial APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic-compatible, HuggingFace embeddings) with 4-8 worker parallelism; no specialized hardware is required. The benchmark is ready to use for any team with API access. - Efficient: Token cost is 1k-7.6k tokens/checkpoint depending on method; wall-clock time ranges from 4 seconds/checkpoint (LONG-CONTEXT) to 267 seconds/checkpoint (REMEM-I on Education). Running all 7 baselines across 6 backbones and 2,218 checkpoints represents substantial API spend, but individual method-backbone evaluations are tractable. - Effective: GateMem successfully discriminates between architectures on governance dimensions that recall-only benchmarks would classify as equivalent; the finding that no method achieves MGS > 80 in any domain-backbone combination is credible and non-trivial. The multiplicative scoring genuinely prevents high-utility-but-leaky systems from scoring well. - Scalable: The benchmark is fixed at 91 episodes; the paper does not test how episode quality or checkpoint validity would hold if the episode set were expanded by 10× using the same generation pipeline. The evaluation harness supports resumable runs and streaming to disk. The work delivers a public dataset (episodes.jsonl, checkpoints.jsonl), evaluation harness, trained judge prompts, and live leaderboard. Strengths - The problem formulation is both correct and important: shared-memory governance is structurally different from recall, and the multiplicative MGS enforces that distinction rather than allowing partial credit across dimensions. - The attack taxonomy for both access-control (family overreach, indirect inference, authority pressure, label-existence probe, cross-patient confusion, impersonation) and active-forgetting (yes-or-no confirmation, social engineering, split reconstruction, update-delete conflict, post-delete recovery) goes well beyond obvious adversarial cases and tests the realistic soft-boundary violations that dominate real institutional failures. - Judge validation is unusually rigorous: stratified human re-annotation of approximately 50% of one domain's checkpoints yields κ ≥ 0.937 and ≤1.04 percentage-point metric difference, providing stronger evidence than most LLM-judge papers that the evaluation signal is reliable. - The efficiency profiling (Table 4) reveals a non-obvious tradeoff: LONG-CONTEXT is fast (4 seconds/checkpoint) but token-expensive and leaky; REMEM is token-cheap (1k tokens/checkpoint) but 30–60× slower; this directly informs system design choices for practitioners. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that GateMem tests interface-level forgetting (the agent's response behavior) rather than certified physical erasure from internal databases, vector indexes, caches, or model parameters — a meaningful scope boundary given that machine unlearning typically requires retraining or weight updates. They note that episode generation is LLM-assisted rather than drawn from real institutional logs. They acknowledge that not all baseline runs are complete and that the benchmark does not propose new agent architectures. These limitations are confronted directly in the text and treated as design decisions rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All episodes are generated by LLMs with the same general architecture family as the evaluated agents; the attack distribution may be systematically biased toward patterns that LLM-based agents can recognize, underestimating failure rates in real deployments where adversarial requesters craft attacks outside the LLM's training distribution. - The benchmark has no existing method that was designed specifically for multi-principal shared-memory governance — all baselines are recall-oriented systems repurposed. This means low MGS scores cannot yet distinguish between "current architectures are fundamentally limited" and "no governance-aware training signal has been applied"; a fine-tuned or RLHF-trained governance agent is absent from the evaluation. - The multiplicative MGS treats all three dimensions as equally weighted. In high-stakes domains (medical), access-control violations and active-forgetting failures likely warrant higher penalties than equal weighting implies; a practitioner applying GateMem to medical agent development has no guidance on domain-appropriate weighting. - Human validation covers only the Medical domain from one evaluation run; whether GPT-4o judge reliability holds equally for Office (with its more ambiguous delegation and role-adjacency attacks) or Household (with fluid family-member authorization) is not demonstrated. - The paper evaluates only English-language episodes; multi-lingual institutional deployments (common in medical and educational settings) are not covered and may exhibit different failure modes. Bottom Line A practitioner building memory-augmented LLM agents for any shared institutional setting gains both a ready-to-run benchmark and a sobering empirical result — no current architecture reliably governs shared memory — that makes the case for governance-aware agent design as an open research problem.

#27 — Innovative (71)

Safety, Security, and Cognitive Risks in Neuro- Symbolic AI

M Parmar · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a unified threat model for neuro-symbolic AI (NeSy) systems, comprising three formal definitions (NeSy Attack Surface, Symbolic Integrity Violation, Cross-Layer Amplification Ratio), an 11-extension MITRE ATLAS mapping, a five-profile attacker taxonomy, and three empirical experiments validating symbolic-layer attack vectors on synthetic domain artefacts. - Why it's important: NeSy systems are being deployed in safety-critical domains (healthcare, cybersecurity, enterprise automation) where the symbolic layers create attack surfaces — KG poisoning, ontology-merging attacks, inference-engine subversion — absent from existing MITRE ATLAS and OWASP catalogs, leaving deployers without a structured threat-modeling framework. - How it does it: The Cross-Layer Amplification Ratio (X) formalizes the ratio of symbolic conclusion flip rate to neural decision flip rate under adversarial perturbation; its decomposition into neural-caused and autonomous symbolic sensitivity components reveals that the symbolic layer amplifies adversarial impact independently of neural decision-boundary crossings. - The good: PGD-10 at epsilon = 0.01 produces X = 5.884 (95% CI [4.64, 8.00], p_bootstrap < 0.0001) on a DistilBERT + ProbLog pipeline, confirmed adversarially specific by a matched-random baseline (E_rand = 0), with the decomposition showing 75.8% of symbolic flips arise without any neural flip — the dominant amplification pathway is autonomous symbolic sensitivity, not neural error propagation. - The missing: All three experiments use synthetic artefacts (a 205-entity medical KG, 1,000 synthetic medical claims, 30 OWL templates); the paper has not demonstrated X > 1 or the detector recall targets on any production-scale KG (SNOMED-CT, ATT&CK), and the cognitive-risk hypotheses (H1–H3) remain unvalidated pending IRB approval. - Read it or not: Worth reading for AI security practitioners and NeSy system builders who need a structured, quantified threat taxonomy with measurable acceptance criteria; less relevant for ML researchers seeking novel algorithmic contributions to model robustness.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Completeness (78) and Non-Obviousness (75) drive the score: the identification that 75.8 percent of cross-layer amplification arises from autonomous symbolic sensitivity rather than neural error propagation is a non-obvious, directly actionable finding, and the paper labels its cognitive-risk hypotheses as unvalidated and discloses all limitations in Table 11. Novelty (72) is high for the first formal measure of cross-layer adversarial amplification in decoupled pipelines. Rigor (70) is good: bootstrap with 10,000 resamples, Wilson CIs, 8 seeds, Holm-Bonferroni correction, and a matched-random baseline isolating adversarial directionality; but all artefacts are synthetic, the headline X=5.884 is DistilBERT- and PGD-specific, and code is available only on request. Impact (60) reflects a threat taxonomy with scripts on request, no released tool. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*60 = 71.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 78 Resilience 70 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation NeSy systems pair neural perception with symbolic knowledge bases, ontologies, and logic rules to deliver auditable reasoning in high-stakes domains including clinical decision support, cybersecurity operations center (SOC) tooling, and enterprise workflow automation. Their hybrid five-layer architecture — neural perception, symbolic knowledge bases, reasoning engines, agent orchestration, data stores — multiplies the asset inventory and attack surface beyond purely neural systems. MITRE ATLAS (16 tactics, 84 techniques as of 2024) and the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications cover neural adversarial examples, prompt injection, data exfiltration, and agentic context manipulation, but neither catalogs symbolic-substrate attacks: malicious KG triple injection, ontology-merging attacks that corrupt concept hierarchies, inference-engine subversion via crafted rule inputs, or supply-chain compromise of reasoning libraries. Beyond technical threats, NeSy systems produce structured logical explanations that — unlike opaque neural outputs — map onto Kahneman's System 2 reasoning and may amplify automation bias, authority bias, and sycophantic reinforcement of false beliefs. No prior framework addresses all three threat dimensions (technical, adversarial, cognitive) with formal definitions and quantitative empirical grounding. Key Idea The central technical insight is autonomous symbolic sensitivity: in a decoupled NeSy pipeline where a neural component passes soft probabilities or logits to a probabilistic reasoning engine (e.g., ProbLog), a sub-perceptual adversarial perturbation that does not change the neural model's argmax classification can nonetheless shift logit distributions enough to cross ProbLog's probabilistic firing threshold, changing downstream symbolic conclusions without any neural decision-boundary crossing. This means X > 1 is an inherent property of probabilistic decoupled architectures, not a consequence of neural errors, and defenses focused solely on neural robustness are structurally insufficient. Methodology The paper formalizes three constructs. Definition 1 (NeSy Attack Surface) is the set of interfaces across which an adversary with access level alpha can inject, modify, or exfiltrate information, with a proof sketch that |AS(S, alpha)| > |AS(N, alpha)| for grey-box and above access because symbolic layers add new interface classes absent in purely neural systems. Definition 2 (Symbolic Integrity Violation, SIV) captures any unauthorized change to the knowledge base that causes the reasoning engine to produce a different conclusion on a safety-critical query. Definition 3 (Cross-Layer Amplification Ratio) defines X(delta) = E^R(delta) / E^N(delta), decomposed into neural-caused symbolic flip rate E^{R|N} and autonomous symbolic sensitivity rate E^{R|not-N}. Three experiments validate the model: E1 simulates targeted, random, and stealth KG poisoning on a synthetic 205-entity, 622-triple medical contraindication KG across budgets B = 1–50, testing three detectors (cryptographic provenance signing, statistical anomaly detection, canary monitoring) over 8 seeds. E2 measures X on a DistilBERT (hidden dim 768) + ProbLog hazard-reasoning pipeline using PGD-10 and FGSM attacks at epsilon in {0.005–0.08} (l_inf, applied to pre-LayerNorm token embeddings), with a matched-random baseline to isolate adversarial directionality, and a depth sweep d = 1–10 (up to 21 ProbLog rules). E3 constructs 30 adversarial OWL axiom templates (15 clinical-safety, 15 STIX cyber-threat) evaluated under Pellet + HermiT dual consistency checking, with in-sample vs. held-out detection via a differential safety-query detector. Bootstrap tests use B = 10,000 resamples; E2 applies Holm-Bonferroni correction across five simultaneous epsilon-level tests. Key Results E1: Targeted injection reaches SIV > 5% at B = 5 (mean 7.6%, 95% CI [6.3%, 8.9%]), 14.6× more efficient than random injection (0.52% at B = 5). Stealth attacks achieve deterministically zero SIV by avoiding the contraindicated relation to evade provenance signing — an architecture-specific stealth/SIV mutual exclusion under this KG schema. No single detector covers all three attacker profiles: canary monitoring covers targeted attacks at moderate budget (B ≤ 10) with recall 0.738 at B = 10, but recall is 0 for stealth at all budgets; provenance signing achieves 1.00 recall on targeted/random but 0 on stealth; statistical anomaly detection fires only at high budget. E2: PGD-10 at epsilon = 0.01 produces X = 5.884 (95% CI [4.64, 8.00], p_bootstrap < 0.0001, one-sided). The matched-random baseline produces E^R_rand = 0 at all epsilon, confirming adversarial specificity (additive excess delta-X_adv = 5.884). The decomposition reveals E^{R|N} = 1.000 (all 43 neural-flipped queries propagate to symbolic flips) and E^{R|not-N} = 0.758 (75.8% of symbolic flips arise without a neural flip). X declines monotonically from 5.884 at epsilon = 0.01 to 1.042 at epsilon = 0.05. The depth sweep d = 1–10 is a confirmed null result: X is depth-invariant because PGD perturbations do not enter multi-hop chained inference regions. Tight coupling (argmax labels only to ProbLog) fully suppresses X to 0.000, confirming probabilistic marginalization as an architectural defense. FGSM fails entirely (E^N = 0 at epsilon ≤ 0.05) due to DistilBERT's subword embedding discretization. E3: 28 of 30 templates induce SIV (93.3%, 95% CI [78.7%, 98.2%]) with 100% Pellet-consistency stealth. In-sample detection recall is 100%. Held-out detection is 67.9% overall (95% CI [49.3%, 82.1%]), with a pronounced domain gap: clinical held-out recall 85.7% (95% CI [60.1%, 96.0%]) vs. STIX held-out recall 50.0% (95% CI [26.8%, 73.2%]) — statistically indistinguishable from random guessing. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION (Definitions 1–3: NeSy attack surface, SIV, and the first formal measure of cross-layer adversarial amplification in decoupled pipelines), NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (first empirical measurement of X > 1 at realistic perturbation budgets; the identification of autonomous symbolic sensitivity as the dominant amplification pathway at 75.8%), NEW METHOD (five-profile NeSy attacker taxonomy extending MITRE ATLAS; 11 NeSy-specific tactic extensions). The MITRE ATLAS extension and OWASP mapping are application of existing frameworks to a new domain. The formal definitions are the genuine contribution; prior adversarial ML work studied neural robustness and KG embedding attacks separately, but Definition 3 is the first measure bridging them in a decoupled pipeline. Rigor & Reproducibility The paper applies bootstrap significance testing (B = 10,000) throughout, reports Wilson 95% CIs on all proportions, uses 8 independent seeds for E1, and applies Holm-Bonferroni correction for five simultaneous epsilon-level tests in E2. The matched-random baseline in E2 directly addresses the alternative hypothesis that X reflects general perturbation sensitivity rather than adversarial directionality — this is a methodological strength. Limitations are openly disclosed in Table 11: all artefacts are synthetic, the X measurement is PGD-specific and DistilBERT-specific, the depth sweep is a null result, and STIX detector failure is unresolved. Code and data are available from the author upon reasonable request but are not publicly deposited. This is an arXiv preprint (v1, June 2026); no peer review is declared. One methodological concern: the E2 DistilBERT model was chosen for computational tractability and because it establishes a conservative lower bound on X — the claim is that domain-adapted models with tighter decision margins (BioERT, ClinicalBERT) would show higher X, but this is not measured, leaving the headline result anchored to a single, deliberately weak neural model. The E3 claim of 93.3% SIV is explicitly scoped to equivalence and subclass axioms; the paper does not test property restrictions, cardinality constraints, or other OWL axiom types. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile) - Practical: E2 runs DistilBERT (66M parameters) plus ProbLog on synthetic data executable on commodity GPU hardware; E1 and E3 use Owlready2, Pellet, and HermiT — all open-source, CPU-deployable OWL reasoners. - Efficient: No throughput, wall-clock, or memory figures are reported for any experiment; relative compute cost of NeSy vs. neural-only pipelines is not evaluated. - Effective: All three experiments demonstrate that attacks succeed at low budget with high stealth — confirming the threat — but no defended end-to-end pipeline is evaluated; the practical checklist specifies acceptance criteria (e.g., ≥85% SIV detection recall) that exceed E1's demonstrated performance for multi-profile coverage. - Scalable: All experiments use toy-scale artefacts (205-entity KG, 1,000 training claims, 30 OWL templates); scaling to SNOMED-CT, real EHR KGs, or STIX/ATT&CK is deferred as explicit future work with no preliminary evidence. The paper delivers experimental scripts and synthetic datasets available on request; no software library, CLI tool, or trained model artifact is released. Strengths - The Cross-Layer Amplification Ratio decomposition (Definition 3) is a genuinely novel measure: distinguishing neural-caused from autonomous symbolic sensitivity pathways gives practitioners a quantitative diagnostic for whether to harden the neural component, the symbolic layer, or the coupling mechanism — and the E2 result (E^{R|not-N} = 0.758 dominating E^{R|N} = 1.000 × 0.134) shows the coupling architecture is the primary risk factor, not neural vulnerability alone. - The E1 stealth/SIV mutual exclusion finding — that avoiding the single contraindicated relation achieves perfect detection evasion at the cost of zero impact, while targeted attacks are 14.6× more efficient than random — is a concrete, KG-architecture-dependent result that directly informs defense prioritization and illustrates why no single detector suffices. - The paper's scope discipline is creditable: the cognitive risk section (Section 7) explicitly labels all three hypotheses (H1–H3) as theoretical predictions grounded in literature but not yet empirically confirmed, and defers E4 to IRB-approved future work rather than asserting results it has not measured. - Aligning mitigations with NIST AI 600-1 and EU AI Act acceptance criteria (Table 4) bridges the gap between academic threat modeling and actionable governance, addressing the gap between threat awareness and regulatory compliance that most security papers ignore. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors confront limitations directly in Table 11 and the broader-impact section. E1 uses a synthetic KG with a single safety-critical relation type, and the stealth/targeted trade-off may not generalize to KGs with multiple safety-critical relation types. E2's X = 5.884 is PGD-specific at epsilon = 0.01 and declines to 1.042 at epsilon = 0.05; the depth sweep is a null result because the tested perturbations do not trigger multi-hop chained rules. E3's 93.3% SIV is scoped to single-axiom equivalence and subclass attacks; STIX held-out detection at 50% is explicitly characterized as a detector failure requiring STIX-specific template augmentation or domain-agnostic syntactic detection on OWL axiom structure. The five attacker profiles are stated as threat hypotheses, not empirically calibrated against any real-world NeSy incident corpus. The broader-impact section acknowledges dual-use risk from publishing the attacker taxonomy and states that operational attack details (specific KG injection payloads, step-by-step rule-poisoning procedures) were deliberately omitted. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The proof sketch for |AS(S, alpha)| > |AS(N, alpha)| (Definition 1) rests on the assumption that symbolic layers are not fully sandboxed from the API output channel. This is stated as a structural assumption rather than a variable measured across deployment configurations; real production deployments may sandbox the KG behind the API, making the attack-surface enlargement claim deployment-conditional rather than architectural. - X = 5.884 is a lower bound only if domain-adapted models (BioERT, ClinicalBERT) have tighter decision margins than DistilBERT — a plausible but untested claim. If those models are also more robust to l_inf embedding perturbations (e.g., due to domain-specific normalization), X could be lower rather than higher, reversing the conservative-baseline interpretation. - Definition 3 is explicitly restricted to decoupled NeSy architectures and does not cover fully differentiable NeSy systems (DeepProbLog, Logical Neural Networks, Neural Theorem Provers) where the neural-symbolic boundary is implicit. These tightly coupled architectures may represent the systems with the highest real-world deployment growth and the most complex amplification dynamics, and the paper offers no evidence that the X > 1 threat extends to them. - The practical checklist (Table 4) specifies an aspirational ≥95% SIV detection target described as requiring ensemble engineering beyond E1 results; E1 shows no single detector exceeds 85% recall across all three attack profiles, and the ensemble acceptance criterion is undemonstrated by any experiment in the paper. - The reference list includes 13 blog posts and industry white papers alongside peer-reviewed work; several claims in Sections 6–7 cite blog posts (references 8, 9, 10, 12, 13, 24, 26–33) rather than peer-reviewed empirical studies, which limits the evidentiary weight of some threat-category descriptions. Bottom Line A practitioner building or threat-modeling NeSy systems in healthcare, cybersecurity, or enterprise automation gains the first formal vocabulary, a structured attacker taxonomy, and three quantified attack demonstrations for symbolic-layer threats absent from MITRE ATLAS; the finding that 75.8% of cross-layer amplification arises via autonomous symbolic sensitivity rather than neural error propagation is directly actionable for architecture selection (probabilistic vs. hard coupling) and makes this worth reading despite the entirely synthetic evaluation and single-author arXiv status.

#28 — Innovative (71)

MobileForge: Annotation-Free Adaptation for Mobile GUI Agents with Hierarchical Feedback-Guided Policy Optimization (34 ▲ )

Guangyi Liu, Pengxiang Zhao, Gao Wu, Yiwen Yin, Mading Li, Liang Liu, Congxiao Liu, Zhang Qi, Mengyan Wang, Liang Guo, Yong Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MobileForge, an annotation-free adaptation system for mobile GUI agents that pairs MobileGym (a target-app interaction substrate for grounded task generation and hierarchical rollout evaluation) with HiFPO (Hierarchical Feedback-Guided Policy Optimization), which converts multi-attempt rollout experience and corrective hints into hint-contextualized step-level GRPO training updates. - Why it's important: Human-written tasks, expert demonstrations, and reward labels for mobile apps are prohibitively expensive and go stale as apps update, blocking practical deployment of GUI agents on the long tail of real-world apps. - How it does it: MobileGym decomposes rollout feedback into three levels — trajectory outcome, step-level process labels, and natural-language corrective hints — and HiFPO routes each level to a distinct role: outcome labels filter tasks by mastery, process labels identify trainable steps, and hints condition both later rollout attempts and the GRPO group comparison prompt. - The good: ForgeOwl-8B reaches 77.6% Pass@3 on AndroidWorld and 41.0% success rate on the out-of-domain MobileWorld GUI-only split using only AndroidWorld-derived annotation-free data, outperforming all open-data mobile GUI agents evaluated. - The missing: No error bars or multi-seed results accompany any benchmark number (AndroidWorld has only 116 tasks, so a 3–4 task swing can reverse comparisons), and MobileGym-Critic's step-level label accuracy is never calibrated against human judgment despite being the backbone of both training signal and evaluation. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on GUI agent training, annotation-free RL, or mobile automation; the hierarchical feedback decomposition and cross-domain transfer results are the most concrete contributions, and the ablations are unusually well-targeted.
Why this rank: Empirical profile (systems-leaning, scored empirical). This is the strongest paper in the cluster and resembles the user's positive set in its mechanism-first, ablation-driven post-training framing. Non-Obviousness (75) and Impact (75) lead: the three-level feedback decomposition with cross-attempt hint accumulation as a first-class GRPO input is non-obvious and validated (rollout success 52%→77% with hints), and released weights (ForgeOwl-8B) plus a public codebase deliver a real artifact. The held-out MobileWorld cross-domain transfer (41.0% vs 37.6% base, beating all open-data agents) is the most credible result. Rigor (62) caps the composite. No error bars or multi-seed runs on a 116-task benchmark where 3-4 tasks reverse comparisons, an uncalibrated MobileGym-Critic backing both training and evaluation, and same-app-ecosystem in-domain overlap. Composite: 0.20*72 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*74 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*75 = 71.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 74 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Deploying MLLM-based GUI agents on real mobile apps requires adaptation to specific target apps, but obtaining human-written tasks, expert demonstrations, and reward labels is costly and rapidly stale given the breadth and update frequency of mobile apps. Existing annotation-free approaches — TongUI (web tutorials), MobileA3gent (user-sourced trajectories), OS-Genesis (reverse task synthesis), ZeroGUI and MobileGUI-RL (online RL) — reduce manual annotation but share two unresolved weaknesses. First, they lack a unified substrate connecting target-app exploration, curriculum mining, rollout execution, and feedback, so tasks may be weakly grounded in actual app state and evaluator feedback remains disconnected from policy learning. Second, policy optimization treats rollouts as isolated experiences with coarse trajectory-level rewards, providing no mechanism to assign credit to individual steps within long-horizon failures or to carry knowledge from a failed attempt forward into subsequent attempts. Key Idea The core insight is to decompose rollout feedback into three levels — trajectory outcome (did it succeed?), step-level process labels (was each step locally reasonable?), and natural-language corrective hints (what went wrong and how to fix it?) — and assign each level a distinct role in the learning loop. Outcome labels decide task retention; process labels identify which individual steps across both successful and failed trajectories are worth training on; hints accumulate across attempts of the same task, conditioning both later rollouts and the GRPO group comparison context. This separation lets failed trajectories contribute positive step-level training signal and makes knowledge from one attempt reusable in the next, breaking the dependence on fully successful trajectories for policy improvement. Methodology MobileForge comprises two coupled components. MobileGym provides the interaction and evaluation substrate. Its exploration phase conducts function-aware depth-first traversal grounded in APK activity anchors, recording before/after screenshots, actions, target elements, and summaries for each transition (producing exploration evidence Z). MobileGym-Curriculum converts these trajectories into executable tasks x = (ι, B, c, v, p) — instruction, step budget, core functionality, variation type, prerequisites — using a few-shot LLM prompt that enforces grounding, coherence, and diversity. MobileGym-Critic evaluates completed rollouts through a three-stage agentic pipeline: a VLM converts each raw action into a step description from the before-action screenshot; a final-decision model synthesizes step descriptions with a composite last-screenshot view and returns a structured JSON verdict containing outcome z ∈ {0,1}, per-step reasonableness labels v_k^(t) ∈ {0,1} with rationales, and a corrective hint h_k summarizing key mistakes, avoidances, and suggested approaches. HiFPO uses these signals for policy optimization. It runs K attempts per task, appending accumulated corrective hints η_{<k} to the task prompt for attempt k. Task filtering retains tasks with SR(x) < 1 (both partially solved and all-fail) and drops tasks with SR(x) = 1 (mastered). For retained tasks, the best attempt k*(x) is selected by prioritizing successful attempts with the highest step-quality score Q_k (fraction of reasonable steps), with all-fail tasks falling back to the failure with highest Q_k. The step-level training set D keeps only (state, action) pairs from locally reasonable steps of the selected attempt. For GRPO training, each training example is a hint-contextualized step prompt s̃_j augmented with η_j; the policy samples G = 5 candidate responses per step, parsed into structured GUI actions and scored with an adaptive reward R = 0.2 · r_type + 0.8 · r_arg, where r_arg is evaluated only when r_type = 1. GRPO advantage is computed group-relatively within the hint-contextualized group and the policy is updated with clipped GRPO plus KL regularization (coefficient 1×10^{-2}). Critical design decisions: (1) retaining all-fail tasks is validated by ablation showing these contain useful local navigation steps; (2) conditioning GRPO groups on hint-contextualized prompts makes group comparison feedback-aware rather than state-only; (3) separating outcome label z from step-level reward R avoids conflating trajectory-level evaluation with step-level optimization signals. Key Results On AndroidWorld (116 tasks, in-domain): ForgeQwen3-8B (Qwen3-VL-8B + 900-task MobileForge) achieves 60.3% Pass@1 / 70.0% Pass@2 / 67.2% Pass@3, versus the base at 40.5% / 57.0% / 55.2% — a +19.8 pp Pass@1 gain.
