The principal novelty is the theoretical unification of TF-CM and SF-DMD under the forward-reverse complementarity framework applied to the AR causal setting, plus the first implementation of TF-sCM/MeanFlow for AR video distillation enabled by a custom-mask FlashAttention-2 JVP Triton kernel. The staged TF→TF-CM→SF-DMD pipeline is an extension of the rCM principle to a new domain (causal AR video) with non-trivial adaptations; the infrastructure recipe (FSDP2 + Ulysses CP + SAC + JVP + KV cache with explicit compatibility design) is a genuine engineering contribution rather than a standard assembly of existing tools. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and meaningful: Table 5 systematically compares 6 initialization strategies for SF-DMD in both generation regimes, with full training curves (Fig. 7), and Fig. 6 provides convergence curves for TF-dCM vs TF-sCM. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple random seeds are reported for any experiment — all comparisons are single-run. The Theoretical appendix (A) rigorously proves that TrigFlow-sCM and RF-sCM induce different MSE objectives and JVP behaviors in finite precision, supporting the RF-native design choice with analysis rather than empiricism alone. Code is available at https://github.com/NVlabs/rcm. Training uses synthetic data generated by the bidirectional Wan2.1-14B teacher (100-step Euler, shift 3.0, CFG 5.0) — no real video data enters training after Stage 1. The Cosmos 3 results are qualitative only (no numbers). The paper is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2606.25473v1, 24 Jun 2026) from NVIDIA and collaborators; results are vendor self-reported and not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability Profile: SYSTEMS (built infrastructure evaluated by deployment). - Practical: Inference runs on a single H100 GPU at 15.9 FPS for 1-step frame-wise generation; training requires multi-GPU with FSDP2 and context parallelism, though exact GPU counts per configuration are not reported. - Efficient: The 1-step model reduces NFE per chunk from 51 (50-step + 1 clean-context encode) to 2, yielding a 22× throughput improvement (0.18 → 15.9 FPS for the 1.3B model, or 0.72 → 15.9 FPS vs the bidirectional 1.3B). The TF-sCM JVP Triton kernel matches TF-dCM with standard FlashAttention-2 in per-iteration speed but lags FlashAttention-3/4 by an unstated margin. - Effective: The distilled 1.3B student surpasses the 50-step 14B bidirectional teacher on VBench-T2V (84.63 vs 83.35); qualitative inspection reveals that DF/TF initializations with higher VBench scores can produce over-smoothed textures, suggesting the metric does not fully capture perceptual quality. - Scalable: All student experiments use Wan2.1-1.3B; no scaling experiments to larger student models are conducted. Infrastructure components (FSDP2, CP) are designed for larger models but scaling behavior of the distillation recipe is unvalidated. The work delivers a software artifact (open codebase), a reusable Triton JVP kernel for custom-mask FlashAttention-2, and demonstrated trained models for streaming video and interactive world modeling. Strengths - The forward-reverse divergence interpretation of TF-CM and SF-DMD unifies what were previously independent empirical design choices under a single theoretical framework, and the analogy to rCM is precise rather than metaphorical. - TF-sCM achieves 10× faster convergence than TF-dCM (same quality within 1-2k vs 10k+ iterations), a practically significant result for expensive video-transformer training pipelines. - The ablation across 6 initialization strategies in two generation regimes with training curves is the most systematic comparison of SF-DMD initialization in the AR video distillation literature, providing clear guidance for practitioners. - The compatibility design section (SAC × FlexAttention, SAC × self-forcing, JVP × FSDP2, JVP × Ulysses CP, KV cache × Ulysses CP) addresses engineering conflicts that would otherwise block practitioners from combining these components, and the open codebase makes these solutions actionable. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly and credibly acknowledge four limitations: (1) frame-wise SF-DMD with 4-step rollouts develops camera drift and collapses after ~1k training iterations; (2) the best TF-CM initialization (TF-sCM) does not always yield the best final SF-DMD model — TF-dCM can be more stable during SF-DMD refinement, achieving a higher peak; (3) fully joint optimization (as in rCM) lowers the VBench ceiling in the causal setting due to distribution gap between causal and bidirectional teachers, forcing a staged pipeline; (4) the Triton JVP kernel lags FlashAttention-3/4 in per-iteration speed. Analyst-Identified Gaps - VBench-T2V is the sole quantitative metric across all 30+ comparisons. The paper itself demonstrates (Fig. 8) that DF/TF initializations score higher on VBench while producing visibly over-smoothed outputs, which is a direct argument that VBench is insufficient for quality discrimination here. The absence of a human preference study is a significant methodological gap given this known limitation. - All student experiments use Wan2.1-1.3B. No ablation or experiment tests whether the recipe degrades or holds for larger students (7B, 14B). The claim of a "scalable" recipe is infrastructure-supported but empirically unvalidated. - The Cosmos 3 interactive world model section reports only qualitative demonstrations; no action-conditioning accuracy, temporal consistency, or user study metric is provided, making the claim that Causal-rCM "enables" an interactive world model an illustration rather than a measured result. - Training exclusively on teacher-generated synthetic data means any systematic bias in the 100-step teacher (color statistics, motion patterns, texture detail) propagates to the student. VBench scores on data drawn from the same synthetic distribution may overestimate quality on real-world video inputs. - The ablation compares initialization strategies for Stage 3 (SF-DMD) but does not test skipping Stage 1 (TF causal adaptation) entirely — it is unclear whether starting Stage 2 from the original bidirectional model directly would produce comparable results, which would simplify the pipeline. Bottom Line A practitioner building AR video diffusion distillation pipelines gains concrete, actionable value from this paper: a justified training recipe, a 10× faster consistency distillation stage, a reusable JVP kernel, and a detailed compatibility guide for large-scale distributed training — but the exclusive reliance on VBench-T2V and single-scale experiments mean the quality claims should be treated as a lower bound on what independent evaluation would show.
Recommended 2606.25473 PDF
#34 — Innovative (70)

FLAT: Feedforward Latent Triangle Splatting for Geometrically Accurate Scene Generation (13 ▲ )

Orest Kupyn, Goutam Bhat, Philipp Henzler, Fabian Manhardt, Christian Rupprecht, Federico Tombari · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FLAT, a feedforward model that decodes triangle-splat scene representations directly from frozen video diffusion latents in a single forward pass, producing real-time-renderable, game-engine-compatible 3D scenes from a single input image. - Why it's important: All prior feedforward latent scene decoders (Wonderland, Lyra, Generative Gaussian Splatting) output volumetric 3D Gaussians, which lack well-defined surfaces and require complex, fragile post-processing to extract usable meshes, blocking direct use in graphics pipelines, physics simulators, and game engines. - How it does it: Two components unlock stable flat-primitive decoding from a compressed latent space: a ray-centered Cholesky-style triangle parameterization that predicts residual orientations around a ray-aligned frame to prevent degenerate triangles, and a product window function that routes gradients to all three triangle vertices during differentiable rasterization rather than only to the active edge. - The good: Triangle splats achieve a cosine surface-normal similarity of 0.853 on held-out views vs. 0.587 for 2DGS and 0.116 for 3DGS trained under identical conditions; opaque mesh conversion from triangles reaches 21.23 PSNR on RealEstate10K, more than 7 dB above GS2Mesh extraction from 3DGS (14.18 PSNR). - The missing: Evaluation covers only indoor room scans (RealEstate10K) and object-centric videos (DL3DV); performance on unbounded outdoor scenes, large-scale driving, or non-Euclidean environments is uncharacterized, and the PSNR metric systematically disadvantages triangles relative to the smoother Gaussian baseline. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building 3D content creation pipelines, simulation assets, or game-engine-ready scene generation — less critical if the application tolerates Gaussian representations without needing explicit surface geometry.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (a trained model evaluated by rendering and geometry quality). Rigor (70) anchors the score: the ablations isolate all three components and the global-rotation failure (PSNR<10) is convincing, but there are no error bars or seeds, baselines are reproduced rather than reimplemented, and no code/weights are confirmed in this preprint. Non-Obviousness (75) is high — the ray-centered Cholesky parameterization plus product window function is a non-trivial pair of fixes neither of which alone works. Novelty (70) is a new method, not a new formulation; the pipeline follows Wonderland/Lyra. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) are solid: the authors candidly flag the PSNR-vs-Gaussian metric bias and the normal-supervision confound that partly inflates the 0.853-vs-0.116 geometry gap. Practical Impact (55) is held back by single-H100 hardware, untested scale, and unconfirmed release. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*55 = 70.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Creating explorable 3D environments from a single image is central to mixed reality, robotics simulation, game asset creation, and autonomous driving. Video diffusion models encode strong multi-view priors but cannot directly supply real-time-renderable representations. Feedforward latent decoders (Wonderland, Lyra) address the render-time bottleneck by mapping diffusion latents to 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) parameters in one pass. However, 3D Gaussians are volumetric blobs without well-defined surface normals, which makes them ill-suited for physics simulation, standard rasterization pipelines, and downstream mesh export. Existing mesh-extraction methods (marching cubes, TSDF, GS2Mesh) applied to Gaussian outputs are sensitive to hyperparameters and fail under the sparse-view, small-scene-scale regime of feedforward generation. The gap addressed here is: can non-volumetric, surface-aligned triangle primitives be decoded stably from compressed video latents, providing geometric accuracy and pipeline compatibility without per-scene optimization? Key Idea The core insight is that feedforward triangle decoding requires two mutually reinforcing fixes: (1) expressing each triangle's shape and orientation in a locally ray-centered coordinate frame — using a lower-triangular (Cholesky-style) 2D shape matrix and residual rotations around a ray-aligned frame — so that the optimizer never encounters the orientation singularities that cause vanishing render support and gradient collapse; and (2) replacing the prior max-edge window function with a product-of-edge-responses formulation that distributes gradient signal to all three triangle vertices simultaneously rather than saturating on the nearest edge. Neither fix alone is sufficient; the ablation shows global rotation prediction leads to immediate model divergence (PSNR < 10), while reverting to the prior window function alone costs ~1.5 dB on RealEstate10K. Methodology FLAT augments a frozen, camera-conditioned video diffusion model (Uni3C, built on Wan-2.1) with a trainable scene decoder. At inference, Uni3C generates denoised video latents z ∈ R^{F'×C'×H'×W'} conditioned on the input image and a target camera trajectory expressed as per-pixel Plücker (RPPC) ray embeddings. The scene decoder — initialized from the Wan-2.1 VAE RGB decoder backbone (73.34M parameters) with camera conditioning injected via zero-initialized blocks — maps the fused video and camera latents to triangle-splat parameters for a 2×2 pixel region per decoder token. Each triangle splat is parameterized by: depth D placing the center at r_o + D·r_d on the anchor ray; a 2×2 lower-triangular shape matrix L (Cholesky decomposition) applied to a canonical equilateral triangle to produce the in-plane geometry while guaranteeing positive area; residual tilt and spin angles around the ray-aligned frame for stable 3D orientation; per-triangle color coefficients; smoothness σ; and opacity o ∈ [0,1]. The window function I_m(p) = (∏_{i=1}^3 clamp(u_{m,i}(p) + ε, 0, 1))^{σ_m} integrates contributions from all three edge responses with a small ε > 0 extending support beyond the exact triangle boundary to prevent zero-gradient regions. Final rendering uses standard alpha compositing in front-to-back depth order. Loss is a weighted sum: L = λ_rgb L_2 + λ_perc L_LPIPS + λ_D L_D + λ_N L_N + λ_O L_O, with λ_rgb=1.0, λ_perc=0.5, λ_D=0.01, λ_N=0.01, λ_O=0.001. Geometry supervision uses pseudo-ground-truth normals from NormalCrafter and depth from MiDaS. Training uses a four-stage progressive schedule on 8 H100 GPUs: 320p/17-view (20K iterations) → 320p/49-view (40K) → 640p/49-view (75K) → 768p/49-view (75K), totaling 210K iterations with AdamW at 1e-4. Training data mixes RealEstate10K, DL3DV (real), and 25K images from S3OD synthesized via Uni3C (synthetic). Two critical design decisions are well-justified: the Cholesky shape matrix prevents degenerate triangles by construction (positive diagonal elements enforced), and residual-around-ray orientation prediction is validated by ablation (global rotation causes divergence). The use of Wan-2.1 VAE decoder initialization rather than a scratch transformer is justified by transfer of local appearance priors but is not compared against an equally large scratch decoder. Key Results On RealEstate10K, FLAT with triangle splats achieves 21.45 PSNR / 0.710 SSIM / 0.245 LPIPS vs. prior state-of-the-art Lyra (21.79 / 0.752 / 0.219) and Bolt3D (21.54 / 0.747 / 0.234). The FLAT 3DGS variant under identical training reaches 22.39 / 0.762 / 0.203, beating all baselines on visual metrics and confirming the training pipeline quality. On DL3DV, FLAT triangles score 20.04 / 0.627 / 0.314 vs. Lyra's 20.09 / 0.583 / 0.313. Geometry quality (cosine normal similarity vs. Metric3D-v2 labels, averaged across both datasets): triangles 0.853, 2DGS 0.587, 3DGS 0.116. Mesh conversion quality on RealEstate10K: triangles 21.23 PSNR / 0.749 SSIM vs. GS2Mesh from 3DGS at 14.18 / 0.619 and TSDF from 2DGS at 15.89 / 0.633. Post-optimization (250–50 steps) improves triangle PSNR from 21.45 to 23.01 and SSIM from 0.710 to 0.790. Baselines (Wonderland, Bolt3D, Lyra) are recent (2025–2026) and competitive; Lyra uses a multi-view distillation strategy that increases pipeline complexity. The decoder runs at 2.70 FPS at 768p / 49 views on a single H100 GPU (9.8 TFLOPs, marginal relative to Uni3C generation cost). No stress-scale test (scenes beyond a single room or short trajectory) is reported. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine novelty is the combination of ray-centered Cholesky parameterization and product window function that makes feedforward flat-primitive decoding from video diffusion latents tractable for the first time. The finding that triangle splats produce substantially better surface geometry than Gaussians under identical training conditions (cosine similarity 0.853 vs. 0.116 for 3DGS) is a concrete empirical contribution. The decoder architecture is a transfer and modification of an existing VAE backbone rather than an original design, and the overall pipeline structure (frozen video diffusion + lightweight decoder) follows Wonderland/Lyra. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations in Table 3 isolate the three key components (rotation parameterization, window function, architecture) and all show meaningful deltas; the global-rotation ablation fails completely, providing strong evidence for the parameterization claim. Multiple datasets (RealEstate10K and DL3DV) are used. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed runs are reported. The geometry metric (NormalCrafter pseudo-GT + Metric3D-v2) introduces a bias toward models trained with normal supervision; 3DGS is trained without normal loss, so its 0.116 cosine similarity partly reflects loss setup rather than representation capacity. Lyra and Bolt3D are closed-source, so FLAT is evaluated against reproduced numbers following their protocol rather than directly reimplemented baselines. Code and model weights are not released in this preprint (project page at flat-splat.github.io exists but release status not stated in the paper). The paper is an arXiv preprint as of 23 Jun 2026; it has not undergone peer review. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (a trained model evaluated by rendering quality and deployed in a conversion pipeline). - Practical: The scene decoder runs on 8 H100 GPUs for training; inference requires a single H100 (or equivalent) to run the full Uni3C + FLAT pipeline, which is commercially available but not commodity hardware. - Efficient: The scene decoder consumes 9.8 TFLOPs and 73.34M parameters at 768p/49-view, described as a marginal fraction of Uni3C's generation cost; 250-step post-optimization adds latency but is optional. - Effective: Triangle splats outperform all prior methods on geometry quality (cosine similarity 0.853) and mesh conversion quality (+7 dB over GS2Mesh), while remaining within ~1.5 dB PSNR of the best visual-quality baseline (FLAT 3DGS) — the gap is partly a metric artifact given Gaussians optimize PSNR directly. - Scalable: Not tested at scale; the system targets single rooms or short camera trajectories (49-frame sequences at 768p). The authors explicitly note that large-scale persistent world generation requires integration with long video generation models, which is unaddressed here. The work delivers a trained model (architecture described in detail) and the project page suggests a demo; no public code or weight release is confirmed in the paper. Strengths - The ray-centered Cholesky parameterization plus product window function combination is a concrete, non-trivial engineering contribution: the ablation shows each component is individually necessary, and the global-rotation baseline fails catastrophically, establishing the difficulty of the problem. - The geometry quality gap between triangle splats and all Gaussian variants under identical training is large and consistent across two datasets (0.853 vs. 0.587 vs. 0.116 cosine similarity), making the surface-accuracy advantage credible rather than cherry-picked. - The fair comparison protocol — training 3DGS, 2DGS, and triangle variants with identical hyperparameters — is the first controlled representation comparison in feedforward latent scene decoding and isolates representation effects from pipeline differences. - Pipeline flexibility (decoder-swap design): attaching the FLAT decoder to any Wan-2.1 variant (text-to-video, video-to-video, long video, etc.) without retraining is a practical advantage with demonstrated text-to-3D qualitative results. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront several limitations. Triangle splats are not optimized for pixel-level PSNR metrics (Gaussians have a natural advantage). Thin elongated structures, reflections, semi-transparent regions, and fine high-frequency details remain failure modes. Opaque mesh conversion produces geometry that is sparser than a clean watertight mesh; local connectivity can be incomplete and surfaces can be oversharpened or fragmented. The model trains on relatively little data compared to modern video generation systems and may improve with dataset scaling. FLAT targets a single generated scene or short camera path, not a persistent large-scale explorable world. These are confronted directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The geometry evaluation relies on NormalCrafter pseudo-GT normals for supervision and Metric3D-v2 for evaluation. Both are estimated rather than ground-truth, and the FLAT triangle model is explicitly trained to predict normals while 3DGS is not. This conflates representation quality with loss design, making the 0.853 vs. 0.116 comparison partially unfair to 3DGS. - Lyra, Bolt3D, and Wonderland are reproduced via an evaluation protocol rather than reimplemented and retrained, so any dataset or camera annotation differences may affect the comparison. - No inference latency breakdown is given for the full pipeline (Uni3C generation + FLAT decoding + mesh conversion); the 2.70 FPS figure covers only scene decoding, and Uni3C's generation cost dominates in practice. - Scale and diversity: both evaluation datasets are indoor/static scenes with limited camera motion. No outdoor, unbounded, or dynamic-scene results are reported, and the synthetic data supplement (S3OD via Uni3C) is object-centric. Generalization to scenes outside this distribution is unverified. - The paper compares to Lyra and Bolt3D on held-out test splits but does not evaluate on the same model's own training distribution, so Goodhart-style overfitting to the training pipeline cannot be fully ruled out. Bottom Line FLAT is a technically solid, well-ablated step that makes feedforward triangle-based scene generation viable for the first time; practitioners building game-engine or simulation pipelines that need real surface geometry from single images should read it, while those who need only novel view synthesis quality can likely stop at Lyra or FLAT's own 3DGS variant.
Recommended 2606.24876 PDF
#35 — Innovative (70)

SpatialAvatar-0: High-Quality 4D Head Avatar with Multi-Stage Reconstruction (3 ▲ )

Yiran Wang, Zeyu Zhang, Yuanming Li, Ziming Wang, Yang Zhao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SpatialAvatar-0, a unified 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) head-avatar pipeline that bridges generalizable feed-forward prediction and per-subject refinement through a shared Flexible Learned and Articulated Model for Expressions (FLAME)-mesh-bound Gaussian representation with a variable-source-count feed-forward generator and a 10K-iteration layout-preserving per-subject refinement loop. - Why it's important: Existing systems force practitioners to choose between generalizable feed-forward avatars (fast but lower fidelity, domain-biased) and per-subject refiners (high fidelity but 300K–600K iterations, ~5–9 hours per identity, with adaptive densification that destroys any feed-forward spatial prior), with no principled path to combining both regimes end-to-end. - How it does it: A parameter-free K-source mean-pool aggregator trained under a monocular-to-multi-view two-phase schedule — anchored by an L2-SP regularizer (λ=10⁻³) and a 25% NeRSemble cross-time sampling mix to prevent identity-prior collapse — feeds into a per-subject refinement loop that freezes FLAME-triangle binding and Gaussian count and replaces densification with three-component anti-spike regularization (scale-freeze warmup, hard log-scale clamp, screen-space aspect-ratio penalty). - The good: On the SplattingAvatar monocular benchmark, the 10K-iteration per-subject refinement surpasses the 300K-iteration GeoAvatar on every reported metric by +1.3 dB PSNR at 60× shorter per-subject schedule (~2 minutes vs. ~4.9 hours on a single RTX 3090). - The missing: The feed-forward stage's per-subject quality on the SplattingAvatar benchmark is omitted from the main paper, so the two pipeline stages cannot be directly compared on a single benchmark, and there is no evaluation on subjects with glasses, hats, or non-frontal source images. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building 3DGS head-avatar systems who need to understand how to unify feed-forward generalization and per-subject refinement without the anisotropy failure modes that adaptive densification introduces.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile (a trained pipeline producing deployable avatars, profile tag EMPIRICAL+SYSTEMS with Impact weighted heaviest). Practical Impact (72) carries the most weight and is well-supported: 265.6 FPS feed-forward inference, ~50ms avatar creation, and a 60x wall-clock reduction (2 min vs 4.9 h) over GeoAvatar at higher quality on a public benchmark. Rigor (72) is strong for a preprint — nine ablation tables identify mechanistically distinct failure modes, plus a genuine cross-domain zero-shot test on VFHQ/HDTF — but there are no error bars, no released code, a 14-day H100 training-compute confound, and a narrow per-subject benchmark. Novelty (68) is integration-driven: the layout-frozen FLAME-bound representation unifying feed-forward and refinement is the real contribution, but UV-aligned binding, the StyleGAN2 decoder, L2-SP, and Jacobian regularization are all prior art. Non-Obviousness (70) reflects the layout-freezing insight that resolves the densification incompatibility. Completeness (70) and Resilience (68) are good with candid limitations, though the feed-forward stage's per-subject quality is omitted from the main benchmark, preventing a clean attribution of Stage-2 gains. Weighted: 0.15*68 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*68 + 0.30*72 = 70.
systems Novelty 68 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 68 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Building animatable 4D head avatars from one or a few portrait images serves telepresence, AR/VR, and digital-human interaction, where both cross-identity generalization and per-subject high-frequency fidelity are required. Two complementary 3DGS paradigms have emerged but remain architecturally incompatible. Generalizable feed-forward predictors train on a single dataset family with a hard-coded source count, inheriting domain bias and lacking multi-view geometric grounding. Per-subject 3DGS refiners recover fine detail but require 300K–600K iterations per identity (~5–9 hours) and rely on adaptive densification (split/clone of Gaussians), which destroys the spatial layout produced by any feed-forward initialization and exposes the well-known 3DGS anisotropy failure: Gaussians elongate along a single axis to overfit training views, catastrophically breaking novel-view rendering when densification is removed. Neither regime alone satisfies production requirements, but no prior work shares a single Gaussian representation across both. Key Idea The central insight is that freezing FLAME-triangle binding and Gaussian count throughout both stages — feed-forward prediction and per-subject refinement — makes the two regimes share a common UV-aligned representation end-to-end without densification. Per-subject refinement then fine-tunes Gaussian attributes (color, rotation, scale, position) at fixed topology, inheriting the identity prior from the feed-forward stage. This preserves reusability of the upstream prior downstream. A complementary insight is that protecting this shared representation during multi-view post-training requires two mechanisms that address distinct collapse modes: an L2-SP anchor to the monocular Phase-1 checkpoint prevents identity-prior forgetting on the small 17-subject NeRSemble set, and a 25% cross-time (same-camera, different-time) sampling mix prevents the cross-camera majority from collapsing the broader identity prior learned on ~15,653 CelebV-HQ subjects. Methodology The pipeline operates over ~58,173 Gaussians per identity, one per valid UV pixel of a 256×256 FLAME UV grid, with each Gaussian rigidly bound to its parent triangle. Stage 1 (feed-forward): A frozen DINOv3 ViT-B/16 backbone with a trainable Dense Prediction Transformer (DPT) head produces a dense feature map and a global CLS token; the dense feature is barycentrically warped to UV space and passed to a StyleGAN2-based UV decoder with Spatial-Feature-Transform (SFT) layers modulated by the source CLS token, producing 32-channel UV features decoded by five parallel heads (local position, rotation, scale, color, opacity). For K>1 source images, a parameter-free mean-pool aggregates per-source UV features and CLS tokens (order-invariant; identity at K=1). A FiLM-modulated residual head conditioned on a 112-d vector of FLAME expression codes, pose codes, and eye codes (shape β excluded to preserve cross-subject generalization) adds per-attribute corrections to position, rotation, scale, and color. A Hutchinson-randomized Frobenius Jacobian penalty (L_jac) enforces per-pixel view-direction invariance on position and scale heads at cost of one backward pass per iteration. Phase-1 training runs 200K iterations on CelebV-HQ (~15,653 subjects) with K ~ Uniform{1,2,3,4} and monocular-temporal sampling. Phase-2 continues from the Phase-1 checkpoint on NeRSemble v2 (16-camera synchronized multi-view capture), using 75% cross-camera draws and 25% cross-time draws, with encoder layer-wise LR decay (γ_LR=0.65), an L2-SP anchor (λ_sp=10⁻³), and no architectural changes. Stage 2 (per-subject refinement): Starting from Stage 1's output at a reference frame (selected by minimum jaw-pose magnitude), 10K photometric iterations optimize Gaussian attributes with FLAME binding and Gaussian count frozen. Three anti-spike components replace adaptive densification: (i) a scale-freeze warmup for the first 500 iterations preventing early anisotropy spikes; (ii) a hard log-scale clamp per iteration after scale unfreezes, providing an absolute upper bound on world-space anisotropy; (iii) a soft screen-space anti-anisotropy penalty L_aspect (ReLU on log-aspect-ratio above τ=0.5, active from iteration 500) that supplies smooth gradients toward isotropic projected footprints. Background compositing uses a per-iteration random RGB background on both rendered and target sides, yielding a symmetric gradient that pulls rendered alpha toward the matte without an explicit silhouette loss. Three critical design decisions: (1) excluding shape β from residual conditioning (well-justified — β is the load-bearing cross-subject generalization axis; conditioning on it would enable per-identity displacement overfitting); (2) Hutchinson Jacobian penalty rather than a two-point finite-difference proxy (justified — finite differences at moderate angular separation confound first-order view sensitivity with higher-order curvature); (3) three complementary anti-spike components rather than one (confirmed by Table 9: each addresses a non-overlapping failure mode, with maxAR reaching >10⁶ on log-clamp removal). Key Results Feed-forward zero-shot (Tables 1–2): On VFHQ (19 test clips, out-of-distribution for SpatialAvatar-0 but in-distribution for GAGAvatar and GPAvatar), Ours-FF achieves PSNR 23.34 dB / SSIM 0.875 / LPIPS 0.077 / CSIM 0.916, leading all 11 baselines and surpassing the in-domain leader GAGAvatar by +1.51 dB PSNR / +0.057 SSIM. On HDTF, Ours-FF achieves PSNR 24.67 / SSIM 0.916 / LPIPS 0.076 / CSIM 0.871, surpassing GAGAvatar by +1.54 dB PSNR. These are cross-domain zero-shot probes: SpatialAvatar-0 never trains on either dataset. Per-subject (Table 4, SplattingAvatar benchmark): Ours+S3 achieves MSE 0.402×10⁻³ / PSNR 33.960 dB / SSIM 0.971 / LPIPS 0.0355, surpassing GeoAvatar (PSNR 32.635, 300K iterations ~4.90h on RTX 3090) on every metric at 10K iterations ~2 minutes (60× shorter wall-clock). Inference runs at 265.6 FPS at 512² resolution with 3.77ms latency on RTX 3090. Ablation (Table 5, held-out CelebV-HQ): Phase-1 alone (row A) reaches PSNR 24.84 / SSIM 0.879. Adding Phase-2 without controls catastrophically degrades to 22.73 / 0.799 (row B). Reintroducing L2-SP anchor + 25% cross-time mix (row C) recovers 24.94 / 0.881. Adding per-subject refinement (row D, Full) yields 29.95 / 0.947 — the largest single jump. Removing the residual head costs 1.67 dB PSNR; removing the Jacobian penalty raises AKD from 1.365 to 1.874. DINOv3-L outperforms the default DINOv3-B by 0.08 dB PSNR (Table 6); the largest source-count gain is K=1→K=2 (+1.21 dB PSNR), with diminishing returns at K=3,4. Anti-spike component ablation (Table 9) shows all three components are non-redundant: removing log-clamp alone causes maxAR to drift past the budget, and removing L_aspect lets Gaussians regrow into screen-space streaks after iteration 500. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the unified representation that allows a single layout-frozen FLAME-bound Gaussian structure to serve both feed-forward generalization and per-subject refinement, combined with the monocular→multi-view two-phase training schedule with L2-SP anchor and cross-time mix, and the three-component anti-spike regularization that replaces densification. The UV-aligned FLAME-bound Gaussian layout is adopted from GaussianAvatars; the StyleGAN2 UV decoder is prior art; L2-SP anchoring is a known transfer learning technique; Jacobian regularization follows Hoffman et al. The contribution is the integration of these components under a design rationale that resolves the specific incompatibility between feed-forward and per-subject 3DGS regimes. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are unusually thorough: nine tables probe pipeline stages (Table 5), backbone capacity and source count (Table 6), four training hyperparameters (Table 7), L2-SP and cross-time mix interaction (Table 8), and anti-spike component interactions (Table 9). Each ablation fixes all other components and identifies mechanistically distinct failure modes. Table 7 includes independent reruns of the same configuration, showing evaluation-order noise of ~0.01–0.05 dB PSNR; formal error bars are not reported. Code and model weights are promised at https://spatialwalk.github.io/SpatialAvatar-0 but have not been released as of this preprint. This is arXiv:2606.15659v1 (14 Jun 2026), not peer-reviewed. The per-subject evaluation on SplattingAvatar uses the last 350 frames per video as test — the dataset is narrow (monocular studio video) and the evaluation protocol exactly matches prior per-subject baselines, providing a fair but limited scope comparison. Training required a single H100 NVL GPU for 14 days; training compute for baselines is not compared. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (a trained pipeline producing deployable avatars). - Practical: Feed-forward inference runs on a single RTX 3090 at 265.6 FPS at 512²; per-subject refinement runs ~2 minutes on RTX 3090. Training requires a single H100 NVL GPU (14 days), which is commercially available. - Efficient: Feed-forward produces an avatar in ~50ms from a single image; per-subject adds ~2 minutes vs. ~4.9 hours for GeoAvatar. The 58K Gaussian budget is fixed regardless of subject complexity, limiting texture resolution for subjects with complex accessories or hair. - Effective: Evidence covers reenactment quality (PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS), identity preservation (CSIM), expression fidelity (AED), pose distance (APD), and keypoint accuracy (AKD) — a broad proxy set. Qualitative renders (Figs. 2–4) are consistent with quantitative claims and show subject-discriminating fine detail (wrinkles, beard, hair edge) preserved across identities. - Scalable: Tested at 512² resolution on single-subject inference; no batch inference or higher-resolution experiments are reported. The fixed Gaussian count does not scale with subject complexity, which the authors acknowledge as a limitation for accessories. The system delivers a trained feed-forward model checkpoint plus a per-subject refinement procedure. Code release is promised but not yet available. Strengths - The layout-preserving refinement design is the key systems contribution: freezing binding and Gaussian count makes the shared representation compatible across regimes, and the 60× wall-clock reduction vs. GeoAvatar at superior quality is a credible, reproducible result measured on a public benchmark with matching inference hardware. - The two-phase training schedule's complementary mechanisms (L2-SP anchor + cross-time mix) are empirically validated as non-redundant: Table 8's 2×2 factorial confirms that each provides partial protection and only their combination recovers full effect, a stronger verification than most training-recipe papers provide. - The cross-domain zero-shot evaluation design is sound: VFHQ and HDTF appear in neither training phase, so the +1.5 dB PSNR margin over in-domain leaders is a genuine generalization test rather than an in-distribution benchmark. - Anti-spike regularization ablation (Table 9) identifies mechanistically distinct failure modes per component — maxAR exceeding 10⁶ on log-clamp removal, screen-space streak regrowth on L_aspect removal — providing an unusually detailed causal account of each design choice. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors identify three limitations directly in Appendix C: (1) large-scale accessories (glasses, hats) are not geometrically modeled and are absorbed into surface texture; (2) the FLAME tracker requires successful frontal face detection on at least one source frame, failing on profiles or heavily occluded faces; (3) per-subject refinement at ~2 minutes precludes truly real-time avatar creation, which is limited to the feed-forward stage. The authors confront these directly and propose two extensions: audio/motion-driven control coupling and full-body extension with body+hand sub-meshes. Acknowledgment is candid rather than minimizing. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The feed-forward stage's per-subject quality on SplattingAvatar is omitted from the main paper "for compactness," so the two stages of the same pipeline cannot be directly compared on the same benchmark — it is unknown whether Ours-FF alone would beat the per-subject baselines or how much Stage 2 contributes relative to the feed-forward initialization specifically on this dataset. - No evaluation on subjects with glasses, hats, dense curly hair, extreme makeup, or non-frontal source images; all test subjects appear to be cooperative studio or broadcast subjects with visible frontal faces. - Training compute (H100, 14 days) is not compared against baseline training costs; the cross-domain generalization advantage over GAGAvatar and GPAvatar may partly reflect greater pretraining compute rather than architectural superiority. - No user study or perceptual evaluation at resolutions above 512²; the claim that subject-discriminating fine detail (forehead wrinkles, malar contour) is preserved rests on qualitative inspection and LPIPS/CSIM proxies. - The per-subject evaluation dataset (SplattingAvatar, monocular studio video, last 350 frames as test) is narrow; independent replication on the full NeRSemble v2 multiview test set or a held-out monocular in-the-wild dataset would substantially strengthen the per-subject generalization claim. Bottom Line A practitioner building production 3DGS head-avatar systems gains concrete, deployable techniques from this work — the layout-preserving refinement loop and the two-phase monocular-to-multiview training schedule with anti-collapse controls are well-motivated and empirically validated — but full adoption warrants an independent replication on diverse in-the-wild subjects and a released codebase, neither of which is available from this preprint.
Recommended 2606.15659 PDF
#36 — Innovative (70)

World Action Models: A Survey (37 ▲ )

Qiuhong Shen, Shihua Zhang, Yue Liao, Qi Li, Zhenxiong Tan, Shizun Wang, Shuicheng Yan, Xinchao Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a unified formal taxonomy and component-level anatomy for World Action Models (WAMs), organizing 200+ existing methods under a shared mathematical framework expressed as a 4-tuple of predictive substrate, action coupling, architectural backbone, and deployment regime. - Why it's important: The WAM field has exploded across three communities (video generation, robot learning, language modeling) in two years, producing hundreds of methods that use incompatible vocabularies for overlapping or identical designs, making comparative evaluation and principled design reasoning nearly impossible. - How it does it: It formalizes every WAM as a parameterized conditional joint distribution p_Θ(s_{t+1:t+H}, a_{t:t+H-1} | o_{≤t}, a_{<t}, l) and then classifies each existing method on four separable axes — what future the model predicts, how action couples to that future, which function family realizes the predictor, and when the model is invoked in the control loop. - The good: The dual-view framework reveals that the Render-and-Decode / Latent-Only / Video-Generation-Free philosophy distinction is orthogonal to the cascaded / joint / post-prediction-head coupling distinction, a conflation that prior survey-style comparisons routinely commit. - The missing: No quantitative comparison of any pair of methods on a shared benchmark appears anywhere in the paper; the taxonomy is organizing but not predictive, so a reader cannot determine which substrate or coupling choice wins at a given task, scale, or data regime. - Read it or not: Essential for researchers designing or evaluating embodied robot-learning systems who need a shared vocabulary; practitioners looking for empirical selection guidance will need to consult individual cited works directly.
Why this rank: Scored using the Theoretical profile (formal framework, no primary experiments). Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (72) drive the composite: the 4-tuple anatomy (substrate / coupling / backbone / deployment) and the orthogonality claim are a genuine NEW FORMULATION, and the chronological 'generate less of the future' convergence pattern is non-obvious synthesis that individual papers obscure. Rigor (70) is solid for a survey: a ~120-method census classified on all four axes with explicit boundary-case handling and a sharp evaluation critique, though it is a non-peer-reviewed preprint and reports no measurements of its own. Completeness (68) and Resilience (72) are high because the authors confront the central gaps directly. no design dominates all properties, no scaling law exists, closed datasets limit verification. Practical Impact (55) is the main drag: the taxonomy is organizing but not predictive, so it gives no empirical selection guidance and ships only a census website, no artifact. Composite: 0.25*72 + 0.20*70 + 0.25*72 + 0.10*68 + 0.15*72 + 0.05*55 = 70.
theoretical Novelty 72 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 68 Resilience 72 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Embodied AI has moved rapidly toward policies that anticipate how the world changes before choosing an action, but the resulting literature is conceptually fragmented. VLA (Vision-Language-Action) models, broad world models, action-grounded video world models, and self-described WAMs are used interchangeably despite having distinct formal contracts. Methods that share the WAM label arrive from the video-generation, robot-learning, and language-model communities; two such methods can agree on the name while sharing almost no implementation detail, while two others can avoid the name and yet build the same thing. The core gap is the absence of a common framework that (a) draws a precise boundary between a VLA, a world model, and a WAM; (b) separates the design axes that practitioners routinely conflate; and (c) makes explicit what each design choice costs in compute, memory, latency, and action-label requirements. Key Idea The single core insight is that any WAM can be pinned down by four separable and orthogonal design axes: the predictive substrate (the representational space in which the future variable s lives), the action coupling (how action enters or exits the prediction), the architectural backbone (the function family that realizes the predictor), and the deployment regime (how often and over what horizon the model is invoked inside the control loop). Formalizing this as WAM ≅ (Φ, F, B, D) decouples questions that were previously entangled: what the model predicts is independent of whether generation is cascaded or joint, and the same substrate can support any backbone family. This 4-tuple is the organizing lens for the entire census of 200+ methods. Methodology The survey develops two complementary views, each applied exhaustively to a census of approximately 120 WAMs drawn from 2023 through mid-2026. The philosophy-level view asks where action prediction sits along the video-generation inference path and produces a mutually exclusive three-way classification: Render-and-Decode (generation runs to pixels before action is decoded), Latent-Only (the video backbone is retained but pixel decoding is bypassed at inference, with action recovered from intermediate denoising states, flow fields, semantic masks, or value maps), and Video-Generation-Free (no pixel-level video backbone in the predictive path, replaced by LLM/VLM trunks, JEPA-style joint-embedding predictors, or non-video diffusion over compact substrates). The component-level anatomy formalizes the unified notation p_Θ(s_{t+1:t+H}, a_{t:t+H-1} | c) where c abbreviates the conditioning context. Predictive substrate (Φ) spans four mathematically typed categories: pixel-grounded (decoded RGB, RGB-D, or VAE/VQ latent in observation space), feature (learned hidden states without a fixed observation decoder, including teacher-target embeddings and VLM-token futures), geometric (physical-coordinate tensors: optical flow in ℝ^{H×2×Hpx×Wpx}, point tracks, depth, pose, motion vectors), and affordance (task-specific spatial maps: value maps, contact likelihoods, segmentation masks, end-effector heatmaps). Action coupling (F) spans three factorization families derived from the joint: action-conditioned rollout (an outer planner or policy q_ψ proposes action chunks and a world predictor evaluates consequences), joint generation (substrate and action chunk emerge from one coupled generative process, trained with L_gen(s) + λL_act(a)), and post-prediction head (the world predictor generates s first, then a smaller expert q_ψ decodes action from it, often with θ frozen). Architectural backbone (B) spans five families: iterative-denoising (video diffusion or flow-matching), autoregressive (causal next-element prediction over serialized substrate and action tokens), joint-embedding predictive (JEPA-style deterministic predictor with stop-gradient target encoder and L2 embedding loss, no pixel decoder), hybrid (shared encoder-decoder trunk with separate generative and action heads), and LLM/VLM (language or vision-language backbone as primary trunk). Deployment regime (D) spans four modes with explicit cost equations: open-loop rollout (C_open(T) = N_fwd(T), invoked once), chunked closed-loop (C_chunk(T,K) = ⌈T/K⌉ N_fwd(K)), single-step closed-loop (C_single(T) = T N_fwd(1), constrained to N_fwd(1) < 1/f_ctrl), and interactive simulator (cumulative KV-cache cost). Five core properties — interactability, causality, persistence, physical plausibility, and generalization — are then examined across the census. A section on data sources categorizes training corpora by action-label fidelity and scale (robot teleoperation, portable human demonstrations, internet-scale egocentric video, simulation, synthetic WAM-generated trajectories). A section on evaluation catalogs visual fidelity metrics, closed-loop benchmarks, and physical plausibility checks, with an explicit critique of the mismatch between metric cost and predictive validity. Key Results This is a survey; it reports no original measurements. Descriptive patterns synthesized from cited works include: (1) Chronologically, the field migrated from Render-and-Decode (dominant in 2023) toward Latent-Only and then Video-Generation-Free as the pixel-rendering cost in the control loop became apparent. (2) Most deployed WAMs use chunked closed-loop control; single-step closed-loop is feasible only with compact substrates. (3) DreamZero achieves 7 Hz closed-loop control with a 14B autoregressive video-diffusion model through key-value-cache observation replacement. (4) MotuBrain reports a weak correlation between its world-model quality score and downstream task success, directly undermining the assumption that visual fidelity tracks action utility. (5) Fast-WAM and GigaWorld-Policy show that dropping the rendered forecast at inference does not degrade control quality, suggesting the video objective is more important as a training signal than as a test-time forecast. (6) EgoScale reports a clean scaling law for dexterous pretraining volume, one of the few quantitative relationships of this type in the survey. All numbers are reported from cited preprints or peer-reviewed papers; the survey itself makes no new empirical claims. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (synthesis). The 4-tuple anatomy and three-way philosophy taxonomy are the survey's primary contributions; no prior survey formalizes WAMs in this way, and the companion census at https://world-action-models.github.io/ extends the catalog. The synthesis finding — that the field is moving toward methods that generate less of the future while preserving what control requires — is an empirical pattern extracted from the census that individual papers do not articulate. The work is a survey/position contribution; it introduces no new algorithm, dataset, or trained model. Rigor & Reproducibility As a survey, ablations and significance tests are not applicable. The census in Tables 1 and 2 covers approximately 120 WAMs with arXiv submission dates, classified on all four axes. Boundary cases are handled explicitly: methods whose generated future is used only as an auxiliary training loss (not in the inference-time action path) are excluded, and methods that disable pixel decoding at inference but retain a video-trained trunk are placed in Latent-Only rather than Video-Generation-Free. The paper notes that Sora-class generators and EgoScale are closed or not yet public, limiting independent verification of their contribution. The companion website maintains the census. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.20781v1, 18 Jun 2026) and has not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: THEORETICAL (formal framework; no primary experimental claim). - Sound: The unified notation (Equation 8) and the three coupling factorizations (Equations 10-12) are derived correctly from the joint distribution, and the four substrate categories are given explicit mathematical types (Equations 13-16); no formal gaps are apparent. - General: The framework covers all existing WAMs reviewed, handles boundary cases (optional-future patterns, interactive simulators, multi-substrate joint cells) explicitly, and is domain-agnostic across manipulation, driving, and aerial settings; the exclusion criterion (future not in the inference-time action path) is a crisp and observable condition. - Tight: The framework is a classification scheme, not a bound; quantitative tightness does not apply. The open challenges (Section 7) name precisely the axes on which the framework provides no quantitative guidance: there is no scaling law over source, stage, quality, model size, and action grounding. - Implementable: The 4-tuple directly prescribes four design decisions a practitioner must make; the companion website at https://world-action-models.github.io/ provides the census as a look-up table. No software artifact or dataset is released. Strengths - The orthogonality of the four design axes is the clearest formal contribution. Separating substrate (what space the future lives in) from coupling (how action enters the prediction) from backbone (which function family realizes the predictor) from deployment regime (when and how often the model is called) eliminates the most common conflation in the literature, where Render-and-Decode is incorrectly equated with cascaded coupling and Latent-Only with joint generation. - The five core properties (interactability, causality, persistence, physical plausibility, generalization) are rigorously defined and applied consistently across the full census. The causality section in particular gives a precise account of how leakage (future frames attending to action tokens) corrupts closed-loop evaluation — a subtle failure mode not prominently discussed in most individual papers. - The evaluation critique (Section 6.2) is direct and specific: visual-fidelity metrics (FVD, FID, LPIPS, PSNR, SSIM) are cheap but weakly tied to downstream success; closed-loop benchmarks measure the right outcome but consume robot or simulator time; physical-plausibility and long-horizon metrics remain underdeveloped. The proposed two-stage protocol (cheap visual screen followed by selective closed-loop tests under explicit compute and memory budgets) is actionable. - The chronological view (Figure 3) makes a concrete empirical claim from the census: the field is converging on methods that generate less of the future while preserving what control requires, which is a non-obvious pattern that individual papers obscure by each claiming a different motivation for their design. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that no design dominates across all five core properties — every gain on one property shifts cost, latency, memory, or error into another — and that WAM design is therefore best understood as a set of competing trade-offs rather than a checklist. They acknowledge the absence of a scaling law over data source, training stage, quality, model size, and action grounding, without which adding more data remains an engineering choice rather than a predictable design decision. Closed or inaccessible datasets (Sora-class generators, EgoScale as of preprint submission) limit independent verification. The authors also state that the boundary between cheap visual screens and expensive closed-loop tests remains the central open question in WAM evaluation. These limitations are confronted directly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The census catalogues design axes but provides no quantitative comparison of any pair of methods on a common benchmark. A reader cannot determine from this survey whether a Latent-Only method with a geometric substrate outperforms a Render-and-Decode method with a pixel-decoded latent on ManiSkill 3, LIBERO, or MetaWorld at any particular scale; the taxonomy is organizing but not predictive. - The boundary between Latent-Only and Video-Generation-Free depends on training history rather than inference behavior: a method that retains a video-trained trunk but disables pixel decoding at inference is Latent-Only, while a method that never had a video backbone is Video-Generation-Free. This makes the taxonomy axis unobservable from a deployed checkpoint and potentially unstable as methods increasingly mix pretraining sources. - No method in the census is annotated with parameter count, training FLOPs, or data volume. Given that open challenges include scaling laws, the absence of scale information in Tables 1-2 limits the utility of the census for researchers trying to understand cost-performance trade-offs. - Cross-embodiment and sim-to-real transfer claims are cited from individual papers with heterogeneous evaluation splits. The survey presents these as evidence for the utility of video pretraining for generalization without noting that VLABench and VidMan find gains dependent on evaluation split rather than appearing uniformly, which the survey itself mentions but does not integrate into its synthesis of the generalization evidence. Bottom Line A robotics or embodied AI researcher designing or evaluating WAMs will find this survey indispensable for establishing shared vocabulary and separating design axes that prior literature conflates; it cannot, however, tell a practitioner which design choices work best at a given task scale or data regime, because it reports no quantitative comparisons of its own.
Recommended 2606.20781 PDF
#37 — Innovative (70)

LingxiDiagBench: A Multi-Agent Framework for Benchmarking LLMs in Chinese Psychiatric Consultation and Diagnosis (19 ▲ )

Shihao Xu, Tiancheng Zhou, Jiatong Ma, Yanli Ding, Yiming Yan, Ming Xiao, Guoyi Li, Haiyang Geng, Yunyun Han, Jianhua Chen, Yafeng Deng · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LingxiDiagBench, a large-scale multi-agent benchmark for evaluating LLMs on Chinese psychiatric consultation and diagnosis, anchored by LingxiDiag-16K, a dataset of 16,000 synthetic EMR-aligned consultation dialogues spanning 12 ICD-10 psychiatric categories derived from 1,709 real outpatient records. - Why it's important: Existing psychiatric AI benchmarks lack real-data grounding, clinician-verified diagnostic labels, and support for dynamic multi-turn consultation, leaving a critical gap between benchmark performance and clinical deployability of LLM-based diagnostic tools. - How it does it: A knowledge-graph extraction pipeline derives demographic and diagnostic distributions from real EMRs; an LLM-powered Patient Agent (with naturalness-enhancing prompts and length control) and four Doctor Agent consultation strategies (Free-form, Symptom-Tree, APA-Guided, APA-Guided + MRD-RAG) interact to generate and evaluate dialogues across both static transcript analysis and live multi-turn consultation. - The good: Cross-dataset transfer validation shows that fine-tuning Qwen3-8B on the synthetic LingxiDiag-16K training split improves 12-class accuracy on real clinical data from 4.1% to 41.4% (+37.3%), demonstrating that the synthetic data encodes clinically transferable knowledge, not merely surface statistics. - The missing: No licensed psychiatrist diagnostic accuracy baseline is reported, leaving unclear how hard the 12-class task is for humans and whether even a 28.5% LLM ceiling represents a meaningful clinical gap or a near-human one. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or evaluating psychiatric AI systems in Chinese clinical settings; those focused on English-language or general medical AI benchmarks will find the task design instructive but the results non-transferable without additional work.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (robust to Systems weighting — same composite). Rigor (72) drives the score: peer-reviewed at KDD 2026, thorough ablations over four consultation strategies across 13+ LLMs, LLM-as-a-Judge validated against two licensed psychiatrists (Kendall W=0.90), dataset and code released. Novelty (70) is genuine — the first Chinese psychiatric consultation benchmark grounded in real outpatient EMRs with a dynamic multi-turn paradigm. Non-Obviousness (65) is anchored by the finding that consultation quality only moderately predicts diagnostic accuracy (r=0.43), a non-trivial result. Completeness (72) and Resilience (68) are strong — limitations confronted directly, though the missing psychiatrist baseline and unaddressed Qwen3-32B contamination concern cap resilience. Impact (70) reflects the released benchmark plus the +37.3pp cross-dataset transfer validating clinical knowledge. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*70 = 70.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Global shortage of psychiatrists combined with the inherent subjectivity of interview-based diagnosis creates barriers to timely mental-health assessment. AI-assisted tools could partially address this bottleneck, but current psychiatric-AI benchmarks have three structural deficits: most are built on template-based synthetic dialogues that lack realistic conversational variability, they rarely include clinician-verified diagnostic labels or comorbidity cases, and almost none support dynamic multi-turn consultation that evaluates information-gathering strategy, which is itself a determinant of downstream diagnostic accuracy. Practitioners building clinical decision-support tools have no reliable way to compare LLMs on realistic psychiatric workflows in Chinese. Key Idea: The core insight is to decouple patient simulation from diagnostic reasoning through an agent-based architecture: a Patient Agent driven by real EMR-derived profiles simulates realistic symptom disclosure, while separate Doctor Agents implement distinct consultation strategies, and a Diagnosis Agent reads the completed dialogue. This decoupling allows the benchmark to measure not only whether a model can diagnose from a completed transcript (static) but whether its consultation behavior produces transcripts that support diagnosis (dynamic). The patient-agent naturalness pipeline — length sampling from real distribution, colloquial-response prompting, gradual symptom disclosure — is the mechanism that distinguishes LingxiDiag-Patient from prior LLM patient simulators. Methodology: The pipeline has three layers. First, the data foundation: 4,500 outpatient recordings from Shanghai Mental Health Center (SMHC, 2023–2024) are preprocessed via automated speech recognition and manual verification, yielding 1,709 well-annotated EMR-transcript pairs across 12 ICD-10 categories (F20, F31, F32, F39, F41, F42, F43, F45, F51, F98, Z71, and Others). A knowledge graph extracts demographic and clinical distributions. LingxiDiag-16K is then generated by sampling from this graph and having a Qwen3-32B-powered APA-guided Doctor Agent interact with a LingxiDiag-Patient agent, followed by LLM polishing. Distribution alignment is verified via Jensen-Shannon divergence on age, gender, and text-length distributions (JS values 0.028–0.072 for key categories). Second, the agent system: the Patient Agent is an LLM prompted with a structured EMR-derived profile plus conversation history; the Doctor Agent implements four strategies — Free-form (unconstrained), Symptom-Tree (MDD-5K decision trees), APA-Guided (five-phase clinical protocol: screening, assessment, deep-dive, risk, closure), and APA-Guided + MRD-RAG (APA-Guided augmented by retrieval of a Chinese clinical guideline chunked at 500 characters, encoded with Qwen3-Embedding-8B, and re-ranked with Qwen3-Reranker-8B to inject the top-3 relevant passages at Assessment and Deep-Dive phases); the Diagnosis Agent applies ICD-10 criteria to the full dialogue. Third, the evaluation framework: LingxiDiagBench-Static evaluates assisted diagnosis (2/4/12-class) and doctor next-question prediction (BLEU, ROUGE-L, BERTScore) on fixed transcripts; LingxiDiagBench-Dynamic evaluates Patient Agent quality (6 dimensions, 1–5 scale) and Doctor Agent quality (5 dimensions, 1–6 scale) plus live classification, all via a three-model LLM-as-a-Judge ensemble (Gemma-3-27B, GPT-OSS-20B, Qwen3-30B-A3B) with median imputation. Classification uses accuracy, macro F1, weighted F1, and Top-1/Top-3 accuracy for the 12-class task. Design decisions: (1) opaque per-run labels (P001..PNNN) to prevent LLM ID-shortening hallucinations — well-justified; (2) multi-model judge ensemble rather than a single LLM judge — well-justified given moderate inter-rater agreement; (3) synthetic data generation using Qwen3-32B, which is also an evaluated model — a potential but unaddressed contamination concern. Key Results: Static evaluation on LingxiDiag-16K: binary (F32 vs. F41) accuracy reaches 0.854 (Gemini-3-Flash) with weighted F1 of 0.851; 4-class macro F1 tops at 0.479 (Qwen3-1.7B and TF-IDF+RF); 12-class accuracy peaks at 0.409 (GPT-5-Mini) with best Overall score 0.548 (Qwen3-32B). On the real LingxiDiag-Clinical dataset, 12-class best Overall is 0.548 (Qwen3-32B), with Top-3 accuracy reaching 0.645 (TF-IDF+LR). Dynamic evaluation: under the best strategy (APA-Guided + MRD-RAG), 2-class accuracy reaches 92.3% (DeepSeek-V3.2), 4-class 43.0% (Grok-4.1-Fast), and 12-class 28.5% (Grok-4.1-Fast). Dynamic performance consistently falls below static, with the performance gap widening for multi-class tasks. LLM-as-a-Judge dialogue quality correlates only moderately with diagnostic classification accuracy (r = 0.43), indicating that consultation quality and diagnostic accuracy are partially orthogonal objectives. Doctor strategy ranking is highly consistent: Kendall W = 0.90, Spearman ρ = 0.80, with APA-Guided consistently outperforming Free-form across models and metrics. Adding MRD-RAG to APA-Guided improves dynamic classification by approximately 5% overall. Cross-dataset transfer: fine-tuning Qwen3-32B on LingxiDiag-16K synthetic training data improves real-data 12-class accuracy from 20.4% to 39.7% and overall score from 0.548 to 0.558. LingxiDiag-Patient agents score 4.07–4.67 out of 5 versus 2.23–4.09 for MDD-5K-Patient counterparts, validated by two licensed psychiatrists (Mann-Whitney U = 631.0, p < 0.001, effect size r = 0.547). Baselines span traditional ML (TF-IDF + SVM/RF/LR) and 13 LLMs including Qwen3 series (1.7B–32B), Baichuan (M2-32B, M3-235B), Gemini-3-Flash, GPT-5-Mini, DeepSeek-V3.2, Kimi-K2-Thinking, Grok-4.1-Fast, Claude-Haiku-4.5, and GPT-OSS-20B — a reasonably current and diverse set at time of writing. Notable finding: TF-IDF+LR achieves the best Top-3 accuracy (0.645) on LingxiDiag-Clinical for 12-class, outperforming all LLMs on that specific metric, suggesting LLMs do not universally dominate traditional methods on structured EMR-based classification. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the first Chinese psychiatric consultation benchmark grounded in real outpatient EMRs with clinician-verified ICD-10 labels, combined with a dynamic multi-turn evaluation paradigm that decouples patient simulation from diagnostic reasoning. The individual components — LLM patient agents, RAG-augmented doctor agents, LLM-as-a-Judge — are assembled from established techniques; the contribution is their integration into a coherent, reproducible evaluation infrastructure for a specific high-stakes domain. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations over consultation strategies are thorough, covering four Doctor Agent variants across 13+ LLMs on both static and dynamic settings. LLM-as-a-Judge reliability is validated against two licensed psychiatrists on 64 matched samples, reporting Kendall W = 0.90 and Spearman ρ = 0.80, providing meaningful grounding for the automated evaluation. Error bars (±SD) are reported for patient agent evaluation (Table 3) but not consistently for classification metrics in Tables 4–5 or for dynamic results in Table 7. The benchmark, dataset (LingxiDiag-16K), and evaluation code are released at https://github.com/Lingxi-mental-health/LingxiDiagBench. The paper is accepted at KDD 2026 (peer-reviewed). A methodological concern: the synthetic dialogue generation uses Qwen3-32B as the Doctor Agent backbone, and Qwen3-32B is also one of the benchmark's evaluated models; the paper does not address whether this creates distributional advantage for Qwen3-32B in static evaluation. The 1,000-sample test set for a 12-class multi-label problem is modest, and no confidence intervals accompany classification tables. The dynamic evaluation test set is not stated separately — it appears to use the same 1,000 samples. Deployability: SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: The benchmark evaluation pipeline runs on commercially available hardware; the LLM evaluations are conducted via API or local inference with models ranging from 1.7B to 235B parameters, all of which have publicly available weights or API access. - Efficient: The dynamic evaluation requires multiple LLM calls per consultation turn (Patient Agent + Doctor Agent + Diagnosis Agent + three Judge models), making per-sample cost significantly higher than static evaluation; the paper acknowledges this but does not quantify token or latency costs. - Effective: The benchmark demonstrably reveals performance gaps invisible to static evaluation (e.g., dynamic 12-class accuracy of 28.5% vs. static 40.9%) and the cross-dataset transfer results confirm the synthetic data supports real-data improvement, establishing practical utility beyond leaderboard ranking. - Scalable: The framework is modular (static diagnosis can run without the full agent pipeline), supports parallel API calls natively, and the patient-agent generation pipeline uses thread pooling, but no throughput numbers at scale are reported. Scaling to other languages or diagnostic systems requires re-running the EMR extraction and distribution alignment pipeline from scratch. The paper delivers a software artifact (dataset + evaluation framework + code). Strengths: - The cross-dataset transfer experiment (Table 9) is the paper's strongest methodological contribution: showing that fine-tuning on LingxiDiag-16K synthetic data improves real-data 12-class accuracy by 37.3 percentage points (Qwen3-8B) provides direct evidence that the synthetic dataset encodes clinically meaningful knowledge, not surface-level distributional mimicry. - The finding that consultation quality (LLM-as-a-Judge, r = 0.43) only moderately predicts diagnostic accuracy is a substantive and non-obvious empirical result with direct implications for how clinical AI systems should be optimized — optimizing the interview does not automatically optimize the diagnosis. - The human expert validation of LLM-as-a-Judge (two licensed psychiatrists, 64 samples, Kendall W = 0.90) is more rigorous than most benchmark papers, which rely entirely on automated metrics. - The patient-agent naturalness enhancements (response-length sampling from real distribution, gradual symptom disclosure prompting) address a documented deficiency in prior LLM patient simulators and are validated by a statistically significant human evaluation (effect size r = 0.547). Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge four scope boundaries directly: (1) results are Chinese-specific and may not generalize to other languages or cultures; (2) the benchmark focuses on initial outpatient diagnosis and does not address treatment planning, follow-up assessment, or longitudinal care; (3) LLM-simulated patients still lack the full diversity of individual patient presentations and communication styles, particularly for atypical cases; (4) LingxiDiag-16K is built on synthetic data from a single center (SMHC) and inevitably differs from authentic clinical encounters in some respects. These are confronted directly rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Three gaps the authors do not address. First, the paper provides no licensed psychiatrist diagnostic accuracy baseline on the 12-class task, making it impossible to contextualize whether 28.5% dynamic accuracy represents a catastrophic failure or a near-human performance on a genuinely difficult multi-label problem — this omission substantially limits clinical interpretation. Second, the synthetic dialogue generation pipeline uses Qwen3-32B as the Doctor Agent backbone, yet Qwen3-32B is one of the primary evaluated models in both static and dynamic settings; the paper does not test whether this creates distributional bias in favor of Qwen3-32B (e.g., by excluding Qwen3-32B from evaluation or testing with only externally generated dialogues). Third, the dynamic evaluation only assesses zero-shot LLM performance; the cross-dataset LoRA fine-tuning experiment (Appendix B) is performed only on static evaluation, leaving open whether fine-tuned models would close the static-to-dynamic performance gap, which the paper identifies as a central finding. Bottom Line: Practitioners building or benchmarking LLM-based psychiatric diagnostic tools for Chinese clinical settings will find LingxiDiagBench the most realistic and rigorously grounded evaluation resource currently available, and the finding that consultation strategy matters as much as model scale is immediately actionable; researchers in other languages or broader medical AI should read for methodology but cannot apply the results directly.
Recommended 2602.09379 PDF
#38 — Innovative (70)

Confidence-Aware Tool Orchestration for Robust Video Understanding (8 ▲ )

Yangfan He, Yujin Choi, Jaehong Yoon · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Robust-TO, an agentic video reasoning framework that propagates per-frame quality degradation estimates as explicit confidence signals through frame selection, tool routing, and evidence synthesis stages, trained end-to-end with a confidence-cost GRPO reward. - Why it's important: Frontier Video-LLMs silently lose 15–30% accuracy under common real-world corruptions (motion blur, glare, occlusion) while self-reported confidence stays flat, a failure mode called the Blind Trust Problem that is unacceptable in forensic video analysis, autonomous driving review, and surveillance. - How it does it: A parameter-free disturbance score (inverse Laplacian variance for blur, HSV luminance deviation for brightness, Sobel edge-density deficit for occlusion) ranks frames by reliability × query relevance, routes decomposed sub-queries to the most corruption-tolerant tool, and scales tool outputs by input quality before three-tier evidence synthesis; the host VLM is trained with GRPO to jointly optimize correctness, confidence-cost trade-off, and sub-query efficiency. - The good: Robust-TO with Qwen3-VL-7B achieves 56.4% on clean UrbanVideo-Bench (vs. Gemini-2.5-Pro at 46.2%) and 54.3% on corrupted inputs (5.8%p above the best open-source baseline), while simultaneously cutting processed frames by 35% and inference time by 86 seconds. - The missing: The disturbance vocabulary covers only blur, brightness, and occlusion; adversarial perturbations, semantic occlusions hidden behind clean foregrounds, and audio-visual misalignment are out of scope, and no error bars or multi-seed variance are reported for the 1–4%p ablation gains. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building video agents for safety-critical or embodied AI settings; the released code, checkpoints, and thorough ablations make it a credible baseline for corruption-robust video reasoning.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (systems-flavored, but evaluation is empirical). Rigor (70) is the strongest pillar: extensive isolating ablations (Tables 3-6, 13-16), strong and current baselines including proprietary frontier models (Gemini-2.5-Pro, GPT-4o) on the same Video-R1 data, released code and checkpoints, and a frozen π_est that directly defuses a Goodhart concern; missing error bars / multi-seed variance under known-high-variance GRPO and synthetic RoVA-only corruptions cap it. Impact (75) reflects the released artifacts, plug-and-play (result, confidence) tool interface, and safety-critical use cases. Completeness (72) is helped by honest scope limitations and concrete case studies; Resilience (65) is weakened because 1-2pp ablation deltas lack statistical grounding and evaluation is confined to short (~25-38s) embodied-navigation videos. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (70) are genuine in making visual-quality degradation a first-class end-to-end-trainable signal, though GRPO-for-tool-use and tool-augmented video reasoning are prior art. This resembles the user's positive calibration set (mechanism-focused, rigorous, artifact-releasing). Composite: 0.20*72 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*75 ≈ 70.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 72 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Video reasoning language models uniformly sample frames and treat every frame as equally informative, ignoring that real-world capture introduces intermittent corruptions (camera shake producing motion blur, oncoming headlights producing glare, moving objects producing partial occlusion). Benchmark evidence from UrbanVideo-Bench and VSI-Bench shows frontier models dropping 15–30% accuracy under these conditions while self-reported confidence stays nearly unchanged. This silent failure is particularly costly in forensic review, post-hoc autonomous driving analysis, and surveillance, where a confidently wrong answer can have serious downstream consequences. Prior agentic video systems reduce uniform frame sampling but return only raw tool outputs without quality signals, so the VLM controller cannot distinguish whether a tool was given sharp, informative frames or blurred, corrupted ones. Key Idea The core insight is that per-frame visual quality degradation must be an explicit, propagated signal throughout the entire agentic reasoning pipeline rather than an implicit variable buried inside raw tool outputs. Concretely, a parameter-free disturbance profile computed from signal-level statistics (no training data, no clean reference frames required) both gates which frames enter downstream tools and scales down tool confidence proportionally to input degradation, so high-performing tools on corrupted inputs are automatically discounted in the final evidence synthesis. Methodology Robust-TO runs as a three-stage pipeline hosted on a VLM (Qwen2.5-VL-7B or Qwen3-VL-7B). Stage 1 (Frame Selection via Quality Profiling): the `assess_quality` tool computes d(f_i) = mean(d_blur, d_bright, d_occl) using inverse Laplacian variance (Eq. 9), HSV luminance deviation (Eq. 10), and Sobel edge-density deficit (Eq. 11). Frames are scored s(f_i) = (1 − d(f_i)) × sim(φ(f_i), ψ(q)) combining reliability and CLIP-based query relevance; the top-K frames (K ∈ [4, 12] chosen adaptively) proceed. Input reliability ρ(F) uses worst-K aggregation (K = ⌈n/3⌉), penalizing batches with any heavily corrupted frames rather than masking corruption via a clean-frame average. Stage 2 (Confidence-Guided Tool Routing): the query is decomposed into atomic sub-queries {sq_1,...,sq_m} targeting single perceptual primitives (spatial, temporal, attribute, text). A two-step routing protocol maps semantic type to candidate tools and then selects among them by dominant corruption type (blur favors `caption_frame` over `detect_objects` because captioning tolerates spatial degradation; brightness distortion favors illumination-robust tools). Every tool returns (r_j, c_j) where c_j = c^intrinsic × ρ(F_j) couples tool self-confidence with input reliability. Stage 3 (Reliability-Aware Evidence Synthesis): evidence items are grouped into HIGH (c ≥ 0.7 and d < 0.3), MEDIUM (all else), and LOW (c < 0.3 or d ≥ 0.7) tiers; the VLM anchors its answer on HIGH-tier evidence, retains MEDIUM only if consistent with the HIGH conclusion, and uses LOW only as a fallback with explicit uncertainty. The host VLM is trained end-to-end with GRPO on ~12K Video-R1 triplets for ~5,000 steps on 4×A100-80GB. The total reward R_total = R_acc + w(R_subq + R_cc^total + R_fmt) combines correctness, confidence-cost trade-off (R_cc = c_j − λ·cost(T_j), λ=0.5), sub-query efficiency (penalizing over- and under-decomposition against a frozen π_est target count m*), and format.
