Three critical design choices: (1) worst-K aggregation for ρ(F) rather than mean, preventing one clean frame from masking batch-level corruption; (2) freezing the sub-query count estimator π_est to prevent reward gaming (unfrozen π_est causes 2.3× reward variance increase and 1.2%p accuracy loss); (3) multiplicative coupling of c^intrinsic with ρ(F_j), ensuring corrupted-input tool calls are discounted even when the tool itself reports high internal confidence. Key Results On clean benchmarks (UrbanVideo-Bench, 1,028 questions; VSI-Bench, 762 questions; 8 tasks total): Robust-TO with Qwen3-VL-7B achieves 56.4% average accuracy, vs. Gemini-2.5-Pro at 46.2%, Video-R1 (Qwen3-VL-7B) at 52.0%, and supervised fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL-7B at 45.8%. Largest absolute gains appear on Appearance Order (72.0% vs. 63.6% for Video-R1 Qwen3) and Landmark Position (61.1% vs. 52.0%). On corrupted benchmarks (5,140 questions, 5 corruption types via RoVA at medium severity applied to 40–60% of frames): Robust-TO with Qwen3-VL-7B achieves 54.3% average vs. Gemini-2.5-Pro at 38.1%, Video-R1 (Qwen3-VL-7B) at 48.5%, and GPT-4o at 32.2%; Robust-TO exhibits the smallest clean-to-corrupted accuracy drop among all methods. Frame selection reduces processed frames from 32 to 20.7 (35% reduction), cuts training time by 16+ hours, and cuts per-sample inference time by 86 seconds, while improving accuracy by 1.6%p. The parameter-free disturbance estimator outperforms NIQE by 7–10%p and BRISQUE by 5–8%p across all 5 corruption types (Table 16). Confidence-guided routing outperforms query-only routing by 6.1%p and a fixed-tool baseline by 10.4%p. Baselines include current strong proprietary models (Gemini-2.5-Pro, GPT-4o, OpenAI-o1) and open-source models trained on the same Video-R1 data; this is a fair and strong comparison set. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the unified (result, confidence) tool interface that propagates input reliability through the full agentic reasoning chain, combined with a confidence-cost GRPO reward that jointly optimizes accuracy, reliability calibration, and tool efficiency. Applying GRPO to video tool use is an extension of prior work (Video-R1, DeepVideo-R1); tool-augmented video reasoning is also prior art (VideoAgent, ToolLLM). The novel element is making visual quality degradation a first-class, end-to-end-trainable reasoning variable rather than a post-hoc filter. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are extensive and informative: Table 3 shows incremental contribution of each pipeline stage (P→C+SQ: +3.3%p; +Tool: +3.5%p; +Conf: +3.2%p; +GRPO: +4.7%p); Tables 4, 5, 6, 13, 14, 15, 16 isolate key-frame extraction, collaborative synthesis, confidence interface, sub-query decomposition modality, frame selection, worst-K aggregation, and disturbance estimators, respectively. No error bars or multi-seed variance are reported; GRPO training is known to have high variance across runs, so 1–2%p ablation differences could be within noise. Code and checkpoints are released at https://rova-v2.github.io/. The disturbance estimator uses three constants (τ_blur=500, μ_ref=0.5, τ_edge=30) calibrated once on a held-out pool, not tuned per benchmark. The frozen π_est estimator directly addresses a Goodhart's Law concern. The paper is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on 4×A100-80GB for training; inference requires only the host VLM plus commodity pretrained tools (GroundingDINO-T, ByteTrack, VideoMAE-v2, PaddleOCR), all commercially available. - Efficient: Frame selection cuts processed frames by 35% and inference time by 86 seconds per sample; on clean videos, overhead is <5%; under heavy corruption, the full multi-tool pipeline can increase latency and the paper acknowledges real-time scenarios may require capping tool calls. - Effective: Achieves 54.3% on corrupted video reasoning vs. 48.5% for the same backbone without Robust-TO; four detailed case studies demonstrate correct answers in concrete failure modes where the standard VLM fails, supporting the claim beyond aggregate numbers. - Scalable: Not tested beyond the ~38-second short-video regime in the evaluation benchmarks; no experiments at higher frame rates, longer videos, or streaming inputs; scalability to additional corruption types beyond the three-dimensional disturbance vocabulary is claimed but not demonstrated. Code and trained checkpoints are publicly released. Strengths - The unified (result, confidence) tool interface is a principled design that decouples reliability propagation from tool implementation; any tool exposing a self-assessment score integrates without pipeline retraining, which is a genuine plug-and-play property. - The parameter-free disturbance estimator requires no training data or clean reference frames, making it deployable in novel domains without recalibration beyond three constants set once. - The GRPO reward design specifically closes known failure modes: the frozen π_est prevents gaming, the confidence-cost term prevents defaulting to expensive tools, and the sub-query efficiency term prevents over-decomposition; each is isolated in ablations and shown to matter. - Frame selection provides a rare alignment of robustness and efficiency: the method simultaneously improves accuracy and reduces compute, rather than trading one for the other. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge four scope boundaries: (1) the disturbance vocabulary covers only blur, brightness, and occlusion, excluding adversarial perturbations, semantic occlusions behind clean foregrounds, and audio-visual misalignment; (2) the frozen π_est caps sub-query decomposition quality at π_est's own capability, which can inject noise in domains where π_est misjudges query complexity; (3) the frame selection score depends on the host VLM's visual encoder φ, which may miscalibrate on corruption types unseen during pretraining; (4) inference latency under heavy corruption can rise beyond the <5% overhead observed on clean videos. The authors also flag the dual-use risk that the disturbance estimator could be reverse-engineered to craft perturbations that appear clean to the quality gating while remaining semantically corrupted. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No error bars or multi-seed training variance are reported; with GRPO's known sensitivity to initialization, ablation deltas of 1–2%p lack statistical grounding. - The corrupted evaluation uses synthetic RoVA masks applied uniformly at medium severity; real-world corruptions are spatially non-uniform, can co-occur, and vary in severity—the gap between the synthetic evaluation protocol and genuine deployment conditions is not quantified. - Both evaluation benchmarks are short-video embodied navigation tasks (avg. 25–38 s at 1 fps). Generalization to longer videos, multi-scene content, or streaming is not evaluated. - The main comparison tables do not match frame budgets between Robust-TO (20.7 frames) and proprietary models (32 frames); Table 12 partially addresses this for the clean case with open-source uniform-sampling baselines only. - Tool routing consistency under distribution shift is not analyzed; the routing is learned via in-context reasoning, so routing variance across semantically equivalent sub-queries is unknown. Bottom Line A practitioner building video agents for forensic analysis, embodied AI, or any safety-critical setting where corrupted inputs cause silent failures should read this paper and evaluate the released codebase as a direct baseline for corruption-robust video reasoning.
Recommended 2606.26904 PDF
#39 — Innovative (69)

ShuntServe: Cost-Efficient LLM Serving on Heterogeneous Spot GPU Clusters

S Jeong, M Song, J Park, K Lee · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ShuntServe, a distributed LLM serving system that jointly optimizes model placement, parallelization strategy, and fault tolerance to maximize throughput-per-cost on heterogeneous spot GPU clusters. - Why it's important: Spot GPU instances offer up to 90% cost savings over on-demand instances, but their high interruption rate and the load imbalance caused by heterogeneous GPU capabilities make them impractical for existing LLM serving systems, locking most self-hosting practitioners into expensive on-demand hardware. - How it does it: ShuntServe combines a roofline model-based analytical throughput estimator (requiring only a one-time per-GPU hardware calibration) with a dynamic programming plus beam search optimizer that jointly assigns pipeline stages, layer counts, and tensor parallelism degree, then recovers from spot interruptions via recomputation-based output-preserving request migration overlapped with concurrent pipeline re-initialization through a shared tensor store. - The good: On a 24-GPU heterogeneous AWS cluster (L4/A10G/L40S), ShuntServe achieves 1.53 req/s on Llama-3.1-70B, a 1.17× improvement over HexGen (the strongest heterogeneous baseline), while simultaneously delivering 31.9% lower cost per throughput than on-demand-only deployment in offline workloads. - The missing: The performance estimator assumes static batch sizes and fixed input/output lengths; it achieves 6.63% average MAPE under those conditions but the authors acknowledge the model does not capture kernel launch overhead, SM utilization, or cache effects that arise in real dynamic-batching workloads, and no dynamic-batching correction is provided. - Read it or not: Read it if you build or operate self-hosted LLM serving infrastructure and are evaluating cloud cost reduction strategies; the DP-based placement algorithm and shared tensor store design are directly implementable on top of vLLM.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Strong across the board: the profiling-free roofline estimator (6.63% MAPE, 0.28 GPU-hr calibration) and the shared-tensor-store concurrent-initialization design give real Novelty (70) and directly implementable Impact (70). Rigor (65) is credible with ablations decomposing fault-tolerance contributions, beam-width sensitivity, error bars on timing, and a real Azure production trace on real AWS heterogeneous hardware; the main caveat is the estimator being validated under static batching while serving uses continuous batching, which the authors acknowledge openly, supporting Completeness (72). No public repo and preprint status are the residual gaps. Weighted composite ~69.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 72 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Organizations that self-host large language models face prohibitive GPU costs: serving Llama-3.1-70B on high-end cloud GPUs can cost tens of thousands of dollars per month. Spot instances reduce this by up to 90%, but GPU spot availability is markedly lower and more volatile than CPU spot availability, and high-end GPU types (H100, B200) were unavailable for the entire 6-day monitoring window the authors observed in AWS us-west-2. Heterogeneous clusters spanning mid-tier GPU types (L4, A10G, L40S) exhibit more stable and complementary availability patterns. However, existing LLM serving systems assume homogeneous GPUs and apply uniform layer partitioning, which makes the weakest GPU the throughput bottleneck. Furthermore, a spot interruption of any single node in a pipeline stalls the entire pipeline, making existing fault tolerance strategies (designed for stateless or CPU workloads) ineffective because LLM autoregressive decoding requires a persistent KV cache that cannot simply be discarded. Key Idea The core insight is that throughput on a heterogeneous GPU pipeline can be predicted analytically using per-GPU roofline characteristics (effective FLOPS, memory bandwidth, network bandwidth) without exhaustive profiling, and this estimator is accurate enough to guide a DP optimizer that jointly assigns asymmetric layer counts and per-stage tensor parallelism degrees. Crucially, each instance is assigned exclusively to one pipeline, so a spot interruption is confined to that pipeline; a shared tensor store process that holds model weights separately from the inference engine then allows the replacement node's engine to be initialized in parallel with ongoing serving on the unaffected pipeline, overlapping downtime with the grace period. Methodology ShuntServe consists of three integrated components. First, the Serving Performance Estimator models each transformer operation (QKV projection, attention, FFN, output embedding) using roofline equations: compute latency = FLOPs / effective_FLOPS, memory latency = MemScanCost × element_size / Mem.BW, op latency = max(compute, memory). Network communication for tensor parallelism (TP) uses the α-β model for AllReduce via Ring topology; pipeline parallelism (PP) inter-stage communication is similarly modeled. Throughput is derived as RPS = B / Σ L_ops(B), where B is the maximum batch size fitting within GPU memory after accounting for model weights and activation tensors. A one-time calibration (≈1,022 seconds total across three GPU types) measures effective FLOPS via compute-bound GEMM, memory bandwidth via GEMV, and network bandwidth via AllReduce, replacing white-paper values that were found to diverge substantially from observed hardware performance (e.g., L4 official FP16 FLOPS = 121 TFLOPS vs. measured ≈55 TFLOPS). Second, the Partitioned Model Placement Optimizer (Algorithm 1) is a DP over pipeline depth and stage count, augmented with beam search (retaining top-k partial placements at each DP entry). For each layer boundary l and stage count s, it considers all available GPU instance types and calls the estimator to score each candidate; complexity is reduced from O(N_L³ × N_s! / Π C(N/n)) to O(N_L³ × k × |I|). TP is restricted to intra-node GPUs only (inter-node bandwidth insufficient). The objective function is throughput/cost with an optional latency penalty: argmax_p [Throughput(p)/Cost(p) × (1 - γ × max(0, latency(p)/SLO - 1))]; in the default configuration γ=0, purely maximizing throughput per dollar. Third, the Fault Tolerance mechanism addresses two sub-problems: preserving in-flight request state and minimizing pipeline downtime. Output-preserving request migration uses recomputation rather than KV cache transfer (KV cache transfer must complete within the spot grace period, which is infeasible for large models at long contexts; on L40S, KV cache transfer exceeds recomputation latency at 64K context length with 9.6% higher overhead). Concurrent initialization decouples the inference engine lifecycle from model weight management via a Shared Tensor Store process that holds weights in a separate process and shares them zero-copy to inference engines via CUDA IPC handles; a new engine on the replacement node can initialize (loading weights from remote storage in ≈61.85s, initializing engine in ≈64.51s) while the existing pipeline continues serving, with total initialization time ≈111.3s on average, staying within AWS's 120s grace period. Key Results Offline throughput (saturated workload, Azure Conversation Dataset trace, avg input 763 tokens, avg output 232 tokens): ShuntServe achieves 1.53 req/s on Llama-3.1-70B, vs. HexGen 1.31, AlpaServe 1.08, vLLM 1.07 req/s. On Qwen3-32B, ShuntServe achieves 4.59 req/s, vs. AlpaServe 4.14, vLLM 4.39, HexGen 3.39. Online latency (Llama-3.1-70B): ShuntServe median TTFT 1.37s and median TPOT 0.82s, vs. vLLM median TTFT 1.64s and TPOT 0.67s; at P90 tail, ShuntServe TTFT is 2.39s vs. vLLM 7.70s (69% reduction). Performance estimator achieves 6.63% average MAPE across 54 feasible configurations (3.49% on L4, 6.68% on L40S, 9.67% on A10G); minimum error 0.03%, maximum 23.72%. Fault tolerance under spot interruption (50-minute window): ShuntServe achieves 1.12 req/s vs. No Handle 0.85 req/s (32% improvement) and Request Migration Only 0.92 req/s on Llama-3.1-70B. Cost efficiency vs. On-demand Only: ShuntServe achieves 31.9% lower cost per throughput (offline) and 31.2% (online mean). Concurrent initialization overhead: node provisioning 41.55s ± 7.54s, Shared Tensor Store loading 61.85s ± 9.59s, Inference Engine initialization 64.51s ± 9.25s, total ≈111.3s, fitting within the 120s AWS grace period. Calibration overhead: 1,022.36 seconds total (≈0.28 GPU-hours vs. ≈1,000 GPU-hours for full evaluation). Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the combination of (1) a profiling-free roofline-based estimator sufficient to drive combinatorial placement search in heterogeneous heterogeneous GPU pipelines, and (2) the shared tensor store architecture that decouples the inference engine from weight management to enable concurrent re-initialization. The DP + beam search optimizer adapts known techniques (DP for stage assignment, beam search for pruning) to the specific joint optimization of layer allocation and per-stage TP degree in a cost-aware heterogeneous setting, which is a meaningful extension beyond prior work (HexGen uses genetic algorithms that get trapped in local optima; AlpaServe uses DP only for homogeneous stage balancing). Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present: the fault tolerance evaluation decomposes contributions of Request Migration and Concurrent Initialization separately (Figure 13), and the beam search width sensitivity analysis (Figure 11) shows throughput stabilizes at k=3. The performance estimator is validated against measured latency across 54 configurations on three GPU types. Error bars and standard deviations are reported for the concurrent initialization timing measurements. The workload uses a real production trace (Azure Conversation Dataset). A significant methodological concern: the estimator is validated under static batching with fixed sequence lengths, while the actual serving uses dynamic batching with variable-length requests; the authors acknowledge this limitation explicitly and note the model does not capture SM utilization, kernel launch overhead, or cache effects. No code repository URL is mentioned in the paper. The paper is a preprint submitted to Elsevier (not yet peer reviewed). The evaluation consumed approximately 1,000 GPU-hours on AWS, which is a meaningful but not extraordinary experimental investment. Baselines (HexGen, AlpaServe, vLLM) are current and appropriate; SpotServe (the most directly comparable spot-instance LLM serving system) is correctly excluded as a baseline because it targets only homogeneous clusters. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS (ShuntServe is a built and deployed system, evaluated on real AWS hardware under real production traces). - Practical: Runs on commercially available AWS GPU instances (g5, g6, g6e instance families with A10G, L4, L40S GPUs); no specialized interconnects required. - Efficient: Calibration costs 0.28 GPU-hours per GPU type; optimizer runs in 15.9s (Llama-3.1-70B, k=1) to 94s (k=11) on the 24-GPU cluster; runtime overhead of fault tolerance is 0.03% of total GPU-hours. - Effective: The system demonstrably solves the stated problem: it delivers 31.9% lower cost per throughput than on-demand instances while maintaining comparable latency (mean and P90 within 2.6%–7.7% of on-demand). The proxy concern is that the throughput estimator is validated under static batching while real serving uses continuous batching, leaving uncertainty about optimizer decision quality in dynamic workloads. - Scalable: The paper tests on a 24-GPU cluster (3 GPU types, 9 instances) and reports beam search execution time on a 76-GPU cluster (7 GPU types, 15 instances), where it reaches 9,433s at k=32 for Llama-3.1-70B. At k=3 (the recommended default), execution time on the 76-GPU cluster is not explicitly stated, but the linear scaling trend with k suggests it remains tractable. Behavior beyond 76 GPUs or with more GPU types is not evaluated. ShuntServe delivers a software artifact: the system is implemented as 1,077 lines of modifications to vLLM v0.8.1 plus 10,363 lines of Python code for the Shared Tensor Store, global server, and scheduler. No public repository URL is provided. Strengths - The roofline-based estimator with one-time hardware calibration is a genuine practical contribution: it achieves 6.63% MAPE while requiring no per-configuration profiling, making it tractable for the combinatorially large heterogeneous placement search space where profiling-based approaches (Vidur, MaverIQ) become infeasible. - The Shared Tensor Store design cleanly solves the concurrent initialization problem by exploiting zero-copy CUDA IPC sharing, preventing the OOM error that would occur if old and new inference engines both loaded model weights independently — this is a non-obvious engineering insight. - The end-to-end cost efficiency analysis (Figure 15) is done correctly: it normalizes cost per performance, not raw cost, properly accounting for performance degradation under interruptions, and includes the temporary billing overlap during concurrent initialization. - The evaluation uses a real production workload trace (Azure Conversation Dataset) rather than synthetic distributions, and the experiment selection (worst-case 50-minute availability window scored by frequency and magnitude of availability changes) is methodologically careful. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge three limitations. First, the static latency estimator does not capture non-linear GPU execution factors (kernel launch overhead, SM utilization, cache effects), achieves up to 23.72% error in specific configurations, and was validated under synthetic fixed-length batching rather than dynamic real-world workloads; they propose integrating an ML-based correction as future work. Second, the recomputation-based migration can incur higher overhead than KV cache transfer for very long contexts (>64K tokens on L40S, 9.6% higher latency), and they propose a hybrid per-request recovery selection as future work. Third, the system assumes a static cluster within a single region and does not dynamically adapt model placement as spot availability fluctuates over time; multi-region and temporal adaptation are identified as future directions. Analyst-Identified Gaps Two gaps go beyond what the authors note. First, the optimization objective (Equation 7) maximizes throughput/cost at the pipeline level, but the load balancing across pipelines uses a simple weighted round-robin by throughput. Under real workload patterns with high variance in request length, the throughput-weighted assignment could mismatch actual pipeline utilization; no analysis of load balancing quality under variable-length workloads is provided. Second, the evaluation cluster has only three GPU types with 24 GPUs total. The beam search sensitivity analysis includes a 76-GPU, 7-type cluster for execution time measurement, but actual serving quality (throughput achieved vs. optimum, latency behavior under interruption) is only measured on the 24-GPU cluster. Whether the 6.63% estimator MAPE holds for GPU types beyond L4/A10G/L40S (e.g., H100, A100, with very different compute-to-memory ratios) is untested. Bottom Line Practitioners considering spot GPU clusters for self-hosted LLM serving will find ShuntServe's placement optimizer and shared tensor store design directly actionable and backed by credible AWS experiments, making this worth reading despite the estimator's known limitations under dynamic batching.
Search Results 2606.18600 PDF
#40 — Innovative (69)

Latency Prediction for LLM Inference on NPU Systems

J Park, S Jeong, J Lee, K Lee · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LENS (Latency Estimator for NPU Systems), a black-box latency predictor for LLM inference on commercial NPUs that requires only two end-to-end measurements per bucket to predict latency for arbitrary input/output length combinations. - Why it's important: NPU-based LLM serving requires searching a large configuration space (batch size, tensor parallelism, bucket selection), but exhaustive measurement is impractical due to per-shape static compilation costs, and all existing GPU-based prediction methods fail on NPUs with errors of 80–493%. - How it does it: LENS treats each NPU bucket (a compiled binary shared by all inputs padded to that boundary) as the atomic prediction unit, solves a 2×2 linear system per bucket from two E2E measurements at different output lengths to extract TTFT and TBT constants, then composes these constants to predict latency for any input/output length. - The good: Achieves 2.15% mean error and 1.69% median error across 248 configurations on four commercial NPUs (Inferentia2, TPU v4/v5e/v6e), three models (1B–14B), and four datasets, with 89.9% of cases within 5% error, while baselines LLM Serving Sim 2.0 and MaverIQ exceed 200% and 33% MAPE respectively in their failure regimes. - The missing: LENS is bound to the NPU on which profiling was conducted; models up to 14B are tested but production deployments at 70B+ with higher tensor parallelism are not evaluated, leaving scaling behavior at that range unaddressed. - Read it or not: Essential for MLSys practitioners operating Google TPU or AWS Inferentia2 serving infrastructure who need to search configuration spaces without exhaustive profiling; less relevant for GPU-only shops.
Why this rank: Systems profile. The reinterpretation of the NPU bucket as the atomic prediction unit, recoverable with exactly two E2E measurements and zero microarchitectural knowledge, is an elegant and non-obvious insight (Non-Obviousness 80, Novelty 72). Rigor (65) rests on unusually broad empirical coverage (2.15% mean error over 248 configs, 4 commercial NPUs spanning distinct compiler stacks, strong baseline comparison where GPU methods fail at 80-493%), but there is no component ablation, no measurement-variance reporting, no public code, and only models up to 14B. Impact (65) reflects a directly usable 18-minute-profiling tool whose absence of a reference implementation limits adoption. Weighted composite ~69.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 70 Resilience 68 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Operating commercial NPU-based LLM serving systems (Google TPUs serve Anthropic's Claude; AWS Inferentia2 serves production traffic) requires searching a multidimensional configuration space: accelerator selection, batch size, tensor parallelism degree, and bucket set. The decode phase of LLM inference is memory-bandwidth-bound, and NPUs address GPU structural mismatches through heterogeneous engine designs (Tensor, Vector, Scalar engines executing concurrently) and weight-stationary systolic arrays. However, three NPU-specific properties block all existing GPU-based latency prediction methods. First, microarchitectural details required for cycle-accurate simulation — systolic array dimensions, on-chip memory layout, HBM timing, NoC routing — are not publicly disclosed by commercial vendors; filling in estimated values causes simulation errors of 232–323% for element-wise operations on Inferentia2. Second, NPU compilers apply graph-level optimizations including kernel fusion and cross-engine execution overlap in ways that are qualitatively different from GPU compilers and unpredictable from per-kernel measurements; applying GPU-based operator-sum methods to NPUs yields 63–493% errors on standard input/output configurations. Third, NPU static compilation requires a separate binary per input shape, so serving systems adopt bucketing (predefined length boundaries such as {128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192}), causing latency to follow a step-function distribution that invalidates all interpolation-based approaches; a single-token increase across the 4096–8192 boundary multiplies TTFT by approximately 2.4× on TPU v6e with Llama 3.1 8B. Key Idea The core insight is that each NPU bucket corresponds to a single compiled binary, so all inputs assigned to that bucket share identical execution regardless of their actual length. The E2E latency of that binary is directly measurable even when the microarchitecture and compiler optimizations are opaque. Within a bucket, E2E latency decomposes into a fixed prefill constant (TTFT_b) and a fixed per-token decode constant (TBT_b). These two scalars can be recovered by solving a 2×2 linear system from exactly two E2E measurements at different output lengths, making full bucket characterization possible with minimal profiling and zero microarchitectural knowledge. Methodology LENS models E2E latency as the sum of prefill and decode phases. Prefill latency for input length l^in is the TTFT of bucket(l^in) = min{b ∈ B | l^in ≤ b}. Decode latency accumulates as the KV cache grows: at step t, KV cache length c_t = l^in + t + 1 determines which bucket binary executes. Because c_t grows monotonically, decode divides into K segments, each with its own TBT_b_j, yielding T_decode = Σ_j l_j^out · TBT_{b_j} (Eq. 5). Full E2E prediction is T_E2E = TTFT_{bucket(l^in)} + Σ_j l_j^out · TBT_{b_j} (Eq. 6). For batched inference, batch prefill sums per-request TTFT values. Batch decode is governed by c_t^max (the maximum KV position across the batch at each step), with TBT normalized by batch size N. Requests with shorter outputs are padded and executed alongside the longest until it completes. For NPU frameworks with optimized attention kernels (ragged paged attention), each request's decode is assigned to its own KV-length bucket, and the per-request TBT is TBT_b / N. The 2-measurement-per-bucket calibration solves T_E2E^(1) = TTFT_b + L_1 · TBT_b and T_E2E^(2) = TTFT_b + L_2 · TBT_b at output lengths L_1, L_2, yielding TBT_b = (T^(2) - T^(1)) / (L_2 - L_1) and TTFT_b = T^(1) - L_1 · TBT_b (Eq. 15). With 8 bucket sizes, LENS requires 14 profiling points per batch, 12 compilations total, and 18 minutes of total profiling time. Three design decisions stand out: treating E2E rather than per-operator latency as the prediction unit (absorbs compiler optimizations), using bucket boundaries rather than actual input lengths as the prediction variable (captures step-function behavior), and minimizing compilations rather than measurement points (since compilation dominates NPU profiling cost). Key Results LENS achieves 2.15% mean error and 1.69% median error across 248 test configurations (4 NPUs × 3 models × 4 datasets × batch sizes 1–32), with 89.9% of cases below 5% error. Per-NPU means: 2.20% (max 8.07%) on Inferentia2-TP8, 2.65% (max 9.05%) on TPU v4-TP4, 2.35% (max 11.47%) on TPU v5e-TP4, 1.46% (max 6.52%) on TPU v6e-TP4. Per-model means: 2.70% for Llama-3.2-1B, 1.61% for Mistral-7B-v0.3, 1.52% for Qwen3-14B. Per-dataset means: 1.21% on ArXiv Summarization (prefill-dominant, 23:1 P:D ratio), 1.41% on Writing Prompts (decode-dominant, 1:23 P:D ratio), 2.49% on ShareGPT, 3.49% on CNN/DailyMail. Baseline comparison on TPU v5e and v6e configurations: LLM Serving Sim 2.0 exceeds 200% error on ArXiv at batch size 32 (per-operator summation fails to capture graph-level NPU compiler optimizations); MaverIQ averages 33% MAPE across all configurations, with CNN errors reaching 40–80% and ShareGPT errors of 50–70% due to its single global TTFT+TBT linear model that cannot represent bucket discontinuities. LENS achieves 5.3% average MAPE on the same configurations. Two case studies demonstrate practical value. Case Study 1: throughput-maximizing batch size is not monotone on NPUs. On TPU v6e with Mistral 7B, CNN and ArXiv peak at batch size 16 while ShareGPT and Writing Prompts peak at batch size 8; LENS predicted throughput matches measured throughput across all batch sizes. Case Study 2: smaller buckets do not always run faster. Subdividing the 4096–8192 range at 512-token steps increases ArXiv E2E latency by 7% over the default because the compiler applies stronger optimizations to the larger default bucket shape, a counterintuitive result that operators cannot anticipate without direct measurement. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contribution is the reinterpretation of the NPU bucketing structure as a natural unit of prediction, combined with the 2-measurement-per-bucket calibration scheme that requires no microarchitectural knowledge. The TTFT/TBT decomposition within a bucket applies a standard linear model, but the insight that this decomposition aligns precisely with NPU compiled binary boundaries — and that two E2E measurements suffice to recover both constants — is non-obvious and not present in prior GPU-based prediction work. Rigor & Reproducibility No traditional ablation study isolates individual LENS components (e.g., what happens if only one measurement per bucket is used, or if a different output-length pair is chosen for calibration). The paper compensates with broad empirical coverage: 248 configurations across 4 commercial NPUs with different compiler stacks and hardware generations. Error bars and run-to-run variance are not reported; TTFT_b and TBT_b estimates are treated as noise-free point estimates. No code repository is mentioned or linked; the paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.18042v2, June 17, 2026) with no stated peer review. The baseline comparison uses vLLM-TPU as the common serving framework, which is LLM Serving Sim 2.0's native codebase — this is methodologically fair since it uses the framework that the baseline was designed for. Deployability Profile: SYSTEMS — LENS is a built prediction tool evaluated by deployment on real commercial NPUs. - Practical: Yes. LENS runs on commercially available hardware (AWS Inferentia2 on NxDI, Google Cloud TPU v4/v5e/v6e on JAX/XLA) with no microarchitectural access required, completing full profiling in 18 minutes. - Efficient: LENS requires 12 compilations and 14 E2E measurements per batch configuration, versus LLM Serving Sim 2.0's 5,000 measurement points (7 hours total profiling) and MaverIQ's 48 compilations (41 minutes). Prediction inference itself is arithmetic composition of pre-measured constants. - Effective: Yes. LENS achieves under 5% error in 89.9% of 248 configurations, correctly identifies throughput-maximizing batch sizes, and surfaces non-intuitive bucket optima that manual reasoning misses. Baselines fail substantially outside their assumption regimes. - Scalable: Only models up to 14B parameters and batch sizes up to 32 are evaluated. The prediction complexity scales with the number of buckets (fixed at 8 here), not model size, so the approach should generalize to larger models, but this is not measured. Performance at 70B+ parameters and larger batch sizes used in production NPU serving is unaddressed. No public software release (code, CLI, library) is mentioned. Strengths - The paper precisely characterizes three structural reasons why GPU prediction methods fail on NPUs, with quantitative evidence for each (232–323% simulation errors from parameter gaps, 80–493% prediction errors from GPU-based methods, 2.4× TTFT jump at a single bucket boundary). This diagnosis is the clearest available treatment of the problem and is valuable independent of LENS. - The 2-measurement-per-bucket profiling scheme is directly calibrated to the NPU's compilation structure, achieving 2.15% mean error while requiring only 18 minutes of profiling — a 23× reduction in profiling time versus LLM Serving Sim 2.0. - Evaluation breadth is notable: four commercial NPU types with distinct compiler stacks (Neuron SDK on Inferentia2 vs JAX/XLA on TPUs) spanning three hardware generations (v4/v5e/v6e), three model families across 1B–14B, and four datasets covering the full prefill/decode ratio spectrum. - The two case studies expose genuine counter-intuitive NPU behaviors (non-monotone batch-size throughput, non-monotone bucket-size latency) that are directly actionable for serving operators and would be missed by GPU-derived intuitions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state two limitations. First, LENS predictions are bound to the NPU and model on which profiling was conducted; whether bucket-level E2E latency follows a consistent scaling relationship across NPU types (that would enable cross-platform transfer without re-profiling) is left as an open empirical question. Second, LENS operates in an offline serving setting and does not account for production system-level factors: queuing delay, inter-stage communication overhead in pipeline parallelism, and request routing across heterogeneous accelerators. The authors present both limitations as concrete directions for future work, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 2-measurement-per-bucket system assumes noise-free E2E measurements. No variance or confidence intervals for TTFT_b and TBT_b estimates are reported, making it unclear how sensitive predictions are to hardware measurement noise or run-to-run latency variation. - The bucket set B = {128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192} is fixed and uniform across all NPUs and experiments. Real deployments may configure different or larger bucket sets; prediction accuracy with 16 or 32 buckets is not evaluated. - Only models up to 14B parameters are tested. Production NPU serving at 70B+ parameters (Llama-3.1-70B, Gemini) typically uses higher tensor parallelism and may exhibit more complex bucket transition patterns due to larger KV caches; the paper makes no claim about this regime. - No public code release is mentioned. An operator wanting to deploy LENS on a new NPU has no reference implementation to adapt. - The baseline LLM Serving Sim 2.0 is evaluated on vLLM-TPU, its native framework; it is not verified whether the baseline achieves lower errors on its own originally reported platform configuration, which could make the comparison appear more favorable to LENS. Bottom Line A practitioner operating Google TPU or AWS Inferentia2 inference serving gains a directly usable, low-overhead latency prediction tool with the strongest empirical validation of any NPU-specific method currently available, and the paper's diagnostic characterization of NPU prediction challenges is the clearest published treatment of why GPU methods transfer so poorly.
Search Results 2606.18042 PDF
#41 — Innovative (69)

UnityShots: Memory-Driven Multi-Shot Audio-Video Generation with Boundary-Aware Gating (19 ▲ )

Jiehui Huang, Yuechen Zhang, Bin Xia, Jiahao Wang, Xu He, Zhenchao Tang, Meng Chu, Xin Tao, Pengfei Wan, Jiaya Jia · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces UnityShots, a memory-driven multi-shot audio-video generation system built on the LTX-2.3 22B dual-stream diffusion transformer, using two fixed-size video memory slots updated at every shot boundary by a gate that fuses visual cut probability, audio RMS energy change, and beat-tracker signals, plus a cross-shot speaker-identity anchor. - Why it's important: Long-form video storytelling requires character appearance, scene context, and speaker voice to persist across shot cuts; end-to-end systems cap sequence length by GPU memory while shot-by-shot systems accumulate identity drift once the conditioning window slides past the opening shot. - How it does it: A boundary score combining TransNetv2 visual cut probability, audio energy change, and beat position drives two monotone gating coefficients that update a long-term memory slot permanently anchored to the opening shot and a short-term slot holding the immediately preceding tail, with a rule-based lower bound preventing the opening anchor from ever being fully erased. - The good: On the authors' 200-sequence multi-cultural benchmark under image-to-video (I2V) conditioning, UnityShots leads all open-source baselines by +0.40 NC and +0.69 Story over the strongest open-source competitor, and leads closed-source Kling on narrative coherence (+0.13 NC) and story (+0.10 Story) while matching it on cross-shot axes. - The missing: The evaluation benchmark is entirely author-constructed using the authors' own tooling (Gemini3-Pro story design, NanoBanana/QwenImage first frames, ElevenLabs TTS reference audio), and the LLM judge (Gemini-2.5-Pro) used for NC/Story/Char/Pace/Cult ratings is not independently calibrated, creating a potential circularity. - Read it or not: Researchers working on long-form video generation or cross-shot identity preservation should read this; the dual-slot memory architecture, boundary gating mechanism, multi-cultural benchmark, and released MCP agent system are all directly usable, but the evaluation independence concerns warrant caution when treating reported margins as definitive.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile (N15/R15/NO10/C15/Res15/I30). Impact (82) is the driver: the work releases model checkpoints, evaluation code, a multi-cultural benchmark, and an MCP agent system, with an O(1) dual-slot memory that improves on linearly-growing keyframe banks. Novelty (70) combines a dual-slot boundary-gated memory, Strata-RoPE, and a 6-region benchmark. The rule-based monotone LTM lower bound, validated by ablation against a failing learned gate, is a real non-obvious insight (Non-Obv 70), and the honest Cult-tradeoff acknowledgment aids Completeness (70). Rigor (55) and Resilience (55) are held back because the entire benchmark is author-constructed with the authors' own tooling and judged by Gemini-2.5-Pro rating Gemini3-Pro-designed narratives (circularity risk), with one-sided audio comparisons; the 32-participant human study partly mitigates this. Capped at 9 shots. Weighted: 0.15*70 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*55 + 0.30*82 = 69.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 55 Impact 82
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Coherent multi-shot video narration requires three signals to persist across shot boundaries: subject appearance, scene context, and speaker voice identity. Existing approaches fail in complementary ways. End-to-end systems (Kling, Seedance, LTX-2, Cinetrans) train a single denoising pass over the full sequence but bound usable length by GPU memory and training context, preventing long-form storytelling. Shot-by-shot systems (FilmWeaver, StoryMem, OneStory, Cut2next) remove the length cap but either maintain keyframe banks that grow linearly with sequence count or lose the opening-shot identity anchor once the conditioning window slides past it, causing cumulative drift in hairline, clothing, skin tone, and facial detail from shot 2 onward. Agent-orchestrated pipelines (DIRECT, UNIVA) wrap pretrained generators under LLM planners but cannot propagate cross-shot signals if the underlying generator was not trained for cross-shot coherence. No prior system jointly addresses the need for both a persistent long-range opening-shot anchor and a fine-grained local transition record, or models shot boundaries as a joint function of visual and musical signals. Key Idea The core insight is to decouple video memory into two fixed-size, asymmetric slots serving complementary roles: a long-term memory (LTM) slot permanently anchored to the opening shot to preserve global identity, and a short-term memory (STM) slot holding the immediately preceding tail to support smooth local transitions. Both slots are updated at every cut by a boundary score that is a weighted sum of visual cut probability (TransNetv2), audio RMS energy change, and beat-tracker beat position. The LTM gating coefficient is bounded strictly above zero by a rule, so the opening-shot anchor is never fully erased regardless of how strong subsequent cuts are — the monotone lower bound is what immunizes the LTM against gradient-driven suppression on long sequences. A discrete cut-type prior (FIRST / CONTINUE / HARD) embedded via AdaLN becomes an inference-time control knob, letting users dial transition strength without retraining. Methodology UnityShots fine-tunes LTX-2.3 (22B, a dual-stream diffusion transformer with Block-Relativistic 3D RoPE) in two stages. Stage 1 trains the reference-identity block on single shots. Stage 2 adds the dual memory slots and boundary-conditioned gating, training on k-shot chunks (k ∈ [3,9]) sampled with bucketed-length sampling, in mixed conditioning mode (I2V p=0.5, T2V p=0.3, R2V p=0.2). The conditioning token sequence at shot N is C^N = [X^ref, L^N, S^N, A^ref, V^N_t ⊕ A^N_t], where L^N ∈ ℝ^{2×C_v} is the LTM slot (2 latent frames), S^N ∈ ℝ^{P_v×C_v} is the STM slot (P_v=4 latent frames ≈ 1s), and A^ref is a fixed speaker-identity token derived from the reference audio clip. Strata-RoPE partitions the temporal positional axis into three non-overlapping bands (current shot [0, T_v−1], STM band [f_lim−P_v, f_lim−1], LTM band [f_lim−2P_v, f_lim−P_v−1]) so attention distinguishes memory strata without extra depth tokens or side networks. The boundary score b_N = τ_N · (α·s_vis + β·s_aud + γ·s_beat) combines s_vis (TransNetv2 visual cut probability), s_aud (RMS energy-change score), and s_beat ∈ {0.25, 0.5, 1.0} (beat position from a postprocessing-free beat tracker). The cut-type prior τ_N acts as an upper envelope: CONTINUE shots never fully reset LTM. Gating coefficients g_ltm(b) = 0.1 + 0.6b and g_stm(b) = 0.3 + 0.7b are monotone on non-overlapping ranges; g_ltm is bounded strictly above zero. A content-aware two-layer MLP (<50K parameters) adds a multiplicative refinement ρ to g_stm by pooling features from outgoing and incoming shots, clamped to [0.5, 1.0] for the first 500 steps. Training uses AdamW (lr = 1×10^-5 for the model, 5×10^-5 for the gate MLP), BFloat16 precision, DeepSpeed Zero, on 4 nodes of 8 NVIDIA A800 GPUs. The diffusion loss is masked to the final shot token only. Training data: 116k cinematic clips and 30k music-video clips (146k annotated shot records total) constructed through a 7-stage pipeline (TransNetv2 segmentation, subtitle removal, WhisperX transcription, DEMUCS audio separation, Qwen3-VL/Qwen3-Omni multimodal captioning, Gemini3-Pro narrative captioning, character anchoring, quality filtering). Three critical design decisions: (1) Fixed-size O(1) slots — well-justified by scalability, though concentrating everything in 6 total latent frames is a lossy compression that the ablations validate empirically rather than theoretically. (2) Rule-based monotone LTM lower bound — directly validated by the ablation showing a fully learned LTM gate degrades −0.29 NC and −0.50 Story at 6+ shots due to gradient-driven anchor suppression. (3) Fixed speaker-identity token rather than sliding audio window — justified by the target (voice timbre, not acoustic trajectory), though the paper does not test longer reference audio clips. Key Results All evaluations are on the authors' 200-sequence multi-cultural benchmark (3–9 shots, 6 ethnic regions, 10+ languages). Metrics: TA (ViCLIP text-video alignment ×100), TSIM (DINOv2 cosine similarity between consecutive shot frames), AES-V / AES-A (visual/audio aesthetics via Audiobox), CLAP (audio-caption similarity), and Gemini-2.5-Pro ratings 1–5 for NC (narrative coherence), Story, Char, Pace, Cult. I2V conditioning: UnityShots(I2V) leads LTX-2 by +3.87 TA (20.62 vs. 16.75), +0.40 NC (4.38 vs. 3.53 — the strongest open-source baseline at NC is MOVA at 3.98), +0.97 Story (4.25 vs. 3.28), and +0.63 Char (4.54 vs. 3.91). Against closed-source Kling: UnityShots leads NC (+0.13), Story (+0.10), and AES-A (+0.47) while Kling leads Char (4.75 vs. 4.54) and AES-V (0.610 vs. 0.563), consistent with Kling's larger training scale providing higher per-frame fidelity. T2V conditioning: UnityShots(T2V) outperforms HoloCine on every shared metric — TA 19.17 vs. 18.16, TSIM 0.451 vs. 0.401, NC 4.13 vs. 3.51, Story 3.83 vs. 3.22. HoloCine generates no audio so audio comparison is one-sided. R2V conditioning: UnityShots(R2V) outperforms DreamID-Omni on 8 of 9 metrics, with largest gaps on multi-shot coherence (+0.52 NC, +0.48 Story, +0.54 Pace) and audio (AES-A +1.01, CLAP +0.008). Long-sequence (6+ shots): UnityShots achieves NC=5.00, Story=5.00, Char=5.00 (Gemini ratings) vs. MOVA 4.71/4.50/4.93 (Table 4), the subset where dual memory has maximum perceptual impact. Surprising ablation finding: adding STM alone lowers NC versus the no-memory baseline (4.30 vs. 4.45), showing that a local short-term anchor without the global LTM anchor actively degrades narrative coherence by competing with the text prompt rather than reinforcing it. Only the full dual-slot system simultaneously exceeds no-memory on all four coherence metrics. Human evaluation (32 participants, pairwise win-rate on 4-shot sequences): UnityShots wins majority preference on identity consistency, audio continuity, text faithfulness, and overall quality against all open-source baselines. Against DreamID-Omni: 63% / 20% / 17% (win/tie/lose) on identity, 65% / 18% / 17% on audio, 67% / 19% / 14% overall. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The dual-slot fixed-size recurrent video memory bank with asymmetric boundary-conditioned gating is a novel architectural mechanism for cross-shot identity persistence in a diffusion transformer; Strata-RoPE is a new positional encoding scheme for distinguishing memory strata without extra tokens; the 200-sequence multi-cultural benchmark with per-boundary transition labels spanning 6 ethnic regions and 10+ languages fills a genuine gap in existing evaluation infrastructure (prior benchmarks are predominantly Western cinematic). The adaptation of persistent memory from language modeling (per [52]) to dual-stream video diffusion with audio-visual boundary signals is genuinely novel in combination, even if individual components (RoPE, AdaLN, diffusion transformers) are established. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: Table 3 isolates +STM only, +LTM only, and full dual-slot across I2V and R2V conditioning. Table 4 ablates LTM gating strategy (Rule+Learned vs. Learned+Learned vs. Rule+Rule) across three sequence-length brackets (3–4, 5, 6+ shots). No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported for the Gemini-based ratings (NC, Story, Char, Pace, Cult); TSIM and TA are deterministic so reproducibility is higher there. Code, model weights, benchmark, and companion agent system are released at https://github.com/JIA-Lab-research/UnityShots under a permissive open-source license. Main concern: the benchmark is fully author-constructed (Gemini3-Pro story design, NanoBanana/QwenImage first frames, ElevenLabs TTS reference audio, proprietary tooling), creating a potential distribution match between benchmark construction and system design. The fairness protocol in Table 2 (QwenImage first frames for all methods) partially addresses the first-frame confound but not the broader benchmark construction concern. All metrics are computed by the authors using shared scripts — consistent but not independently verified. Deployability (SYSTEMS profile) - Practical: The companion MCP agent system exposes per-shot generation as Claude Code-compatible tools and runs cloud-only without a local GPU, accessible to any user with a Claude Code, Claude Desktop, or VS Code MCP client. The base model (LTX-2.3, 22B) requires A800-class hardware for fine-tuning. - Efficient: The dual-slot memory bank is O(1) in sequence length (2+4 latent frames regardless of shot count), a direct improvement over linearly growing keyframe banks. Inference compute per shot relative to the base LTX-2.3 is not separately quantified beyond the added memory slot tokens. - Effective: Component ablations (Tables 3–4) confirm the full dual-slot system is necessary — neither slot alone achieves the same NC, and the rule-based LTM gate outperforms the learned gate at 6+ shots. Evidence is measured on the authors' own benchmark, not an independent one. - Scalable: Long-sequence robustness is demonstrated up to 6+ shots, with cumulative NC advantage growing with shot count (Figure 8b). The paper does not test sequences beyond 9 shots (the upper training bound), so behavior at 20–50 shots remains a claim rather than a measurement. The work delivers model checkpoints, evaluation code, the benchmark, and a companion MCP agent system under open-source license. Strengths - The asymmetric LTM/STM design with a rule-based monotone lower bound on the LTM coefficient is directly validated by the ablation demonstrating that a learned LTM gate fails on long sequences due to gradient-driven suppression — this is a real empirical insight that would not be obvious from prior memory-augmented generation literature. - The boundary score combining visual (TransNetv2), audio energy, and beat-tracker signals into a single gating signal correctly models the multi-modal nature of cinematic and music-video cuts, with the beat-tracker weight γ=1.0 for music-video vs. γ=0.0 for cinematic content reflecting genuine domain understanding. - The fixed speaker-identity reference token achieves cross-shot voice timbre preservation at O(1) cost with no sliding audio window, and the random reference-audio dropout (p=0.1) enabling text-only fallback is a practical engineering decision that broadens deployment scenarios. - The multi-cultural benchmark spanning 6 ethnic regions and 10+ languages with per-boundary transition labels addresses a real gap in evaluation infrastructure for multi-shot generation, where existing benchmarks are predominantly Western cinematic. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state (Section 5): occasional instability in subtitle rendering across shots, and visual degradation in scenes with multiple simultaneous speakers or compositionally complex layouts, both attributed to base-model capacity. They also directly acknowledge the identity-conditioning / cultural-authenticity tradeoff — the Cult metric drops from 4.20 (no memory) to 3.27–3.33 (memory-augmented) because the identity token suppresses culturally-specific visual details the backbone would otherwise infer from text prompts. The authors note this is a systemic property of the memory mechanism, confirmed by the per-region ablation showing the Cult gap is consistent across all six ethnic regions (0.81–0.96 magnitude), not a bias toward any one group. This is a credibility-positive acknowledgment. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The evaluation benchmark is entirely author-constructed using the authors' own tooling. An independently assembled benchmark (e.g., licensed short films with known shot boundaries and human-verified character identities) would provide cleaner evidence that the system generalizes beyond its own construction distribution. - The Gemini-2.5-Pro LLM evaluator rates narratives designed by Gemini3-Pro during benchmark construction — potential circularity if the story structure and vocabulary used in construction are what Gemini favors in evaluation. No human vs. Gemini calibration is reported for NC/Story/Char/Pace beyond the 32-participant user study (which covers only 4-shot sequences and does not include Cult or Pace ratings). - Sequence length in both training and evaluation is bounded at 3–9 shots. The paper claims the O(1) memory design supports unlimited length in principle, but performance at 20–50 shots (a realistic short-film scale) is not tested or even estimated. - Audio comparisons against Kling (ambient audio) and HoloCine (no audio) are structurally one-sided: AES-A and CLAP favor speaker-anchored audio by construction, making the audio margins misleading as a measure of audio quality per se. - The content-aware STM MLP (<50K parameters) is warm-up clamped for only 500 steps and is not separately ablated against the rule-based g_stm baseline; its isolated contribution to the full system is unclear. Bottom Line Practitioners building story-to-video pipelines will find the dual-slot memory architecture, boundary gating mechanism, MCP agent system, and released benchmark directly actionable, but the fully author-constructed evaluation benchmark and LLM-judged metrics mean the reported margins should be treated as promising internal evidence rather than independently validated claims.
Recommended 2606.21661 PDF
#42 — Innovative (69)

BioMatrix: Towards a Comprehensive Biological Foundation Model Spanning the Modality Matrix of Sequences, Structures, and Language (19 ▲ )

Qizhi Pei, Zhimeng Zhou, Yi Duan, Yiyang Zhao, Wei Li, Han Guo, Liang He, Chengping Li, Chang-Yu Hsieh, Conghui He, Rui Yan, Lijun Wu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BioMatrix, a multimodal biological foundation model that natively integrates molecular sequences (SMILES and SELFIES), molecular 3D structures, protein sequences, protein 3D backbone structures, and natural language within a single decoder-only next-token prediction backbone, achieving SOTA or competitive performance on 77 of 80 downstream biological tasks. - Why it's important: Every prior multimodal biological model either confines itself to a single entity type or covers multiple entities without explicit structural modeling and natural language in one unified token space, forcing practitioners to stitch together specialized models for cross-modal and cross-entity tasks such as structure-grounded affinity prediction and text-conditioned conformer generation. - How it does it: All modalities are discretized into a single extended Qwen3 vocabulary through a unified tokenization scheme — SELFIES-aligned joint 1D+3D tokens for molecules (via a modified MolStrucTok with a branch-decoupled VQ-VAE decoder) and factored residue-level AA tokens paired with GCP-VQVAE structure tokens for proteins — then trained under one next-token prediction objective on a 304.4-billion-token continual pretraining corpus followed by task-group-specific supervised fine-tuning. - The good: On property-conditioned molecular conformer generation, BioMatrix-4B reduces MAE 3-4× over the strongest prior method NExT-Mol on all three electronic-structure targets (ε_HOMO: 205→53 meV, ε_LUMO: 235→54 meV, Δε: 297→81 meV), directly validating the value of placing property tokens and geometry tokens in the same discrete space under one objective. - The missing: No entity-level contamination filtering was performed between the 304.4B-token pretraining corpus and the downstream SFT/evaluation benchmarks, so the degree to which benchmark overlap inflates the 77/80 SOTA claim is unknown and unquantified. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers building biological foundation models or seeking a single generalist backbone for molecule–protein discovery tasks should read this; practitioners looking for a plug-and-play tool for structure-based docking or complex-level ligand design should note that BioMatrix cannot currently represent biomolecular complexes (disjoint 3D token spaces for molecules and proteins).
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (large-scale systems paper). Novelty (78) is the strongest dimension — a genuinely new unified discrete tokenization reconciling molecular and protein 1D/3D structures with natural language under one next-token objective, with no prior model spanning all five modality-entity combinations. Non-Obviousness (72) is high: the SMILES-vs-SELFIES typology and the 3-4× MAE reduction on property-conditioned conformer generation validate the design hypothesis rather than merely not hurting. Rigor (62) is materially reduced by the absence of entity-level decontamination across the 304.4B-token corpus (the 77/80 SOTA claim is uncertain in magnitude), no error bars or multiple seeds, reliance on ESMFold as a proxy oracle, and unreported training cost; it remains a preprint, though baselines are current and ablations informative. Completeness (68) and Resilience (62) are held up by unusually honest limitation disclosure but pulled down by the contamination caveat. Impact (78) reflects released weights and code and a credible generalist backbone. Weighted: 0.20*78 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*78 = 69.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 68 Resilience 62 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Biomolecular entities — small molecules and proteins — are described by three complementary views: 1D sequences (SMILES, SELFIES, amino acid strings), 3D spatial structures, and natural-language annotations encoding experimental context and biological knowledge. Practical tasks in drug discovery, protein engineering, and mechanism understanding routinely require reasoning over multiple views and multiple entity types simultaneously: a text-conditioned molecule design query, a structure-grounded binding affinity prediction, or a joint protein sequence–structure co-generation task each demand capabilities that no single prior model provides. Existing models split along two axes: models that fuse sequence, structure, and text (e.g., BioT5+, SciReasoner) handle only a single entity type (molecules or proteins, not both), while models spanning multiple entity types (AlphaFold3, LOGOS) either omit natural language or rely on adapter-based designs where biomolecular content enters via continuous encoder features rather than natively generated discrete tokens. The gap is integration: reconciling heterogeneous tokenizers for molecules and proteins with the text vocabulary under a single generative objective. Key Idea The single core technical insight is that all five modalities — molecular 1D line notations (SMILES, SELFIES), molecular 3D conformers, protein 1D amino acid sequences, protein 3D backbone coordinates, and natural language — can be serialized into one extended discrete vocabulary and consumed by a standard autoregressive next-token prediction objective, making tasks that were previously architecturally distinct (folding, inverse folding, captioning, affinity regression, cross-entity co-generation) simply different conditional generation patterns over the same token stream. The enabling trick is the pairing strategy: for molecules, each SELFIES atom token is composed with its MolStrucTok structural code into a single joint vocabulary entry (11,294 entries covering 99% of corpus co-occurrences), binding chemical identity to local 3D geometry in one token; for proteins, sequence and structure tokens are kept factored (26 AA tokens + 4,096 GCP-VQVAE codes independently) to avoid a prohibitively large joint vocabulary at protein lengths. Methodology BioMatrix proceeds in three stages. First, a unified tokenization scheme is constructed: the molecular structure tokenizer adapts MolStrucTok with a branch-decoupled VQ-VAE decoder (four separate MLP heads, one per geometric component — radial distance, polar angle, azimuthal magnitude, azimuthal sign) replacing the original shared trunk, improving QM9-2014 per-component RMSD by ~0.1 Å; the protein structure tokenizer adopts GCP-VQVAE Large as-is, encoding backbone geometry into a 4,096-entry codebook via a SE(3)-equivariant GCPNet encoder and reconstructing backbone coordinates via a transformer decoder. Modality-specific boundary tokens delimit heterogeneous segments within the token stream. New vocabulary embeddings are initialized by averaging frozen Qwen3 subword embeddings of natural-language descriptions of each new token (description-based initialization), avoiding random-initialization instability. Second, continual pretraining is performed on 304.4 billion tokens spanning four categories: general and scientific text (105.3B), molecule-centric data from PubChem, PCQM4Mv2, MolTextNet (73.7B, exposing both 1D and 3D views with stochastic composition), protein-centric data from UniRef50, RCSB PDB, Swiss-Prot, TrEMBL, and AlphaFold DB (77.4B, using a three-view instance pattern to expose isolated and jointly grounded views), and cross-entity interleaved data including biomedical text with inline entity structures via BERN2 named entity linking, and molecule–protein/protein–protein interaction data from BindingDB, STITCH, CrossDocked2020, AlphaSeq, and PPIRef (48.0B). Training runs for 36.4K steps (~1 epoch) on 64 H100 GPUs with batch size 1,024 and maximum sequence length 8,192, using AdamW with cosine schedule and peak LR 2×10⁻⁴. Third, supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is performed across 80 tasks in 6 categories. A key design decision: rather than one monolithic SFT model, a small set of task-group-specific variants is trained because biological SFT data is pattern-monolithic within sub-tasks, and mixing large-data sub-tasks with small-data ones causes large-data gradients to swamp small-data ones. Three critical design decisions that are well-justified: (1) the branch-decoupled decoder — motivated by geometric heterogeneity of the four descriptor components, empirically validated by RMSD improvement; (2) joint molecule tokens over factored — motivated by the need for per-atom alignment between SELFIES and 3D codes, empirically validated by compact vocabulary; (3) factored protein tokens — motivated by the near-prohibitive size of 71,737 AA–structure joint pairs, with the tradeoff accepted explicitly. Key Results BioMatrix achieves SOTA or competitive performance on 77 of 80 tasks (arXiv preprint, not peer-reviewed). Selected headline numbers follow. Molecule 1D: unconditional generation on GuacaMol — validity 0.999, uniqueness 1.0, novelty 0.977 for BioMatrix-1.7B (SELFIES), best overall balance among LLMs; name conversion I2F EM 94.09% (BioMatrix-4B SMILES, vs. SciReasoner-8B at 92.65%); property prediction HIV accuracy 97.56% (vs. literature SOTA 97.00%); molecule captioning METEOR 61.5, EM 65.07 (surpassing literature SOTA 51.5 and 31.70); forward synthesis EM 77.94% (best among LLMs, vs. literature SOTA 78.70%); MoleculeQA total 73.78% (vs. prior best MolCA-1.3B at 64.79%). Molecule 3D: unconditional generation FCD 0.064/0.066 (matching training distribution, competitive with NExT-Mol at 0.070); bond-length MMD residual of 1.05 (vs. NExT-Mol 1.15×10⁻¹) closed to 0.23 after MMFF refinement; property-conditioned generation MAE: ε_HOMO 53 meV, ε_LUMO 54 meV, Δε 81 meV (all best, 3–4× improvement over NExT-Mol). Protein 1D: fold type prediction family/superfamily accuracy 87.25%/30.22% (best); annotation prediction keyword F1 91.26%, GO term F1 86.33% on UniProtSeq (competitive with SciReasoner-8B); inverse folding AAR 75.50% (best, vs. DPLM-2-3B at 61.67%). Protein 3D: folding TM=0.84/RMSD=5.38 (competitive with DPLM-2-3B at TM=0.86/RMSD=4.54, below ESMFold 0.93/2.84); sequence-structure co-generation scTM=0.977/scRMSD=1.50 (best, edging above native-PDB reference 0.904/4.62); unconditional backbone generation scTM=0.963/scRMSD=3.07 (best among tested methods including RFDiffusion 0.914 and DPLM-2-650M 0.945). Interaction: 1D molecule–protein affinity RMSE=1.268 on PDBBind (best), 1.030 on BindingDB (new SOTA vs. literature 1.340); 3D structure-grounded affinity PDBBindv2020 RMSE=1.260, Pearson=0.737, MAE=0.972 (best on 3/4 metrics, surpassing TankBind at RMSE=1.346). Substitution component editing (SubComp SR=0.6082 for BioMatrix-4B SMILES) trails Claude-3.5 (0.8104), a notable gap the paper discusses but does not close. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The unified tokenization design that reconciles molecular and protein 3D structures with sequence and natural language in a single decoder-only vocabulary is genuinely new; no prior model simultaneously achieves native multimodality across all five modality-entity combinations shown in Table 1. The large-scale empirical evidence that a single next-token backbone can match or surpass specialized models on 77/80 tasks is a substantive finding, not merely a combination of known techniques applied to a new setting. The SMILES-vs-SELFIES task-representation match (surface anchoring favors SMILES; validity-by-construction favors SELFIES; neutral on semantic tasks) is an unanticipated empirical finding with immediate practical design implications. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: the branch-decoupled decoder vs. monolithic decoder on QM9-2014 RMSD; the three-view instance pattern vs. sequence-only or structure-only views (motivated qualitatively); SMILES vs. SELFIES across all 1D molecular tasks serving as a large-scale controlled comparison. Error bars and significance tests are absent; no multiple seeds are reported for any benchmark. The 1.7B vs. 4B comparison provides a within-family scaling check, but the two checkpoints are not identical in parameter count, making it a scale study rather than a variance study. Code is released at GitHub (QizhiPei/biomatrix) and model weights on HuggingFace (QizhiPei/biomatrix). The paper does not explicitly state which checkpoints are released — task-group SFT variants, the all-tasks merged model, or both. A methodological concern: the paper explicitly acknowledges no entity-level decontamination between the 304.4B-token pretraining corpus and the SFT/evaluation corpora. Molecules and proteins appearing in PubChem, Swiss-Prot, or RCSB PDB during pretraining may also appear in benchmarks like MoleculeQA, OPI, or PFUD. This is not a controlled benchmark setting, and the extent of contamination is unknown. The paper correctly frames its goal as a strong base model rather than a contamination-controlled benchmark claim, but the 77/80 headline number should be interpreted with this caveat. Baselines are current and strong: SciReasoner (2025), DPLM-2 (2025), NExT-Mol (2025), TankBind, RFDiffusion, ESMFold. The evaluation uses official benchmark splits and protocols uniformly, which is a positive. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems paper — a trained model evaluated at scale across 80 tasks). - Practical: Training requires 64 H100 GPUs; inference requires a 1.7B or 4B parameter model plus modality-specific de-tokenizers (MolStrucTok decoder and GCP-VQVAE decoder), all of which run on commercially available hardware at inference time. - Efficient: Continual pretraining costs are not reported explicitly (GPU-hours, FLOPs, or wall-clock are not stated); inference costs are comparable to Qwen3-1.7B/4B plus VQ-VAE decoding passes, which is modest relative to diffusion-based geometric baselines requiring iterative denoising. - Effective: Evidence is strong across 77/80 tasks, with the largest improvements on cross-modal and cross-entity tasks where specialized models face architectural barriers; the three tasks where BioMatrix trails (SubComp, certain small-data interaction sub-tasks) are identified. - Scalable: Pretraining scale (304.4B tokens, ~1 epoch) and inference tested only at standard benchmark scales (molecules up to drug-like size, proteins up to typical PDB chain length); behavior on significantly longer proteins, macromolecular assemblies, or very large molecule libraries is not evaluated or claimed. The paper delivers a software artifact: trained model weights and code are publicly released. Strengths - The unified discrete token space is demonstrated to work across all five modality-entity combinations with a single next-token objective, and the empirical gains on cross-modal tasks (3-4× MAE reduction on property-conditioned conformer generation; new SOTA on structure-grounded binding affinity without any pair-specific architecture) directly validate the design hypothesis rather than simply observing that it does not hurt. - The residue-aligned tokenization scheme makes protein folding and inverse folding mathematically symmetric token-level alignment problems on the same vocabulary, and the AAR=75.50% on inverse folding (vs. DPLM-2-3B at 61.67%) without any direction-specific head validates this architectural choice concretely. - The large-scale SMILES vs. SELFIES comparison, run under an otherwise identical recipe, yields a clean empirical typology with a mechanistic explanation (context-dependent token semantics in SELFIES; surface regularity in SMILES), which is a practical design principle the field has lacked. - The discussion of why task-group SFT outperforms monolithic SFT on biological data (within-sub-task pattern monolithicity causes large-data gradients to overwhelm small-data ones) is an honest and informative negative result about a standard training recipe. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront five limitations: (1) molecular and protein 3D structures use disjoint codebooks anchored in disjoint geometric reference frames, so BioMatrix cannot represent or generate biomolecular complexes and is inapplicable to structure-based docking or pocket-conditioned ligand design; (2) no entity-level decontamination was performed between pretraining and evaluation corpora; (3) domain specialization partially erodes general-purpose language and reasoning capabilities inherited from Qwen3, and no benchmarks of general instruction-following or reasoning are reported; (4) the task-group SFT recipe leaves a non-trivial gap on small-data sub-tasks relative to the all-tasks merged model, and closing this gap via curriculum design or better data balancing is an open problem; (5) coverage does not extend to nucleic acids, carbohydrates, or lipids. The authors confront these directly without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps The most consequential unacknowledged issue beyond what the authors state is evaluation dependence on ESMFold as a proxy oracle: protein structure quality (pLDDT, scTM for co-generation and backbone generation) is assessed via ESMFold predictions of generated sequences rather than experimentally determined structures. This introduces ESMFold's own error and biases into the evaluation loop — a model that generates sequences easy for ESMFold to predict confidently will be systematically rewarded, regardless of whether those sequences fold well experimentally. The paper uses this consistently across tasks but never quantifies the ESMFold proxy gap. A second gap is the absence of computational cost reporting. Pretraining GPU-hours are not stated, preventing practitioners from estimating whether reproducing BioMatrix is feasible. Given the 304.4B token corpus on 64 H100 GPUs at 36.4K steps, the cost is substantial and not transparent. The baseline selection for interaction tasks is not always fully current: the PDBBindv2020 3D affinity comparison omits recent 2025 structure-based affinity models beyond TankBind (2022), potentially understating how much headroom specialists have. The claim "77 out of 80 tasks" aggregates tasks of very different difficulty, data scale, and benchmark quality. No statistical significance testing, confidence intervals, or per-task variance estimates accompany any reported number, making it impossible to determine how many of the 77 wins are within benchmark noise. Bottom Line BioMatrix is the most complete attempt to date at a unified biological foundation model spanning all five modality-entity combinations, and its concrete gains on cross-modal and cross-entity tasks — particularly the 3-4× MAE improvement on property-conditioned conformer generation and the SOTA inverse folding AAR — make it a credible starting point for practitioners who need a single generalist backbone, though the absence of contamination controls, lack of complex-level 3D support, and unreported training cost limit immediate production use.
Recommended 2606.22138 PDF
#43 — Innovative (68)

SproutRAG: Attention-Guided Tree Search with Progressive Embeddings for Long-Document RAG (8 ▲ )

Amirhossein Abaskohi, Issam H. Laradji, Peter West, Giuseppe Carenini · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SPROUTRAG, an attention-guided hierarchical RAG framework that constructs a binary tree over sentence-level chunks using learned inter-sentence attention from a 1.3B-parameter sentence-level language model (SLLM), enabling multi-granularity retrieval via hierarchical beam search without any inference-time LLM calls. - Why it's important: RAG systems that use fixed-size or delimiter-based chunking force a hard tradeoff between fine-grained precision and cross-sentence contextual coherence, and existing hierarchical methods either rely on LLM-generated summaries that discard fine-grained details or require expensive LLM calls during retrieval. - How it does it: During offline indexing the SLLM's inter-sentence attention matrices are aggregated across heads and layers via learned scalar weights, and these weights guide bottom-up binary tree construction; internal nodes store mean-pooled progressive embeddings, and retrieval uses hierarchical beam search to collect candidates at multiple tree levels. - The good: SPROUTRAG achieves best precision on all four evaluation benchmarks and improves Information Efficiency (IE = Recall × Precision) by 6.1% on average over the strongest baseline, using only 4,382 online tokens per query and 193ms latency. - The missing: The SLLM encoder is fixed to SentenceVAE (1.3B parameters); the paper does not test whether the attention-guided tree structure transfers to a different SLLM architecture, leaving encoder sensitivity uncharacterized. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building multi-granularity RAG systems over long structured documents (legal, scientific, multi-hop QA) who need to balance retrieval precision and contextual coherence without inference-time LLM overhead.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (trained retrieval pipeline, code released). Rigor (70) and Non-Obviousness (68) anchor the Innovative score: the ablation suite is thorough (training objectives, tree/retrieval design, beam width, lambda across all four benchmarks with 3-run std), and the learned head-layer attention weighting to defeat proximity bias is a non-obvious contribution with a clean ablation (uniform averaging drops Dragonball IE 39.05 to 30.15). Novelty (66) reflects a real integration of learned attention-weighted hierarchy construction with multi-granularity beam search, trained without inference-time LLM calls, though components are individually known. Training uses a different corpus (CLaRa) than the four evaluation benchmarks, reducing Goodhart concerns. Resilience (66) and Completeness (70) are capped by encoder sensitivity left uncharacterized (fixed to SentenceVAE 1.3B), no significance tests, and PageIndex winning on end-to-end HotpotQA/WebQuestions F1, which the paper attributes to generation strategy without isolating it. Weighted: 0.20*66 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*66 + 0.10*68 = 68.
empirical Novelty 66 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 70 Resilience 66 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation RAG systems segment documents into retrievable chunks and select the most relevant ones to construct the LLM's context. Fine-grained chunking (sentence or proposition level) offers high precision but fragments related evidence, making it hard to recover multi-sentence arguments or clauses. Coarse chunking preserves coherence but introduces irrelevant text that dilutes the signal. This tradeoff is acute for cross-paragraph retrieval tasks such as multi-hop QA and legal document search, where answering a query correctly requires synthesizing evidence scattered across several sentences. Prior approaches address this through LLM-guided chunking (Meta-Chunking, MoC), hierarchical summarization trees (RAPTOR), pairwise sentence expansion with LLM filtering (SAKI-RAG), or graph-based entity relations (GraphRAG, LightRAG). Each sacrifices either retrieval efficiency, fine-grained detail, or cross-chunk dependencies. No existing method jointly learns an evidence hierarchy and retrieval embeddings from the same model without inference-time LLM calls.
