Key Idea The core insight is that a sentence-level transformer's inter-sentence attention already encodes semantic co-relevance between sentence pairs, but naive uniform averaging over heads and layers introduces a proximity bias (nearby sentences receive high attention regardless of semantic content). By introducing learnable scalar weights per (layer, head) pair and training them jointly with a retrieval contrastive objective and an attention regularization loss, the model discovers which attention heads best reflect global semantic co-relevance for document structure. This learned weighted attention then guides a bottom-up binary tree construction, where the most mutually attended sentence pair is merged first, their parent stores a progressive embedding (mean of children), and the process recurses. At retrieval time, beam search over the resulting tree collects candidates at leaf (sentence), internal (multi-sentence), and subtree granularities without any additional LLM calls. Methodology SPROUTRAG runs in two phases. Offline indexing: (1) A document D is split into sentence-level chunks S = {s1,...,sn}. (2) An SLLM (SentenceVAE, 1.3B parameters) encodes the full sequence, producing sentence embeddings {e(si)} and attention matrices Attn^(l,h) ∈ ℝ^(n×n) for each layer l and head h. (3) A weighted aggregation A_ij = Σ_{l,h} w_{l,h} Attn^(l,h)_{ij} is computed, where w_{l,h} = softmax(α_{l,h}) are learned scalars. The matrix is symmetrized: M_ij = (A_ij + A_ji) / 2. (4) A binary tree is built bottom-up by iteratively merging the pair of active nodes with the highest mutual attention score; the parent embedding is e(p) = (e(u) + e(v)) / 2; the parent's relation to remaining nodes is updated by single-linkage: M_{pr} = max(M_{ur}, M_{vr}). (5) Each internal node thus stores a progressive embedding compositionally representing its subtree. Online retrieval: The query is encoded to e(q) via the same SLLM. Hierarchical beam search starts at the root with beam B0 = {v_root}; at each step, children of beam nodes are scored by cosine similarity sim(q, v) = e(q)^T e(v) / (||e(q)|| ||e(v)||) and the top-b nodes are retained. All visited nodes whose similarity exceeds a learned threshold δ are collected as candidates C. Candidates are reranked by QWEN3-RERANKER-4B, and top-k are passed to QWEN3-8B for answer generation. Training uses a joint objective L = L_ret + λ L_attn. L_ret is InfoNCE contrastive loss (temperature τ = 0.05) over query-passage pairs from 30K CLaRa examples. L_attn = -1/|G| Σ_{(si,sj)∈G} log((A_ij + A_ji)/2) encourages co-relevant sentence pairs within a positive passage to receive high mutual attention. Training runs for 3 epochs with AdamW (lr 2×10^{-5} for SLLM, 1×10^{-3} for aggregation scalars, batch 32, 8 NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs). Default beam width b = 5, λ = 0.1. Three critical design decisions: (1) Learned head-layer weights instead of uniform averaging — ablation confirms uniform averaging drops Dragonball IE from 39.05 to 30.15 due to proximity bias. (2) Single-linkage (max) update for parent-to-remaining relations — preserves long-range connections as the hierarchy grows; justified by contrast with greedy search ablation (IE 32.86 vs 39.05). (3) Joint training combining L_ret and L_attn — removing L_ret drops Dragonball IE to 27.67; removing L_attn drops it to 31.83, confirming both are load-bearing. Key Results Retrieval is measured by Information Efficiency (IE = Recall × Precision), averaged over k ∈ {1, 3, 5} on four benchmarks: - Dragonball: SPROUTRAG 39.05 ± 0.38 vs best baseline MoC 30.99 ± 0.44 (+8.06 points) - SCI-DOCS: SPROUTRAG 97.66 ± 0.21 vs best baseline MoC 93.01 ± 0.34 (+4.65 points) - LegalBench-RAG: SPROUTRAG 19.74 ± 0.29 vs best baseline MoC 14.84 ± 0.27 (+4.90 points) - MS MARCO: SPROUTRAG 55.53 ± 0.40 vs best baseline SAKI-RAG 48.70 ± 0.44 (+6.83 points) SPROUTRAG achieves best precision on all four benchmarks. Gains are consistent across cutoffs k = 1, 3, and 5 (Tables 4–6). End-to-end generation (Table 2): HotpotQA F1 = 76.47 and WebQuestions F1 = 68.12, versus PageIndex's 79.36 / 70.81 (best) and GraphRAG's 72.18 / 64.73. SPROUTRAG's online cost is 4,382 Tok/Q and 193ms latency vs REFRAG (5,436 / 492ms), ReflectiveRAG (11,274 / 1,186ms), GraphRAG (16,238 / 2,317ms), and PageIndex (24,620 / 2,847ms). Baselines span 2024–2026 and include strong recent methods; the comparison is reasonably current. All generation experiments use the same QWEN3-8B generator and QWEN3-RERANKER-4B for baselines that require them, ensuring fair comparison. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the joint formulation of learned attention-weight aggregation for document hierarchy construction and multi-granularity hierarchical beam search retrieval, trained end-to-end without inference-time LLM calls. Individual components (contrastive retrieval training, attention-guided clustering, beam search) are each known; the novelty lies in their integration and the specific formulation of a progressive-embedding binary tree whose structure is learned jointly with retrieval quality. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough: Table 3 tests training objectives (3 variants), tree and retrieval design (4 variants), and hyperparameter sensitivity (beam width, λ) across all four benchmarks. Results are averaged over three independent runs with standard deviations reported throughout; no formal significance tests are run, but error bars are consistently small. Code is available on GitHub (mentioned in footnote 1; the SLLM base is at https://github.com/cavedweller509/SentenceVAE). Training data (30K CLaRa examples) is a different corpus from the four evaluation benchmarks, reducing Goodhart's Law concerns. One methodological note: the paper does not report the domain distribution of CLaRa relative to the test benchmarks, so the degree to which training distribution matches evaluation distribution is not fully characterized. The progressive embedding (mean pooling of children) is a fixed architectural choice; more expressive compositions are not ablated. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS — a trained RAG retrieval pipeline evaluated on standard benchmarks. - Practical: Inference requires only the 1.3B-parameter SLLM plus a 4B reranker; both run on commercially available A100 or equivalent GPUs, and the 193ms per-query latency is deployment-feasible. - Efficient: Online inference uses 4,382 Tok/Q, 3.6× fewer than REFRAG and 5.6× fewer than PageIndex; offline fine-tuning on 30K examples with 8 A100 80GB GPUs is a one-time cost that transfers across datasets without per-benchmark retraining. - Effective: IE improvements over all baselines on all four benchmarks, confirmed by ablations showing each component contributes, indicate the method genuinely solves multi-granularity retrieval rather than a proxy; end-to-end generation gains on HotpotQA and WebQuestions are smaller than retrieval gains, suggesting the generator is a bottleneck. - Scalable: Not evaluated at corpus scales beyond standard benchmarks; the binary tree has O(n) nodes for n sentences, so indexing scales linearly, but beam search traversal cost and the impact of tree depth at very large document counts are not measured or discussed. Software artifact: code available on GitHub; it is a trainable RAG pipeline, not a general-purpose library. Strengths - The learned head-layer weighting directly addresses proximity bias in transformer attention, with a clear ablation showing uniform averaging causes substantial IE degradation (39.05 → 30.15 on Dragonball); this is a non-obvious and well-justified contribution. - Achieving best precision on all four benchmarks simultaneously demonstrates the tree structure genuinely avoids retrieving over-broad or weakly-relevant context, not just a recall-precision tradeoff artifact. - The joint training objective cleanly integrates retrieval alignment and hierarchy learning in a single loss, eliminating the pipeline brittleness of methods that construct hierarchy in a separate step. - The efficiency profile (193ms, 4,382 Tok/Q) is substantially better than LLM-heavy alternatives, with a practical path to deployment on standard GPU hardware. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge three limitations: (1) the binary tree structure may be suboptimal when several sentences jointly form a semantic unit that should be grouped together; multi-branch trees could better model many-to-many dependencies; (2) the upfront fine-tuning cost is higher than using an off-the-shelf retriever without adaptation; (3) the tree is built offline and fixed, making it potentially inflexible for queries requiring query-specific evidence reorganization. These are confronted honestly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The SLLM encoder is fixed to SentenceVAE (1.3B parameters, trained for next-sentence prediction). Whether the attention signals in other SLLM architectures (e.g., standard bi-encoders, larger models) yield equally useful trees is unexamined; results may be architecture-specific. - The progressive embedding is mean pooling of children; this is ablated only against an embedding-similarity tree variant, not against attention-pooled or gated composition functions. The choice is likely suboptimal for deep subtrees. - Final task performance shows PageIndex wins on HotpotQA (79.36 vs 76.47 F1) and WebQuestions (70.81 vs 68.12 F1). The paper attributes this to PageIndex's reasoning-heavy LLM search, but does not test whether feeding SPROUTRAG's retrieved evidence to a comparable reasoning-heavy generator would close the gap — the comparison conflates retrieval quality with generation strategy. - The CLaRa training corpus domain composition relative to the four test benchmarks is not disclosed; the claimed cross-dataset generalization is demonstrated empirically but its robustness to large distribution shifts (e.g., highly specialized domains) is uncharacterized. - Evaluation documents are not characterized by length distribution; the behavior of hierarchical beam search on very short documents (where the tree is shallow) or extremely long documents (where the tree depth grows) is not reported. Bottom Line SPROUTRAG is a well-executed system paper with consistent retrieval gains, a thorough ablation study, and a compelling efficiency profile; practitioners building RAG systems over long structured documents — particularly legal or scientific corpora requiring multi-sentence evidence synthesis — should read it.
Recommended 2606.18381 PDF
#44 — Innovative (67)

Tangram: Hiding GPU Heterogeneity for Efficient LLM Parallelization

Y Tao, PF Silvestre, M Wagenländer, P Pietzuch · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TANGRAM, a system that enables existing homogeneous LLM parallelizers to generate high-throughput parallelization plans for heterogeneous GPU clusters by decoupling parallelization planning from GPU heterogeneity through a GPU island abstraction and dynamic-programming-based plan composition. - Why it's important: GPU clusters grow increasingly heterogeneous as organizations accumulate multiple GPU generations, yet existing heterogeneous parallelizers sacrifice expert parallelism, context parallelism, and memory-saving techniques (activation recomputation, ZeRO) to keep their search spaces tractable, yielding plans that are outperformed by homogeneous parallelizers even on heterogeneous hardware. - How it does it: TANGRAM groups GPUs with similar compute, memory, and interconnect characteristics into homogeneous "GPU islands," decomposes the model into contiguous "model slices," queries any existing homogeneous parallelizer for partial plans on each island/slice pair, and composes those partial plans into a globally workload-balanced pipeline via dynamic programming. - The good: TANGRAM achieves up to 2.3x higher training throughput than heterogeneous baselines (Metis, Sailor) on heterogeneous clusters while exactly matching homogeneous parallelizers (Aceso, Alpa) on homogeneous clusters, with pruning policies reducing planning time 27x (from 5.1 h to 12 min) without any plan quality degradation. - The missing: The largest evaluated cluster is 32 GPUs across 4 GPU types on Azure Cloud VMs; production clusters at hundreds or thousands of GPUs with 8+ GPU types are not tested, and two 2025-2026 baselines that appear in the comparison table (HyperTron, HARP) are absent from all experiments. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems researchers and infrastructure engineers managing mixed-generation GPU fleets who already use or are choosing a homogeneous parallelizer; the island abstraction is conceptually clean, the integration API is narrow and practical, and the empirical gains at small-to-medium scale are convincing.
Why this rank: Systems profile. The GPU-island decoupling insight (heterogeneity need not be co-optimized with parallelism selection) is a clean, non-obvious conceptual contribution (Non-Obviousness 75, Novelty 72), and the 4-function parallelizer interface plus pruning policies (27x planning-time reduction with no quality loss) make it practically reusable (Impact 68). Rigor (60) is the limiter: single-run measurements on co-tenant Azure VMs with no error bars, three baselines modified for comparison, and two recent baselines (HyperTron, HARP) in the taxonomy table but absent from all experiments. The 32-GPU/4-type scale is far below production, leaving Resilience (62) dependent on un-tested extrapolation. Weighted composite ~67.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Organizations that train LLMs regularly upgrade GPU infrastructure by purchasing newer GPUs while retaining older generations to amortize capital expenditure, producing clusters that mix compute capabilities (e.g., T4 vs. H100), memory capacities (16 GB vs. 94 GB), and interconnect bandwidths (PCIe vs. NVLink). Effective LLM training on such clusters requires choosing a parallelization plan — a combination of data, tensor, pipeline, context, and expert parallelisms plus memory-saving techniques (activation recomputation, ZeRO, memory offloading, gradient accumulation). Automatic heterogeneous parallelizers (Metis, Sailor, AMP, HetAuto) jointly search parallelism strategies and GPU placement, but GPU heterogeneity causes the search space to expand by up to 10^13 relative to the homogeneous case for a 40-layer model on a 512-GPU cluster with two GPU types, reaching 10^28 with four types. To stay tractable, these systems omit expert parallelism, context parallelism, and all memory-saving techniques, and they consequently underperform recent homogeneous parallelizers like Aceso by up to 2x on heterogeneous hardware. Key Idea Parallelization strategy selection can be decoupled from GPU placement because real clusters are typically composed of a small number of homogeneous GPU subgroups ("GPU islands") acquired in bulk purchases. By exposing these islands to existing homogeneous parallelizers as uniform compute surfaces, TANGRAM lets each parallelizer exploit its full feature set within an island. The heterogeneity is then handled entirely at the inter-island boundary through pipeline parallelism, which is communication-efficient (only activations cross island boundaries), memory-efficient (no model or data replication across islands), and orthogonal to intra-island parallelisms. Methodology TANGRAM operates as a four-stage pipeline. (1) GPU island construction: TANGRAM profiles each GPU node for compute capacity c_g, memory capacity m_g, and intra-node bandwidth b_g, then applies agglomerative clustering to partition the cluster into disjoint islands I that satisfy three formal constraints — compute/memory homogeneity within tolerance ε_c and ε_m (max/min ratio ≤ 1 + ε), intra-node network parity within ε_i, and inter-node network proximity (intra-island links at least as fast as any link leaving the island). (2) Model decomposition: TANGRAM interacts with any LLM parallelizer through a generic 4-function interface (slice_model, concatenate, profile, parallelize) to extract the smallest model atoms and merge them into contiguous model slices of varying sizes. (3) Model-slice/island enumeration with pruning: TANGRAM queries each parallelizer for partial plans on every model-slice/island pair, then prunes the enumeration using three policies — redundancy pruning (discard duplicate model slices identified by hash signature), imbalance pruning (discard pairs that would over- or under-subscribe island compute relative to cluster capacity), and infeasibility pruning (discard pairs predicted to exceed GPU memory). Queries are distributed across worker nodes. (4) Pipeline composition: A dynamic programming algorithm constructs sequences of model-slice/island plans to cover the full model, iteratively building sequences by prepending model-slice/island plans, bounding permissible stage imbalance by the slowest existing stage, memoizing feasible sequences, and eliminating resource-limited islands that would always introduce bottlenecks. The final pipeline uses 1F1B scheduling. Critical design decisions: (a) pipeline parallelism for inter-island composition is well-justified because it minimizes cross-island communication to activations only; (b) model layers as model atoms — rather than operators — is validated empirically in §6 to balance load and planning time; (c) the 4-function interface is the key abstraction enabling integration with diverse parallelizers (Alpa, Aceso, Mist, Galvatron) without modification to their internals. Key Results On heterogeneous cluster C1-compute-het (32 GPUs: 16 T4 + 16 V100-16GB on PCIe nodes), training GPT-3 6.7B, TANGRAM (paired with Aceso) achieves iteration times of ~20-30 s depending on T4/V100 ratio, versus Metis at ~60-140 s (2.1x speedup), Sailor at ~35-70 s (2.1x), Alpa at ~35-65 s (1.6x), and Aceso-heterogeneous at ~25-45 s (1.4x). On homogeneous T4-only and V100-only subsets, TANGRAM exactly reproduces Aceso's plans and matches its throughput, outperforming Metis by 3.1x and Sailor by 1.5x. For GShare MoE models (2.4B, 7.1B, 10B on C1-compute-het), TANGRAM-Alpa delivers 1.6x speedup at 2.4B, 2.1x at 7.1B, and 1.1x at 10B over standalone Alpa. For Mixtral-8x7B on C2-modern-het (4×A100 + 4×H100), TANGRAM reduces iteration time from 30 s to 22 s (1.4x) for the 16-layer trimmed model; for Qwen3-30B-A3B, improvement is 1.1x-1.1x. Pruning ablation: naïve enumeration takes 18,320 s; redundancy pruning alone cuts to 2,676 s (7x); imbalance pruning adds another 4x; infeasibility pruning adds 1.1x; total pruning yields 415 s (44x enumeration reduction, 27x end-to-end). Planning time scales roughly 4x as GPU islands increase from 1 to 4 on C3-full-het (31 s to 982 s for GPT-3 13B). Baselines include Alpa (2022, homogeneous), Aceso (2024, homogeneous), Metis (2024, heterogeneous), Sailor (2025, heterogeneous) — reasonably current, though HyperTron (2025) and HARP (2026) listed in Table 1 are not experimentally compared. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The GPU island abstraction and the three-phase architecture (island construction, partial plan enumeration with heterogeneity-guided pruning, DP pipeline composition) constitute a genuinely novel systems contribution. The decoupling insight — that heterogeneity need not be co-optimized with parallelism selection — is the paper's core conceptual contribution and is not a straightforward extension of prior work. Individual components (agglomerative clustering, DP, 1F1B scheduling) are standard, but their composition in this context is non-trivial and the integration API design enables reuse of an entire ecosystem of parallelizers. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and credible: §6.3 validates TANGRAM on homogeneous clusters (it degrades to the baseline parallelizer's result, confirming correctness), and §6.5 quantifies each pruning policy's contribution independently. However, no error bars, multiple seeds, or statistical significance tests are reported; each data point appears to be a single run, which is standard in systems papers but limits confidence when speedups are modest (1.1x-1.3x). Code is anonymized at submission time (GitHub URL redacted) and is therefore not yet publicly available. Experiments run on Azure Cloud VMs rather than a dedicated cluster, introducing potential noise from VM co-tenancy. Three baselines received modifications: Aceso's search budget was capped at 100 s, Metis's memory estimator was replaced with Aceso's, and Sailor's plan filtering was deactivated — these are described and appear reasonable but create mild comparability concerns. HyperTron and HARP appear in Table 1 with year-2025/2026 citations but are absent from all evaluation figures without explicit justification. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (TANGRAM is a built system evaluated on real GPU clusters). - Practical: Yes — all experiments use commercially available GPU instances (T4, V100-16/32 GB, A100-80 GB, H100-94 GB) on Azure Cloud VMs. - Efficient: Planning time is ~12-16 min for a 13B-parameter 40-layer model on 4 GPU islands after pruning, which the paper argues is negligible relative to multi-day training runs; profiling overhead per model-slice/island pair is not separately quantified. - Effective: On heterogeneous clusters, TANGRAM consistently finds higher-throughput plans than all evaluated baselines by leveraging the full parallelism feature set of homogeneous parallelizers; on homogeneous clusters it exactly reproduces baseline plans with no degradation, confirming the approach does not introduce lossy approximation. - Scalable: The 32-GPU clusters tested are small relative to real production deployments. Planning time grows roughly 4x per island doubling (1 island: 31 s → 4 islands: 982 s for GPT-3 13B); at 8-16 islands this trajectory suggests hours of planning time, but this regime is not tested and the paper does not claim it scales beyond 4 islands. TANGRAM delivers a software system integrating with Alpa, Aceso, Mist, and Galvatron runtimes; code is anonymized at submission but the paper indicates it will be open-sourced. Strengths - The decoupling insight is practically grounded: the observation that bulk GPU purchases naturally produce clusters with homogeneous subgroups is empirically supported and justifies the island abstraction without introducing approximation error inside islands. - The 4-function generic parallelizer interface (slice_model, concatenate, profile, parallelize) is a narrow but expressive systems contribution that enables TANGRAM to inherit the full feature set of any homogeneous parallelizer, including those supporting expert parallelism, context parallelism, and memory-saving techniques that heterogeneous parallelizers cannot afford. - The three pruning policies achieve a 27x planning time reduction with no change to composed plan quality — the ablation makes this a concrete, verifiable claim rather than an approximation. - Compatibility with Galvatron's Fully Sharded Expert Parallelism (FSEP) for Mixtral and Qwen3-MoE extends TANGRAM's utility to the current model landscape without requiring changes to TANGRAM's core logic, demonstrating real architectural generality. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge that TANGRAM's inter-island composition is restricted to pipeline parallelism, excluding plans that would require cross-island tensor parallelism. They note that for large MoE models (10B) where T4 and V100 memory is insufficient, TANGRAM cannot balance workloads and loses its advantage. They also acknowledge that some baseline modifications (Metis memory estimator, Sailor plan filtering) were necessary for fair comparison and that results at larger cluster scales are not provided. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The scale gap is significant: 32 GPUs across 4 types on Azure VMs is far below the scale of production LLM training clusters. Planning time at 8-16 islands is uncharted; the roughly linear-to-superlinear growth in Fig. 13 does not support extrapolation with confidence. - HyperTron (2025) and HARP (2026) appear in Table 1's taxonomy as the most recent systems but receive no experimental comparison. HARP uses ILP+XLA for heterogeneous PP planning, which makes it a directly relevant baseline; its omission is not justified in the text. - Single-run measurements on cloud VMs cannot distinguish algorithmic effects from infrastructure noise for modest speedups (1.1x-1.3x seen in MoE experiments). The absence of error bars or repeated runs is a reproducibility concern. - The sensitivity of island construction quality to the tolerance parameters (ε_c, ε_m, ε_i) is not studied. The paper asserts that bulk purchase patterns make island construction robust, but clusters composed of spot instances or diverse hardware mixes (common in academic or mid-scale commercial settings) may not satisfy this assumption, and no degradation analysis is provided. - Profiling-based execution time estimates drive the DP composition; the accuracy of these estimates and their effect on final plan throughput (vs. actual measured throughput) are not evaluated. Bottom Line Practitioners managing mixed-generation GPU fleets who already use Alpa, Aceso, or a comparable homogeneous parallelizer will gain 1.4x-2.3x throughput at 16-32 GPU scale with a clean integration path; the system has not been validated at production cluster scale and two recent baselines are conspicuously absent from the evaluation.
Recommended 2606.16907 PDF
#45 — Innovative (67)

Running the Gauntlet: Re-evaluating the Capabilities of Agents Beyond Familiar Environments (11 ▲ )

Mykola Vysotskyi, Runqi Lin, Grzegorz Biziel, Michal Zakrzewski, Sebastian Montagna, Damian Rynczak, Shreyansh Padarha, Kumail Alhamoud, Zihao Fu, William Lugoloobi, Kai Rawal, Hanna Yershova, Xander Davies, Taras Rumezhak, Guohao Li, Fazl Barez, Baoyuan Wu, Arkadiusz Drohomirecki, Yarin Gal, Chris Russell, Christopher Summerfield, Adam Mahdi, Volodymyr Karpiv, Philip Torr, Adel Bibi · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GauntletBench, a web-based benchmark of 100 vision-intensive tasks across five less-covered professional applications (Video Editor, Workflow Builder, 3D Modeller, Flight Analyser, Circuit Designer) designed to evaluate agent generalisation on temporal perception, graphical understanding, and 3D reasoning. - Why it's important: Existing agent benchmarks saturate on popular consumer-facing applications and simple interaction patterns, masking critical capability gaps; a benchmark that frontier agents cannot yet approach at human-level is necessary to drive meaningful progress measurement. - How it does it: Each application runs as a controlled, deterministic web environment with canvas-based rendering that deliberately blocks DOM/accessibility-tree shortcuts, forcing genuine visual perception; an automated two-stage LLM-as-a-judge pipeline (gpt-4o-mini for pairwise trajectory parsing, gpt-5.1 for final outcome scoring) combines with application-specific tailored objective evaluators to produce binary success rates and graded progress rates. - The good: The human-to-agent gap is stark and unambiguous: non-expert annotators achieve 80.8% success rate while the best agentic system (Claude-Opus-4.6 Computer Use) achieves only 19.1%, confirming that saturation on existing benchmarks does not generalise to professional-grade visual tasks. - The missing: The benchmark covers only five domains with 20 tasks each (100 total), and the human baseline uses only 3 annotators per application (15 total), which is too small to establish robust variance estimates or rule out task-selection bias. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or evaluating computer-use agents, particularly those who need a benchmark that is not yet near saturation; less relevant for practitioners focused on text-heavy form-filling or information-retrieval workflows.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (72) and Novelty (70) drive the score — five purpose-built canvas-rendered professional apps that block DOM shortcuts force genuine visual reasoning, and the 80.8% human vs. 19.1% best-agent gap is the cluster's cleanest calibration point. Rigor (62) is above the cluster norm: mean ± std over three runs, scale/reasoning/vision ablations, and judge validation by Cohen's κ against human annotations. The pull-downs are scale-related — only 5 domains, 100 tasks, 3 annotators per app with no inter-annotator agreement reported, and purpose-built apps not validated against real production software (Completeness 70, Resilience 62). Weighted composite: 0.15*70 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*62 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*62 + 0.30*72 = 67.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 70 Resilience 62 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Current web-agent benchmarks, including WebArena, REAL, and MiniWoB++, concentrate on consumer applications such as e-commerce, CRM, and form-based workflows, where agents now approach or exceed human performance on many tasks. This saturation means the benchmarks no longer differentiate capability levels among frontier systems, and they largely ignore perceptual skills required for professional software: reading timelines, interpreting node-graph canvases, reasoning about 3D spatial relationships, and tracking moving objects on a map. Researchers evaluating agents on existing benchmarks may therefore overestimate real-world readiness, because the tested applications do not exercise the visual and temporal reasoning that professional tools demand. Key Idea: The core insight is that forcing agents to operate exclusively through visual perception — by building applications on canvas-based rendering that exposes no meaningful DOM or accessibility tree — eliminates the shortcut that most current agents exploit (reading HTML structure rather than understanding the visual interface). Combined with applications deliberately chosen from less-covered professional domains, this design ensures that the benchmark measures genuine visual and spatial reasoning rather than structured-data retrieval dressed as agent evaluation. Methodology: GauntletBench is structured as a modular four-component pipeline: (1) environments, (2) applications, (3) tasks, and (4) an evaluation engine. Environments follow the standard MDP formulation E = (S, A, O, T, R) and expose a high-level action space (click, type, select, scroll, navigate) via a browser automation layer (Playwright). Agent observations are screenshots for all five applications; Video Editor and Workflow Builder additionally expose an accessibility tree, though it is insufficient alone for task completion. Five web applications are built with React/Next.js and canvas rendering; three (3D Modeller, Flight Analyser, Circuit Designer) are screenshot-only. Each application contains 20 tasks at three calibrated difficulty levels: 2 easy, 9 medium, 9 hard. Tasks follow a standardised three-level structure: application background, task description, and required result format. Evaluation uses two complementary metrics: Success Rate (binary, all checkpoints must pass) and Progress Rate (1–5 LLM-judged graded score). The LLM judge runs in two stages: Stage 1 uses gpt-4o-mini to parse consecutive screenshot pairs into structured trajectory events; Stage 2 uses gpt-5.1 to assign a final 1–5 score grounded primarily in visible final state. Each application additionally embeds a tailored objective evaluator that applies domain-specific matching: ±100 ms timestamp tolerance and fuzzy colour matching for Video Editor; UUID remapping and edge-structure matching for Workflow Builder; tolerance-aware and symmetry-aware matching for 3D Modeller; order-invariant set equality for Flight Analyser; truth-table equivalence for Circuit Designer. Three critical design decisions: (a) canvas-only rendering for three applications — justified to eliminate accessibility-tree shortcuts; (b) a two-stage judge rather than a single LLM call — justified by empirical agreement with human annotations (GPT-5.1 achieves the highest human agreement in Table 5); (c) tailored per-application evaluators rather than exact string matching — justified by the fact that human annotators cannot perform frame-precise edits, so strict matching would penalise correct solutions. Key Results: Across 14 frontier agents evaluated on 100 tasks (averaged over 3 independent runs), all open-source MLLM agents (Gemma-3-27B, Mistral-Large-3, Qwen-3-VL, Llama-4-Maverick) achieve 0.0% average success rate. API-based agents perform marginally better: Claude-Opus-4.6 reaches 12.3% SR (2.44 PR), Gemini-3.1-Pro 13.2% (2.84 PR). Among closed-source agent frameworks, Claude-Opus-4.6 Computer Use achieves the best result at 19.1% SR (3.1 PR), followed by Gemini Enterprise at 13.7% and GPT-5.4 Computer Use at 4.3%. Human annotators (CS background, 3 per application, 40-minute task limit) achieve 80.8% SR and 4.0 PR — a gap of more than 60 percentage points over the best agent. By application, Flight Analyser and Circuit Designer are the hardest for agents (best SR ≤ 11.7%), while Workflow Builder and Video Editor are comparatively more tractable for Claude-Opus-4.6 CU (43.3% and 43.3% on easy tasks respectively, but degrading sharply at medium and hard). Ablations on model scale show that larger models in a family improve both SR and PR while consuming fewer tokens per task (GPT-5.4 uses 307K ± 406K tokens at 2.3% SR vs. GPT-5.4-Nano at 504K ± 802K tokens and 0.0% SR). Extended reasoning helps sufficiently capable base models (Gemini-3.1-Pro jumps from 6.3% to 13.2% SR with high reasoning) but adds no benefit for weaker models (Qwen-3-VL with/without extended reasoning: identical). Enabling visual inputs improves average progress rates by 43.5% for Qwen and 15.5% for GPT families on the screenshot-accessible applications, confirming the visual grounding requirement. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark itself — five custom professional web applications, 100 tasks, tailored evaluators, and a two-stage LLM judge — is a genuine infrastructure contribution. The empirical finding that even the best frontier agentic system reaches only 19.1% success on human-feasible tasks, while human annotators exceed 80%, is a meaningful calibration point that prior benchmarks could not reveal. The individual components (LLM-as-a-judge, browser automation, canvas-based rendering) are combinations of known ideas applied to a new evaluation context. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: model scale, reasoning mode, and vision-vs-text-only are each tested. All results are reported as mean ± standard deviation over three independent runs, providing variance estimates. The LLM judge is validated against human annotations on 25 uniformly sampled trajectories per application; GPT-5.1 achieves the highest Cohen's κ and macro F1 (Table 5), providing principled judge selection. Code, task descriptions, and application links are stated as publicly available at the paper's listed homepage. The human baseline uses only 3 annotators per application (15 total across 5 applications), which is small for establishing tight variance bounds. The applications are purpose-built rather than mirroring real production software, which introduces a potential construction bias: tasks may be calibrated to human intuition in ways that systematically disadvantage agents trained on real-world application distributions. The paper evaluates only 100 tasks total, which limits statistical power for per-application and per-difficulty conclusions. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built benchmark infrastructure evaluated by deployment against 14 agents and human annotators). - Practical: All five web applications run in a standard browser via Playwright; no specialised hardware is required beyond what is needed to run the frontier models being evaluated. - Efficient: Evaluation cost scales with the model under test; the benchmark infrastructure itself (judge calls, evaluator scripts) adds gpt-4o-mini and gpt-5.1 API calls per trajectory but the authors do not report the monetary cost of running the full evaluation suite, which is a practical gap for labs considering adoption. - Effective: The benchmark credibly discriminates among current agents (range 0–19.1% SR) and between agents and humans (19.1% vs. 80.8%), validating its claim of measuring a genuine capability gap rather than a proxy. - Scalable: The modular design supports adding custom tasks and applications, and the authors state the harness scales to multiple annotators; no stress test of running at higher task counts or with many simultaneous agents is reported. The software artifact (homepage, code, task repository) is delivered; benchmark adoption will depend on whether the custom web applications and their dependencies can be reliably reproduced by external groups. Strengths: - The deliberate canvas-based rendering that blocks DOM shortcuts is a well-motivated design choice that cleanly isolates visual reasoning from structured-text parsing, making measured performance more interpretable. - The two-stage LLM judge with empirical human-agreement validation (Table 5, Cohen's κ and F1 across all five applications) provides principled confidence in the automated metric rather than assuming LLM judge accuracy. - The per-application tailored evaluators avoid the false failures that exact-match scoring would introduce (e.g., frame-level timestamp tolerance, truth-table equivalence for circuits), making the binary success metric more credible than those in several competing benchmarks. - The efficiency analysis (tokens consumed, steps consumed) is a genuine addition beyond typical benchmark papers, and the counter-intuitive finding — larger models are simultaneously more accurate AND more token-efficient — is informative for practitioners choosing models. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that GauntletBench covers only five specialised domains and a subset of interaction paradigms, which may not capture the full diversity of real-world scenarios and could introduce evaluation bias. They note that constraining agents to visually grounded interaction may limit their full potential in settings where structured observations would legitimately be available. These are stated directly in Section 5 and represent genuine scope boundaries rather than minimisation. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The human baseline of 3 annotators per application is too small to support robust distributional claims; with 15 total evaluators for 100 tasks (averaged over 3 runs = 300 task completions) the human variance is undercharacterised, and a single atypical annotator could skew per-application human SR by several points. The paper does not report inter-annotator agreement for human trajectories, which is the natural complement to the LLM-judge agreement validation. The five applications are purpose-built and not validated against real professional software in the same category, so it is unclear whether agent failure on GauntletBench generalises to failure on actual production Video Editors or Circuit Simulators. The benchmark evaluates closed-source agent frameworks as black boxes (GPT CU, Gemini Enterprise, Claude CU) with no ablation of framework-specific scaffolding choices, making it impossible to separate model capability from framework design. Finally, the paper does not evaluate whether the benchmark itself is robust to overfitting: with 100 tasks and the application code publicly available, future models fine-tuned on GauntletBench traces could artificially inflate scores without genuine generalisation. Bottom Line: GauntletBench is a well-engineered benchmark that fills a real gap — professional-grade, vision-intensive web tasks where agents demonstrably fail — and the 80%-vs.-19% human-agent gap is the clearest quantitative statement of frontier agent limitations to appear in a benchmark paper to date; researchers building or evaluating computer-use agents should treat this as required reading.
Recommended 2606.14397 PDF
#46 — Innovative (67)

PlanBench-XL: Evaluating Long-Horizon Planning of LLM Tool-Use Agents in Large-Scale Tool Ecosystems (79 ▲ )

Jiayu Liu, Qihan Lin, Cheng Qian, Rui Wang, Emre Can Acikgoz, Xiaocheng Yang, Jiateng Liu, Zhenhailong Wang, Xiusi Chen, Heng Ji, Dilek Hakkani-Tür · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PlanBench-XL, an interactive benchmark of 327 retail tasks over 1,665 tools that evaluates whether LLM agents can solve long-horizon planning tasks under retrieval-mediated partial tool visibility and controllably corrupted tool environments. - Why it's important: Real-world agentic deployments expose agents to large, noisy tool ecosystems where relevant tools must be discovered iteratively and retrieved tools may be missing, silently wrong, or misleading — a failure mode that every existing tool-use benchmark abstracts away by assuming a fixed, visible, or cleanly retrieved toolset. - How it does it: A scalable LLM-based pipeline maps typed retail datatypes to executable tools and a backend database, generates solvable queries via state-graph reachability, then evaluates agents through an interactive retrieval loop with optional retrieval-time blocking that replaces path-critical tools with semantically similar alternatives of three distinct failure types while guaranteeing at least one valid solution path always remains. - The good: The benchmark quantifies a sharp and consequential brittleness: GPT-5.4 achieves 51.90% accuracy in the default setting but collapses to approximately 11.36% when only the longest recovery path is preserved, a cliff that prior benchmarks could not surface. - The missing: The benchmark is instantiated in a single retail domain with 56 datatypes, and the self-defined retriever controls reproducibility at the cost of not capturing real-world retrieval noise from imperfect ranking, noisy documentation, and changing tool catalogs. - Read it or not: Researchers building or evaluating LLM tool-use agents should read Sections 4–6; the trajectory failure taxonomy and model-specific termination fingerprints (Sections 6.1–6.3) provide actionable diagnostic insights that go well beyond aggregate accuracy numbers.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (72) are high — combining retrieval-mediated partial tool visibility with controlled retrieval-time blocking (three failure types, guaranteed solvability) is a novel design that surfaces a sharp brittleness cliff (51.9% to ~11% when only the longest recovery path remains), and the finding that the failure is tool selection not discovery (valid tool already retrieved in 78% of pre-drift cases) is a genuinely useful reframing. Rigor (60) is held back by single-domain instantiation, a self-defined retriever, and correlation claims (r = 0.902 etc.) computed over only 10 model data points. Completeness (70) and Resilience (62) are solid with honest limitations. Composite pulled below 75 mainly by single-domain scope and the statistically weak correlation evidence. Weighted composite: 0.20*75 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*62 = 67.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 70 Resilience 62 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM agents deployed against enterprise MCP servers or large API platforms cannot receive the full tool catalog in context, so they rely on retrieval to access relevant subsets of a tool space that may contain hundreds or thousands of candidates. Retrieved tools can be missing, stale, misleading, or functionally broken. Long-horizon tasks compound this problem because agents must infer implicit intermediate sub-goals, retrieve tools for those sub-goals, and adapt when a planned tool-use path is disrupted mid-execution. Existing benchmarks fail to capture this setting: most assume a fixed and visible toolset (ToolBench, RestBench, ACEBench), rely on one-shot retrieval (ToolRet), or evaluate robustness to noisy tools without combining this with active search over large tool spaces (AgentNoiseBench, OpaqueToolsBench). No prior benchmark simultaneously requires iterative tool discovery over a large retrieval-mediated space, implicit sub-goal inference, bi-directional exploration, and runtime adaptation when solution paths are corrupted. Key Idea The core technical insight is to decouple task generation from evaluation via a state-graph reachability structure: tasks are specified as (initial datatype set, target datatype set) pairs, valid solution paths are enumerated over the tool-composition graph at construction time, and solvability is guaranteed by design. This formalism then enables the retrieval-time blocking module to selectively corrupt path-critical tools while preserving at least one valid path, turning the blocking ratio and blocking type into independent experimental controls — so adaptive planning capacity can be measured under precise, reproducible perturbations rather than random noise. Methodology The construction pipeline has four stages. First, 56 retail domain datatypes (e.g., `person_name`, `purchase_status`) are enumerated; M_gen proposes tool functions for all (D_in, D_out) datatype pairs, and M_fil removes unreasonable candidates, yielding a tool library of 1,665 tools augmented with paired noisy distractor tools. Second, a backend database instantiates concrete attribute values; each tool call matches input arguments to backend records and returns output datatype values, with noisy tools returning fixed uninformative responses. Third, queries are generated as (D_0, Y) pairs, filtered by state-graph path enumeration for solvability (Π(r) ≠ ∅), instantiated with concrete backend values, verbalized as natural-language queries by M_gen, and retained only when the shortest valid solution requires at least five distinct tool calls (L* ranging 5–9). Fourth, the retriever supports three complementary modes — input-conditioned (forward anticipation from available evidence), output-conditioned (backward anticipation from target outcome), and input-output-conditioned (bridging) — grounded to tool signatures to ensure reliability. The blocking module identifies T_blk ⊆ T at evaluation time and replaces blocked tools at retrieval with one of three alternatives: explicit failure (error: endpoint unavailable), implicit failure (silently returns a wrong value), or semantically misleading (functionally different but superficially compatible tool). Seven metrics are reported: Accuracy (%), Executed Ground-Truth Datatype Precision (EGT Prec.), Average Turns, Mean Explored Datatypes (Mean EDT), Search-to-Call Ratio (S/C Ratio), Invalid Tool Call Rate (ITCR), and Untrusted Input Rejection Rate (UIRR). Critical design decision 1 — blocking preserves solvability — is well-justified as it separates difficulty from unsolvability, enabling fair comparison. Decision 2 — three failure types with independent controls — is well-justified for diagnosing which failure mode is most disruptive. Decision 3 — self-defined retriever rather than a live system — is a reproducibility-realism tradeoff that the authors acknowledge. Key Results In the default (no-block) setting across ten models, Gemini-3.1-Pro achieves the highest accuracy at 77.06% with 91.47% EGT Precision; GPT-5.4 achieves 51.90%; most other models fall below 60%; Qwen3-8B and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct score 0.00%. Under blocking, performance degrades sharply: GPT-5.4 falls to approximately 30% under standard blocking and to approximately 11.36% when only the longest recovery path is preserved. Implicit failure blocks are most damaging across all models, with UIRR rising to 11.99% under implicit failures versus 9.67% under explicit failures. Mean EDT correlates with accuracy at Pearson r = 0.902 across models; output-conditioned retrieval frequency correlates at r = 0.800; EGT Precision correlates at r = 0.781; ITCR negatively correlates at r = -0.443. Gemini-3.5-Flash achieves the highest S/C ratio (10.44) and most turns on average (57.87) yet only 52.19% accuracy, demonstrating that retrieval frequency alone does not drive success. Forced continuation (up to 5 extra prompts after incorrect termination) yields at most approximately 5 percentage point accuracy gains, confirming test-time compute scaling is insufficient for adaptive recovery. Model-specific failure fingerprints are stable across settings: GPT-5.4 surrenders in 77.3% of default failures; DeepSeek-V4-Flash commits wrong tool values in 58.8% of default failures; Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct fabricates values in 81.7%; Gemini-3.5-Flash ends in search exhaustion in 90.8% of failures. Prior to failed non-progress calls, the model had already retrieved a valid progress-capable tool in 78.0% of default cases, identifying tool selection rather than tool discovery as the primary bottleneck. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The simultaneous combination of retrieval-mediated partial tool visibility, bi-directional exploration support, implicit sub-goal inference, and retrieval-time blocking with three independently controlled failure types in a reproducible, scalable generation pipeline is genuinely novel as a benchmark design. Individual elements — noisy tools, long-horizon tool use, retrieval-augmented tool access — exist separately in prior work, but no benchmark integrates all of them with the controlled perturbation structure that enables the paper's diagnostic analysis. Rigor & Reproducibility This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.22388v1, 21 Jun 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Code is promised under MIT license and the dataset under CC license; a GitHub and dataset link appear on the title page, but release status at time of writing is not confirmed independently. A global random seed (σ_0 = 42) ensures reproducible stochastic retrieval. Blocking fairness is guaranteed: all models face identical blocked tools within a setting. Confidence intervals are reported in Appendix C.1. Ten models spanning proprietary (GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.1-Pro, Gemini-3.5-Flash) and open-source (Qwen3 family, Llama-3 family, DeepSeek-V4-Flash) families are evaluated, providing a reasonably broad base. The error analysis applies a well-defined progress-call taxonomy to full trajectory traces. One methodological concern: the correlation-based claims (r = 0.902, r = 0.800, etc.) are observational across 10 data points (one per model), making causal inference from these correlations unreliable despite the suggestive values. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (a benchmark system evaluated by running agents against it in an interactive loop). - Practical: Open-source models run via vLLM on standard GPU hardware; closed-source models use vendor APIs; no exotic infrastructure is required. - Efficient: Per-query cost is non-trivial (up to 100 turns per query × 327 queries × 10 models), but the benchmark is a one-time evaluation harness, not a deployed pipeline; compute cost is not quantified in the paper. - Effective: The benchmark successfully stratifies 10 models across a 0%–77% accuracy range and reveals failure modes (trajectory drift, selection failure, value contamination from implicit failures) that aggregate tool-call accuracy benchmarks cannot surface, confirming it measures something diagnostically real. - Scalable: The generation pipeline is LLM-based and the authors claim it extends to additional domains; no empirical demonstration of this claim is provided in the paper, and the current evaluation covers only 327 queries with L* ≤ 9 tool calls, leaving much longer horizons untested. The paper delivers an interactive benchmark environment and dataset; a reusable proof technique does not apply. Software release is promised but not yet independently verified. Strengths - The retrieval-time blocking mechanism is principled and controllable: by guaranteeing at least one valid path always remains and varying block ratio and block type independently, it enables a clean dissection of adaptive planning that prior robustness benchmarks cannot achieve. - The trajectory failure taxonomy (No Traction / Irrecoverable Drift / Weak Recovery / Format Error) paired with progress-call annotation provides unusually fine-grained diagnostic value; the finding that valid progress-capable tools were already retrieved in 78% of cases before drift occurs reframes the problem from tool discovery to tool selection, a specific and actionable insight. - Seven orthogonal metrics capture exploration breadth, execution precision, and tool reliability separately, avoiding the single-number collapse that obscures distinct failure modes in most benchmarks. - The scalable LLM-based construction pipeline (datatypes → tools → backend → queries → ground-truth paths via state-graph reachability) is a reusable methodology for extending the benchmark to other domains. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors state three limitations clearly: (1) PlanBench-XL is currently instantiated only in the retail domain, which may not capture the breadth of real-world tool-use scenarios; (2) the blocking mechanism models three representative failure types but not more complex dynamic failures found in real systems; (3) the self-defined retriever enables reproducibility at the cost of not fully capturing real-world retrieval noise from imperfect ranking, noisy documentation, and deployment-specific retrievers. The authors confront these directly in the Limitations section and Appendix E, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All 327 queries derive from a single retail domain; the diversity claim rests entirely on the typed datatype schema, and whether 56 datatypes adequately stress-test generalist agents is untested. Task quality validation in Appendix G relies on partial manual inspection whose scope is not quantified. - Tasks require 5–9 tool calls (L* ≤ 9), but real enterprise workflows routinely require dozens or hundreds of dependent API calls; findings may not extrapolate, and the paper makes no attempt to extrapolate. - No fine-tuned or tool-specialized agents are evaluated (e.g., ToolR0, Toolformer variants). The paper claims PlanBench-XL serves as an RL training playground but reports zero RL agent results. - Ground-truth path enumeration at construction time may not anticipate all valid solutions that use noisy distractor tools in creative combinations, potentially causing the evaluation harness to penalize correct but unanticipated trajectories. - Pearson correlations across 10 models are used to support directional claims (e.g., backward retrieval drives accuracy), but 10 data points provide insufficient statistical power to distinguish correlation from confounding by model size or training data. Bottom Line PlanBench-XL is the most rigorous publicly available benchmark for diagnosing long-horizon adaptive planning under retrieval-mediated tool uncertainty, and any researcher building tool-use agents, MCP-style deployments, or agentic robustness methods should read it.
Recommended 2606.22388 PDF
#47 — Innovative (67)

OPID: On-Policy Skill Distillation for Agentic Reinforcement Learning (35 ▲ )

Shuo Yang, Jinyang Wu, Zhengxi Lu, Yuhao Shen, Fan Zhang, Lang Feng, Shuai Zhang, Haoran Luo, Zheng Lian, Zhengqi Wen, Jianhua Tao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OPID (On-Policy Skill Distillation), a framework that extracts hierarchical hindsight skills from the current policy's own completed trajectories and distills them into dense token-level training signals that augment outcome-based RL for LLM agents. - Why it's important: Long-horizon agentic RL provides only sparse terminal rewards, giving no credit-assignment signal at intermediate decisions; existing skill-based methods that retrieve from external libraries suffer from train/test distribution mismatch that causes skill-conditioned agents to collapse without inference-time skill retrieval. - How it does it: After each episode, an LLM analyzer extracts one episode-level skill (workflow/failure-avoidance rule) and sparse step-level skills at critical timesteps; a critical-first router selects the appropriate skill per step; the old policy re-scores the same sampled response under both the original and skill-augmented context, and the log-probability difference becomes a token-level advantage added to the GRPO episode advantage. - The good: OPID outperforms the strongest competing method on ALFWorld and WebShop by +5.0 and +6.2 points respectively on Qwen3-1.7B, and with 60% of training data matches full-data GRPO (71.9 vs 75.0 on Qwen2.5-3B), while requiring no analyzer, retrieval, or privileged context at inference time. - The missing: The paper never ablates the quality of the analyzer (GLM-5.2); if the analyzer produces poor or noisy skills, the magnitude of the skill advantage signal (mean absolute value 2.612e-4) is so small relative to the episode advantage (mean 2.176) that it is unclear how robust the gains are to analyzer degradation. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building RL training pipelines for multi-turn LLM agents, particularly those already using GRPO-style training, because the on-policy hindsight extraction idea is concrete, code is released, and the ablations cover the main design decisions.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (72) and Rigor (60) anchor the score: the core trick of having the frozen old policy score the same sampled response under a skill-augmented context as a pure log-prob difference is clever and well-motivated, and Propositions 1-3 plus component ablations (critical-first routing +6.8 pp, both skill levels contribute) give it more theoretical grounding than its peers. Novelty (70) is a genuine new method with consistent +5 to +13 pt gains across three benchmarks and three model scales, and code is released. Resilience (62) is pulled down by the unaddressed concern that the skill advantage (mean abs 2.6e-4) is four orders of magnitude below the episode advantage (2.176), so the gain may partly reflect a second-forward-pass regularization effect rather than skill content; the analyzer (GLM-5.2) is never ablated and all numbers are single-run. This work aligns well with the user's positive on-policy-distillation calibration signal, supporting placement just below the top.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 72 Resilience 62 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-turn LLM agents trained with outcome-based RL receive only a terminal reward signal that reveals whether an episode succeeded but not which intermediate decisions caused success or failure. In long-horizon settings such as household manipulation (ALFWorld), web shopping (WebShop), and search-augmented QA, a single early mistake can propagate through 15-30 interaction steps before the outcome is observed, making credit assignment very hard. Prior work has introduced skill-conditioned distillation to provide dense token-level guidance, but these methods retrieve skills from external libraries or persistent memories. Two failure modes result: the maintenance burden of curating skill libraries, and distribution mismatch between the retrieved skill context and the states the current policy actually encounters. The mismatch is especially severe for multi-turn agents, where small deviations from the assumed trajectory cause state drift that makes a retrieved skill unreliable. OPID addresses this by treating completed on-policy rollouts as the source of skill supervision, so the distillation signal is automatically matched to the current policy's distribution. Key Idea: The core insight is that a completed trajectory contains all the information needed to derive both a global behavioral rule (episode-level skill) and precise local guidance at pivotal states (step-level skill), and that this knowledge can be distilled back into the policy by having the old policy itself score the same sampled response under a skill-augmented context. No external teacher, regenerated action, or inference-time skill retrieval is required; the skill advantage is a pure log-probability difference between two forward passes of the frozen old policy over an already-sampled response. Methodology: OPID extends GRPO-style training in three stages applied after each on-policy rollout group. Stage 1 — On-policy skill extraction: an LLM analyzer (GLM-5.2, temperature 0.4, max 4096 tokens) maps the serialized trajectory record to one episode-level skill $s^\text{ep}$ (a natural-language workflow or failure-avoidance rule) and a sparse set of step-level skills $\{s^\text{step}_{\tau,t}\}_{t \in \mathcal{C}_\tau}$ at up to 5 critical timesteps for ALFWorld/WebShop and 2 for Search-QA. Stage 2 — Critical-first skill routing: for each timestep $t$, the routed skill is $s^\text{step}_{\tau,t}$ if $t$ is identified as critical, and $s^\text{ep}$ otherwise. This prevents conflating coarse workflow guidance with precise local decision guidance. Stage 3 — Skill-based self-distillation: the selected skill is injected into the interaction history via a deterministic injection function $H$; the old policy (frozen) scores the same sampled response $y_{\tau,t}$ under both the original history $h_{\tau,t}$ and the skill-augmented history $\tilde{h}_{\tau,t}$; the token-level skill advantage is $A^\text{skill}_{\tau,t,\ell} = (\ell^\text{skill}_{\tau,t,\ell} - \ell^\text{old}_{\tau,t,\ell}) m_{\tau,t,\ell}$. The combined OPID advantage is $A^\text{OPID} = A^\text{ep} + \lambda_\text{skill} A^\text{skill}$ with $\lambda_\text{skill} = 0.001$, optimized with a clipped PPO objective and KL regularization coefficient 0.01. Critical design decisions: (a) using the old policy for both scoring passes preserves on-policy consistency and avoids a separate teacher model — well-justified; (b) critical-first routing rather than superimposing both skill levels — ablated and shown to improve ALFWorld average by +6.8 points over naive superposition; (c) extracting skills post-hoc from completed trajectories rather than on-the-fly — justified by distribution matching arguments but introduces an analyzer compute overhead not quantified in the paper. Key Results: On ALFWorld with Qwen2.5-3B, OPID achieves 84.3% average success rate vs. GRPO at 75.0% (+9.3 points) and vs. the strongest baseline SDAR at 55.8 (figure 1 shows the gap clearly). On Qwen2.5-7B, OPID reaches 90.0% vs. GRPO 81.2% (+8.8 points) and vs. SDAR 88.3 (+1.7 points). On Qwen3-1.7B, OPID achieves 58.9% vs. GRPO 46.1% (+12.8 points) and exceeds the strongest baseline by +5.0 points. On WebShop (success rate), OPID scores 74.2% on Qwen2.5-3B vs. GRPO 63.3% (+10.9) and 64.8% on Qwen3-1.7B vs. SDAR 58.6% (+6.2). On Search-based QA with Qwen2.5-3B, OPID achieves 45.0 average accuracy vs. GRPO 36.4% (+8.6) and equals the strongest baseline (Skill-GRPO* at 44.6 uses privileged inference-time skills; OPID uses none). On unseen ALFWorld splits, OPID achieves 78.6% vs. GRPO 70.9% (+7.7). Sample efficiency: OPID with 60% of training data (71.9) approaches full-data GRPO (75.0); with 80% it exceeds it (78.9 vs. 75.0). Ablation shows both skill levels contribute: removing episode-level skills drops ALFWorld avg from 84.3 to 74.1; removing step-level drops to 79.1. Critical-first routing vs. no routing: 84.3 vs. 77.5. A notable counter-finding is that Skill-GRPO (skills at training time, removed at inference) underperforms plain GRPO across all model scales on ALFWorld (e.g., 65.2 vs. 91.2 for Skill-GRPO vs. GRPO avg on Qwen2.5-3B), confirming that distribution mismatch from retrieved skills is genuinely damaging. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel contribution is the on-policy hindsight extraction pipeline combined with self-distillation that uses the old policy's own forward pass as the teacher signal, eliminating external skill libraries and inference-time retrieval. The hierarchical skill structure and critical-first routing are also new. The underlying components — GRPO, PPO clipping, KL distillation, self-distillation — are standard; the novelty lies in how they are composed and in the on-policy extraction design choice. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover both skill hierarchy components and the routing mechanism, and they show clear monotonic contributions. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed runs are reported across any table or figure; all numbers are single-run point estimates. The preprint (arXiv:2606.26790v1) has not been peer reviewed. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/jinyangwu/OPID/tree/main. The skill coefficient $\lambda_\text{skill} = 0.001$ is very small, producing mean absolute skill advantages of 2.612e-4 vs. episode advantages averaging 2.176 — four orders of magnitude smaller — raising a question about whether the measured gains are driven by the skill signal or by some interaction with the KL penalty and clipping regime. This sensitivity is not ablated. The analyzer (GLM-5.2) is not ablated: the paper never tests what happens with a weaker or noisier analyzer, leaving the robustness of the method to analyzer quality unstated. All evaluation is on established public benchmarks with standard protocols, which is a methodological strength. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper trains and evaluates agents on three agentic benchmarks. - Practical: Training runs on 8 Nvidia A100 80G GPUs for 150 steps; the inference-time policy requires no additional hardware beyond standard LLM serving. - Efficient: Each training iteration requires two forward passes of the frozen old policy per trajectory step (original and skill-augmented context), plus one analyzer call per completed trajectory; the paper does not quantify this overhead in wall-clock or token terms, which is a gap for practitioners. - Effective: On all three benchmarks and all three model scales, OPID consistently improves over GRPO and matches or exceeds the best hybrid skill-distillation baselines, with the weakest result being Search-QA on Qwen3-1.7B where gains are negligible; the evidence credibly supports the stated contribution. - Scalable: The paper tests models at 1.7B, 3B, and 7B parameters; no experiments at 70B or larger scales are reported, and the paper does not claim or test scalability beyond 7B. The method's dependence on an analyzer LLM call per trajectory may become a bottleneck at high rollout throughput. The work delivers a software artifact (training code, released). Strengths: - The on-policy extraction design is principled: by sourcing skills from the current policy's own rollouts, OPID eliminates the train/test context mismatch that, as the paper empirically demonstrates, causes Skill-GRPO to severely underperform plain GRPO when skills are removed at test time. - The theoretical analysis (Propositions 1-3) provides a concrete characterization: the unclipped OPID skill loss is exactly a relative-KL surrogate, on-policy collection eliminates the outer context-occupancy mismatch, and critical-first routing approaches oracle teacher selection under a specialization assumption. These are genuine results, not hand-waving. - The critical-first routing mechanism is a well-motivated and empirically verified design choice: the ablation shows +6.8 points vs. naive skill superposition, validating the hypothesis that granularity should match decision criticality. - The inference-time cleanness is a concrete practical advantage: OPID distills skills into model weights during training, so the deployed policy requires no analyzer, retrieval, or privileged context — a meaningful operational simplification relative to Skill-GRPO* and SDAR. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that OPID is only evaluated on embodied reasoning, web navigation, and search-augmented QA benchmarks, and explicitly call out OdysseyArena, Agent-Bench, WebArena, Mind2Web, and VisualWebArena as natural extensions. They acknowledge that the current two-level skill structure could be enriched with search-discovered reasoning patterns or reusable thought structures from prior work. They note that the training pipeline incurs analyzer and paired-scoring costs that could be reduced via speculative decoding methods such as DOUBLE. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The analyzer quality is entirely unablated: every result uses GLM-5.2 as the analyzer, and the paper never tests what happens with a cheaper or less capable analyzer or with random skill assignments. Since the skill advantage is four orders of magnitude smaller than the episode advantage ($\lambda_\text{skill} = 0.001$, mean absolute values 2.612e-4 vs. 2.176), it is plausible that the benefit comes partly from the regularization effect of the second forward pass rather than from the semantic content of the extracted skills. No experiment separates these. The paper reports no error bars or multi-seed results on any experiment, making it impossible to assess statistical significance of improvements like the +0.4 point gain on Search-based QA (Qwen2.5-3B) or the mixed Qwen3-1.7B Search-QA result. The training horizon is short (150 steps); longer training curves and potential skill quality degradation over training are not examined. Finally, the paper does not measure the wall-clock overhead of running the analyzer and double-scoring pass per batch, which is information a practitioner needs to evaluate whether OPID's sample efficiency advantage offsets its per-step compute increase. Bottom Line: A practitioner designing RL post-training for multi-turn LLM agents will gain a concrete, code-released method with consistent multi-benchmark gains and a clean inference-time footprint, though they should treat the single-run numbers with caution and plan to ablate the analyzer choice for their own setting.
Recommended 2606.26790 PDF
#48 — Innovative (67)

Re-Rooting-Based Fault-Tolerant One-to-All Broadcasting in Dense Eisenstein--Jacobi Networks

B Albader · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a re-rooting-based fault-tolerant one-to-all broadcasting method for dense Eisenstein–Jacobi (EJ) networks that handles up to two static node failures by relocating the effective broadcast source without modifying the underlying broadcast algorithm or adding redundant structures. - Why it's important: Faulty internal forwarding nodes in the standard EJ broadcast tree silently prevent complete message delivery, and existing fault-tolerant approaches (redundant spanning trees, adaptive rerouting) impose storage, control overhead, and implementation complexity that are unattractive for NoC and many-core accelerator deployments. - How it does it: The method selects a new source NS such that every faulty node lies exactly at graph distance t (the network diameter) from NS, making faulty nodes leaf-level nodes that receive but never forward; the theoretical enabler is a proved Boundary-Difference Coverage Lemma showing every node in H_n can be written as the difference of two distance-t boundary nodes, guaranteeing a valid NS always exists for any one- or two-fault configuration. - The good: The proposed method achieves 100% broadcast success in all 40,000 simulation trials across five network sizes (N = 331 to 120,601) and four fault-placement modes under one- and two-fault scenarios, while the baseline drops to near-0% success for large networks. - The missing: The only quantitative baseline is the unmodified fault-free algorithm; no comparison against redundant spanning trees, independent spanning trees, or adaptive rerouting under the same fault model is provided, making the 2t worst-case overhead hard to contextualize competitively. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers or architects working on algebraic interconnection network topologies (hexagonal mesh, EJ, NoC); practitioners seeking a general fault-tolerant broadcast solution for other topologies will find limited transferable content.
Why this rank: Scored using Theoretical profile (formal Boundary-Difference Coverage Lemma + proved guarantees are the core contribution, despite the EMPIRICAL Deployability tag). Non-Obviousness (70) and Novelty (65) anchor the score: the B_t − B_t = H_n decomposition is a genuinely new structural result for dense EJ networks, but the re-rooting paradigm itself is borrowed, so this is a topology-specific instantiation rather than a new problem class. Rigor (70) is solid on the theory side — complete constructive proofs, a deterministic method with SD = 0 across 40,000 trials, and an exhaustively-verified three-fault counterexample that proves the bound is tight — but it is held back because the only experimental baseline is the unmodified fault-free algorithm, no code is released, and it is an unreviewed preprint. Completeness (70) and Resilience (68) are helped by honest scope-bounding (static faults only, link failures and the S-operational assumption acknowledged) yet hurt by the missing quantitative comparison against redundant/independent spanning trees. Practical Impact (45) is limited: O(√N) selection is cheap and points to a usable algorithm, but applicability is confined to the EJ topology class with no released artifact. Weighted: 0.25*65 + 0.20*70 + 0.25*70 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*68 + 0.05*45 = 67.
theoretical Novelty 65 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 68 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Dense EJ networks are degree-six algebraic interconnection topologies with N = 3n² − 3n + 1 nodes, diameter t = n − 1, and vertex transitivity; they appear in parallel computers, Network-on-Chip architectures, and many-core accelerators because the standard one-to-all broadcast completes in exactly t parallel steps propagating through six hexagonal sectors. The vulnerability is structural: the broadcast tree is a single spanning structure, so any faulty node at graph distance less than t from the source becomes a forwarding bottleneck, silently cutting off all downstream nodes in its sector. Prior fault-tolerant approaches for hexagonal and EJ networks require either maintaining multiple redundant spanning trees (extra storage, changed broadcast procedure) or adaptive rerouting at runtime (continuous fault-status propagation, recomputed routes). No lightweight method exploiting EJ-specific geometry existed that could preserve the original broadcast algorithm while tolerating internal node failures. Key Idea The core insight is that in dense EJ networks, every node at graph distance exactly t from a given source is a leaf in the standard broadcast tree — it receives but does not forward. If a new source NS is chosen so that all faulty nodes lie at distance exactly t from NS, the original broadcast algorithm remains correct without any modification. The theoretical key is the Boundary-Difference Coverage Lemma (Lemma 1): for every node A in H_n, there exist two distance-t boundary nodes U, V such that A = U − V (mod α). This means the relative displacement between any two faulty nodes can always be decomposed as a boundary-node difference, guaranteeing a common distance-t source exists for any pair of faults. The proof is constructive: for the first hexagonal sector, explicit boundary pairs are given, and the result extends to all six sectors via EJ rotation symmetry (multiplication by ω is a graph automorphism). Methodology The method operates in two sequential phases. In the source-selection phase, the algorithm scans the distance-t boundary B_t — which contains exactly 6t nodes — to find a valid NS. For a single faulty node F_1, any node in B_t(F_1) = F_1 + B_t is a valid NS; Algorithm 2 (OneFailureFindingNS) scans these 6t candidates and returns the first operational one in O(t) time. For two faulty nodes F_1, F_2, Algorithm 3 (TwoFailureFindingNS) computes the displacement A = F_2 − F_1 (mod α), then searches B_t for a boundary node U such that U − A is also a boundary node, setting NS = F_1 + U; membership in B_t uses the closed-form distance D(x,y) = max{|x|, |y|, |x+y|} in constant time per test, giving overall O(t) = O(√N) complexity. In the broadcast phase, the message is unicast from original source S to NS (at most t hops, proved safe because faulty nodes cannot block this path since d(F, NS) = t prevents F from being an internal node on S→NS), followed by the standard t-step EJ broadcast from NS. Total worst-case communication time is bounded by 2t. Critical design decisions: (1) Static fault model — faults are known before broadcast begins, avoiding runtime detection overhead but ruling out dynamic/transient faults; this choice is well-justified for the target hardware setting but sharply limits generality. (2) Source assumed operational — S itself is never faulty, which is an unstated but load-bearing assumption. (3) Leaf-level placement via relocation — the entire broadcast procedure is preserved unchanged; only the source identity shifts, so no routing tables or control structures require modification. Key Results The boundary-difference property B_t − B_t = H_n is validated numerically for all five tested network sizes (t = 10, 25, 50, 100, 200; N = 331 to 120,601), confirming the lemma. In 40,000 broadcast trials, the proposed method achieves 100% success and zero reachability variation (SD = 0 in all cases, Table 4) under both one- and two-fault scenarios. The baseline algorithm succeeds in only 0.225%–9.125% of trials under one fault and 0.225%–3.925% under two faults at the same sizes. Average total broadcast steps match the 2t bound (e.g., 345.1 at t = 200 with one fault, vs. a 400-step ceiling). Source-selection runtime averages 0.002–0.037 ms for networks up to N = 120,601; candidates checked stays well below 6t for most configurations. The three-fault counterexample in H_4 with F_1 = 0, F_2 = 5, F_3 = 14 gives B_3(F_1) ∩ B_3(F_2) ∩ B_3(F_3) = ∅ (verified by enumeration), proving the two-fault guarantee is tight. The only direct comparison baseline is the unmodified fault-free algorithm; no quantitative comparison against any alternative fault-tolerant method is provided. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The Boundary-Difference Coverage Lemma (B_t − B_t = H_n) is a structural algebraic result about dense EJ networks not previously established; it is genuinely novel and enables the two-fault guarantee. The re-rooting paradigm is not new in general (source relocation appears in other network reliability contexts), but its application to EJ hexagonal geometry — exploiting sector symmetry and boundary leafhood — is new for this topology class. The three-fault tight lower bound is a non-trivial negative result that precisely characterizes the method's limits. The overall contribution is a topology-specific instantiation of a known paradigm, not a paradigm shift. Rigor & Reproducibility The main theoretical results (Lemma 1, Theorem 1, Proposition 1) carry complete, self-contained proofs with explicit constructions. The three-fault limitation is proved by exhaustive intersection enumeration on H_4, not just claimed. Experiments run 1000 independent trials per configuration across 5 network sizes, 2 fault counts, and 4 fault-placement modes (40,000 total).
