No error bars are needed for the proposed method because it is deterministic (SD = 0 confirmed in Table 4). The paper reports no code repository and releases no software artifact. The experimental comparison is methodologically weak: the only baseline is the original fault-free algorithm, not any alternative fault-tolerant approach. The boundary-difference validation (Table 2) is simulation-backed but redundant with the proof. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.18712v1); it has not been peer reviewed. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL (algorithmic, with formal guarantees; no hardware implementation). - Practical: Source selection requires only integer arithmetic modulo N and the closed-form EJ distance formula; it runs in < 0.037 ms at N = 120,601 on a standard processor with no specialized hardware. - Efficient: Source-selection cost is O(t) = O(√N) with a constant factor of at most 6; communication overhead is at most t additional hops (one extra diameter) versus the fault-free baseline, with no extra storage beyond a precomputed boundary set of size 6t. - Effective: The method provably and empirically delivers to all non-faulty nodes under every one- and two-fault configuration; this directly solves the stated problem, not a proxy, under the static-fault assumption. - Scalable: Tested up to N = 120,601 nodes (t = 200); the O(√N) complexity and 2t communication bound scale favorably. No hardware or VLSI-scale validation exists, and behavior under link failures or dynamic faults is not addressed. No software artifact, dataset, or trained model is released. Strengths - The Boundary-Difference Coverage Lemma is a clean, non-trivial algebraic structural result with a complete constructive proof, providing a sound theoretical foundation rather than a heuristic argument. - The algorithm is topology-specific in a productive way: it exploits EJ vertex transitivity and the hexagonal sector structure to achieve O(√N) source selection with zero additional routing structures, giving a genuine complexity advantage over redundant-tree methods. - Proposition 1 (three-fault counterexample) demonstrates intellectual honesty — the authors prove exactly where their guarantee ends rather than leaving the boundary unstated or conjectured. - The simulation design is systematic with four qualitatively distinct fault-placement modes (random, near-source, critical-position, close-pair), providing meaningful coverage of adversarial scenarios rather than best-case testing. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state: (1) the static fault model assumes faults are known pre-broadcast and do not change during propagation; dynamic or transient failures require repeated re-rooting, online detection, or adaptive recovery, which are out of scope; (2) link failures are not addressed — a failed link interrupts communication even if both endpoint nodes are operational, requiring a different formulation; (3) the two-fault guarantee does not extend to arbitrary three-fault configurations; (4) no implementation-level quantitative comparison with redundant-tree or adaptive-routing methods is provided. The authors treat these limitations as honest scope boundaries rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The most significant gap is the absence of any quantitative comparison against a competing fault-tolerant method (independent spanning trees, local recovery, adaptive rerouting) under the same EJ network and fault model. Table 6 is qualitative only. Without this, the claimed 2t overhead advantage cannot be verified against real alternatives. - The scheme assumes S is always operational and that at least one path from S to NS is unblocked. Neither assumption is validated when S is near-faulty nodes or when the relocation path passes through high-failure-density regions. Section 7.3 argues safety of the relocation path, but only under the assumption that d(F, NS) = t — not under link failures. - For two faults, the worst-case scan checks up to 6t candidates, but the average-case scan depth varies substantially by fault-placement mode (avg 7.3 candidates at t = 10 vs. 152 at t = 200 for two faults). The paper does not characterize which fault configurations trigger near-worst-case scans. - The boundary-difference property is specific to dense EJ networks; the paper does not discuss whether analogous results hold for Gaussian networks, general circulant graphs, or other algebraic topologies — limiting cross-applicability of the key lemma. - No throughput or concurrent-broadcast analysis exists; the 2t bound applies to a single isolated broadcast, and the overhead under repeated or pipelined broadcasts (common in parallel computing) is not discussed. Bottom Line A designer building broadcast primitives for dense EJ hexagonal mesh NoC or parallel interconnect systems gains a provably correct, zero-extra-structure, O(√N) fault-tolerance scheme for up to two static node failures; the work is narrowly scoped and not useful outside the EJ topology class or when dynamic faults and link failures are the primary concern.
Search Results 2606.18712 PDF
#49 — Innovative (66)

Bifrost: Hybrid TEE-FHE Inference for Privacy-Preserving Transformer and LLM Serving

C Chen, K Qin, X Zhang, C Zhang, D Gu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Bifrost, a hybrid TEE–FHE serving runtime that routes linear transformer projections to an untrusted CKKS-accelerated GPU and non-linear operators plus ciphertext refresh to a trusted CPU TEE, reducing projected end-to-end latency by 9.25× over pure FHE on GPT-2 (1.5B) and 9.91× on LLaMA 3 (8B). - Why it's important: Cloud LLM inference exposes user prompts and intermediate activations to the cloud software stack and accelerator runtime; for inputs containing source code, legal text, or medical records this confidentiality gap has no efficient prior solution. - How it does it: It exploits operator affinity — linear projections are GEMM-dominated and FHE-friendly, while normalization, RoPE, Softmax, and gating are precision-fragile under FHE — to split execution at the transformer operator boundary rather than the architectural layer boundary, and replaces expensive homomorphic bootstrapping with a decrypt-then-encrypt (DtE) refresh executed entirely inside the CPU TEE. - The good: TEE-side plaintext execution of non-linear operators is 398× faster than GPU FHE for GELU and DtE refresh is 13.2× faster than GPU-based bootstrapping, providing strong microbenchmark justification for both halves of the architectural split. - The missing: The headline 9.25× and 9.91× speedup numbers compare against a projected Pure FHE baseline built from operator microbenchmarks using Euston's estimation methodology, not a directly deployed end-to-end competitor; and the entire evaluation is single-tenant, single-GPU. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and engineers in privacy-preserving ML inference, confidential computing, or FHE systems design — the operator-affinity split and DtE primitive are reusable ideas, and the memory-boundary finding is a concrete architectural constraint not visible in prior benchmark-only work.
Why this rank: Systems profile. The operator-affinity split (linear projections to FHE GPU, non-linear operators to CPU TEE) and the decrypt-then-encrypt refresh primitive replacing homomorphic bootstrapping are genuinely novel (Novelty 75, Non-Obviousness 78), grounded in measured data (linear ops = 97.5% of decode time, DtE 13.2x faster than bootstrapping). Completeness (75) is strong: the paper separates three KV-cache measurement tiers and confronts its limitations without minimization. Rigor (60) and Resilience (62) are capped by the central caveat that the 9.25x/9.91x headline speedups are against a projected (not deployed) pure-FHE baseline, plus single-tenant single-GPU evaluation, no code, and very high absolute latency (~280x the plaintext TEE floor) holding Impact at 58. Weighted composite ~66.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 75 Resilience 62 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Cloud LLM inference exposes user prompts and intermediate activations to the cloud provider's host OS, hypervisor, accelerator driver stack, and GPU runtime. For sensitive inputs (source code, business plans, medical records, legal text) this is a direct confidentiality problem with no satisfactory existing solution. Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) protects accelerator-side computation cryptographically, but non-linear operators in modern LLMs — normalization, RoPE, Softmax, gating, KV-state transitions, and ciphertext refresh — are either too depth-hungry, precision-fragile, or control-heavy to run efficiently on an FHE accelerator. CPU Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) such as Intel TDX execute these operators exactly in plaintext, but a CPU-TEE-only path creates a trusted-CPU latency floor far below GPU-accelerated throughput. Neither mechanism alone handles the full autoregressive serving path: FHE alone fails on non-linear depth and refresh cost; TEE alone is the latency floor, not a path to using fast untrusted accelerators. The serving-system gap is a runtime that assigns each transformer operator to the right execution domain, manages KV-cache state across the trust boundary, and respects the prefill/decode asymmetry of autoregressive generation. Key Idea The trust boundary and the transformer layer boundary should not coincide. Regular linear projections (QKV, output, gate-up, down) are stable, data-parallel, and GEMM-dominated — they belong on an accelerator-backed FHE path. Non-linear operators (LayerNorm, RoPE, Softmax, SwiGLU gating, attention control, KV-state transitions) are precision-fragile or depth-hungry under FHE — they belong in the CPU TEE as plaintext computations executed through decrypt-then-encrypt windows. DtE (ct' = Enc_pk(Dec_sk(ct))) provides the same logical effect as homomorphic bootstrapping — resetting noise and restoring decryptability — but exploits the TEE's existing decryption authority rather than the homomorphic path, making it 13.2× faster. This single observation drives both the operator partition and the refresh strategy. Methodology Bifrost partitions each transformer block into two affinity classes. FHE-friendly operators (linear QKV/output/gate-up/down projections) run on an untrusted GPU via CKKS BSGS-GEMM on pre-encoded weight diagonals. TEE-friendly operators (normalization, RoPE, Softmax, gating, attention-side control, DtE refresh) run inside the CPU TEE: the TEE decrypts only the minimal intermediate state needed, executes the operator exactly in plaintext, and immediately re-encrypts the output before resuming the FHE path. CKKS parameters are ring dimension N=8192 (4096 complex slots), coefficient modulus [60,30,30,30] bits, 128-bit security under RLWE. The boundary-aware scheduler (Algorithm 1) runs inside the TEE and jointly chooses tile size, persistent-cache vs. fallback-encode decisions, and DtE refresh insertion points. Tiling partitions large-ring GEMM evaluations into sequences of small-ring tile evaluations; 2×2 tiling of a 1024×1024 GEMM yields 9.01× end-to-end speedup (270.6s to 30.0s) by improving GPU GEMM efficiency at the cost of additional TEE-boundary crossings. Three design decisions are explicitly justified: (1) DtE replaces bootstrapping because T_transfer + T_Dec + T_Enc < T_FHE_refresh holds by a wide margin (6.59ms vs. 87ms); (2) tiling is chosen jointly with boundary-crossing cost rather than independently by the GPU kernel scheduler; (3) FHE-path depth is kept dominated by linear layers (D_FHE^hybrid ≈ D_lin) by removing non-linear depth contributions. Bifrost⁺ adds a prefill/decode (PD) split: prompt prefill and prompt-side KV construction run entirely inside the CPU TEE without FHE; only the decode-side state handoff enters the hybrid ciphertext path, breaking the TTFT scaling with prompt length. The prototype is implemented as a SecureLLM engine drop-in for nano-vLLM, with the FHE backend built on HEonGPU and the TEE running under Intel TDX. Key Results All primary measurements run on a single Alibaba Cloud instance with 24 vCPUs, 128 GiB system memory, and one NVIDIA H20 (96 GiB) GPU under Intel TDX; models are GPT-2 (124M) and Qwen3 (0.6B) for direct CKKS/FHE measurements. Pure FHE rows for GPT-2 (1.5B) and LLaMA 3 (8B) are projected from measured primitive costs using Euston's estimation methodology, not direct deployments. End-to-end latency (TTFT / total / steady-state decode in seconds, P=16): GPT-2 (124M): Pure FHE (proj.) 579.2/1090.2/34.1 → Bifrost 62.2/120.6/3.9 → Bifrost⁺ 4.3/63.4/3.9. Qwen3 (0.6B): Pure FHE (proj.) 1946.8/2748.5/114.5 → Bifrost 352.3/506.6/22.0 → Bifrost⁺ 23.0/177.3/22.0. Estimator-style projected comparison: Bifrost vs. Pure FHE is 9.25× on GPT-2 (1.5B) and 9.91× on LLaMA 3 (8B). Bifrost⁺ reduces TTFT on GPT-2 (124M) from 62.2s to 4.3s at P=16 and from 352.3s to 23.0s on Qwen3 (0.6B) at P=16 (14.6–45.8× and 15.3–53.4× over direct CKKS/FHE). Operator microbenchmarks: TEE vs. GPU FHE speedup is 398× for GELU, 23× for LayerNorm, 17× for Softmax, 13.2× for DtE vs. GPU bootstrapping. Decode cost breakdown for GPT-2 (124M) fully cached: GEMM 97.5%, DtE 2.3%, non-linear operators <0.2%. For Qwen3 (0.6B) with 23/28 layers cached: GEMM 64.2%, fallback weight encoding 34.4% — exposing GPU memory residency as the first-order scaling constraint at this parameter count. GPT-2 (774M) already hits the H20 96 GiB memory wall when encoding all layers. Baselines are strong for the FHE side (Euston is the current pure-FHE state of the art); the CPU TEE endpoint (13.74 ms/tok on GPT-2 (124M), 58.55 ms/tok on Qwen3 (0.6B)) is correctly framed as the latency floor that Bifrost does not try to beat. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW METHOD. The operator-affinity split for TEE+FHE hybrid LLM serving and the DtE primitive replacing homomorphic bootstrapping are genuinely novel system contributions. Adapting PD disaggregation to a TEE–FHE trust boundary (Bifrost⁺) applies a known serving idea from plaintext infrastructure (DistServe, Splitwise) to a new security context. Prior hybrid work (BLB, TEEFHE, HT2ML) explores TEE+FHE partitioning but none instantiates a full autoregressive serving runtime with KV-cache management, operator-affinity scheduling, and PD-split semantics while keeping the accelerator ciphertext-only. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and well-structured: Bifrost (operator split only), Bifrost⁺ (operator split + PD split), and Pure FHE (projected baseline) are kept as separate rows throughout, and Table 6 isolates the PD split's contribution to TTFT. No statistical error bars or multi-seed runs are reported; measurements appear to be single runs. No code release is announced and no repository URL is mentioned — the prototype is described but not published. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.17421v1, 16 Jun 2026) and has not yet undergone peer review. The most important methodological concern is that the 9.25× and 9.91× headline speedups compare Bifrost against a projected Pure FHE baseline, not a directly deployed competitor — the paper is explicit about this distinction and documents the estimation methodology, but it is a substantive caveat. The KV-cache measurement separation (ShadowCipherKV simulated, real CKKS encrypted KV, packed CKKS KV kept as three distinct classes) reflects unusual care in not conflating performance claims across incompatible measurement regimes. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built serving runtime evaluated by deployment on real hardware. - Practical: Runs on a commercially available cloud instance (Alibaba Cloud, H20 GPU, Intel TDX CPU), but requires a CC-capable instance with both a TDX-enabled CPU and a GPU FHE backend, a configuration that is available but not ubiquitous across cloud providers. - Efficient: Steady-state decode is 3.88–3.94s/token on GPT-2 (124M) and 22.03–22.25s/token on Qwen3 (0.6B), approximately 280× and 380× slower than the plaintext CPU TEE floor respectively; this is the unavoidable cost of ciphertext-only accelerator delegation, not a regression from the paper's target deployment point. - Effective: The system genuinely keeps prompts and intermediate activations hidden from the untrusted accelerator; the leakage contract is stated precisely and boundary behavior is validated through the serving stack, not just operator microbenchmarks. - Scalable: Single-tenant, single-GPU evaluation only; GPT-2 (774M) already hits the H20 96 GiB memory wall, and Qwen3 (0.6B) at 23/28 cached layers pays a 34.4% fallback weight-encoding penalty; multi-GPU partitioning and multi-tenant admission control are explicitly deferred to future work. The work delivers a software runtime (nano-vLLM SecureLLM extension with boundary scheduler and CKKS serving stack) but no public code release is stated. Strengths - The operator-affinity argument is grounded in measured data: linear projections consume 97.5% of per-token decode time on GPT-2 (124M) and map naturally to CKKS GEMM, while non-linear operators consume <0.2% and are exactly the operators where FHE approximation introduces numerical risk. This justifies the split from first principles rather than by heuristic partitioning. - DtE as a refresh primitive is a concrete systems contribution: 13.2× faster than GPU bootstrapping, zero change to the trust boundary, and it eliminates a major cost driver from the encrypted path while removing the need for polynomial approximations of non-linear operators. - The measurement methodology distinguishes projected estimates from direct measurements, separates three KV-cache measurement tiers, and explicitly names which result rows are Bifrost targets vs. CPU-TEE reference anchors — a level of transparency that is uncommon in systems security papers. - The end-to-end serving substrate (request routing, phase-aware policy, KV-cache materialization) goes beyond operator microbenchmarks and surfaces memory residency as the dominant scaling constraint for models ≥0.6B — a finding that pure-benchmark evaluation would miss. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors state four limitations directly and without minimization: (1) memory, not arithmetic, is the dominant boundary — fallback weight encoding already costs 34.4% of Qwen3 (0.6B) decode time with 5 layers uncached, and GPT-2 (774M) hits the memory wall entirely; (2) evaluation is single-accelerator and single-tenant, with multi-GPU partitioning and admission control deferred; (3) the threat model excludes rollback/fork attacks, denial-of-service, microarchitectural side channels, and coarse request metadata (length, timing, traffic volume); (4) encrypted KV overhead (~1.25 MiB/token/layer on GPT-2 (124M)) is modest for short prompts but grows with context length. Confronting these directly is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 9.25× and 9.91× headline speedups — the primary claim of the paper — compare against a projected baseline, not a deployed system. Even granting Euston's methodology, a top-tier reviewer would require at least one direct end-to-end measurement against a deployed pure-FHE competitor. - No multi-tenant evaluation: in production, concurrent requests compete for TEE memory, encrypted weight residency, and KV-cache space; the single-tenant numbers may be optimistic for real deployment. - Absolute latency remains very high: 3.88s/token for GPT-2 (124M) is approximately 280× slower than plaintext CPU TEE. The paper correctly frames this as the cost of the ciphertext-only delegation model, but provides no discussion of which user-facing applications would tolerate this latency or what SLA targets motivate the design. - Private model weights are explicitly out of scope. For proprietary model deployments, ciphertext–ciphertext kernels are needed; the paper acknowledges this but does not prototype or estimate the additional cost. - The threat model excludes all metadata side channels (request length, timing, traffic volume). For the sensitive applications that most motivate this work (medical, legal, financial), these channels are real attack surfaces and the gap between the system's leakage contract and a full confidentiality guarantee is not quantified. Bottom Line A practitioner building privacy-preserving LLM inference infrastructure gains real, actionable design principles from this work: the operator-affinity split and DtE primitive are reusable, the measurement methodology sets a useful standard, and the memory-boundary finding is a concrete constraint that will shape the next generation of encrypted serving systems.
Search Results 2606.17421 PDF
#50 — Innovative (66)

GUI vs. CLI: Execution Bottlenecks in Screen-Only and Skill-Mediated Computer-Use Agents (23 ▲ )

Xiao Zhou, Siyue Zhang, Yilun Zhao, Jinbiao Wei, Tingyu Song, Arman Cohan, Chen Zhao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a matched execution-layer benchmark of 440 desktop tasks across 18 applications and 12 workflow categories where screen-only GUI agents and skill-mediated CLI agents receive identical task goals, initial states, and executable final-state verifiers, enabling the first controlled comparison of interaction modality in computer-use agents. - Why it's important: Existing GUI and CLI agent benchmarks vary task content, initial states, and verifiers simultaneously across modalities, making it impossible to determine whether observed performance gaps stem from model capability, task difficulty, or interaction design; practitioners investing in GUI interfaces versus CLI skill layers need this distinction. - How it does it: The benchmark holds task goal, initial state, and verifier fixed while restricting each agent to its modality-native action space (screen-only operations for GUI; CLI-Anything skill invocations for CLI), then uses a verifier-guided patched-skill diagnostic to quantify how much of the CLI deficit is attributable to skill coverage gaps versus fundamental modality constraints. - The good: The patched-skill diagnostic directly shows that repairing skill coverage gaps raises CLI success from 48.2% to 69.3%, while original CLI-Anything skills cover only 37.6% of verifier checkpoints, attributing the majority of the CLI performance deficit to incomplete skill interfaces rather than model limitations. - The missing: The patched-skill setting uses verifier information during repair, making it a coverage-controlled upper bound rather than an out-of-the-box deployable baseline, and the paper does not evaluate unconstrained hybrid agents that combine GUI and CLI, which is how production systems actually operate. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners designing skill layers for CLI-based computer-use agents should read this; it provides a concrete decomposition of where agent failures originate and a reusable benchmark with modality-controlled evaluation.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (72) and Novelty (70) carry it — the matched execution-layer protocol (task, initial state, verifier held fixed while varying modality) converts a confounded comparison into a controlled one, and the patched-skill diagnostic cleanly attributes most of the CLI deficit to 37.6% skill coverage rather than model reasoning. The result that GPT-5.4 via GUI beats a stronger Codex GPT-5.5 via CLI is surprising and actionable. Rigor (58) is the limiter: no significance tests or confidence intervals on 5-11 point pass-rate gaps, the coverage metric is checkpoint-level not task-level, and the benchmark inherits OpenComputer's task distribution and only includes apps with existing CLI skills. Completeness (72) benefits from explicit honesty about the patched-skill upper-bound and excluded hybrid agents. Weighted composite: 0.20*70 + 0.25*58 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*62 = 66.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 72 Resilience 62 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Computer-use agents can act on desktop software through either graphical interfaces (GUI) or programmatic command interfaces (CLI). Choosing between or combining these modalities is a central design decision for production systems, yet existing benchmarks evaluate GUI and CLI agents on different tasks with different initial states and different verifiers. This conflation means a performance gap between GUI and CLI agents cannot be attributed to interaction modality: it could equally reflect task difficulty, verifier stringency, or model capability. Practitioners building skill-mediated CLI systems (e.g., CLI-Anything-style application harnesses) lack evidence about whether their agents fail because of model reasoning limitations or because their skill libraries do not expose the operations the verifier requires. The paper addresses this by constructing a matched evaluation suite where modality is the only experimental variable. Key Idea The core technical insight is that holding task goal, initial state, and final-state verifier fixed while enforcing modality-native action constraints converts an observational comparison into a controlled experiment; the additional patched-skill diagnostic then uses verifier checkpoints as ground truth to measure skill coverage directly, separating recoverable coverage gaps from irreducible modality-specific failure modes. Methodology Benchmark construction follows a three-stage pipeline. Stage I selects applications from OpenComputer for which CLI-Anything skills are available, ensuring every application is executable through both modalities. Stage II rewrites GUI-oriented step-by-step instructions into modality-agnostic goal descriptions that specify target outcomes without naming interface elements or action sequences; tasks whose success depends exclusively on one modality's affordances are removed, and the task set is balanced across the GUI-CLI execution distribution. Stage III involves manual inspection of every rewritten task to verify modality-agnosticity and absence of procedural cues. The resulting 440 tasks span 12 workflow categories (Visual Design, Audio, Knowledge, CAD & 3D, Graphics Debugging, Documents, Video & Streaming, Spreadsheets, Presentations, Communication, Game, Web) across 18 applications. GUI agents receive a sandboxed Ubuntu desktop screenshot at each step and may only produce screen-level actions (click, drag, type, scroll, keyboard shortcuts); direct code execution, shell commands, and file edits are prohibited. CLI agents are restricted to CLI-Anything skill invocations and bash for discovery and read-only verification; direct artifact mutation via Python, sed, awk, or file-editing tools is prohibited. Evaluation is final-state-based: each task contains executable verifier checkpoints that read persisted application state, and the full pass rate requires all checkpoints to pass. The patched-skill diagnostic runs a four-phase verifier-coverage pipeline: map verifier checkpoints to existing skills, repair Partial/Fail checkpoints, validate repaired skills against verifier-observable application state, and update skill documentation. The key design decisions are: (1) prohibiting hybrid actions to isolate modality effects (explicitly acknowledged as unrealistic for production); (2) using executable verifier checkpoints rather than reference trajectory matching; (3) using opaque per-run labels in the patched-skill pipeline to prevent skill documentation from leaking verifier structure to the model. Key Results The strongest GUI agent, GPT-5.4, achieves a 59.1% full pass rate at 455.8 seconds per task. The strongest original CLI agent, Codex GPT-5.5, reaches 48.2% at 188.1 seconds per task. Under the patched-skill diagnostic, Codex GPT-5.5 reaches 69.3% at 162.6 seconds, exceeding the best GUI agent overall while running 2.4x faster. Original CLI-Anything skills cover only 37.6% of verifier checkpoints, establishing skill incompleteness as the dominant bottleneck. GUI agents are more stable across workflow categories: GPT-5.4 ranges from 42.9% (CAD & 3D) to 88.2% (Web). CLI agents are far more variable: Codex GPT-5.5 ranges from 9.8% (Graphics Debugging) to 100.0% (Game and Communication). GUI agents dominate Audio, Presentations, Communication, and Web workflows, where the interface directly encodes the required operation sequence. CLI agents are competitive or stronger on Visual Design, CAD & 3D, Documents, Video & Streaming, and Game, where target state can be represented as structured artifacts or application-level operations. Procedural grounding (adding step-by-step GUI workflow cues to task instructions) on a 176-task subset raises GUI full pass rate from 59.7% to 60.2% and average reward from 0.7401 to 0.7576, while reducing average task time from 397.0 to 314.8 seconds, suggesting workflow knowledge reduces exploration cost but does not eliminate grounding failures. Manual annotation of 80 failed CLI trajectories shows 93.8% concentrate on skill coverage and contract gaps; 80 failed GUI trajectories split between workflow execution failures (61.3%) and UI navigation/control discovery failures (38.7%). Baselines include six recent frontier GUI models (GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Opus 4.7, EvoCUA-32B, Qwen3.5-27B, Kimi-K2.6) and four CLI models (Codex GPT-5.4, Codex GPT-5.5, Claude Code Sonnet 4.6, Claude Code Opus 4.7), all evaluated May-June 2026; these are current frontier models. Novelty Classification NEW BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The matched execution-layer protocol that holds task, initial state, and verifier constant while varying modality is methodologically novel in the computer-use agent literature. The finding that original CLI skill coverage is only 37.6% of verifier checkpoints and that this coverage gap accounts for the majority of the GUI-CLI performance differential is a genuine empirical contribution; the complementary failure taxonomies for GUI and CLI provide a structured decomposition that prior work does not offer. The benchmark itself is constructed by adapting OpenComputer tasks rather than collecting new tasks from scratch, and the CLI-Anything skill layer is an existing artifact. Rigor & Reproducibility The paper evaluates six GUI and four CLI frontier models, providing reasonable breadth across the model dimension. The patched-skill diagnostic is explicitly labeled as a coverage-controlled upper bound and not an out-of-the-box baseline, which is an important methodological honesty. The error taxonomy is grounded in 80 manually annotated failed trajectories per modality with trajectory-level examples in Appendix A.4. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported for any of the pass-rate comparisons; given that differences of 5-10 percentage points across 440 tasks are discussed as meaningful, the absence of statistical testing is a gap. Code and data are linked from the first page (GitHub and data repositories). The benchmark is derived from OpenComputer tasks, raising a question about whether OpenComputer's task distribution over-represents workflows that favor one modality, which is not analyzed. The verifier-coverage metric counts checkpoints, not tasks; the relationship between checkpoint-level coverage and task-level pass rate is not directly modeled. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Both agents run on a sandboxed Ubuntu desktop VM (Docker container, x86_64), which is commercially available hardware with no specialized requirements. - Efficient: GUI agents average 397-455 seconds per task; original CLI agents average 188 seconds; patched CLI 162 seconds. CLI is 2.4-2.8x faster than GUI for equivalent or better accuracy when skills are complete. - Effective: Evaluation uses executable final-state verifier checkpoints reading persisted application state rather than proxy metrics or reference trajectories, providing direct evidence of task completion. - Scalable: Tested on 440 tasks across 18 applications; no experiments at larger task count, more applications, or longer workflow horizons. The claim that skill construction is the central scaling bottleneck for CLI agents is supported by the 37.6% coverage finding but not by experiments at scale. The paper delivers a benchmark dataset with modality-matched tasks and verifiers, and the patched-skill pipeline methodology, but does not deliver a software library or trained model. Strengths - The controlled experimental design is the central contribution: holding task goal, initial state, and verifier constant while varying modality converts an otherwise confounded comparison into interpretable evidence. This is nontrivial given that tasks originally written for GUI execution must be genuinely solvable through CLI, which required substantial curation effort. - The patched-skill diagnostic provides a clean empirical decomposition: the 37.6% baseline coverage rising to 100% coverage (yielding 69.3% pass rate) directly quantifies how much of the CLI shortfall is recoverable from skill engineering rather than model capability. This reframes a qualitative observation into a measurable quantity. - The complementary failure taxonomies for CLI (skill coverage gaps, implicit default reconstruction errors, unobservable application semantics) and GUI (UI navigation failures, workflow execution failures, self-checking gaps) are derived from grounded trajectory analysis and provide actionable targets for system improvement rather than generic failure categories. - The finding that GPT-5.4 operating through GUI outperforms Codex GPT-5.5 operating through CLI despite Codex GPT-5.5 being a stronger underlying model is a concrete, surprising result that carries direct implications for agent system design choices. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge that the benchmark isolates modality effects by restricting agents to modality-native actions, which makes real deployed hybrid systems (combining GUI, terminal, scripts, APIs) out of scope; results should not be read as the maximum achievable performance of unconstrained agents. The patched-skill diagnostic uses verifier information during repair, so the 69.3% figure is a coverage-controlled diagnostic upper bound rather than a deployable CLI baseline. The failure taxonomy assigns a single primary cause per failure, but GUI failures in particular often mix control discovery and workflow execution, making the proportions a coarse summary rather than mutually exclusive causal labels. The authors engage with these limitations directly, which strengthens the paper's credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals are reported for any pass-rate comparison; the main result (48.2% vs. 59.1%) is an 11-percentage-point gap over 440 tasks and the absence of error bars prevents assessing whether workflow-level differences (e.g., CLI's 9.8% on Graphics Debugging) reflect genuine modality effects or small-sample noise. The 37.6% skill coverage metric is computed over verifier checkpoints, not tasks; a skill library that covers 37.6% of checkpoints could cover 0% or 100% of tasks depending on whether checkpoint failures cluster within tasks or distribute across them, and this relationship is not analyzed. The procedural grounding experiment (Section 7.2) produces a 0.5-percentage-point improvement in full pass rate (59.7% to 60.2%), which is negligible and suggests the experiment's 176-task subset may be underpowered or that grounding provides minimal benefit beyond faster execution, but neither interpretation is pursued. The benchmark is constructed by adapting OpenComputer tasks that already had CLI-Anything skill support; applications without existing skill coverage are excluded by design, which likely selects for applications where the CLI skill layer is more mature, potentially understating the real-world CLI coverage gap. Bottom Line Practitioners designing skill-mediated CLI agents gain a specific, actionable diagnosis from this paper: incomplete skill interfaces are the dominant bottleneck, not model reasoning, and the verifier-coverage pipeline methodology provides a concrete procedure for measuring and closing that gap.
Recommended 2606.24551 PDF
#51 — Innovative (66)

BrainG3N: A Dual-Purpose Tokenizer for Controllable 3D Brain MRI Generation (7 ▲ )

Max Van Puyvelde, Ibrahim Gulluk, Wim Van Criekinge, Olivier Gevaert · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a two-phase MAE-CNN tokenizer for 3D brain MRI that decouples a frozen self-supervised encoder (for clinically informative embeddings) from a lightweight CNN decoder (for voxel reconstruction), and demonstrates that a flow-matching diffusion transformer trained on those frozen embeddings supports both cross-sectional conditional generation and longitudinal forecasting. - Why it's important: Existing 3D latent-diffusion pipelines optimize the tokenizer against a reconstruction objective, which biases encoder embeddings toward voxel fidelity at the expense of clinical content — making the same embedding space unsuitable for both downstream clinical tasks and controllable generation. - How it does it: The encoder (a 3D ViT masked autoencoder pretrained on 35,309 volumes) is frozen after Phase 1; only a linear projection and a CNN decoder are trained in Phase 2, so the encoder never sees a reconstruction gradient and its clinical representations are preserved. - The good: On a 23-task frozen linear-probing benchmark evaluated on the same corpus with identical splits, the encoder outperforms or matches BrainIAC, BrainSegFounder, and MedicalNet on 21 of 23 tasks, with particularly large margins on IDH1 mutation (AUC 0.937 vs. 0.805 for the next best) and brain-age regression (MAE 4.43 y vs. 7.33 y). - The missing: The tokenizer architecture comparison against AutoencoderKL (AKL) is conducted at 1,100 volumes (a single tumor cohort), not at the full 35,309-volume scale — the claim that MAE-CNN is the right architectural choice rests on a small-scale ablation, not a full-scale rerun. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on 3D medical image generation or brain-MRI foundation models; the frozen-encoder trick is straightforward but the large-scale corpus, competitive probing results, and honest treatment of longitudinal limitations make this a useful reference point.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (58) and Practical Impact (55) pull the composite below 70: the authors themselves describe the frozen-encoder trick as straightforward with a 2D natural-image precedent, and code/weights are only promised, with no downstream task (synthetic-data augmentation) actually measured. Rigor (70) is the strongest dimension — 23-task probing with 5-fold subject-grouped CV and error bars, identical splits/probe code against three baselines, and a nearest-neighbor memorization audit — but the headline architectural ablation runs at 1,100 volumes rather than the full 35,309, no significance tests are reported, and the pretraining distribution-mismatch confound (acknowledged) means the probing margin conflates architecture with data scale. Completeness (72) benefits from a notably candid limitations section. Weighted: 0.20*68 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*55 = 66.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Neurology and neuro-oncology rely on 3D brain MRI for diagnosis (tumor grading, IDH1 mutation status, Alzheimer's staging) and longitudinal monitoring. Generative models could augment under-represented patient cohorts, enable privacy-preserving synthetic data sharing, and simulate disease trajectories — but direct voxel-space generation at full 160×192×160 resolution is computationally infeasible, so the field uses latent diffusion. Latent diffusion places two competing demands on a tokenizer: the encoder must capture clinical phenotypes that downstream classifiers can read off, and the decoder must reconstruct anatomically faithful voxels. Standard CNN-VAE (AutoencoderKL) tokenizers train encoder and decoder jointly against a reconstruction + KL loss, which pulls the encoder toward preserving local voxel statistics rather than global clinical semantics. The result is a latent space that is well-suited for image synthesis but poorly suited for clinical classification without fine-tuning. No prior 3D brain-MRI pipeline had demonstrated that a single frozen embedding space could serve both roles simultaneously. Key Idea Freeze the encoder after self-supervised masked-autoencoder pretraining, and train the diffusion tokenizer's decoder against a separate reconstruction objective on top of a linear projection of the frozen features. Because the encoder gradient is blocked during Phase 2, the reconstruction signal cannot corrupt the clinically informative representations learned in Phase 1. The encoder's 1,200-token output (each 1,152-dimensional) is mean-pooled to a single 1,152-dim vector for linear probing and projected to 1,200×32 for the diffusion model — two independent consumers of the same frozen feature space. Methodology Phase 1 trains a 3D MAE encoder: a 12-layer ViT (hidden dimension 1,152, 16 attention heads, MLP 4,608) with 16³ patches producing 1,200 tokens per volume. During pretraining, 70% of patches are randomly masked; a separate 8-layer ViT decoder reconstructs the 840 masked patches from 360 visible ones under per-patch MSE. The 35,309 training volumes span 18 public cohorts (ADNI, PPMI, NKI-RS, UCSF-PDGM, HCP-YA, IXI, and 12 others), four modalities (T1, T2, FLAIR, T1c), and 10 clinical categories; all volumes are registered to the SRI24 atlas via ANTs affine registration, skull-stripped with HD-BET, N4 bias-field corrected, and center-cropped to 160×192×160 at 1 mm isotropic. Phase 2 freezes the MAE encoder and trains a linear projection P ∈ ℝ^{1152×32} plus a 3D CNN decoder (ResBlocks + attention, 43.7M parameters) under voxel ℓ1 loss; the bottleneck d'=32 is chosen by sweeping {32, 128, 512} and selecting the smallest dimensionality that preserves IDH1 probing AUC within 3% of the full 1,152-dim encoder output (0.861 vs. 0.883). The conditional DiT is a 12-block flow-matching transformer (hidden dimension 1,152, 18 heads) trained on the 1,200×32 projected token sequences with adaLN-Zero modulation by a 6-component conditioning vector (disease 8-way, sex, modality 4-way, site 19-way, age continuous, IDH1 binary); flow-matching objective is ||v_θ(x_t, t, c) − (ε − x_1)||²₂ with classifier-free guidance at s=2.0. The longitudinal DiT variant replaces the noise-to-data interpolant with a stochastic Brownian-bridge interpolant between baseline and follow-up latents, conditioned on diagnosis, sex, baseline age, CDR score, and Δt. Critical design decisions: (1) Frozen encoder — justified by showing that joint MAE-AKL training degrades probing AUC relative to the frozen variant; the ablation is convincing within its 1,100-volume scope. (2) d'=32 bottleneck — trading reconstruction quality (PSNR 32.54 vs. 35.79 dB at full 1,152 dims) for a 43× reduction in diffusion model input dimensionality; the justification is quantitative and explicit. (3) Flow matching over DDPM — adopted from DiT literature without ablation against alternative generative objectives. Key Results On the 23-task linear-probing benchmark (5-fold subject-grouped stratified CV on all 35,309 volumes), the frozen MAE encoder outperforms or matches BrainIAC, BrainSegFounder, and MedicalNet on 21 of 23 tasks. Key numbers from Table 1 on the 8 reported tasks: IDH1 mutation AUC 0.937 ± 0.020 (BrainIAC 0.805 ± 0.042); tumor grade AUC 0.921 ± 0.019 (BSF 0.799 ± 0.036); disease 8-way AUC 0.948 ± 0.002 (BrainIAC 0.908 ± 0.004); brain-age MAE 4.43 ± 0.07 y (BrainIAC 7.33 ± 0.11); MMSE R² 0.427 (best modality, Table 6). The two non-wins — GDS depression and UPDRS-III — are near-floor tasks for every encoder. Controllable generation (Table 2a): disease HC-vs-AD probe agreement under COND = 0.99 vs. NULL = 0.92; age Pearson r = 0.93 under COND vs. r = 0.98 for real data; sex COND agreement 0.93 vs. NULL 0.43 F baseline; IDH1 COND agreement 0.52 vs. NULL 1.00 wildtype (weak, attributed to rare mutant class in the T1c tumor subset). Pooled 3D-FID: gen-vs-recons = 34.4, gen-vs-real = 107.3, recons-vs-real = 77.4 (the tokenizer reconstruction floor). Longitudinal forecasting (Section 3.4): Pearson r = 0.716 between requested and predicted brain-age advance on 64 held-out ADNI baselines; slope = 0.268, meaning the model recovers approximately 27% of true aging magnitude. The FID values are competitive with prior 3D medical generative work (FIDs 40–120 per the cited AKL paper), though cross-paper FID comparisons carry known caveats about reference set composition. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The core novelty is the frozen-MAE-as-diffusion-tokenizer design applied to 3D volumetric medical imaging at scale; a 2D precedent exists in natural imaging. The large-scale pretraining corpus (35,309 volumes, 18 cohorts, 200+ sites) is an engineering contribution, not a released dataset. The dual-use demonstration — same frozen space serving linear probing and conditional generation — is the substantive claim. The DiT architecture and flow-matching objective are standard components applied to a new domain. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations address two architectural choices (bottleneck dimension and MAE-vs-AKL tokenizer), both at 1,100-volume scale rather than the full 35,309-volume corpus (the authors acknowledge this). The full 23-task probing uses 5-fold subject-grouped stratified CV with error bars (mean ± std across folds); no formal significance tests are reported, though the margins on headline tasks are large enough to be practically meaningful. The head-to-head with BrainIAC, BrainSegFounder, and MedicalNet is conducted using identical splits and probe code, which is a credibility positive, but cohort distributions differ between the authors' corpus and the baselines' pretraining sets — the authors explicitly flag this as a potential confounder. BrainDINO, the most recent competitor (arXiv:2604.27277), is excluded because it has not released encoder weights. CFG scale is swept (s ∈ {1.5, 2.0, 3.0}), and a nearest-neighbor memorization audit in latent space (0/1,088 synthetic samples below the 5th-percentile real-to-real distance) is included. Code and model weights are promised under a research-use license but are not yet released at preprint time. Total training cost is stated: ~200 H100-hours. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile) - Practical: The system trains on 4×H100 nodes and runs linear probes on a 16-core CPU in ~2 hours per task; inference requires the frozen encoder and CNN decoder, both feasible on a single GPU. - Efficient: The 70% masking ratio means only 360 of 1,200 tokens enter the encoder per step, enabling larger per-GPU batch sizes than full-volume CNN-VAE at the same memory budget; DiT training runs ~1,100 epochs on 4 H100s. - Effective: Linear probing results on the full 35,309-volume corpus are strong for classification tasks (IDH1 AUC 0.937, tumor grade 0.921); regression tasks are mixed (brain-age MAE 4.43 y is good, MMSE R² 0.427 is modest, GDS and UPDRS-III are near-floor for all encoders). - Scalable: The method is tested at 35,309 volumes (the largest reported 3D brain-MRI pretraining corpus in this space); behavior at 10× that scale is not evaluated or predicted. The paper delivers code and weights under a research-use license (pending release). Strengths - The frozen-encoder design solves a real tension in latent diffusion pipelines with a clean architectural fix, and the head-to-head probing at matched splits is the right experimental protocol for measuring the gain. - The 35,309-volume pretraining corpus spanning 18 cohorts, 200+ sites, four modalities, and ten disease categories is the largest reported for 3D volumetric brain MRI, and the harmonized preprocessing pipeline is documented in enough detail to reproduce. - The probe-to-controllability transfer test (train a probe on real data, apply it to generated samples) provides a semantically grounded evaluation of generation quality beyond FID, directly connecting clinical representation to generative control. - The memorization audit via nearest-neighbor L₂ distances in the latent space addresses a legitimate privacy concern for synthetic medical data and is more convincing than FID alone. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors state four limitations directly: (1) findings are established only on brain MRI; transfer to CT, whole-body MRI, and ultrasound is untested; (2) the longitudinal bridge sampler recovers only ~27% of true aging magnitude (slope 0.268), attributed to the stochastic-bridge regularizer interpolating toward the conditional mean; (3) the AKL architectural comparison is at 1,100 volumes, not the full 35,309-volume scale; (4) pretraining distribution mismatch between their corpus and published baselines conflates architecture advantage with data advantage. They also note that acquisition confounders (field strength, scanner vendor) are detectable in the embeddings (probe AUC > 0.94), so cohort-matched evaluation is required for any downstream application. This is a notably candid limitations section. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 3D-FID metric is computed on 2D slices (5 central slices per anatomical view via Inception-V3), not on 3D volumes directly; this is an admitted compromise that may miss 3D structural coherence failures invisible in axial/coronal/sagittal slices. - IDH1 controllability (cond agreement 0.52, barely above the 0.88 wildtype prior's effective ceiling) is the weakest conditioning axis, and the explanation (rare mutant class in a small T1c subset) is plausible but not formally tested — a classifier trained on real T1c data with matched class balance would clarify whether the issue is CFG failure or data scarcity. - The longitudinal model is evaluated only on ADNI T1 subjects; whether it generalizes to non-AD disease trajectories (e.g., glioma progression, Parkinson's) is not assessed, though the training data includes CDR and disease labels. - The paper does not include a sample-efficiency analysis — whether the probing advantage over BrainIAC persists when only 10% or 1% of the 35,309-volume corpus is used for pretraining, which would clarify how much of the gain is architecture versus data scale. - No downstream task uses the generated samples (e.g., training a classifier on synthetic data and evaluating on real data), so the practical utility of the generated images for data augmentation remains a claim rather than a measured result. Bottom Line This preprint makes a technically clean contribution — decoupling the MAE encoder from the diffusion decoder is the right move and the probing results on 35,309 volumes convincingly support it — and any researcher building 3D brain-MRI generative pipelines or foundation encoders should read it, though the longitudinal magnitude attenuation and the unresolved distribution-mismatch confound in the head-to-head comparison leave room for follow-up work.
Recommended 2606.19651 PDF
#52 — Innovative (66)

WorldLines: Benchmarking and Modeling Long-Horizon Stateful Embodied Agents (3 ▲ )

Yehang Zhang, Jianchong Su, Haojian Huang, Yifan Chang, Tianhao Zhou, Xinli Xu, Yingjie Xu, Yinchuan Li, Zexi Li, Ying-Cong Chen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces WorldLines, a benchmark for evaluating long-horizon stateful embodied agents in household environments, along with ObsMem, an observer-grounded memory framework that separates event evidence, world-state trails, epistemic beliefs, and commitments into typed memory views for structured retrieval. - Why it's important: Existing long-term memory benchmarks test only text retrieval and QA, while existing embodied benchmarks confine evaluation to short episodes where state does not persist; no prior benchmark requires agents to maintain and exploit persistent, partially observable household state across days and then act on it. - How it does it: WorldLines generates cross-day household traces via a closed-loop Director-Actor-Executor LLM pipeline grounded in Habitat/HSSD scenes, then converts those traces into evidence-linked evaluation samples with programmatically enforced cutoffs; ObsMem gates incoming events through an observer visibility check and routes each event to one or more typed memory tracks (Event, State, Belief, Commitment) that are queried compositionally at answer time. - The good: ObsMem improves Memory QA Judge score by 0.138 over the strongest baseline (A-mem: 0.575 vs. ObsMem: 0.713) and improves StateMultiHop Event R@5 by 0.236 over A-mem (0.216 vs. 0.452), demonstrating that typed state trails substantially improve event-grounded recall on the hardest question family. - The missing: The planning evaluation rests on only 21 samples, making those results statistically fragile; there is no significance testing anywhere in the paper, and the benchmark is entirely synthetic with no validation against real household robot logs. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on embodied agent memory or long-horizon household assistance, specifically for the benchmark construction methodology and the typed-track memory architecture; practitioners expecting deployment-ready code should note the gap between ObsMem's structured inputs and what real perception systems deliver.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (benchmark + system). Novelty (72) is decent: WorldLines is the first benchmark coupling cross-day, partially observable embodied world state with evidence-linked QA and planning, and ObsMem's observer-gating plus epistemic belief tracking is a credible extension. The composite is pulled down by Rigor (62) and Practical Impact (58). Rigor suffers from no error bars or significance tests anywhere, a 21-sample planning probe too small to support its claimed 0.14 advantage, fully synthetic data with ideal entity annotations, questions and judge from the same GPT model family, and unconfirmed code release. Impact is low because ObsMem depends on structured entity IDs and visibility annotations that real perception does not deliver, and no code release is confirmed. The judge is validated against humans (κ=0.71) and ablations isolate each component, which keeps Completeness reasonable, but the small-sample findings are presented more firmly than the evidence warrants.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 68 Resilience 62 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Household robots operating over days must answer questions like "where did Bob put the coffee timer?" and generate plans that respect current object locations, device states, and past commitments. Prior long-term memory benchmarks (LongMemEval, LoCoMo, RealMem) evaluate text retrieval and dialogue consistency but decouple memory from physical world states. Existing embodied benchmarks (ALFWorld, Habitat 3.0, BEHAVIOR-1K) evaluate navigation and manipulation in short, stateless episodes. Neither genre tests whether an agent can maintain a correct, partially observable world model across multi-day interleaved activity and then use that model for downstream QA and task planning. The central challenge is that objects move outside the robot's view, device states change unobserved, and spoken reports may conflict with direct observations, making flat text-retrieval memories unreliable for state-centric questions. Key Idea The core insight is that household memory is not a single retrieval problem but a composition of semantically distinct evidence types: what happened (events), what is currently true (world state), what the robot is confident it knows (beliefs), and what is obligated in the future (commitments). A memory system that conflates these types into a single text store cannot reliably distinguish a reported claim from a direct observation, a current state from an overwritten one, or a routine from an anomalous update. ObsMem operationalizes this by assigning each incoming event to typed tracks at write time and composing those tracks at query time according to question intent, rather than performing one undifferentiated similarity search. Methodology WorldLines construction proceeds through four stages. First, household worlds are defined using Habitat/HSSD scenes, curating interactive objects, receptacles, and controllable devices into an executable scene graph with affordance constraints. Second, a project-planning LLM assigns each family a multi-day "life theme" (e.g., meal preparation, home organization) and generates a project day-beat schedule specifying phases, milestones, and disruptions without binding concrete actions. Third, a closed-loop trace generator runs each day as: a Director LLM produces a session plan with time segments; actor LLMs (resident and robot) propose utterances and action sequences; a deterministic Executor validates entity references, affordance preconditions, and action constraints before committing state changes, ensuring only valid transitions enter the trace. Carry-forward memory (extracted notes on state changes, unresolved plans, preferences) is passed to later days, coupling each day's context to prior history. Fourth, evidence-linked evaluation samples are mined by indexing event timelines and state histories, generating question-answer candidates with GPT-4o-mini under evidence constraints, manually verifying answerability and absence of post-cutoff leakage, then setting programmatic cutoffs that hide future events from evaluated agents. Two task families result: Memory QA (StateSingleHop, StateMultiHop, TemporalMemory sub-tasks) and Embodied Task Planning (next-step or full-plan generation). ObsMem operates as an online memory layer: each event passes an observer gate that writes it only if the robot is in the visibility set, tagging utterances additionally as reported-provenance; the State Track maintains per-(entity, attribute) histories and current snapshots via argmax-timestamp selection; the Belief Track assigns epistemic status (fresh, stale, uncertain, contradicted) via deterministic thresholds on intervening events, actors, and contradicting evidence; the Commitment Track stores future obligations from utterances. Episode consolidation periodically synthesizes episode cards augmenting but not replacing raw event records. At query time, a query planner (LLM) classifies intent and selects relevant memory views; an evidence selector (LLM) reranks and combines up to five typed candidate records for the answer generator. Key Results All systems are evaluated on 310 Memory QA samples using google/gemini-3.5-flash as the answer backbone and GPT-4o as judge (validated at Fleiss' κ = 0.71 agreement with human annotators, 0.82 Spearman correlation). ObsMem achieves Judge 0.713 and Perfect Rate 69% versus A-mem (0.575, 53%), Mem0 (0.554, 53%), GraphMem (0.457, 39%), and MemoryOS (0.312, 29%). On Event R@5 (event-level retrieval), ObsMem reaches 0.537 vs. A-mem's 0.355 and Mem0's 0.378. The most revealing diagnostic is the Session Any@5 vs. Event R@5 gap: A-mem achieves Session Any@5 of 0.839 (frequently retrieves the correct session) but Event R@5 of only 0.355 (rarely retrieves the exact state-changing event), while ObsMem narrows this gap to 0.879 vs. 0.537. On StateMultiHop Event R@5, ObsMem (0.452) improves over A-mem (0.216) by 0.236. On the 21-sample planning probe, ObsMem leads on all four dimensions: Plan Judge 0.684, State Consistency 0.721, Precondition Validity 0.702, Memory Use 0.690. Ablation on 62 diagnostic samples shows the evidence selector is most critical (removing it drops Judge by 0.264), followed by episode consolidation (-0.145) and world-state retrieval (-0.102); belief-track removal has the smallest overall effect (-0.048) but eliminates Hidden Judge performance (0.278 → 0.000) on the 6 hidden-until-observed questions. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (WorldLines: first benchmark combining cross-day embodied traces with persistent, partially observable world state, physical actions, and evidence-linked QA + planning tasks) and NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (ObsMem: typed-track observer-grounded memory framework). The core benchmark construction methodology — closed-loop LLM trace generation with deterministic executor validation and programmatic cutoff enforcement — is genuinely novel as an approach to generating evidence-grounded evaluation data. ObsMem's typed-track architecture extends prior structured memory ideas (MemGPT, MemoryOS, StructMem) by adding explicit observer gating and epistemic belief tracking; the combination is novel but each individual component draws on prior work. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations isolate four individual mechanisms of ObsMem and show each matters, with the evidence selector providing by far the largest contribution. No error bars or significance tests appear anywhere; with 310 QA samples and 21 planning samples, differences of 0.05 in Judge score are not statistically validated. The planning evaluation at 21 samples is too small to draw firm conclusions. Questions and reference answers are generated by GPT-4o-mini and judged by GPT-4o — both from the same model family, which risks systematic alignment between the question generator and the judge in ways that may not reflect human difficulty. The judge validation covers 80 samples across all methods, which is reasonable for the QA evaluation but leaves the planning judge unvalidated. Code and data availability are not stated in the paper. The benchmark relies on Habitat/HSSD scenes (publicly available), but the trace generation pipeline and ObsMem implementation are described in enough detail to reimplement; reproducibility from the paper alone would require non-trivial engineering effort. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (benchmark) + SYSTEMS (ObsMem framework). - Practical: WorldLines trace generation runs on standard LLM APIs; ObsMem is a software framework requiring no specialized hardware, though it depends on structured entity IDs and visibility annotations that require perception infrastructure in real deployments. - Efficient: ObsMem incurs higher latency than flat-text baselines (5.13s vs. A-mem's 3.35s) due to typed retrieval and belief-aware reranking, and uses 562 prompt tokens on average; it returns fewer records (3.4 on average vs. 5.0 for others) but with higher precision. - Effective: Evidence on the 310-sample QA benchmark and the 21-sample planning probe shows ObsMem improves answer quality and state consistency; however, all evidence is on synthetic, simulation-grounded data with ideal entity annotations, not real households. - Scalable: The benchmark contains 310 QA samples and 21 planning samples — small by standard benchmarking norms. ObsMem's state trail is structured rather than parametric, so it scales linearly with trace length, but no experiments test scaling to longer horizons (weeks rather than days) or denser environments. ObsMem is delivered as a described software framework; code release is not confirmed in the paper. Strengths - The benchmark design is methodologically careful: programmatic cutoffs prevent future information leakage, evidence chains are manually verified for answerability, and the judge is validated against human annotators with inter-rater agreement reported. - The Session Any@5 vs. Event R@5 diagnostic metric cleanly separates coarse session recall from precise event grounding, revealing a failure mode invisible to session-level metrics alone — this is a genuine empirical contribution beyond the new data. - The observer-gating formalism (Equation 1) explicitly distinguishes directly observed events from reported claims, a distinction prior memory systems collapse; the belief confidence decay mechanism (Equation 3) handles partial observability in a principled rather than heuristic way. - The closed-loop Director-Actor-Executor trace generation pipeline, in which only affordance-validated actions enter the state trace, is a non-trivial engineering contribution that prevents the hallucinated-state contamination common in LLM-generated embodied data. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge two limitations. WorldLines is confined to simulated environments and does not cover perception noise, actuation errors, or open-ended human behavior in real homes. ObsMem requires entity identifiers, visibility annotations, and action schemas that would need to come from perception, localization, and grounding modules in real deployment, and introduces additional latency. The authors treat these as future work directions (real robot logs, visual observations, full physical simulation, more efficient retrieval with visual perception integration). These are honest and non-minimized acknowledgments. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The planning evaluation on 21 samples is too small to support the reported conclusions; a 0.14 Plan Judge advantage for ObsMem over A-mem could plausibly be within noise, yet the paper presents it as a confirmed finding. - All five systems use the same answer backbone (google/gemini-3.5-flash), so the results measure how well each memory system serves one specific model's inference; generalization to other LLM backbones is unvalidated. - Questions are generated by GPT-4o-mini and judged by GPT-4o; the shared model family may bias question difficulty and judge alignment in ways that favor approaches whose retrieval outputs look more GPT-idiomatic. - The benchmark's temporal scope (a few household days per family, implied by the project beat design) is not precisely quantified — the paper does not report the total number of household families, average days per family, or total event count, making it hard to calibrate whether the 310 QA samples provide adequate coverage of the stated question families. - ObsMem's belief threshold hyperparameters (λ_I, λ_m, λ_A) are described as fixed implementation hyperparameters but no sensitivity analysis or ablation on their values is provided. - The benchmark is currently preprint-only with no peer review; its construction quality depends substantially on the authors' manual verification effort, which is not independently audited. Bottom Line WorldLines fills a genuine gap by being the first benchmark that couples long-term agent memory with persistent, partially observable embodied world states and executable planning tasks, and ObsMem's typed-track architecture makes a credible empirical case for observer-grounded memory; however, the planning evaluation at 21 samples is too small to rely on, and the fully synthetic setting with ideal entity annotations limits claims about real-world readiness.
Recommended 2606.18847 PDF
#53 — Innovative (66)

Behaviour-driven adaptive feedback in LLM-guided 3D spatial tasks: effects on trust, reflection, and task performance

L Wu, S Xu, W Liao, S Fujimura · tandfonline.com · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a behaviour-driven adaptive feedback system that monitors real-time user actions (pauses >10s, repeated backtracking, layout reorganisation) in a browser-based 3D Soma cube assembly environment and triggers GPT-4.1-generated natural language guidance at behaviourally inferred moments of cognitive difficulty. - Why it's important: Users in complex 3D spatial tasks frequently experience strategic confusion and cognitive overload that neither scheduled proactive feedback nor user-requested assistance handles well; timed systems interrupt at wrong moments while request-based systems fail when users don't know they need help. - How it does it: Three rule-based detectors (pause, backtrack, strategy-change) evaluate timestamped action logs, apply a priority hierarchy and 60-second cooldown, then construct a GPT-4.1 prompt encoding the current assembly state and behavioural context to generate spatially grounded guidance. - The good: Adaptive feedback reduced task completion time by 46% vs. no feedback (262.6s vs. 486.5s) and 21% vs. request-based (333.7s), with a large effect size (η²=.430) on difficulty-matched tasks — the most compelling quantitative result. - The missing: One task per condition (N=30, single university, Japan) precludes learning trajectory analysis, and individual spatial ability — which almost certainly moderates adaptive feedback benefit — was not measured. - Read it or not: Worth reading for HCI researchers designing LLM-driven tutoring or task-guidance systems; the reversed H2 finding (adaptive feedback rated more appropriate than user-controlled feedback) is a genuine empirical surprise with design implications.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (controlled HCI study with a systems component). Rigor (70) drives the score: within-subjects Latin Square with verified absence of order effects, ART ANOVA matched to violated normality assumptions, bootstrap mediation, and public code release — tempered by preprint status, an unvalidated 2-item appropriateness scale, and one task per condition. Non-Obviousness (70) is high because the reversed H2 (system-triggered feedback rated more appropriate than user-controlled) falsifies a foundational HCI assumption, and the differential mediation result identifies two distinct pathways. Novelty (60) is moderate: a new empirical finding plus a system that recombines GPT-4.1 with rule-based detectors rather than introducing new algorithms. Completeness (72) is strong from honest, specific limitation acknowledgment; Resilience (62) is pulled down by the absence of any learning-transfer or retention measure, so 'learned faster' cannot be separated from 'did less thinking.' Practical Impact (55) reflects an open-source artifact offset by API dependence in the stated offline target domains. This paper most resembles the user's positive set: rigorous controlled comparison with a genuine empirical surprise. Weighted composite: 0.20*60 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*55 = 66.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 72 Resilience 62 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Complex 3D spatial assembly tasks impose high cognitive demands: users simultaneously coordinate multiple elements, maintain spatial reference frames, and follow procedural instructions, frequently experiencing strategic confusion, cognitive impasse, and backtracking. Traditional static guidance (pre-recorded tutorials, proactive feedback on predetermined schedules) misaligns with dynamic individual cognitive states; prior work shows that timed proactive assistance can reduce perceived appropriateness when delivered at wrong moments. Request-based systems preserve user autonomy but fail when users are uncertain they need help or hesitant to seek it. The gap is a system that detects actual cognitive difficulty from observable behaviour and delivers assistance at the right moment — a capability that requires both behavioural recognition and contextually aware language generation. The target domains include technical training, virtual assembly, industrial inspection, and rehabilitative spatial therapy, all of which share scalability and consistency problems when relying on expert human instructors. Key Idea The core insight is that three observable action patterns — prolonged pause (signalling working memory impasse), repeated piece backtracking (signalling strategic uncertainty), and multi-piece layout reorganisation (signalling fundamental approach revision) — serve as proxy indicators of distinct cognitive state transitions, and that these signals can be detected with simple rule-based algorithms and used to trigger GPT-4.1 with a spatially rich context prompt so that guidance arrives at a behaviourally appropriate moment rather than at a user-determined or scheduled moment. The paper's most interesting sub-finding is that this behavioural triggering actually yields higher perceived appropriateness than user-controlled request-based assistance, contradicting a foundational HCI assumption about the primacy of user control. Methodology The system (llm-soma-r) uses a React + Vite frontend, Node.js + Express backend with SQLite logging, and OpenAI GPT-4.1 API at temperature=0.3. Three detection algorithms operate on timestamped action logs: (A1) pause detection flags inactivity gaps >10,000ms; (A2) backtracking detection flags ≥2 returns of any Soma piece to a previously serialised position within a 60-second window; (A3) strategy change detection flags layout snapshots where ≥2 pieces changed position or orientation. A hierarchical priority scheme (pause > backtrack > strategy-change) selects one active trigger per detection cycle, and a 60-second per-category cooldown prevents feedback saturation. Assembly state is abstracted into a natural language description encoding piece positions, adjacency relationships, and progress assessment; this description plus behavioural context composes the user-facing LLM prompt. Response validation checks spatial feasibility (ensuring suggested actions reference available pieces) and linguistic consistency (colour-coded terminology) before display. Study design: within-subjects Latin Square (N=30; 13 female, 17 male; age M=28.6, SD=3.4; university community; no prior Soma cube experience), three conditions (No Feedback, Request-Based Feedback, Adaptive Feedback), one 3-star-difficulty task per condition drawn randomly from three balanced alternatives, yielding 90 individual sessions of 60-80 minutes each. Task difficulty equivalence was verified by manufacturer ratings and confirmed by grayscale silhouette presentation (removing colour cues). Behavioural monitoring ran transparently in the background. Primary measures (7-point Likert): trust (adapted 2-item Human-Computer Trust Scale), perceived feedback appropriateness (2 study-developed items), reflective engagement (adapted Kember et al. 2000 scale), cognitive load (SASSI Cognitive Demand subscale), perceived task difficulty (NASA-TLX mental demand dimension). Task completion time was objectively logged. Confirmatory hypotheses (H1: trust, H3: reflection) used Bonferroni-corrected α=.025; all others α=.05. Assumption violations (confirmed by Shapiro-Wilk and Levene's tests) led to Aligned Rank Transform (ART) ANOVA as the primary inferential method; mediation used mixed-effects models with 1000-sample bootstrap. Order effects were pre-tested and confirmed non-significant. Critical design decisions: (1) Latin Square counterbalancing to eliminate order confounds — empirically verified. (2) Manufacturer-rated equivalent difficulty across all nine tasks — removes task complexity as a confound. (3) Hidden real-time monitoring — prevents reactance and preserves natural behaviour. The backtracking threshold (2 within 60s) was empirically derived during pilot testing (N=6) with no theoretical grounding, which the authors acknowledge. Key Results H1 (Trust): Supported. Adaptive (M=6.03, SD=0.88) > Request-based (M=5.55, SD=0.82); ART ANOVA F(1,58)=9.15, p=.004, r=.370 (medium effect). H2 (Feedback Appropriateness): Not supported — reversed. Adaptive (M=5.62, SD=0.78) > Request-based (M=5.33, SD=0.74); ART ANOVA F(1,58)=4.16, p=.046, r=.260 (small-medium effect). The predicted direction (user-control favours appropriateness) was empirically wrong. H3 (Reflective Engagement): Supported. Adaptive (M=5.77, SD=0.70) > Request-based (M=5.37, SD=0.79); ART ANOVA F(1,58)=6.07, p=.017, r=.310 (medium effect). H4 (Task Completion Time): Supported. F(2,87)=33.35, p<.001, η²=.430 (large effect). All pairwise differences significant: adaptive vs. no feedback mean difference=223.9s (p<.001); request-based vs. no feedback=152.9s (p<.001); adaptive vs. request-based=71.1s (p=.034). H5 (Cognitive Load): Not supported. ART ANOVA F(2,87)=0.15, p=.858; means near-identical (~5.1-5.25) across conditions. Perceived task difficulty: F(2,87)=4.22, p=.018; adaptive (M=4.70) significantly lower than no feedback (M=5.57, p=.016); adaptive vs. request-based (p=.112) not significant. Mediation: Trust fully mediates feedback type → appropriateness (indirect effect β=0.275, 95% CI [0.044, 0.526], p=.024; 98.2% of total effect). Trust does NOT significantly mediate feedback type → reflective engagement (indirect effect β=0.129, 95% CI [-0.084, 0.360], p=.196). These differential pathways are a theoretically meaningful finding. Behavioural analysis: Fewer backtracking events in feedback-enabled conditions; no significant difference in strategy changes across conditions. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The system (llm-soma-r) is a novel integration of rule-based behavioural recognition with LLM-generated spatial guidance in a controlled HCI experiment. The empirically genuine novelty is the reversed H2 finding — that behaviourally-triggered adaptive feedback earns higher appropriateness ratings than user-controlled assistance — which challenges a long-standing HCI design principle. The technical pipeline (behavioural detection + LLM context prompting) recombines known components (GPT-4.1 API, React/Node.js stack, threshold-based state detection) rather than introducing fundamentally new algorithms, but the controlled empirical comparison across three conditions in a spatial task context is a meaningful contribution to the adaptive feedback literature. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly released at https://github.com/Sheswlk/llm-soma-adaptive-feedback-system. Within-subjects design effectively controls individual differences. Latin Square counterbalancing with confirmed absence of order effects. ART ANOVA is appropriate for violated normality assumptions, with Shapiro-Wilk and Levene's tests explicitly reported. Bootstrap mediation (1000 samples) is appropriate for small samples. Power analysis targeted 95% power for d=0.5 (medium effect) at N=30 under parametric assumptions; authors acknowledge actual power for ART tests may differ. Pilot study (N=6) refined thresholds. Methodological concerns: (1) One task per condition precludes learning trajectory analysis — a substantial limitation for educational claims. (2) Backtracking threshold (2 occurrences within 60s) was empirically calibrated during pilot, not theoretically derived, and is fixed across individuals despite likely heterogeneity in problem-solving pace. (3) The 2-item appropriateness scale was developed for this study with no prior validation; with only 2 items, internal consistency (α) cannot be meaningfully computed and is not reported for this scale. (4) Trust and appropriateness are not measured in the no-feedback condition, which prevents a true baseline. (5) GPT-4.1 API introduces response variability that temperature=0.3 reduces but does not eliminate; no analysis of variability's effect on outcomes is reported.
