Deployability (EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS profile) - Practical: Yes; runs on a standard 15.6-inch 4K UHD laptop via web browser with OpenAI API access — no specialised hardware required. - Efficient: LLM API calls for feedback generation: average latency and API cost per session are not reported, making efficiency benchmarking against simpler rule-based alternatives impossible. - Effective: The 46% reduction in task completion time on difficulty-matched tasks is strong proximal evidence; however, no downstream learning measures (retention, transfer, skill acquisition) were collected, so effectiveness for educational or training goals is claimed but not measured. - Scalable: The architecture is web-based and designed to support concurrent users, but no load testing or multi-user deployment data are reported; the current study ran one participant at a time. The work delivers a functioning open-source web system and detailed algorithmic appendices (A1-A5) enabling replication. Strengths - The reversed H2 finding — adaptive system-initiated feedback rated as more appropriate than user-controlled request-based feedback — is a credible empirical challenge to established user-control design principles, supported by a pre-registered directional hypothesis that was explicitly tested and falsified. - The differential mediation result (trust fully mediates appropriateness, accounts for 98.2% of effect; trust does NOT mediate reflection, p=.196) reveals two distinct psychological pathways and avoids the common paper failing of treating all outcomes as arising from a single mechanism. - Task performance effect size (η²=.430, 46% time reduction) is large and practically meaningful for training contexts; it is measured objectively and on difficulty-matched tasks, making it the study's most credible quantitative claim. - The within-subjects Latin Square with manufacturer-verified equal-difficulty task matching effectively isolates feedback strategy as the causal variable, providing cleaner causal inference than between-subjects or uncontrolled-difficulty designs. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) one task per condition limits learning trajectory and adaptation pattern analysis, recommending 5-7 tasks per condition in future work; (2) the backtracking threshold lacks theoretical grounding and is fixed despite individual differences in problem-solving pace; (3) no individual difference moderators (spatial ability via Mental Rotation Test, technology self-efficacy, need for control, cultural dimensions) were measured; (4) LLM response variability despite temperature control may affect user experience; (5) the single cultural context (Japan) limits generalisability; (6) sequential trigger evaluation misses co-occurring behavioural patterns. These are confronted directly and credited as credibility-positive acknowledgements. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No learning transfer or retention measure. Faster completion could reflect better strategic guidance or simply the system substituting for user problem-solving effort. For the stated application domains (technical training, medical training), skill acquisition — not speed — is the outcome that matters. The paper cannot distinguish "learned faster" from "did less thinking." - Task difficulty equivalence rests solely on manufacturer star ratings for a toy puzzle product. No psychometric calibration (blind rater agreement, item response theory, or pilot completion times) was conducted. - The 2-item feedback appropriateness scale was developed for this study with no prior validation, no internal consistency statistic reported, and no comparison against established HCI appropriateness measures. Treating it as a validated scale inflates the evidentiary weight of H2. - Demand characteristics are unaddressed. In a 60-80 minute within-subjects session where participants experience all three conditions, the adaptive condition is the most technologically sophisticated and visible — social desirability pressure could inflate trust and appropriateness ratings for it without any mechanism beyond novelty. - GPT-4.1 API dependence: the three stated target application domains (medical training, industrial assembly, rehabilitation) frequently operate in offline, privacy-sensitive, or air-gapped environments. The feasibility of API-dependent LLM integration in these contexts is not discussed. - No analysis of which specific behavioural trigger (pause vs. backtrack vs. strategy-change) was most commonly activated or most predictive of outcome improvement — collapsing all triggers under "adaptive feedback" leaves the active ingredient unidentified. Bottom Line The reversed appropriateness finding and differential mediation pathways are genuine empirical contributions worth the read for HCI researchers working on adaptive AI guidance systems, but the one-task-per-condition design and absence of any learning transfer measure mean practitioners should not yet treat the educational effectiveness claims as established.
Search Results
#54 — Innovative (66)

V-Zero: Answer-Label-Free On-Policy Distillation with Contrastive Evidence Gating for Fine-Grained Visual Reasoning (19 ▲ )

Haoxiang Sun, Zhihang Yi, Langxuan Deng, Yuhao Zhou, Peiqi Jia, Jian Zhao, Li Yuan, Jiancheng Lv, Tao Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces V-Zero, a training framework that improves fine-grained visual reasoning in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) by gating on-policy distillation with a contrastive visual evidence signal derived from paired positive and negative image crops, requiring no annotated textual answer labels. - Why it's important: Fine-grained visual reasoning is a critical bottleneck for MLLMs answering questions about localized image regions, and existing solutions either require costly reinforcement learning with hand-designed reward rules or large-scale annotated reasoning traces — both expensive and prone to catastrophic forgetting. - How it does it: The teacher model replays each student-sampled trajectory under two additional visual conditions (a task-relevant region crop versus an irrelevant downsampled crop), and the log-probability gap between these two views becomes a trajectory-level gate that reweights the per-trajectory token-level distillation loss toward the positive teacher view. - The good: V-Zero trains a 4B student to 76.9 average across six benchmarks in 4.8 hours on 8× RTX PRO 6000 GPUs, outperforming ZwZ-8B (76.4) and all compared 7B agentic systems while using over 5× less training time than SFT baselines and over 10× less than RL baselines. - The missing: The positive crop (z+) comes directly from the ZwZ 23K training set, which contains human-annotated question-relevant regional crops; the method therefore depends on region-level human annotation at training time, a dependency the "answer-label-free" framing systematically downplays. - Read it or not: Researchers working on MLLM post-training, visual grounding, or label-efficient distillation should read this; practitioners seeking a drop-in replacement for RL-based visual reasoning training will find it practically useful.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (76) are the top contributors: the contrastive evidence gate — using the teacher's log-probability gap between a task-relevant and an irrelevant crop as a label-free trajectory-quality signal — is a NEW METHOD with a NEW-FORMULATION component (negative-free stop-gradient OPD), and the analyst notes it has no direct precedent in the cited literature. Rigor (62) is solid for a preprint: ablations span five axes (gate presence, crop quality, teacher size, group size, training steps), the random-crop collapse to 72.5 concretely proves the signal does real work, and baselines are independently re-run; it is held back by absent seeds/error bars and code that is promised but unreleased. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) are credible but bounded — the 'answer-label-free' framing systematically downplays the human-annotated region crops that supply the positive view, and the core assumption that teacher certainty-gain correlates with trajectory correctness is not directly validated, both exploitable by a skeptical reviewer. Practical Impact (55) reflects a useful training recipe and a 4B student beating an 8B SFT model in 4.8 GPU-hours, but no released weights and single-backbone scope. The methodology and on-policy-distillation framing resemble the user's positive calibration set. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*76 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*55 = 66.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 76 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Fine-grained visual reasoning requires MLLMs to identify task-relevant local image regions (small text, specific objects, spatial relations in dense scenes) rather than relying on global scene understanding. Standard MLLMs fail this task when answers depend on localized visual search. Two established training approaches each carry high cost: RL-based agentic methods (DeepEyes, Thyme, Pixel Reasoner) require costly exploration and predefined verifiable reward rules; supervised fine-tuning on annotated image-text data (as in Zooming without Zooming, ZwZ) risks catastrophic forgetting and demands large-scale textual supervision. On-Policy Distillation (OPD) sidesteps both problems by training on the student's own trajectories with dense teacher supervision, but standard OPD provides only token-level correction — once a trajectory drifts toward an incorrect reasoning path, the teacher can only suggest locally plausible next tokens without assessing whether the trajectory as a whole is on track. Key Idea The core insight is that a teacher model's change in log-probability when given task-relevant visual evidence (versus irrelevant evidence) is a reliable proxy for how well a student trajectory is grounded in the correct visual region — and this signal is computable without any ground-truth answer label. By contrasting teacher log-probabilities under a positive crop (the target region) and a negative crop (a random irrelevant region), V-Zero derives a trajectory-level quality score that reweights which student rollouts should be emphasized during token-level distillation. This separates evidence assessment (done by the teacher via contrastive views) from imitation (done by aligning the student to the positive teacher distribution). Methodology V-Zero operates in three stages per training step. First, the student (Qwen3.5-4B) samples G=8 sibling rollouts from the full image and question using autoregressive sampling. Second, the teacher (Qwen3.5-27B) replays each rollout under three conditions: (a) full image only, (b) full image plus a positive crop z+ (a task-relevant region supplied by the training dataset), and (c) full image plus a negative crop z- (an equal-size crop sampled randomly from outside the target region in a 2× downsampled image). Per-token log-probability gaps Δ_k^(g) = ℓ_{+,k}^(g) − ℓ_{-,k}^(g) are averaged to a trajectory-level score p^(g), which is z-normalized across siblings to produce an advantage a^(g). Third, this advantage becomes a stop-gradient gate w^(g) = clip(1 + a^(g), 0, 2), which scales the per-trajectory contribution to the reverse-KL distillation loss against the positive teacher view. The training objective is sampled-token reverse-KL minimization (Eq. 24), with both the gate and the log-ratio score detached from the student gradient. Critical design decisions: (1) using log-prob gap rather than answer correctness as the quality signal — justified as label-free but assumes teacher certainty gain from the positive crop correlates with trajectory correctness, an assumption not directly validated; (2) discarding the negative view from the distillation target (used only for gating), cleanly separating evidence assessment from imitation; (3) within-group normalization (GRPO-style), a standard variance-reduction technique. Training uses VeRL with batch size 32, PPO mini-batch 16, lr=1e-6, max response length 2,048 tokens, for 60 steps on 8× RTX PRO 6000 96GB GPUs. Key Results On six benchmarks (VStar, HR-Bench 4K, HR-Bench 8K, ZoomBench, MME-RealWorld, MMStar), V-Zero-4B achieves an average of 76.9, compared with 73.7 for the Qwen3.5-4B backbone (+3.2), 74.2 for ZwZ-4B, and 76.4 for ZwZ-8B — beating the 8B SFT model with a 4B student. Individual gains over the backbone: +4.7 on VStar (89.0 vs. 84.3), +3.4 on HR-4K (87.8 vs. 84.4), +2.5 on HR-8K (82.6 vs. 80.1), +5.6 on ZoomBench (57.8 vs. 52.2). Among 7B agentic baselines (DeepEyes, Thyme, Pixel Reasoner, DeepEyesV2), V-Zero-4B outperforms all on available benchmarks, though these use different backbone families (Qwen2.5 vs. Qwen3.5), making the comparison cross-system rather than controlled. The OOD MMStar score of 74.4 (vs. 71.8 for Qwen3.5-4B backbone) indicates that general multimodal capability is preserved. Ablations show: removing the gate reduces perception average from 79.2 to 78.0; using random crops for both views collapses performance to 72.5 (below no-gate OPD), confirming the positive crop quality is load-bearing. The hardware-adjusted speedup over ZwZ (>5×) and DeepEyes (>10×) is explicitly qualified as conservative due to RTX PRO 6000 having weaker BF16 throughput than H100. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.25319v1). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The contrastive evidence gating mechanism — using paired positive/negative visual crops to derive a trajectory-level quality gate for label-free OPD — is the primary novel contribution. The reframing of OPD as negative-free stop-gradient alignment is NEW FORMULATION: a genuine analytic contribution that clarifies why standard OPD lacks trajectory-level discrimination. Individual components (OPD, contrastive learning, image cropping as visual evidence) are individually established; their specific integration for reward-free, label-free visual reasoning training is new. The idea of using teacher log-probability change under informative vs. uninformative visual context as a quality proxy has no direct precedent in the cited literature. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover four design axes: gating presence, crop quality (positive vs. random), teacher size (9B vs. 27B), rollout group size (G=4 vs. G=8), and training steps (30 to 70). This covers the main design choices but leaves the gate clipping bounds (w_min=0, w_max=2) unablated — sensitivity to these hyperparameters is unknown. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported, making it impossible to assess result variance. The paper re-runs all backbone comparisons independently (marked with *), which is a credibility-positive practice. Code and dataset are promised at https://github.com/eVIgroup-SCU/V-Zero but not released at submission time. A key methodological concern: the training data (23K samples from ZwZ) includes human-annotated question-relevant regional crops used as the positive view z+; the method's performance depends critically on the quality of these annotations (the random-crop ablation drops 6.7 points). This means the system is not annotation-free at the data-curation level, only at the label level per training example. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The 4B student performs standard full-image inference post-training with no added tool calls; training requires 8× RTX PRO 6000 96GB GPUs, which are commercially available but high-end. - Efficient: Each training rollout requires three teacher forward passes (full, positive, negative), approximately tripling teacher inference cost relative to standard OPD; the 4.8h total training time suggests this overhead is manageable in practice. - Effective: The contrastive gate provides +1.2 points perception average over vanilla OPD (79.2 vs. 78.0), and the full system provides +3.2 over the base model; the gains are consistent across four of five fine-grained benchmarks. - Scalable: Tested only on one student size (4B) and one training set (23K examples); no scaling experiments across student sizes, dataset sizes, or MLLM families are presented, so generalization beyond the tested configuration is a claim without experimental support. The work delivers a training recipe and promises code/data; no trained model weights are released. Strengths - The negative-free stop-gradient alignment view of OPD (Eq. 10–13) is a concise and correct theoretical lens that explains both OPD's efficiency and its trajectory-level blind spot; this framing is useful beyond this paper for understanding the OPD design space. - The ablation in Table 2 is a strong internal validation: random crops (Rand. R R) scoring 72.5 — well below both no-gate OPD (78.0) and V-Zero (79.2) — demonstrates that the contrastive signal is doing real work, not merely adding regularization noise. - Eliminating inference-time tool use (no zoom operations at test time) is a practical strength: the visual grounding benefit is internalized into the student's weights, avoiding latency overhead. - The honest acknowledgment that training speedup comparisons are conservative due to hardware differences, and that cross-backbone comparisons are not controlled ablations, reflects appropriate epistemic care for a preprint. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) the HR-8K gating benefit is small because 8K resolution already provides sufficient visual detail in the full image, limiting gating utility; (2) cross-backbone comparisons with 7B baselines (DeepEyes, etc.) are not controlled and should be read as cross-system results; (3) individual benchmarks peak at different training steps, so the step-60 checkpoint is a perception-average optimum that may not be best for all tasks; (4) the wall-clock speedup is conservative due to hardware differences. These are confronted directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The "answer-label-free" framing requires qualification: z+ comes from the ZwZ 23K dataset, which contains human-annotated question-relevant regional crops. The method requires these region annotations to generate useful positive views; without them, the system degrades substantially (random crop ablation: 72.5). The paper does not discuss how V-Zero would perform if positive crops were obtained via automated methods (e.g., attention-based crop selection or object detection), which would determine whether the annotation dependency is fundamental. - No ablation of a same-size or smaller teacher (e.g., 4B teacher with 4B student) is provided; it is unknown whether the 27B → 4B gap is necessary or whether a 9B/4B self-distillation variant could work comparably. - The three-teacher-pass overhead per rollout is not broken down in the training cost table; the paper reports total wall-clock time but not how much is student rollout vs. teacher evidence evaluation, making it hard to project costs to larger rollout groups. - Only one student backbone (Qwen3.5-4B) is evaluated; transferability to other MLLM families (e.g., LLaVA, InternVL, Llama-based) is entirely undemonstrated. - MMStar is the sole OOD evaluation; tasks like mathematical reasoning or code understanding — where fine-grained visual reasoning is not the bottleneck — are absent, leaving the catastrophic forgetting question partially open. Bottom Line Practitioners seeking a fast, label-efficient post-training recipe for MLLM visual grounding should read this work; the contrastive evidence gating idea is sound and the efficiency gains are real, but users should account for the region-annotation dependency in the training data before treating the method as fully annotation-free.
Recommended 2606.25319 PDF
#55 — Innovative (65)

CLI-Universe: Towards Verifiable Task Synthesis Engine for Terminal Agents (27 ▲ )

Zhanbo Hua, Yifan Yao, Weihao Xie, Yongchi Zhao, Minghao Liu, Ruizhi Qiu, Zhewei Huang, Zun Wang, Yiyan Ji, Yunhai Ye, Letian Zhu, Xinping Lei, Han Li, Zhiyuan Ma, Zili Wang, Zhaoxiang Zhang, Jiaheng Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CLI-Universe, a three-stage synthesis pipeline that generates high-quality, verifiable terminal-agent training tasks by combining structured capability taxonomy, evidence-guided deep research over real technical materials, and multi-stage executable verification, producing CLI-Universe-6K, a dataset of 6,000 distilled trajectories. - Why it's important: Open-source terminal agents lag far behind proprietary systems on benchmarks like Terminal-Bench 2.0, and the bottleneck is not model capacity but training data quality; existing synthesis pipelines scale task volume without guaranteeing that tasks are genuine, non-trivial, or unambiguously verifiable. - How it does it: Each task candidate is first defined by sampling a four-dimensional taxonomy (domain, skill type, capability, engineering pillar), grounded in specific tools and failure modes via an agentic deep research pass over real repositories and documentation, then instantiated in a Docker environment and subjected to role-separated test and solution agents with rubric-gated test construction, hint-conditional filtering, and fail-to-pass checking. - The good: Fine-tuning Qwen3-32B on only 6,000 CLI-Universe trajectories achieves 33.4% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, outperforming all open-source models at or below 32B parameters and surpassing several open-weight models an order of magnitude larger (e.g., 480B Qwen3-Coder at 23.9%, 1T Kimi-K2-Instruct at 27.8%). - The missing: The dataset contains only 6,000 trajectories across domains defined by human curation, and the quality ceiling of both task synthesis and solution generation is bounded by the capability of the underlying LLM agents, so systematic blind spots in the frontier models propagate into the training set. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on terminal-agent training data, agentic benchmarking, or synthetic data pipelines; the evidence-guided refinement and multi-stage verification design translate directly to other agentic domains beyond CLI.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (62) and Novelty (72) anchor the score — integrating structured capability taxonomy, agentic evidence-guided grounding, and role-separated multi-stage verification is a disciplined synthesis pipeline, and fine-tuning Qwen3-32B on 6k trajectories to 33.4% on TB-2.0 (beating 480B and 1T open-weight models) is a strong empirical demonstration. Rigor (62) is helped by clean component ablations, success-only vs. all-trajectory comparison (+5.2), teacher-model swap, and external validation against TB-2 official tests (91% pass, 88% semantic match), but no error bars and ablations only on a 1k subset. Impact is capped because code and weights are unreleased and synthesis compute cost is never reported, which matters under the Systems profile's 30% Impact weight. Weighted composite: 0.15*72 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*68 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*62 + 0.30*62 = 65.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 68 Resilience 62 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-based terminal agents must execute multi-step workflows in real command-line environments, but open-source models remain substantially weaker than proprietary systems on benchmarks like Terminal-Bench 2.0 (33.4% for the best open-source 32B model vs. 57.8% for Claude-Opus-4.5). The gap is not primarily a capacity problem: as the paper shows, base Qwen3-32B scores only 3.4% on TB-2.0 without targeted training data. Existing synthesis pipelines address the volume problem by converting surface-level artifacts (repository code, Docker traces, documentation templates) into tasks, but produce ambiguous instructions, shallow execution paths, and brittle tests that provide weak learning signal even when they run successfully. The result is that adding more trajectories from these pipelines yields diminishing returns, because failures and non-solutions pollute the supervision signal. Key Idea: The core insight is that task quality, not task volume, is the binding constraint, and that quality can be systematically enforced by (a) generating each task from a structured capability specification so it exercises a specific, identifiable skill, (b) grounding each specification in real technical evidence so the task is realistic rather than abstract, and (c) applying a multi-stage executable filter that independently verifies both the task itself and the solution trajectory, retaining only examples where an unsolved environment demonstrably transitions to a solved state. Methodology: CLI-Universe operates in three stages. Stage 1 (Task Blueprint Construction) seeds task ideation by sampling anchor points across four orthogonal dimensions: Domain (16 categories from Software Engineering to Cryptography), Skill Type (8 categories from Algorithmic to Deployment), Capability (11 reasoning behaviors such as Error Recovery, Long-Horizon Planning, Constraint Satisfaction), and Engineering Pillar (6 activity types such as Debugging, DevOps, Feature Creation). For each anchor, an LLM brainstorms task ideas scored on creativity, technical groundedness, and feasibility; top candidates proceed to evidence-guided refinement, where a dedicated research agent iteratively searches repositories, documentation, issue threads, and usage examples to ground the task in specific tools, realistic constraints, known failure modes, and concrete input/output contracts. Refined candidates are compiled into blueprints (instruction, internal hint, environment checklist) and validated by human and LLM judges using quality rubrics covering clarity, difficulty, and robustness; acceptance rate rises from 72% to 91% (human) and 75% to 93% (LLM) post-rubric review. Stage 2 (Environment Realization) materializes each blueprint into a Docker image: assets are acquired from public sources and adapted (format normalization, fault injection, parameter adjustment) or synthesized from scratch when unavailable; environments undergo smoke tests for dependency installation, service startup, and filesystem layout. Stage 3 (Executable Verification) applies three sequential filters: (i) role-separated test construction, where a test agent and a solution agent operate independently (neither sees the other's output) and the test agent iteratively refines tests against correctness, determinism, and edge-case rubrics; (ii) hint-conditional filtering, where a hint-free agent attempt that succeeds causes the task to be discarded as trivially solvable, retaining only tasks where the hint is genuinely necessary; (iii) fail-to-pass checking, where the synthesized tests must fail on the initial environment and pass after executing the hinted solution trajectory. End-to-end retention is 33.6%: 30% of ideas are rejected at ideation, 14% yield invalid blueprints, 14% fail environment construction, and 8.4% are filtered by fail-to-pass. Training uses multi-turn supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on Kimi-K2.6-generated trajectories with Qwen3 (8B/14B/32B) students, 32 NVIDIA H200 GPUs, AdamW with peak LR 1e-5, 5 epochs, 64K context. Key Results: CLI-Universe-32B achieves 33.4% avg@4 on Terminal-Bench 2.0 under the Terminus 2 scaffold with 200 turns, outperforming SkillSynth-32B (29.6%), Nemotron-Terminal-32B (27.4%), TerminalTraj-32B (22.0%), and the 480B Qwen3-Coder (23.9%) and 1T Kimi-K2-Instruct (27.8%). Gains are monotonically increasing with model size: 8B achieves 10.9 (+8.4 over Qwen3-8B baseline of 2.5), 14B achieves 23.0 (+19.0 over 4.0), 32B achieves 33.4 (+30.0 over 3.4). On out-of-domain benchmarks, CLI-Universe-32B improves over Qwen3-32B by +11.3 on BFCL v4 (58.0 vs 46.7) and +11.6 on VitaBench (27.0 vs 15.4). Per-category breakdown on TB-2.0 shows largest gains in Data Processing (+62.5), Machine Learning (+50.0), Data Querying (+50.0), and Model Training (+43.8); Video Processing and Games show no gain. The proprietary gap remains large: Claude-Opus-4.5 scores 57.8% on TB-2.0. Ablation on a 1k-task subset with Qwen3-32B shows the full pipeline scores 26.7; removing the asset strategy costs 6.2 points (down to 20.5), removing query rubrics costs 3.4 points (down to 23.3), and removing test-case rubrics costs 3.9 points (down to 22.8). Success-only filtering (6k trajectories) outperforms keeping all 10k trajectories including failures and incomplete runs by +5.2 points (33.4 vs 28.2), confirming quality filtering dominates volume. Teacher model comparison: Kimi-K2.6 reaches 33.4 and DeepSeek-V4-Pro reaches 31.2 with matched trajectory counts, indicating the pipeline is not locked to a specific frontier model. Data scaling shows no saturation at 6,000 trajectories on Qwen3-8B. Synthesized tests agree with Terminal-Bench 2 official tests at 91% pass rate (official solutions on CLI-Universe tests) and 88% semantic match (Codex/GPT-5.4 judge). Evidence-guided refinement increases task difficulty by 3.45× in solver turns and reduces pass rate by 13.3 points relative to unrefined tasks. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The genuinely novel element is the combination of structured capability specification with evidence-guided deep research and role-separated multi-stage verification; each component individually has precedents (taxonomy-based generation in SkillSynth and Nemotron, Docker-based environments in TerminalTraj, fail-to-pass checking in code synthesis), but the integration of an agentic evidence-grounding pass that grounds abstract capability anchors in specific real-world technical constraints before environment construction has not been demonstrated in prior terminal-agent synthesis work. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover three pipeline components and two data-side choices (trajectory selection and teacher model), each isolated cleanly. The comparison of synthesized tests against Terminal-Bench 2 official ground truth (91% pass rate, 88% semantic match) provides external validation of test quality. Error attribution uses an independent LLM judge (Codex/GPT-5.4) on two rollout attempts per task. No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported on any TB-2.0 numbers; avg@4 reduces variance but no confidence intervals are given. The CLI-Universe-6K dataset and pipeline code are not released as of the preprint; no repository URL is stated, which limits reproducibility. The ablation is conducted on a 1k-task subset rather than the full 6k, which may not fully represent the pipeline's behavior at scale. The paper is a preprint (arXiv 2606.22883v1, 2026-06-23) and has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — the paper describes a built synthesis pipeline and evaluates fine-tuned models by deployment on Terminal-Bench. - Practical: The fine-tuned models (Qwen3-8B through 32B) run on commercially available hardware; the 32B model was trained on 32 NVIDIA H200 GPUs, which is large but accessible at cloud providers. - Efficient: The synthesis pipeline discards two-thirds of candidates, making generation cost approximately 3× the cost of the retained data; pipeline cost per retained trajectory is not stated, and the compute cost of evidence-guided refinement (multi-agent research loops) is not quantified. - Effective: The models demonstrably improve on TB-2.0 by 30 points at 32B scale, outperform 10-100× larger open-source baselines, and transfer to two out-of-domain benchmarks; the evidence for effectiveness is strong within the tested scope. - Scalable: The data scaling curve for Qwen3-8B shows no saturation at 6,000 trajectories, suggesting the pipeline can continue generating useful supervision; whether the pipeline itself scales to 100k+ trajectories without degradation in task quality or diversity is not tested or analyzed. The paper delivers the CLI-Universe-6K dataset as an artifact; the pipeline code and fine-tuned model weights are not released in this preprint. Strengths: - The fail-to-pass + hint-conditional filtering combination is a principled approach to measuring task non-triviality and solution validity simultaneously, avoiding the common failure mode of keeping easy tasks that provide no learning signal. - The evidence-guided refinement step is empirically validated to increase genuine difficulty (3.45× solver turns, -13.3 pass rate), distinguishing it from taxonomy expansion that merely broadens surface topic coverage without raising task quality. - The role-separated test and solution agents (neither sees the other's output) directly address the test-contamination problem in self-supervised pipeline design, where the solution agent could otherwise write tests that trivially pass its own approach. - The error attribution study on TB-2.0 failure modes (9-mode taxonomy across Execution, Coherence, Verification) provides actionable diagnostic information: CLI-Universe-32B's failure profile shifts from Verification-dominated (frontier models: 47-60%) to Execution-dominated (44%), with step repetition at 23% vs 0-7% for frontiers, identifying the specific capability gap that remains. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note three limitations. First, all pipeline stages — ideation, environment construction, solution generation, and test construction — rely on LLM agents, so the quality ceiling of the synthesized data is bounded by the capability of the underlying frontier models. Second, CLI-Universe narrows but does not close the gap to proprietary systems (33.4% vs 57.8% for Claude-Opus-4.5 on TB-2.0), and whether larger open-source base models or reinforcement learning can close this gap is unexplored. Third, the current dataset is only 6,000 trajectories, and scaling the pipeline to larger and more diverse task pools may unlock further gains but is left to future work. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Compute cost of the synthesis pipeline is never reported. Given that evidence-guided refinement involves multi-agent research loops and two-thirds of candidates are discarded, the cost per retained trajectory could be substantial relative to simply sampling more trajectories from a teacher model; this matters for reproducibility and practical adoption. - The ablation is conducted on a 1k-task subset and reports TB-2.0 scores on Qwen3-32B only; it is unclear whether the relative importance of pipeline components holds at 8B and 14B scales or at the full 6k trajectory count. - No baseline compares CLI-Universe-6K against a matched-volume sample of trajectories from Kimi-K2.6 on pre-existing high-quality task sets (e.g., hand-curated Terminal-Bench 1 tasks), so it is not possible to isolate how much of the gain comes from the synthesis pipeline versus the teacher model and SFT recipe. - The Video Processing and Games categories show zero gain at 32B, but the paper does not analyze why; if the taxonomy does not produce diverse-enough tasks in these categories, the failure is a systematic gap in coverage rather than a model limitation. - Human evaluation of blueprint quality uses accept/reject rubrics but not calibration on inter-annotator agreement, so the 91% human accept rate carries unknown variance. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or training terminal agents gains immediately actionable methodology from this work: the evidence-guided grounding + role-separated verification design is the most disciplined synthesis pipeline yet demonstrated for this domain, and the 30-point gain at 32B on only 6,000 trajectories makes a strong case that task quality dominates task volume; the lack of released code or weights is the main obstacle to direct adoption.
Recommended 2606.22883 PDF
#56 — Innovative (65)

Are We Ready For An Agent-Native Memory System? (89 ▲ )

Wei Zhou, Xuanhe Zhou, Shaokun Han, Hongming Xu, Guoliang Li, Zhiyu Li, Feiyu Xiong, Fan Wu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents a systematic experimental evaluation of 12 representative agent memory systems across five benchmark workloads (11 datasets) using a four-module decomposition framework that isolates the contributions of memory representation/storage, extraction, retrieval/routing, and maintenance. - Why it's important: Practitioners choosing among streaming-log, knowledge-graph, hierarchical, and hybrid memory architectures have no principled empirical basis for selection; prior benchmarks treat memory as a black box and report only end-to-end task scores, hiding which design decisions actually matter. - How it does it: The paper formalizes agent memory as a tuple M_sys = (R, S, Q, U), uses unified time-overhead traces to fairly compare 12 systems plus 2 baselines, then generates controlled single-module ablation variants to causally isolate each component's contribution. - The good: The backbone robustness ablation across four LLMs reveals that stronger models improve answer expression but not evidence localization, cleanly separating where memory architecture versus model capability is the binding constraint. - The missing: All five benchmarks are offline curated datasets; no evaluation in live tool-calling, streaming, or multi-agent deployment settings is provided, leaving the real-world latency and correctness trade-offs unverified. - Read it or not: Engineers selecting or designing agent memory infrastructure should read this; it is the most systematic comparative study available and its 11 actionable findings directly map to concrete design choices.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (systematic evaluation study, no new system). Rigor (60) and Completeness (65) pull the composite down. The four-module (R,S,Q,U) decomposition organizes existing descriptions rather than introducing a new abstraction, so Novelty (65) is moderate; the genuine contribution is controlled single-module ablation at scale. No significance tests anywhere despite many sub-12 absolute scores where noise could explain findings, an undisclosed author-affiliation conflict (MemOS, A-MEM), offline-only benchmarks, and no formal limitations section all reduce Rigor and Completeness. Practical Impact (72) is the strongest dimension — the released testbed plus actionable workload-to-architecture mapping and the conservative-maintenance finding are directly useful. Weighted: 0.20*65+0.25*60+0.15*70+0.15*65+0.15*62+0.10*72 = 65.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM agents depend on persistent memory to maintain stateful context across multi-turn interactions, tool executions, and long sessions that exceed context-window capacity. The dominant evaluation methodology treats the entire memory stack as a monolithic black box and measures only end-to-end task success (F1, BLEU, Exact Match), which cannot explain why a system succeeds or fails, which internal component is the bottleneck, or what operational costs arise at deployment. Four architectural families have emerged in the literature: stream-and-reflection systems (e.g., MemoryBank), hierarchical tiered systems (e.g., MemGPT/Letta), knowledge-graph systems (e.g., Mem0, Zep), and composite hybrid systems (e.g., A-MEM, MemOS). Prior benchmarks (LoCoMo, LongMemEval) evaluate only a handful of systems, use single task types, and omit systems-level concerns such as index construction time and update robustness. No study has conducted a controlled, multi-dimensional comparison from a data management perspective. Key Idea The core technical move is to treat agent memory as a data management system rather than an agent component, formalizing it as M_sys = (R, S, Q, U) — representation and storage, extraction, retrieval and routing, and maintenance — and then generating controlled ablation variants that modify exactly one module while holding the rest constant. This decomposition converts an opaque system-level comparison into a set of isolable causal experiments, enabling the paper to answer not just "which system wins?" but "which design decision drives the difference and under what workload?" Methodology The evaluation covers 12 memory systems (MemoChat, Mem0, MEM1, MemAgent, MemTree, Zep, Mem0-graph, Cognee, LightMem, SimpleMem, MemOS, MemoryOS, A-MEM, Letta) and 2 reference baselines (Long Context, Embedding RAG). All systems run under unified time-overhead traces. Five research questions structure the evaluation: RQ1 (Overall Effectiveness) uses three benchmark families: LoCoMo (conversational QA, long-horizon episodic reasoning), LongMemEval/MemoryAgentBench (multi-session factual retrieval, LLM Judge Accuracy, ROUGE-L), and DB-Bench/LifelongAgentBench (procedural execution, Exact Match, Task Success Rate). RQ2 (Retrieval Fidelity) uses LoCoMo with source-level gold evidence annotations, reporting Recall@k and Recall@10 over six evidence-distance bins (1-5 to 26-31 session turns). RQ3 (Update Robustness) compares 11 systems on Knowledge Update and Temporal Reasoning slices from LongMemEval, plus a backbone ablation across Qwen3-8B, DeepSeekChat, GPT-5.4-mini, and GPT-5.4. RQ4 (Long-Horizon Stability) tests LongBench (context-length difficulty), LongMemEval (session count bins), and LoCoMo (evidence-distance drift). RQ5 (Operational Cost) measures Avg. Operation Latency/Query and Normalized Utility for 8 systems. Section 5 conducts fine-grained ablations with controlled module variants: M1 ablates representation via LightMem (User-Only Raw vs. Summary vs. Compressed) and MemTree (Flat vs. Deeper Tree); M2 ablates extraction via MemoChat (Heuristic Topic vs. LLM Topic) and MemOS (Fast vs. Fine Memorize); M3 ablates retrieval via A-MEM (Hybrid-Balanced vs. Hybrid Sparse-Leaning) and SimpleMem (No Planning vs. Planning Only vs. Planning + Reflect); M4 ablates maintenance via MemoChat (default vs. Topic1) and MemoryOS (default vs. Delayed-Flush vs. Conservative-Merge). No new loss function or training procedure is introduced; all systems use their published configurations. Key Results RQ1: No single architecture dominates. On LongMemEval, Zep reaches 48.0 LLM Judge Accuracy; on LoCoMo, MemOS reaches 11.5 Exact Match (vs. Embedding RAG at 1.6); on DB-Bench, Long Context achieves 48.2 EM and MemoChat achieves 55.4 Task Success Rate. Methods with full workload coverage (MemoryOS, MemOS) remain closest to the Pareto frontier overall. RQ2: SimpleMem achieves the highest Recall@1 (39.0), but A-MEM and MemTree dominate at Recall@5/10 (59.7/80.5 and 85.9/80.5 respectively). Embedding RAG drops sharply beyond the shortest evidence-distance bin; graph and hierarchical systems maintain stable recall across bins up to 26-31 session turns. RQ3: Zep leads Knowledge Update (44.4 Substring EM, 36.8 ROUGE-L F1); Cognee leads Temporal Reasoning (18.7 Substring EM, 35.8 ROUGE-L F1). MemOS maintains the highest LoCoMo Exact Match (8.9) across all backbone variants. Backbone strength shifts absolute scores more than it changes system rankings: MemOS Answer F1 across the four backbones spans 32.2, 41.2, 38.6, 41.2 — consistent ordering with modest variance. RQ4: On LongBench, SimpleMem accuracy changes from 35.2 (Short) to 34.9 (Long) while Long Context drops from 42.6 to 19.0. On LoCoMo, Embedding RAG Answer F1 falls from 37.1 to 7.4 as evidence distance grows, while graph-organized systems (MemOS, Cognee, MemoryOS) remain substantially higher across all bins. RQ5: LightMem achieves 48.3 Normalized Utility at 3.67 s/query. MemTree achieves 63.5 at 15.9 s. MemoryOS achieves 82.0 at 28.6 s. Cognee and Zep exceed 84 utility only after 116.5 s and 155.1 s respectively, an order-of-magnitude latency difference for modest incremental utility. Ablations: M1 shows LightMem User-Only Raw outperforms User-Only Summary on all four metrics (LoCoMo EM 24.2 vs. 8.5, Ans F1 38.9 vs. 15.6; LongMemEval Substr EM 26.0 vs. 11.7, ROUGE-L F1 31.4 vs. 17.4). M2 shows MemoChat Heuristic Topic > LLM Topic (Substr EM 23.0 vs. 7.3). M3 shows SimpleMem Planning Only achieves 90.6 Strict Recall vs. 86.4 for No Planning; Planning + Reflect does not improve further (88.6). M4 shows MemoryOS Conservative-Merge improves Ans F1 from 23.2 to 23.5 and Substr EM from 22.4 to 22.8 over default, while Delayed-Flush lowers both to 20.6/19.5. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (evaluation framework). The four-module formalization (R, S, Q, U) organizes existing descriptions from the literature rather than introducing a technically new abstraction. The genuinely novel contribution is the controlled single-module ablation methodology applied at scale across 12 systems and 5 workloads, which no prior benchmark achieves. The 11 empirical findings — particularly that workload alignment rather than architecture class determines performance, and that conservative consolidation outperforms aggressive summarization — are new empirical results, not restatements of prior claims. Rigor & Reproducibility This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.24775v1, 23 Jun 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Code and the evaluation framework are publicly released at https://github.com/OpenDataBox/MemoryData. Error bars and significance tests are absent throughout; given that many absolute score differences are small (e.g., MemOS 8.9 vs. MemoryOS 3.2 Exact Match on LoCoMo, where all systems score below 12), noise could plausibly explain some findings. The backbone robustness ablation (4 LLMs × 6 systems) is methodologically sound. A potential conflict of interest exists: multiple evaluated systems (MemOS, A-MEM) and several cited works originate from the authors' affiliated institutions (Shanghai Jiao Tong University, MemTensor Shanghai Technology Co.), but this is not disclosed. The evaluation uses publicly available benchmarks, which mitigates Goodhart's Law risk. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (systematic evaluation study; no new system is trained or deployed). - Practical: The evaluation framework runs on commercially available hardware using published system configurations; the released testbed is usable by practitioners. - Efficient: The paper explicitly quantifies the cost spectrum: LightMem at 3.67 s/query sits at the efficient frontier; Cognee and Zep require 116-155 s/query for incremental utility gains; operational cost is governed by maintenance scope (localized vs. global rewrite) rather than architecture class alone. - Effective: The evidence shows that effectiveness is workload-specific, not universal; the paper provides concrete workload-to-architecture mapping (graph for temporal relational, hierarchical for long-context, hybrid-filtered for exact-state grounding), which is actionable. - Scalable: All evaluations use standard benchmark sizes (LoCoMo, LongMemEval, DB-Bench); no stress-scale testing at 10x-100x session volume or memory size is performed, and the paper does not test scalability claims empirically. The paper releases the evaluation framework and testbed; it does not release a new memory system, trained model, or dataset beyond the benchmark harness. Strengths - The four-module decomposition enables the first controlled, fair ablation study that isolates individual component contributions; prior survey-style papers catalog systems but cannot assign credit to specific design choices. - The unified time-overhead trace methodology levels the evaluation playing field across 12 architecturally heterogeneous systems that otherwise differ in infrastructure and setup assumptions. - The backbone robustness ablation cleanly separates memory-architecture quality from LLM backbone quality, establishing that well-structured memory (Zep, MemOS) preserves its ranking advantage regardless of backbone, while weaker-organized memory benefits more from a stronger LLM — a non-obvious and practically important finding. - The operational cost analysis (RQ5) introduces Normalized Utility as a principled metric combining accuracy and latency, providing the first empirical cost-utility frontier for agent memory selection. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper has no formal limitations section. The conclusion describes the work as a "comprehensive review" and notes the testbed will be released, but does not acknowledge evaluation gaps, benchmark coverage limits, or the absence of real-deployment testing. Authors do not address the potential bias from evaluating systems affiliated with their own institutions. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All five benchmarks are offline, curated datasets with known gold answers; no evaluation in live multi-agent, streaming, or production tool-calling settings is provided, leaving real-world latency and correctness behavior unverified. - No statistical significance testing is reported anywhere; given small absolute differences between systems on some benchmarks (e.g., LoCoMo EM scores uniformly below 12 for most systems), several findings may not be robust to noise. - Potential author-affiliation bias: MemOS, A-MEM, and several reference systems originate from SJTU or MemTensor, which are the authors' institutions, but the paper does not acknowledge or control for this. - The LLM backbone ablation tests only 6 systems × 4 backbones on a single benchmark (LoCoMo); the interaction between backbone and architecture type is not factorial, so conclusions about backbone robustness are partially confounded. - Operational cost analysis omits memory/storage footprint, which is critical for edge and mobile deployment where RAM is the binding constraint, not latency. - The study captures a snapshot of 12 systems as of mid-2026; the preprint does not acknowledge that the benchmark and system set may already be dated relative to faster-moving open-source development in this area. Bottom Line Any practitioner selecting, designing, or benchmarking agent memory infrastructure will gain directly actionable findings here that are unavailable elsewhere — specifically the workload-to-architecture mapping, the operational scaling rule, and the evidence that conservative maintenance outperforms aggressive summarization — making this the most useful empirical reference in the space despite its lack of statistical rigor and real-deployment validation.
Recommended 2606.24775 PDF
#57 — Innovative (65)

Software Delegation Contracts: Measuring Reviewability in AI Coding- Agent Work

V Schmalbach · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a controlled pilot study and reusable experimental harness that measures how explicit software delegation contracts affect the reviewability of AI coding-agent work packages across 64 agent runs and 192 blinded model-based reviews. - Why it's important: As AI coding agents take on delegated software tasks, human reviewers need sufficient evidence to accept or reject the returned work; this paper shows that agents almost never volunteer that evidence spontaneously, so harness builders who do not ask explicitly will not receive it. - How it does it: It compares three prompt conditions (bare issue prompt vs. explicit delegation contract vs. contract plus mandatory evidence-bundle template) on a seeded TypeScript task repository using both model-free mechanical scoring (hidden acceptance tests, mutation checks) and three-reviewer blinded rubric scoring per run. - The good: Evidence sufficiency improved in 22 of 30 paired comparisons and never worsened (+0.83 on a 5-point scale, p < 0.0001, Cliff's delta = 0.66), and residual-risk and reviewer-checklist sections appeared at exactly 0% baseline vs. 100% with the evidence bundle. - The missing: All 192 reviews were produced by Sonnet 4.6 model-based reviewers from the same Claude family as the agents under test, creating an in-family bias the paper acknowledges but cannot resolve; no human reviewer replication is included. - Read it or not: Practitioners building AI coding-agent harnesses who want quantified, immediately actionable defaults for work-package structure should read this; researchers wanting human-validated reviewability evidence or results beyond small well-specified tasks should treat it as a pilot pending a follow-up study.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The correctness/reviewability dissociation and the 0%-spontaneous vs 100%-on-demand demand-elasticity result are genuinely novel (Novelty 65, Non-Obv 62) and operationally precise. Completeness (70) is a positive: the model-free mechanical scoring layer is independent of reviewer validity, and the authors confront five validity threats directly. Resilience (58) is the limiting dimension: all 192 reviews come from the same Claude family as the agents under test, the study was designed/executed/analyzed by a Claude-family model, and the C-arm is a 4-run preview, so every magnitude is a lower bound pending human replication. Rigor (68) is solid for a pilot but bounded by single 600-line repo, 10 tasks, and no inter-reviewer agreement statistics. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*68 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*60 = 65.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 70 Resilience 58 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation When humans delegate software tasks to AI coding agents, the binding question for the reviewer is no longer only whether the patch works but whether it can be reviewed: was the scope respected, what evidence supports the fix, and what residual risks remain? Prior agent-evaluation work such as SWE-bench and SWE-agent measures issue-resolution rates; it does not measure whether returned work packages give reviewers sufficient evidence to accept or reject the work with confidence. In practice, an agent's work package is assembled from whatever the agent volunteers in its final reply, which is typically a diff and perhaps a test-run summary. Schmalbach's prior conceptual work (reference [4]) proposed the software delegation contract as the right unit of analysis, defined as the tuple (task T, authority A, work package W, acceptance context C), but produced definitions and a coding scheme rather than measurements. The gap this paper closes is: does making that contract explicit reliably change what reviewers receive, and at what token and latency cost? Key Idea Evidence is demand-elastic with respect to the contract, not the task. Agents comply nearly perfectly with explicit evidence requirements but never anticipate them unprompted: no agent in 60 A/B runs spontaneously wrote a residual-risk section or a reviewer checklist, while 100% produced both when the evidence bundle required them. The operationally important insight is that reviewability is a contract-controlled property of the work package, not an emergent property of agent capability, so harness builders must ask for evidence explicitly or accept that it will not appear. Methodology The authors built a ~600-line dependency-free TypeScript HTTP API (toy-ts-api) with users/notes routes, HMAC-token authentication, and a 29-test suite. Starting from a clean base commit, they created ten seeded task branches across five families: failing-test fix (T01, T02), validation bug (T03, T04), missing tests (T05, T06), scoped refactor (T07, T08), and docs update (T09, T10). Each branch introduced a realistic regression committed with a plausible message. Each task record stored the seed commit, allowlisted files, frozen paths, the expected test command, and private ground-truth notes. Three experimental conditions were applied: (A) a realistic issue-style baseline prompt written as a competent colleague would write it, deliberately including natural scope hints to avoid a straw-man comparison; (B) an explicit delegation contract structured as {Objective, Non-goals, Authority (allowed paths, allowed commands, forbidden actions), Expected tests, Required evidence, Acceptance criteria}, with task content informationally equivalent to A; and (C) condition B plus a mandatory work-package template with seven fixed sections (Summary, Changed files with reasons, Commands run, Tests and results, Known limitations, Residual risks, Reviewer checklist). Condition C ran as a four-run preview only (T01 and T03 on both model tiers). Agent runs used Claude Code on two model tiers: Sonnet 4.6 (stronger) and Haiku 4.5 (weaker). Each agent received a fresh single-branch clone with a fixed harness preamble restricting it to no commits, no package installation, and no network access. The full grid was 10 tasks x {A, B} x {Sonnet, Haiku} (replicate 1) + 10 tasks x {A, B} x Sonnet (replicate 2) + 4 C-previews = 64 runs and 30 matched A/B pairs. Mechanical scoring was model-free: changed files vs. seed commit, forbidden-action flags, hidden acceptance tests (T01-T04, T07, T08), mutation checks (T05, T06), and static checks (T09, T10). Blinded review used three independent Sonnet 4.6 reviewer instances per run, each seeing the neutral issue-form task description, the diff, the contributor's report (with revealing paths redacted), and the visible CI result, but never the condition label. Reviewers scored six dimensions on a fixed rubric: scope compliance (1-5), evidence sufficiency (1-5), reviewer confidence (1-5), ambiguity (1-5, lower better), repair required (binary), and a four-level decision. Per-run scores are the median of three reviewers. Primary analysis uses Wilcoxon signed-rank on the 30 A/B pairs with Cliff's delta; secondary analysis uses 90 judge-level paired scores per metric. Critical design decisions: (1) using deliberately good baseline prompts makes the evidence-sufficiency improvement more conservative and credible; (2) the model-free mechanical layer gives correctness measurements reliability independent of reviewer validity; (3) keeping tasks small and well-specified creates a triviality ceiling for correctness but cleanly isolates the reviewability question. Key Results On objective outcomes, all 64 runs passed hidden acceptance checks and zero scope violations occurred in either condition; both conditions were objectively correct, confirming that on small well-specified tasks capable models do not need a contract to succeed. On reviewability: evidence sufficiency rose from 3.90 to 4.73 (+0.83, 22 of 30 pairs improved, 0 worsened, Wilcoxon p < 0.0001, Cliff's delta = 0.66); reviewer ambiguity fell from 1.30 to 1.07 (9 pairs improved, 2 worsened, p = 0.035, judge-level p = 0.0001); reviewer confidence rose +0.20 (p = 0.058, not significant). Scope compliance moved slightly against contracts (-0.13, p = 0.046) because contracts elicited more thorough output that reviewers occasionally read as over-delivery relative to the minimal ask. Work-package element frequencies: changed files with reasons: 7% (baseline), 93% (contract), 100% (evidence bundle); commands run: 7%, 70%, 100%; known limitations: 0%, 80%, 100%; residual risks: 0%, 0%, 100%; reviewer checklist: 0%, 0%, 100%. Mean report length grew from 769 to 1,661 to 2,716 characters. Cost: +13.0% agent tokens (20,087 to 22,690, p = 0.0002), +38.3% wall-clock time (32.7s to 45.2s, p < 0.0001), +23.3% tool invocations (7.9 to 9.7, p = 0.0012), +44.7% patch size in bytes. The contract effect was roughly twice as large for Haiku (evidence +1.00, ambiguity -0.50, confidence +0.40) versus Sonnet (evidence +0.75, ambiguity -0.10, confidence +0.10). The four-run C-arm scored evidence sufficiency 5.0 and ambiguity 1.0 on all four runs vs. 4.0/1.25 for matched baseline cells. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The empirical dissociation between correctness (saturated at 100% in both conditions) and reviewability (reliably improved by contracts) on small coding tasks is a genuinely novel finding; no prior work had measured this with controlled paired experiments and model-free mechanical ground truth. The reusable experimental harness (seeded task repository, paired prompts, hidden acceptance tests, mutation checks, blinded review pipeline) is a new tool for this class of study. The delegation-contract concept itself is imported from prior conceptual work by the same author; the contribution here is the first measurement, not the first formulation. Rigor & Reproducibility The mechanical scoring layer is model-free and produces the most reliable measurements in the paper; the hidden tests, mutation checks, and scope-analysis scripts are preserved verbatim in the companion evidence artifact. Three independent blinded reviewers per run with median aggregation is a reasonable robustness strategy, and the paper demonstrates in Section 4.6 that one cross-batch mis-attribution error was absorbed by the median. No inter-reviewer agreement statistics beyond the paired test results are reported. No error bars or confidence intervals appear for the cost metrics. The C-arm ran on four runs, which the paper explicitly flags as insufficient to estimate its effect; the near-perfect C-arm scores are described as encouraging but not load-bearing. The most critical methodological concern is in-family bias: the coding agents (Sonnet 4.6, Haiku 4.5), the blinded reviewers (Sonnet 4.6), and the orchestrating research assistant that generated the task environment, ran all conditions, computed statistics, and drafted the manuscript are all deployments of the Claude model family. The paired design removes constant bias between conditions but does not eliminate family-specific stylistic interactions. No human-reviewer replication is included. The companion evidence artifact preserves all prompts, patches, work packages, reviews, and analysis scripts; the author's website is listed but no DOI or permanent identifier is given in the paper. Deployability EMPIRICAL profile. Practical: the harness runs on standard hardware using the Claude Code CLI with no specialized infrastructure. Efficient: contract overhead is +13% tokens and +38% wall-clock on a ~600-line repository with tasks completing in 33-45 seconds baseline; overhead at larger codebase scales or longer tasks is not measured. Effective: blinded reviewer scores on evidence sufficiency improve reliably and the 0%-to-100% demand-elasticity result for residual-risk and reviewer-checklist sections is directly interpretable, but whether higher evidence-sufficiency scores translate to better human accept/reject decisions is not measured. Scalable: tested on a single ~600-line repository with 10 small tasks; the paper explicitly restricts its conclusions to "small, well-specified tasks" and does not claim or test behavior at larger scales or on tasks where baseline runs fail. The paper delivers a companion evidence artifact (harness, prompts, patches, reviews, scripts) and a reusable design specification, stopping short of a packaged library or installable CLI. Strengths - The dissociation between objective correctness (ceiling at 100% in both conditions) and reviewability (reliably improved under contracts) is a clean, falsifiable empirical result that directly operationalizes the claim that contracts function as a control-and-review mechanism rather than a correctness mechanism. - The model-free mechanical layer (hidden acceptance tests, mutation checks) gives correctness measurements high reliability independent of the LLM-reviewer validity concern, separating the two measurement tracks cleanly. - The demand-elasticity finding (0% spontaneous vs. 100% on-demand for residual-risk and reviewer-checklist sections) is operationally precise: it tells harness builders exactly which work-package fields agents will and will not volunteer, enabling targeted contract design. - The model-tier stratification (Haiku benefit approximately 2x Sonnet benefit) provides a concrete actionable signal that contracts matter most precisely when delegating to cheaper or weaker executors. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors confront five validity threats directly in Section 6: (1) all 192 reviews are from LLM-based reviewers in the same Claude family as the agents, creating in-family bias that paired design mitigates but does not eliminate; (2) full blinding of report structure is structurally impossible when report structure is the treatment; (3) task triviality means authority-control effects (H3) and correctness effects remain untested; (4) results generalize only to a single ~600-line TypeScript repository and two related model families; (5) the C-arm ran as a four-run preview insufficient to estimate its effect. Section 9 additionally discloses that the study was designed, executed, analyzed, and written by a Claude-family model under the author's prior written design. The authors identify human-reviewer replication on a subset as the most important follow-up. This is a credibility-positive level of transparency for a pilot study. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The paired design removes constant reviewer bias between conditions but does not rule out interaction effects: Sonnet 4.6 reviewers scoring Sonnet 4.6 agent outputs in condition A vs. B may share model-version-specific stylistic preferences in ways that differ from how human reviewers would respond to the same structural changes. The paper cannot rule this out and the magnitude of any such interaction is unknown. - The paper measures no human decision quality outcome: whether higher reviewer confidence or lower ambiguity scores actually improve human accept/reject accuracy or reduce review time is entirely unmeasured. The claim that reviewability matters (citing Bansal et al. 2019 on team value depending on verification cost) is taken as given, not itself empirically tested. - Ten tasks in one repository with five task families is a narrow sample; task-family effects vary widely (docs-update evidence +1.33, duplication-refactor ambiguity +0.67 but confidence -0.67), suggesting the headline aggregate may be dominated by task families where baseline prompts leave the most unreported, and a balanced larger study could yield materially different effect sizes. - No ablation isolates which contract elements drive the evidence-sufficiency improvement: the full structured contract is the intervention, and the paper cannot say whether the Non-goals clause, the Authority boundary, or the Required evidence section is doing the most work. This matters for minimal-overhead harness design. - The entire study was not only reviewed but designed, executed, and statistically analyzed by a Claude-family model. While the mechanical metrics are model-free, the study design choices themselves (which tasks to seed, which metrics to report, which statistical tests to apply) were generated by the model family under evaluation. Independent human replication from scratch, not just human replication of the review scoring, is the missing validation. Bottom Line A practitioner building an AI coding-agent harness who wants evidence-backed defaults for work-package structure should read this paper; the demand-elasticity result, the correctness/reviewability dissociation, and the Haiku/Sonnet stratification are directly actionable, but every reviewability magnitude should be treated as a lower bound on what an independent human-reviewer replication might or might not confirm.
Search Results 2606.17099 PDF
#58 — Innovative (65)

Distilling Examples into Task Instructions: Enhanced In-Context Learning for Real-World B2B Conversations (2 ▲ )

Guy Rotman, Adi Kopilov, Danit Berger Zalmanson, Omri Allouche · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces knowledge-extraction methods (Criteria-Ex, Criteria-De, Description-Ex, Description-Cr) that distill labeled few-shot examples into compact structured criteria lists or prose task descriptions, replacing raw example concatenation in ICL prompts for B2B sales conversation classification. - Why it's important: Standard ICL degrades from 71.5% to 60.7% macro-F1 as example count grows from 0 to 100 shots, making it unreliable for high-stakes B2B applications where transparency and efficiency matter — the paper provides a direct fix. - How it does it: A single LLM call analyzes a sample of labeled examples and produces either a positive/negative criteria list or a prose task description that replaces the examples in the classification prompt, capping token usage at roughly 200–600 tokens regardless of example count. - The good: Description-Ex holds stable at 71.5%–72.6% macro-F1 from 0 to 100 shots while the Examples baseline drops 10.8 points over the same range, and the advantage is consistent across all five tested LLMs and all five classification tasks. - The missing: The entire evaluation is conducted on a single proprietary dataset of 50 calls from one company's platform (Gong.io); no external B2B corpus validates whether the extracted criteria generalize across organizations or industries. - Read it or not: Worth reading for NLP practitioners building LLM-based classification systems in specialized low-resource domains where context-length degradation is a real operational problem; less compelling for researchers seeking fundamental ICL theory.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Completeness (72) and Rigor (66) anchor an Innovative score: thorough ablations across 5 LLMs, 5 tasks, and 6 shot counts; public code plus a new Call Playbook dataset; peer-reviewed acceptance at Findings of ACL 2026. The core finding (ICL degrading 10.8 macro-F1 points from 0 to 100 shots, fixed by distilling examples into compact criteria/descriptions) is practically useful and the distillation-to-instructions pipeline is a real new method distinct from token-level compression. Resilience (64) and Rigor are capped by the single proprietary 50-call Gong.io corpus (training, test, and labels co-originate), thin 200-instance test sets where 1 F1 point is ~2 errors, no significance testing on the 1-3 point margins that decide method rankings, and missing inter-annotator agreement. Novelty (60) is moderate. A method-level integration, not a new formulation. Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*66 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*64 + 0.10*66 = 65.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 66 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 72 Resilience 64 Impact 66
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Extracting actionable intelligence from B2B sales calls — classifying conversation segments into sales-relevant categories such as Business Goals, Decision Criteria, Decision Makers, Decision Making Process, and Pain Points — is essential for automating deal strategy at scale. This problem is structurally hard for ICL: each call snippet averages 270–280 words, so concatenating 100 labeled examples pushes prompt length to roughly 25K tokens. Prior work and the authors' own measurements confirm that long-context model performance degrades severely under these conditions (Li et al., 2024; Lee et al., 2025). Token-level compression methods (LLMLingua-2, Selective Context) nominally address this but fail because they prune tokens within examples rather than changing representational form, yielding fragmented, incoherent residuals. The gap this paper closes is between ICL's theoretical suitability for low-resource specialized domains and its practical failure under real B2B prompt lengths. Key Idea The core insight is that few-shot examples are raw material for knowledge extraction, not direct inference signals. Instead of asking a classifier LLM to implicitly pattern-match across concatenated verbose examples, a first LLM pass distills those examples into explicit, token-compact, task-specific rules (structured criteria lists) or task narratives (prose descriptions). These derived representations replace the examples entirely, so prompt length becomes O(1) with respect to example count rather than O(N), while exposing classification logic in human-readable form that domain experts can directly refine. Methodology The framework consists of four steps, shared across all methods. Step 1: randomly sample N labeled examples from the training set, preserving class balance. Step 2: run a one-time knowledge extraction LLM call that consumes those examples and the user's high-level intent to produce one of four knowledge representations: (a) Criteria-Ex — a list of 5–10 positive conditions indicating concept presence and 5–10 negative conditions indicating absence, derived directly from examples; (b) Criteria-De — same criteria format, derived from a Description-Ex output rather than examples directly; (c) Description-Ex — a prose task description derived from examples; (d) Description-Cr — a prose description derived from Criteria-Ex output. A fifth method, Summary-Ex, compresses each example to 3–5 sentences without changing representational form, serving as an extractive intermediate baseline. Step 3: substitute extracted knowledge (or compressed examples) into a fixed classification prompt template that includes the user intent and a chain-of-thought rationale request. Step 4: query the LLM for a binary classification label. All classification prompts share the same template structure; variation comes only from the knowledge section. Three critical design decisions: (1) using the same LLM for extraction and classification — justified for consistency but introduces potential circularity when the same model's generation style informs its own classification; (2) requiring each generated criterion to be grounded in at least two provided examples — justified to reduce hallucination, and qualitatively validated in Appendix H; (3) placing positive examples/criteria before negative ones in all prompts — justified by early ablations showing superior ordering effects. Evaluation covers 5 LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet 3.7, Claude Haiku 3, Mistral Large, Mistral Small), 5 shot counts (0, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100), 5 binary B2B classification tasks, and 5 random repetitions per configuration, reporting macro-averaged F1. Temperature is set to 0 for all models. Key Results The Examples baseline degrades from 71.5% macro-F1 at 0-shot to 60.7% at 100-shot, averaged across all models and tasks — a 10.8 percentage point drop. Summary-Ex also degrades, to 64.7% at 100 shots, showing that within-example compression alone does not resolve context-length sensitivity. Description-Ex holds at 71.5%–72.6% across the full 0–100-shot range. Criteria-De achieves the highest area-under-the-curve averaged over shot counts at 76.3%, followed by Criteria-Ex (76.2%), Description-Cr (75.9%), Description-Ex (75.6%), all well above Summary-Ex (72.0%) and Examples (69.3%). Against token-level compression baselines at a fixed 50% compression rate: LLMLingua-2 falls from 71.4% to 62.1% (a 9.3-point drop), and SC (Selective Context) declines from 71.4% to 62.1% as well. Both stay below 50% throughout, confirming that extractive token pruning fails the scaling test. Claude Sonnet 3.7 is the best single model overall at 77% macro-F1 peaking at 25 shots. The Decision Makers task reaches 80% F1 across methods, while Pain Points spreads from 55% to 75% depending on method (description-based methods decisively outperform on this abstract concept). Token costs: Examples scale linearly to 25K tokens at 100 shots; criteria methods stabilize under 600 tokens; description methods under 200 tokens. Inference time reductions at 100 shots: 70% (criteria) and 57% (description) versus Examples. Cross-model distillation: criteria generated by Sonnet 3.7 or Mistral Large improve Haiku 3 and Mistral Small respectively by 1–5 F1 points. Descriptions generated by larger models hurt smaller models, suggesting structured criteria transfer more reliably across model families than prose descriptions. Human-in-the-loop: 4/5 annotators improved Description-Ex (gains up to 2.65%); 3/5 improved Criteria-Ex (gains up to 2%). One annotator who paraphrased without semantic generalization reduced Criteria-Ex by 1.3 points, confirming that interpretability enables — but does not guarantee — useful human refinement. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The knowledge distillation pipeline converting examples into explicit task instructions for ICL is genuinely distinct from token-level compression (which stays in example-space), prompt optimization (which restructures example presentation), and fine-tuning (which requires parameter updates). The iterative variants (Criteria-De, Description-Cr) that chain two extraction passes are incremental extensions of the same idea. The Call Playbook dataset — 50 real B2B calls annotated for five sales concepts with expert supervision — is a new public benchmark. Individual components (few-shot ICL, chain-of-thought, LLM-as-extractor) are standard; the combination into an example-to-instruction distillation pipeline for this domain is the contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Each of the 6 shot-count configurations is repeated 5 times with different random samples, and averaged metrics are reported; however, no confidence intervals or significance tests appear in the paper, leaving unclear whether 1–3 point F1 differences between methods are statistically reliable. Ablations across methods, models, and tasks are thorough. Code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/gong-io/call-playbook. Inter-annotator agreement on the original dataset labels is not reported, which is a meaningful omission given the subjectivity of B2B concept detection. The qualitative analysis of generated criteria (Appendix H, 3,000+ criteria examined) is descriptive rather than independently validated. Self-reported numbers from a dataset the authors constructed and evaluated at their own company warrant some skepticism — the gold labels and the test distribution are both proprietary artifacts from Gong.io. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Yes; runs on commercially available LLM APIs (Azure for GPT-4o, AWS Bedrock for Claude and Mistral), no specialized hardware required, no model fine-tuning. - Efficient: Criteria methods use ~600 tokens and description methods ~200 tokens independent of N, versus 25K tokens for Examples at 100 shots; inference time is reduced 57–70% at high shot counts; the one-time extraction overhead is a single LLM call per intent, performed once. - Effective: Evidence is solid within the tested domain — the methods prevent characteristic ICL degradation and outperform token-level compression baselines consistently across five models and five tasks. Generalization beyond the 50-call single-company dataset is undemonstrated. - Scalable: Prompt token cost is O(1) in example count — the core advantage. Scaling to multi-class settings is not tested; the authors note extracted representation grows linearly with class count, and minority class under-representation at low N may degrade criteria quality. Test sets contain only 200 balanced examples per task, so conclusions about behavior under distribution shift or large-scale deployment are not supported by evidence. The work delivers a software artifact (code + dataset) and is accepted to Findings of ACL 2026 (peer-reviewed; originally posted as arXiv preprint arXiv:2606.15641).
