empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 62 Resilience 55 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Text-to-image models trained on elaborated captions exhibit strong prompt adherence but collapse semantically: repeated sampling from the same prompt yields images that differ only in visually insignificant details or reflect a single dominant mode, regardless of the richness of possible interpretations. Creative users — designers, content creators, artists — exploring an underspecified prompt cannot browse meaningfully different alternatives using existing tools. Prior diversity methods (CADS, Guidance Interval, Power-Law CFG, Contextual Repulsion) perturb the latent denoising process to increase sample spread but offer no explicit control over which dimensions vary; SGI filters a large candidate pool but is bounded by the base model's inherent diversity ceiling. None of these approaches make the axes of variation interpretable or navigable. Key Idea The central insight is that modern prompt-adherent T2I models can be treated as controllable renderers rather than stochastic samplers: making explicit semantic choices at the text-prompt level — encoded as structured JSON scene specifications — and organizing those choices into a hierarchical tree allows diversity to arise from reasoned exploration of the semantic space rather than from sampling noise. Because each branching point names a single semantic aspect and locks all prior decisions, every leaf node in the tree is a specific, traceable, interpretable interpretation of the original prompt. Methodology The method operates over a space S of fully specified scene interpretations encoded as structured JSONs. A VLM first expands the user prompt p into an initial scene s₀. The output is a rooted tree (V, E) where each directed edge encodes a semantic constraint c transforming parent scene s₁ into child scene s₂. Three requirements govern tree structure: (i) Semantic Structuring — all children of a node vary along a single shared semantic aspect a; (ii) Heterogeneity — sibling constraints realize that aspect in distinct, non-overlapping ways; (iii) Plausibility — each constraint remains logically consistent with the original prompt and all ancestor constraints. Tree expansion proceeds node-by-node through a four-agent pipeline. The Context Analyst identifies mutable details d_i in the current JSON — those not fixed by the locked constraint path C_s — to restrict candidate changes to unconstrained scene elements. The Brainstormer groups mutable details into candidate high-level semantic aspects a_i, scores each by impact on narrative, layout, and style, and proposes branching candidates. The Decision Maker selects the single highest-impact aspect a* orthogonal to the branch history and generates 2–3 constraints {c_i} pushing siblings into clearly different regimes. The Critic validates proposed constraints against the original prompt and accumulated constraints for adherence and logical consistency, revising failures before committing. All agents use Gemini 2.5 Flash with fixed temperatures (0.4 or 0.7 per role) and token limits (4K–8K). The tree uses branching factor 3 (two distinct children plus one identity-preserving branch) expanded to depth 3, yielding 27 leaf nodes. Each node renders to an image via FIBO's VLM-based prompt expander and T2I generator. Key Results Evaluation covers 50 randomly sampled MS-COCO prompts with all baselines implemented under the same FIBO backbone and equal VLM call budgets. Semantic Browsing achieves Vendi Score 3.34 and DINO Similarity 0.61, the highest diversity and lowest similarity of any method; the nearest competitor is CADS at Vendi 3.29 / DINO 0.67. Aesthetic Score is 6.52, competitive with baselines (range 6.28–6.56). VQAScore is 0.90, slightly below Stochastic VLM Seeding and Power-Law CFG (both 0.93), though the gap is small. Human preference study with 25 participants across 12 prompts: Semantic Browsing achieves diversity win rates of 65.1%–92.4% and overall preference win rates of 66.7%–87.9% against all four baselines. Structural evaluation across 17,550 node pairs from 50 trees: pairwise DINO distance rises monotonically from 0.168 at 1 hop to 0.452 at 5 hops, confirming the tree hierarchy encodes meaningful semantic distance. Hierarchical Consistency (VLM-as-judge of constraint adherence) scores 0.87/1.0 for the full workflow. Ablation results: removing the Context Analyst drops Hierarchical Consistency to 0.82; removing the Critic drops VQAScore to 0.87; merging Brainstormer and Decision Maker reduces mean pairwise DINO distance by ~7% at 5-hop distance (0.473 → 0.439). Replacing Gemini 2.5 Flash with ChatGPT-4.5 yields Vendi 3.30 / Aesthetic 6.72 / VQAScore 0.94, closely matching Gemini results. Scaling ablation (Table 4): width scaling (D=1) yields Vendi 2.05→3.13 at gallery sizes 5→20 with VQAScore dropping from 0.94 to 0.91; depth scaling (BF=1) yields Vendi 1.79→2.36 at sizes 5→20 with VQAScore dropping from 0.81 to 0.75. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM + NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The structured diversity task itself — generating a hierarchical gallery where every branching point corresponds to an explicit, named semantic decision — is a genuinely new problem formulation; prior work treats diversity as an unstructured property of a flat image set. The multi-agent tree-expansion workflow that simultaneously enforces Semantic Structuring, Heterogeneity, and Plausibility is a new method. The individual components (VLM agents, structured JSON prompting, T2I rendering, self-correction critics) are existing techniques; the contribution is their compositional architecture and the formal requirements imposed on the tree. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations isolate the role of each agent (Tables 2 and 3) and are informative, though the effect sizes for individual agents are modest (0.05 drop in Hierarchical Consistency, 0.03 drop in VQAScore). No error bars or significance tests appear in the main tables. The 50-prompt evaluation set is small; results on a larger or more varied prompt distribution would strengthen claims. The human study (25 participants, 12 prompts) is adequate for qualitative validation but underpowered for statistical conclusions. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.23679v1) with acknowledgment of ECCV 2026 reviewers, indicating it is under review. Full agent system prompts are published in Appendix Figures 17–20, significantly aiding reproducibility. A project page is listed (saradorfman1.github.io/SemanticBrowsing-webpage/) but no open-source code repository is confirmed. A notable concern: the VQAScore gap (0.90 ours vs. 0.93 for the simplest baseline) is attributed to evaluator bias toward conventional low-variance compositions rather than to a genuine prompt adherence deficit; this attribution is asserted, not measured with a human prompt-adherence judge. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper builds and evaluates a complete image generation system. - Practical: Runs on commercially available VLM APIs (Gemini 2.5 Flash, ChatGPT-4.5) and T2I backbones (FIBO, FLUX.2); no specialized hardware beyond GPU for T2I rendering is required. - Efficient: Semantic Browsing requires 10.2 seconds and 15.9K tokens per 27-image gallery; token usage scales sublinearly with branching factor (23K at BF=5 to 26.5K at BF=20), making it comparable in cost to post-hoc optimization baselines (11.4 seconds, 9.7K tokens). - Effective: Achieves the best quantitative diversity scores on 50 MS-COCO prompts and wins human preference across all four baseline comparisons, confirming the method solves its stated problem on the tested distribution. - Scalable: Only tested at galleries up to 27 images on short, concrete captions; behavior on abstract prompts, much larger galleries, or under higher-stakes generation constraints (e.g., brand consistency, safety filtering) is not characterized. The paper delivers a working system; full agent prompts are published in the appendix. No open-source code repository is cited as of submission date. Strengths - The formalization of structured diversity as a hierarchical tree with three explicit, testable requirements (Semantic Structuring, Heterogeneity, Plausibility) is a clean conceptual contribution that cleanly distinguishes the work from unstructured diversity methods and yields interpretable evaluation criteria. - The semantic-topological correlation evidence (DINO distance monotonically increasing from 0.168 at 1 hop to 0.452 at 5 hops across 17,550 pairs) provides quantitative, geometry-level proof that the tree hierarchy encodes meaningful semantic relationships rather than visual noise. - The multi-agent role separation is empirically justified: ablating any single agent measurably degrades a specific, predicted requirement (Context Analyst → Hierarchical Consistency drops 0.05; Critic → VQAScore drops 0.03; role merger → 7% DINO drop at 5-hop distance), confirming the decomposition is load-bearing rather than decorative. - The model-agnostic design is demonstrated concretely: Gemini→GPT-4.5 VLM swap and FIBO→FLUX.2 backbone swap both preserve structured diversity with nearly identical metrics. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors state that quality depends on (a) the underlying T2I model's ability to faithfully realize fine-grained prompt modifications and (b) the VLM agents' ability to propose rich and diverse semantic alternatives, which they describe as currently limited relative to the full breadth of possible interpretations. They also note that user-specified semantic aspects constrain the scope of resulting variations, potentially narrowing exploration. These limitations are stated directly and credibly, without minimization. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Evaluation is restricted to 50 short, concrete MS-COCO noun-phrase captions; there is no test on abstract, metaphorical, or highly compositional prompts where semantic decomposition may be ambiguous or fail. - The human study measures pairwise preference for diversity and overall quality but does not measure task utility — whether the tree structure helps users locate a desired output faster than browsing an equivalently large flat diverse gallery is never tested, which is the primary practical claim. - No ablation tests alternative decompositions (e.g., two agents instead of four); the paper ablates removing agents individually but not combining them differently, leaving open whether a simpler architecture would suffice. - The VQAScore deficit (0.90 vs. 0.93 for the simplest baseline) is attributed to evaluator bias without a direct human-judge measurement of prompt adherence; if the deficit reflects genuine semantic drift from accumulated constraints, that would be a meaningful limitation. - Total end-to-end latency including T2I rendering for a 27-image gallery is not reported; only the agentic overhead (10.2 seconds) is stated, making it difficult to assess real-world throughput. Bottom Line A practitioner building creative exploration or design assistance tools gains a directly actionable, well-validated method: the structured-diversity formulation is novel, the multi-agent workflow is empirically justified, and the full agent prompts are published, making replication straightforward without waiting for code release.
Recommended 2606.23679 PDF
#75 — Innovative (62)

From Genomes to Ecological Predictions: Using Metabolic Modelling for Uncovering Penicillium expansum Suppression by Native Apple Fruit Epiphytic Bacteria

R Bartuv, VY Zhimo, VK Sharma, T Shalem, S Droby… · researchsquare.com · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a validated computational framework that translates metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) from the apple carposphere into curated genome-scale metabolic models (GSMMs), then uses dynamic flux balance analysis (dFBA) to predict and mechanistically stratify the biocontrol potential of native epiphytic bacteria against the postharvest pathogen Penicillium expansum. - Why it's important: Postharvest blue mold caused by P. expansum destroys significant fractions of apple harvests globally, and identifying which native microbiome members suppress it currently requires slow, empirical culture-based screening with no mechanistic insight into why any given strain works. - How it does it: Automated GSMM drafts built from 23 high-quality MAGs were subjected to a four-stage manual curation pipeline guided by experimentally validated prototrophic phenotypes and a Universal BiGG-EC Dictionary, then used in Monte Carlo-perturbed dFBA co-culture simulations to compute a metabolic resource overlap (MRO) suppression gradient, with computational secretion profiling added to capture non-metabolic (VOC-mediated) antagonism. - The good: A strong positive correlation (Spearman r = 0.83, p = 0.005) between simulated pathogen biomass suppression and experimental P. expansum growth restriction in co-culture plate assays directly validates the MRO mechanism, and the secretion-profile analysis correctly identified Brevundimonas nasdae as a VOC-toxicity agent despite its weak metabolic competitiveness. - The missing: The predictive gradient completely failed to correlate with disease outcome in the actual fruit wound bioassay (Spearman r = -0.07, p = 0.865), exposing a fundamental gap between plate-based competitive dynamics and in-planta biocontrol efficacy that the framework does not yet resolve. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on microbiome-guided biocontrol or MAG-to-model pipelines; the manual curation protocol and its demonstration that automated drafts are insufficient for ecological prediction are the most transferable contributions.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (70) and Completeness (70) lead: the discovery that dFBA secretion profiles detect VOC-mediated antagonism (correctly flagging B. nasdae as an acetaldehyde/ethanol secretor despite weak metabolic competitiveness) is a non-obvious insight, and the paper confronts its central failure directly. Novelty (65) is solid — a phenotype-guided MAG-to-GSMM curation pipeline with multi-assay cross-validation in the carposphere. Rigor (58) is the limiting dimension: a Research Square preprint with 9-isolate Spearman correlations that are statistically fragile, 10 Monte Carlo draws, and no held-out validation against curation overfitting, though code, models, and raw sequences are publicly deposited. Resilience (55) is undercut by the fruit-wound bioassay's complete predictive failure (r=-0.07), which the framework raises but does not resolve. Impact (55) reflects released SBML models and a reproducible workflow that predict in-vitro but not in-planta efficacy. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*58 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*55 = 62.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Postharvest spoilage by P. expansum (blue mold) causes substantial economic losses in apple production. Current biocontrol strategies rely on empirical isolation of antagonists through culture-dependent screening, which is labor-intensive, provides no mechanistic understanding of how suppression works, and cannot leverage the growing body of metagenomic data from the carposphere. The specific gap is the absence of a predictive pipeline that connects static genomic sequence data directly to dynamic ecological predictions of pathogen suppression, without requiring cultivation of every community member. Prior metabolic modeling work on microbial communities has been conducted in gut and soil systems but not applied to the agriculturally critical carposphere context. Key Idea The central insight is that automated GSMM reconstructions from MAGs are structurally defective for ecologically realistic simulation media (all 23 draft models produce zero growth on biologically relevant apple-like or Davis Minimal Broth media), but that a targeted, phenotype-guided manual curation pipeline can repair these topological gaps in a genomically principled way, after which the curated models produce dFBA simulations whose predicted competitive sugar consumption accurately ranks the biocontrol potential of native isolates. A secondary insight is that dFBA secretion profiles can identify volatile-toxic mechanisms that fall entirely outside the scope of resource-competition modeling, enabling a two-tier mechanistic classification of antagonism. Methodology Shotgun metagenomic data from Malus domestica 'Golden Delicious' epiphytic microbiomes (471 million 150 bp reads, 71 Gbps) were binned using MetaWRAP v1.3.1 (MetaBAT2, MaxBin 2.0, CONCOCT), yielding 23 high-quality MAGs (>90% completeness, <5% contamination by CheckM) plus a reference Methylobacterium radiotolerans genome. Automated GSMMs were built with CarveMe v1.6.2 using the Gurobi solver, without gap-filling, to establish a baseline. A curated P. expansum GSMM was built using a published 24-species Penicillium model translated into BiGG namespace as a custom CarveMe template. Manual curation followed a four-stage pipeline: (1) diagnostic simulation to identify unproduced biomass components and trace metabolic bottlenecks backward through the network; (2) iterative gap-filling using a two-tier decision hierarchy (Tier 1: reactions supported by strain-specific metagenomic EC catalogue; Tier 2: parsimony-minimal universal reactions from BiGG when no genomic support exists); (3) stoichiometric and energetic consistency enforcement (mass/charge balance, salvage pathways for byproduct recycling); and (4) physiological validation by simulating growth on four carbon sources (glucose, fructose, sucrose, sorbitol) matched to experimental OD600 data from cultured isolates. Curation was performed for five isolates with available physical strains, then extended to four additional community members. Dynamic FBA co-culture simulations were parameterized using an in-silico Apple-like Medium based on published apple metabolome composition (fatty acids, sugars, organic acids, aromatic compounds, amino acids at ratio-matched molar concentrations). Michaelis-Menten kinetics governed substrate uptake at each 1-hour time step; Vmax was sampled from a normal distribution with 10% coefficient of variation across 10 Monte Carlo iterations to propagate kinetic uncertainty. Simulations ran for 72 hours. Spearman rank correlations were used to relate simulated metrics to experimental outcomes across three assay types: co-culture plate assay on PDA, fruit wound bioassay on Golden Delicious apples, and double-plate VOC assay isolating gaseous-phase effects. Three critical design decisions: (i) deliberate avoidance of automated gap-filling before curation, ensuring models reflect raw genomic content and preventing over-tuned phenotypic reproduction — well-justified; (ii) 10% Monte Carlo Vmax variability as a proxy for kinetic uncertainty in the deterministic dFBA framework — pragmatic but the 10% figure is not empirically derived; (iii) prioritization of genomic evidence (Tier 1) over universal parsimony (Tier 2) in gap-filling — biologically sound but leaves some gaps filled with universal reactions that lack experimental confirmation. Key Results All 23 automated draft GSMMs failed to produce biomass on biologically relevant media (growth rate = 0.0 h⁻¹), confirming that automated reconstruction is insufficient for ecological simulation. After curation, all nine target models successfully grew on the in-silico Apple-like Medium. dFBA co-culture simulations produced a suppression gradient: C. terrigena predicted to reduce P. expansum biomass by 67% and M. radiotolerans by 33% relative to monoculture control (Welch's t-test, p < 0.01 for the top cluster); A. pittii and B. nasdae were predicted as weak competitors with minimal P. expansum biomass reduction. Simulated total sugar consumption (glucose + fructose + sucrose) showed a Spearman r = -0.92 (p = 0.0005) correlation with predicted P. expansum biomass, validating the MRO mechanism computationally. Co-culture plate assay validation: Spearman r = 0.83 (p = 0.005) between predicted P. expansum biomass and experimental radial growth diameter; Spearman r = -0.74 (p = 0.02) between simulated sugar consumption and experimental inhibition. These are strong correlations for 9 data points across a biologically diverse community. By contrast, the fruit wound bioassay yielded Spearman r = -0.07 (p = 0.865) between predicted suppression gradient and actual lesion size, a complete predictive failure. B. nasdae achieved near-zero lesion diameter in fruit wounds despite weak metabolic competitiveness. The VOC double-plate assay confirmed B. nasdae suppressed P. expansum via gaseous emissions alone (mean colony diameter ~14–18 mm at 7 DPI versus ~48–50 mm for controls); the dFBA secretion profile uniquely predicted B. nasdae co-secretion of acetaldehyde (893 mmol/gDW) and ethanol (298 mmol/gDW), consistent with known synergistic antifungal activity of this pair. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The novel contribution is the phenotype-guided, genomically anchored manual curation pipeline for MAG-derived GSMMs applied to ecological prediction of biocontrol efficacy, and the demonstration that dFBA secretion profiling can predict VOC-mediated antagonism mechanisms from genomic data. The use of FBA/dFBA for microbial community modeling is well-established; applying it to the carposphere with experimental cross-validation across multiple assay types, and explicitly demonstrating the prerequisite of manual curation, is the genuine advance. The finding that resource competition and VOC toxicity require mechanistically distinct computational approaches to detect is a substantive empirical contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility The curation pipeline is transparently documented with a full modification log (Additional file 5) and strain-specific pathway diagrams (Supplementary Figs. 2–9). MEMOTE scores for stoichiometric consistency improved across all five curated training-set models (0.17%–6.29% gains in consistency sub-scores; total MEMOTE score gains of 0.55%–4.87%). Phenotypic validation on four carbon sources was conducted in biological triplicate with OD600 quantification. Co-culture and fruit wound assays used biological replicates (triplicate for plate assays, five replicates of 10 wounds for fruit assays). Monte Carlo simulation with 10 iterations introduces statistical distribution but 10 draws is a small sample for robust uncertainty quantification. All MAG FASTA files, curated GSMMs in SBML format, and the dFBA workflow are publicly available at https://github.com/FreilichLab/Apple_Peel_Microbiome_GSMMs; raw metagenome sequences are deposited at NCBI BioProject 948808. The paper is a preprint on Research Square (posted June 15, 2026) and has not undergone peer review. The co-culture plate validation uses only 9 bacterial isolates, providing a small sample for Spearman correlation analysis — the p-values are nominally significant but the effect sizes drive the result. No cross-validation was performed to guard against overfitting the curation pipeline to the training isolates. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: The computational pipeline runs on standard academic hardware using COBRApy, CarveMe, and Gurobi; experimental validation requires standard microbiology equipment and apple fruit — fully accessible to agricultural research labs. - Efficient: dFBA with 10 Monte Carlo iterations for 9 species models over 72 simulated hours is computationally light; the bottleneck is manual curation labor, which the paper estimates required hundreds of annotation-guided decisions per model — not yet automatable from the described protocol. - Effective: The framework predicts co-culture plate outcomes well (Spearman r = 0.83) but fails to predict fruit bioassay outcomes (Spearman r = -0.07), meaning it currently predicts in-vitro competitive dynamics, not field-relevant biocontrol efficacy. - Scalable: The curation pipeline was applied to 9 isolates with physical strains; extension to all 23 MAGs or to community-level simulations with multi-species dFBA (not pairwise) has not been demonstrated and would require substantially more curation effort and combinatorial simulation. The work delivers curated SBML models and a reproducible Python workflow as software artifacts. Strengths - The demonstration that all automated GSMM drafts fail on ecologically realistic media, and that manual curation restores predictive capability, is a critical result that challenges the common practice of using uncurated MAG-derived models for community simulations. - The two-tier gap-filling hierarchy (genomic evidence first, parsimony second) is a principled and reproducible curation protocol that avoids the arbitrary over-fitting common in manual curation, and the full modification log enables exact reproduction. - The discovery that dFBA secretion profiles can detect VOC-mediated suppression — correctly identifying B. nasdae as an acetaldehyde/ethanol secretor despite poor sugar competitiveness — demonstrates that standard biomass-maximizing FBA contains latent mechanistic information about non-growth-associated phenomena that is underutilized. - The multi-assay experimental design (co-culture plates, fruit wounds, VOC double-plate) directly tests both the prediction and its mechanistic assumptions, rather than validating only against the same assay type used to calibrate the model. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge that dFBA inherently omits non-growth-associated maintenance (NGAM) costs, spatial dynamics, and regulatory constraints, causing theoretical biomass yields to represent maximal stoichiometric limits rather than realistic cellular physiology. They note that direct inhibitory toxicity from soluble metabolites is not captured by the standard biomass-maximizing objective. They acknowledge that the secreted toxic metabolites (acetaldehyde, ethanol, selenite) predicted by the models are computational outputs that require direct experimental quantification to confirm their physical presence and functional role in the carposphere. They call for multi-kingdom modeling (bacteria + yeasts + host chemistry) and incorporation of host cuticular polymers into future simulation environments. The authors confront limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The fruit wound bioassay failure (r = -0.07) is the most serious unresolved problem. The paper attributes it to non-metabolic mechanisms like VOC production and implicitly to environmental differences between plate and fruit contexts, but provides no quantitative analysis of what fraction of the community-level suppression hierarchy shifts and why. This is not an acknowledged limitation — it is framed as a finding that motivates the VOC analysis — but the underlying drivers remain unexplained for several isolates (e.g., S. yunnanensis performs well in fruit wounds but was not highlighted in either the MRO or VOC analysis). - Spearman correlations across 9 data points are statistically fragile; a single anomalous isolate shifts the rank correlation substantially. No permutation tests or leave-one-out robustness checks are presented. - The curation pipeline was developed using 5 isolates as a training set and then applied to 4 additional isolates without held-out validation — it is not demonstrated that curation decisions made for the training set do not encode implicit knowledge of the competitive outcomes. - B. nasdae VOC identification relies on dFBA predicting fermentative overflow metabolism as the optimal growth strategy in the apple medium, but the paper does not experimentally confirm that B. nasdae actually secretes acetaldehyde and ethanol during growth on apple-mimicking media; the computational prediction and the VOC assay outcome are consistent but the causal link is indirect. - The Apple-like Medium parameterization uses published apple metabolome ratios from a 2007 food chemistry paper, but the carposphere nutrient profile available to surface-colonizing bacteria depends on exudate composition and physical accessibility, neither of which is characterized for this variety and condition. Bottom Line A practitioner designing microbiome-guided biocontrol interventions against postharvest pathogens gains a validated curation protocol and a concrete demonstration that metabolic modeling can stratify native antagonists by mechanism, but must be cautious: the framework predicts in-vitro competitive dynamics accurately while failing entirely to predict in-planta efficacy, and that gap is the central unsolved problem this work raises rather than resolves.
Search Results
#76 — Innovative (61)

DRFLOW: A Deep Research Benchmark for Personalized Workflow Prediction

MTI Khondaker, R Li, M Abdul-Mageed… · arxiv.org · generating environments for agent testing in enterprises - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DRFlow, a benchmark of 100 tasks (1,246 reference workflow steps grounded in 3,900+ heterogeneous artifacts) for evaluating LLM agents on personalized workflow prediction from enterprise data sources, together with DRFlow-Agent (DRFA), a four-component reference agent. - Why it's important: Existing deep research benchmarks measure free-form report quality; they do not test whether agents can recover an applicable organizational procedure, resolve conditional branches using personal evidence, and output a followable, user-specific action sequence — the capability enterprises actually need for compliance, HR, and clinical workflows. - How it does it: Each task pairs company-side procedure documents with user-side personal artifacts; solving the task requires first inferring the branching generic workflow from the company evidence and then resolving which branches apply by matching user-specific facts drawn from emails, chats, and personal PDFs. - The good: Human annotators and the LLM judge agree at Pearson r = 0.883 on F1 scores across 10 evaluated tasks, validating the automated metrics, and even the strongest configuration (DRFA + Claude-Opus-4.7) reaches only 69.14 F1, confirming the benchmark is not close to saturated. - The missing: All tasks are fully synthetically generated by GPT-5.2, which is also one of the evaluated backbone models; there is no real-enterprise validation, and GPT-family models may be systematically favored by the construction and judge pipeline from the same provider. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers building enterprise deep-research agents should read this — it is the only benchmark that jointly measures procedure recovery, conditional branch resolution, and personalization from heterogeneous artifacts — but should treat the numbers as directional given the synthetic construction and small task count.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The benchmark fills a documented gap — Table 1 shows no prior work jointly covers procedure recovery, conditional branch resolution, and personalization from heterogeneous private artifacts (Novelty 70) — and the seven-metric suite plus human-judge validation (Pearson r = 0.883) is more thorough than typical (Completeness 70). Rigor (52) is the primary drag: tasks are fully synthetic, generated by GPT-5.2 which is also an evaluated backbone, judged by GPT-4o from the same provider family, with a 15-task ablation subset whose ±4-8 F1 std makes several conclusions statistically marginal. Resilience (55) suffers from this provider-family circularity and lack of any real-enterprise validation. The DRFA agent itself is application of known planning patterns, not a new algorithm. Weighted composite: 0.20*70 + 0.25*52 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*65 = 61.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 70 Resilience 55 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Enterprise users who ask "what steps do I need to take?" for policy compliance, eligibility determination, clinical procedures, or HR processes need actionable personalized sequences, not narrative summaries. Prior deep research benchmarks — GAIA, DeepResearch Bench, DRACO, ResearchRubrics — all evaluate free-form answers or rubric-scored reports and do not assess whether an agent can produce structured, followable workflows. Workflow generation works such as AutoFlow and Chat2Workflow assume the target procedure is largely specified in the input; they do not evaluate discovery of the applicable procedure from scattered heterogeneous artifacts, nor personalization via user-specific conditional branch resolution. No prior benchmark occupies the intersection of heterogeneous enterprise evidence retrieval, structured procedural output, and user-specific workflow instantiation. Key Idea Personalized workflow prediction decomposes into two grounding operations: recovering a generic branching procedure embedded across company-side artifacts (PDFs, DOCX, shared files, email threads), then resolving which conditional branches apply by matching user-specific personal evidence to the user's particular context. By making these two stages explicit — and by generating both supporting insights and distractors for each evidence type — the benchmark separately tests retrieval, condition resolution, and personalization rather than collapsing them into a single generation score. Methodology DRFlow is constructed through a six-stage synthesis pipeline. Stage S1 jointly generates a task seed: a deep research question Q_i, a company persona, a user persona, and their relationship. Stage S2 composes the generic workflow W^g_i as a branching action sequence with prerequisites and conditional decision points drawn from the company context. Stage S3 generates company-side supporting insights E^{c+}_i and domain-adjacent distractors E^{c-}_i, each materialized into realistic files (PDF, DOCX) via a sparse-evidence injection process that embeds task-relevant content inside otherwise realistic surrounding text. Stage S4 resolves W^g_i branches using user context C^u_i to produce the reference personalized workflow W^p_i, which is human-verified. Stage S5 generates user-side supporting insights and distractors as personal artifacts (email, chat, local documents). Stage S6 generates two additional distinct-but-domain-adjacent cases whose evidence is injected into the same pool to create case-level disambiguation pressure (the "mixed" split). GPT-5.2 is used as the generation model throughout. Evaluation uses seven metrics: Factuality (whether each predicted step cites supporting evidence), Recall (fraction of reference steps recovered), Precision (fraction of predicted steps validly aligned to reference steps), F1 (task-wise harmonic mean of recall and precision), Topology Order (pairwise ordering consistency between aligned steps), Condition Resolution (whether the predicted step correctly instantiates the condition using personal evidence), and Personalized Comprehensiveness (whether user-specific operational details are preserved). LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4o) scores the prompt-based metrics; text-embedding-3-small retrieves the top five relevant chunks for factuality verification. DRFA (DRFlow-Agent) operates through four components: Research Planning (decomposes the DR question into generic and personal evidence collection objectives); Action Planning (translates investigation areas into concrete tool-call sequences with expected outputs); Adaptive Action Planning (two sub-functions — gap finding assesses evidence balance across generic vs. personal workflow needs; conditional action planning constructs an intermediate generic workflow, identifies unresolved branches, and generates targeted retrieval actions); Workflow Generation (constructs first the generic then the personalized workflow from collected evidence). The agent runs up to a fixed iteration budget (10 in all main experiments). Three critical design decisions: (1) separating generic and personal evidence collection to ensure both sides are covered — well-justified by the two-stage grounding structure; (2) explicit conditional action planning to identify and close unresolved branches — justified by ablation showing CAP alone contributes ~5 F1 on Condition Resolution; (3) a fixed iteration budget rather than adaptive stopping — partially justified by the observation that 10 iterations outperforms 15 on most metrics, suggesting over-planning harms factuality. Key Results On the original 50-task split (Table 2), averaged over four backbone models (GPT-5.2, Claude-Opus-4.5, Gemini-3.1-pro, DeepSeek-v3.2), DRFA achieves 57.93 average F1 versus 47.91 for DRBench Agent (DRBA) and 9.84 for a Basic no-planning agent — a 10.02 F1 absolute gain for DRFA over DRBA. DRFA improves average Condition Resolution from 20.19 to 38.21 and Personalized Comprehensiveness from 37.69 to 56.54. Topology Order is high across all agents (>88), indicating step ordering is not the primary challenge. The best individual configuration is DRFA + GPT-5.2 at 63.90 F1. On the mixed split (Table 3), DRFA average F1 drops to 43.91 and Condition Resolution to 33.74, confirming that case-level disambiguation substantially degrades performance. On recent frontier models (Table C.2, 15-task ablation subset, 5 models), DRFA raises average F1 from 63.16 (DRBA) to 68.06 and Condition Resolution from 33.96 to 48.16; Claude-Opus-4.7 + DRFA achieves the highest F1 at 69.14. Standard deviations across 3 runs range from ±4 to ±8 F1 points, indicating moderate variance. Ablation (Table 4, 15-task subset): removing gap finding drops average F1 from 56.12 to 50.35; removing conditional action planning drops it to 51.77; removing both drops to 43.85. Cost-performance analysis (Figure G.1) shows Kimi-k2.6 at 68.54 F1 for $0.044/report is a strong Pareto point versus Claude-Opus-4.7 at 69.14 F1 for $0.395/report. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The benchmark is genuinely novel in combining heterogeneous enterprise evidence retrieval, conditional branch resolution, and personalized structured output evaluation — Table 1 documents that no prior benchmark simultaneously covers private data, heterogeneous application types, personalization, actionable steps, structured output, and an extensible pipeline. The DRFA agent is an application of established planning patterns (gap finding, conditional planning, iterative tool calling) to the specific workflow prediction setting rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover both planning components (GF and CAP) across four backbones (Table 4), with standard deviations reported across three runs on a 15-task subset (Table E.1). Human evaluation on 10 tasks yields Fleiss' κ = 0.663 (substantial agreement) and Pearson r = 0.883 between human-average and LLM-judge F1, providing meaningful validation of the automated metrics. Code is publicly available at github.com/ServiceNow/drflow; benchmark data promised upon acceptance. The containerized execution environment (Nextcloud, Mattermost, IMAP email, file sharing in Docker) improves reproducibility. Three concerns warrant flagging: (1) the benchmark is generated by GPT-5.2, which is also one of the evaluated backbone models, creating a potential circular evaluation risk that is acknowledged but not fully mitigated; (2) GPT-4o serves as the LLM judge for metrics, and both the generation and judging models come from the same provider family as GPT-5.2; (3) the 15-task ablation subset is small enough that the reported ±4–8 F1 standard deviations make several ablation conclusions statistically marginal. Human unanimous agreement is only 57.2%, meaning 42.8% of step-match decisions required majority voting, indicating genuine task ambiguity that the LLM judge may handle inconsistently. This is a preprint; results have not yet been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (benchmark with an evaluated reference agent and containerized infrastructure). - Practical: All backbone models are accessed via the OpenRouter API using commercial endpoints; the benchmark environment runs in standard Docker containers with open-source services (Nextcloud, Mattermost, IMAP), so deployment requires no specialized hardware. - Efficient: Per-workflow report cost ranges from approximately $0.005 (DeepSeek-V3.2) to $0.395 (Claude-Opus-4.7) per task based on Figure G.1; no latency or wall-clock figures are reported. - Effective: DRFA achieves 57.93 average F1 on the original split, leaving roughly 42% of reference steps unrecovered or incorrectly aligned; average Condition Resolution of 38.21% means most conditional branches are not correctly resolved even by the proposed agent, indicating the benchmark captures a genuinely unsolved problem. - Scalable: The data generation pipeline is claimed extensible to new domains, but no stress test at larger task or domain counts is reported; the benchmark's current 100-task scale is modest. The paper delivers a benchmark dataset (to be released upon acceptance), containerized evaluation infrastructure, and agent code at github.com/ServiceNow/drflow. Strengths - The benchmark fills a documented gap: procedure recovery plus conditional branch resolution plus personalization from heterogeneous private data has no prior benchmark, and Table 1 documents this across 12 existing systems methodically rather than by selective comparison. - The seven-metric evaluation suite decomposes workflow quality along operationally distinct dimensions, providing diagnostic signal that a single score would obscure; in particular, the separation of Factuality, Condition Resolution, and Personalized Comprehensiveness reveals that current agents fail along structurally different dimensions. - The human-LLM judge validation (Pearson r = 0.883 on F1 across 10 tasks with three independent annotators) is more thorough than most benchmark papers provide, and the finding that DRFA's factuality deficit traces to aggressive evidence fusion rather than worse retrieval (Appendix H) gives mechanistic insight beyond headline numbers. - The mixed split, which injects evidence from two additional distinct cases into the same pool, is a principled stress test of case-level disambiguation that reveals a 14 F1-point performance gap from original to mixed, exposing a real failure mode absent from simpler benchmarks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: synthetic data may not capture real institutional ambiguity and variation; the benchmark covers a finite set of 5 domains and task families and should not be interpreted as exhaustive; LLM-as-a-judge introduces variance and may be sensitive to domain-specific terminology or partially correct condition resolutions; the ablation is conducted on a 15-task subset to manage cost and may not capture full variance; and stochastic model behavior and API changes in closed-source models affect reproducibility. The authors confront these limitations explicitly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The fully synthetic construction (companies, personas, documents, emails, chats all LLM-generated by GPT-5.2) is not validated against real enterprise workflows. No held-out real-organization test set exists, and the claim that tasks "cover realistic procedural settings" rests on human annotator approval of plausibility rather than expert domain validation. It is unknown whether real enterprise procedures have systematically different branching structures or ambiguity profiles. - GPT-5.2 is both the generation model and one of the evaluated backbone models; GPT-4o judges semantic alignment of steps generated partly by its provider-family sibling. This circularity is acknowledged in the limitations but not mitigated — there is no cross-provider validation study showing that the judge's F1 rankings are stable when a non-OpenAI judge is used. - The 100-task scale (50 original, 50 mixed) is small for a benchmark; the 15-task ablation subset is very small. Standard deviations of ±4–8 F1 points imply that multiple ablation comparisons (e.g., GF vs. CAP contributions) lie within one standard deviation of each other, making it difficult to conclude which planning component is more important with confidence. - The paper does not analyze how much of the Condition Resolution deficit (~61% of conditions unresolved even for the best agent) stems from retrieval failure (the relevant personal evidence was not fetched) versus reasoning failure (the evidence was fetched but the branch was not resolved). Without this decomposition, it is unclear where future work should focus. - Human annotator unanimous agreement is only 57.2%; the remaining 43% resolved by two-of-three voting could mask systematic LLM judge bias on the harder disambiguation cases, yet the Pearson validation is computed over task-level averages rather than individual step decisions, potentially averaging away this concern. Bottom Line DRFlow addresses a real gap in the agent evaluation landscape — personalized enterprise workflow prediction is both practically important and genuinely underserved — but its fully synthetic construction, small scale, and provider-family circularity mean practitioners should treat reported numbers as directional capability estimates and validate against real organizational data before drawing deployment conclusions.
Search Results 2606.18191 PDF
#77 — Innovative (61)

When Coordination Becomes Predictive: Actor-Enriched Multi-Horizon Forecasting of Process Performance

A Leribaux, R Oyamada, J De Smedt, A Polyvyanyy… · researchsquare.com · business process mining or process mining - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces an online, multi-horizon process-level forecasting framework that enriches daily time series of process KPIs with actor-behavior signals (continuation, interruption, handover-idle, handover-busy counts and durations) extracted from event logs, then evaluates six model families on three BPIC datasets at 1-, 3-, and 7-day prediction horizons. - Why it's important: Operational managers need process-level forecasts of elapsed and remaining time across all open cases to plan workload allocation, yet existing predictive process monitoring methods either predict individual case outcomes or build aggregate forecasts without modeling how resource coordination dynamics drive future performance. - How it does it: The core mechanism is classifying every consecutive-event transition in an event log into one of four behavior types via formal rules on resource identity and concurrency, aggregating these into daily count and duration series, and then appending those series as exogenous regressors to any time series forecasting model. - The good: SHAP analysis consistently shows actor-behavior features — particularly handover-idle variability (Cum_Count_HI_rolling_std14) — rank among the top predictors, providing interpretable mechanistic evidence, not just predictive signal. - The missing: The paper evaluates on only three datasets from the same BPIC benchmark family, never ablates individual behavior types (C vs. I vs. HI vs. HB in isolation), and does not report the absolute scale of the KPI units, making it difficult to assess operational significance of RMSE reductions. - Read it or not: Worth reading for process mining and business process management researchers who want a principled, reproducible framework for incorporating resource dynamics into aggregate forecasting; practitioners outside that community will find the gains modest and dataset-specific.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The dominant drivers are Rigor (68) and Completeness (62): Wilcoxon tests over 10 seeds, error bars, a strictly enforced online chronological split, and public code are credible, but the preprint status, the absence of any per-behavior-type ablation, and unstated KPI units cap both. Novelty (58) is moderate because the four-way actor taxonomy is reused from the same group's prior work; the genuine new contribution is the online multi-horizon task formulation plus the SHAP-grounded finding that handover-idle variability leads future performance. Resilience (55) is the main limiter: gains are inconsistent across model families and datasets (recurrent models actively degrade on BPIC2012 remaining time), and the 'dynamic operational environments' claim extrapolates beyond three BPIC logs from two domains. Weighted: 0.20*58 + 0.25*68 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*55 ≈ 61.
empirical Novelty 58 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 62 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Business process management teams monitor performance through aggregated daily indicators — average elapsed time and average remaining time across all open cases — rather than per-case predictions. Most predictive process monitoring (PPM) work targets the case level, predicting the next event or remaining time for a single instance. The handful of process-level forecasting approaches that exist (De Smedt and De Weerdt 2021; Yu et al. 2025; Pourbafrani et al. 2020) rely on historical workload counts and KPI lags but treat resources as static categorical attributes, missing the temporal coordination dynamics that drive delays — specifically, how interruptions, idle handovers, and busy handovers introduce inter-case dependencies. The gap is that resource interaction behavior, modeled as a time-varying signal, has not been integrated into process-level KPI forecasting in an online, multi-horizon setting. Key Idea: The key insight is that the four-way actor-behavior classification from Leribaux et al. (2025b) — continuation, interruption, handover-idle, handover-busy — can be converted from case-level transition labels into daily aggregate signals (count and accumulated duration per type), and those signals carry predictive information about future process performance that is orthogonal to the information in lagged KPI values. Variability in handover-idle durations and counts is the specific signal the paper identifies as most predictive via SHAP. Methodology: The pipeline has five stages. (1) Event log enrichment: each consecutive event transition within a case is labeled with one of four behavior types using formal definitions based on resource identity and whether the same resource processed another case between the two events; transitions are then aggregated into eight daily series (four behavior types × count and duration). (2) Feature engineering: for both the baseline (process-performance KPI lags only) and actor-enriched settings, the same feature template is applied to each input series — current value, lag-1 through lag-7, rolling means/standard deviations/maxima over 3/7/14-day windows, a 7-day z-score, and a peak indicator. (3) Model training: six model families are evaluated — SARIMAX, ARX-HAC, XGBoost, LightGBM, GRU with attention, and LSTM with attention. Tree-based models use a flattened feature vector; recurrent models use a 15-day lookback sequence. All models predict the change (delta) in the target KPI at horizon h, not the absolute value; the prediction is reconstructed by adding the delta to the current observed value. (4) Evaluation uses RMSE, MAE, and R², with 10 random seeds for ML/DL models and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests for significance. (5) SHAP analysis on the best-performing LightGBM model aggregates feature importance across all targets and horizons. Critical design decisions: (a) predicting changes rather than absolute values — motivated by residual forecasting practice but not ablated to verify it helps; (b) strict chronological train/test split (80/20) with online information constraint — well-justified and operationally realistic; (c) fixed lookback window of 15 days for recurrent models and 14 days for tree-based models — arbitrary and not ablated. Key Results: On BPIC2017 (the most complex dataset), the actor-enriched GRU+Attn reduces RMSE for Avg_Elapsed_Time from 114.314 to 101.672 (ΔRMSE = 12.642) and MAE from 77.763 to 67.740 at the 7-day horizon (Wilcoxon p = 0.002); for Avg_Remaining_Time, it reduces RMSE from 49.551 to 40.345 and MAE from 40.035 to 31.395 at the 7-day horizon (p = 0.001). SARIMAX on BPIC2017 shows the largest proportional gain: RMSE for Avg_Remaining_Time drops from 115.373 to 30.005 at the 7-day horizon. On BPIC2012, tree-based models benefit most from actor enrichment — LightGBM reduces RMSE for Avg_Remaining_Time from 69.223 to 52.106 at the 7-day horizon — but recurrent models degrade for Avg_Remaining_Time (LSTM+Attn increases RMSE from 28.624 to 41.521 at the 3-day horizon, Wilcoxon confirms consistent degradation). On BPIC2011, the remaining-time task is already near-deterministic from KPI history alone (R² ≈ 0.999–1.000 for all models, both baseline and actor-enriched), so actor enrichment adds negligible value; Avg_Elapsed_Time shows modest gains (GRU+Attn: RMSE 17.004 → 16.469 at 7-day). The aggregate ranking across all datasets shows LightGBM consistently among the top actor-enriched models. Actor enrichment benefits increase with forecasting horizon: the proportion of statistically significant RMSE improvements grows from the 1-day to 7-day horizon for deep learning models and from 1-day to 3-day for classical models. The paper is a preprint; results are not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The actor-behavior classification scheme is adapted from the same group's prior work (Leribaux et al. 2025a, 2025b), so it is not new here. The genuinely novel contributions are: formalizing the online, multi-horizon process-level forecasting task with the specific actor-enriched multivariate time series representation, and demonstrating empirically that handover-idle variability is a leading indicator of future process performance. The integration of the four-behavior taxonomy into a forecasting pipeline and the multi-horizon evaluation across model families is a meaningful engineering and empirical step beyond the prior offline single-step work. Rigor & Reproducibility: The statistical testing is appropriate: Wilcoxon signed-rank tests over 10 random seeds for ML/DL models, with p-values tabulated (Tables 8 and 9). Error bars (mean ± standard deviation) are reported for all ML/DL results. Classical models are deterministic and reported without seeds. The 80/20 chronological split enforces the online constraint rigorously. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/aurelieleribaux-1/actor-enriched-online-process-forecasting and all three BPIC datasets are publicly available. Key methodological gaps: (a) no ablation of individual behavior types — the paper cannot say whether HI alone drives the gains or whether all four are needed; (b) the baseline feature engineering is identical to the actor-enriched feature engineering applied to each actor-behavior series, so the comparison is clean but it is unclear whether a simpler scalar feature (e.g., daily handover count only) would achieve most of the gain; (c) the units of the KPI targets are not explicitly stated in the paper, making it impossible to assess whether RMSE reductions of ~10–17 units are operationally meaningful. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile): - Practical: The full pipeline runs from raw event logs using standard Python ML libraries; no specialized hardware is required. - Efficient: Feature construction scales linearly with log size; model training uses XGBoost/LightGBM or small GRU/LSTM architectures — runtime is not reported but is not a concern at the scale of the three test logs. - Effective: Evidence shows actor enrichment improves forecasting for complex, uncertain processes (BPIC2017, BPIC2012 elapsed time); it fails to help or actively hurts for near-deterministic tasks (BPIC2011 remaining time, BPIC2012 remaining time under recurrent models). - Scalable: Tested only on three BPIC event logs (hundreds to low thousands of cases/day at most); no stress-scale evaluation. The paper claims the approach is compatible with continuous monitoring dashboards but provides no evidence at industrial scale. The work delivers a public software artifact (GitHub repository with full preprocessing, feature extraction, and model code). Strengths: - The online information constraint is formalized precisely and enforced consistently throughout — feature construction for each fold uses only data available up to that point, a standard that much prior PPM work violates. - The formal taxonomy of actor behaviors (Definitions 5–8) produces an interpretable, computationally cheap set of signals; the SHAP analysis then closes the loop by showing these signals are predictively meaningful, not just descriptively interesting. - Testing six model families (spanning linear, tree-based, and recurrent architectures) and reporting results per dataset, target, and horizon makes the claims about model-family interaction transparent and reproducible. - The ceiling-effect diagnosis for BPIC2011 (near-perfect baseline R²) is honest and informative: the authors correctly interpret the weak enrichment gains as evidence that the task is near-deterministic, not as a failure of the method. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront five limitations: (1) the pairwise transition classification misses multi-actor collaboration structures and long-range coordination patterns; (2) sensitivity to timestamp granularity may cause misclassification when logging is coarse or asynchronous; (3) all actors are treated uniformly with no role or organizational structure; (4) activity semantics and task complexity are ignored (a short admin interruption is treated identically to a long complex one); (5) evaluation is limited to three BPIC datasets from different process domains. These are acknowledged with appropriate candor. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Three critical gaps go unacknowledged. First, no ablation of individual behavior types: the SHAP analysis shows HI variability is the most important actor-behavior feature, but the paper never tests whether dropping I, C, or HB signals leaves performance unchanged — if HI alone delivers 90% of the gain, the four-type taxonomy is overcomplicated. Second, the recurrent model degradation on BPIC2012 Avg_Remaining_Time is left unexplained beyond "actor enrichment is not universally beneficial" — the paper should investigate whether this reflects overfitting, collinearity among actor-behavior features and the KPI target, or a data-specific artifact; this is a dogs-not-barking omission for practitioners who might deploy GRU models. Third, all three datasets are from the BPIC competition, which draws from specific organizational domains (financial services, healthcare); the claim that actor enrichment is especially valuable for "dynamic operational environments" is extrapolated well beyond three datasets from two domains. Bottom Line: This is a solid, well-executed empirical paper with clean experimental design and a genuine contribution — proving that resource coordination dynamics carry predictive signal for process-level KPI forecasting, particularly at longer horizons — but the scope is narrow (three datasets, no ablation of behavior types), the gains are inconsistent across model families and datasets, and the absence of KPI units makes it hard to judge operational impact.
Search Results
#78 — Innovative (60)

MemSlides: A Hierarchical Memory Driven Agent Framework for Personalized Slide Generation with Multi-turn Local Revision (13 ▲ )

Ye Jin, Yangyang Xu, Jun Zhu, Yibo Yang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MemSlides, a hierarchical memory agent framework that separates long-term user profile memory and tool memory from session-scoped working memory to enable personalized, multi-turn slide deck authoring with scoped local revision. - Why it's important: Existing slide generation systems force users to re-specify layout, style, and content preferences in every session, and multi-turn editing regenerates full decks, overwriting already-aligned content and introducing unintended drift. - How it does it: A Plan-Act-Guard revision protocol issues an explicit execution contract (inferred scope, target slide paths, selector hints, coverage gate) restricting every edit to the minimal affected slide region, while a three-tier memory hierarchy (user profile, working, tool memory) routes preferences by their natural lifetime across jobs, sessions, and individual operations. - The good: User profile memory improves persona-alignment scores by 1.19 to 3.08 points across four dimensions over SlideTailor and DeepPresenter on 10 personas with GLM-5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro; tool memory injection raises Strict Verify After Change from 0.310 to 0.534 and reduces Time to First Correct Edit from 609.5 s to 242.5 s across 9 diagnostic matched pairs (sign-test p = 0.0195). - The missing: The profile bank is synthetically constructed from 30 controlled persona-intent entries, not from real users, and the tool-memory evaluation covers only 9 selected matched pairs with Gemini 3.1 Pro pairs restricted to a single graphic-designer persona, making distribution-level generalization fragile. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building personalized document-generation agents or multi-turn editing systems; practitioners should note the synthetic evaluation base before applying the architecture claims to real-user deployments.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (built agent framework, inference-only). Two dimensions cap the composite: Rigor (55) and Novelty (60). The three-tier memory hierarchy plus Plan-Act-Guard protocol is a coherent domain-specific composition of established components (long-term/working/tool memory, RAG), not a new mechanism. Evidence is thin — a synthetic 30-entry profile bank rather than real users, tool-memory results from only 9 hand-selected matched pairs (all Gemini pairs a single persona), low-powered sign tests, and no internal ablation isolating the hierarchy from merely having any persistent profile. Completeness (62) is helped by honest, specific limitations. Practical Impact (65) reflects released source but no packaged library/CLI and pending eval artifacts. Weighted: 0.15*60+0.15*55+0.10*58+0.15*62+0.15*55+0.30*65 = 60.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 62 Resilience 55 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Slide deck authoring is time-consuming and cognitively demanding; recent LLM-based systems (PPTAgent, DeepPresenter, SlideTailor) now produce complete, visually polished decks but treat personalization as an implicit byproduct of prompting rather than a first-class design concern. Two specific gaps motivate this work. First, user preferences in layout, content emphasis, and visual style are not retained across separate authoring jobs, so users must re-specify them in every session. Second, multi-turn revision currently regenerates full decks or large deck regions, which competes with context pressure from full-deck history, overwrites already-aligned slides, and introduces unintended drift. No existing system combines persistent user-preference memory with locality-preserving multi-turn editing for the presentation-authoring domain. Key Idea The core insight is to decompose personalization into three memory signals with distinct lifetimes: long-term user profile memory captures stable cross-job layout, style, and content preferences organized by persona and intent bucket; session working memory carries active temporary constraints from the current revision dialogue; and tool memory stores reusable execution patterns (round-scope agent lessons and operation-scope tool-chain fragments) for reliable local edits. Each signal is routed, reconciled, and consolidated through explicit lifecycle operations rather than lumped into a single undifferentiated context buffer. This separation allows the system to avoid polluting persistent preferences with transient session instructions while still preserving carryover constraints across revision rounds. Methodology MemSlides formalizes personalized presentation as a stateful multi-turn authoring task: initial generation $S_0 = G_{init}(x, P_u, \tau)$ and per-round revision $S_t = G_{edit}(S_{t-1}, x, P_u, \tau, z_t)$, where $P_u$ is persistent user profile memory, $\tau$ is an optional task-time template, and $z_t$ is the updated session state. Three memory modules operate at different granularities. User profile memory $P_u$ is organized by intent and six preference dimensions (theme, content, visual, layout, template, general); at job start, intent-matched profile buckets are reconciled with the current request via explicit override/enrich/skip logic, and at job end, stable interaction signals are consolidated back via $P_u^+ = C(P_u, H)$, which filters transient task instructions from being promoted to persistent preferences. Working memory $A_t = U(A_{t-1}, r_t)$ appends newly exposed preferences, supersedes conflicts, and retains non-conflicting items across rounds. Tool memory is two-grained: round-scope task experience (agent lessons, tool-error summaries, auto-extracted patterns, available for an entire modify job) and operation-scope tool-chain experience (reasoning-tool-observation chains segmented into reusable fragments retrieved before similar future operations). The revision protocol follows Plan-Act-Guard: Plan builds an execution contract specifying inferred scope (local/global), target slide paths, selector hints, and coverage requirements; Act applies the minimal effective edit using batch CSS updates, semantic batch styling, or snapshot-based local patches; Guard binds patches to snapshot content hashes, triggers stale-snapshot rebinding on hash mismatch, and blocks round finalization until all targeted slides are modified or explicitly confirmed compliant. Three critical design decisions: (1) separating profile from working memory prevents session-specific constraints from corrupting long-term preferences; (2) the execution contract makes scope control explicit rather than implicit in the prompt; (3) hash-bound Guard verification enforces locality as a checked state rather than a model decision. Key Results Personalization (Table 1, 0-10 scale, averaged over 3 personas per model): with GLM-5, MemSlides scores 9.00 / 8.78 / 8.56 / 8.89 on Content / Structure / Visual / Specificity versus DeepPresenter at 6.67 / 7.61 / 5.28 / 7.22 and SlideTailor at 4.44 / 4.89 / 4.00 / 3.89. With GPT-5, MemSlides leads SlideTailor on all four dimensions but DeepPresenter leads on Structure and Visual. Averaged across three model families, MemSlides improves over DeepPresenter by 1.37 / 0.53 / 1.66 / 1.19 and over SlideTailor by 2.73 / 2.95 / 2.79 / 3.08 points. General deck quality (Table 2, 1-5 scale): MemSlides with GPT-5 achieves the best Avg. (4.17 vs. 3.99 for DeepPresenter and 3.60 for SlideTailor) and with Gemini 3.1 Pro achieves the best Style (4.10) and Diversity (0.463), showing that persona-alignment gains do not trade off against general quality. Localized revision (Table 3, 9 diagnostic matched pairs): tool memory injection raises Closed-Loop Completion from 0.815 to 0.963 overall, Strict Verify After Change from 0.310 to 0.534, reduces Time to First Correct Edit from 609.5 s to 242.5 s, and lowers Core Tool Time Ratio to 0.327× (memory-injected runs use 32.7% of the core tool time of no-injection runs). Sign tests on 9 pairs: Strict Verify p = 0.0195, Core Tool Time Ratio p = 0.0195 (paired evidence); Closed-Loop Completion p = 0.3125 (directional only). All experiments are inference-only; no model training is performed. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the three-tier hierarchical memory architecture with explicit per-job reconciliation, session-level working memory evolution, and intent-aware end-of-job consolidation, combined with the Plan-Act-Guard scoped revision protocol for presentation authoring. The individual components (long-term memory, working memory, tool experience, RAG-style retrieval) are each well-established in prior agent memory work; the contribution is their domain-specific composition with explicit lifetime semantics tailored to the slide-authoring loop. Rigor & Reproducibility The persona-alignment evaluation uses three blind LLM votes per dimension under anonymous arm labels, with judges receiving only deck images and persona summary — not the original prompt, system identity, or memory condition. This design credibly isolates persistent memory effects from prompt-level task conditioning. The general-quality evaluation reuses released PPTEval-style prompts from DeepPresenter. Ablations cover each memory module: Table 1 for user profile memory, Table 3 for tool memory, and Figure 9 qualitative cases for working memory. Error bars are absent from Tables 1 and 2. Table 9 reports a non-parametric one-sided sign test over 9 matched pairs, which provides formal but low-powered statistics. The profile bank is synthetic: 30 entries constructed from 10 occupational personas × 3 intent buckets via controlled authoring interactions followed by a seeded completion step using occupation-grounded preference registries; no real-user study validates that the profile structure captures actual user preferences. The tool-memory evaluation uses 9 selected matched pairs, not a random sample, and all 3 Gemini 3.1 Pro pairs use a fixed graphic-designer persona with hard modify cases, raising selection-bias concerns. Source code is publicly available; evaluation artifacts are pending documentation and licensing finalization. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — MemSlides is a built agent framework evaluated by running inference against hosted LLM APIs. - Practical: Runs inference-only against hosted APIs (GPT-5, GLM-5, Gemini 3.1 Pro) orchestrated from a standard Linux workstation (48 CPU threads, 503 GiB RAM, NVIDIA A40 GPUs used only for image rendering); no GPU training required. - Efficient: Tool-memory injection reduces Core Tool Time Ratio to 0.327× and Time to First Correct Edit from 609.5 s to 242.5 s per modify round; profile-memory experiments consumed 49.82M prompt tokens across 49 runs (Table 5), which is high in API cost but not a per-run blocker for production use. - Effective: Persona-alignment gains are consistent and statistically meaningful across GLM-5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro; tool-memory gains on Strict Verify and Core Tool Time Ratio reach p = 0.0195. However, the evidence base is a synthetic profile bank and 9 diagnostic pairs, not a live-user deployment, so effectiveness in the wild is unvalidated. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 30 profile entries and single-session multi-turn scenarios. The paper does not characterize memory reconciliation overhead as profile size grows or report revision-round latency across longer sessions. The framework is released as source code; no packaged library or CLI is provided, and evaluation artifacts are pending release. Strengths - The memory lifecycle design (intent-matched routing at job start, session-level working-memory evolution, intent-aware consolidation at job end filtering transient instructions from persistent preferences) is technically coherent and addresses a real failure mode that prior work ignores. - The Plan-Act-Guard protocol with execution contracts and hash-bound patch verification is a concrete engineering contribution that reduces unintended scope expansion; the qualitative Figure 5 and Figure 8 comparisons illustrate the locality difference convincingly. - The blind judging protocol for persona-alignment evaluation is methodologically careful: system identity, original prompt, and memory condition are all hidden from judges, which credibly separates persistent memory effects from current-context conditioning. - Pair-level transparency in Table 8 and the paired sign test in Table 9 allow readers to assess the distribution of tool-memory effects rather than relying solely on aggregate statistics. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state (Section 6) that evidence is scoped to controlled persona-alignment judgments, diagnostic matched-pair modify settings, and qualitative working-memory cases; the profile bank and edit requests are proxies rather than real-user deployment studies; and future work should add broader human studies, randomized edit sets, and stronger memory consent, deletion, and sensitive-preference safeguards. Section A.11 acknowledges misuse risks including audience over-targeting and preference leakage from persistent profiles. These limitations are stated with specificity and confront the gaps rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The tool-memory evaluation covers 9 selected pairs (3 per model family), with all Gemini 3.1 Pro pairs using a single graphic-designer persona under hard modify conditions. This is too few pairs to estimate the distribution-level benefit or to characterize failure modes; the paper's own framing as a "diagnostic" setting is honest, but the aggregate Table 3 numbers will be read as representative by most readers. - The profile bank's synthetic construction via seeded completion means the evaluation tests whether MemSlides correctly uses a pre-built profile, not whether its consolidation mechanism correctly learns stable preferences from real noisy user feedback. The consolidation operator $C(P_u, H)$ is described formalism but never ablated in isolation. - Working-memory evidence is entirely qualitative (2 cases, Figure 9). No quantitative metric evaluates carryover correctness, incorrect persistence of dropped preferences, or preference supersession accuracy across rounds. - All three baselines are external systems evaluated under the same judge protocol, but no internal ablation compares MemSlides variants (e.g., profile memory only vs. profile + working memory vs. full system) on the same decks, making it impossible to attribute the persona-alignment gain specifically to the hierarchical design versus simply having any persistent profile signal. - Session-level latency from the user perspective is not reported; the paper characterizes per-operation tool time but not the wall-clock cost of a full multi-round authoring session including memory retrieval and consolidation. Bottom Line This preprint offers a technically grounded memory architecture for personalized presentation agents that researchers in multi-session document generation should read, but practitioners should treat the quantitative claims cautiously until the evaluation is extended beyond a synthetic profile bank and 9 diagnostic matched pairs to real-user studies at distribution scale.
Recommended 2606.17162 PDF
#79 — Innovative (60)

OpenAnt: LLM-Powered Vulnerability Discovery Through Code Decomposition, Adversarial Verification, and Dynamic Testing

N Korda, G Evron · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OpenAnt, an open-source six-stage pipeline that combines static reachability filtering, LLM-based vulnerability detection, adversarial attacker simulation, and automated exploit generation in ephemeral Docker containers to discover exploitable vulnerabilities in real-world codebases at repository scale. - Why it's important: Traditional SAST tools produce false positive rates up to 40% and only ~20% of developers actively use them; existing LLM-based analyzers reduce false positives marginally but cannot confirm whether a flagged pattern is actually exploitable by a remote attacker, leaving practitioners with unverified findings. - How it does it: Static call-graph reachability filtering eliminates 96.4% of functions before any LLM call, then two sequential LLM stages (constrained attacker simulation, automated exploit generation and execution) independently confirm whether each candidate is exploitable in practice. - The good: 144 of 190 adversarially-confirmed findings (75.8%) were independently validated by automatically-generated exploits executing inside sandboxed containers across 8 real-world projects spanning 64,132 functions and 6 programming languages, providing concrete exploit evidence rather than pattern matches. - The missing: No head-to-head comparison against any existing tool (Semgrep, CodeQL, or other LLM analyzers) on the same 8 projects, and no recall measurement is possible because no ground truth for these repositories exists. - Read it or not: Security researchers and practitioners building LLM-driven analysis pipelines should read it; the closed-loop exploit-validation design and honest treatment of benchmark contamination are the most developed public treatment of this architecture, but the absence of baselines limits how much the numbers can be trusted in isolation.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Impact (72) carries the score: an open-source Apache-2.0 pipeline producing 144 automatically-generated, container-executed exploits is concrete deployable evidence no other open LLM security analyzer offers, and the 16x cost reduction via reachability filtering is practical. Novelty (65) reflects the closed-loop adversarial-verification + ephemeral exploit-execution architecture. The composite is pulled down by Rigor (50) and Completeness/Resilience (50 each): no baseline comparison against Semgrep/CodeQL on the same 8 projects, undefined recall, single-run with no variance, and 44.7% of confirmed findings concentrated in one project (Flowise). The missing responsible-disclosure process is a further credibility gap. Weighted: 0.15*65 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*60 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*50 + 0.30*72 = 60.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 50 Resilience 50 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Software security teams relying on Static Application Security Testing (SAST) tools face a well-documented false positive problem: large-scale evaluations report false positive rates from single digits to over 40% depending on configuration, and roughly 80% of developers do not actively use SAST in their workflows. Alert fatigue caused by irrelevant warnings reduces trust and increases the probability that real vulnerabilities are ignored during triage. LLM-based analyzers can reason semantically about data flows and attacker-controlled input rather than matching syntactic patterns, but applying them at repository scale introduces two unsolved problems: the lost-in-the-middle degradation that occurs when relevant code appears in the middle of very long contexts, and the inability to determine whether a theoretically flagged vulnerability is practically exploitable by a remote attacker with realistic constraints. The gap OpenAnt addresses is this last-mile problem: distinguishing exploitable vulnerabilities from theoretical ones at repository scale, with cost low enough for routine use. Key Idea The core insight is that two sequential verification stages with fundamentally different mechanisms — adversarial attacker simulation (an LLM models a constrained remote attacker attempting multiple exploitation paths, with a victim-harm requirement that filters self-impact scenarios) followed by automated exploit generation and execution (an LLM generates Dockerized test environments that are run against real code) — can empirically distinguish exploitable vulnerabilities from false positives, closing the loop that pure static analysis cannot close.
