The practical enabler is aggressive static reachability filtering before any LLM call, which makes the cost of LLM reasoning tractable by focusing analysis on the ~4% of code reachable from external entry points. Methodology OpenAnt implements a six-stage pipeline. Stage 1 parses repositories using AST libraries (native Python/Go AST, ts-morph for JavaScript/TypeScript, tree-sitter for C/C++, Ruby, PHP) and builds a bidirectional call graph. Stage 2 transforms extracted functions into self-contained analysis units by inlining transitive dependencies up to three levels deep, then performs BFS from identified entry points (HTTP handlers, CLI argument processors, WebSocket handlers, file-reading interfaces) to retain only externally reachable functions; this reduces 64,132 total functions to 2,281 (96.4% reduction). Stage 3 uses a Claude Sonnet 4 agent with tool-assisted codebase navigation to classify each unit as Exploitable / Vulnerable-internal / Security-control / Neutral; only Exploitable units proceed. Stage 4 uses Claude Opus 4 with a language-agnostic prompt to answer three questions per unit (code behavior, input origin, security risk) and produces one of five findings: vulnerable / bypassable / inconclusive / protected / safe. Stage 5 runs adversarial verification using Claude Opus 4: the model simulates a remote attacker constrained to browser-based interaction with no server-side access or administrative credentials, must attempt multiple exploitation strategies, and must identify a victim other than the attacker (self-impact scenarios are rejected). Stage 6 runs dynamic verification: Claude Sonnet 4 generates a Dockerfile, test script, requirements file, and optional Docker Compose configuration per finding; the environment is executed in an isolated container with read-only filesystem, 512 MB memory, single CPU, no privilege escalation, and a 120-second timeout; outputs are classified as CONFIRMED/NOT_REPRODUCED/BLOCKED/INCONCLUSIVE/ERROR with up to three retries on error. All generated artifacts are destroyed after execution. Temperature is set to 0 at all stages. Key Results Across 8 real-world repositories (OpenSSL, Flowise, eShopOnWeb, n8n, WordPress, object-browser, paperless-ngx, Rails) spanning 6 languages and 64,132 total functions: reachability filtering produced 2,281 reachable units (96.4% reduction); exposure classification produced 586 exploitable units; vulnerability detection flagged 376; adversarial verification confirmed 190 (49.5% elimination rate); dynamic testing confirmed 144 (75.8% confirmation rate). Confirmed findings span 30+ vulnerability classes including IDOR/Missing Authorization (29 confirmed, 21 dynamically verified), Mass Assignment/Privilege Escalation (26/20), SSRF (25/18), Path Traversal (18/16), XSS (17/10), SQL/NoSQL Injection (11/8). Command Injection achieved 100% dynamic confirmation; Path Traversal 88.9%; Authentication Bypass 83.3%. Flowise produced 85 of 190 confirmed findings (44.7% of total), while Rails produced 2 and OpenSSL produced 3. Total pipeline cost was $1,461.25 across all 8 projects; the estimated cost without reachability filtering is $23,700 (a 16x reduction). Stage 3 exposure classification accounts for 72.9% of total cost. No baselines against existing SAST tools or other LLM analyzers are reported; the evaluation presents no recall measurement. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The integrated closed-loop architecture combining reachability-filtered unit generation, adversarial constrained-attacker simulation with a victim-harm requirement, and automated ephemeral exploit generation/execution is genuinely novel as an open-source system. Individual components — call-graph analysis, LLM security reasoning, exploit generation — exist separately in prior work; the specific adversarial verification design (multi-path attacker simulation under realistic constraints, victim requirement) is a new filtering mechanism not present in Semgrep, CodeQL, Google's Big Sleep, PentestGPT, or Anthropic/OpenAI's reported internal tools. The real-world evaluation strategy that avoids synthetic benchmarks due to contamination concerns is also a methodological contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is released as open source at https://github.com/knostic/OpenAnt under the Apache 2.0 license. No ablation studies are presented; the paper does not evaluate what fraction of findings would survive with or without the adversarial verification stage beyond reporting the 49.5% elimination rate as a net result. No comparison against any external tool on the same projects is provided, making it impossible to determine whether OpenAnt finds more or fewer vulnerabilities than a free Semgrep scan. No repeated runs or variance measurements are reported; temperature=0 reduces but does not eliminate stochastic variation. The deliberate avoidance of synthetic benchmarks (motivated by the benchmark contamination concern, well-cited at refs 14-17) is intellectually honest but means recall is entirely undefined. The paper does not state whether discovered vulnerabilities were disclosed to project maintainers or CVEs filed, which is a significant omission given that 144 working exploits were generated against actively-used open-source projects. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: Runs on standard compute with Docker and Claude API access; cost is $6–$443 per project depending on repository size, and the open-source release makes the system redeployable. - Efficient: Reachability filtering reduces LLM API cost by ~16x (from ~$23,700 to $1,461.25 total); however Stage 3 agentic exploration still dominates at 72.9% of cost, and large repositories like OpenSSL cost $442 individually, suggesting cost scales super-linearly with repository complexity. - Effective: 144 confirmed exploits with working generated test environments constitute concrete evidence of effectiveness for input-driven vulnerability classes; the system explicitly does not target logic bugs, specification violations, or memory-safety issues requiring runtime instrumentation. - Scalable: Tested on repositories up to 18,500 functions (Grafana, mentioned in the filtering example but not in the main evaluation); the largest evaluated project is n8n at 16,009 functions; repositories with hundreds of thousands of functions are untested, and the per-project cost variation suggests analysis cost for very large monorepos may be prohibitive without incremental analysis. Strengths - The closed-loop exploit validation stage (Stage 6) provides a class of evidence that no other open-source LLM security analyzer currently produces: 144 working, automatically-generated exploits executed in sandboxed containers, not inferences from static patterns. - The adversarial verification design (multi-path attacker simulation, victim-harm requirement, realistic attacker constraints) is a principled mechanism for false positive elimination that reduces flagged findings by 49.5% before the expensive dynamic testing stage, and the victim requirement specifically eliminates a well-known category of SAST false positives (self-impact scenarios). - The benchmark contamination analysis in Section 3 is the most careful treatment of this methodological problem among LLM security papers reviewed: the paper identifies specific structural artifacts in Juliet (CWE-named filenames) and OWASP Benchmark that enable shortcut learning, and cites contamination rate data (4.9x higher pass rates on leaked vs. non-leaked samples) to justify the evaluation strategy. - The single language-agnostic prompt across 6 programming languages, validated by finding vulnerabilities in all 6, demonstrates that semantic taint-flow reasoning genuinely generalizes across language ecosystems — a non-obvious result that has practical implications for multi-language organizations. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge four limitations: (1) LLM capability dependence, particularly for subtle semantic vulnerabilities and domain-specific invariants, noting that performance follows underlying model quality; (2) cost scalability, where large repositories cost several hundred dollars each and organizations must prioritize targets; (3) dynamic verification coverage, where race conditions, distributed system exploits, and complex multi-service scenarios cannot be reproduced in isolated Docker containers; (4) absence of formal guarantees, since failure to reproduce dynamically does not prove a vulnerability is impossible. A fifth limitation — context window constraints for very large functions or deep call chains — is also acknowledged. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No baseline comparison: the paper evaluates OpenAnt in isolation. A single Semgrep or CodeQL run on the same 8 projects costs nothing and would immediately reveal whether OpenAnt's 190 confirmed findings represent improvement over existing free tools. The omission makes the result impossible to contextualize competitively. - Flowise concentration: 85 of 190 confirmed findings (44.7%) come from a single project, Flowise. This concentration suggests OpenAnt may be most effective on workflow automation platforms with loose authorization patterns rather than demonstrating uniform effectiveness across diverse application types. The diversity claim across 8 projects requires qualification. - Responsible disclosure gap: 144 working exploits were generated against actively-maintained open-source projects with 10,000+ GitHub stars each. The paper does not mention CVE filing, maintainer notification, or any coordinated disclosure process. This is a significant omission for a systems security paper. - The adversarial verification false negative rate is unknown: the paper reports that adversarial verification eliminates 49.5% of Stage 4 findings, but does not investigate what fraction of the eliminated 186 findings were genuine vulnerabilities that the attacker simulation failed to exploit. If the attacker constraints are too restrictive, the stage could systematically eliminate real vulnerabilities. - Single-run evaluation with no variance: despite temperature=0, LLM outputs vary; a single run provides no confidence interval on the 190/144 finding counts. Prior LLM security papers report 5–15% run-to-run variance in detection outcomes. Bottom Line Practitioners designing LLM-based security analysis pipelines will find the closed-loop exploit validation architecture and adversarial verification design in OpenAnt worth studying closely, but the absence of any baseline comparison against existing tools and the undefined recall make it impossible to assess where this system sits relative to the alternatives it claims to improve upon.
Search Results 2606.19149 PDF
#80 — Innovative (60)

Agentic Automated Program Repair: Debugger Agents for C to Rust Translation: Optimizing LLM Context for Industrial-Scale Legacy Code Migration

K Cikota · diva-portal.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a controlled empirical comparison of four LLM prompting architectures for Automated Program Repair (APR) of C-to-Rust compile-time errors, finding that raw terminal output outperforms structured JSON, Chain-of-Thought, and Reflexion-based memory. - Why it's important: Industrial C-to-Rust migration pipelines stall on Rust's borrow checker and lifetime constraints; the APR debugging loop is the bottleneck, and choosing the wrong prompt architecture wastes 2-3x compute while resolving fewer errors. - How it does it: A Python orchestrator applies four isolated, guardrail-protected debugging loops — each seeing identical baseline Rust code — to six open-source C repositories across up to 10 `cargo check` iterations per file, using GPT-4o throughout. - The good: A Friedman non-parametric test (p=0.007) confirms the strategy ranking is statistically significant, and the raw Baseline strategy achieved the only full compilation success (http-parser, 0 residual errors) while using the fewest tokens. - The missing: The experiment evaluates compile success only — no runtime correctness, functional equivalence, or memory-footprint validation — so a "successful" patch may silently break the program's behavior; this is a large unclosed gap. - Read it or not: Read it if you are building or optimizing C-to-Rust or similar compile-time migration pipelines and want an empirically grounded argument against adding Reflexion or CoT overhead; skip it if you need peer-reviewed confidence or results at monolithic scale.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The two dimensions carrying the composite are Non-Obviousness (72) and Rigor (58): the 'Complexity Paradox' finding inverts the prevailing assumption that Chain-of-Thought and Reflexion memory help LLM debugging, and the design isolates prompting strategy as the sole variable with a correct Friedman test (p=0.007) and honest reporting that Bonferroni-corrected pairwise significance is unachievable at N=5. Novelty (62) is a genuine empirical finding rather than method invention, and code is publicly released. Completeness (60) and Resilience (55) are pulled down by the compile-success-only construct, which leaves runtime functional equivalence entirely unvalidated, plus single-model (GPT-4o), single-run (temp=1.0, no error bars), N=6 scope and master's-thesis non-peer-reviewed status. Practical Impact (55) reflects a released, directly actionable pipeline for C-to-Rust migration teams. Weighted: 0.20*62 + 0.25*58 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*55 = 60.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Legacy C and C++ codebases underpin critical infrastructure that is chronically vulnerable to memory-safety exploits. Rust's ownership and borrow-checker model eliminates those vulnerabilities at compile time, but LLM-generated C-to-Rust translations almost never compile on the first attempt. The bottleneck has therefore shifted to iterative APR: feeding `rustc` error output back to an LLM repair agent until compilation succeeds. No established best practice exists for how to structure the context window given to the LLM debugger. Existing APR literature focuses on runtime logic errors in dynamically typed languages (Python, Java) using test-suite feedback — a categorically different environment from the deterministic, static output of `rustc`. Engineers building industrial translation pipelines (e.g., Softwerk AB's 5-million-line Delphi-to-C# project) lack quantitative guidance on whether investing in Chain-of-Thought reasoning or Reflexion memory actually helps, or merely burns tokens. Key Idea: The Rust compiler is itself an unambiguous root-cause analyzer — its `note:` and `help:` annotations already contain the correct fix suggestion in many cases. Interposing an LLM to re-diagnose errors before patching (Chain-of-Thought) or to maintain a memory of failed attempts (Reflexion) dilutes the compiler's deterministic signal with probabilistic text and constrains the model's search space to paths it has already ruled out. The paper calls this the "Complexity Paradox": in strictly rule-based environments with few valid syntactic solutions per error, agentic reasoning wrappers designed for open-ended ambiguity become cognitive straitjackets. Methodology: The experiment runs a two-phase Python pipeline (run_pilot.py). Phase 1 performs a single zero-shot C-to-Rust translation using GPT-4o and validates it through a Translation Fidelity Gate (≥95% symbol coverage). Phase 2 clones the baseline four times and runs each strategy's repair loop independently against the identical broken Rust code, capped at 10 `cargo check` iterations. The four strategies are: S1 Baseline (raw terminal output, capped at 8,000 characters); S2 Structured (deserialized JSON diagnostics, up to 20 errors, stripped of `note:` and `help:` annotations); S3 Diagnose-then-Patch (two separate GPT-4o calls — a `diagnose_agent` produces ROOT CAUSE / TARGET SYMBOLS / FIX PLAN text, which a `repair_agent` then acts on); S4 Memory/Reflexion (S3 plus a growing `MemoryEntry` buffer prepended to both calls, explicitly instructing the model to avoid previously failed paths). A guardrail mechanism rejects patches that shrink LOC below 70%, drop function count below 80%, introduce `unimplemented!()` stubs, or increase total compiler errors by more than 3. An error priority heuristic sorts macro, FFI, symbol, syntax, and type errors before feeding the top candidate to the LLM. Six open-source C repositories serve as datasets: sds (1,300 LOC), http-parser (2,575 LOC), tinyexpr (734 LOC), linenoise (1,762 LOC), kilo (1,308 LOC, 91.4% fidelity, override), and cJSON (4,672 LOC, 40.4% fidelity, override). Token Efficiency is defined as (E_baseline − E_final) / (total prompt + completion tokens / 1,000). Statistical testing uses the Friedman test (omnibus) and pairwise Wilcoxon signed-rank tests with Bonferroni correction. Key Results: Strategy 1 (Baseline) was the best-performing strategy on 4 of 6 datasets. http-parser achieved 100% compile success (57 → 0 errors in 7 attempts, 81,011 total tokens, efficiency 0.704). On linenoise, S1 reduced 166 errors to 3 (98.2%) with a single-step drop from 145 to 13 errors on Attempt 2. On sds, S1 fixed 69.3% (75 → 23) versus S4's 24.0% (75 → 57), despite S4 consuming 385,593 tokens versus S1's 195,627. On tinyexpr, S1 fixed 89.7% (29 → 3) versus S4's 13.8% (29 → 25). Strategy 2 (Structured JSON) marginally outperformed Baseline only on kilo (91.9% vs 85.5%) and tied on linenoise (efficiency 0.749 vs 0.745). Strategy 4 (Memory/Reflexion) ranked last on every dataset where progress was made, at consistently 2-3x token cost. cJSON produced a 0% repair rate across all four strategies (76 errors, flat trajectory), because 60% of function bodies were absent in the baseline translation. The Friedman test confirmed strategy differences were statistically significant for both error reduction (p=0.007) and token efficiency (p=0.050). Post-hoc Wilcoxon pairwise tests did not achieve significance under Bonferroni correction due to the N=5 effective sample size (cJSON's universal flat line reduced the mathematical p-value floor to 0.0625). Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING, NEW METHOD (pipeline design). The core novel contribution is the empirical inversion of the prevailing assumption that CoT and memory architectures improve LLM-driven debugging. The "Complexity Paradox" framing — that agentic reasoning overhead hurts in deterministic environments — is a genuine observation backed by statistically significant data, not a straightforward combination of known techniques applied to a new setting. The guardrail mechanism and Translation Fidelity Gate are engineering contributions with practical value but are not algorithmically novel. Rigor & Reproducibility: The experimental design appropriately isolates prompting strategy as the sole independent variable and holds model (GPT-4o), temperature (1.0), top_p (1.0), and iteration budget (10) constant. The Friedman omnibus test is appropriate for N=6 non-parametric data; Wilcoxon pairwise tests with Bonferroni correction are correctly applied, and the authors honestly report that pairwise significance is mathematically unachievable at N=5. Ablations within each strategy (e.g., effect of the guardrails, effect of the fidelity gate threshold) are absent. No error bars or repeated runs are reported — each strategy runs once per dataset, so LLM stochasticity at temperature=1.0 is an uncontrolled source of variance the authors acknowledge but do not quantify. Code and telemetry are publicly released at github.com/kemalcikota113/master-thesis. This is a master's thesis, not a peer-reviewed publication; results and vendor API costs are self-reported. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile): - Practical: The pipeline requires only a Python environment, the `rustc`/`cargo` toolchain, and OpenAI API access — all commercially available. - Efficient: S1 Baseline consumed 81,011–218,715 tokens per dataset across 10 attempts; S4 consumed up to 411,116 tokens for the same budget at lower output quality. At GPT-4o pricing, S4 imposes a 2-3x cost penalty over S1 with inferior results. - Effective: The pipeline resolves syntactic and compile-time semantic errors demonstrably well on structurally complete translations (up to 98.2% error reduction, one full compilation success); it provides zero recovery from structurally incomplete translations (cJSON: 0%). - Scalable: Not tested. The study covers 700–4,672 LOC self-contained repositories. The authors explicitly warn that monolithic, multi-file architectures with deep dependency trees would challenge the raw terminal output approach once `rustc` logs exceed the LLM's context window — the very scenario where Structured JSON might recover relevance. Strengths: - The decoupled experimental design — translating once and forking four independent repair loops from the same baseline — is methodologically sound and prevents translation quality from confounding strategy comparisons. - The Translation Fidelity Gate and the deliberate inclusion of two gate-failing datasets (kilo and cJSON) yield a concrete, empirically validated concept: the "Fidelity Ceiling," beyond which no prompt strategy can recover. - The token efficiency metric (errors fixed per 1,000 tokens) is a practically meaningful measure aligned with real deployment cost concerns. - The guardrail mechanism (LOC retention, function count, placeholder detection, regression tolerance) is a well-engineered safeguard against superficial compile satisfaction via code deletion — a known failure mode the authors correctly anticipated. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors candidly identify four validity threats: (1) model stochasticity at temperature=1.0 produces non-deterministic patches even for identical prompts; (2) construct validity is bounded to syntactic compilation — a passing `cargo check` does not guarantee runtime functional equivalence; (3) external validity is bounded to medium-sized, self-contained repositories (700–4,600 LOC); (4) statistical power is constrained by N=6 datasets, making Bonferroni-corrected pairwise significance mathematically impossible. These are confronted directly, which is creditable. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The experiment uses a single model (GPT-4o) and a single fixed translation agent, meaning it is unknown whether the Complexity Paradox holds for models with different training distributions (e.g., Codestral, Claude 3.5, DeepSeek-Coder). The raw Baseline feeds up to 8,000 characters of terminal output with no validation that this fits within the model's effective attention range for code tasks; the truncation behavior is not analyzed. The cJSON failure is correctly interpreted as a Fidelity Ceiling effect, but the threshold choice (95%) is arbitrary and not ablated — the paper does not show what happens at 80% or 70% thresholds for the remaining datasets. Patch functional correctness is entirely unvalidated: the winning S1 patches include cases where the LLM cloned data structures in memory to satisfy the borrow checker, which may silently change runtime performance or memory semantics. No comparison with non-LLM baselines (e.g., C2Rust transpiler output as a starting point, or targeted heuristics for common error codes like E0515) is provided. Bottom Line: This thesis delivers a credible, practically actionable finding — raw `rustc` terminal output beats structured and agentic prompting wrappers for compile-time APR — but its status as a single-model, N=6, non-peer-reviewed master's project means practitioners should treat it as a strong directional signal, not a settled result.
Search Results
#81 — Innovative (60)

Beyond Domains: Reusing Web Skills via Transferable Interaction Patterns

S He, Y Cui, F Wu, X Ma, J Lu, Y Li, B Ding… · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SKILLMIGRATOR, a web agent that stores induced skills as transferable interaction patterns (TIPs) — each pairing a validated skill with a structural accessibility-tree skeleton — and retrieves them via a combined text-plus-layout similarity score to enable cross-domain skill reuse beyond both the same website and the same application domain. - Why it's important: LLM-based web agents must invoke a policy LLM for every primitive browser action, making long-horizon tasks expensive; reducing these calls without sacrificing task success rate directly cuts latency and deployment cost at scale. - How it does it: At induction time each skill is paired with a cleaned accessibility-tree skeleton; at retrieval time the agent scores candidates by combining frozen sentence-encoder cosine similarity (text signal) with APTED tree edit distance (layout signal), then gates on a threshold before grounding abstract slot references to live DOM elements via Hungarian assignment. - The good: The ablation isolates the layout signal as the dominant contributor: removing it drops WebArena success rate 6.5 points (45.7% to 39.2%), nearly erasing the gain over the no-skill ReAct baseline, which credibly validates the core design claim. - The missing: SKILLMIGRATOR's WebArena task success rate (45.7%) sits 3.6 points below PolySkill (49.3%), yet the paper repeatedly describes this as "matched success rate"; no cost-per-successful-task analysis is provided to justify whether the 8.5% call reduction offsets this accuracy gap in practice. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and engineers building cross-site web automation who want a concrete, reproducible technique for layout-conditioned skill retrieval; less relevant for those primarily targeting maximum task success rate rather than inference efficiency.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (62) and Resilience (52) pull the composite down: results average three seeds and the layout-signal ablation is clean, but no code is released, the GPT-4.1 judge has ~15% human disagreement yet tenths-of-a-percent SR gaps are reported as distinct, and the repeated 'matched success rate' framing collapses under scrutiny given the real 3.6-point WebArena shortfall versus PolySkill (45.7% vs 49.3%). Novelty (65) and Non-Obviousness (70) are the strengths. The layout-conditioned tree-edit-distance retrieval key is a non-obvious, well-isolated contribution. Practical Impact (45) is held back by no released artifact and only an 8-10% LLM-call reduction. Composite: 0.20*65 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*52 + 0.10*45 = 60.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 58 Resilience 52 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM-based web agents in the ReAct paradigm emit one policy LLM call per primitive browser action (fill, click, press, scroll), so trajectory cost grows linearly with task length. Skill-reuse systems such as ASI, SkillWeaver, and PolySkill compress repeated action sequences into callable routines, but retrieve them using semantic signals: instruction text similarity, workflow summaries, or site metadata. This retrieval strategy fails cross-domain because two tasks phrased differently may share the same DOM-level interaction pattern (fill labeled inputs, click submit), while two textually similar tasks may require completely different control flow. Figure 2 in the paper quantifies this directly: on a 60-task Mind2Web subset evaluated with GPT-4.1, skill reuse rates for ASI, SkillWeaver, and PolySkill drop sharply as the evaluation moves from cross-task to cross-website to cross-domain splits, while average step counts rise. The gap is the absence of a structural retrieval signal capable of identifying equivalent interaction patterns across sites with different layouts, vocabularies, and element labels. Key Idea The core technical insight is to index each induced skill not only by its semantic intent but also by a structural sketch of the accessibility tree at the moment the skill was validated. This sketch — a cleaned tree carrying semantic role and element name per node, with all live element references stripped — captures the page's interaction topology in a form that is portable across sites. At retrieval time, layout similarity (computed as 1 minus the normalized APTED tree edit distance between the stored skeleton and the current page's accessibility tree) is combined with semantic text similarity to produce a score that can recognize a "fill-and-submit" pattern on a forum, an e-commerce admin, and a developer tool as the same skill, even when field labels, button text, and DOM structure differ in surface form. Methodology Skill induction reuses the ASI and PolySkill pipelines without modification; SKILLMIGRATOR's contribution is the storage format and retrieval mechanism. Each skill k is stored as a TIP tuple (ι_k, σ_k, Φ_k, τ_k): a one-sentence natural-language intent, an operation template σ drawn from a fixed set mined by clustering training trajectories on action shape (e.g., fill-and-submit, search, click-by-text), a slot schema Φ of abstract field descriptors each with a descriptor string and a synonym pool mined from co-clustered trajectories, and the cleaned accessibility-tree skeleton τ at induction time. No live element references (ref=eN identifiers) are stored. Retrieval scores each candidate skill k against the current subtask s_i and last observation ō_{i-1} as: score(k, s_i, ō_{i-1}) = α · cos(e(s_i ‖ ρ(ō_{i-1})), e(δ_k)) + (1-α) · L(k, ō_{i-1}), where ρ(ō_{i-1}) is a one-line page summary listing the heading, principal form controls, and primary button labels; δ_k is a rich descriptor concatenating intent with all slot descriptors and synonyms; e(·) is frozen sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2; and L uses APTED tree edit distance. α=0.6 and β=0.20 are tuned on a held-out 10% of training trajectories. The top-1 skill passes the gate if score ≥ β; otherwise the agent falls back to primitive ReAct. Execution proceeds in two stages. Stage A (value parse) runs Hungarian assignment on the slot schema Φ_{k*} against an instantiation dict D extracted from the task spec by the planner, with a literal-match bonus for verbatim dict entries; unbound slots fall back to span extraction from instruction text using a candidate cue grammar. Stage B (slot-to-ref binding) scores every interactive node in the current observation against each slot's descriptor and synonym set using embedding cosine similarity, restricts to compatible semantic roles (textbox-like for fillable slots), and runs a second Hungarian solve to bind abstract slots to live element references. Unbound required slots escalate to ReAct. Each operation template has a fixed deterministic plan that emits fill/click/press primitives once Φ_k is bound, eliminating LLM calls for these steps. Two critical design decisions: (1) Storing tree skeletons stripped of live refs makes skills genuinely portable — this is well-justified by the cross-domain motivation. (2) The synonym pool per slot is mined offline from co-clustered training trajectories — this is justified by the paraphrase coverage ablation (removing synonyms costs 1.3 SR points on WebArena), though the mining procedure is not described in detail. Key Results On WebArena (812 executable tasks, 6 domains): SKILLMIGRATOR achieves 45.7% task success rate (SR) with average LLM-action count N̄=5.4, versus PolySkill at 49.3% SR and N̄=5.9. This is a 3.6-point SR reduction for an 8.5% call count reduction. ReAct (no skills) achieves 38.5% SR at N̄=6.5 as the baseline. On Mind2Web cross-domain split with GPT-4.1 judge (63.4% human agreement rate): SKILLMIGRATOR (+Update) achieves 63.0% SR at N̄=6.2 versus PolySkill (+Update) at 63.4% SR and N̄=6.9, an effectively matched SR with 10.1% fewer LLM calls. Cross-domain skill reuse reaches 35.4% for SKILLMIGRATOR (+Update) versus 31.0% for PolySkill (+Update), with a smaller library (49 vs. 56 skills). The orthogonality experiment shows SKILLMIGRATOR + PolySkill achieves N̄=5.3 at 47.7% SR — lower N̄ than either standalone — demonstrating the retrieval layer composes cleanly with existing libraries. Ablations (Table 4b) show the layout signal alone accounts for the dominant SR effect; the gate trades N̄ against SR as a precision/recall knob; Claude-4 as backbone raises SR to 54.7% at N̄=5.1 versus 45.7% at N̄=5.4 for GPT-4.1, with the gain attributed to the backbone rather than the retrieval design. Results are means over three random seeds. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contribution is the TIP representation — pairing skills with accessibility-tree structural sketches — and the combined text+layout scoring using tree edit distance for cross-domain retrieval. The slot schema with synonym pools and the two-stage Hungarian grounding adapt well-established cross-domain slot-filling techniques (Liu et al. 2020, Wang et al. 2021) to the web-agent setting. The claim of being the first to study reusable web skills across sites beyond a single domain is credible given the related work landscape as of June 2026. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations isolate the layout signal, synonym pool, and gate individually on both WebArena and Mind2Web cross-domain (Table 4b), making the component contributions legible. Results are averaged over three random seeds (stated in Appendix B). The GPT-4.1 judge for Mind2Web has ~85% agreement with human judgments, and the paper acknowledges this; small SR differences (e.g., 63.0% vs. 63.4%) are within plausible judge noise, yet the paper reports these as distinct. Hyperparameters are tuned on a held-out 10% of training trajectories with no test-set overlap — methodologically sound. Skill induction uses identical pipelines to ASI and PolySkill, isolating the retrieval and grounding contribution. No code or model weights are publicly released; the Playwright MCP framework is external but SKILLMIGRATOR-specific code has no stated repository. The frozen sentence encoder is not fine-tuned on SKILLMIGRATOR data, which avoids Goodhart's Law concerns for the text retrieval signal. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (a built system evaluated on standard web-agent benchmarks). - Practical: Runs on commercially available hardware; the retrieval stack uses frozen sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (22M parameters) and APTED, both CPU-feasible; the LLM backbone is an external API call (GPT-4.1 or Claude-4). - Efficient: Reduces average LLM-action count by 8-10% versus prior state-of-the-art; the additional retrieval cost per step (embedding computation plus APTED tree edit distance) is not quantified in wall-clock terms but is negligible relative to LLM latency. - Effective: On Mind2Web cross-domain the evidence supports matched SR with measurably fewer calls; on WebArena the 3.6-point SR gap versus PolySkill is real and the paper's "matched success rate" framing is an overstatement. - Scalable: Tested on up to 812 tasks across 137 websites; library sizes remain 31-71 skills. Performance at 10x-100x website scale is not evaluated and not claimed. No software artifact is released; the system delivers results in paper form only. Strengths - The layout signal via tree edit distance is a principled, non-obvious retrieval key for cross-domain transfer, and the ablation provides direct evidence for its necessity (6.5-point SR collapse without it on WebArena). - The TIP tuple design — stripping live element refs and storing only role+name-labelled skeletons — solves the portability problem cleanly without requiring site-specific adaptation at induction time. - The orthogonality experiment (Section 4.3) demonstrates that SKILLMIGRATOR behaves as a retrieval layer that composes with existing skill-induction systems rather than competing with them, a useful architectural property for adoption. - Variable-arity handling through per-slot Hungarian assignment means a three-slot instantiation dict correctly fills only the three matching textboxes on a five-field form, avoiding spurious fills that would inflate action counts or corrupt submissions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors state in Appendix D that layout-conditioned retrieval weakens on visually isomorphic pages whose control flow diverges, where multimodal cues (visual features) would be a natural complement to the text-only setting. Operation templates are mined from observed action shapes, so modalities outside this distribution (drag-and-drop, modal dialogs) fall back to ReAct. They include a brief ethical statement that reduced automation cost may scale automated web actions and that deployments should respect host-site rate limits and access controls. These limitations are stated with reasonable directness rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 3.6-point SR shortfall on WebArena versus PolySkill (49.3% vs. 45.7%) is repeatedly characterized as "matched success rate" without a cost-per-successful-task calculation; for a deployment where task completion matters more than LLM call count, the trade-off may be unfavorable and the framing obscures this. - Skill induction is entirely borrowed from ASI and PolySkill; it is unknown whether TIPs are compatible with alternative or future induction methods, limiting the generality of the storage format claim. - The sentence encoder (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) is a 2019-era model with 22M parameters; no ablation compares against larger or more recent encoders, leaving open whether the text signal is the binding constraint for the α sensitivity plateau seen above α=0.6. - Mind2Web SR differences at the tenths-of-a-percent level (e.g., 63.0% vs. 63.4%) are reported as meaningfully distinct despite the GPT-4.1 judge having ~15% disagreement with humans; statistical significance tests are absent. - The synonym pool mining procedure and the operation template clustering are not described in sufficient detail for independent reproduction. Bottom Line A practitioner building cost-sensitive, cross-site LLM web automation will find the TIP retrieval design and layout-signal ablation directly actionable; a practitioner who needs maximum task success rate should weight the 3.6-point WebArena gap against PolySkill carefully before adopting this approach. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.17645v1, 16 Jun 2026); results are not peer-reviewed.
Citations 2606.17645 PDF
#82 — Innovative (60)

PSyGenTAB: A Privacy-Preserving Framework for Synthetic Clinical Tabular Data Generation via Constrained Optimization

A Ilaty, H Shirazi, M Chitale, K Hegde, D Ramesh… · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PSyGenTAB, a model-agnostic wrapper that enforces configurable patient-privacy constraints during synthetic clinical tabular data generation by formulating the problem as an Augmented Lagrangian Method (ALM) constrained optimization. - Why it's important: Hospitals cannot share patient records across institutional boundaries under HIPAA and GDPR, and existing synthetic data generators either memorize training records (creating re-identification risk) or apply differential privacy so aggressively that clinically meaningful inter-feature correlations and rare-disease signals are destroyed. - How it does it: ALM adaptively updates a Lagrange multiplier at each training epoch so that whenever the generator's privacy score P(θ) drops below a configurable threshold P_min, a progressively larger quadratic penalty steers the generator back into the privacy-feasible region without requiring a fixed regularization weight. - The good: On the Diabetes Health Indicators dataset (n=253,680), PSyGenTAB simultaneously raises composite clinical utility from U=0.89 to U=0.98 and maintains privacy at P=0.54, directly contradicting the common assumption that stronger privacy necessarily degrades downstream usefulness. - The missing: On the Adult Census dataset PSyGenTAB's Black-Box membership inference AUC rises to 0.74 versus the unconstrained baseline's 0.59, meaning the ALM constraint actually worsens adversarial membership inference vulnerability on this dataset; the paper acknowledges this but offers no mitigation. - Read it or not: Read it if you build or evaluate synthetic EHR pipelines and need a drop-in privacy constraint mechanism compatible with existing transformer- or GAN-based tabular generators; skip it if you require formal (ε,δ)-DP certification or work primarily with unstructured clinical text or imaging.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (65) is anchored by recasting synthetic clinical data generation as a hard-constrained ALM optimization with formal multi-dimensional utility and privacy composites — a NEW FORMULATION over prior single-metric or post-hoc-regularizer approaches. Impact (65) reflects an open-source, model-agnostic, drop-in wrapper. Rigor (55) is the binding constraint: no error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple seeds for any metric, fixed and unablated composite weights, partial circularity between the utility metric and the SDMetrics evaluation, and arXiv preprint status. Resilience (55) is reduced by the Black-Box MIA regression on Adult (0.59→0.74) and the CTAB-GAN+ degradation on Diabetes, both disclosed but unmitigated, plus the absence of any formal (ε,δ)-DP guarantee. Completeness (65) is helped by honest limitation acknowledgment. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*65 = 60.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Clinical AI development is bottlenecked by the inability to share patient-level electronic health records (EHRs) across institutional boundaries due to HIPAA and GDPR. Synthetic data generation is an attractive workaround, but existing methods fail in one of two ways. High-fidelity generators such as REaLTabFormer and CTAB-GAN+ memorize training records, creating re-identification risk that regulators and IRBs cannot approve. Differentially private alternatives (DP-GAN, DP-CTGAN, DP-SGD) inject enough noise to destroy the subtle inter-feature correlations and rare-phenotype signals (e.g., prediabetes at 1.8% prevalence, rare cancer subtypes) that clinical AI depends on. No prior method jointly optimizes both objectives during generation; privacy is treated as a post-hoc regularizer or a fixed architectural constraint, forcing practitioners to accept unstable trade-offs. Key Idea The core insight is to recast synthetic health data generation as a constrained optimization problem with privacy as a hard constraint rather than a loss term: minimize clinical utility loss (1 - U(θ)) subject to P(θ) ≥ P_min. The ALM converts this into an unconstrained sequence of sub-problems by augmenting the objective with an adaptive Lagrange multiplier λ and a quadratic penalty coefficient μ. Whenever the generator violates the privacy constraint, λ increases automatically, growing the penalty until the constraint is restored, without requiring manual tuning of a fixed regularization weight. Methodology PSyGenTAB defines two composite scores, each normalized to [0,1]. Clinical utility U is a weighted sum of six dimensions: marginal distribution fidelity (F_marg, measured via KS test, KL divergence, Jensen-Shannon divergence, and Range Coverage per feature type), rare clinical event and anomaly preservation (F_rare, via IQR outlier detection for continuous and minority-class frequency alignment for categorical features), joint association fidelity (F_joint, via Pearson/Spearman correlation matrix Frobenius-norm difference and normalized mutual information for mixed-type pairs), state-space diversity (C_div, via Category Coverage and Regularized Support Coverage), temporal dynamics consistency (T_dyn, via Dynamic Time Warping and temporal auto-correlation), and downstream predictive efficacy (E_pred, via TSTR and TRTS protocols with balanced accuracy, F1, and AUC-ROC across Decision Tree, Random Forest, and Logistic Regression classifiers). Privacy-preservability P combines distance to closest record D_c (Gower distance for mixed-type data, with Nearest Neighbor Distance Ratio normalization), distributional overfitting discrepancy Δ_dist (quantile-specific behavior difference between real and synthetic distributions), and duplication/memorization rate R_dup (Exact Match Ratio between D_syn and D_real). The ALM objective is L_AL(θ,λ) = (1-U(θ)) + λ(P_min - P(θ)) + (μ/2)(P_min - P(θ))², with λ updated as λ ← max(0, λ + μ(P_min - P)) each epoch and μ increased by growth factor α when the constraint remains violated. A sampling-time variant applies the same ALM penalty to steer or filter samples from a pre-trained generator, enabling one generator to serve multiple P_min thresholds without retraining. PSyGenTAB is applied to two architectures: REaLTabFormer (transformer-based autoregressive joint tabular distribution model) and CTAB-GAN+ (conditional Wasserstein GAN with gradient penalty for mixed-type tabular data). Three critical design decisions: (1) per-feature-type divergence measures in F_marg are clinically grounded but the composite weight vector ω is not learned from data and its sensitivity is unablated; (2) the privacy constraint is configurable by IRB/data-use-agreement requirements, which is clinically motivated but P_min selection guidance is absent; (3) the model-agnostic design is well-justified through evaluation on both architectures. Key Results Evaluation spans 8 clinical datasets (Breast Cancer n=569, Diabetes n=253,680, Heart Failure n=299, Hypothyroid n=3,772, Liver Disorders n=345, Lung Cancer n=309, Obesity n=2,111, Parkinsons n=195) and 3 generalization datasets (Adult Census n=48,842, PIR Vision Sensor/IoT n=7,651, VN Banking n=100,000). For REaLTabFormer with ALM versus unconstrained RTF: on Diabetes, U improves from 0.89 to 0.98 with P maintained at 0.54 vs 0.55; on Breast Cancer, U improves from 0.82 to 0.91 and P from 0.38 to 0.44; on Adult, U is maintained at 0.96 while P increases from 0.31 to 0.49. Downstream TSTR with Random Forest on the Adult dataset achieves 83.87% accuracy versus the 85.69% Train-on-Real, Test-on-Real (TRTR) ceiling. Exact match reproductions on Adult drop from 8 (unconstrained RTF) to 3 (PSyGenTAB ALM). Linkage attack success rate drops from 1.04 to 0.86 on Adult and from 0.87 to 0.70 on Heart Failure. FAITH clinical validity composite scores exceed 0.97 for PSyGenTAB across all datasets where generative failure does not occur. Counter-intuitive finding: CTAB-GAN+ with ALM on Diabetes produces U=0.93, P=0.33, degrading both metrics relative to the unconstrained CTAB-GAN+ baseline (U=0.98, P=0.70), indicating that GAN training dynamics interact poorly with the ALM penalty on large imbalanced datasets. A second counter-intuitive finding: PSyGenTAB Black-Box membership inference AUC on Adult rises to 0.74 versus 0.59 for the unconstrained RTF, indicating that higher statistical fidelity preserves population-level distributions in ways that inadvertently strengthen adversarial membership inference. Both PSyGenTAB and RTF completely fail on Hypothyroid (KL divergence > 11.4 for both), attributable to extreme dataset sparsity and deterministic medical rules. The SDMetrics open-source quality score favors PSyGenTAB on Diabetes (0.97 vs 0.96) and Obesity (0.88 vs 0.86), while the commercial MostlyAI Accuracy metric favors unconstrained RTF on most benchmarks (0.99 vs 0.98 on Diabetes), reflecting that commercial tools reward raw distribution overlap (which also rewards memorization). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The formal, multi-dimensional composite definitions of clinical utility U and privacy-preservability P are a genuine contribution over prior task-specific or single-metric evaluations. Applying ALM to the privacy-utility constrained optimization in synthetic tabular health data appears to be the first such use in this domain. The underlying components (ALM, REaLTabFormer, CTAB-GAN+, DCR, MIA evaluation, TSTR/TRTS protocols) are all established; PSyGenTAB's novelty is their principled integration into a jointly optimized constrained learning problem. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly available at https://github.com/ArshiaIlaty/PsyGenTAB, with hardware specified (NVIDIA GPU A10 24GB VRAM, AMD EPYC 7742, 256 GB RAM) and software versions documented (PyTorch 2.1.0, CUDA 12.2, scikit-learn 1.3.1). Ablations are present in the sense that three optimization regimes are compared per architecture (baseline, DP-SGD, and ALM), and both training-time and sampling-time ALM variants are evaluated. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed runs are reported for any metric in the main paper, making it impossible to assess statistical significance of improvements. The composite weight vectors (ω for U and λ for P) are fixed and not ablated, leaving uncertainty about sensitivity to these hyperparameters. The paper evaluates PSyGenTAB partly on the same benchmark structure used to define the utility metric (circular: F_marg is in both U and the SDMetrics pipeline), though TSTR downstream utility and MIA evaluations are independent. The Hypothyroid dataset failure is disclosed. This is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2606.18518v1, 16 Jun 2026) and has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL and SYSTEMS (built framework with deployment-oriented evaluation). - Practical: PSyGenTAB runs on a single NVIDIA A10 (24 GB VRAM) with standard PyTorch; no exotic hardware is required, and the sampling-time variant requires no retraining. - Efficient: The paper does not report training time or throughput comparisons between PSyGenTAB and unconstrained baselines; the ALM overhead per epoch is not quantified, which is a missing operational cost disclosure. - Effective: On 7 of 8 clinical datasets, PSyGenTAB-ALM with REaLTabFormer improves or matches both U and P versus the unconstrained baseline; TSTR downstream accuracy on Adult reaches 83.87% vs 85.69% TRTR ceiling, confirming functional clinical utility. However, the Diabetes MIA finding (PSyGenTAB Black-Box AUC 0.52 vs RTF 0.60, so PSyGenTAB is slightly better here) and Adult finding (PSyGenTAB 0.74 vs RTF 0.59, so PSyGenTAB is worse) are inconsistent, suggesting effectiveness depends on dataset characteristics in ways the framework does not fully control. - Scalable: The largest dataset evaluated is VN Banking at n=100,000. The Diabetes dataset at n=253,680 is the largest clinical dataset. No experiments at 10x-100x scale or with high-dimensionality EHR feature spaces (the largest feature set evaluated is 55 features in PIR Vision) are conducted; the claim of applicability to large-scale cross-institutional EHR sharing extrapolates beyond the evidence. PSyGenTAB delivers a software artifact (open-source Python framework with ALM objective functions and FAITH validation scripts). Strengths - The reframing of synthetic health data generation as a hard-constrained optimization problem is principled and practically motivated: encoding P_min directly into the ALM objective provides an auditable, quantitative record of privacy assurance aligned with HIPAA Expert Determination and GDPR Article 89 requirements. - The six-dimensional utility composite U and three-component privacy composite P are more clinically grounded than single-metric evaluations, capturing rare-phenotype preservation and inter-feature associations that downstream classifiers actually rely on. - The empirical demonstration that privacy and utility can improve simultaneously on 7 of 8 clinical datasets (with REaLTabFormer) challenges the widely assumed antagonism between the two objectives and is the most credible result in the paper. - The FAITH framework (Factuality, Alignment, Integrity, Tracking) provides a clinically interpretable decomposition of synthetic data reliability that is orthogonal to statistical fidelity metrics and catches failure modes (clinically impossible profiles, spurious correlations, out-of-distribution physiological values) that standard KL/JS divergences miss. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state three limitations: (1) PSyGenTAB is validated exclusively on structured tabular data and has not been evaluated on imaging, free-text clinical notes, or multi-modal records; (2) privacy assurances are empirical rather than formal, providing no (ε,δ)-DP guarantee, which may be insufficient in regulatory contexts requiring formal certification; (3) the ALM objective is non-convex due to deep generative models and non-linear privacy metrics, so global optimality cannot be guaranteed and convergence is assessed only empirically. The authors confront these limitations directly, which adds credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The Black-Box MIA AUC increases from 0.59 (RTF) to 0.74 (PSyGenTAB) on the Adult dataset, meaning PSyGenTAB worsens the most practically relevant adversarial privacy metric on the largest non-clinical generalization benchmark. The paper acknowledges this but does not propose a mitigation or bound the conditions under which this occurs. A practitioner deploying PSyGenTAB on census-scale data with a non-linear attacker would face higher membership inference risk than the unconstrained baseline. - The composite weight vectors ω (for U) and the weights within P (λ1, λ2, λ3) are fixed by design and never ablated. It is unknown whether the observed improvements in U are driven primarily by F_rare and F_joint (the clinically important dimensions) or by the more easily satisfied F_marg term. - All clinical datasets are small by EHR standards (n=195 to n=3,772 for 6 of 8 datasets). Real-world cross-institutional datasets contain millions of records with hundreds of features; the framework's behavior at that scale is untested. - CTAB-GAN+ with ALM degrades both U and P on Diabetes relative to the unconstrained CTAB-GAN+ baseline (U: 0.93 vs 0.98; P: 0.33 vs 0.70). The paper attributes this to GAN adversarial dynamics interacting with the ALM penalty, but no architectural or hyperparameter fix is proposed, leaving users without guidance on when to avoid PSyGenTAB with GAN-based generators. - The sampling-time ALM variant is described but not compared quantitatively against the training-time variant in terms of privacy-utility trade-off; it is only applied to RTF, making the claim of a flexible post-training option underevaluated. - P_min selection is left entirely to the data custodian with only regulatory framing as guidance. No sensitivity analysis shows how U degrades as P_min increases from 0.5 to 0.9, which is the critical operational question for deployment. Bottom Line Practitioners building privacy-preserving synthetic EHR pipelines will gain a directly deployable, open-source ALM wrapper that demonstrably preserves minority-class clinical signals while reducing memorization on transformer-based generators, but they must account for the membership inference regression on certain dataset types and the complete absence of formal DP guarantees before deploying in regulatory contexts that require certified privacy.
Citations 2606.18518 PDF
#83 — Innovative (60)

ComPart: Community-Guided Post-Coarsening for High-Quality Hypergraph Partitioning

Y Zhu, Z Guo, Y Wu, M Li, J Wang, Z Xie · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ComPart, a multi-level hypergraph partitioning framework that applies community detection in the post-coarsening stages (initial partitioning and uncoarsening) rather than restricting the coarsening phase as all prior work does. - Why it's important: Hypergraph partitioning governs inter-device communication in MPSoC task mapping and multi-FPGA prototyping; existing methods are stuck with vertex-wise FM-style refinement that reliably falls into local optima, leaving cut quality on the table. - How it does it: During uncoarsening, ComPart periodically detects community structure, classifies sub-communities relative to partition boundaries, and reassigns them jointly via ILP (exact for small problems, 1%-gap approximate for medium, V-cycle for large), enabling large-scale coordinated moves that FM cannot make. - The good: ComPart achieves 5.74% cut reduction over KaHyPar at k=3 on Titan23 and 2.68% over SHyPar at k=3 on ISPD98, with consistent wins across both benchmark families and all tested k values. - The missing: No runtime data is reported anywhere; wall-clock time is equalized across ComPart, hMETIS, and KaHyPar but the actual budgets, per-phase breakdowns, and ILP solver costs are never stated, making it impossible to evaluate practical throughput. - Read it or not: Read it if you build or research hypergraph partitioning tools for EDA applications; the post-coarsening community paradigm and the hypergraph locally-dense decomposition are both transferable ideas, though the missing runtime data and preprint status warrant caution before deployment.
Why this rank: Empirical profile (has a theoretical sub-result but the contribution is an empirical partitioner). It edges out L001 on Novelty (72, NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION) and Rigor (55) because the post-coarsening community paradigm is a high-leverage reframing, the locally-dense decomposition is generalized to weighted hypergraphs with three proven theorems, and an ablation isolates the U and I components plus four community-detection strategies. Non-Obviousness (75) is high: redirecting community detection from coarsening to uncoarsening/initial-partitioning is simple in hindsight but unexploited by prior work. Impact (40) is the main drag: the code is not released and the ILP solver is unnamed, blocking adoption. Completeness (55) and Resilience (58) are limited by the complete absence of runtime data, single-run cut sizes with no error bars, and a comparison asymmetry (SHyPar/SpecPart k=2 numbers cited rather than re-run). Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*40 = 60.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 55 Resilience 58 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Hypergraph partitioning is NP-hard and central to two high-stakes EDA flows: hardware/software co-design for heterogeneous MPSoCs (where the partition determines which tasks run on which processor and what inter-processor communication must cross chip boundaries) and multi-FPGA logic emulation (where partition boundaries become inter-FPGA channels with strict bandwidth and latency constraints). The dominant multi-level framework (KaHyPar, hMETIS, PaToH) follows a coarsen-then-uncoarsen pipeline, with local refinement methods such as Fiduccia-Mattheyses operating one vertex at a time during uncoarsening. This vertex-wise view reliably converges to local optima. A line of work integrates community detection to constrain coarsening — KaHyPar uses Louvain, SHyPar uses flow-based community structure — but no prior work applies community detection in the post-coarsening stages (initial partitioning and uncoarsening/refinement), leaving a structural tool unused precisely where the solution space is being navigated. Key Idea The core insight is that community structure detected at intermediate uncoarsening levels can serve as the atomic unit of global optimization: instead of moving single vertices, the algorithm moves entire sub-communities simultaneously using ILP, making large-scale coordinated jumps that FM-style methods cannot reach. Independently, at the coarsest level, locally-dense decomposition (a convex-optimization technique previously limited to graphs) is generalized to hypergraphs with positive vertex weights, providing a principled initialization that reveals hierarchically nested dense substructures rather than relying on heuristic seeds. Methodology ComPart is built on KaHyPar's n-level architecture. During uncoarsening, at α=4 geometrically spaced levels (sized according to a Kronecker product spacing principle), a two-stage procedure runs. Stage 1 applies one of four igraph community detection algorithms (Label Propagation, Walktrap, Louvain, Fast Greedy) to the current hypergraph or its clique-model graph expansion. Hyperedges with fewer than S1=100 pins are expanded to cliques directly; those with 100–200 pins are queued and expanded as needed to ensure connectivity; those with ≥200 pins are discarded. Detected communities are classified as intra-partition (contained within one block) or edge communities (spanning block boundaries); edge communities are intersected with partition boundaries to form sub-communities. Stage 2 reassigns sub-communities jointly. The formulation is a binary ILP: minimize total cut weight, subject to balance constraints. For small clustered hypergraphs, exact ILP is solved warm-started from the current partition. For medium ones, ILP terminates when the optimality gap drops below 1%. For large ones, the ILP is replaced by KaHyPar's V-cycle. The small/medium/large thresholds are not explicitly stated in numerical terms. For initial partitioning at the coarsest level, locally-dense decomposition finds a nested sequence ∅ = B0 ⊂ B1 ⊂ ... ⊂ Bγ = V where each Bi maximizes (WE(Bi) - WE(Bi-1)) / WV(Bi \ Bi-1). The paper proves this decomposition is unique (Theorem 1) and is equivalent to a quadratic programming problem (Theorem 3), solved via Block-Coordinate Descent using a per-hyperedge water-filling update. Convergence to the global optimum is guaranteed by continuous differentiability and blockwise strict convexity of the objective. Three critical design decisions: (1) geometric level spacing during uncoarsening — motivated by analogy to Kronecker products but not empirically ablated; (2) ILP warm-starting from the current partition — justified by convergence acceleration, reasonable; (3) clique model with size cutoffs — standard approximation whose impact on hyperedge weight coverage on Titan23 is not quantified. Key Results On the Titan23 benchmark (22 circuits with characteristically large hyperedges), against hMETIS, K-SpecPart, KaHyPar, and SHyPar: normalized geometric-mean cut sizes (relative to KaHyPar = 1.0) are 0.964 (k=2), 0.943 (k=3), and 0.955 (k=4) for ComPart versus 0.972/unspecified/unspecified for SHyPar (best prior baseline). This represents 3.62% improvement over KaHyPar and 0.87% over SHyPar at k=2, and 5.74% over KaHyPar at k=3. On the ISPD98 benchmark (18 circuits with smaller hyperedges): ComPart achieves 0.979/0.959/0.989 at k=2/3/4 versus SHyPar's 0.981/0.985/1.016. ComPart wins on 16 of 18 designs at k=3 and all 18 at k=4. Ablation (Fig. 3): the uncoarsening community component (U) alone achieves 3.6% improvement over KaHyPar; the initial partitioning component (I) alone provides 0.7%; combined (I+U) yields the best result, outperforming SHyPar by a visible margin. Community detection comparison (Fig. 4): Fast Greedy and Louvain clearly outperform Label Propagation and Walktrap in the uncoarsening phase; all four beat KaHyPar, and Fast Greedy/Louvain beat SHyPar. Baselines are current (SHyPar published 2025, GenPart 2024) and strong. SpecPart and SHyPar k=2 results are cited from their papers rather than reproduced under equal time budgets, which is a comparison asymmetry. No error bars or multiple runs are reported. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION. The post-coarsening paradigm for community-guided optimization is genuinely novel — the observation that community detection has been applied exclusively to the coarsening phase, despite post-coarsening stages being equally accessible, is a clear reframing that no prior work has exploited. The generalization of locally-dense decomposition to weighted hypergraphs (Theorems 1–3 with proofs) is a new theoretical formulation. Standard components — FM refinement, V-cycle, ILP for partitioning, and the community detection algorithms themselves — are existing tools assembled in a novel configuration. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation present and meaningful: Section 4.2.3 isolates U and I components, and Fig. 4 compares four community detection strategies. However, no error bars or repeated runs are reported; cut sizes are single-point estimates, and partitioning quality is known to be stochastic. Code is not released (no URL or repository mentioned). The ILP solver used is not named, which matters for reproducibility and practical deployment. The comparison methodology has an important asymmetry: SpecPart and SHyPar (k=2) numbers are taken from the original papers, potentially on different hardware and without the same wall-clock budget given to ComPart. For SHyPar at k>2 on ISPD98, the authors ran the source code themselves, but it is not confirmed that SHyPar was given the same time budget as ComPart. The paper is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2606.18131v1, 16 Jun 2026) and has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on a single CPU core (Intel Xeon Gold 6438Y+ @2.00GHz, Ubuntu 22.04.5); no GPU or distributed compute required. The unnamed ILP solver is a dependency that may require a commercial license (e.g., Gurobi or CPLEX) if open-source alternatives prove too slow. - Efficient: No runtime numbers are reported. The wall-clock equalization scheme confirms that ComPart, hMETIS, and KaHyPar were given the same total time budget, but neither the budget value nor per-phase timing breakdowns appear anywhere in the paper, making cost-of-adoption unknowable from this work. - Effective: Evidence is consistent — ComPart achieves lower normalized cut sizes than all baselines on both benchmark families across all tested k values, with ablations confirming that both components contribute. The improvement is real within the evaluated setting. - Scalable: The largest circuits in Titan23 and ISPD98 are the extent of testing. SHyPar required over 20 hours on medium-sized Titan23 designs at k>2, signaling that the broader method class is sensitive to scale. ComPart's ILP-based Stage 2 has worst-case exponential complexity, mitigated by tiering, but behavior at 10× the tested vertex count is untested and unclaimed. The work delivers an implementation based on KaHyPar (C++17, g++11.4.0) but does not release it publicly. Strengths - The post-coarsening paradigm is a genuine conceptual contribution: recognizing that community detection has been universally applied only to coarsening and redirecting it to uncoarsening and initial partitioning is a simple, high-leverage reframing with clear empirical payoff. - The theoretical treatment of locally-dense decomposition on weighted hypergraphs is rigorous: three theorems with complete proofs, a convex QP formulation, and a convergence-guaranteed BCD algorithm — stronger foundations than any competing initialization heuristic. - The ILP-based sub-community reassignment provides a principled global optimization step that transcends what FM-style local search can reach, and the tiered fallback (exact → approx → V-cycle) is a reasonable practical accommodation. - Results are consistent across two structurally different benchmark families (large vs. small hyperedges) and three values of k, which reduces the risk that the gains are benchmark-specific. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper does not contain an explicit limitations section. The authors note that SHyPar took over 20 hours on medium-sized Titan23 designs at k>2 (limiting comparison scope), and that hyperedges with ≥200 pins are discarded in the clique model. No other failure modes, parameter sensitivity, or scalability boundaries are explicitly discussed. The authors do not directly confront the missing runtime data. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No runtime data anywhere. The paper never reports how long individual runs take, what wall-clock budget was used, or what fraction of that budget ILP solving consumes. This is the single most consequential omission for practitioners. - The ILP solver is unnamed. Whether it is Gurobi, CPLEX, GLPK, or a custom implementation has direct consequences for reproducibility and licensing. - Comparison asymmetry: SpecPart and SHyPar k=2 numbers are taken from original papers without hardware or time-budget normalization. This likely favors ComPart. - No statistical validation: single-run cut sizes with no confidence intervals, despite stochastic initialization affecting multi-level solvers. - The Kronecker product justification for geometric level spacing is heuristic and never empirically ablated; the choice of α=4 also lacks sensitivity analysis. - ILP size thresholds (small/medium/large) and the 1% optimality gap for medium problems are unexplained fixed choices with no ablation. - The fraction of total hyperedge weight discarded by the ≥200-pin cutoff in Titan23 (which is characterized by large hyperedges) is not reported; this gap in structural coverage may affect solution quality in ways not captured by the results. Bottom Line ComPart is a technically sound and empirically well-supported advance for EDA hypergraph partitioning; researchers and tool developers in this space should read it, but the complete absence of runtime data prevents any judgment on whether the quality gains are affordable in practice.
Search Results 2606.18131 PDF
#84 — Moderately Innovative (59)

EnterpriseClawBench: Benchmarking Agents from Real Workplace Sessions (57 ▲ )

Jincheng Zhong, Weizhi Wang, Che Jiang, Kai Tian, Zhenzhao Yuan, Junlin Yang, Dianqiao Lei, Kaiyan Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EnterpriseClawBench, a benchmark of 852 reproducible enterprise agent tasks constructed from real internal workplace sessions, paired with a multidimensional evaluation protocol that jointly reports harness–model performance, artifact delivery quality, cost, runtime, and skill-class transfer. - Why it's important: Existing agent benchmarks use simulated or hand-authored tasks that do not reflect naturally occurring enterprise demand, so measured scores may not predict real deployment behavior; harness effects and skill generalization are also systematically underreported. - How it does it: A construction pipeline converts raw proprietary workplace sessions through four mechanical gates (length, fixture-recovery, redaction-recovery, network-reachability), LLM-assisted self-containment review, prompt rewriting, taxonomy labeling, and sandbox preflighting to produce reproducible single-turn tasks with rubrics. - The good: The benchmark remains unsaturated at a best score of 0.663 (Codex/GPT-5.5 on the 120-task Lite subset), and quantifies that harness choice shifts Sonnet 4.6 scores by up to 0.18 points — larger than many model-tier gaps — which is a concrete, actionable finding. - The missing: Benchmark data is not released because sessions contain internal enterprise content, so the primary reusable contribution is the construction protocol; other organizations cannot run, extend, or replicate the leaderboard directly. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or evaluating enterprise agent systems, specifically for its harness–model interaction analysis and multidimensional rubric design; skip if you need a publicly accessible benchmark for direct comparison.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. The real-session provenance (5,291 raw workplace sessions sanitized into 852 reproducible tasks) is a genuine differentiator from all six comparable benchmarks (Novelty 72), and the harness-model interaction finding (harness shifts Sonnet 4.6 by up to 0.18, exceeding many model-tier gaps) plus visual-judge miscalibration are non-obvious, actionable results. The composite is dragged down by Impact (50) and Rigor (52): the benchmark data is NOT released, so under the Systems profile's 30% Impact weight only the protocol is reusable; there are no significance tests on 120-task Lite scores, single-organization provenance, an unreported self-containment gate precision (78% rejection), and a March-May 2026 contamination window overlapping evaluated models' training. Weighted composite: 0.15*72 + 0.15*52 + 0.10*68 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*58 + 0.30*50 = 59.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 65 Resilience 58 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Enterprise agents must read heterogeneous file inputs, invoke tools across persistent Linux workspaces, and deliver typed business artifacts (spreadsheets, slides, reports, HTML pages, code) under cost and latency constraints. Three gaps remain open in the existing evaluation literature. First, benchmarks such as Workspace-Bench, EntWorld, and EnterpriseBench use simulated, human-authored, or public-domain tasks rather than naturally occurring employee requests, leaving a realism gap. Second, prior work either ignores the agent harness or treats it as a fixed implementation detail; WildClawBench shows harness effects matter, but the magnitude is not systematically characterized across many model–harness pairings at enterprise task difficulty. Third, reusable skills are becoming operational assets in enterprise agent deployments, but no existing benchmark evaluates skill generalization at the task-class level — that is, whether a skill distilled from in-domain executions transfers to held-out tasks of the same subclass. Key Idea The core insight is that a production enterprise agent deployment already generates the ground-truth inputs, tool traces, and delivered artifacts needed for benchmark construction; the problem reduces to a sanitization and reproducibility pipeline. By applying staged mechanical gates to filter out unrecoverable or network-dependent sessions, then rewriting surviving sessions into self-contained single-turn prompts with recovered fixtures, the pipeline converts naturally occurring employee requests into reproducible benchmark tasks without manual task authoring. This preserves realistic task difficulty while achieving the reproducibility needed for controlled evaluation. Methodology The pipeline starts from 5,291 raw TaskInstances extracted from enterprise agent sessions at Frontis.AI (March–May 2026, 100+ employees). Four parallel mechanical gates reduce the pool to 3,813 usable candidates: a length filter (minimum 10 effective user characters), a fixture-lookup gate (input files must be recoverable from session source paths), a redaction-recovery gate (redacted URLs or file paths must be reconstructable from local context or unique host matching), and a public-network gate (external URLs must be stably reachable at construction time). An LLM self-containment review then drops sessions whose task objective cannot be inferred from the available context, yielding 852 benchmark tasks. Each surviving session is rewritten into a standalone single-turn prompt by an LLM, assigned to one of 7 role classes and 45 skill subclasses via LLM taxonomy classification (confidence thresholded), and annotated with expected deliverable types (MD, TXT, HTML, DOC, Slides, Sheet, PDF, JSON, Code). Tasks are packaged with hard rules (required file type, file count, non-emptiness, tracebacks, unreplaced placeholders) and five-dimension semantic rubrics (grounded accuracy, task relevance, substantive depth, practical utility, communication quality). A 120-task Lite subset is manually audited. Evaluation runs 5 harnesses (ClaudeCode, Codex, DeepAgents, Hermes, OpenClaw) crossed with up to 10 models in independent non-stateful sandboxes; text-extractable outputs go to a Sonnet 4.6 text judge, while HTML, slides, spreadsheets, and images are rendered and routed to a visual judge. Three critical design decisions: (1) harness–model combination as the primary evaluation unit, justified because harness behavior (approval gates, delegated subtasks, execution truncation) independently determines whether the artifact-writing loop completes; (2) semantic rubrics decomposed across five axes rather than a scalar, justified by the finding that communication quality and grounded accuracy diverge systematically; (3) skill evaluation at the task-class level using a creator–consumer matrix rather than individual task injection, justified by the observation that skill quality depends on both creator and consumer model independently. Key Results The best Lite leaderboard score is 0.663 (Codex/GPT-5.5); the 32-combination average is 48.6%, confirming the benchmark is far from saturated. Harness effects dominate for Claude-family models: Sonnet 4.6 scores 0.62–0.64 under ClaudeCode, DeepAgents, and OpenClaw but drops to 0.458 under Hermes, a 0.16–0.18 gap attributable to runtime-level mismatch (Hermes blocks active environment probing and truncates long execution traces before artifact writing completes). Cost–score relationship is log-like: large gains from low-cost to mid-cost systems, diminishing returns beyond that, with Hermes/Claude-family as an outlier (high cost, below-expected score). Role-class heatmaps show GPT-5.5 is the most robust generalist; marketing and finance/ops tasks are hardest across all models (company-specific conventions underrepresented in pretraining). Artifact-type heatmaps show GPT-5.5 leading on HTML/code/JSON, Opus 4.6 on spreadsheets/slides. Visual judge scores are systematically inflated relative to human scores: visual-route human calibration yields MAE 0.303 and Spearman -0.259, while text-route yields MAE 0.134 and Spearman 0.790. Scalability check on the full 852-task set (DeepAgents × 4 models) preserves the Lite ranking (GPT-5.5 > Sonnet 4.6 > Haiku 4.5 > GPT-4.1-mini), supporting pipeline validity. Skill-transfer experiments on the frontend-page-generation subclass: GPT-5.5 as skill creator yields mean delta +0.0681 with no negative transfers; Haiku 4.5 as creator yields mean delta -0.0941 (degrades two of four consumer harnesses); Kimi K2.6 is positive on average (+0.0518) but concentrated in strong consumers. Novelty Classification NEW BENCHMARK/DATASET (construction protocol for enterprise agent tasks from real sessions), NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (harness–model interaction magnitude and visual judge miscalibration), NEW METHOD (multidimensional evaluation framework with creator–consumer skill-transfer matrix). The benchmark-from-sessions construction pipeline is genuinely novel: all six comparable benchmarks in Table 1 use either human-authored or simulated tasks. The harness-first evaluation framing and skill-class-level transfer protocol are meaningful additions to the field. The individual evaluation components (LLM judges, semantic rubrics, sandbox execution) are each adapted from existing practice. Rigor & Reproducibility The benchmark data is not released; only the construction and evaluation protocol is the reusable contribution, which limits independent replication. The 120-task Lite subset is manually audited, but inter-rater reliability for that audit is not reported. LLM-judge reliability is measured: Sonnet 4.6 vs. GPT-5.4 text-route correlation is ρ = 0.918 (1,853 cases); visual-route correlation is ρ = 0.866 (1,428 cases). Human calibration on 48 packets confirms text-judge reliability (Spearman 0.790) and exposes visual-judge failure (Spearman -0.259), which the authors treat as an open problem rather than minimizing. No error bars or significance tests are reported on the main leaderboard scores; with 120 tasks, small score differences between adjacent models may not be statistically distinguishable. No code or trained model release is mentioned.
