The LLM taxonomy classifier assigns role and skill labels with reported confidence (e.g., 0.92 in the case study), but the aggregate error rate of taxonomy assignment is not reported. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — the benchmark construction pipeline and evaluation harness are the built artifact. - Practical: The evaluation harness runs in standard sandboxed Linux environments via publicly accessible LLM APIs; any organization with API access to the tested models can run the protocol, though it requires reproducing their own session archive. - Efficient: Per-task agent cost ranges from approximately 1.0m to 12.7m CNY for top-performing combinations (Figure 1 annotations), making full-set evaluation non-trivial at scale; the construction pipeline processes 5,291 sessions but the compute cost of that pipeline is not stated. - Effective: The protocol surfaces harness–model effects and role-class/artifact-type breakdowns invisible to single-score benchmarks, and the five-axis rubric identifies grounded accuracy as the primary bottleneck; the visual judge route does not yet reliably agree with human scores (Spearman -0.259). - Scalable: The automated pipeline scales to the full 852-task set and preserves rank ordering, but benchmark growth depends on continued access to internal session data from the originating organization, making external scaling path unclear. The paper delivers a construction protocol and evaluation framework in paper form; no software artifact or dataset is publicly released. Strengths - Real-session provenance: Tasks derive from naturally occurring employee requests, not author-crafted scenarios, which is a genuine differentiator from all six comparable benchmarks in Table 1 and makes task difficulty ecologically valid. - Harness–model interaction quantification: Demonstrating that harness choice moves Sonnet 4.6 by up to 0.18 score points — exceeding model-tier differences in many comparisons — is a concrete, non-obvious result with direct practical implications for enterprise deployment decisions. - Multidimensional diagnostic profile: The five-axis rubric plus hard rules plus cost/runtime reporting exposes that current systems are better at communication quality than grounded accuracy, a finding that a scalar score would obscure and that points to a specific failure mode (evidence retrieval from large input files). - Creator–consumer skill-transfer matrix: The design of evaluating skill generalization via held-out tasks in the same class, with independent creator and consumer models, is a principled framing that produces a non-obvious result: creator model quality dominates transfer outcome, and skill-creation and skill-consumption ability are not correlated. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors identify three limitations directly: (1) single-organization provenance — results may not represent all enterprise contexts; (2) data not released — internal sessions, attachments, and business artifacts cannot be shared; (3) visual LLM judges are imperfect, especially for fine-grained artifact evaluation, necessitating judge ablation and human correlation as calibration steps. The authors confront these forthrightly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No significance testing: With 120 Lite tasks, score differences of 2–5 percentage points between adjacent leaderboard entries are likely within noise; the paper does not discuss statistical uncertainty or confidence intervals on any scores. - Skill transfer from a single subclass: All skill-transfer experiments use the frontend-page-generation subclass. Whether the creator–consumer interaction pattern holds for heterogeneous subclasses (HR/admin, finance/ops) is completely untested. - Training data contamination risk: Sessions are from March–May 2026, overlapping with the training and fine-tuning window of several evaluated models (GPT-5.5, Sonnet 4.6). Potential contamination of benchmark tasks into model training is not discussed. - Self-containment gate precision unreported: The LLM self-containment review drops 3,813 → 852 sessions (78% rejection rate). Its precision and recall relative to human judgment are not reported, so the quality and representativeness of surviving tasks cannot be independently assessed. - LLM taxonomy error rate: Role-class and skill-subclass assignments are LLM-generated; the aggregate error rate of this classification step and its downstream effect on skill-transfer experimental validity are not reported. Bottom Line A practitioner building or comparing enterprise agent systems gains concrete, actionable findings — particularly the harness–model interaction magnitude and multidimensional rubric design — but the unreleased benchmark limits use to methodology inspiration rather than direct adoption, and the single-organization provenance bounds how far the empirical conclusions generalize.
Recommended 2606.23654 PDF
#85 — Moderately Innovative (59)

Library-Aware Doubles and Iterative Repair for Large Language Model- Generated Unit Tests in OpenSIL Firmware

MT Bach, Y Zheng, H Razafindranto, T Alam, A Leather… · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a multi-agent LLM workflow that automatically generates, repairs, and coverage-refines unit tests for AMD's openSIL firmware C codebase under EDK II build constraints. - Why it's important: Low-level firmware unit tests fail routinely due to strict build-system requirements (missing headers, duplicate symbol definitions, mismatched INF metadata), making manual authoring expensive and error-prone in ways that high-level LLM generation cannot address without structured repair. - How it does it: The workflow combines library-aware test double retrieval from a Chroma vector database, template-constrained LLM drafting that confines edits to approved TODO blocks, and an iterative compile-dispatch-repair loop driven by compiler/linker logs and LCOV per-line coverage maps. - The good: On 76 openSIL functions the workflow compiles and links 73 (96.1%), and on a 48-function subset the line-coverage-analysis-only configuration reaches 98.8% mean line coverage versus 73.9% without coverage guidance. - The missing: The entire evaluation runs on one branch of one proprietary codebase under one build system, with no mutation-score or oracle-quality assessment, so whether generated tests actually catch bugs is entirely unevaluated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for engineers building automated UT pipelines for compiled-language systems with strict build environments; the deep/shallow double distinction and the VDB-vs-coverage trade-off finding are both non-obvious and directly actionable.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (built tool deployed on a real firmware codebase). Practical Impact (58, weight 30) anchors the composite: the pipeline produces deployable UT artifacts and demonstrates 96.1% compile/link on 76 functions and 98.8% LCA-only line coverage, but the implementation cannot be released due to AMD IP restrictions, capping Impact. Non-Obviousness (72) is the strongest dimension — the deep/shallow double distinction and the counter-intuitive finding that VDB retrieval reduces coverage under fixed iteration budgets are both genuine, actionable insights. Novelty (60) is integration-level (new system + empirical finding) over established components. Rigor (55) and Resilience (55) are limited by single-codebase/single-branch evaluation, no ablations isolating components, no significance tests, coverage-as-sole-proxy with zero mutation/oracle assessment, and an undefined 48-of-76 subset that raises selection-bias risk for the headline numbers. Completeness (62) benefits from forthright threats-to-validity engagement. Weighted: 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*72 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*55 + 0.30*58 = 59.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 62 Resilience 55 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Firmware engineers validating AMD's openSIL C library face a costly manual workflow: tests must satisfy EDK II's strict build constraints (specific include rules, INF metadata files, no duplicate production-symbol definitions), resolve hardware-interfacing dependencies through stubs, mocks, and fakes, and iterate through compile-link-run cycles before coverage can even be measured. Direct LLM generation without these safeguards routinely produces tests that fail to compile or link. Functions that call XFER dispatch tables or Ip2Ip API tables require behavior-carrying doubles wired to specific one-shot mock helpers, while sibling functions in the same translation unit introduce additional unresolved symbols that need minimal link-time stubs. The gap is the absence of an integrated pipeline that handles dependency isolation, build-system compliance, and coverage-guided refinement as a single automated loop. Prior LLM-based UT work targets managed languages (Java, Python, TypeScript) where these constraints do not apply. Key Idea The core insight is that firmware UT failures arise primarily from build-wiring failures (wrong headers, duplicate symbols, absent INF library entries) rather than from incorrect test logic, so the LLM's role should be structured as a constrained repair task where each iteration is driven by concrete compiler/linker diagnostics and LCOV hit/miss annotations rather than by semantic test design. This is operationalized by splitting test doubles into two kinds: deep doubles that carry enough behavior to exercise the intended code path through cross-module calls, and shallow stubs that exist solely to satisfy the linker for sibling functions in the same source file. The workflow retrieves existing library doubles before synthesizing new ones, confines all generated code to template-approved TODO blocks, and maintains a do-not-redefine list to prevent the LLM from introducing symbols that would collide with already-linked libraries. Methodology The pipeline is an 11-node LangGraph StateGraph implemented in Python (≥3.8), with LangChain ChatOpenAI handling LLM calls and Chroma providing vector retrieval. Three LLMs are assigned to distinct stages: GPT-4.1-mini for the shallow-stub and setup nodes (repetitive, template-driven tasks), o4-mini for drafting and assembly (consistent multi-file code generation), and o3 for the repair/refinement node (careful reasoning over compiler and coverage logs). The workflow proceeds through five logical phases. Retrieval: three Chroma queries pull (i) functions with similar dependency profiles, (ii) existing UT code for related functions as drafting examples, and (iii) reusable stub/mock/fake libraries for the FUT's dependencies, along with their required include directives and usage constraints. Drafting: write_unittest_node produces the initial UT header, UT source, INF, and iteration JSON using retrieved context. Shallow stubs: write_shallow_stub_node generates minimal link-time-only bodies for sibling functions. Assembly: assemble_node merges drafts into the fixed template layout and filters any symbol on the do-not-redefine ban list. Iterative repair loop: resolve_node reads compiler/linker error output and LCOV per-line hit/miss annotations and applies targeted minimal edits until compile success, dispatcher pass, and ≥90% line coverage are all reached or the iteration limit is exhausted. Three critical design decisions: (a) the deep/shallow double split is well-justified because conflating the two causes either duplicate-symbol linker failures or insufficient isolation; (b) retrieving existing doubles before synthesizing new ones is well-justified to avoid duplicate definitions; (c) template-constrained edits are well-justified for symbol hygiene but limit the repair search space in ways the paper does not quantify. Key Results Across 76 FUTs, the workflow compiles and links 73 (96.1%); 3 failures involve unresolvable deep dependencies or build constraints that exceed the default 10-iteration repair limit. The direct LLM-only baseline (no repair loop, no retrieval) achieves mean line coverage of 55.2% (Small), 44.7% (Medium), 21.5% (Large), and 12.3% (XFER/Ip2Ip). The no-LCA/no-VDB configuration of the full pipeline reaches 78.9%, 70.7%, 71.3%, and 57.8% respectively. On a 48-function subset evaluated under both augmented configurations, LCA-only (coverage feedback enabled, VDB disabled) reaches 98.8% mean line coverage (Small 100%, Medium 100%, Large 94.3%, XFER/Ip2Ip 99.4%), while LCA+VDB reaches 94.7% (Small 100%, Medium 99.6%, Large 79.9%, XFER/Ip2Ip 95.5%). Median repair iterations are 2 for Small and Medium functions in most configurations, rising to 6 for Large under LCA-only and 9.5 under LCA+VDB. Median per-FUT cost is approximately $0.40–$0.50, median wall-clock time approximately 640–710 seconds, and median token usage approximately 250K–300K. The counter-intuitive finding is that LCA+VDB yields lower coverage than LCA-only despite adding retrieval context, because VDB injection increases prompt length and introduces additional constraints that consume iteration budget disproportionately for Large and XFER/Ip2Ip functions. Baselines are operationally appropriate but do not include symbolic execution (KLEE) or evolutionary test generation (EvoSuite) in the firmware setting. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The integrated combination of deep/shallow double discrimination, VDB-backed library reuse before synthesis, template-constrained multi-agent orchestration, and LCOV-driven iterative repair in a firmware C / EDK II context has not been demonstrated before. Each individual component (RAG-augmented code generation, compile-repair loops, coverage-guided refinement) is known from prior work; the novelty lies in their integration under firmware-specific constraints and in the empirical finding that retrieval context can reduce coverage under fixed iteration budgets. Rigor & Reproducibility The paper compares three configurations but provides no traditional ablation that isolates individual components (e.g., template constraints alone, or deep/shallow split alone, or o3 vs. o4-mini in the repair node). No significance tests or confidence intervals are reported; results are presented as medians and box plots over 48–76 FUTs. The workflow implementation cannot be released due to AMD intellectual property restrictions; only pseudocode supplementary material is provided. The openSIL codebase is publicly available (MIT license on the phoenix_poc branch), but the pipeline is not, severely limiting reproducibility. LLM model versions (GPT-4.1-mini, o4-mini, o3) are named but these are API-accessed models whose behavior can change. The 3 failed FUTs are not individually analyzed. The 48-function subset used for the LCA vs. LCA+VDB comparison is not shown to be representative of the full 76; selection criteria are not stated. Deployability Profile: SYSTEMS — a built tool evaluated by deployment on a real firmware codebase. - Practical: Runs on a Windows machine with EDK II installed and a WSL subsystem for LCOV; all components use commercially available LLM APIs. - Efficient: Median cost approximately $0.40–$0.50 per FUT and median wall-clock time approximately 640–710 seconds are non-trivial but plausible for enterprise firmware workflows where manual UT authoring takes hours per function; token usage of approximately 250K–300K per FUT adds non-negligible API cost at scale. - Effective: 98.8% mean line coverage (LCA-only, 48-function subset) is strong evidence that the coverage target is achieved, but coverage is the sole proxy; whether generated assertions detect functional bugs is not evaluated. - Scalable: Tested on 76 functions from one codebase branch; the paper does not claim or test scalability beyond this, and explicitly scopes conclusions to the studied configuration. The system produces deployable UT artifacts (UT source, UT header, INF, iteration JSON) but the pipeline itself is not publicly released. Strengths - The deep/shallow double distinction is a genuine, non-obvious engineering insight for firmware C: it correctly identifies that the two failure modes (cross-module behavior isolation vs. sibling-call link-time fallout) require qualitatively different doubles, and the 96.1% compile rate validates the design. - The coverage-guided repair loop is grounded in exactly the signals developers use manually, and the empirical result that median iterations stay at 2 for Small and Medium functions confirms it converges efficiently for the common case. - The counter-intuitive VDB finding — that retrieval context improves runtime for complex cases but costs coverage under fixed iteration budgets — is a concrete, actionable trade-off that practitioners designing similar pipelines need to know and that the paper characterizes clearly. - Honest engagement with the threats to validity (Section 7.2) correctly identifies construct, external, ablation, and conclusion validity concerns without minimizing them. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge four threats: (1) construct validity — compile success and LCOV coverage do not prove semantic correctness or oracle quality; (2) external validity — results should not generalize to other firmware stacks, languages, or build systems; (3) ablation validity — the quality of pre-existing test doubles affects outcomes but is not isolated; (4) conclusion validity — 76 FUTs from one branch reflect the studied configuration, not UT generation broadly. They also note that complex functions with deep dependencies and pointer-heavy patterns remain problematic, and that a quantitative productivity comparison against manual UT authoring was not conducted. The authors engage with these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No comparison against symbolic execution (KLEE) or evolutionary test generation (EvoSuite) in the firmware setting; citing their limitations is not a substitute for empirical comparison in this domain. - The 3 failed FUTs (3.9%) are not analyzed individually; whether failure modes are addressable by workflow extension or reflect fundamental limits is not discussed. - Test oracle quality is completely unexamined: no mutation score, no injected-fault detection rate. The generated tests exercise lines but whether their assertions would detect a subtle firmware bug is unknown. - The 48-function subset used for the LCA vs. LCA+VDB comparison is not defined — which 48 of the 76 and why — raising the possibility of selection bias in the headline coverage numbers. - No model ablation within the workflow: the mixed GPT-4.1-mini / o4-mini / o3 configuration is used throughout; whether a single model or a different assignment would perform differently is not tested. - The per-FUT cost figures (~$0.40–$0.50) are not situated against manual developer time estimates, which is the primary practical efficiency argument the paper implies but does not substantiate. Bottom Line Practitioners building automated UT pipelines for firmware C or other compiled-language systems with strict build environments will find validated, actionable design decisions here — the deep/shallow double split, the retrieve-before-synthesize pattern, the VDB-vs.-coverage iteration budget trade-off — even though the absence of public code, single-codebase evaluation, and missing oracle assessment limit the strength of the generalization claims.
Search Results 2606.19725 PDF
#86 — Moderately Innovative (59)

Simulation and process mining: review and outlook

E Sulis, L Genga, N Martin · link.springer.com · simulation environments for business processes - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents a systematic literature review (SLR) of 105 peer-reviewed papers on the integration of business process simulation (BPS) and process mining, combining PRISMA-guided database search with Gephi-based bibliometric network analysis and structured paper coding across five variable groups to map the field's methods, tools, domains, and open challenges. - Why it's important: Building accurate BPS models currently requires expensive manual expert elicitation of parameters such as activity durations, resource schedules, and routing logic; understanding which of these tasks can actually be automated via process mining is essential for practitioners, yet no prior cross-domain, cross-paradigm synthesis existed. - How it does it: It decomposes the research question into a per-task answerability problem by mapping each of 22 BPS modeling tasks to the process mining algorithms and tools that address them, using co-occurrence, co-authorship, and citation network analysis to surface thematic structure and community patterns, then structured two-annotator coding to populate the framework. - The good: The task-level mapping in Table 5 extends the canonical Martin et al. [4] framework with 6 previously unlisted BPS modeling tasks (waiting time specification, multitasking, entity flow time, looping behavior, resource workload distribution, resource cooperation relationships), each grounded in the reviewed literature and revealing concrete automation gaps. - The missing: The temporal trend analysis bins papers into intervals of unequal duration (10 years vs. 3 years) without per-year normalization, the co-occurrence network uses abstract text only rather than full-paper text, and 36 of 105 reviewed papers report no validation — a field-level weakness the review correctly flags but cannot remediate. - Read it or not: Worth reading for BPS practitioners and process mining researchers who need an actionable map of where automation tools exist and where expert knowledge remains unavoidable; it can be skipped by researchers seeking algorithmic advances rather than a field survey.
Why this rank: Scored using Theoretical profile (systematic review synthesizing existing literature). Completeness (75) and Rigor (72) carry the score: PRISMA-conformant search with a fully populated flow diagram, reported Cohen's kappa (0.53–0.63), a node/edge perturbation sensitivity check, and a public coded dataset make this an unusually well-documented SLR that frankly confronts the field's 34%-no-validation problem. Novelty (50) and Non-Obviousness (50) are inherently capped because the contribution is organization and gap identification — the six new BPS modeling tasks extend Martin et al. rather than introduce a method. Resilience (65) is solid but dinged by unequal time bins without per-year normalization and abstract-only co-occurrence text. Impact (55) reflects a reusable Table 5 decision guide and public repository. Weighted: 0.25*50 + 0.20*72 + 0.25*50 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.05*55 ≈ 59.
theoretical Novelty 50 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 75 Resilience 65 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Building a BPS model requires specifying a large number of parameters — entity arrival rates, activity durations, routing probabilities, resource roles, schedules, and queue disciplines — that traditionally required costly expert interviews and manual observation. Process mining algorithms extract behavioral insights from event logs and can, in principle, automate at least some of this parameter extraction. However, the literature applying process mining to BPS model construction is fragmented: some studies target specific domains (healthcare, manufacturing), others target single simulation paradigms (Discrete-Event Simulation only), and no prior work provides a structured, cross-domain, cross-paradigm inventory of which BPS modeling tasks are actually automated by PM techniques and with what tools. This knowledge gap prevents practitioners from efficiently identifying usable methods and prevents researchers from identifying the most impactful open problems. Key Idea The central insight is that the general question "how well does process mining support BPS?" is underdetermined — it becomes tractable only when decomposed into per-task questions: which of the ~22 distinct BPS modeling tasks (control-flow definition, duration estimation, arrival rate modeling, resource scheduling, etc.) can be addressed by which PM algorithms, and to what degree? By operationalizing this task-level decomposition as a structured coding scheme applied uniformly across 105 papers, the review makes concrete gaps visible: control-flow and duration are well-supported (79 and 68 papers, respectively), while resource schedules (20 papers), waiting time specification (25 papers), multitasking, looping behavior, and resource cooperation are nearly unaddressed by PM automation and typically require domain expert input. Methodology The methodology follows two stages. The search stage adheres to PRISMA [20]: four databases — Scopus (602 records), Web of Science (293), IEEE Xplore (105), and ACM Digital Library (13) — were queried for papers combining process mining and simulation, yielding 1,013 initial records. After removing 311 duplicates, 702 papers entered title-and-abstract screening, reducing to 206. Full-text reading and eligibility checks (nine exclusion criteria including no executable method, process mining used only for validation, simulation without PM as a core topic) produced a final set of 105 papers. Two annotators coded each paper independently; disagreements were resolved by a third reviewer. Inter-annotator agreement was 75–85% raw agreement with Cohen's kappa 0.53, 0.59, and 0.63 (moderate to substantial) across the three annotator pairs. The analysis stage combines two complementary approaches. First, exploratory network analysis via Gephi: a co-occurrence network of abstract terms (after stemming, stopword removal, and part-of-speech filtering; Louvain modularity clustering; sensitivity-tested by perturbing 5% of nodes and 10% of edges, with cluster count stable at 5–6), a co-authorship network, and a directed citation network analyzed by in-degree and betweenness centrality. Six thematic clusters emerged: resource/time management, process modeling and data-driven approaches, BPM, validation and evaluation, healthcare systems, and systems/industrial optimization. Second, structured paper coding across five variable groups: (i) paper focus (simulation type, automation degree, analysis goal, industry); (ii) tooling (simulation and PM tools); (iii) data (synthetic vs. real-life, public vs. private); (iv) validation strategy (quantitative, qualitative, expert-based, none); and (v) BPS modeling tasks (which of 22 tasks, which PM algorithms). The task list was derived inductively: Martin et al. [4] provided a starting point, and new tasks were added as they emerged during coding. Critical design decisions: (1) inclusion of all three simulation paradigms (DES, ABM, SD) rather than DES only — distinguishing this review from all four identified comparators; (2) requirement for executable methods, excluding papers that reference PM conceptually without any computational contribution; (3) combination of network analysis and paper coding as complementary lenses to answer the two research questions independently. Key Results From the 105 included papers (63 conference, 42 journal; spanning 2004–2025): 68/105 aim to generate an executable simulation model; 32 use PM to support specific modeling tasks without full model generation. DES dominates at 90/105; ABM appears in 9 papers, SD in 5. 85/105 use real-life data; 38 use synthetic data. 36/105 (34%) report no validation; 55 use quantitative validation only; 7 combine quantitative and qualitative. Healthcare is the top domain (26 papers), followed by manufacturing (12). 20/105 are fully automated; 85 are semi-automated. For BPS modeling tasks, control-flow definition is addressed by PM in 79/105 papers, making it the dominant use case. Duration estimation appears in 68 papers, entity arrival rate in 49, gateway routing logic in 44, resource roles in 24, resource schedules in 20, and waiting time specification in 25. Tasks such as multitasking, looping behavior, and resource cooperation relationships each appear in fewer than 10 papers. The Heuristics Miner (9 papers), Fuzzy Miner (12), Split Miner (12), and Inductive Miner (11) are the most used discovery algorithms. ProM is the dominant PM tool (31 papers), with Python-based solutions growing from near-zero before 2020 to 15 papers in 2021–2025. The most active research community clusters around Wil van der Aalst (degree 19 in co-authorship network) and Marlon Dumas (degree 19); 261 individual authors are identified across 53 connected components, indicating a fragmented research landscape with several autonomous groups. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW FORMULATION. The paper extends the BPS modeling task framework from Martin et al. [4] with 6 empirically grounded new tasks, constituting a genuine framework update. The bibliometric network analysis layer adds a structural perspective not previously applied to this subfield. The core literature synthesis is competent execution of standard SLR methodology (PRISMA) applied to a previously uncharted intersection; the survey's contribution is organization and gap identification, not a new algorithm or formal result. Rigor & Reproducibility Inter-annotator agreement is explicitly reported (Cohen's kappa 0.53–0.63), which is a credibility positive and unusual for SLRs in this domain. Network clustering sensitivity analysis (perturbation of 5% nodes, 10% edges) confirms structural stability. All SLR process files, coding spreadsheets, and network images are publicly released at https://t.ly/VIIGc. The PRISMA flow diagram is fully populated (Fig. 2) with counts at each exclusion step, enabling replication of the search. The review itself makes no empirical claims about PM algorithm accuracy or BPS model quality — it characterizes what the literature reports — so Goodhart's Law concerns apply only to the reviewed papers, not to the review methodology. A methodological concern: 34% of reviewed papers report no validation, and many qualitative validations provide insufficient procedural detail to judge reliability. The review correctly identifies this as a field-level problem requiring standardized validation frameworks. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: THEORETICAL (systematic review synthesizing existing literature; no new experiments run by the authors). - Sound: PRISMA methodology is correctly applied; inclusion/exclusion criteria are explicit, the nine exclusion codes are tabulated (Table 2), and inter-annotator agreement is quantified. The network sensitivity analysis confirms robustness of the thematic clusters. - General: Findings apply to the BPM/simulation intersection as sampled from four major academic databases (2004–2025, peer-reviewed, English only); generalization to grey literature, vendor white papers, and non-English research communities is explicitly out of scope. - Tight: Not applicable (no formal bounds). Task coverage counts depend on the coding scheme's operational definitions, which are described but not with enough precision to guarantee independent replication at the level of individual paper assignments. - Implementable: Directly implementable — Table 5 maps 22 BPS modeling tasks to PM algorithms and tools, providing a decision guide for practitioners selecting automation approaches for specific modeling problems. The public repository extends usability. The paper delivers a publicly available coded dataset and updated BPS modeling task taxonomy as reusable artifacts. Strengths - The per-task decomposition framework (Table 5) is the paper's most durable contribution: it extends the Martin et al. [4] foundational taxonomy with 6 empirically discovered tasks and makes the automation gap concrete and navigable for practitioners. - The dual-method design is genuinely complementary — network analysis surfaces latent community and thematic structure without author-imposed categories, while paper coding provides structured taxonomic depth. Few SLRs combine both. - The discussion in Section 6 is substantive rather than perfunctory: it derives five specific research challenges (validation standardization, semi-automation prevalence, model interpretability, concept drift adaptation, ABM/DES paradigm selection) directly from the survey data, each accompanied by concrete future research directions. - Cross-paradigm scope (DES + ABM + SD) is unique among the five identified comparable surveys, enabling the concrete finding that ABM is used in only 10/105 papers despite its theoretical advantages for multi-entity process interactions — a gap the paper credibly attributes to tooling and familiarity rather than unsuitability. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge that 36/105 papers perform no validation, weakening the evidence base. They note that qualitative expert-based validations lack standardized procedures, making reliability hard to judge. They acknowledge that resource behavior modeling tasks are underserved partly due to sparse event log data rather than algorithmic limitations. They explicitly scope out papers where PM is used conceptually or only for validation without model construction, which excludes some relevant work. These are frank and substantive acknowledgements rather than pro forma hedges. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The temporal trend analysis (Section 5.5) uses four unequal time bins (2007–2016 = 10 years; 2017–2019 = 3 years; 2020–2022 = 3 years; 2023–2025 = 3 years), making raw counts in the earliest bin non-comparable to later bins without per-year normalization. The 20-paper 2007–2016 bin against 29-paper 2023–2025 bin understates the acceleration. - The co-occurrence network analyzes abstract text only, not full-paper text, potentially missing algorithm and tool names that appear primarily in methodology sections. This limitation is not discussed. - The classification of papers as "fully automated" vs. "semi-automated" uses a binary distinction (20 vs. 85 papers) without specifying the operational threshold, making this coding decision the hardest to replicate independently. - The paper does not break down validation absence by conference vs. journal venue. Given the 60/40 conference/journal split, the 34% no-validation rate almost certainly skews toward conference papers, and presenting this breakdown would sharpen the practical implication. - The paper includes only one paper using the Object-Centric Process Mining (OCPM) paradigm, and the discussion correctly flags this as a gap; however, the review's inclusion criteria (requiring PM as a core topic in a simulation context) may have systematically excluded early-stage OCPM-simulation work that exists as preprints or workshop papers not indexed in the four databases. Bottom Line A BPS practitioner or process mining researcher who needs to understand which simulation modeling tasks can be automated today, with what tools, and where the real gaps are will find this review the most organized and current entry point available; researchers seeking algorithmic contributions will find nothing here beyond the synthesis.
Search Results
#87 — Moderately Innovative (59)

Fast LeWorldModel (16 ▲ )

Yuntian Gao, Xiangyu Xu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Fast LeWorldModel (Fast-LeWM), a latent world model that replaces autoregressive one-step rollout with parallel action-prefix prediction, enabling all future latents in a planning horizon to be estimated in a single forward pass. - Why it's important: Autoregressive rollout in LeWM-style planners dominates CEM solve time and accumulates latent prediction errors that compound with horizon length, degrading planning reliability at practically relevant scales. - How it does it: A causal Transformer encodes all action prefixes simultaneously; a parallel latent predictor maps each prefix token plus the anchor latent directly to its corresponding future latent, supervised by dense per-prefix targets that force the model to learn how cumulative action effects evolve across horizons. - The good: Fast-LeWM reduces dynamics-module time 3.9x (31.4s to 8.0s) and CEM solve time 48% (54.4s to 28.3s) on Two-Room while raising average planning success from 85.8% to 90.5% (92.0% with self-consistency) across four tasks, all at comparable model size (17.9M vs. 18.0M parameters). - The missing: The entire evaluation uses H=5 planning horizon on exactly the four tasks from the LeWM paper; the central claim that benefits grow with horizon is supported only by open-loop error growth plots, not by planning success experiments at longer horizons, and no error bars are reported. - Read it or not: Researchers building JEPA-style world models for goal-conditioned visual planning should read this paper; those working on reward-based RL, high-dimensional action spaces, or longer-horizon tasks will find the empirical scope too narrow to transfer conclusions directly.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Rigor (58) and Resilience (48) pull the composite down: the ablations are well-designed and code is released, but there are no error bars or multiple seeds anywhere, margins are as small as 2pp, and the method is developed and evaluated exclusively on the primary baseline's four-task suite (Goodhart risk). Resilience suffers because the central thesis — benefits grow with horizon — is supported only by open-loop error-slope plots, never by planning-success experiments beyond H=5. Novelty (65) is a clean NEW METHOD (parallel action-prefix interface replacing autoregressive rollout) but not a new formulation, and Completeness (50) is hurt by the absent limitations section and unaddressed DINO-WM gap. Composite: 0.20*65 + 0.25*58 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*48 + 0.10*62 = 59.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 50 Resilience 48 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Latent world models built on Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) support reconstruction-free, reward-free, goal-conditioned visual planning. The dynamics bottleneck in LeWM-style systems is that the transition model F_φ(z_t, a_t) is local: it predicts only the next latent z_{t+1}. To evaluate a candidate action sequence of length H under Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) planning, the model must apply F_φ autoregressively H times, generating intermediate imagined latents z_{t+1},...,z_{t+H-1} even though only z_{t+H} is scored against the goal. This creates two coupled problems: (1) sequential rollout becomes a wall-clock bottleneck when CEM evaluates many candidate sequences, and (2) errors introduced at early imagined states propagate into all later predictions, so open-loop rollout error compounds with horizon length. The result is a system that is both slow and increasingly unreliable for longer-horizon planning. Key Idea Instead of predicting the next latent from the current imagined latent one step at a time, predict every future latent directly from the observed anchor latent z_t and a compact summary of the corresponding action prefix. A causal Transformer over the action sequence produces prefix tokens where token k encodes the accumulated effect of actions a_t,...,a_{t+k-1}. A parallel predictor then maps (z_t, p_{t,k}) to ẑ_{t+k} for all k in one forward pass. Because predictions are anchored to the real observed latent rather than to prior predicted latents, sequential error propagation is structurally eliminated rather than merely regularized. Methodology Fast-LeWM has three components. (1) A shared visual encoder f_θ maps observations to latent states z_t ∈ R^d, following LeWM without modification. (2) An Action-Prefix Encoder E_ψ, implemented as a causal Transformer (3 layers, 6 heads, per-head dimension 32, token dimension 192, sinusoidal positional encodings) over a state-action token sequence. The current latent z_t is mapped via a two-layer MLP (hidden 768) to a state token prepended as the 0-th position; the remaining positions are action embeddings. Causal masking ensures position k attends only to a_t,...,a_{t+k-1}, outputting prefix token p_{t,k} that encodes the accumulated effect of the first k actions conditioned on the current state context. (3) A Parallel Latent Predictor G_φ: a 6-layer action-modulated residual MLP (latent dimension 192, hidden 2048, fusion width 768, AdaLN-zero modulation, dropout 0.1) that maps (z_t, p_{t,k}) to ẑ_{t+k} for all k simultaneously. Training objective: L_prefix = (1/H) Σ_{k=1}^H ||ẑ_{t+k} - z_{t+k}||_2^2, plus SIGReg anti-collapse regularization (L_AP = L_prefix + λ SIGReg(Z)). This dense multi-horizon loss directly supervises every prefix token with its corresponding future observed latent, replacing LeWM's single next-step prediction loss. At planning time, each CEM candidate action sequence passes through one prefix-encoding pass and one parallel prediction pass, yielding the terminal estimate ẑ_{t+H} without any sequential rollout. An optional self-consistency penalty C^(m) = C^(m)_goal + β||ẑ^(m)_{t+H} - z̄^(m)_{t+H}||_2^2 (β=1) scores candidates by the agreement between the direct H-step prefix estimate and a two-step prefix decomposition estimate, at no extra model calls. Three critical design choices: (a) Causal masking in the prefix encoder, which is well-justified to prevent future action leakage while enabling parallel processing; (b) Dense per-prefix supervision vs. terminal-only supervision, which the ablation directly validates as essential; (c) State token conditioning of the prefix encoder, which the ablation shows provides measurable benefit (w/o state token drops Two-Room from 98% to 94%, Reacher from 88% to 82%) with minimal overhead. Key Results On four goal-conditioned visual control tasks (Two-Room, Reacher, PushT, OGBench-Cube) using the same offline datasets and CEM protocol as LeWM: Fast-LeWM achieves average success rate 90.5% vs. 85.8% for LeWM; Fast-LeWM+Self-Consistency achieves 92.0%. Per-task: Two-Room 98% vs. 87% (+11pp), Reacher 88% vs. 86% (+2pp), PushT 96% vs. 96% (tied), Cube 80% vs. 74% (+6pp). Fast-LeWM underperforms DINO-WM on Two-Room (98% vs. 100%) and Cube (80% vs. 86%), though DINO-WM uses pretrained DINOv2 visual features rather than learning representations end-to-end. Planning speed on Two-Room (single NVIDIA 4090): dynamics-module time 8.0s vs. 31.4s (3.92x faster); CEM solve time 28.3s vs. 54.4s (1.92x, 48% reduction). Fast-LeWM requires 1 model call per candidate vs. 5 for LeWM. Open-loop MSE loss growth rate (least-squares linear slope over 50 raw steps, Figure 3): Fast-LeWM slope is consistently smaller than LeWM across all four tasks at both H=25 and H=50 prediction lengths. Initial prediction error is also lower in most tasks. Physical state probing on PushT (Table 3): MLP probes on Fast-LeWM outperform LeWM on all three measured variables — agent location (MSE 0.001 vs. 0.004), block location (0.000 vs. 0.001), block angle (0.009 vs. 0.021). Linear probe performance is comparable, suggesting Fast-LeWM encodes richer nonlinearly-accessible physical state. Ablations (Table 4): Long-Action LeWM (5→25-step action block, no prefix structure) achieves only 71% average success (vs. 90.5% for Fast-LeWM), confirming that the prefix structure and dense supervision — not simply longer temporal span — drive the gains. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The action-prefix prediction interface for latent world models, combining causal prefix encoding with dense multi-horizon supervision and parallel latent prediction, is the core contribution. Individual components (causal Transformers, multi-step prediction losses, JEPA-style latent world models) are established; the novelty is the specific formulation that replaces the autoregressive rollout interface with a parallel prefix interface during both training and planning, yielding simultaneous speed and accuracy benefits. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations in Table 4 cover the three main design choices (prefix structure, dense supervision, state conditioning) and are informative. No error bars or multiple training seeds are reported for any table; with margins as small as 2pp (Reacher) across only 4 tasks, statistical significance cannot be assessed. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/Yuntian-Gao/Fast-LeWorldModel. No pretrained weights or dataset splits are separately distributed; experiments use LeWM's offline dataset protocol. This is a preprint and has not been peer-reviewed. Timing measurements are on a single NVIDIA 4090 and are hardware-specific. The method is evaluated exclusively on the same task suite as the primary baseline (LeWM), which introduces risk that development choices were inadvertently tuned to this distribution. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Yes; 17.9M parameters trained and evaluated on a single NVIDIA 4090. - Efficient: Dynamics-module time 3.92x lower than LeWM (8.0s vs. 31.4s) at comparable parameter count; the savings come from replacing H sequential model invocations with 1 parallel invocation. - Effective: Improves or matches LeWM success rate across 4 tasks while also reducing open-loop prediction error; MLP probing results confirm the learned latent space encodes richer physical state. Evidence is convincing on the tested benchmarks. - Scalable: Tested only at H=5 planning horizon with action skip 5 (25 primitive steps total) on small-scale manipulation and navigation environments. The paper argues benefits should grow with horizon based on open-loop error growth plots but provides no planning success experiments at H>5. No evaluation on longer action sequences or higher-dimensional action spaces. A software artifact (code) is released; no trained model weights are provided. Strengths - The prefix formulation simultaneously addresses speed and error accumulation with a single architectural change, rather than treating them as separate problems; the ablation confirms both contributions independently. - Dense per-prefix supervision produces latent representations with richer nonlinearly-accessible physical state information, as validated by MLP probing on PushT — a result not obviously predictable from the speed motivation alone. - The ablation design is well-chosen: Long-Action LeWM isolates the effect of temporal span, Terminal-only Fast-LeWM isolates the contribution of dense vs. endpoint-only supervision, and w/o state token isolates context conditioning. Each ablation changes exactly one factor. - The self-consistency planning term is a zero-cost extension at inference time that extracts signal from an already-available prefix decomposition, adding 1.5pp average success without any model change. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors do not include a limitations section or paragraph. The conclusion is uniformly positive. No mention is made of the narrow evaluation horizon (H=5), the absence of error bars, the restriction to four tasks from the primary baseline's benchmark, or the gap relative to DINO-WM on two of the four tasks. This absence is a credibility negative. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No error bars or multiple seeds across any quantitative table. The Reacher gain (+2pp) and the Cube gain (+6pp) could plausibly fall within run-to-run variance, yet this is unverifiable from the reported numbers. - The paper's core claim — that benefits grow with planning horizon — is supported only by open-loop error growth slope comparisons, not by planning success experiments at H>5. A 10-step or 20-step horizon experiment would directly validate or challenge the central thesis. - All four evaluation environments come from the LeWM benchmark. No independent task or environment was added to test generalization of the prefix design outside the original benchmark distribution. - The comparison to DINO-WM (which uses a pretrained DINOv2 backbone) conflates the effect of the dynamics interface with the quality of the visual backbone. Fast-LeWM does not close the gap with DINO-WM on Two-Room or Cube despite the stronger dynamics model, suggesting visual representation quality may be a larger factor than the dynamics interface at the tested scale. - No evaluation on longer action sequences (H>5) or higher-dimensional action spaces; it is unknown whether the causal Transformer prefix encoder scales gracefully to much longer action vectors or sparser reward landscapes. Bottom Line Practitioners building JEPA-style latent world models for goal-conditioned visual planning should read this paper: the action-prefix reformulation is a well-motivated, structurally sound change that measurably improves both planning speed and accuracy at no parameter cost, with code available for direct adoption.
Recommended 2606.26217 PDF
#88 — Moderately Innovative (59)

DataClaw0: Agentic Tailoring Multimodal Data from Raw Streams (65 ▲ )

Cong Wan, Zeyu Guo, Zijian Cai, Jiangyang Li, SongLin Dong, Lin Peng, Xiangyang Luo, Zhiheng Ma, Yihong Gong · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DataClaw₀, a 9B-parameter VLM trained via SFT and GRPO to convert raw multimodal streams (tutorial videos, robot trajectories, GUI logs) into intent-conditioned, schema-aligned structured training data. - Why it's important: High-quality multimodal supervision is the primary bottleneck for post-training foundation models, and existing passive annotation pipelines hallucinate or produce low-density outputs when applied to long, temporally complex streams. - How it does it: A two-stage pipeline first extracts deterministic factual anchors (timestamps, object states, OCR text, GUI events) bottom-up without any LLM, then synthesizes structured supervision top-down using a strong VLM conditioned on those anchors plus a domain-specific intent; the trained model is further aligned via GRPO with rule-based rewards for format compliance, spatio-temporal anchor fidelity, and reasoning conciseness. - The good: Downstream fine-tuning on DataClaw₀-generated data matches or exceeds Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview annotation quality on end-to-end task completion metrics (GUI Task Success Rate 15.6% vs. 14.2%, VQA Overall Accuracy 33.2% vs. 31.5%, video FVD 288.6 vs. 295.4) at equal data budget, using a 9B open-source model instead of a proprietary API. - The missing: The central claim that anchors reduce hallucination relative to direct VLM synthesis is never ablated; the evaluation benchmark has only 200 examples and was constructed by the same group using the same pipeline, creating Goodhart's Law risk; no statistical significance tests accompany any comparison. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building multimodal post-training data pipelines who need a controllable alternative to proprietary annotators; less compelling for those focused on VLM architecture or rigorous benchmark design.
Why this rank: Systems profile (built pipeline + trained 9B model judged by downstream task performance). Impact (62) carries the largest weight — a 9B open model plus lightweight anchor scripts matching Gemini-3.1-Pro annotation quality at equal data budget is a credible practitioner alternative, but code/data release is unconfirmed and the cost/latency comparison vs. API annotators is missing. Rigor (52) and Resilience (52) pull the composite down: the central claim that anchors reduce hallucination is never ablated against direct VLM synthesis, the 200-example DataClaw₀-val was built by the same team with the same pipeline (Goodhart, with the Sequence metric reusing the GRPO R_anchor formula), and no significance tests accompany 1–3 pp downstream gaps. Novelty (68) and Completeness (58) are solid — the anchor-then-synthesize decomposition and the DataClaw₀-O scaling-instability negative result are real contributions, honestly disclosed. Composite: 0.15*68 + 0.15*52 + 0.10*62 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*52 + 0.30*62 ≈ 59.
systems Novelty 68 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 58 Resilience 52 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Researchers and practitioners assembling multimodal post-training data face a structural problem: raw streams such as hours-long tutorial videos, embodied agent trajectories, and GUI operation logs are noisy, temporally redundant, and structurally misaligned with downstream training objectives. Existing pipelines either apply heuristic sampling and coarse captioning (cheap but low-density) or directly prompt general VLMs to generate question-answer pairs (adequate for short curated inputs, but prone to hallucination, temporal incoherence, and schema violations on long high-entropy sequences). The gap is the absence of a learnable, intent-conditioned annotation capability that can filter task-irrelevant content, ground evidence in verifiable visual facts, and restructure raw observations into schema-valid supervision for arbitrary downstream use cases spanning GUI navigation, embodied AI, video generation, and instructional content. Key Idea The core insight is that multimodal annotation should be decomposed into a deterministic grounding step and a learned semantic synthesis step. Factual Anchors (timestamps, object states, OCR text, GUI interaction events, scene descriptions, trajectory boundaries) are extracted bottom-up by lightweight domain-expert scripts without any generative model, providing verified low-level facts that cannot be hallucinated. A strong VLM then performs top-down synthesis conditioned on these anchors plus a domain-specific intent instruction, handling higher-order reasoning such as subtask prediction, failure-mode identification, and long-horizon task reconstruction. This separation confines the generative component to a much lower-entropy conditioning space than raw pixel streams, reducing hallucination while preserving flexible intent-driven schema generation. Methodology The data construction pipeline has five stages: (1) candidate discovery scans raw videos or GUI trajectories for segments likely to support the target intent; (2) anchor and context packaging converts selected segments into compact requests containing sampled frames, action traces, timestamps, and fragment descriptions; (3) intent-conditioned VLM synthesis calls a strong VLM with a task-specific prompt and a strict output JSON schema; (4) validation and materialization parses generated JSON, validates temporal ranges and required fields, and clips or extracts media artifacts; (5) corpus export writes normalized training samples and separate artifact mapping files. The resulting 34K-sample corpus spans five domains: Daily Life (16,121 samples, 46.4%), Embodied AI (7,381, 21.3%), GUI Agents (5,732, 16.5%), Education (3,243, 9.3%), and AIGC/World Models (2,240, 6.5%). DataClaw₀ is trained in two phases on Qwen3.5-9B. SFT runs for 1 epoch on 34K cleaned instruction examples. GRPO then optimizes with three deterministic rule-based rewards: R_format (AST/regex parser verifying strict schema compliance, binary success/failure), R_anchor (trajectory-shape similarity measuring MAE between predicted and ground-truth frame-wise distributions after normalizing both to K=50 uniformly distributed alignment points and applying exp(-d_shape/0.10)), and R_eff (dynamic CoT length regularization penalizing outputs whose reasoning length falls outside the top-50% reference range within each rollout group). Reward weights are λ_fmt=0.7, λ_anc=1.0, λ_eff=0.3. GRPO normalizes rewards within a sampled group of G outputs per input, then maximizes a clipped importance-sampling objective with KL regularization against the SFT policy. Training uses 8× A100 GPUs. Two deployment variants are defined: DataClaw₀-O (single unified model trained jointly on all domains) and DataClaw₀-E (domain-decoupled expert ensemble with routing to GUI, Embodied, Daily Life, Education, and AIGC specialists at inference). Three critical design decisions: (1) anchors as hallucination guards — motivated by failure analysis of direct VLM annotation on long sequences, but not ablated empirically against synthesis-without-anchors; (2) expert routing over unified training — justified by the DataClaw₀-O scaling curve, which shows severe oscillation (score drops from 53.60 to 47.23 at 7/15 data fraction, then rebounds), while DataClaw₀-E reaches a stable 68.86; (3) R_anchor for spatio-temporal grounding — ablation shows GRPO without R_anchor increases Semantic score slightly but drops Sequence score (70.11 vs. 71.96), confirming the reward's role in preserving temporal fidelity at a small cost to semantic accuracy. Key Results On DataClaw₀-val (200 examples, 5 domains), evaluated on Field, Semantic, and Sequence scores: DataClaw₀-E achieves overall Field=97.53, Semantic=74.94, Sequence=48.86, comparable to Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview (98.12 / 73.85 / 58.50) and GPT-4o (97.27 / 75.15 / 49.43). DataClaw₀-E consistently outperforms DataClaw₀-O (87.65 / 62.46 / 44.82). On the Fuzzy-intent subset, DataClaw₀-E scores Sequence=50.31, above GPT-4o (42.57) and Claude Sonnet 4.6 (42.70), though below Gemini (58.50). Proprietary models retain a Semantic advantage in some settings (GPT-4o: 75.15 overall vs. DataClaw₀-E: 74.94). Downstream fine-tuning (Table 2, equal data budget, same raw streams processed by three annotators): on GUI navigation (AgentNet, base Qwen3.5-4B), DataClaw₀ achieves SSR=38.2%, TSR=15.6% vs. Gemini's SSR=39.5%, TSR=14.2% and zero-shot baseline TSR=1.2%. On action video generation (Ego4D, base Wan2.2-I2V-5B, 200 training clips), DataClaw₀ achieves FVD=288.6, Consistency=75.8%, Contact mAP=51.2 vs. Gemini's FVD=295.4, Consistency=76.2%, Contact mAP=48.5. On spatio-temporal VQA (ReMoT, base Qwen3.5-4B), DataClaw₀ achieves Partial Acc=52.1%, Overall Acc=33.2% vs. Gemini's 53.4% / 31.5%. DataClaw₀ leads on end-to-end completion metrics; Gemini leads on partial/step-level metrics. A notable negative finding: the unified DataClaw₀-O model exhibits unstable scaling under mixed-domain training, with severe task interference between heterogeneous multimodal extraction tasks. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine novelty lies in (a) formalizing multimodal data annotation as a learnable agentic capability with a defined optimization objective (Equation 3), (b) the anchor-then-synthesize pipeline architecture that separates deterministic grounding from learned semantic synthesis, and (c) the DataClaw₀-val benchmark targeting data tailoring quality specifically. The GRPO training recipe and rule-based reward design follow DeepSeek-R1-style approaches applied to a new domain. Expert routing is a standard practice adapted here. The combination is applied to a genuinely new problem setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for reward design (Table 3a: 4 conditions) and expert routing (Table 3b: cross-domain routing). Both are informative: the R_anchor ablation shows a 1.85-point Sequence improvement, and the routing ablation shows near-zero performance when an Embodied expert handles GUI data (Field=0.00). The downstream evaluation controls for data quantity through equal-budget sampling from the same raw streams, which is methodologically sound. Concerns: (1) DataClaw₀-val has 200 examples constructed by the same team using the same pipeline, creating potential in-distribution favoritism. (2) No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals are reported for any Table 1 or Table 2 numbers; differences of 1–3 percentage points between DataClaw₀ and Gemini on downstream tasks could be within noise given small fine-tuning sets (200 clips for video generation). (3) The anchor component's contribution is not ablated — the comparison of anchor-then-synthesize vs. direct VLM synthesis without anchors is the most important missing experiment. (4) The Sequence metric used in DataClaw₀-val is computed via the same trajectory-shape similarity (exp(-d_shape/τ)) used as R_anchor during GRPO training, a Goodhart's Law risk. (5) Code and dataset release status are not confirmed in the paper text, only a project page URL is provided. This is a preprint. Deployability Profile: SYSTEMS (a built pipeline and trained model evaluated by downstream task performance). - Practical: DataClaw₀-E runs as a 9B-parameter model, requiring a single multi-GPU server for fine-tuning and a single GPU for inference; the anchor extraction scripts are lightweight and domain-specific, so a new domain requires writing new scripts, not re-training. - Efficient: Training 34K samples on 8× A100 with SFT+GRPO is feasible; downstream fine-tuning uses 200 clips on 8× A100 80GB, suggesting modest data requirements. Inference throughput cost vs. calling Gemini or GPT-4o APIs directly is not benchmarked, which is the practically relevant comparison. - Effective: Downstream results credibly support the claim that DataClaw₀-generated data produces end-to-end task completion gains comparable to Gemini annotation at equal data budget, across three independent downstream tasks with standard base models. - Scalable: The current corpus is 34K samples, which the authors acknowledge is moderate. The DataClaw₀-O scaling curve shows instability at larger data fractions; DataClaw₀-E scales more stably but only within the current 34K regime. Claims of million-scale extension are aspirational. The paper delivers a trained DataClaw₀-9B model and the DataClaw₀-val benchmark; a project page is provided but full code and data release are not explicitly confirmed. Strengths - The anchor-then-synthesize decomposition provides a principled reduction in hallucination surface area by separating deterministic grounding from learned generation; this architectural insight is transferable to other annotation pipelines beyond the specific domains tested. - The DataClaw₀-O scaling instability finding is a substantive negative result that both motivates the expert architecture and flags a general risk for multi-domain unified annotators, backed by a concrete scaling curve rather than a hand-wavy claim. - The downstream evaluation is the strongest part of the paper: using the same raw streams processed by three different annotators at equal data budget is a clean isolation of annotation quality, and the end-to-end task success metrics (TSR, FVD, Overall Accuracy) are more meaningful than intermediate metrics. - The R_anchor reward design, computing trajectory-shape similarity at K=50 uniformly distributed alignment points via exponential MAE mapping, is a concrete non-trivial extension of GRPO to structured temporal generation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge three limitations: (1) dataset scale remains moderate (34K) compared with large-scale pretraining corpora, and scaling to millions of samples is deferred; (2) the system currently requires user-provided raw streams and does not yet support fully autonomous data collection from interactive environments (simulation, games, GUI emulators); (3) temporal hallucination persists in failure cases, where the model infers chronologically plausible but temporally incorrect event sequences from spatial cues alone, acknowledged as an open challenge for LLM-based architectures. The authors confront these directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The most critical missing experiment is an ablation of anchor extraction vs. direct VLM synthesis without anchors. The entire pipeline motivation rests on anchors reducing hallucination, but Table 3 does not include a condition where the synthesis VLM is called directly on raw frames without precomputed anchors. - DataClaw₀-val's 200-example size and construction by the same authors using the same pipeline methodology creates an implicit circularity: the benchmark may reflect DataClaw₀'s construction biases rather than domain-general data tailoring quality. - No cost or latency comparison between DataClaw₀ annotation and Gemini/GPT-4o API annotation is provided. The practical adoption argument requires knowing whether a 9B local model + anchor scripts is faster and cheaper than API calls. - The GRPO reward weights (λ_fmt=0.7, λ_anc=1.0, λ_eff=0.3) are reported without sensitivity analysis; given that the five domains have very different temporal structures (GUI has dense action sequences, Daily Life has sparse events), fixed weights may under-serve some domains. - Downstream fine-tuning uses 200 training clips for video generation and unspecified sample counts for GUI and VQA; the generalization of DataClaw₀'s data efficiency advantage to larger training regimes is untested. - Table 1 reports DataClaw₀-E with Embodied Sequence=15.26 and Daily Sequence=42.59, substantially below Gemini (33.14 and 47.25 respectively), which are not discussed in the text. Bottom Line A practitioner building multimodal post-training data pipelines will find the anchor-then-synthesize architecture, expert-routing analysis, and downstream evaluation methodology worth studying closely, but should treat the efficiency claims cautiously given the missing anchor ablation, the 200-example benchmark, and the absence of statistical tests.
Recommended 2606.21337 PDF
#89 — Moderately Innovative (58)

LLM-as-Code Agentic Programming for Agent Harness

J Qi, Z Fu, J Gao, W Zhang, H Yan, X Wu, X Zhao · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes Agentic Programming, a paradigm in which program code owns all control flow and LLMs are invoked as typed leaf functions ("LLM-as-Code") only at reasoning or generation nodes, instantiated via a Python @agentic decorator. - Why it's important: LLM-orchestrated agents fail on long-horizon tasks through three compounding architectural failures (token explosion, control-flow hallucination, unreliable completion) that stronger models or better prompts cannot eliminate by construction. - How it does it: Control flow is written as ordinary code; the execution history forms a DAG whose context per call is bounded by call depth O(depth) rather than total steps O(steps), so no call ever carries the full task history. - The good: A GUI automation agent built on this paradigm achieves 86.8% overall success on OSWorld in at most 15 steps, exceeding the 80.4% achieved by the strongest prior system (Holo3-35B-A3B) using 100 steps. - The missing: No ablation isolates whether the OSWorld gain comes from code-controlled loops, DAG context management, the 15-step forcing function, or some combination; the paper evaluates only one benchmark despite motivating the work with long-horizon software engineering tasks (SWE-bench). - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone designing agent orchestration frameworks or reasoning about reliability of LLM pipelines; less useful for practitioners who need ablation-backed evidence before changing architecture.
Why this rank: Systems profile. The driver is Non-Obviousness (78): the category-error framing unifying token explosion, control-flow hallucination, and unreliable completion under one architectural root cause is a genuine conceptual contribution, and the O(depth) vs O(steps) context bound is precise and mechanistic. Novelty (72) is high on the new formulation, tempered because the constituent mechanisms draw on CodeAct, DSPy, and LangGraph. The headline OSWorld result (86.8% vs 80.4%, same Claude Sonnet 4.6 base at 72.1%) cleanly isolates architecture from model capability. But Rigor (45) is the binding weakness: zero ablations, so the gain cannot be attributed to code control, DAG context, or the 15-step cap; single benchmark despite SWE-bench motivation; no code, no error bars; workshop preprint. Resilience (48) and Completeness (50) reflect the unexamined 15-step constraint and the one-benchmark evidence base. Weighted: 0.15*72+0.15*45+0.10*78+0.15*50+0.15*48+0.30*60 = 58.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 50 Resilience 48 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Mainstream LLM agent frameworks (ReAct, AutoGen, OpenHands, MetaGPT) assign control flow to the LLM: the model decides which tools to call, in what order, and when to stop. For short tasks this appears adequate, but on long-horizon tasks three failures compound. Token explosion: context grows O(steps × avg_output) until truncation destroys earlier reasoning. Control-flow hallucination: the model reports completion before verification runs. Unreliable completion: the model abandons a correct earlier hypothesis when distracted by more recent outputs. The paper argues all three originate from the same root cause: assigning deterministic operations (looping, branching, sequencing, termination) to a probabilistic system. This is a category error, not an implementation shortfall, and no prompt engineering or model scaling eliminates it because each control decision is drawn from a distribution whose per-step accuracy below 1 compounds over steps. Key Idea The core insight is that deterministic and probabilistic operations require different computational substrates, and the solution is to separate them cleanly: program code owns the control graph, and LLMs are invoked as ordinary typed functions only at nodes requiring reasoning or generation. Context then tracks the call tree rather than a flat growing log, so each call's input is bounded by its ancestor chain (O(depth)) rather than the entire execution history (O(steps)). Completed subtrees collapse to one-line summaries, nothing is discarded at the harness level, but no call ever sees the full history. Methodology The paradigm has four components. First, the code-driven workflow: the agent's sequence of actions is written as Python; an @agentic decorator marks LLM call sites, hiding prompt template, model selection, and input/output schema behind a typed function signature. The surrounding code owns loops and branches; the LLM is invoked only inside those loops. Second, DAG-structured context: the execution history is the call graph (a tree in the simple case, a DAG once parallel branches merge). A running call sees its full ancestor chain; when a call returns, its frame collapses to a summary that the parent retains. This bounds context length at O(depth), not O(steps). Third, multi-agent collaboration: concurrent agents are sibling branches of one call graph, each scoped to its own path; outputs merge where branches rejoin, using typed return values rather than free-form inter-agent conversation. Fourth, self-programmed evolution: a meta-function proposes replacement @agentic functions when tests fail; the replacement is committed as code only after passing the test suite, converting a probabilistic proposal into a deterministic executable. Critical design decisions: (a) the call graph is unfolded at runtime rather than declared in advance (contrast with LangGraph's pre-declared graph), which is appropriate for tasks where needed steps are only known after earlier LLM judgments — well-justified; (b) context release via stack unwinding rather than a shared state object — avoids the problem where a probabilistic loop corrupts explicit state, though it requires that subtree outputs be adequate summaries; (c) the @agentic decorator homogenizes LLM calls with ordinary function calls — enables standard software engineering practices (unit tests, mocking, localized failure) but the paper does not specify how the runtime builds prompts or handles streaming, so the full implementation is opaque. Key Results The primary result is the OSWorld GUI automation benchmark. The authors' agent achieves 86.8% overall success rate (Chrome 93.5%, Multi-Apps 80.0%, OS 100.0%) with a 15-step maximum, compared to 80.4% for Holo3-35B-A3B (100 steps), 78.3% for OpenAPA with Gemini-3.1-pro (100 steps), and 72.1% for Claude Sonnet 4.6 used as a general model (100 steps). The authors' agent uses the same underlying model (Claude Sonnet 4.6) as one of the baselines, directly isolating the architectural contribution from model capability. Baselines are drawn from the OSWorld public leaderboard accessed 2026-06-02. A second agent — an overnight research pipeline — is mentioned but not evaluated quantitatively. No statistical significance tests, error bars, or multiple-seed results are reported. This is a preprint accepted at the KDD 2026 Workshop on Agentic Software Engineering (AgenticSE), not a main-venue peer-reviewed result. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The category-error framing unifying token explosion, hallucination, and unreliable completion under one architectural root cause is a genuine conceptual contribution. The DAG-structured context with call-depth-bounded inputs is a novel design. However, the individual mechanisms draw from existing work: code as the action representation (CodeAct), Python-composed pipelines (DSPy), code-defined graphs (LangGraph), and self-improving pipelines. The contribution is in the integration and the explicit argument that code must own the loop, not just the action. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations are reported; the paper cannot attribute the OSWorld gain to any specific component (code control flow, DAG context, or the 15-step forcing function). No error bars or significance tests accompany the benchmark numbers. No code repository is referenced; the @agentic decorator and runtime are described conceptually but not released. The comparison against baselines from a public leaderboard is sound but introduces uncontrolled differences in prompt design and model versions. The 15-step cap versus 100-step baselines makes the result harder to interpret: the agent wins in fewer steps, but it is unknown whether the agent would succeed or fail on tasks requiring more than 15 steps. The paper cites its own production systems as evidence but provides no independent evaluation of those systems. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS (the paper builds and evaluates a deployed GUI automation agent). - Practical: The framework runs on standard Python with any LLM API, requiring no specialized hardware beyond what the underlying model demands. - Efficient: The DAG context design reduces per-call token cost from O(steps × avg_output) to O(depth × avg_output), a substantial reduction for deep tasks, though no token-count comparison against baselines is reported. - Effective: The OSWorld result (86.8% vs. 80.4% best prior, same base model at 72.1%) provides direct evidence of effectiveness on GUI automation; no evidence is provided for the software engineering use case that motivates the work. - Scalable: The 15-step cap is presented as a strength but is also a constraint; the paper does not report what happens at greater depths or on tasks with unknown step counts, and the claim that O(depth) context management scales cleanly is not tested beyond the GUI domain. The work delivers a conceptual framework and a @agentic decorator pattern with a reference implementation, but no public library or CLI. Strengths - The category-error argument (deterministic vs. probabilistic operations) is precise and unifies three previously disparate failure modes under one root cause, making it actionable for framework designers. - The DAG context bound (O(depth) vs. O(steps)) is a concrete, quantifiable architectural improvement with clear mechanistic justification, not a heuristic. - The OSWorld result is striking: the same base model (Claude Sonnet 4.6) achieves 72.1% as an unstructured orchestrator and 86.8% under Agentic Programming, with 85% fewer steps. - The comparative analysis of related work (LangGraph, DSPy, CodeAct, Reflexion) is unusually precise and honest about the boundary between this work and its predecessors. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that Agentic Programming does not subsume every agent use case: fully exploratory tasks with no known structure (open-ended brainstorming, research without a stage model) may benefit from LLM-driven orchestration. The claim is narrowed to structured workflows. No further limitations are acknowledged. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The core empirical claim rests on one benchmark (OSWorld GUI automation); the paper's stated motivation is long-horizon software engineering tasks, yet no SWE-bench or similar evaluation is reported. - Without ablations, the source of the improvement is unknown: code-controlled iteration, DAG context scoping, the 15-step cap (which prevents step-burning regardless of paradigm), or some combination. - The 15-step maximum is framed as an advantage but is a binding constraint whose consequences for harder tasks are unexamined. - The "overnight research pipeline" is mentioned as a second production agent but contributes zero quantitative evidence. - No implementation is released; the @agentic runtime's prompt construction, streaming handling, and error recovery are described at an abstraction level that does not permit reproduction. - The paper does not address dynamically structured workflows where required steps cannot be known before the run begins, which is common in real software engineering tasks. Bottom Line The category-error framing and the OSWorld result together make this worth reading for anyone building or evaluating agent orchestration systems, but the single-benchmark evaluation without ablations means the architectural claims are better treated as a well-motivated hypothesis than an empirically settled result.
Recommended 2606.15874 PDF
#90 — Moderately Innovative (58)

TelcoAgent: A Scalable 5G Multi-KPM Forecasting With 3GPP-Grounded Explainability

G Kim, D Ron, S Singh, S Moogi, P Gajjar, V Koduri… · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TelcoAgent, a three-pipeline LLM framework that combines zero-shot time-series foundation model (TSFM) forecasting with an autonomously constructed 3GPP knowledge graph and ReAct-based causal reasoning to forecast 7 KPMs across 200 5G cells and generate numerically verified, operationally grounded recommendations without site-specific retraining. - Why it's important: Network operators managing hundreds of 5G base stations cannot afford per-cell supervised model retraining pipelines, and raw ML forecasts without causal explanations leave operators unable to take targeted corrective action before degradation occurs. - How it does it: Three sequential LLM agents automatically parse 13 3GPP specification documents into a confidence-filtered causal knowledge graph; Chronos-2 performs zero-shot multivariate forecasting; a ReAct agent applies PAX-TS perturbation-based attribution to compute a cross-channel sensitivity matrix and retrieves directed causal paths from the knowledge graph to ground recommendations in traceable protocol logic. - The good: Chronos-2 zero-shot forecasting outperforms all three per-cell supervised baselines (N-BEATS, GRU, MLP) across all 7 KPMs on a 3-month, 200-cell real operator dataset, achieving nRMSE of 0.12–0.19 vs. 0.16–0.26 for N-BEATS, while most supervised models reach MASE > 1 (worse than a seasonal naive baseline). - The missing: Explanation quality is assessed entirely by ORANSight-2.0, an LLM judge developed by overlapping authors, introducing a circular evaluation; no domain-expert evaluation, no alternative explainability baseline, and no closed-loop test showing that applied recommendations actually improve network performance. - Read it or not: Telecom ML practitioners and O-RAN system architects should read this for the zero-shot forecasting benchmark and the automated 3GPP knowledge graph construction pipeline; researchers focused on explainability methodology should note the evaluation circularity before treating the explanation scores as ground truth.
Why this rank: Systems profile.
