Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — AIR is deployed in Kuaishou production and evaluated by a real A/B test. - Practical: The online serving path requires only vector similarity search and MHA fusion; the offline path requires batch LLM processing (Qwen3-4B) plus a vector index, all runnable on commercial hardware. - Efficient: Online latency of 20.134ms is 397× below direct LLM invocation; offline LLM processing cost per user-event and total index size are not stated, making total system cost of ownership unclear. - Effective: The A/B test directly measures GMV and order count on live Kuaishou traffic, providing genuine evidence that the system solves the stated business problem rather than a proxy. - Scalable: The system is claimed to support Kuaishou's 400M+ user base; horizontal scaling via parallel intent queries is described, but index update frequency, staleness handling as user behavior evolves, and performance under index size growth are not analyzed. The system delivers a deployed production service but no released software artifact, dataset, or trained model. Strengths - The offline-to-online decoupling pattern solves a real and general industrial constraint; the architecture is transferable to any platform that wants LLM semantics without LLM latency. - The A/B test with +3.446% GMV on 5.08% of Kuaishou production traffic is high-quality evidence, substantially stronger than academic benchmark comparisons alone. - The ablation clearly demonstrates that Intent Retrieval and Intent Tree are not merely additive modules but strongly coupled components whose joint removal causes 34–60% performance collapse, giving meaningful insight into the system's structure. - The target-aware retrieval mechanism — filtering behaviors by cosine similarity to the candidate item — directly attacks the noise problem in multi-domain histories and is validated by the ablation results. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper does not include a limitations section and the conclusion does not identify failure modes, scope boundaries, or cases where AIR underperforms. This omission is notable for a system paper claiming industrial deployment; a practitioner cannot assess from the paper when AIR would fail or degrade. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The central approximation assumption — that composing atomic per-event intents is semantically equivalent to holistic sequence-level LLM reasoning — is never empirically tested. The paper shows AIR is faster than a real-time LLM baseline but never shows it achieves comparable recommendation quality to one. - The 400× throughput comparison is against naive online Qwen3-4B inference. Optimized alternatives (batched LLM inference, a distilled smaller model, or a retrieval-augmented LLM with a shortened context) are not discussed or compared. - Public benchmarks use 2014 Amazon data with 9K–52K users — several orders of magnitude below the industrial setting. The claim that results on these datasets validate the method at industrial scale is not supported. - The gains over LLMCDSR on Food-Kitchen (0.78% and 3.07% HR@10) are within plausible random variation given the absence of significance tests and the small dataset size. - Action-weighting hyperparameters α, β, γ and temporal decay λ are critical inputs to the intent tree but are never reported or ablated; a reviewer cannot assess their sensitivity. - Offline intent staleness is not addressed: how frequently atomic intent pairs must be regenerated when user profiles, video content, or product catalogs change at platform scale is an operational question with real cost implications that the paper ignores. Bottom Line A practitioner building LLM-powered recommendation at a content-commerce platform gains a deployable architectural pattern and credible production evidence of business impact; a program committee reviewer will note that the approximation quality of atomic intent composition is unvalidated, the public benchmark gains are often marginal, and no code or data is released.
Recommended 2606.10357 PDF
#107 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Semantically Oriented Method оf Knowledge Presentation in Artificial Intelligence Systems for Cyber Security

Y Kostiuk, E Molchanova, P Skladannyi, V Sokolov… · preprints.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces an Information-Semantic System (ISS) that transforms raw network telemetry (Primary Semantic Information, PSI) into a compact set of semantic predicates (Secondary Semantic Information, SSI) via frame-slot structures and production rules, then uses a sliding short-term memory (STM) window and rule-based inference to map multi-event temporal patterns to MITRE ATT&CK techniques with SHAP/LIME explanations. - Why it's important: SOC analysts using ML-only IDS face black-box decisions, no automatic MITRE ATT&CK mapping, and high false-positive rates that inflate triage time; no existing system closes all three gaps in a single real-time pipeline. - How it does it: Raw attributes are discretized into semantic predicate categories (e.g., AuthFailureObserved, BruteForceSuspect, DoSSuspect) via threshold rules and a port/protocol dictionary, accumulated over a time window Δt in the STM buffer, then matched against production rules that fire ATT&CK technique assignments with confidence scores and trigger SHAP/LIME explanation generation. - The good: Ablation (Table 12) shows removing the reasoning module alone drops F1 from 0.92 to 0.82 (ΔF1=−0.10), providing credible evidence that semantic reasoning — not just feature engineering — drives the improvement. - The missing: The production SOC validation uses controlled scenario injections into depersonalized background traffic under NDA; the claimed 18–34% FPR reduction and 22–38% triage time reduction cannot be independently verified, and the critical STM threshold parameters θ₁–θ₅ are not publicly released. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building semantic enrichment layers for SIEM/XDR or researchers developing neuro-symbolic IDS; skip if you need peer-reviewed evidence or a deployable system that does not require significant custom ontology engineering.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Rigor (62) is the high point — five-run ablations isolating the reasoning module (ΔF1=+0.10), ANOVA/paired t-tests, error bars, and code+artifacts released on GitHub/Zenodo — but it is dragged down by preprint status, threshold parameters θ₁–θ₅ left unreleased, and reliance on stale KDD/NSL-KDD benchmarks. Novelty (55) is integration-level: every component (ontological IDS, production rules, SHAP/LIME, sliding-window buffers) exists in prior work, with the dataset-agnostic PSI→SSI abstraction as the genuine new piece. Resilience (48) is the weakest dimension — the operational FPR/triage claims rest on controlled injections into anonymized traffic that cannot be independently verified, and the θ-parameters appear tuned on the same CICIDS-2017 used for evaluation (Goodhart risk). Completeness (58) benefits from honest, direct limitation acknowledgment but is hurt by an ambiguous SOTA comparison on a different subset. The composite lands in the Moderately Innovative band: solid neuro-symbolic engineering with credible ablation evidence, but not a new formulation and not backed by verifiable real-world results.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 58 Resilience 48 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Security Operations Centers process heterogeneous telemetry streams (XDR/EDR signals, IAM logs, NetFlow) through ML-based intrusion detection systems that achieve reasonable classification accuracy but cannot produce causal explanations, cannot automatically map detections to MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques, and generate false-positive volumes that overwhelm analyst triage capacity. Ontological and knowledge-graph approaches provide interpretability and formal reasoning but rely on static rules, require expensive manual ontology maintenance, and do not scale to streaming event volumes in high-load SOC/SIEM environments. XAI approaches (SHAP, LIME) generate post-hoc explanations but do not integrate with semantic knowledge structures or support real-time attack reconstruction. No existing system simultaneously provides automatic semantic event transformation, temporal pattern matching for multi-step attack detection, MITRE ATT&CK alignment, and inline explainability within a single near-real-time analytical cycle. Key Idea The central insight is a two-level semantic compression: raw 80-dimensional event feature vectors (PSI) are mapped to a small, dataset-agnostic set of semantic predicates (SSI) via frame-slot structures and threshold-based discretization rules, such that a single fixed rule base can operate identically across datasets with different feature spaces (80 features in CICIDS-2017, 41 in KDD/NSL-KDD). All downstream reasoning — temporal accumulation in the STM buffer, MITRE ATT&CK technique matching, and XAI explanation generation — then operates on the compact SSI representation rather than on raw feature vectors, eliminating the need to retrain or re-tune reasoning rules when the underlying telemetry schema changes. Methodology The ISS pipeline has five stages. (1) PSI collection: SIEM events arrive as raw feature records; categorical fields are one-hot encoded and numerical features are normalized using min-max scaling with SMOTE-based class balancing. (2) PSI→SSI transformation: a frame parser maps attribute groups to PascalCase SSI predicates through threshold-discretization rules (e.g., {dst_port=22, failed_login_cnt>0, flow_pkts/s=high} → {Service(SSH), AuthFailureObserved=true, BruteForceSuspect=true, AnomalyRate=high}) and a port/protocol dictionary; five attribute groups (ports/protocol, authentication/access, rate/flags, scanning indicators, burst/variability) cover the principal feature dimensions of all three benchmark datasets. (3) STM-based temporal accumulation: STMΔt(t) = {(fi, τi, wi)} stores the last N SSI facts with timestamps τi and exponential decay weights wi; this enables detection of frequency, repetition, and step-sequence patterns that span multiple events and are required to match ATT&CK techniques such as T1110 (Brute Force) or T1046 (Network Service Scanning). (4) Rule-based inference: production rules Ri = ⟨id, Qi, Pi, (A→B)i, Ni⟩ fire when SSI predicates plus STM temporal conditions are jointly satisfied, producing technique assignments {(Tj, Confj)} with confidence scores determined by predicate presence and temporal constraint satisfaction. (5) XAI generation: SHAP and LIME are applied to explain which SSI predicates most influenced each classification decision; explanations are formed at the semantic predicate level, not at raw feature level. The ontology is stored in Neo4j; the neural components (LSTM, adaptive semantic compression module) were trained on an NVIDIA RTX-3090 (24 GB VRAM) under Ubuntu 22.04 / TensorFlow 2.12. Three critical design decisions: (a) frame-slot compression as cross-dataset normalization layer (justified by ablation: ΔF1=+0.04 for frame structure); (b) STM sliding window for temporal coherence (justified conceptually by the multi-event nature of ATT&CK techniques, but Δt window size and threshold parameters θ₁–θ₅ are not sensitivity-tested); (c) SHAP/LIME at SSI level (justified by interpretability requirements, but the mapping from SSI explanations back to raw log lines — the actual analyst need — is not demonstrated). Key Results On CICIDS-2017 (2.67M records, 80 features, binary Normal vs. Attack, 70/15/15 split, SMOTE-balanced), ISS achieves Precision=0.94, Recall=0.91, F1=0.92, AUC=0.98, versus SVM (F1=0.84, AUC=0.92), Random Forest (F1=0.87, AUC=0.94), 1D-CNN (F1=0.86, AUC=0.95), BiLSTM (F1=0.85, AUC=0.94), and Transformer-IDS (F1=0.89, AUC=0.96). In multi-class configuration, per-class F1 scores are: Normal=0.96, DoS=0.93, PortScan=0.91, BruteForce=0.89, Infiltration=0.86. Cross-dataset generalization: KDD Cup 99 (494K, 41 features) gives ISS F1=0.90 vs. RF F1=0.86, LSTM F1=0.88; NSL-KDD (125K, 41 features) gives ISS F1=0.89 vs. RF F1=0.84, LSTM F1=0.87. Extended comparison with additional anomalies (Table 15): ISS achieves F1=0.90, XAI coverage=93.4%, false positive rate=4.1% vs. Random Forest 7.3% and LSTM 6.8%. Ablation (Table 12): removing frame structure ΔF1=−0.04; removing semantic generalization operators ΔF1=−0.07; removing reasoning module ΔF1=−0.10; removing XAI component ΔF1=0.00. Per-event latency: PSI→SSI=0.42 ms, reasoning=0.18 ms, XAI=0.07 ms, total≈0.67 ms/event, throughput≈1,490 events/s. RAM: ISS inference 3.2 GB (vs. ML-only 250 MB), +24–26% overhead across 10K–1M event scales. Scalability: latency increases from 0.61 ms (1,000 events, 120 rules) to 0.74 ms (100,000 events, 412 rules), quasi-linear. In two anonymized operational SOC/SIEM environments (Env-A: financial sector hybrid cloud, 8–15K events/min; Env-B: manufacturing/telecom on-prem, 12–25K events/min), controlled injection of T1110/T1046/T1071/T1499 scenarios produced FPR reduction of 18–27% (Env-A) and 21–34% (Env-B), and triage time reduction of 22–35% and 25–38%, at PSI→SSI p50 latency of 18–26 ms end-to-end. In SOTA comparison mode on a CICIDS-2017 subset, ISS achieves F1=0.968 vs. Transformer-IDS F1=0.981 and GNN-IDS F1=0.973, with full XAI and semantic reasoning that the neural models lack. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The contribution is the integration of semantic frame-based PSI→SSI compression, STM-windowed temporal reasoning, and post-hoc XAI (SHAP/LIME) into a unified inference pipeline aligned with MITRE ATT&CK — a combination not found in prior work as a whole. Individual components (ontological IDS, production rule systems, SHAP/LIME, sliding-window buffers for temporal detection) each exist in prior work; the genuine novelty lies in the PSI→SSI abstraction layer that makes the rule base dataset-agnostic, and in the STM mechanism that bridges single-event classification and multi-event ATT&CK technique identification within the same pipeline. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and cover four component configurations with five independent runs, ANOVA, and paired t-tests (p < 0.05); this is the methodological high point. ROC and precision-recall curves are provided for CICIDS-2017 attack classes (AUC 0.970–0.982). Error bars appear in Figure 14 showing ISS variability is substantially lower than ML baselines. Code and semantic artifacts are published at GitHub (YKostiuk-uk/Semantic-oriented-knowledge-representation-for-cyber-defense) and Zenodo (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.17945425). However, the STM threshold parameters θ₁–θ₅ and the exact number and content of production rules are not released, which is a reproducibility gap for the temporal matching subsystem. The production SOC validation cannot release raw logs (NDA), so the FPR and triage-time claims rest on injected scenarios in anonymized environments and are not independently reproducible. The SOTA comparison (Table 16) uses a different CICIDS-2017 subset than the main experiments, making the cross-table F1 comparison ambiguous. CICIDS-2017 has well-documented labeling artifacts (e.g., Saturday classes with near-perfect separability); the paper uses only Monday/Tuesday sessions, mitigating but not eliminating this concern. The paper is a preprint with no peer review; all quantitative claims should be treated accordingly. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile) - Practical: Inference runs on CPU with 3.2–3.8 GB RAM, requiring no GPU after training; the neural training phase needs an NVIDIA RTX-3090 (24 GB VRAM), which is commercially available. - Efficient: ISS adds 0.29 ms latency per event over ML-only (0.67 vs. 0.38 ms) and 24–26% additional RAM at 10K–1M event scale; XAI generation adds 0.07 ms/event and 8–12% CPU overhead, which the authors characterize as acceptable for SOC workloads. - Effective: F1=0.92 on CICIDS-2017 and claimed 18–34% FPR reduction in operational environments; the operational claims use controlled injection rather than organic attack traffic, so effectiveness on uncontrolled live traffic is unconfirmed. - Scalable: Latency scales quasi-linearly from 0.61 to 0.74 ms as events and rules grow from 1K/120 to 100K/412; the system has not been tested beyond 10⁶ events or in multi-tenant configurations, and the paper notes that knowledge graph density growth can degrade reasoning latency at scale. The delivered artifact is a Python implementation with Neo4j, REST API for SIEM integration, and a Minimal Reproducibility Package; production deployment would require custom ontology engineering per environment. Strengths - The PSI→SSI abstraction layer that normalizes heterogeneous feature spaces (80 vs. 41 features across three datasets) to a single set of semantic predicates is a concrete engineering contribution that demonstrably reduces dataset-specific rule tuning. - Ablation evidence (Table 12) isolates the reasoning module as the single largest performance contributor (ΔF1=+0.10), giving credible support to the claim that semantic reasoning — not just the feature pipeline — adds value beyond ML-only baselines. - The STM short-term memory mechanism for multi-event temporal accumulation directly addresses the structural limitation of single-event IDS classifiers for ATT&CK techniques that inherently span multiple events (brute force, port scanning, command-and-control beaconing). - Cross-dataset portability is validated empirically: the same rule base transfers to KDD Cup 99 and NSL-KDD without retraining, achieving F1 within 0.02 of CICIDS-2017 performance. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly and creditably address: (1) generalization is tested only on CICIDS-2017, KDD Cup 99, and NSL-KDD, which do not cover multi-stage attacks, dynamic cloud environments, or modern Zero Trust access scenarios; (2) reasoning module performance depends on ontology completeness and currency, with maintenance being resource-intensive; (3) scalability degrades as the number of rules and active predicates grows, with reconciliation latency increasing in high-load environments; (4) the baseline comparison set is incomplete — additional Transformer and GNN variants and hybrid neuro-symbolic models were not included; (5) production SOC integration was tested in an isolated configuration with injected rather than organic attack traffic, and non-functional requirements (fault tolerance, access policy coordination) were not evaluated. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The five STM matching thresholds (θ₁–θ₅) and the Δt window size are critical parameters that directly determine the FPR/recall tradeoff; no sensitivity analysis is provided, and they were presumably tuned on the same CICIDS-2017 dataset used for evaluation, raising Goodhart's Law concern. - The SOTA comparison (Table 16) uses a different, unspecified CICIDS-2017 subset from the main experiments; the resulting F1=0.968 for ISS vs. 0.981 for Transformer-IDS cannot be cleanly compared to the main experiment's F1=0.92, obscuring the actual accuracy gap to strongest baselines. - SHAP/LIME explanations operate at the SSI predicate level; the paper does not demonstrate that an analyst can trace a predicate-level explanation back to specific raw log lines, which is the actual operational requirement for SOC triage and audit. - KDD Cup 99 and NSL-KDD are widely acknowledged as non-representative of modern network environments; using them as generalization benchmarks is methodologically weak, especially given that newer modern datasets (CSE-CIC-IDS2018, TON_IoT, Bot-IoT) that the paper mentions are available but not used. - The "operational validation" uses telemetry generated by "controlled injections into background streams" of depersonalized events — this is not the same as detecting organic attacks in uncontrolled production traffic, and the claimed FPR/triage improvements cannot be attributed to the method alone versus the controlled experimental setup. Bottom Line A practitioner building a semantic enrichment layer for SIEM/XDR will find the PSI→SSI abstraction and STM temporal matching genuinely useful, and the ablation evidence is credible, but the unverifiable operational claims, unreleased threshold parameters, and preprint status mean this work requires independent reproduction before deployment decisions.
Search Results
#108 — Moderately Innovative (54)

Performance Analysis of Infrastructure for Distributed Large Language Model Training and Inference

B Chen · escholarship.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This thesis introduces an analytical, hardware-aware performance modeling framework (the compute frontend of RAPID-LLM) that takes LLM workload, hardware, and hybrid parallelism configurations as input and estimates end-to-end training and inference runtime without running the workload on hardware. - Why it's important: Evaluating parallelism strategies and hardware configurations for large-scale LLM clusters requires running multi-thousand-GPU jobs; analytical models make this exploration feasible before committing to hardware or software changes, which can cost millions of dollars per training run. - How it does it: The framework decomposes each Transformer block into GEMM and pointwise operators, constructs a hierarchical operator-level computation graph that encodes 6 parallelism axes (DP, TP, PP, SP, CP, EP) and their communication collectives, then estimates critical-path runtime using a GEMM timing module and a roofline model, combined with a memory feasibility filter. - The good: On large-scale GPT training configurations spanning 64 to 3072 A100 GPUs, the framework achieves a mean absolute percentage error of 10.8%, outperforming STAGE (which substantially underestimates) and covering more configurations. - The missing: No code or model weights are publicly released, and validation is limited to A100 hardware using published reference runtimes rather than independently collected measurements; accuracy on H100 or non-GPU accelerators is unknown. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems researchers and hardware architects who need a fast design space exploration tool for hybrid-parallel LLM workloads; can be skipped by those who need deployment-accurate profiling rather than trend analysis.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Operator-level GEMM-shape tracking for modern variants (GQA, MLA with matrix absorption, MoE with EP) under 6-axis hybrid parallelism with an integrated memory-feasibility filter is a useful tool contribution (Novelty 55, Non-Obviousness 58). Rigor (48) is the limiter: utilization correction factors are fit separately for training and inference against the same validation configurations (calibration-inflated MAPE with no held-out set), no component ablation, A100-only validation against a single published reference source, and no public code. Master's thesis (committee, not peer review). Weighted composite ~54.
systems Novelty 55 Rigor 48 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Training and serving large language models at scale requires multi-GPU clusters where the choice of parallelism strategy (combinations of data, tensor, pipeline, sequence, context, and expert parallelism) jointly determines whether a workload even fits in device memory and how fast it runs. Existing tools occupy poor positions in the fidelity-speed tradeoff: trace-driven simulators (SimAI, ReaLLM) require executing the workload on target hardware first, ruling out future hardware exploration; cycle-level simulators (GPGPU-Sim, Accel-Sim) are too slow for cluster-scale sweeps; and coarse analytical tools (STAGE, MLSynth, LLMCompass, GenZ, Vidur) operate at tensor or layer granularity and miss performance variation from operator shapes, GPU tiling behavior, and memory hierarchy effects that differ between MHA, GQA, MLA, FlashAttention-style kernels, and MoE routing. As modern LLM architectures introduce heterogeneous operator shapes, conditional compute, and all-to-all communication, a faster yet operator-aware modeling approach is needed for hardware/software co-design. Key Idea The core insight is that all dominant computations in a Transformer block reduce to a small vocabulary of GEMM instances (with explicit 4D tensor shapes encoding batch, sequence, hidden, and output dimensions) and bandwidth-sensitive pointwise kernels, and that each parallelism axis predictably transforms those shapes and inserts specific communication collectives. By decomposing each architecture variant into this vocabulary and building a hierarchical three-level computation graph (intra-layer TP×CP graph → pipeline graph → DP-level gradient synchronization), the framework achieves operator-level fidelity without constructing an impractically large fully-expanded graph across all layers, microbatches, and GPU replicas. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. First, operator-level modeling maps each Transformer block component into GEMM instances and pointwise operators. GEMM execution time is queried from an internal GEMM timing module that accounts for GPU memory hierarchy; pointwise operators use a roofline formulation: t_op = max(F_op / (η_comp · P_peak), Q_op / (η_mem · B_mem)), where η_comp and η_mem are hardware utilization correction factors fit separately for training and inference. Architecture variants (GQA, MLA, MoE, FlashAttention) are handled by changing projection dimensions and GEMM shapes rather than special-casing; MLA inference uses a matrix absorption formulation that avoids constructing full Q/K/V tensors. MoE layers assume balanced routing (T_e ≈ kT/E tokens per expert) and insert all-to-all communication for EP. Second, a computation graph G = (V, E) is constructed where nodes are operators or communication primitives and edges capture data and synchronization dependencies; the hierarchical construction avoids |V_full| ∝ |V_layer| · L · m · N_dp scaling by building at three levels. Third, critical-path simulation computes T(v) = t(v) + max_{u:(u,v)∈E} T(u) to estimate end-to-end runtime. Fourth, a memory estimator computes M_peak = M_static + M_act for each configuration and prunes those exceeding device capacity M_device, with M_static covering parameters, gradients, optimizer states (with ZeRO sharding factors), and KV cache, and M_act tracking live activation tensors during graph traversal under the selected recomputation policy (none, selective, full). Critical design decisions: (i) hierarchical graph construction avoids exponential graph expansion, well-justified; (ii) lightweight per-modality utilization correction factors absorb hardware-specific deviations, justified for speed but weakens generality; (iii) balanced routing assumption for MoE, not justified empirically. Key Results Validation uses two ground-truth sources: published runtimes for Llama2 (7B, 13B, 70B) and Llama3-70B inference on A100 systems, and first-party DDP training measurements on a local 4×A100 80GB PCIe system. For inference, the framework predicts latency within approximately 20% of measured values across varying TP degrees and input/output token lengths (200/200 to 20000/2000 tokens), consistently better than GenZ and Vidur on most configurations. For small-scale training (Llama2-7B, seq len 4096, 4×A100 PCIe, 6 parallelism configurations), error is below 13%, while STAGE and MLSynth both exceed 30% error on most cases. For large-scale training (GPT 175B to 1T parameter models, 64 to 3072 A100 GPUs, 6 configurations with full and selective activation recomputation), the framework achieves 10.8% mean absolute percentage error against published reference runtimes; STAGE substantially underestimates and skips configurations it cannot support. Case studies demonstrate: (i) a Llama 3.1-405B training parallelism sweep across 128 to 4096 A100 80GB GPUs (batch 256, 33k sequence length) where memory feasibility filters most configurations at low GPU counts, and pipeline parallelism dominates at small scale while TP/CP become preferable above 2048 GPUs; (ii) a Groq LPU-inspired vs A100 inference comparison for Llama 3.3-70B showing the Groq-like configuration (up to 80 TB/s SRAM bandwidth, 230 MB per chip) achieves lower decode latency but higher prefill latency relative to A100 (312 TFLOPS FP16, ~2 TB/s HBM bandwidth, 80 GB capacity); (iii) a memory-aware decode throughput sweep showing the Groq-like configuration becomes feasible only above 128 devices due to SRAM capacity constraints, while A100 is feasible from a single device. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The framework is an extension of prior analytical frontend work (STAGE, MLSynth, RAPID-LLM) rather than a conceptually new method; the genuine novelty lies in combining operator-level GEMM shape tracking for modern LLM variants (GQA, MLA with matrix absorption, MoE with EP) with 6-axis hybrid parallelism modeling and integrated memory feasibility filtering in a single fast analytical tool. The hardware-software co-design case studies constitute new empirical findings about parallelism strategy selection under memory constraints. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations isolate the contributions of individual components (GEMM model, communication model, memory model, utilization correction factors). No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported. The utilization correction factors are fit to training vs inference separately, which constitutes calibration against the validation set and may inflate reported accuracy. Large-scale training validation relies entirely on published reference runtimes from a single published source rather than independent measurement. Code is not publicly released (not mentioned anywhere in the thesis). The work is a UCLA Master's thesis reviewed by a three-person committee (Gupta, Kao, Sehatbakhsh), not through conference or journal peer review. STAGE is the primary training competitor but is clearly weaker (omits selective recomputation, substantially underestimates). No comparison to LLMCompass or Vidur on training configurations. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS (an analytical modeling tool, evaluated against measured runtimes). - Practical: Runs analytically without any hardware; can model hypothetical future accelerators by changing hardware parameters, making it suitable for pre-deployment exploration. - Efficient: Much faster than cycle-level simulation for cluster-scale sweeps; the thesis does not report the tool's own execution time, so precise modeling overhead is unknown. - Effective: Achieves 10.8% MAPE on large-scale training and <13% on small-scale training, which the thesis correctly characterizes as suitable for trend analysis and design space exploration but not precise deployment prediction; the 20% inference error bound is wider. - Scalable: The hierarchical graph construction is designed to avoid O(L·m·N_dp) graph expansion; demonstrated on workloads up to 4096 GPUs and 1T-parameter models, though wall-clock sweep time is not reported. The work delivers a software framework (no public release); no trained model weights or datasets are involved. Strengths - Explicit operator-level GEMM shape tracking for GQA, MLA (including matrix absorption formulation for inference), and MoE within a unified operator-graph framework, which prior coarse tools miss. - Hierarchical three-level graph construction that avoids combinatorial graph explosion while preserving TP×CP intra-layer, PP inter-stage, and DP gradient-synchronization effects in the same model. - Memory feasibility filter integrated into the sweep loop, which correctly prunes configurations that appear fast but are infeasible, enabling realistic parallelism strategy recommendations rather than optimistic ones. - Empirical case studies yield genuinely useful design insights: the Groq LPU vs A100 decode vs prefill trade-off and the memory-capacity bottleneck at small device counts are concrete and actionable. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (i) the analytical model may not capture all low-level execution details for non-GPU architectures such as Groq LPU, which may require RTL simulation or detailed compiler information; (ii) the inference model does not support advanced KV cache management techniques (paging, compression, offloading) used in production serving systems; (iii) the framework estimates only runtime, throughput, and memory feasibility, omitting energy consumption, hardware area, and system cost. These limitations are stated directly in the conclusion chapter, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Utilization correction factors are fit separately for training and inference, effectively calibrating against the same validation configurations used to report accuracy; no held-out test set is used, so reported MAPE may overstate generalization accuracy. - Validation is entirely on A100 hardware; accuracy on H100, MI300X, or other current-generation accelerators is unknown and not discussed. - The balanced routing assumption for MoE (T_e ≈ kT/E) is stated without empirical justification; load imbalance in production MoE models can substantially change per-expert GEMM shapes and all-to-all communication volume. - The Groq LPU comparison in Section 6.2 explicitly ignores memory capacity, then Section 6.3 adds capacity back in; the separation is pedagogically useful but the Section 6.2 result (Groq-like has lower decode latency) is misleading for practical use without the capacity caveat. - No comparison against LLMCompass or Vidur on training benchmarks; STAGE is the only training baseline, and it is demonstrably weaker. - No public code release limits reproducibility and adoption. Bottom Line For ML systems researchers and hardware architects who need rapid, memory-aware design space exploration across parallelism strategies and hardware configurations for large LLM workloads, this thesis delivers a useful analytical tool with validated 10-13% training accuracy, though the absence of a public release, restricted hardware validation coverage, and calibration-inflated accuracy metrics mean it should be treated as a well-executed graduate-level systems contribution rather than a production-grade simulator.
Search Results
#109 — Moderately Innovative (54)

Safe integration of Large Language Models into industrial process control: a multi- agent architecture with P&ID-grounded validation

D Schall · link.springer.com · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Autonomous Action Execution (AAE) framework, a four-layer multi-agent architecture that safely integrates LLMs into industrial process control by confining LLM inference to an observation-only Monitor layer and validating every proposal through a deterministic, P&ID-grounded Verification Agent before any SCADA write occurs. - Why it's important: Direct LLM deployment in IEC 61511-governed process industries risks catastrophic equipment damage or loss of life from hallucinated or physically impossible control actions, and no prior architecture provides deterministic structural checks grounded in plant engineering documentation. - How it does it: The core mechanism is architectural separation of concerns: the LLM proposes actions, and an architecturally isolated Verification Agent runs six P&ID graph-traversal checks (tag existence, actuatability, SCADA controllability, loop relevance, fail-state consistency, downstream impact via BFS) that operate on the plant's own engineering documentation independently of the LLM. - The good: Across 43 injected known-bad proposals spanning five synthetic scenarios, the P&ID-grounded validator achieves TPR = 1.000 (zero false negatives) on covered structural failure modes, confirming the core architectural safety claim within the tested scope. - The missing: All five evaluation scenarios use synthetically generated data with only 14-23 tags per scenario (vs. thousands in real plants), only one LLM (Claude Sonnet 4) is tested, ground truth rubrics were authored by the same person who built the system, and the FPR estimate rests on only 8 negative samples. - Read it or not: Worth reading for process-control engineers and AI safety architects designing advisory LLM systems in safety-critical settings; it provides the clearest published argument that safety is an architectural problem rather than a model accuracy problem, and maps directly to IEC 61511 and LOPA concepts.
Why this rank: Systems profile (15/15/10/15/15/30). Practical Impact (45) is capped by a research prototype available only on request with fully manual P&ID-to-JSON translation. Completeness (70) is the standout: nine honestly enumerated limitations and a quantified FPR class-imbalance problem. Rigor (48) is held back despite real ablations (B0-B3) and an N=50 variance study with error bars, because all data is synthetic at 14-23 tags, ground-truth rubrics were self-authored, the TEP benchmark is likely training-contaminated, and the LLM-independence claim is untested across models. Resilience (50) suffers from the FPR=0.750 estimate resting on 8 negative samples (CI [0.41,0.93]). Novelty (60) and Non-Obviousness (62) credit the genuinely useful 'safety is architectural not accuracy' framing grounded in IEC 61511. Weighted: 0.15*60+0.15*48+0.10*62+0.15*70+0.15*50+0.30*45 = 54.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 48 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 70 Resilience 50 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Safety-critical process industries (chemical plants, refineries, batch reactors) governed by IEC 61511 require high-reliability control decisions where incorrect actions can cause equipment damage, environmental harm, or loss of life. LLMs offer natural-language reasoning over Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), alarm analysis, and trend interpretation that human operators traditionally handle, but they are non-deterministic, prone to hallucination, and lack intrinsic understanding of physical constraints such as control loop topology, valve fail states, and interlock logic. Prior work on LLMs for industrial applications (FaultExplainer, anomaly detection, root cause analysis via knowledge graphs) evaluates model accuracy but does not address architectural integration into safety-critical control systems. The key gap is the absence of architecturally deterministic checks against structural LLM failure modes; existing LLM safety approaches operate at the text output level (NeMo Guardrails) or rely on human-in-the-loop oversight without grounding in the plant's own engineering documentation (P&IDs), leaving no mechanism to catch physically impossible proposals before they reach SCADA. Key Idea The key insight is that safety in LLM-assisted process control is fundamentally an architectural problem, not a model accuracy problem: even a 99.9%-accurate LLM will eventually hallucinate a physically impossible action, so the correct response is not to improve the LLM but to interpose an architecturally independent deterministic Verification Agent that operates on the plant's P&ID graph and enforces structural consistency regardless of what the LLM proposes or how it was prompted. The P&ID, already the authoritative engineering document in process industries, becomes the machine-checkable ground truth. Methodology The AAE framework implements four layers communicating via MQTT v5.0. Layer A (Monitor Agents) are stateless, horizontally scalable microservices that run every Δt seconds per process area: each captures an HMI screenshot (parsed by Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct, a 3-billion-parameter vision-language model), verbalizes historian tags, loads a P&ID subgraph, runs the text-level aggregation pipeline, and invokes Claude Sonnet 4 to generate structured action proposals (action type, target SCADA tag, proposed value, confidence 0-1, rationale, priority P0-P3). Layer B (Orchestrator) deduplicates proposals by SCADA tag and resolves conflicts via weighted vote w_i = c_i·a_i / Σ_j c_j·a_j where c_i is confidence and a_i is anomaly score. Layer C (Verification Agent) is architecturally isolated from the LLM with no shared model weights, memory, or communication channel; it runs six P&ID graph-traversal structural checks on each proposal (tag existence, actuatability, SCADA controllability, loop relevance, fail-state consistency, downstream impact via BFS on pipe edges) and issues a gate verdict: PASS, WARN, APPROVAL, or BLOCK per equation Gate(a_i). Layer D (Execution Agent) is the only agent with write access to SCADA via OPC-UA; P0 conditions are SIS-owned and never executed by the AAE; P1/P2/P3 actions route based on priority plus verification verdict. The text-level aggregation framework converts heterogeneous plant data into structured LLM context via five pluggable analyzers: AlarmAnalyzer (active alarms from SCADA), TrendAnalyzer (directional trend detection including stuck-detector for N consecutive identical samples), TopologyAnalyzer (control loop definitions, fail states from P&ID), SOPAnalyzer (troubleshooting steps from SOP documents), and PIDEnricher (broken loop detection, downstream impact chains via BFS). The ProposalValidator runs six structural checks using the P&ID represented as a traversable graph following ISA-5.1 conventions plus a scenario-specific forbidden-action rubric. Three critical design decisions: (1) architectural isolation of the Verification Agent from the LLM, well-justified as the foundational safety property; (2) per-run opaque label substitution (P001..PNNN) replacing real SCADA tags in LLM prompts to prevent ID-shortening hallucinations, justified but limiting cross-label reasoning; (3) static P&ID graph validation with no temporal state-machine, acknowledged as a limitation but pragmatically tractable. Key Results Context quality (RQ4): The AAE pipeline surfaces on average 5.8 alarms, 7.4 trends, 1.4 broken control loops, and 6.0 SOP steps per scenario versus zero derived insights for the raw JSON baseline, confirming that the analyzers surface information categorically absent from raw data (Table 8). Root cause identification: 100% hit rate in both baseline and AAE conditions across all five scenarios, indicating LLM diagnostic reasoning is sound; failures are in action selection, not diagnosis. Safety compliance is scenario-dependent: AAE prevents safety violations on TEP_Stripper where baseline fails (safety=0 vs. 1), but both fail on Dryer (safety=0). AAE introduces hallucinations the baseline does not produce on TEP_Stripper (25%) and Dryer (20% mean), attributed to context budget overflow from longer structured prompts; the validator catches all of these. Error injection study (43 proposals, 35 unsafe, 8 safe): TPR = 1.000 (zero false negatives on covered failure modes), FPR = 0.750 (6/8 safe proposals flagged as conservative WARN), precision = 0.854, F1 = 0.921; per-category: hallucinated tags (100% FAIL), sensor writes (100% FAIL), physical contradictions (100% FAIL), forbidden actions (100% WARN/FAIL). The FPR is computed over only 8 negative samples with a Wilson 95% CI of approximately [0.41, 0.93], making it unreliable for deployment planning. Statistical robustness (N = 50 LLM runs, temperature 0.7): root cause hit rate is deterministic (100%, std=0 in all scenarios); safety violations occur in 10% of runs (TEP_Reactor, TEP_Separator, TEP_Stripper, PolyReactor) to 70% of runs (Dryer); the validation layer flags every invalid proposal across all 50 runs (100% catch rate on covered categories). Graduated baselines (B0-B3) show that removing P&ID enrichment (B2) drops SOP coverage from 90% to 35% while the full pipeline (B3) provides the best coverage-safety balance, though the Dryer scenario demonstrates a safety regression (violation rate rises from 0 to 0.2) attributable to the larger B3 context misleading the LLM. All tests use 14-23 tags per scenario; real plants have thousands of tags. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The genuine novel contribution is the combination of (1) a four-layer architectural pattern that confines LLM inference to an advisory-only role with an architecturally independent deterministic verification layer grounded in plant P&ID graph traversal, and (2) the text-level aggregation framework that converts SCADA/P&ID/SOP data into structured LLM context via pluggable analyzers with derived safety-critical insights (broken loop detection, fail-state warnings, downstream impact chains). Applying LLMs to process control fault diagnosis is not new; the novel move is treating safety as an architectural constraint enforced by a separate deterministic layer operating on engineering ground truth rather than as a property to optimize in the LLM. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present: the graduated baseline comparison (B0-B3) across all five scenarios provides component-level attribution, and the statistical variance study (N=50 LLM runs) characterizes stochastic behavior with mean ± std per scenario. Error bars are reported for all statistical robustness results. Prototype source code (text-level aggregation pipeline, P&ID validator, evaluation harness in Python) and scenario data are available from the corresponding author on request, with plans for permissive open-source release; no code repository URL is provided yet. Significant methodological concerns: (1) ground truth rubrics were constructed by the system designer, who is also the sole author, creating evaluation bias; (2) all five scenarios use synthetically generated data, not production SCADA captures; (3) the TEP benchmark (3 of 5 scenarios) has 3000+ citations and is likely in Claude Sonnet 4's training corpus, creating best-case contamination bias the paper acknowledges; (4) the FPR is estimated on only TN + FP = 8 negative samples, rendering the 0.750 point estimate uninformative; (5) only one LLM tested, leaving the core claim of LLM-independence unverified by any cross-model experiment; (6) the P&ID-to-JSON translation was performed manually by the author, eliminating a confound but also a major deployment step. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS (a built tool evaluated by synthetic deployment on a workstation). - Practical: The text-level aggregation pipeline runs on a standard Intel Core i7-1370P workstation (32 GB DDR5) without GPU; the vision encoder (Qwen2.5-VL-3B) is edge-deployable; LLM inference requires Claude Sonnet 4 via AWS Bedrock; full deployment requires MQTT broker, OPC-UA gateway, and a maintained P&ID JSON model. - Efficient: Text-level aggregation completes in 24.2 ms mean (σ = 3.1 ms); LLM inference adds 2-8 seconds per proposal (dominant cost); P0 (imminent safety) actions bypass the AAE path entirely and remain SIS-owned, so latency is acceptable for informational and optimization actions but not for emergency response. - Effective: Within the tested synthetic scenarios and the six covered structural failure modes, the validator achieves zero false negatives; however, temporally unsafe sequences, multi-step coordination errors, rate-of-change violations, and operationally suboptimal but structurally valid proposals are explicitly out of scope and not caught. - Scalable: Only tested at 14-23 tags per scenario; real plants have thousands of tags; BFS traversal complexity and LLM prompt size at production scale are uncharacterized; the Monitor layer scales horizontally, the Verification Agent is a singleton with warm standby, but the prompt-engineering implications at thousands of tags are explicitly deferred. The work delivers a Python research prototype available on request, not a production-ready library. The P&ID-to-JSON translation pipeline is fully manual in this paper, representing a major operationalization gap. Strengths - The core architectural insight, that safety requires structural independence of the verification layer from the LLM rather than model improvement, is grounded in IEC 61511 and LOPA principles and is the correct framing for this class of system; the paper makes this argument clearly and supports it with the zero-false-negative result on covered failure modes. - The graduated baseline comparison (B0-B3) provides systematic component attribution that is rare in systems papers, including the counter-intuitive finding that partial enrichment (B2, alarm+trend only) improves safety compliance over full enrichment (B3) on the worst-case Dryer scenario by giving the LLM less material to misuse. - The statistical robustness study (N=50 LLM runs with temperature 0.7) quantifies stochastic variability and confirms that the validation layer's catch rate is deterministic even when the LLM's safety compliance varies from 10% to 70% of runs by scenario. - The discussion section honestly enumerates nine explicit limitations, quantifies the FPR class-imbalance problem, flags TEP training contamination, and treats human-factors risks (alert fatigue, trust miscalibration, cognitive friction) as open empirical questions rather than solved problems. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly enumerate nine limitations in Section 6.5: all data is synthetic (no production SCADA captures); the P&ID validator is static (no temporal state-machine, no time-sequenced interlocks, no mode-dependent permissives); only Claude Sonnet 4 is tested; no multi-fault scenarios; no operator study; scalability at thousands of tags is uncharacterized; temporal validation gap (sequential action safety not checked); conservative FPR estimated on only 8 negative samples; potential training contamination from the widely published TEP benchmark. Section 6.3 additionally identifies alert fatigue, trust miscalibration in both directions, and continuous verification cognitive friction as uncharacterized empirical risks. The authors confront limitations directly and frame them as a future-work agenda rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The FPR of 0.750 with Wilson 95% CI [0.41, 0.93] resting on 8 negative samples is not a deployable safety metric; a practitioner cannot distinguish "validator is appropriately conservative" from "validator is broken" on this evidence. This is the most serious hole in the quantitative case. - All P&ID models were manually transcribed by the system's sole author, who also authored the ground truth rubrics; this conflation of builder and evaluator is a validity threat that independent domain expert annotation would need to resolve before the evaluation can be trusted. - The AAE demonstrably increases hallucination rate on two scenarios (Dryer: 23%, TEP_Stripper: 10% vs. 0% baseline) with no proposed mitigation; the paper explains the mechanism (context budget overflow) but does not show how to avoid it, leaving practitioners with a design choice between safety-relevant context and hallucination risk. - The claim that "architectural safety properties are independent of LLM choice" is stated as a design property but supported by zero cross-model experiments; whether the hallucination regime and validator interaction holds for GPT-4, Llama, or smaller models is entirely untested. - P&ID-to-JSON translation is manually performed for all five scenarios, with automated OCR/CAD-export pipelines deferred to future work; the operational deployment path is therefore incomplete as described. - At 14-23 tags, the evaluation covers a fraction of the state space of real plants; neither the BFS traversal time nor the prompt token count at 10,000-tag topologies is characterized, leaving scalability as an open architectural question rather than a measured property. Bottom Line A practitioner designing an LLM advisory layer for industrial process control gains a concrete, IEC-61511-aware architecture and the strongest published argument for why that safety must be architectural rather than prompt-based, but should treat the validation results as preliminary pilot evidence (synthetic scenarios, 14-23 tags, single LLM, self-annotated ground truth, FPR on 8 samples) and plan for substantial engineering work on P&ID digitization and real-plant validation before any production deployment claim can be made.
Search Results
#110 — Moderately Innovative (54)

A Framework for Evaluating Agentic Skills at Scale

M Shaposhnikov, N Fortuin, S Stipcich, MI Gorinova… · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a scalable evaluation framework that synthesizes executable tasks directly from agent skill content and measures the marginal behavioral impact of those skills across 19 model/harness configurations via with-skill vs. without-skill controlled trials. - Why it's important: No reusable methodology exists for evaluating whether an individual, newly authored skill actually changes agent behavior — existing benchmarks use fixed, hand-authored task suites that cannot assess an arbitrary skill in isolation. - How it does it: A pipeline of specialized LLM agents (environment engineering, task generation, validation) generates realistic tasks with hidden rubrics from skill content, then an LLM-as-judge (Sonnet 4.6) scores agent solutions against instruction-following and goal-completion rubrics in both conditions. - The good: GLM 5.1, an open-weights model, reaches an overall score of 91.1 with a skill at $0.89/scenario — within 1.6 points of Opus 4.8 (92.7) at $3.26/scenario — providing a 3-4x cost reduction with negligible quality loss for skill-augmented workloads. - The missing: The with-skill condition explicitly informs the agent which skill to use, so the scores measure skill utility once invoked rather than end-to-end deployment utility, which also depends on skill-selection ability — a confound the paper acknowledges but does not quantify. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners choosing model tiers for skill-augmented agents or authoring skills and needing a diagnostic tool; those seeking a general agent benchmark should look elsewhere, as goal completion saturates and the evaluation effectively reduces to instruction-following.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The per-skill diagnostic capability (rubric-level with/without deltas) and the concrete cost-quality numbers across 19 models (e.g., GLM 5.1 at 91.1 for $0.89 vs. Opus 4.8 at 92.7 for $3.26) are the real value (Novelty 62). Rigor (45) is the dominant pull-down: no pipeline ablations, no error bars or significance tests across 38,000 trajectories, a single unvalidated judge (Sonnet 4.6 with only manual spot-checks), tasks/rubrics/judgments all LLM-generated from the same distribution creating an undetectable circular loop, and harness differences confounded with model family. Resilience (50) and Completeness (58) are further weakened because goal completion saturates above 90%, collapsing the central metric to instruction-following, and an undisclosed registry conflict of interest (Tessl operates a primary skill source). Weighted composite: 0.20*62 + 0.25*45 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*55 = 54.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 58 Resilience 50 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Agent skills — reusable, portable knowledge artifacts packaged as structured Markdown files (SKILL.md) — are proliferating across agent platforms and registries, yet no reusable methodology exists to determine whether a specific skill improves agent behavior on the tasks it targets. A skill author faces a concrete, unanswered question: does my newly authored skill change what the agent does, in which direction, and for which models? Prior benchmarks (SkillsBench, related works) use small, fixed, hand-authored task sets that cannot assess an arbitrary new skill in isolation and lack domain breadth, making it impossible to draw conclusions across the heterogeneous real-world skill corpus distributed across community registries. Key Idea: The core insight is that skill content itself is a sufficient specification for generating realistic evaluation tasks: because a skill encodes the exact workflows, conventions, and API patterns the agent should follow, an LLM agent can infer plausible user requests that would exercise the skill — without requiring external user logs or hand-authored tasks. Pairing this automated synthesis with a hidden-rubric, with/without-skill controlled trial design isolates the skill's marginal behavioral contribution from the model's baseline capability. Methodology: The pipeline runs in four stages. First, an environment engineering agent classifies each skill's dependencies into 16 categories (CLI access, auth/credentials, runtime, MCP server, database, git state, browser, etc.) and determines which can be instantiated autonomously; skills requiring databases, MCP servers, multi-turn interaction, git state, local services, or pre-populated external state are discarded in fully autonomous mode. Second, a task generation agent synthesizes up to three diverse task proposals per skill (or skill combination), resolving missing inputs by retrieving public resources or synthesizing from scratch, and produces an executable task specification with a description, inputs folder, verifiable environment, and two rubric sets (instruction-following and goal-completion), each scored 0–100 via natural-language assertions on a 1–10 scale. Third, a validation agent checks executability, rubric leakage, and task-description consistency, discarding failures. Finally, a solver agent attempts each task under two conditions — with skill (agent explicitly informed of skill relevance and access) and without skill — and a judge agent (Sonnet 4.6) grades both solutions against the hidden rubrics. Three critical design decisions: (1) explicit skill disclosure in the with-skill condition, which isolates skill utility once invoked but conflates it with skill-selection ability; (2) Snyk security filtering plus permissive-license filtering (MIT, Apache 2.0) to reduce harmful skills; (3) a single fixed judge model with no inter-rater reliability quantification. Applied to 500 skills from ~100 organizations across 14 domains, yielding ~1,000 tasks and ~38,000 valid trajectories across 19 model configurations spanning Anthropic (Haiku 4.5, Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.7/4.8), OpenAI (GPT-5.4 nano/mini/full), Google (Gemini 3 Flash through 3.5 Flash), DeepSeek V4 Flash/Pro, GLM 5.1, Kimi K2.6, MiniMax 2.7, Qwen3-Coder-Next, and Nemotron 3 Nano/Super — run through Claude Code, Codex CLI, or OpenHands harnesses. Key Results: Across all 19 models, skill access yields an overall score improvement of 5.5 to 22 points (Table 4), driven primarily by instruction-following. Goal completion saturates with skill access for most frontier models (>90%), making instruction-following the effective discriminating metric. With-skill instruction-following scores span 25.2 (Nemotron Nano 30B) to 88.0 (Opus 4.8), a 62.8-point spread; GLM 5.1 reaches 85.0, within 3 points of the top proprietary models. The cost-quality finding is concrete: GLM 5.1 achieves 91.1 overall at $0.89/scenario vs. Opus 4.8 at 92.7 for $3.26/scenario and Opus 4.7 at 92.3 for $3.94/scenario. Kimi K2.6 underperforms expectations — only a 7.1-point skill delta and 60.1 instruction-following with skill — suggesting the model does not utilize skill content effectively. Skills benefit smaller models more than larger ones within families (Haiku vs. Opus, GPT-5.4 nano vs. GPT-5.4). By domain, the largest instruction-following uplifts accrue to Media & File Processing (+38.1 points) and Productivity & Communication (+32.5), which encode specific procedural workflows; the smallest uplifts appear in Testing/QA (+16.7) and Scientific Computing (+17.0), which encode general principles. Baselines are the respective without-skill conditions for the same model; no comparison to human-authored or hand-tuned task suites is reported. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The automatic task synthesis pipeline that generates executable evaluation tasks from skill content is novel; the per-skill diagnostic capability (identifying which rubric criteria a skill most influences) is absent from prior skill benchmarks. The LLM-as-judge scoring, with/without-skill contrast design, and environment categorization taxonomy are standard or straightforward extensions of known techniques. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations on pipeline design choices (single-agent vs. multi-agent task generation, automated vs. human-in-the-loop quality impact, rubric granularity sensitivity). No error bars, significance tests, or multiple-run statistics are reported across the 38,000 trajectories. The judge model is a single fixed instance (Sonnet 4.6); inter-rater reliability between judge and human ground truth is assessed only by "manual spot checks" with no quantification. The evaluation dataset is released at huggingface.co/datasets/tesslio/task-evals-for-skills; the pipeline code is not open-sourced. A concern: tasks, rubrics, and judgments are all LLM-generated from the same distribution, so systematic biases in the generator/judge models could inflate or deflate scores consistently without detection. A further concern is that using different agent harnesses (Claude Code, Codex, OpenHands) for different model families confounds model ability with harness quality in cross-model comparisons. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.17819v1); results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems + measurement study). - Practical: The evaluation pipeline requires only standard cloud API access; no specialized hardware or infrastructure is needed. - Efficient: Per-scenario costs span $0.03 (Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite) to $3.94 (Opus 4.7) depending on model; the pipeline itself runs autonomously at scale without stated compute constraints, though the framework code is not publicly released. - Effective: The framework detects meaningful behavioral differences between models and skill conditions, and the per-skill rubric analysis correctly identifies which skill components change agent behavior; whether rubric scores track real-world downstream quality (maintenance, correctness, safety) beyond instruction-following proxies is not validated at scale. - Scalable: Tested at 500 skills / 1,000 tasks / 19 models; the pipeline is designed to extend to larger corpora, but performance at 10x scale (5,000 skills, production-level skill registries) is not demonstrated or claimed with evidence. The work delivers a released evaluation dataset, not a reusable software artifact; the pipeline itself is not open-sourced. Strengths: - The per-skill diagnostic capability (Section 5.5) is genuinely actionable: by generating multiple tasks and inspecting rubric-level with/without deltas, a skill author can identify which components of their skill are doing real behavioral work vs. which are redundant — a capability absent from all prior skill benchmarks. - The cost-quality decomposition (Table 4) translates directly to deployment decisions with concrete per-scenario dollar figures across 19 models, making the trade-off between model tier and skill augmentation quantitative rather than qualitative. - The domain-level finding that procedural/workflow skills (Media & File Processing, +38.1) vastly outperform principle/guideline skills (Testing/QA, +16.7) gives skill authors a concrete design heuristic with numerical backing. - Scale: 38,000 trajectories across 14 domain categories is substantially broader than any prior skill evaluation, enabling cross-family comparisons not previously possible. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note four limitations: (1) the with-skill condition discloses skill relevance, which is unrealistic and decouples skill utility from skill-selection ability — the latter deserves its own study; (2) the single LLM judge (Sonnet 4.6) introduces bias, especially on aesthetic or stylistic rubric criteria; (3) the dataset is biased toward software-engineering domains, where most publicly released skills currently live; (4) filtering out skills requiring databases, MCP servers, multi-turn interaction, git state, and local services excludes a non-trivial fraction of real-world skills. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: First, Tessl operates the skill registry at tessl.io/registry, which is one of the primary sources used to collect skills — a conflict of interest that is not acknowledged in the paper. Second, goal completion saturates above 90% for nearly all frontier models with skill access, which the authors themselves attribute to the synthetic tasks being insufficiently challenging; this means the paper's central metric of "skill utility" in practice collapses to instruction-following, leaving goal completion uninformative despite appearing as a separate dimension. Third, the pipeline is entirely LLM-generated end-to-end (task synthesis, rubric generation, judgment), creating a circular evaluation loop where systematic biases in the underlying models (e.g., rubrics that favor responses that look like skill content verbatim) cannot be detected without external ground truth at scale. Fourth, harness differences are confounded with model differences: Gemini models run through OpenHands while Anthropic models run through Claude Code, and Codex CLI handles OpenAI models — making cross-family instruction-following comparisons partially attributable to harness quality rather than model capability alone. Fifth, no analysis of rubric validity is presented — whether the automatically generated rubrics actually capture what a domain expert would consider correct skill adherence. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or deploying agent skills gains real value from this paper's per-skill diagnostic methodology and the concrete cost-quality numbers in Table 4; the evaluation design's explicit skill disclosure and goal-completion saturation mean the scores measure an upper bound on instruction-following rather than end-to-end deployment utility, so treat the reported deltas as optimistic estimates.
Search Results 2606.17819 PDF
#111 — Moderately Innovative (54)

Improved Large Language Diffusion Models (32 ▲ )

Shen Nie, Qiyang Min, Shaoxuan Xu, Zihao Huang, Yuxuan Song, Yong Shan, Yankai Lin, Wayne Xin Zhao, Chongxuan Li, Ji-Rong Wen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces iLLaDA, an 8B masked diffusion language model trained from scratch on 12T tokens with fully bidirectional attention that substantially closes the gap between diffusion and autoregressive language models on general, mathematical, and code benchmarks. - Why it's important: Masked diffusion LMs offer structural advantages over autoregressive models in bidirectional reasoning, long-horizon planning, and data-constrained regimes, but prior work (LLaDA) lagged Qwen2.5 7B by ~12 points average, limiting real-world adoption. - How it does it: iLLaDA combines four practical improvements over LLaDA: scaled pre-training (12T vs 2.3T tokens), grouped-query attention with tied embeddings, a unified SFT format that applies the same masking objective to prompt and response tokens across a continuous 25B-token corpus for 12 epochs, and confidence-based scoring for multiple-choice evaluation. - The good: iLLaDA-Base reaches an average of 63.9 across 8 benchmarks versus 63.3 for Qwen2.5 7B Base, demonstrating that fully bidirectional diffusion pre-training from scratch is now quantitatively on par with a strong autoregressive baseline at the same scale. - The missing: The paper provides no ablation isolating the contribution of the 12T pre-training tokens versus the architectural and SFT changes; the instruct gap versus Qwen2.5 7B Instruct (67.1 vs 77.1) is attributed to missing RL alignment without any experiment to test that claim. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers tracking the diffusion-LM scaling frontier or practitioners building non-autoregressive systems; practitioners who need a deployed instruct model today should note that iLLaDA-Instruct still trails Qwen2.5 7B Instruct by 10 points average.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Practical Impact (78) is the strongest dimension — the work releases 8B model weights and training code at production scale, directly enabling follow-on diffusion-LM work. Novelty (55) is moderate and capped by the paper's own ENGINEERING-OPTIMIZATION classification: every component (GQA, tied embeddings, continuous-corpus SFT, confidence decoding) is imported from the AR literature, with the genuinely new result being the empirical demonstration that masked diffusion reaches Qwen2.5 7B Base parity (63.9 vs 63.3). Rigor (48) is the dimension that pulls the composite down hard — the single most load-bearing ablation, isolating the 2.3T→12T pre-training jump from the architectural/SFT changes, is absent, so the central 'it's the recipe not the data' claim is unsupported; no error bars, no seeds, and per-benchmark inference-hyperparameter tuning add degrees of freedom. Resilience (50) is weak for the same reason: a tough reviewer immediately notes the data-volume confound and the unexplained instruct gap (67.1 vs 77.1) attributed to missing RL without experiment. Completeness (55) is helped by honest, unminimized limitation acknowledgment but hurt by the missing confound controls. Weighted: 0.20*55 + 0.25*48 + 0.15*52 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*78 = 54.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 48 Non-Obv 52 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Autoregressive (AR) large language models dominate language generation, but non-autoregressive masked diffusion LMs offer distinct structural benefits: fully bidirectional context, stronger performance in reversal and bidirectional reasoning tasks, better data reuse under repeated training, and natural integration with multimodal architectures. LLaDA (the predecessor) established proof-of-concept at 8B scale with 2.3T pre-training tokens, but its benchmark scores fell 10–12 points below contemporaneous AR models like Qwen2.5 7B, leaving a gap that prevented practical adoption. The problem is identifying which combination of scaling, architectural, and training-recipe changes closes that gap without changing the fundamental masked diffusion formulation. Key Idea The central insight is that the LLaDA performance gap is not a fundamental limitation of masked diffusion but a consequence of under-resourcing: too little pre-training data, a sub-optimal SFT format that pads and siloes prompt from response tokens, a likelihood-based scoring rule mismatched to multiple-choice evaluation, and architectural choices (multi-head attention, untied embeddings) that inflate memory cost and parameter count without improving quality. Replacing these with standard AR-model engineering choices while keeping the masked diffusion objective intact is sufficient to reach parity with Qwen2.5 7B Base. Methodology Pre-training uses the LLaDA masked diffusion objective: given a clean sequence x₀, sample masking ratio t ~ U[0,1], mask each token independently with probability t, and minimize the cross-entropy over masked tokens weighted by 1/t (Eq. 1). The backbone is a dense Transformer with RMSNorm, SwiGLU, RoPE, grouped-query attention (GQA, 8 KV heads vs 32 in LLaDA), and tied input/output embeddings; these reduce KV-cache memory and parameter count from 8.02B to 7.62B (non-embedding parameters identical at 6.98B). iLLaDA pre-trains on 12T tokens at sequence length 8192 with 30% probability of randomly splitting sequences into two sub-segments (random-length training). The learning rate warms to 2×10⁻⁴, stays flat, then switches to cosine decay when the loss plateaus. SFT reformats the standard approach: rather than padding response tokens within a batch, iLLaDA concatenates all instruction examples into a continuous 25B-token corpus and samples 8192-token windows, applying the same masking objective to prompt, response, and EOS tokens jointly. This trains for 12 epochs with LR warmed to 5×10⁻⁶ then linearly decayed. For multiple-choice inference, confidence-based scoring iteratively reveals the ground-truth token at the position with highest model confidence and accumulates log-probabilities (Eq. 2), rather than computing a likelihood upper bound. For open-ended generation, variable-length block decoding appends mask blocks and iteratively reveals high-confidence positions. Critical design decisions: (1) GQA is justified by future KV-cache compatibility for diffusion LMs, which is plausible but not demonstrated here; (2) the unified SFT corpus format is justified by variable-length generation compatibility and empirically validated via epoch ablation; (3) confidence scoring is justified by a 3-task ablation (Table 4). Key Results All results are from a preprint (arXiv:2606.25331, June 2026) and are not peer-reviewed. Baselines are LLaDA 8B (2.3T tokens), Dream 7B (fine-tuned from Qwen2.5 on 0.6T diffusion tokens), and Qwen2.5 7B (18T tokens, AR). Base model 8-benchmark average: iLLaDA 63.9, LLaDA 51.1, Dream 61.4, Qwen2.5 63.3. Largest base gains over LLaDA: BBH +21.6 (71.3 vs 49.7), ARC-Challenge +14.9 (60.8 vs 45.9), HumanEval +14.6 (50.0 vs 35.4), MBPP +17.8 (57.8 vs 40.0). Instruct model 7-benchmark average: iLLaDA 67.1, LLaDA 54.5, Dream 60.2, Qwen2.5 77.1. iLLaDA-Instruct gains over LLaDA: MATH +14.5 (56.7 vs 42.2), HumanEval +16.5 (65.9 vs 49.4), GSM8K +11.5 (89.0 vs 77.5). Confidence scoring improves over likelihood scoring by 1.3 (PIQA), 0.6 (ARC-C), and 2.3 (HellaSwag). SFT epoch ablation shows monotone improvement from epoch 3 to 12 on GSM8K (84→89), MATH (48→56), and MMLU-Pro (48→52). The base model slightly outperforms Qwen2.5 7B Base on average despite using the diffusion paradigm; the instruct model lags by 10 points average, with the largest gaps on MATH (56.7 vs 75.5), HumanEval (65.9 vs 84.8), and MBPP (58.0 vs 79.2). A notable anomaly: for HumanEval at base, Dream 7B (57.9) outperforms iLLaDA (50.0) despite Dream's narrower training regime. Novelty Classification ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The contribution is genuinely novel as an improved training recipe that reaches AR parity for diffusion LMs, but every individual component (GQA, tied embeddings, continuous corpus SFT, confidence-based decoding, variable-length generation) applies existing techniques from the AR-model literature to the diffusion setting; no new algorithm, loss function, or theoretical result is introduced. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present but narrow: confidence vs likelihood scoring is tested on 3 benchmarks (Table 4), and SFT epoch count is swept at 3/6/9/12 epochs on 3 benchmarks (Figure 1). No ablation isolates the effect of 12T vs 2.3T pre-training data, GQA vs multi-head attention, tied vs untied embeddings, or random-length training. Error bars and significance tests are absent throughout. No multi-seed results are reported. Model weights and code are publicly released at https://github.com/ML-GSAI/LLaDA. The comparison with Qwen2.5 7B is not fully controlled: iLLaDA uses 12T diffusion pre-training tokens while Qwen2.5 used 18T AR tokens, making token-efficiency claims difficult to interpret. Benchmark-specific inference hyperparameters (block lengths, generation budgets) are tuned per benchmark and disclosed in Appendix A, which is appropriate but introduces degrees of freedom that may inflate results relative to a fixed-setting evaluation. The repetitive reasoning loop mitigation for iLLaDA-Instruct (gradually increasing stop-thinking token probability) is a post-hoc heuristic that is not ablated. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (the paper trains and evaluates a large model system). - Practical: iLLaDA 8B runs on commercially available GPU hardware; the FlashAttention variable-length kernel and GQA are standard components compatible with existing inference stacks. - Efficient: GQA reduces KV-cache memory relative to LLaDA's multi-head attention; tied embeddings reduce total parameters by ~0.4B; no wall-clock throughput numbers are reported, so the compute cost versus AR inference remains unquantified. - Effective: iLLaDA-Base matches Qwen2.5 7B Base on average (63.9 vs 63.3) across 8 benchmarks, providing credible evidence it solves the stated goal at the base level; iLLaDA-Instruct does not match Qwen2.5 7B Instruct (67.1 vs 77.1), so the instruct claim is only partially supported. - Scalable: The paper tests only at 8B scale; no scaling law experiments, no results at 1B or 70B scale; the claim that the recipe generalizes to larger models is asserted but not evaluated. The work delivers trained model weights and training code as a public artifact. Strengths - The base model achieving 63.9 average versus Qwen2.5 7B Base's 63.3, despite using fewer pre-training tokens (12T vs 18T) and the diffusion objective, is a concrete and meaningful empirical result that shifts the prior on diffusion LM capability. - The unified SFT corpus format (continuous masking over prompt+response) is a non-obvious design choice that naturally enables variable-length generation and is empirically validated via the epoch sweep. - Public release of weights and code at a production-relevant scale (8B) makes the result reproducible and directly useful for follow-on work on RL alignment and multimodal extensions. - The confidence-based scoring rule for multiple-choice evaluation addresses a real mismatch between the diffusion model's inference mechanism and standard benchmark protocols, and the ablation (Table 4) concretely validates the choice. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state two limitations: (1) iLLaDA has not been aligned with reinforcement learning, which they believe explains most of the instruct gap versus Qwen2.5 7B Instruct; (2) the study is limited to 8B scale due to compute constraints, with no matched comparison at other scales. The authors also disclose the repetitive reasoning loop failure mode in Appendix A. These are stated directly and without minimization. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The single most important missing ablation is pre-training data volume: the jump from 2.3T (LLaDA) to 12T tokens could explain most of the benchmark gains without any architectural or SFT change. No intermediate checkpoint at 2.3T or 5T tokens is provided. - Dream 7B outperforms iLLaDA on HumanEval at base (57.9 vs 50.0) and on MATH at base (39.6 vs 38.4), which the paper does not explain or investigate. Dream is fine-tuned from Qwen2.5, giving it an 18T token foundation; a proper controlled comparison would require either matching training budgets or disentangling initialization from post-training data. - No throughput or latency numbers are reported. The practical viability of diffusion LM inference depends critically on wall-clock generation speed versus AR models, and this is omitted entirely. - The benchmark-specific inference hyperparameter tuning (block lengths varying from 3 to 512 per task) is disclosed but not ablated, creating a potential for benchmark-specific over-tuning that the paper does not address. - The stop-thinking token heuristic for repetitive reasoning loops is an unablated post-hoc fix that could affect MATH and MMLU-Pro results in unquantified ways.
