Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that the survey sample cannot claim comprehensive sector representation; points raised by participants may not reflect the views of the sector as a whole. International benchmarking contacts are indicative rather than definitive. The report acknowledges failure to produce an economic baseline and key performance indicators due to complexity, time, and resource constraints, and calls for a standalone follow-on economic assessment. The inventory is described as non-exhaustive, noting that companies with only partial AAA activity may be missed due to SIC-code search constraints. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimising them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The 41-respondent survey has no power analysis, no weighting to correct for the SME over-representation (56% of respondents versus 46% of the inventory), and no comparison to a non-respondent characterisation — making it impossible to assess non-response bias. Workshop discussions with 10–15 participants per session are treated as thematic rather than statistically generalisable, but the report's PESTLE and SWOT tables do not always maintain that hedging, occasionally reading as sector-wide findings. The international benchmarking relies on SDI/FCDO contacts who "do not directly represent AAA sectors"; no primary data from comparator-country sector practitioners was collected, reducing the benchmarking's evidential weight. The recommendation to develop a "dynamic sector intelligence platform" does not address data governance, funding model, or maintenance responsibility — omissions that historically determine whether such platforms survive beyond initial investment. The report groups aquaculture (a £1.196 billion turnover sector with large multinationals) alongside early-stage agritech SMEs without disaggregating barriers by sub-sector maturity, which risks conflating structurally different commercialisation challenges. Bottom Line: Practitioners in Scottish agri-food policy and enterprise will find useful landscape orientation and a sharp international comparison here, but should treat the survey findings as indicative signals rather than statistically grounded sector characterisation, and commission the explicitly missing economic baseline before making major resource allocation decisions.
Search Results
#149 — Moderately Innovative (40)

Adoption, assimilation and sustainability outcomes of AI and Industry 4.0 Technologies in supply chain

CJ Okpeku · repository.essex.ac.uk · simulation environments for business processes - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This thesis introduces an integrated three-study framework that empirically models how TOE (Technological-Organisational-Environmental) factors and Technology Readiness Index (TRI) motivators/inhibitors drive AI and Industry 4.0 adoption, and how subsequent assimilation translates into supply chain sustainability performance via dual Dynamic Capabilities mediators (Technological Capability and Organisational Agility), tested on Nigerian firms. - Why it's important: Organisations invest heavily in AI and I4.0T yet frequently fail to achieve sustainability gains, and nearly all adoption research comes from developed economies — leaving the mechanisms linking adoption to outcomes poorly understood in emerging market contexts where infrastructure constraints and institutional factors differ substantially. - How it does it: Two cross-sectional online surveys of Nigerian managers (n=192 and n=196) feed Covariance-Based Structural Equation Models (CB-SEM) in AMOS, with TRI as a mediator between TOE factors and adoption (Study 2) and Technological Capability plus Organisational Agility as parallel mediators between assimilation and Triple Bottom Line sustainability performance (Study 3). - The good: Study 3 reveals that social sustainability loads more strongly on the higher-order sustainability construct than environmental sustainability in the Nigerian context — a direct inversion of developed-economy literature — and offers a theoretically grounded explanation tied to labour market structure, weak environmental monitoring, and leapfrogging effects. - The missing: Both empirical studies draw from the same overlapping respondent pool, making the structural models statistically non-independent; this is acknowledged but cannot be remedied post hoc, so Study 3 findings are best read as covariance patterns rather than independently corroborated evidence. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on digital transformation in emerging economies or need an empirical anchor for the claim that assimilation matters more than adoption; skip if you need generalizable causal evidence — the cross-sectional single-source design from a single country precludes that.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (30) is the dominant drag: Study 3's structural SRMR sits at 0.118–0.133 (above the 0.08 ceiling), the two studies draw from overlapping respondent pools so they cannot corroborate each other, CMB rests only on the weak Harman's single-factor test, and all sustainability outcomes are self-reported perceptions with no objective triangulation. Resilience (35) is low — the cross-sectional single-source single-country design precludes the causal claims the framework implies. Novelty (50) reflects a real but modest contribution: the TRI-as-mediator integration and the Nigeria social-over-environmental sustainability inversion. This resembles the user's negative-calibration set (applied survey-modeling, emerging-market framing, no released artifact) more than the positive set. Weighted: 0.20*50 + 0.25*30 + 0.15*40 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*35 + 0.10*35 ≈ 40 — hypothesis-generating, not hypothesis-confirming.
empirical Novelty 50 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 50 Resilience 35 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Despite widespread investment in AI and Industry 4.0 Technologies (I4.0T) — encompassing IoT, blockchain, cloud computing, big data analytics, robotic process automation, cyber-physical systems, and additive manufacturing — supply chain organisations consistently fail to achieve the sustainability performance improvements these technologies promise. The literature on technology adoption in supply chains concentrates on developed economies, overlooks the distinction between adoption (committing to a technology) and assimilation (routinising it in workflows and inter-organisational processes), and treats sustainability one-dimensionally by emphasising environmental outcomes while neglecting social and economic dimensions. In contexts such as Nigeria, with fragmented digital infrastructure, skills gaps, high power distance (PDI=80), and collectivist culture, the drivers of adoption and the mechanisms through which assimilation generates sustainability value may operate differently from Western models. Existing frameworks (TOE, TAM, UTAUT, DCT) were developed for static technology contexts and do not account for AI's adaptive, autonomous, and opaque characteristics. Key Idea The thesis's core claim is that adoption and assimilation are distinct stages in a value creation process, and that the gap between them — and between assimilation and sustainability outcomes — is explained by mediating constructs that existing frameworks treat as exogenous or ignore entirely. In Study 2, TRI motivating and inhibiting factors mediate the path from TOE environmental/organisational/technological factors to adoption decisions. In Study 3, two Dynamic Capability constructs — Technological Capability (the ability to integrate, reconfigure, and deploy digital tools) and Organisational Agility (market sensing, operational adjustment, and network reconfiguration) — partially mediate the path from assimilation to Triple Bottom Line sustainability performance, with assimilation also retaining a significant direct effect (partial mediation). Methodology Study 2 (Chapter 3): A deductive quantitative survey (n=192 Nigerian managers, ≥managerial level) was administered via Qualtrics. Constructs operationalise six TOE dimensions — Technology Characteristics, Relative Advantage, Organisational Resources, Organisational Culture, Competitive Pressure, External Support — plus TRI Motivating Factors and TRI Inhibiting Factors as mediators, with AI/I4.0T adoption as the outcome. Missing data were handled via Regression Based Imputation; Exploratory Factor Analysis (PAF, varimax) with Kaiser criterion reduced items, followed by Confirmatory Factor Analysis in AMOS. The structural model uses CB-SEM with bootstrap mediation (indirect effects via AMOS plugin). CFA fit: CFI=0.949, SRMR=0.053, RMSEA=0.046, TLI=0.941. Structural model fit: CMIN/df=1.724, CFI=0.909, SRMR=0.084, RMSEA=0.062. Common Method Bias assessed via Harman's single-factor test (first factor=27.87% of variance, below 50% threshold — though this test is widely considered inadequate). Critical design decisions: treating motivating and inhibiting factors as separate constructs rather than poles of a single scale is well-justified theoretically; using CB-SEM with relatively small n (192) and a model of 9 constructs creates parameter-to-sample-size pressure that is not discussed. Study 3 (Chapter 4): A second survey wave drew from the same Nigerian respondent recruitment channels (LinkedIn, direct email; 454 invited, 196 valid). Constructs: Technology Assimilation (TAS, 5 items; Chatterjee et al. 2002 routinisation/infusion stages), Technological Capability (TC, 7 items), Organisational Agility (OA, 7 items), and a higher-order Sustainability Performance (SUS) construct comprising Economic (ECP, 4 items), Social (SOP, 3 items), and Environmental (ENP, 4 items) sub-dimensions. EFA (KMO=0.919) yielded 6 factors explaining 73.28% of variance. CFA fit: CMIN/df=1.744, CFI=0.951, SRMR=0.045, RMSEA=0.062. Initial structural model fit was marginal (CMIN/df=2.257, CFI=0.917, SRMR=0.133). A higher-order CFA for sustainability improved fit (CFI=0.949, SRMR=0.051, RMSEA=0.063). The final higher-order structural mediation model (bootstrapping 5,000 samples) showed CMIN/df=2.049, CFI=0.930, RMSEA=0.073, SRMR=0.118 — SRMR consistently exceeds the 0.08 threshold throughout Study 3's structural models, which the thesis acknowledges but does not fully resolve. Key Results Study 2: Cloud Computing is the most adopted technology (72.6% of surveyed organisations), followed by Big Data Analytics (56.8%) and AI (40.5%); Blockchain (2.6%), Drones (3.7%), and 3D Printing (4.2%) remain at early-adopter or innovator stage. All six TOE dimensions significantly predict TRI Motivating Factors (H1a–H6a, all p<0.001 or p<0.05); inhibiting factors are predicted by Relative Advantage (H2b, p<0.004), Organisational Resources (H3b, p<0.001), Organisational Culture (H4b, p<0.001), and Competitive Pressure (H5b, p=0.002), but NOT by Technology Characteristics (H1b, p=0.284) or External Support (H6b, p=0.218). The strongest path to adoption runs through TRI Motivating Factors (β=1.364, p<0.001). Counterintuitively, TRI Inhibiting Factors also show a significant positive relationship with adoption (β=0.236, p=0.016), interpreted as awareness of barriers prompting proactive mitigation. Study 3: TAS predicts TC (β=0.536, p<0.001) more strongly than OA (β=0.353, p<0.001). Both mediators independently predict sustainability: TC→SUS (β=0.241, p<0.001); OA→SUS (β=0.278, p<0.001). TAS retains a significant direct effect on SUS (β=0.264, p<0.001), confirming partial mediation. Total effect of TAS on SUS = 0.477, decomposed as: direct 53.7%, through TC 26.4% (b=0.126, p=0.011, VAF=26.4%, small-medium effect), through OA 19.9% (b=0.095, p=0.039, VAF=19.9%, small effect). A notable finding: social sustainability (SOP) loads more strongly on the higher-order SUS construct than environmental sustainability (ENP), inverting the pattern dominant in developed-economy literature. The TC-OA correlation (0.791) marginally exceeds the Fornell-Larcker discriminant validity threshold (√AVE=0.783), though HTMT ratio (0.781) stays below 0.85, providing partial reassurance. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. Genuine novelty lies in: (1) positioning TRI as a structural mediator within the TOE framework rather than a parallel adoption model — this integration has not been empirically tested; (2) the dual-mediation model (TC and OA as parallel Dynamic Capability pathways from assimilation to sustainability); (3) the emerging economy context and the social-over-environmental sustainability inversion in Nigeria. The individual theoretical components (TOE, TRI, DCT, TBL) are all established; novelty derives from their structural combination and the Nigerian empirical setting. Rigor & Reproducibility SEM fit indices are reported consistently and meet commonly accepted thresholds for the measurement models, but Study 3's structural models show SRMR values of 0.118–0.133, which exceed the standard 0.08 ceiling and are only defended by noting other indices fall within range. No ablations test whether specific TRI items or capability sub-dimensions drive results. Error bars and significance levels are reported; bootstrapping (2,000–5,000 samples) is used for mediation. No code, data, or reproducible analysis files are released — this is a PhD thesis. A serious methodological concern is that Chapters 3 and 4 both draw from the same recruitment channels and substantially overlapping respondent pools, meaning the two structural models are not statistically independent. The thesis discloses this explicitly but the implication — that findings across the two chapters cannot serve as mutual corroboration — receives insufficient analytical weight. Harman's single-factor test is used for CMB detection in both studies; this is the weakest available CMB diagnostic and does not substitute for marker-variable or procedural separation approaches. All sustainability outcomes are self-reported managerial perceptions, not objective metrics (emissions, waste, wages), introducing potential social desirability bias toward overstatement. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: The research delivers survey-based insights actionable by managers and policymakers without specialised hardware; the framework could be operationalised for organisational assessments using standard survey tools. - Efficient: Not applicable in the computational sense; data collection required a single survey round per study with standard SEM software (SPSS 28, AMOS 26). - Effective: The evidence establishes significant covariance patterns consistent with the hypothesised mediation chains, but cross-sectional single-source design precludes causal claims; whether managers who invest in TC and OA will improve measured (rather than perceived) sustainability outcomes remains untested. - Scalable: The model is tested on mid-to-large Nigerian firms across 13 sectors; generalisability to smaller firms, other sub-Saharan African contexts, or developed economies is asserted but not tested. No software artifact, dataset, or replication package is provided. Strengths - The integration of TOE and TRI into a unified mediation model is theoretically grounded and fills a documented gap; prior work treats them as competing rather than complementary frameworks. - The dual-mediation finding that TC and OA contribute differently — TC offers a stronger indirect pathway while OA shows a stronger direct relationship with sustainability performance once established — adds empirical nuance to Dynamic Capabilities Theory beyond the common finding that dynamic capabilities matter. - The Nigeria-specific finding that social sustainability dominates over environmental sustainability in the higher-order construct, with three structural explanations (labour market salience, weak environmental reporting, leapfrogging), is the most intellectually distinctive contribution and challenges developed-economy bias in the literature. - The three paradoxes identified (Adoption vs. Application; Complexity vs. Capability; Speed vs. Strategy) are well-framed and provide concrete hypotheses for future longitudinal or moderating-variable studies. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) overlapping respondent pools across Chapters 3 and 4, making results non-independent and interpretable only as covariance patterns; (2) cross-sectional design precluding causal inference and temporal sequencing of adoption, assimilation, and capability development; (3) single-country (Nigeria) sample limiting generalisability; (4) under-representation of small firms and public/multinational companies; (5) self-reported data risk with potential social desirability bias; (6) SRMR above optimal threshold in the mediation model. The authors confront these limitations with reasonable candour, which represents a credibility strength. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Harman's single-factor test is widely criticised as insufficient for detecting CMB; neither the marker-variable technique nor a confirmatory factor analysis with a common method factor is attempted, leaving the cross-sectional single-source inflation of path coefficients unquantified. - The positive and significant path from TRI Inhibiting Factors to adoption (β=0.236, p=0.016) is treated as an interesting finding but not tested for reverse causality or moderation; it could reflect a sample selection artifact where firms that have already adopted are more aware of inhibitors. - Discriminant validity between TC and OA (Fornell-Larcker criterion violated; HTMT=0.781) raises unresolved concerns about whether two separate mediators are truly being distinguished; a moderated mediation or sequential mediation model might better capture their interplay. - The SLR (Chapter 2) relies on a CIMO-Symphony Orchestra Matrix synthesised by the authors without an inter-rater reliability check or PRISMA-style quality scoring of included studies, weakening its evidentiary basis. - Sustainability outcomes are perceptual; no triangulation against firm-level secondary data (ESG reports, emissions filings, financial statements) is attempted, and the thesis does not address whether perceptual and objective measures of sustainability correlate in the Nigerian context. - The thesis treats AI and I4.0T as a unified adoption construct despite motivating their conceptual distinction in Chapter 1; differential adoption effects are not tested. Bottom Line This thesis provides a well-structured and theoretically motivated empirical investigation of a genuinely important question, and the Nigerian context and social-sustainability inversion finding are the most distinctive contributions, but the overlapping samples, consistently elevated SRMR in structural models, reliance on Harman's test for CMB, and perceptual sustainability measures collectively limit confidence in the quantitative findings; readers seeking causal mechanisms should treat the results as hypothesis-generating rather than hypothesis-confirming.
Search Results
#150 — Incremental (39)

Research on the pathways for high-quality development of the Longdong comprehensive energy and chemical industry base through new quality productivity

F Yang, C Xi, H Zhang, Z Wen, X Lu, Z Liu · link.springer.com · digital twins for enterprises - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper characterizes the industrial structure of 1,072 energy and chemical enterprises in Gansu's Longdong region using Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) concentration analysis and chi-square tests, then proposes sector-specific development pathways for oil/gas, coal, and new energy under China's "dual carbon" and "new quality productivity" policy frameworks. - Why it's important: The Longdong base faces a structural tension between fossil-fuel dependence (HHI_industry ≈ 0.24, high concentration) and China's mandatory 2060 carbon neutrality target, and no prior study has provided an enterprise-level empirical baseline for this nationally designated energy reserve. - How it does it: It combines a near-complete census of registered enterprises from government taxation and credit databases with HHI arithmetic and chi-square independence tests, then layers operational case vignettes from named oilfield and coal companies onto a government-planning-document-derived 2030 energy mix forecast. - The good: The enterprise census — 1,072 firms cross-tabulated by ownership type, industry sector, district, and registered capital — is more granular than typical Chinese regional strategy papers that work solely from aggregate provincial output statistics. - The missing: The unit of analysis is enterprise count, not output value or employment, making the HHI a poor proxy for actual economic concentration; no counterfactual analysis, independent energy-mix modeling, or comparison with analogous resource-transition regions (Xinjiang, Shanxi) is provided. - Read it or not: Worth scanning for researchers tracking Chinese provincial energy transition policy or studying resource-concentrated industrial clusters; skip if seeking generalizable methods or transferable quantitative insights.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (industrial-structure analysis with policy synthesis). Completeness (55) and Resilience (45) are the relative strengths: the 1,072-firm census is more granular than typical Chinese regional strategy papers, and the authors honestly label the pathways 'exploratory and directional,' confront the enterprise-count-vs-output-value flaw, and publish in a peer-reviewed venue (Discover Sustainability). Rigor (40) is capped because the quantitative work is two HHI computations plus two chi-square tests with no regression isolating digitalization effects, operational figures are self-reported by named firms without independent verification, and data is only available on request. Novelty (35) reflects a genuinely new regional baseline but a standard HHI method; Non-Obviousness (25) and Impact (35) are limited by no comparative region analysis and no released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*35 + 0.25*40 + 0.15*25 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*45 + 0.10*35 = 39.
empirical Novelty 35 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 25 Completeness 55 Resilience 45 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation The Longdong region of Gansu Province — covering Qingyang and Pingliang cities — holds reserves of 7.88 billion tons of petroleum, 236 billion tons of coal, 2 trillion cubic meters of natural gas, and 22.2 million kW of technically exploitable new energy capacity, making it a nationally designated strategic energy base. Despite this resource breadth, the industrial structure is heavily concentrated in electricity, heat, gas, and water production (39.9% of all 1,072 enterprises; HHI_industry ≈ 0.24), creating systemic fragility. China's "3060" dual-carbon targets — peak emissions by 2030, neutrality by 2060 — require this traditionally fossil-fuel-anchored base to restructure. National policy has invoked "new quality productivity" (新质生产力) as the guiding framework for technology-driven industrial upgrading, but specific implementation pathways for a geographically remote, resource-dependent regional cluster like Longdong have not been empirically grounded or systematically laid out. Key Idea The paper's organizing claim is that Longdong's transformation does not require abandoning fossil fuel industries but rather digitally upgrading them — deploying 5G+ABCD (Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, Big Data) technologies to raise efficiency and reduce carbon intensity while cross-subsidizing new energy development — and simultaneously positioning hydrogen production and the "East Data, West Computing" computing-power corridor as new economic anchors. There is no single algorithmic trick; the paper assembles standard digital-industrial concepts into a regionally contextualized roadmap and backs it with an enterprise-level industrial structure baseline that prior work lacked. Methodology The empirical section uses a census approach: enterprise data retrieved from the National Enterprise Credit Information Publicity System (Gansu), the Gansu Provincial Tax Service of the State Taxation Administration, and local government platforms, compiled as of March 2025. The sample is 1,072 registered energy and chemical enterprises across Qingyang (1,018 entries) and Pingliang (418 entries). Regional concentration is measured by HHI_region = Σ s_i² where s_i is district-level enterprise share; industry concentration is computed the same way by sector. A chi-square test (χ²=0.09, df=1, p=0.76) checks ownership-structure differences between cities; a second chi-square test (χ²=128.3, df=2, p<0.001, Cramer's V=0.35) tests association between ownership type and industry affiliation. Three critical design decisions: (1) using enterprise count rather than output value or employment as the unit of analysis — defensible given data availability but limits economic interpretation; (2) collapsing 30+ legal entity types into "private" vs. "non-private" for chi-square testing, which is expedient but coarse; (3) deriving the 2030 energy mix projection from government planning documents and National Bureau of Statistics conversion coefficients rather than independent modeling. The strategy sections synthesize 14th Five-Year Plans, National Energy Administration action plans, and operational vignettes from named companies. Key Results HHI_region = 0.16 (moderate geographic concentration; Xifeng District holds 35% of all enterprises). HHI_industry = 0.24 (high sectoral concentration; electricity/heat/gas/water production alone accounts for 428 of 1,072 firms). State-owned enterprises constitute only 5.64% of the enterprise population. Chi-square confirms no significant ownership-structure difference between Qingyang and Pingliang (p=0.76) but significant association between ownership type and industry (χ²=128.3, p<0.001, Cramer's V=0.35). Registered capital concentrates in the 5–50 million yuan range; two outliers dominate: Qingyang Xinzhuang Coal Industry Co. at 4.493 billion yuan and Huating Coal Industry Group at 3.117 billion yuan. Operational vignettes report: Xifeng oilfield electrification retrofits on 120 beam-pumping units reduced per-well electricity consumption by 15–20%, saving 2 million kWh annually; AI-optimized water injection in Qingcheng/Huachi blocks raised single-well production by ~5% and cut ineffective energy consumption by ~8%; Longdong Oilfield achieved CO₂ capture and utilization of approximately 69,000 tons in 2023 with 52,000 tons reduced January–October 2024. The 2030 energy mix projection targets coal at 48.7%, crude oil 21.2%, natural gas 8.8%, new energy 17.7%, hydrogen/storage 3.5%. These operational figures are sourced from named company reports, not controlled experiments. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (industrial structure characterization of a previously undocumented regional cluster) + policy synthesis. HHI application to a Chinese regional energy base is methodologically standard; no novel technique is introduced. The genuine novelty is the enterprise-level census baseline for Longdong, which prior national and provincial strategy documents lacked. The "Longdong model" roadmap is a standard combination of national policy frameworks (dual carbon, new quality productivity, East Data West Computing) applied to a specific regional context. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations or scenario comparisons in the strategy sections. The quantitative analysis is limited to descriptive statistics, two HHI computations, and two chi-square tests; no regression analysis isolates the effect of digitalization on any productivity measure. No error bars on any operational performance figures. The enterprise dataset is available on reasonable request from the corresponding author but is not deposited in a public repository, and no code is released. The paper's conclusion explicitly states the findings are "exploratory and directional" requiring "verification through subsequent, more systematic empirical research" — an honest self-assessment. Published in Discover Sustainability (Springer, peer-reviewed), article in press; the manuscript note says final editing has not occurred. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL (industrial structure analysis) combined with policy analysis. - Practical: The statistical analysis uses publicly available government registry data and requires only basic spreadsheet arithmetic, fully replicable by any regional planning office. - Efficient: No novel computational tool is introduced; all analysis is achievable with standard statistical software. The operational case vignettes describe deployments by named firms using commercially available IoT, 5G, and AI tools. - Effective: The descriptive statistics credibly document high industrial concentration and private-enterprise dominance. The operational vignettes (15–20% electricity savings, ~5% production gains) are cited from company-level reports, not independently verified experiments; attribution to specific interventions is asserted, not proven. - Scalable: The census approach is replicable in other Chinese regional energy bases. The claim that the resulting framework constitutes a transferable "Longdong model" is asserted without comparative evidence from structurally similar regions. The paper delivers no software artifact, dataset deposit, trained model, or reusable proof technique. It stops at paper form. Strengths - The enterprise census (1,072 firms cross-tabulated across ownership type, district, industry, registered capital, and growth trend from 1985 to 2024) provides a more granular empirical foundation than typical Chinese regional policy papers that cite only aggregate provincial output or GDP figures. - The authors quantify industrial concentration numerically (HHI_industry = 0.24) and correctly interpret it as a systemic risk factor rather than merely asserting it qualitatively — this is the paper's most useful analytical contribution. - The authors explicitly acknowledge that their pathway proposals are theoretical and call for enterprise-level pilot validation; this honest framing is a credibility positive in a genre prone to overclaiming. - The 2020–2024 progression of digital deployment at the Xifeng oilfield (IoT sensor rollout in 2020, industrial internet platform connecting 1,200 oil wells by 2023, AI-optimized water injection schemes) provides a concrete documented trajectory rather than purely aspirational claims. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify: (1) limited access to micro-level operational data — energy efficiency, carbon emissions, and digital investment at the firm level — constraining enterprise-level productivity analysis; (2) inability to differentiate digital adoption capacity across ownership types and firm scales; (3) proposed development pathways are largely theoretical and require enterprise-level case-study validation; (4) reliance on publicly available data limits precision; (5) government-university-enterprise collaboration proposals remain in a theoretical discussion stage. These limitations are stated directly in the conclusions and future research sections, with no apparent minimization. Analyst-Identified Gaps The enterprise count unit of analysis is a misleading proxy for market structure: 310 single-person natural-person LLCs in Qingyang carry the same unit weight in the HHI as Qingyang Xinzhuang Coal Industry Co. (registered capital 4.493 billion yuan); an output-value or employment-based HHI would yield a structurally different picture and likely show far higher concentration. The chi-square analysis collapses a 30+-category ownership taxonomy into two groups, eliminating heterogeneity between central state enterprises, local state enterprises, and private firms — the very distinctions most relevant to digital transformation capacity. The 2030 energy mix projection (Figure 14: coal 48.7%, new energy 17.7%) is arithmetic applied to official planning targets, not a forecast — but the paper frames it as a research output. The "new quality productivity" concept, drawn from a 2023 political speech, is adopted uncritically as an analytical framework without operationalization: no metric for new quality productivity is defined, measured, or tested. The paper conflates nationally headquartered firms (Changqing Oilfield, a PetroChina subsidiary) with locally registered entities, which may distort the geographic enterprise count. No comparison is drawn with analogous resource-base transitions in Xinjiang, Inner Mongolia, or Shanxi, which would enable assessment of whether the proposed pathways are regionally distinctive or generic. Bottom Line A practitioner or policy researcher focused on China's provincial energy transition gains a useful empirical baseline on Longdong's industrial structure and a readable catalogue of current digitalization deployments, but the paper offers no rigorous economic analysis, no independent modeling, and no transferable methodology beyond a standard HHI calculation applied to a new region.
Search Results
#151 — Incremental (38)

Current Trends of Designing E-Governance Ontology for Cloud-Based Smart and Trusted Citizen-Centric Services: A Systematic Literature Review

ZG Tadesse, DP Sharma, M Abebe, M Samuel · survey.amu.edu.et · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents a PRISMA 2020-based systematic literature review (SLR) of 52 peer-reviewed articles (2017–2025) that identifies quantified research gaps and key design features required for adaptive e-governance ontologies supporting cloud-based, citizen-centric public services. - Why it's important: Existing e-governance ontologies lack cross-country adaptability, semantic interoperability, and intelligent personalization, blocking governments in developing nations — notably Ethiopia targeting E-government 3.0 by 2030 — from delivering trustworthy digital public services. - How it does it: Searches across 10 databases (Scopus, WoS, IEEE Xplore, ACM, Springer, ScienceDirect, PubMed, MDPI, ProQuest, Semantic Scholar) yielded 343 records; PRISMA filtering reduced this to 52 included studies, which were coded by artifact type and analyzed against three research questions to produce a five-category gap taxonomy with percentage distributions. - The good: The gap quantification is specific and structured: 37% of reviewed articles cite semantic integration/interoperability failure, 21% cite government process management limited at the semantic level, 19% cite lack of common national language or reference frameworks, 13% cite lack of semantic web technology awareness, and 10% cite legal/policy barriers. - The missing: No novel ontology, algorithm, or prototype is proposed or implemented; gap categories overlap substantially ("semantic interoperability" and "government process management at semantic level" are not mutually exclusive), and no inter-rater reliability coefficient is reported for the coding of all 52 papers. - Read it or not: Worth a quick read for researchers entering the e-governance ontology space in developing-country contexts who need a structured gap map; practitioners designing production systems will find stronger guidance in the primary sources cited (AD-eGIE, COLABOR-I, Web3-DAO, BTNAD, etc.).
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (PRISMA-based systematic literature review, no system built). Rigor (40) carries transparency credit for full search strings, a PRISMA flow diagram, and per-paper gap coding, but is pulled down by single-coder categorization with no inter-rater reliability, overlapping gap categories that make the 37%/21% split potentially an artifact, an unexplained n=49-vs-52 discrepancy, and 21 unretrieved studies. Novelty (35) is a synthesis product, the quantified gap map and Ethiopian/Sub-Saharan framing, with no algorithm, system, or dataset. This closely resembles the user's negatively-rated review papers. Composite: 0.20*35 + 0.25*40 + 0.15*30 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*35 + 0.10*40 = 38.
empirical Novelty 35 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 30 Completeness 45 Resilience 35 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Governments worldwide are transitioning from traditional e-governance portals to cloud-based smart governance that delivers personalized, interoperable, and trusted citizen services. Current e-governance ontologies, frameworks, and models fail on three axes: they are not transferable across countries with different governance cultures and legal systems; they lack semantic interoperability mechanisms that allow heterogeneous government agencies to exchange data coherently; and they offer no intelligent personalization layer. The gap is especially acute in developing countries like Ethiopia, which has committed to "E-government 3.0" by 2030 but possesses no country-specific ontological framework. No prior survey systematically maps the gap landscape across these multiple dimensions simultaneously with quantified frequencies. Key Idea The review's organizing insight is that e-governance ontology deficits cluster into five orthogonal categories — lack of common language/reference frameworks; lack of semantic data integration; government process management limited to the syntactic level; legal and policy barriers; and insufficient practitioner awareness of semantic web technologies — and that the dominant blocker, accounting for 37% of surveyed articles, is semantic interoperability failure rather than infrastructure shortfalls. This framing usefully disaggregates what is often treated as a single "interoperability" problem, but the insight is a synthesis product rather than a technical contribution; there is no identifiable algorithmic or formal key idea. Methodology The SLR follows PRISMA 2020 guidelines. The identification step drew 343 records from Scopus (n=161), Springer (n=76), IEEE Xplore (n=72), ACM (n=23), MDPI (n=4), ScienceDirect (n=5), PubMed (n=2), and DPI (n=2). After removing 36 duplicates and 76 records for other reasons, 231 were screened; 158 were excluded (irrelevance n=133, pre-2017 n=17, non-English n=3, full-text unavailable n=4, non-academic n=1), leaving 73 sought for retrieval. Of these, 21 were not retrieved and 52 were included. Three research questions guided coding: (RQ1) key features for an adaptive cloud-based e-governance ontology; (RQ2) existing ontologies and their limitations in dynamic environments; (RQ3) implementation challenges and future directions. Studies were coded by artifact type (architecture 4%, framework 25%, model 19%, ontology 23%, other 29%) and by five gap categories using tabulation. Critical design decisions include restricting to 2017–2025 (argued by relevance to current technology waves, but not substantiated by sensitivity analysis) and requiring English full text (excludes potentially relevant African-language or Arabic-language governance work without acknowledging the bias). A single research team performed all coding with no reported inter-rater reliability check. Key Results Among the 52 included studies: 37% (19 articles) cite lack of semantic integration/interoperability as their primary gap; 21% (11 articles) cite government process management limited at the semantic level; 19% (10 articles) cite lack of common national language or reference frameworks; 13% (7 articles) cite lack of awareness of semantic web technologies; and 10% (5 articles) cite legal and policy barriers. Artifact distribution: 25% frameworks, 23% ontologies, 19% models, 4% architectures, 29% other. 49% of included articles are indexed in Scopus. Publication trend peaked at 9 articles in both 2017 and 2019, dropped to 2 in 2022, and recovered to 5 in 2025. Eight design features for adaptive e-governance ontologies are synthesized: smart/intelligent (ML, DL, LLMs); adaptability and dynamic knowledge representation; trustworthiness and accountability; citizen-centric design; cloud-based deployment; interoperability; scalability; security. Twelve specific named artifacts are catalogued with their design focus and addressed features, including AD-eGIE (deep learning for dynamic data structures), COLABOR-I (AI-driven e-collaboration), Web3-DAO (blockchain governance), BTNAD (naming ambiguity elimination), and NigOeGov (citizen-centric ontology categorization for Nigerian federal ministries). Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (in the meta-analytic/SLR sense). The contribution is a structured synthesis of gap distributions across 52 studies, with quantified percentage frequencies per category, and a compiled 8-feature design taxonomy. There is no new method, algorithm, formal result, system, or dataset. The review applies standard SLR methodology (PRISMA 2020) to a moderately specialized domain intersection (ontology engineering + e-governance + cloud computing + developing-country context). What is genuinely novel relative to prior surveys is the Ethiopian/Sub-Saharan framing and the five-category quantified gap decomposition; the technical literature surveyed is largely documented elsewhere. Rigor & Reproducibility The PRISMA 2020 flow diagram is provided with record counts at each stage, and the full search strings are reported across multiple Boolean combinations covering OWL, RDF, semantic web, e-governance, smart government, and geographic specifiers (Ethiopia, Africa, developing country). Per-paper gap coding is presented in Table IV (52 entries with year and research gap). However, no inter-rater reliability coefficient (Cohen's kappa or similar) is reported; gap categories such as "semantic interoperability" and "government process management at the semantic level" overlap substantially, making the 37%/21% split potentially an artifact of the single coder's categorization choices. The abstract states that 49 peer-reviewed repositories were selected while the body and PRISMA diagram report n=52 — a three-paper discrepancy that is unexplained. The 21 studies not retrieved (29% of those sought) are not characterized; selective retrieval failure could bias the gap distribution. No software, dataset, or coding rubric is released. The paper is published in EIJET (Ethiopian International Journal of Engineering and Technology), Volume 4, Issue 1, 2026 — a young regional journal whose peer review depth is unknown and which is not indexed in Scopus or WoS; findings should be read at the confidence level of a regional peer-reviewed venue. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL (meta-analytic SLR; no primary experiment, no system built). - Practical: The eight-feature taxonomy and five-category gap map are usable as design checklists by ontology engineers targeting cloud e-governance applications; no special hardware is required. - Efficient: The synthesis covers 52 studies over an 8-year window using tabulation-level coding, which is appropriate for the SLR form but delivers less precision than quantitative meta-analysis or citation-weighted gap importance scores. - Effective: Gap identification relies entirely on self-reported limitations in reviewed papers rather than independent practitioner validation, expert surveys, or failed-deployment case analysis; whether semantic interoperability truly dominates over legal barriers in practical implementation difficulty is unverified by any primary data. - Scalable: Not applicable to the SLR artifact itself; claims about the scalability requirements of future ontologies are unverified assertions drawn from the surveyed literature. The paper stops at paper form; no software artifact, prototype ontology, or annotated dataset is released. Strengths - The PRISMA 2020 protocol is followed with structural transparency: full search strings, flow diagram, inclusion/exclusion criteria, and per-paper gap coding (Table IV, 52 entries) are all provided, enabling partial reproducibility of the selection stage. - The five-category gap taxonomy with quantified distributions (37%/21%/19%/13%/10%) converts what prior reviews treat as a qualitative list of challenges into a prioritized frequency map — a useful input for research agenda-setting. - Table III catalogues 12 named artifacts with artifact type, key features addressed, and design focus in a single comparable view, which reduces the effort required for a newcomer to locate and differentiate existing work. - The developing-country and Ethiopian contextual framing is underrepresented in the ontology engineering literature; articulating the absence of country-specific e-governance ontologies for Sub-Saharan Africa as a first-class research gap is a legitimate contribution to the community discourse. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that future work must focus on developing specific modeling methods for ontology entities, validating proposed solutions in real-world scenarios, and exploring scalability and adaptability for growing data volumes. They acknowledge that no information is available on developed e-governance ontologies within the Ethiopian context specifically. These scope boundaries are stated honestly. The authors do not acknowledge the single-coder limitation, the n=49/n=52 inconsistency, the non-retrieval bias risk from 21 missing studies, or the overlap between gap categories. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No inter-rater reliability measure is reported; the percentage breakdowns (37%, 21%, etc.) are presented as findings but reflect a single coder's categorization decisions across 52 papers without any validation pass. - The abstract reports n=49 while the PRISMA diagram and body report n=52; this three-paper discrepancy is unexplained and undermines confidence in the count-based findings. - 21 studies (29% of those sought for retrieval) were not obtained; the paper does not attempt to characterize whether these are systematically from certain regions, languages, or publication types, which could distort the gap distribution. - The 2017 lower inclusion bound excludes foundational ontology engineering work predating the cloud/AI convergence wave (e.g., early OWL-based e-government standards) without sensitivity analysis showing this cutoff does not alter conclusions. - The 8-feature design taxonomy (Table II) assigns equal implied weight to all eight features (LLM-based intelligence and basic security appear as co-equal checklist items) without evidence about which features most determine deployment success. - The paper does not compare its findings against prior SLRs on the same intersection (e.g., Agbaegbu et al. 2021 on ontologies in cloud computing [ref 65], Altahir et al. 2023 on semantic web for e-government [ref 68]); citing these as primary sources rather than as parallel reviews leaves the incremental contribution relative to existing surveys unquantified. Bottom Line This SLR delivers a quantified gap map for the e-governance ontology design space that is more structured than prior narrative reviews, but the single-coder methodology, unresolved n=49/52 inconsistency, 21 unretrieved studies, and absence of any proposed or validated artifact limit its utility for practitioners beyond providing a literature entry point.
Search Results
#152 — Incremental (38)

Generative AI and Dynamic Capabilities for Sustainable Supply Chain Performance: Systematic Literature Review

G Lama · osuva.uwasa.fi · simulation environments for business processes - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This thesis presents a systematic literature review (SLR) of 35 peer-reviewed articles to map how Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) supports sensing, seizing, and reconfiguring dynamic capabilities, and how those capabilities connect to Triple Bottom Line (TBL) sustainability outcomes in international supply chains. - Why it's important: Organizations deploying GenAI in global supply chains need a theoretical account of why technology adoption sometimes fails to produce sustainability gains, and the capability-mediated model developed here offers a structured answer — GenAI benefits do not flow automatically but depend on governance maturity, absorptive capacity, and cross-border institutional context. - How it does it: The review maps 35 articles onto Teece's Dynamic Capabilities View (DCV) and the Knowledge-Based View (KBV), organizes findings across six GenAI application clusters and three capability dimensions, and synthesizes a conceptual framework in which GenAI functions as a generative meta-capability whose sustainability payoff is moderated by organizational, institutional, and contextual factors. - The good: The central empirical finding — that GenAI-to-sustainability effects are consistently capability-mediated and not direct, corroborated by multiple quantitative studies using PLS-SEM across China, India, the USA, Taiwan, and South Africa — is the most credible result, grounded in actual structural equation evidence from primary studies. - The missing: The corpus is 35 articles, 80% from 2025–2026, concentrated in China (n=7) and India (n=4), with no studies from Sub-Saharan Africa, Latin America, or the Middle East; the geographic and temporal coverage is too narrow to support the international business generalizations the thesis draws. - Read it or not: Skip for practitioners and researchers seeking causal evidence; useful only as a structured reading list for someone entering the GenAI-supply-chain literature who wants a DCV-organized entry point — but note it is a master's thesis, not a peer-reviewed publication.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (SLR synthesizing largely empirical primary studies). The composite is held below 40 by Rigor (40) and Novelty (35): the PRISMA protocol is documented and the Boolean string is replicable, but quality assessment used a single rater with no inter-rater reliability, the review was not pre-registered, the 83% subject-area pre-screen risks systematic exclusion of interdisciplinary work, and the central 'GenAI-to-sustainability is capability-mediated' formulation is an acknowledged extension of Teece (2025) and Bag et al. (2025), not a new theoretical move. Completeness (50) is helped by direct, honest limitation engagement, but the international-business generalizations overreach a 35-paper, Asia-concentrated corpus (Resilience 38). It is a master's thesis, not peer-reviewed, which caps Rigor. Weighted: 0.20*35 + 0.25*40 + 0.15*35 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*38 + 0.10*25 = 38.
empirical Novelty 35 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 50 Resilience 38 Impact 25
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multinational enterprises (MNEs) and supply chain managers operate under simultaneous pressures: escalating ESG regulatory requirements (EU taxonomy, human rights due diligence directives, carbon pricing systems), geopolitical disruption, and the rapid commercial availability of GenAI tools such as LLMs, GANs, and agentic AI. The academic literature on GenAI in supply chains has expanded sharply since 2023, but three gaps remain: (1) the mechanism linking GenAI adoption to dynamic capability development is under-theorized; (2) the pathway from dynamic capabilities to TBL sustainability outcomes is rarely traced empirically; (3) international business factors — institutional heterogeneity, digital divides across subsidiaries, MNE coordination costs — are treated as peripheral rather than structural conditions. The thesis aims to synthesize 35 studies to close these gaps and produce a multi-level integrative framework. Key Idea: The organizing insight is that GenAI should be understood not as an exogenous technological input but as a generative meta-capability — a higher-order organizational capacity that amplifies sensing, seizing, and reconfiguring capabilities, whose sustainability payoff is then fully or partially mediated by collaborative routines (green supply chain collaboration, circular economy implementation, stakeholder co-creation) and conditioned by governance maturity and institutional context. This reframes the technology-adoption question: instead of asking whether GenAI adoption improves performance, the framework asks what organizational and institutional prerequisites convert GenAI inputs into capability-enabled sustainability outcomes. The idea is not genuinely novel — Teece (2007, 2025) and Bag et al. (2025) make closely related arguments — but the thesis applies it systematically to the GenAI-sustainability domain. Methodology: The review follows PRISMA 2020 guidelines (Page et al., 2021) and the three-step Tranfield et al. (2003) SLR protocol. Scopus and Web of Science Core Collection were searched on 21 March 2026 using a structured Boolean string combining GenAI terms ("Generative AI," "GenAI," "LLM," "ChatGPT"), supply chain terms ("supply chain," "logistics," "sustainab," "ESG"), and DCV terms ("dynamic capabilit," "sensing," "seizing," "reconfigur*"). The search returned 433 records; subject-area filters reduced this to 74; after deduplication (n=10 removed), title-abstract screening (n=20 excluded), and full-text eligibility assessment (n=9 excluded), 35 articles were retained. Each article was quality-assessed on a 0–18-point rubric (six criteria scored 0–3: clear objectives, theoretical grounding, methodological rigor, validity of findings, contribution, IB relevance); thresholds were ≥16 (high confidence), 12–15 (caution), <12 (exclude with justification). Data extraction used a structured form capturing bibliographic details, capability dimensions addressed, sustainability dimensions, GenAI technology examined, and key findings. Synthesis was thematic, organized by the three research sub-questions. The review was not pre-registered and received no external funding. Three critical design decisions: (1) the aggressive subject-area filter before human screening removed 83% of initial records (359/433), risking exclusion of interdisciplinary papers indexed under computing or engineering; (2) the quality rubric is author-developed without inter-rater reliability testing; (3) thematic synthesis rather than meta-analytic aggregation means effect-size heterogeneity across the 19 quantitative PLS-SEM studies is unexamined. Key Results: Of 35 included studies, 19 were quantitative (predominantly PLS-SEM or CB-SEM, survey-based), 4 qualitative, 4 mixed-methods, and 3 SLRs. Publication concentration is extreme: 0 papers in 2020–2021, 1 in 2022, 0 in 2023, 6 in 2024, 14 in 2025, and 14 in the first quarter of 2026 — virtually the entire corpus postdates ChatGPT's commercial release. Geographic coverage: China (n=7), cross-border/global (n=5), India (n=4), Europe (n=2), North America (n=2), other Asia (n=2). Primary findings: (1) GenAI strengthens sensing capabilities most directly and consistently, through large-scale processing of heterogeneous unstructured data (ESG disclosures, geopolitical signals, operational anomalies). (2) Seizing is contingent on governance maturity and task-technology alignment — GenAI enables scenario analysis and multimodal decision support, but resource mobilization requires organizational readiness. (3) Reconfiguring requires institutionalized learning across organizational boundaries; it is the highest-order and most conditionally realized capability. (4) Environmental TBL outcomes are the most robustly evidenced; Li et al. (2024) show green supply chain collaboration and circular economy implementation fully mediate the GenAI–performance relationship in a Chinese manufacturing sample (direct GenAI effect becomes statistically negligible once mediators are controlled). (5) Social outcomes are the least evidenced and most context-dependent. (6) Economic outcomes are consistently indirect and capability-mediated; Joshi et al. (2026) document that market performance remains substantially driven by direct external valuation rather than GenAI-mediated capability. No paper in the corpus reports system-level KPIs across the full SCOR model; evidence concentrates on Plan and Enable functions. Specific quantitative benchmarks from primary studies (e.g., standardized path coefficients, R² values, sample sizes) are cited descriptively but not pooled or formally compared. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION. The thesis does not introduce a new method, system, dataset, or algorithm. It reformulates the relationship between an existing technology class (GenAI) and an established theoretical framework (DCV/KBV) in the supply chain sustainability domain. The specific contribution — treating GenAI as a generative meta-capability within DCV and integrating KBV to expose the knowledge routines through which GenAI inputs become organizationally meaningful — represents a synthesis of existing ideas applied systematically to a new technology-domain pairing. The IB framing (treating institutional heterogeneity and digital divides as cross-cutting boundary conditions rather than control variables) adds a modest theoretical angle. Rigor & Reproducibility: The SLR protocol is documented and largely replicable: the Scopus Boolean string is printed verbatim, the PRISMA flowchart shows all exclusion counts, and the inclusion/exclusion criteria are explicit. However, quality assessment was conducted by a single rater without inter-rater reliability checks, which is a methodological weakness for any SLR. The review was not pre-registered, so the framework's categories could have been shaped post-hoc by the literature rather than defined a priori. The quality scoring rubric (0–18 scale) is not validated against an established instrument (e.g., CASP or Mixed-Methods Appraisal Tool). Code, data, and model weights are not applicable — the primary studies' own data are not pooled or made available. The 35-article corpus is small; the 83% pre-screening exclusion via subject-area filters is aggressive and risks systematic exclusion of relevant interdisciplinary work indexed in computer science or engineering databases (IEEE Xplore, ACM DL, DBLP are not searched). The thesis is a master's-level work from the University of Vaasa; it has not undergone peer review at publication. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (the primary studies reviewed are largely empirical; this SLR synthesizes their findings). - Practical: The resulting conceptual framework and managerial implications are actionable without specialized hardware; deployment barriers exist at the level of the primary studies' recommendations (governance infrastructure, absorptive capacity), not at the framework level. - Efficient: Not applicable directly; the SLR itself is a desk-based synthesis. Primary studies' AI implementations vary widely in compute cost, which the thesis does not characterize. - Effective: The framework successfully organizes 35 heterogeneous studies into a coherent narrative and produces the specific claim that GenAI-sustainability effects are capability-mediated; this is supported by multiple primary studies. However, it rests on thematic aggregation, not formal evidence synthesis, so effect-size magnitude and direction variability across studies remain unknown. - Scalable: The conceptual framework claims applicability across MNE contexts and institutional settings, but the empirical base covers only Asia (primarily China and India), with 2 European and 2 North American studies — scale to Sub-Saharan Africa, Latin America, or ASEAN is asserted rather than evidenced. The thesis delivers a paper-form conceptual framework and a structured reading list (Appendix 1: 35-paper extraction table); it does not deliver a software artifact, dataset, or trained model. Strengths: - The central claim that GenAI-sustainability effects are fully or partially mediated by collaborative capabilities — not direct — is supported by multiple primary PLS-SEM studies across different national contexts, making it the most credible takeaway for practitioners. - The integration of KBV with DCV to expose the knowledge-acquisition and knowledge-integration routines through which GenAI inputs become organizationally meaningful is a useful theoretical move that goes beyond simply relabeling GenAI as "another IT capability." - The moderator taxonomy (organizational micro-foundations, institutional factors, contextual factors including firm size and industry) is more granular than most prior treatments and correctly identifies governance maturity as a necessary condition rather than an enabler. - The IB lens — treating digital divides, MNE subsidiary coordination, and cross-jurisdictional ESG fragmentation as structural boundary conditions — is a more substantive angle than most supply chain AI papers, which treat international context as scenery. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge: (1) restriction to two databases (Scopus and Web of Science) and English-language literature; (2) exclusion of grey literature and industry practice documents; (3) rapid GenAI technology evolution means recent agentic-AI and multimodal developments may fall outside the review window; (4) synthesis quality depends on reporting completeness of primary studies; (5) thematic analysis carries interpretive bias risks. The geographic concentration in Asia is noted descriptively as a limitation. The thesis engages these limitations directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The thesis does not acknowledge several important weaknesses. First, the aggressive pre-screening filter (359/433 records excluded at the subject-area stage) almost certainly excludes relevant GenAI-supply-chain papers published in computing and engineering outlets — the review's claim to cover the literature is overstated. Second, with 83% of the corpus from 2025–2026, many reviewed papers are themselves very recent and unvalidated by replication; the thesis treats early-stage conceptual papers from this period as evidence with the same confidence as established empirical findings. Third, the quality rubric was applied by a single rater with no interrater reliability statistic — any quality-score-based exclusion decisions are unstatable in confidence terms. Fourth, the "theoretical contributions" are reframings rather than novel theoretical moves: repositioning GenAI as a meta-capability within DCV is an extension of Teece (2025) and Bag et al. (2025), which the thesis itself cites as making essentially this argument. Fifth, the thesis conflates claims from the primary studies with evidence: vendor-style assertions (e.g., DHL, Amazon, Walmart efficiency gains) are cited as empirical grounding in the introduction without noting they are self-reported. Sixth, no sensitivity analysis or subgroup analysis is conducted to assess whether findings from China-based PLS-SEM surveys generalize — the geographic extrapolation to MNE global strategy is asserted, not tested. Bottom Line: A master's thesis that competently organizes a small, very recent corpus around a useful DCV-mediation framework, but makes broader international business claims than its 35-paper, Asia-concentrated, unregistered synthesis can support; researchers who need a structured entry point to the 2024–2026 GenAI-supply-chain literature will find the extraction table useful, while those seeking causal evidence or generalizable theory should look to the primary studies directly.
Search Results
#153 — Incremental (38)

Facilitating AI Implementation

F ANDERSSON, E PALM · odr.chalmers.se · genai manufacturing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This Master's thesis develops the AKSEA Framework, a five-area non-sequential decision-support tool for management teams at manufacturing SMEs (small and medium-sized enterprises) to plan and execute GenAI and AI agent adoption, grounded in a PRISMA-guided literature review of 15 papers and semi-structured interviews with 14 participants across 8 Gothenburg-region companies. - Why it's important: Manufacturing SMEs employ 2.1 million people in Sweden but have GenAI adoption rates of only 30.8% (vs 71.9% for large firms), and existing implementation frameworks are either too sector-generic, too technology-broad, or restricted to single business functions, leaving management teams without actionable GenAI-specific guidance. - How it does it: Inductive thematic analysis of literature and interview transcripts surfaces 15 Influencing Factors, which are then deductively mapped onto the Technology-Organisation-Environment-Individual (TOEI) framework and synthesized into five Priority Areas (Responsibility, Competence, Strategy, Engagement, AI Policy) arranged in a non-sequential architecture to accommodate firms at varying digital maturity. - The good: The empirical discovery that variation within individual SMEs is as large as variation between firms — a minority of employees become proficient while the broader workforce stays disengaged — directly motivates the non-sequential design and is a genuine, under-reported finding. - The missing: The AKSEA Framework has never been applied by an independent sample; all validation comes from workshop feedback sessions with a subset of the same 8 companies that provided interview data, meaning there is no outcome evidence that the framework changes adoption behavior. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners in SME digital transformation or researchers mapping the Nordic manufacturing GenAI landscape; skip if you need empirical evidence of framework efficacy, as none is provided.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (30) and Resilience (35) pull the composite down: the AKSEA artifact is a Swedish-language 60-slide PowerPoint with no outcome evidence, and the framework's validation is circular — all 5 workshops used subsets of the same 8 companies that supplied the interview data, so no independent sample ever tested efficacy. The GTC-network recruitment biases the sample toward change-ready firms, weakening external resilience. Rigor (40) is mixed: a credible PRISMA literature review offsets a thin qualitative strand (n=14, no saturation analysis, no inter-rater reliability). The intra-firm-equals-inter-firm competence-variation finding is a genuine, under-reported empirical insight (Non-Obviousness 38, Completeness 55 with honest limitation acknowledgment), but the 15 Influencing Factors are largely inherited from prior TOE/TAM literature, leaving GenAI specificity unproven. Weighted: 0.15*35 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*38 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*35 + 0.30*30 = 38.
systems Novelty 35 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 38 Completeness 55 Resilience 35 Impact 30
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Manufacturing firms globally, and Swedish manufacturing SMEs in particular, have the lowest GenAI adoption rates of any firm-size category: 30.8% for small enterprises vs 71.9% for large firms (Statistics Sweden, 2025). These firms face compounded constraints — limited capital, thin IT staff, no dedicated change-management function — that make general frameworks designed for large enterprises or mixed-sector SMEs poorly matched to their reality. Survey data from Tillväxtverket (2025) confirms that Swedish SMEs perceive existing guidance as too abstract and too academic. The parallel research gap: three frameworks identified in the literature (Sarkar 2026, Amarasuriya and Abston 2026, Sánchez et al. 2025) either omit AI agents entirely, cover all AI technologies without GenAI specificity, or address only one business function (procurement), none with geographic or manufacturing-sector grounding. Key Idea The central insight is that the dominant barrier to GenAI adoption in manufacturing SMEs is not technical readiness but organisational and individual readiness, and that a framework structured as non-sequential Priority Areas rather than a linear roadmap better serves firms whose digital maturity varies widely both across companies and within the same firm. This design principle follows directly from the empirical finding that implementation in all 8 interviewed companies is currently driven by individual employee initiative rather than formal organisational strategy, producing a fragmented internal landscape that a sequential roadmap cannot address. Methodology The study uses a qualitative multi-method design. The first strand is a quasi-systematic literature review: a Scopus search (23 February 2026) using a compound string on change management, technology implementation, digital transformation, and SMEs yielded 141 records; PRISMA screening with dual-blind review via Rayyan.ai produced 15 included articles. The second strand is primary data collection: 14 participants across 8 manufacturing SMEs recruited through Gothenburg Technical College's (GTC) industry network, covering roles from CEO to Production Manager to Accounting (Table 3.1). Interviews were recorded, auto-transcribed with the Chalmers AI Portal tool, manually verified, and translated from Swedish. Analysis proceeded in two stages: inductive thematic analysis following Braun and Clarke (2006) developed initial codes independently for literature (5 themes) and interviews (8 themes), which were then iteratively merged into 15 Influencing Factors and deductively categorised into the four TOEI pillars. Framework development went through four versions (1.0 through 3.0) co-developed with practitioner feedback via 5 workshops with a subset of the 8 companies, each concluding with an anonymous survey. Critical design decisions: the decision to conduct literature review and interviews in parallel rather than sequentially traded depth of theoretical grounding for temporal efficiency; the decision to omit environmental/regulatory questions from the interview guide (to focus on internal readiness) resulted in the Environment TOEI dimension being underpowered by empirical data; and the decision to author the final framework in Swedish and distribute it as a 60-slide PowerPoint prioritises practitioner accessibility over academic transferability. Key Results The study identifies 15 Influencing Factors distributed across TOEI: Technology (2 factors: Data Security T1, Digital Infrastructure T2), Organisation (6: Resources O1, Top Management Support O2, Change Management Competence O3, Culture of Communication and Collaboration O4, Strategy O5, Digital Competence O6), Environment (5: Competitive Pressure E1, Customer Pressure E2, Government Support E3, Laws and Regulations E4, Collaborative Networks and Partnerships E5), and Individual (2: Attitude I1, Perceived Usefulness I2). Empirical findings from interviews: all 8 SMEs have experimented with GenAI, primarily via ChatGPT and Microsoft Copilot for text generation and information retrieval; 6 of 8 companies cite data security as a primary concern; only 2 of 8 have any AI policy; none has a long-term AI strategy; 1 of 8 (Company G) has deployed an expert chatbot with internal knowledge access — a capability 3 other companies identified as desired but unachieved. A key divergence from the literature: Swedish SMEs did not emphasise hierarchical top-management mandates but instead described successful change as requiring buy-in from all levels simultaneously, which the authors attribute to flat Scandinavian organisational culture. The AKSEA Framework resulting from synthesis contains 5 Priority Areas: Responsibility (defines Change Management Group, Decision-makers, Employees roles), Competence (User Competence for all staff + Coordination Expertise for the Change Management Group; integrates SECI model), Strategy (current-state assessment, SMART goal-setting, use-case toolbox, pilot project emphasis), Engagement (motivation via Self-Determination Theory, culture change via Kotter's accelerators), and AI Policy (GDPR and EU AI Act compliance, references to Swedish regulatory authorities DIGG and IMY). Workshop feedback was quantitative (survey) and qualitative (verbal); summary appears in Appendix D but no aggregate adoption-outcome data is reported. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The contribution is a practitioner-facing decision-support artifact (the AKSEA Framework) tailored to a specific underserved context (GenAI + AI agents in Gothenburg manufacturing SMEs). The theoretical apparatus — TOEI, Kotter's Eight Accelerators, SECI, Self-Determination Theory, SMART goals — consists entirely of established models. The synthesis and the non-sequential Priority Area architecture applied specifically to GenAI/AI-agent adoption represent genuine contextual novelty, but the study introduces no new concept, method, or formal result. Rigor & Reproducibility The literature review is the methodologically strongest component: PRISMA protocol, single database (Scopus, which is appropriate for academic rigour), dual-blind independent screening, documented inclusion/exclusion criteria. The interview component is weaker: n = 14 across 8 companies with purposive GTC-network recruitment; no saturation analysis; no inter-rater reliability reported for thematic coding; no member-checking beyond workshop sessions. The framework validation has a circular problem: all 5 workshops were conducted with subsets of the same 8 companies that generated the interview data — no independent sample ever assessed AKSEA. No quantitative outcome measures (adoption rate change, implementation completion rate, competence scores) were collected before or after framework exposure. The thesis is unpublished at the peer-review level; all results should be treated as preprint-equivalent in terms of confidence. The AKSEA Framework exists as a Swedish-language 60-slide PowerPoint deck (Appendix E), which is not a reproducible or independently deployable research artifact. Deployability (SYSTEMS profile) - Practical: The framework requires only a management workshop and the ability to navigate a PowerPoint presentation; no hardware or software infrastructure is needed beyond what SMEs already possess. - Efficient: The resource cost of the framework itself is low; the cost of implementing any specific Priority Area recommendation (e.g., building a Change Management Group, conducting a digital maturity assessment, rolling out training) depends entirely on the firm's starting state and is not estimated anywhere in the thesis. - Effective: Workshop feedback from a non-independent sample suggests perceived usefulness and practical relevance, but no pre/post adoption outcome measurement exists. Effectiveness on actual GenAI implementation rates is unproven. - Scalable: The non-sequential design accommodates firms at different digital maturity levels within the Gothenburg manufacturing context, but the framework's geographic specificity (Swedish regulatory references, Swedish language) and sector specificity limit direct deployment outside that context without adaptation. The authors explicitly flag this. The work delivers a practitioner PowerPoint artifact and a thesis document. It does not deliver code, a trained model, a dataset, or a tested deployment. Strengths - The finding that intra-firm variation in AI competence is comparable in magnitude to inter-firm variation is a concrete, empirically grounded insight that challenges aggregate adoption statistics and has direct implications for how training programs should be designed. - The non-sequential Priority Area architecture is a principled design response to that heterogeneity finding, not an arbitrary choice, which distinguishes AKSEA from the linear roadmaps of Sarkar (2026) and Sánchez et al. (2025). - PRISMA-guided systematic literature review with Rayyan.ai blind mode is a credible methodological choice for the secondary data strand, providing a replicable evidence base. - Authoring the framework in Swedish and embedding it in a 60-slide PowerPoint directly addresses the practitioner complaint (documented via Tillväxtverket 2025) that existing guidance is too abstract. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront the following: small and potentially self-selected interview sample (14 participants, 8 companies that volunteered via GTC's network); the parallel-methods design traded sequential depth for speed; the interview guide underrepresented environmental/regulatory factors; the framework has inherent subjectivity in its design choices; geographic specificity constrains generalisability; and rapid AI technology evolution risks making the framework obsolete. These are stated plainly and without minimisation, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The validation is not independent. All workshop feedback came from a subset of the same 8 companies that generated the interview data; no external SME ever evaluated AKSEA. This is a circular confirmation problem that the authors do not fully acknowledge as a validity threat. - The 15 Influencing Factors are almost entirely drawn from prior TOE and TAM literature on general technology adoption and Industry 4.0; the claim of GenAI specificity rests on the use-case toolbox in the Strategy Priority Area, not on the factor structure itself. The thesis does not demonstrate which (if any) factors are meaningfully different for GenAI versus prior emerging technologies. - The sample is systematically biased toward change-ready firms: companies recruited through GTC's industry network are by definition already engaged with educational and industrial training institutions, making them unrepresentative of the typical isolated manufacturing SME that the framework targets. - The study never tests whether the Influencing Factors have differential importance across firms or contexts. All 15 factors are treated as equally relevant, with no attempt to rank or weight them, even though an SME-facing decision-support tool would benefit enormously from knowing which 3 factors to address first. - The framework exists only as a Swedish-language PowerPoint deck. It has not been released as a public digital tool, piloted without researcher facilitation, or tested by a company that discovered it independently. Transferability from researcher-led workshops to self-directed SME use is entirely untested.
Search Results
#154 — Incremental (38)

Bridging the Communicative Gap: Translating Technical Expertise into Business Value in B2B Deep-Tech Startups

M Zinou · diva-portal.org · simulation environments for business processes - new results
